Groups, Ô¨Årst encounter
Chapter II
In this chapter we introduce groups, we observe they form a category (called Grp),
and we study ‚Äògeneral‚Äô features of this category: what are the monomorphisms and
the epimorphisms in this category? what is the appropriate notion of ‚Äòequivalence
relation‚Äô and ‚Äòquotients‚Äô for a group? does a ‚Äòdecomposition theorem‚Äô hold in Grp?
and other analogous questions.
In Chapter III we will acquire a similar degree of familiarity with rings and
modules. A more object-oriented analysis of Grp (for example, a treatment of the
famous Sylow theorems, ‚Äòcomposition series‚Äô, or the classiÔ¨Åcation of Ô¨Ånite abelian
groups) is deferred to Chapter IV.
1. DeÔ¨Ånition of group
1.1. Groups and groupoids.
Joke 1.1. DeÔ¨Ånition: A group is a groupoid with a single object.

This is actually a perfectly viable deÔ¨Ånition, since groupoids have been deÔ¨Åned
already (in Example I.4.6); but most mathematicians would Ô¨Ånd it ludicrous to
introduce groups in this fashion, or they will at the very least politely express
doubts on the pedagogical eÔ¨Äectiveness of doing so. In order to redeem myself, I
will parse this deÔ¨Ånition right away to show what it really says. If ‚àó is the lone
object of such a groupoid G,
HomG (‚àó, ‚àó) = AutG (‚àó)
(because G is a groupoid!), and this set carries all the information about G. Call
this set G. Then (by deÔ¨Ånition of category) there is an associative operation on G,
with an identity 1‚àó , and (by deÔ¨Ånition of groupoid, which says that every morphism
in G is an isomorphism) every g ‚àà G has an inverse g ‚àí1 ‚àà G.
41
42
 II. Groups, Ô¨Årst encounter
That is what a group is1 : a set G with a composition law satifsying a few key
axioms, i.e., associativity, existence of identity, and existence of inverses.
1.2. DeÔ¨Ånition. Now for the oÔ¨Écial deÔ¨Ånition. Let G be a nonempty set, endowed
with a binary operation, that is, a ‚Äòmultiplication‚Äô map
‚Ä¢ : G √ó G ‚Üí G.
Our notation will be
‚Ä¢(g, h) =: g ‚Ä¢ h
or simply gh if the name of the operation can be understood. The careful reader
may have expected that we should write h ‚Ä¢ g, in the style of what we have done
for categories, but this is what common conventions dictate2 .
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.2. The set G, endowed with the binary operation ‚Ä¢ (brieÔ¨Çy, (G, ‚Ä¢),
or simply G if the operation can be understood) is a group if
(i) the operation ‚Ä¢ is associative, that is,
(‚àÄg, h, k ‚àà G) :
 (g ‚Ä¢ h) ‚Ä¢ k = g ‚Ä¢ (h ‚Ä¢ k);
(ii) there exists an identity element eG for ‚Ä¢, that is,
(‚àÉeG ‚àà G) (‚àÄg ‚àà G) :
 g ‚Ä¢ eG = g = eG ‚Ä¢ g;
(iii) every element in G has an inverse with respect to ‚Ä¢, that is,
(‚àÄg ‚àà G) (‚àÉh ‚àà G) :
 g ‚Ä¢ h = eG = h ‚Ä¢ g.
 
Example 1.3. Since we explicitly require G to be nonempty, the most economical
way to concoct a group is by letting G = {e} be a singleton. There is only one
function G √ó G ‚Üí G in this case, so there is only one possible binary operation
on G, deÔ¨Åned by
e ‚Ä¢ e := e.
The three axioms trivially hold for this example, so {e} is equipped with a unique
group structure.
This is usually called the trivial group; purists should call any such group a
trivial group, since every singleton gives rise to one.
 
Example 1.4. The reader should check carefully (cf. Exercise 1.2) that (Z, +),
(Q, +), (R, +), (C, +), and several variations using ¬∑ (for example, the subset
{+1, ‚àí1} of Z, with ordinary multiplication) all give examples of groups. While
very interesting in themselves, these examples do not really capture at any intuitive
level ‚Äòwhat‚Äô a group really is, because they are too special. For example, all these
examples are commutative (see ¬ß1.5).
 
1 From this perspective, Joke 1.1 is a little imprecise: the group is not the groupoid G, but
rather the set of isomorphisms in G, endowed with the operation of composition of morphisms.
2 Not without exceptions; see for example permutation groups, discussed in ¬ß2.

1.3. Basic properties. From the ‚Äògroupoid‚Äô point of view, the identity eG would
be denoted 1‚àó . It is not uncommon to omit G from the notation (when the group

1 and 0 are popular alternatives, depending on the context. In any case, for any
given group G this element is unique. That is, no other element of G can work as
an identity:
Proposition 1.6. If h ‚àà G is an identity of G, then h = eG .
Incidentally, this makes groups pointed sets in the sense of Example I.3.8: every
group has a well-deÔ¨Åned distinguished element.
Proof. Using Ô¨Årst that eG is an identity and then that h is an identity, one gets3
h = eG h = eG .

identity.)

then h1 = h2 .

1.4. Cancellation. ‚ÄòCancellation‚Äô holds in groups. That is,
Proposition 1.8. Let G be a group. Then ‚àÄa, g, h ‚àà G
ga = ha =‚áí g = h,
 ag = ah =‚áí g = h.
Proof. Both statements are proven by multiplying (on the appropriate side) by a‚àí1
and applying associativity. For example,
ga = ha =‚áí (ga)a‚àí1 = (ha)a ‚àí1 =‚áí g(aa‚àí1) = h(aa‚àí1 ) =‚áí geG = heG
=‚áí g = h.
The proof for the other implication follows the same pattern.
 
Examples of operations which do not satisfy cancellation, and hence do not
deÔ¨Åne groups, abound. For instance, the operation of ordinary multiplication does
not make the set R of real numbers into a group: indeed, 0 ‚Äòcannot be cancelled‚Äô
since 1 ¬∑ 0 = 2 ¬∑ 0 even if 1 = 2. Of course, the problem here is that 0 does not
have an inverse in R, with respect to multiplication. As it happens, this is the only
problem with this example: ordinary multiplication does make
R‚àó := R  {0}
into a group, as the reader should check immediately.
1.5. Commutative groups. One axiom not appearing in the deÔ¨Ånition of group
is commutativity: we say that the operation ‚Ä¢ is ‚Äòcommutative‚Äô if
(iv) (‚àÄg, h ‚àà G) :
 g ‚Ä¢ h = h ‚Ä¢ g.
We say that two elements g, h ‚Äòcommute‚Äô if gh = hg. Thus, in a commutative
group any two elements commute.
‚ÄòCommutative groups‚Äô are important objects: they arise naturally in several
contexts, especially as ‚Äòmodules over the ring Z‚Äô (about which we will have a lot to
say in Chapter III and beyond). When they do so, they are usually called abelian
groups.
The notation used in treating abelian groups diÔ¨Äers somewhat from the stan-
dard notation for groups. This is to emphasize the ‚ÄòZ-module structure‚Äô, and it is
helpful when an abelian group coexists with other operations‚Äîa situation which
we will encounter frequently.
Thus, the operation in an abelian group A is, as a rule, denoted by + and is
called ‚Äòaddition‚Äô; the identity is then called 0A ; and the inverse of an element a ‚àà A
is denoted ‚àía (and maybe should be called the ‚Äòopposite‚Äô ?). The ‚Äòpower‚Äô notation
is of course replaced by ‚Äòmultiple‚Äô: 0a = 0, and for a positive integer n
na = a + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + a,
 (‚àín)a = (‚àía) + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ (‚àía) .

 
n times
 n times
The reader should keep in mind that at this stage ‚Äòna‚Äô is a notation, not the
result of applying a binary operation to two elements n, a of A. Indeed, n ‚àà Z may
very well not be an element of A in any reasonable sense. Moreover, it may very
46
 II. Groups, Ô¨Årst encounter
well be that na = ma even if n = m, in spite of the fact that ‚Äòcancellation‚Äô works
in groups.
The qualiÔ¨Åer ‚Äòabelian‚Äô and the notation 0A , ‚àía, etc., are mostly used for com-
mutative groups arising in certain standard situations: for example, the notions
of rings, modules, vector spaces are deÔ¨Åned by suitably enriching a commutative
group, which is then promoted to ‚Äòabelian‚Äô for notational convenience.
There are other situations in which commutative groups arise naturally, without
triggering the ‚Äòabelian‚Äô notation. For example, the group (R‚àó , ¬∑) mentioned at the
end of ¬ß1.4 is commutative, but its operation is indicated by ¬∑ (if at all), and its
identity element is written 1. History, rather than logic, is often the main factor
determining notation.
1.6. Order.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.9. An element g of a group G has Ô¨Ånite order if 6 gn = e for some
positive integer n. In this case, the order |g| is the smallest positive n such that
gn = e. One writes |g| = ‚àû if g does not have Ô¨Ånite order.
 
By deÔ¨Ånition, if gn = e for some positive integer n, then |g| ‚â§ n. One can be
more precise:
Lemma 1.10. If gn = e for some positive integer n, then |g| is a divisor of n.
Proof. As observed, n ‚â• |g| by deÔ¨Ånition of order, that is, n ‚àí |g| ‚â• 0. There must
then exist7 a positive integer m such that
r = n ‚àí |g| ¬∑ m ‚â• 0 and
 n ‚àí |g| ¬∑ (m + 1) < 0,
that is, r < |g|. Note that
g r = g n‚àí|g|¬∑m = g n ¬∑ (g|g|)‚àím = e ¬∑ e‚àím = e.
By deÔ¨Ånition of order, |g| is the smallest positive integer such that g|g| = e. Since
r is smaller than |g| and gr = e, r cannot be positive; hence r = 0 necessarily. This
says
0 = n ‚àí |g| ¬∑ m,
that is, n = |g|¬∑m, proving that n is indeed an integer multiple of |g| as claimed.
 
This lemma has the following immediate and useful consequence, which we
encourage the reader to keep Ô¨Årmly in mind:
Corollary 1.11. Let g be an element of Ô¨Ånite order, and let N ‚àà Z. Then
gN = e ‚áê‚áí N is a multiple of |g|.
6Of course in an abelian group we would write the following prescription as ng = 0.
7 Purists may object that here I am surreptitiously using fairly sophisticated information
about Z, namely the ‚Äòdivision algorithm‚Äô, hence essentially the fact that Z is a Euclidean domain!

be open about it and admit that yes, I am assuming that my readers have already acquired a
thorough familiarity with the operations of addition and multiplication among integers. Shame
on me!
1. DeÔ¨Ånition of group
 47
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.12. If G is Ô¨Ånite as a set, its order |G| is the number of its elements;
we write |G| = ‚àû if G is inÔ¨Ånite.
 
Cancellation implies that |g| ‚â§ |G| for all g ‚àà G. Indeed, this is vacuously true
if |G| = ‚àû; if G is Ô¨Ånite, consider the |G| + 1 powers
g0 = e, g, g2 , g3, . . . , g |G|
of g. These cannot all be distinct; hence
(‚àÉi, j) 0 ‚â§ i < j ‚â§ |G| such that g i = g j .
By cancellation (that is, multiplying on the right by g‚àíi )
gj‚àíi = e,
showing |g| ‚â§ (j ‚àí i) ‚â§ |G|.
We will soon be able to formulate a much more precise statement concerning
the relation between the order of a group and the order of its elements: if g ‚àà G
and |G| is Ô¨Ånite, then the order of g divides the order of G. This will be an
immediate consequence of Lagrange‚Äôs theorem; cf. Example 8.15.
Another general remark concerning orders is that their behavior with respect
to the operation of the group is not always predictable: it may very well happen
that g, h have Ô¨Ånite order in a group G, and yet |gh| = ‚àû, or |gh| = your favorite
positive integer: work out Exercise 1.12 and Exercise 2.6 if you don‚Äôt believe it.
On the other hand, the situation is more constrained if g and h commute. In
the extreme case in which g = h, it is easy to obtain a very precise statement:

order ‚àÄm ‚â• 0, and in fact8

2. Examples of groups
2.1. Symmetric groups. In ¬ßI.4.1 we have already observed that every object A
of every category C determines a group, called AutC (A), namely the group of auto-
morphisms of A. In a somewhat artiÔ¨Åcial sense it is clear that every group arises in
this fashion (cf. Exercise 1.1); this fact is true in more ‚Äòmeaningful‚Äô ways, which will
become apparent when we discuss group actions (¬ß9): cf. especially Theorem 9.5
and Exercise 9.17.
50
 II. Groups, Ô¨Årst encounter
In any case, this observation provides the reader with an inÔ¨Ånite class of very
important examples:
DeÔ¨Ånition 2.1. Let A be a set. The symmetric group, or group of permutations
of A, denoted SA , is the group AutSet (A). The group of permutations of the set
{1, . . . , n} is denoted by Sn .
 
The terminology is easily justiÔ¨Åed: the automorphisms of a set A are the set-
isomorphisms, that is, the bijections, from A to itself; applying such a bijection
amounts precisely to permuting (‚Äòscrambling‚Äô) the elements of A. This operation
may be viewed as a transformation of A which does not change it (as a set), hence
a ‚Äòsymmetry‚Äô.
The groups SA are famously large: as the reader checked in Exercise I.2.1,
|Sn| = n!. For example, |S70 | > 10100 , which is substantially larger than the
estimated number of elementary particles in the observable universe.
Potentially confusing point: The various conventions clash in the way the op-
eration in SA should be written. From the ‚Äòautomorphism‚Äô point of view, elements
of SA are functions and should be composed as such; thus, if f, g ‚àà SA = AutSet(A),
then the ‚Äòproduct‚Äô of f and g should be written g ‚ó¶ f and should act as follows:
(‚àÄp ‚àà A) :
 g ‚ó¶ f (p) = g(f (p)).
But the prevailing style of notation in group theory would write this element
as f g, apparently reversing the order in which the operation is performed.
Everything would fall back into agreement if we adopted the convention of writ-
ing functions after the elements on which they act rather than before: (p)f rather
than f (p). But one cannot change century-old habits, so we have no alternative
but to live with both conventions and to state carefully which one we are using at
any given time.
Contemplating the groups Sn for small values of n is an exercise of inestimable
value. Of course S1 is a trivial group; S2 consists of the two possible permutations:


2 ‚Üí 2

ee = f f = e,

In practice we cannot give a new name to every diÔ¨Äerent element of every permuta-
tion group, so we have to develop a more Ô¨Çexible notation. There are in fact several
possible choices for this; for the time being, we will indicate an element œÉ ‚àà Sn by
listing the eÔ¨Äect of applying œÉ underneath the list 1, . . . , n, as a matrix9. Thus the

On the other hand, these six elements are all distinct‚Äîthis may be checked by

would get x = y‚àí1 by cancellation, and this cannot be since the relations tell us

of S3 :
S3 = {e, x, y, xy, y 2 , xy 2 }.
In the process we have veriÔ¨Åed that S3 may also be described as the group ‚Äògener-
ated‚Äô by two elements x and y, with the ‚Äòrelations‚Äô x2 = e, y 3 = e, yx = xy 2 .
More generally, a subset A of a group G ‚Äògenerates‚Äô G if every element of G
may be written as a product of elements of A and of inverses of elements of A. We
will deal with this notion more formally in ¬ß6.3 and with descriptions of groups in
terms of generators and relations in ¬ß8.2.
2.2. Dihedral groups. A ‚Äòsymmetry‚Äô is a transformation which preserves a struc-
ture. This is of course just a loose way to talk about automorphisms, when we may
be too lazy to deÔ¨Åne rigorously the relevant category. As automorphisms of objects
of a category, symmetries will naturally form groups.
One context in which this notion may be visualized vividly is that of ‚Äògeometric
Ô¨Ågures‚Äô such as polygons in the plane or polyhedra in space. The relevant category
could be deÔ¨Åned as follows: let the objects be subsets of an ordinary plane R2 and
let morphisms between two subsets A, B consist of the ‚Äòrigid motions‚Äô of the plane
(such as translations, rotations, or reÔ¨Çections about a line) which map A to a subset
of B. A rigorous treatment of these notions would be too distracting at this point,
so I will appeal to the intuition of the reader, as I do every now and then.
From this perspective, the ‚Äòsymmetries‚Äô of a subset of the plane are the rigid
motions which map it onto itself; they clearly form a group.
The dihedral groups may be deÔ¨Åned as these groups of symmetries for the
regular polygons. Placing the polygon so that it is centered at the origin (thereby
excluding translations as possible symmetries), we see that the dihedral group for
a regular n-sided polygon consists of the n rotations by 2œÄ/n radians about the
origin and the n distinct reÔ¨Çections about lines through the origin and a vertex or a
midpoint of a side. Thus, the dihedral group for a regular n-sided polygon consists
of 2n elements; I will denote11 this group by the symbol D2n .
Again, contemplating these groups, at least for small values of n, is a wonder-
ful exercise. There is a simple way to relate the dihedral groups to the symmetric
groups of ¬ß2.1, capturing the fact that a symmetry of a regular polygon P is de-
termined by the fate of the vertices of P . For example, label the vertices of an
equilateral triangle clockwise by 1, 2, 3; then a counterclockwise rotation by an
angle of 2œÄ/3 sends vertex 1 to vertex 3, 3 to 2, and 2 to 1, and no other symmetry
of the triangle does the same.

but I am trying to illustrate the fact that the relations are ‚Äòall we need to know‚Äô.
11 Unfortunately there does not seem to be universal agreement on this notation: some ref-
erences use the symbol Dn for what I call D2n here.

D6 ‚Üí S3 and D8 ‚Üí S 4 .

homomorphisms between D6 and S 3 ; we say that these groups are isomorphic
(cf. ¬ß4.3). We will study these concepts very carefully in the next several sections.

2.3. Cyclic groups and modular arithmetic. Let n be a positive integer. Con-
sider the equivalence relation on Z deÔ¨Åned by12
(‚àÄa, b ‚àà Z) :
 a ‚â° b mod n ‚áê‚áí n | (b ‚àí a).
This is called congruence modulo n. We have encountered this relation already, for
n = 2, in Example I.1.3. It is very easy to check that it is an equivalence relation,
for all n; the set of equivalence classes is often denoted by Zn , Z/(n), Z/nZ, or Fn .
We will opt for Z/nZ, which is not preempted by other notions13 . I will denote
by [a]n the equivalence class of the integer a modulo n, or simply [a] if no ambiguity
arises.
The reader should check carefully that Z/nZ consists of exactly n elements,
namely
[0]n , [1]n , ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ , [n ‚àí 1]n .
We can use the group structure on Z to induce an (abelian) group structure on
Z/nZ. In order to do this, we deÔ¨Åne an operation + on Z/nZ, by setting ‚àÄa, b ‚àà Z
[a] + [b] := [a + b].
Of course we have to check that this prescription is well-deÔ¨Åned; luckily, this is
very easy: the following small lemma does the job, as it shows that the result of
the operation does not depend on the representatives chosen for the classes.
Lemma 2.2. If a ‚â° a mod n and b ‚â° b  mod n, then
(a + b) ‚â° (a + b)
 mod n.
Proof. By hypothesis n | (a ‚àí a) and n | (b ‚àí b); therefore ‚àÉk, ‚àà Z such that
(a ‚àí a) = kn,
 (b ‚àí b) = n.
Then
(a + b ) ‚àí (a + b) = (a ‚àí a) + (b ‚àí b) = kn + n = (k + )n,
proving that n divides (a + b ) ‚àí (a + b), as needed.
 
Therefore, we have a binary operation + on Z/nZ. It is immediately checked
that the resulting structure is a group. Associativity is inherited from Z:
([a] + [b]) + [c] = [a + b] + [c] = [(a + b) + c] = [a + (b + c)] = [a] + [b + c] = [a] + ([b] + [c]);
and so are the identity [0] and ‚Äòinverse‚Äô ‚àí[a] = [‚àía].
It is also immediately checked that the resulting groups Z/nZ are commutative,
as the abelian-style notation suggests:
[a] + [b] = [a + b] = [b + a] = [b] + [a].

I trust that this material is not new to the reader, who should in any case check

The abelian groups thus obtained, together with Z, are called cyclic groups;
a popular alternative notation for the group (Z/nZ, +) is Cn . This is adopted
especially when one wants to use the ‚Äòmultiplicative‚Äô rather than ‚Äòadditive‚Äô notation;
thus we can say that Cn is generated by one element x, with the relation xn = e.
Cyclic groups are tremendously important, and we will come back to them in
later sections. For the time being we record the fact that the element
[1]n ‚àà Z/nZ
generates the group, in the sense that every other element may be obtained as a
multiple of this element. For example, if m ‚â• 0 is an integer, then

Proposition 2.3. The order of [m]n in Z/nZ is 1 if n | m, and more generally
n
|[m]n | =

Proof. If n | m, then [m]n = [0]n . If n does not divide m, observe again that
[m]n = m [1]n and apply Proposition 1.13.
 
Remark 2.4. As a consequence, the order of every element of Z/nZ divides n =
|Z/nZ|, the order of the group. We will see later (Example 8.15) that this is a
general feature of the order of elements in any Ô¨Ånite group.
 
Corollary 2.5. The class [m]n generates Z/nZ if and only if gcd(m, n) = 1.
This simple result is quite important. For example, if n = p is a prime integer,
it shows that every nonzero class in the group Z/pZ generates it. In any case,
it allows us to construct more examples of interesting groups. The reader should
check (or recall; cf. Exercise 2.14) that there also is a well-deÔ¨Åned multiplication
on Z/nZ, given by
[a]n ¬∑ [b]n := [ab]n .
This operation does not deÔ¨Åne a group structure on Z/nZ: indeed, the class [0]n
does not have a multiplicative inverse. On the other hand, for any positive n denote
by (Z/nZ)‚àó the subset of Z/nZ consisting of classes [m]n such that gcd(m, n) = 1:
(Z/nZ)‚àó := {[m]n ‚àà Z/nZ | gcd(m, n) = 1}.
This subset is clearly well-deÔ¨Åned: if m ‚â° m mod n, then gcd(m, n) = 1 ‚áê‚áí
gcd(m , n) = 1 (Exercise 2.17), so the deÔ¨Åning property of classes in (Z/nZ)‚àó is
independent of representatives.
Proposition 2.6. Multiplication makes (Z/nZ)‚àó into a group.
56
 II. Groups, Ô¨Årst encounter
Proof. Simple properties of gcd‚Äôs show that if gcd(m1 , n) = gcd(m2 , n) = 1, then
gcd(m1m2 , n) = 1. (For example, if a prime integer divided both n and m1m2 , then
it would necessarily divide m1 or m2 , and one of the two gcd‚Äôs would not be 1.)
Therefore, the product of two elements in (Z/nZ)‚àó is an element of (Z/nZ)‚àó , and
¬∑ does deÔ¨Åne a binary operation
(Z/nZ)‚àó √ó (Z/nZ)‚àó ‚Üí (Z/nZ)‚àó .
It is clear that this operation is associative (because multiplication is associative
in Z); [1]n is an element of (Z/nZ)‚àó and is an identity with respect to multiplication;
so all we have to check is that elements of (Z/nZ)‚àó have multiplicative inverses
in (Z/nZ)‚àó .
This follows from Corollary 2.5. If gcd(m, n) = 1, then [m]n generates the
additive group Z/nZ, and hence some multiple of [m]n must equal [1]n :
(‚àÉa ‚àà Z) :
 a ¬∑ [m]n = [1]n ;
this implies
[a]n [m]n = [1]n .
Therefore [m]n does have a multiplicative inverse in Z/nZ, namely [a]n. The reader
will verify that gcd(a, n) = 1, completing the proof.
 
For instance, [8]15 has a multiplicative inverse in (Z/15Z)‚àó . Tracing the argu-
ment given in the proof,
2 ¬∑ 8 + (‚àí1) ¬∑ 15 = 1,
and hence [2]15 ¬∑ [8]15 = [1]15 : the multiplicative inverse of [8]15 is [2]15 .
For n = p a positive prime integer, the group ((Z/pZ)‚àó , ¬∑) has order (p ‚àí 1).
We will have more to say about these groups in later sections (cf. Example 4.6).

3. The category Grp
Groups will be the objects of the category Grp. In this section we deÔ¨Åne the
morphisms in the category and deal with simple properties of these morphisms.
3.1. Group homomorphisms. As we know, a group consists of two distinct
types of information: a set G and an operation15
mG : G √ó G ‚Üí G
satisfying certain properties. For two groups (G, mG) and (H, mH ), a group homo-
morphism
œï : (G, mG ) ‚Üí (H, mH )
is Ô¨Årst of all a function (usually given the same name, œï in this case) between the
underlying sets; but this function must ‚Äòknow about‚Äô the operations mG on G, mH
on H. What is the most natural requirement of this sort?
Note that the set-function œï : G ‚Üí H determines a function
(œï √ó œï) : G √ó G ‚Üí H √ó H :
we could invoke the universal property of products to obtain this function (cf. Ex-
ercise 3.1), but since we are dealing with sets, there is no need for fancy language
here‚Äîjust deÔ¨Åne the function by
(‚àÄ(a, b) ‚àà G √ó G) :

3. The category Grp
 61
3.4. Products et al. The categories Grp and Set look rather alike at Ô¨Årst: given
a group, we can ‚Äòforget‚Äô the information of multiplication and we are left with a set;
given a group homomorphism, we can forget that it preserves the multiplication
and we are left with a set-function. A concise way to express this fact is that there
is a ‚Äòfunctor‚Äô Grp ; Set (called in fact a ‚Äòforgetful‚Äô functor); we will deal with
functors more extensively much later on, starting in Chapter VIII.
However, there are important diÔ¨Äerences between these two categories. For
example, recall that Set has a unique initial object (that is, ‚àÖ) and this is not the
same as the Ô¨Ånal objects (that is, the singletons). Also recall that a trivial group
is a group consisting of a single element (Example 1.3).
Proposition 3.3. Trivial groups are both initial and Ô¨Ånal in Grp.
This makes trivial groups ‚Äòzero-objects‚Äô of the category Grp.
Proof. It should be clear that trivial groups are Ô¨Ånal: there is only one function
from a set to a singleton, that is, the constant function; this is vacuously a group
homomorphism.
To see that trivial groups are initial, let T = {e} be a trivial group; for any
group G, deÔ¨Åne œï : T ‚Üí G by T (e) = eG . This is clearly a group homomorphism,
and it is the only possible one since every group homomorphism must send the
identity to the identity (Proposition 3.2).
 
Here is a similarity: Grp has products; in fact, the product of two groups G, H
is supported on the product G √ó H of the underlying sets.
To see this, we need to deÔ¨Åne a multiplication on G √ó H; the catchword here
is componentwise: deÔ¨Åne the operation in G √ó H by performing the operation on
each component separately. Explicitly, deÔ¨Åne ‚àÄg1 , g2 ‚àà G, ‚àÄh1, h2 ‚àà H
(g1 , h1 ) ¬∑ (g2 , h2 ) := (g1 g2 , h1 h2 ).
This operation deÔ¨Ånes a group structure on G √ó H: the operation is associative, the
identity is (eG , eH ), and the inverse of (g, h) is (g‚àí1 , h‚àí1 ). All needed veriÔ¨Åcations
are left to the reader. The group G √ó H is called the direct product of the groups G
and H.
Also note that the natural projections
œÄG uu

(deÔ¨Åned as set-functions as in ¬ßI.2.4) are group homomorphisms: again, this follows
immediately from the deÔ¨Ånitions.

in Grp.

What about coproducts? They do exist in Grp, but their construction requires
handling presentations more proÔ¨Åciently than we do right now, and general coprod-
ucts of groups will not be used in the rest of the book; so the reader will have to
deal with them on his or her own. For an important example, see Exercise 3.8;
more will show up in Exercises 5.6 and 5.7, since free groups are themselves par-
ticular cases of coproducts. The reader will Ô¨Ånally produce the coproduct of any
two groups explicitly in Exercise 8.7. For now, just realize that the disjoint union,
which works as a coproduct in Set (Proposition I.5.6), is not an option in Grp: there
is no reasonable group structure on the disjoint union. The coproduct of G and H
in Grp is denoted G ‚àó H and is called the free product of G and H.
3.5. Abelian groups. The category Ab whose objects are abelian groups, and
whose morphisms are group homomorphisms, will in a sense be more important for
us than the category Grp. In many ways, as we will see, Ab is a ‚Äònicer‚Äô category17
than Grp. Again the trivial groups are both initial and Ô¨Ånal (that is, ‚Äòzero‚Äô) objects;
products exist and coincide with products in Grp. But here is a diÔ¨Äerence: unlike
in Grp, coproducts in Ab coincide with products. That is, if G and H are abelian
groups, then the product G√óH (with the two natural homomorphisms G ‚Üí G√óH,
H ‚Üí G √ó H) satisÔ¨Åes the universal property for coproducts in Ab (cf. Exercise 3.3).
When working as a coproduct, the product G √ó H of two abelian groups is often
called their direct sum and is denoted G ‚äï H.
There is a pretty subtlety here, which may highlight the power of the language:
even if G and H are commutative, the product G √ó H does not (necessarily) satisfy
17 As we will see in due time (Proposition III.5.3), Ab is one instance of a general class of
categories of ‚Äòmodules over a commutative ring R‚Äô (for R = Z). Unlike Grp, these categories are
abelian, which makes them very well-behaved. We will learn two or three things about abelian
categories in Chapter IX.

the universal property for coproducts in Grp, even if it does in Ab. For an explicit
example, see Exercise 3.6.

 II. Groups, Ô¨Årst encounter
4. Group homomorphisms
4.1. Examples. For any two groups G, H, the set HomGrp (G, H) is certainly
nonempty: at the very least we can deÔ¨Åne a homomorphism G ‚Üí H by sending
every element of G to the identity eH of H. Thus, HomGrp (G, H) is a ‚Äòpointed set‚Äô
(cf. Example I.3.8).
There is a purely categorical way to think about this: since Grp has zero-
objects {‚àó} (recall that trivial groups are both initial and Ô¨Ånal in Grp; cf. Proposi-
tion 3.3), there are unique morphisms
G ‚Üí {‚àó},
 {‚àó} ‚Üí H
in Grp; their composition is the distinguished element of HomGrp (G, H) mentioned
above; we will call this element the trivial morphism.
More ‚Äòmeaningful‚Äô examples can be constructed by considering the groups en-
countered in ¬ß2: for instance, the function D6 ‚Üí S3 deÔ¨Åned in ¬ß2.2 is a homomor-
phism. Such examples will likely all be instances of the notion of group action. In
general, an ‚Äòaction‚Äô of a group G on an object A of a category C is a homomorphism
G ‚Üí AutC (A);
that is, a group G ‚Äòacts‚Äô on an object if the elements of G determine isomorphisms
of that object to itself, in a way compatible with compositions. If C = Set, this
means that elements of G determine speciÔ¨Åc permutations of the set A. For exam-
ple, the symmetries of an equilateral triangle (that is, elements of D6 ) determine
permutations of the vertices of that triangle (that is, permutations of a set with
three elements), and they do so compatibly with composition; this is what gives
us homomorphisms D6 ‚Üí S3 . We say that D6 ‚Äòacts on the set of vertices‚Äô of the
triangle.
The reader can (and should) construct many more examples of this kind. We
will have much more to say about actions of groups and other algebraic entities in
later sections.
Here is an example with a diÔ¨Äerent Ô¨Çavor: the exponential function is a homo-
morphism from (R, +) to the group (R>0 , ¬∑) of positive real numbers, with ordinary
multiplication as operation. Indeed, ea+b = ea eb . A similar (and very important)
class of examples may be obtained as follows: let G be any group and g ‚àà G any
element of G; deÔ¨Åne an ‚Äòexponential map‚Äô g : Z ‚Üí G by
(‚àÄa ‚àà Z) :
 g (a) := g a .
Then g is (clearly) a group homomorphism. The element g generates G if and only
if  g is surjective.
One concrete instance of this homomorphism (in the abelian environment, thus
using multiples rather than powers) is the ‚Äòquotient‚Äô function œÄn : Z ‚Üí Z/nZ,
a ‚Üí a ¬∑ [1]n = [a]n :
with the notation introduced above, this is [1]n . This function is surjective; hence
[1]n generates Z/nZ. In fact, as observed in ¬ß2.3 (Corollary 2.5), [m]n gener-
ates Z/nZ if and only if gcd(m, n) = 1.

On the other hand, is there a nontrivial group homomorphism (for example)
C4 ‚Üí C7 ? Note that there are 74 = 2,401 set-functions from C4 to C7 (cf. Ex-
ercise I.2.10); the question is whether any of these functions (besides the trivial
homomorphism sending everything to e) preserves the operation. We already know
that a homomorphism must send the identity to the identity (Proposition 3.2), and
that already rules out all but 343 functions (why?); still, it is unrealistic to write
all of them out explicitly to see if any is a homomorphism.
The reader should think about this before we spill the beans in the next sub-
section.

4.2. Homomorphisms and order. Group homomorphisms are set-functions pre-
serving the group structure; as such, they must preserve many features of the the-
ory. Proposition 3.2 is an instance of this principle: group homomorphisms must
preserve identities and inverses. It is also clear that if œï : G ‚Üí H is a group homo-
morphism and g is an element of Ô¨Ånite order in G, then œï(g) must be an element
of Ô¨Ånite order in H: indeed, if g n = eG for some n > 0, then
œï(g)n = œï(gn ) = œï(eG) = eH .
In fact, this observation establishes a more precise statement:
Proposition 4.1. Let œï : G ‚Üí H be a group homomorphism, and let g ‚àà G be an
element of Ô¨Ånite order. Then |œï(g)| divides |g|.
Proof. As observed, œï(g)|g| = e H ; applying Lemma 1.10 gives the statement.
 
Example 4.2. There are no nontrivial homomorphisms Z/nZ ‚Üí Z: indeed, the
image of every element of Z/nZ must have Ô¨Ånite order, and the only element with
Ô¨Ånite order in (Z, +) is 0.
There are no nontrivial homomorphisms œï : C4 ‚Üí C7 . Indeed, the orders of
elements in C4 divide 4 (cf. Proposition 2.3), and the orders of elements in C7
divides 7. Thus, the order of each œï(g) must divide both 4 and 7; this forces
|œï(g)| = 1 for all g, that is, œï(g) = e for all g ‚àà C4 .
 
Of course the order itself is not preserved: for example, 1 ‚àà Z has inÔ¨Ånite
order, while [1]n = œÄn (1) ‚àà Z/nZ has order n (with notation as in ¬ß4.1). Order is
preserved through isomorphisms, as we will see in a moment.
4.3. Isomorphisms. An isomorphism of groups œï : G ‚Üí H is (of course) an
isomorphism in Grp, that is, a group homomorphism admitting an inverse
œï‚àí1 : H ‚Üí G
which is also a group homomorphism. Taking our cue from Set, if a homomorphism
of groups is an isomorphism, then it must in particular be a bijection between the
underlying sets. Luckily, the converse also holds:
Proposition 4.3. Let œï : G ‚Üí H be a group homomorphism. Then œï is an
isomorphism of groups if and only if it is a bijection.
Proof. One implication is immediate, as pointed out above. For the other impli-
cation, assume œï : G ‚Üí H is a bijective group homomorphism. As a bijection, œï
has an inverse in Set:
œï‚àí1 : H ‚Üí G;
we simply need to check that this is a group homomorphism. Let h1 , h2 be elements
of H, and let g1 = œï‚àí1 (h1 ), g2 = œï‚àí1 (h2 ) be the corresponding elements of G. Then
œï‚àí1 (h1 ¬∑ h2 ) = œï‚àí1(œï(g 1 ) ¬∑ œï(g2 )) = œï‚àí1(œï(g1 ¬∑ g2)) = g1 ¬∑ g2 = œï‚àí1(h1) ¬∑ œï‚àí1 (h2 )
as needed.
 
4. Group homomorphisms

Example 4.4. The function D6 ‚Üí S3 deÔ¨Åned in ¬ß2.2 is an isomorphism of groups,
since it is a bijective group homomorphism. So is the exponential function (R, +) ‚Üí
(R>0 , ¬∑) mentioned in ¬ß4.1. If the exponential function g : Z ‚Üí G determined by

 
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.5. Two groups G, H are isomorphic if they are isomorphic in Grp
in the sense of ¬ßI.4.1, that is (by Proposition 4.3), if there is a bijective group
homomorphism G ‚Üí H.
 
We have observed once and for all in ¬ßI.4.1 that ‚Äòisomorphic‚Äô is automatically
an equivalence relation. We write G ‚àº
 = H if G and H are isomorphic.
Automorphisms of a group G are isomorphisms G ‚Üí G; these form a group
AutGrp (G) (cf. ¬ßI.4.1), usually denoted Aut(G).
Example 4.6. We have introduced our template of cyclic groups in ¬ß2.3. The
notion of isomorphism allows us to give a formal deÔ¨Ånition:
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.7. A group G is cyclic if it is isomorphic to Z or to Cn = Z/nZ for
some19 n.
 
Thus, C2 √ó C3 is cyclic, of order 6, since C2 √ó C3 ‚àº
 = C6 . More generally
(Exercise 4.9) Cm √ó Cn is cyclic if gcd(m, n) = 1.
The reader will check easily (Exercise 4.3) that a group of order n is cyclic if
and only if it contains an element of order n.
There is a somewhat surprising source of cyclic groups: if p is prime, the
group ((Z/pZ)‚àó , ¬∑) is cyclic. We will prove a more general statement when we have
accumulated more machinery (Theorem IV.6.10), but the adventurous reader can
already enjoy a proof by working out Exercise 4.11. This is a relatively deep fact;
note that, for example, (Z/12Z)‚àó is not cyclic (cf. Exercise 2.19 and Exercise 4.10).
The fact that (Z/pZ)‚àó is cyclic for p prime means that there must be integers a
such that every nonmultiple of p is congruent to a power of a; the usual proofs of
this fact are not constructive, that is, they do not explicitly produce an integer with
this property. There is a very pretty connection between the order of an element of
the cyclic group (Z/pZ)‚àó and the so-called ‚Äòcyclotomic polynomials‚Äô; but that will
have to wait for a little Ô¨Åeld theory (cf. Exercise VII.5.15).
As we have seen, the groups D6 and S3 are isomorphic. Are C6 and S3 iso-
morphic? There are 46,656 functions between the sets C6 and S3, of which 720 are
bijections and 120 are bijections preserving the identity. The reader is welcome to
list all 120 and attempt to verify by hand if any of them is a homomorphism. But
maybe there is a better strategy to answer such questions. . . .
 
Isomorphic objects of a category are essentially indistinguishable in that cate-
gory. Thus, isomorphic groups share every group-theoretic structure. In particular,
19This includes the possibility that n = 1, that is, trivial groups are cyclic.

 II. Groups, Ô¨Årst encounter
Proposition 4.8. Let œï : G ‚Üí H be an isomorphism.
‚Ä¢ (‚àÄg ‚àà G) : |œï(g)| = |g|;
‚Ä¢ G is commutative if and only if H is commutative.
Proof. The Ô¨Årst assertion follows from Proposition 4.1: the order of œï(g) divides
the order of g, and on the other hand the order of g = œï‚àí1 (œï(g)) must divide the
order of œï(g); thus the two orders must be equal.
The proof of the second assertion is left to the reader.
 
Further instances of this principle will be assumed without explicit mention.
Example 4.9. C6 ‚àº
 = S3 , since one is commutative and the other is not. Here is
another reason: in C6 there is 1 element of order one, 1 of order two, 2 of order
three, and 2 of order six; in S3 the situation is diÔ¨Äerent: 1 element of order one, 3
of order two, 2 of order three. Thus, none of the 120 bijections C6 ‚Üí S3 preserving
the identity is a group homomorphism.
Note: Two Ô¨Ånite commutative groups are isomorphic if and only if they have
the same number of elements of any given order, but we are not yet in a position
to prove this; the reader will verify this fact in due time (Exercise IV.6.13). The
commutativity hypothesis is necessary: there do exist pairs of nonisomorphic Ô¨Ånite
groups with the same number of elements of any given order (same exercise).
 
4.4. Homomorphisms of abelian groups. I have already mentioned that Ab
is in some ways ‚Äòbetter behaved‚Äô than Grp, and I am ready to highlight another
instance of this observation. As we have seen, HomGrp (G, H) is a pointed set for
any two groups G, H. In Ab, we can say much more: HomAb (G, H) is a group (in
fact, an abelian group) for any two abelian groups G, H.
The operation in HomAb (G, H) is ‚Äòinherited‚Äô from the operation in H: if œï, œà :
G ‚Üí H are two group homomorphisms, let œï + œà be the function deÔ¨Åned by
(‚àÄa ‚àà G) :
 (œï + œà)(a) := œï(a) + œà(a).
Is œï + œà a group homomorphism? Yes, because ‚àÄa, b ‚àà G
(œï + œà)(a + b) = œï(a + b) + œà(a + b) = (œï(a) + œï(b)) + (œà(a) + œà(b))
!
= (œï(a) + œà(a)) + (œï(b) + œà(b)) = (œï + œà)(a) + (œï + œà)(b).
Note that the equality marked by ! uses crucially the fact that H is commutative.
With this operation, HomAb(G, H) is clearly a group: the associativity of + is
inherited from that of the operation in H; the trivial homomorphism is the identity
element, and the inverse20 of œï : G ‚Üí H is deÔ¨Åned (not surprisingly) by
(‚àÄa ‚àà G) :
 (‚àíœï)(a) = ‚àíœï(a).
In fact, note that these conclusions may be drawn as soon as H is commutative:
HomGrp (G, H) is a group if H is commutative (even if G is not). In fact, if H is
20 Unfortunate clash of terminology! I mean the ‚Äòinverse‚Äô as in ‚Äògroup inverse‚Äô, not as a
possible function H ‚Üí G.

5. Free groups
5.1. Motivation. Having become more familiar with homomorphisms, we can
now contemplate one fancier example of a group. The motivation underlying this
new construction may be summarized as follows: given a set A, whose elements
have no special ‚Äògroup-theoretic‚Äô property, we want to construct a group F (A)
containing A ‚Äòin the most eÔ¨Écient way‚Äô.
For example, if A = ‚àÖ, then a trivial group will do. If A = {a} is a singleton,
then a trivial group will not do: because although a trivial group {a} would itself
be a singleton, that one element a in it would have to be the identity, and that
is certainly a very special group-theoretic property. Instead, I propose that we
construct an inÔ¨Ånite cyclic group a whose elements are ‚Äòformal powers‚Äô an, n ‚àà Z,
and we identify a with the power a1 :
a := {¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ , a‚àí2 , a‚àí1, a0 = e, a1 = a, a2 , a3, ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ };
we take all these powers to be distinct and deÔ¨Åne multiplication in the evident
way‚Äîso that the exponential map
a : Z ‚Üí a,
 a (n) := an
is an isomorphism. The fact that ‚Äòall powers are distinct‚Äô is the formal way to
implement the fact that there is nothing special about a: in the group F ({a}) = a,
a obeys no condition other than the inevitable a0 = e.
Summarizing: if A is a singleton, then we may take F (A) to be an inÔ¨Ånite
cyclic group.
The task is to formalize the heuristic motivation given above and construct a
group F (A) for every set A. As I often do, I will now ask the reader to put away
this book and to try to Ô¨Ågure out on his or her own what this may mean and how
it may be accomplished.
5. Free groups
 71
5.2. Universal property. Hoping that the reader has now acquired an individual
viewpoint on the issue, here is the standard answer: the heuristic motivation is
formalized by means of a suitable universal property. Given a set A, our group F (A)
will have to ‚Äòcontain‚Äô A; therefore it is natural to consider the category F A whose
objects are pairs (j, G), where G is a group and
j : A ‚Üí G
is a set-function21 from A to G and morphisms
(j1 , G1 ) ‚Üí (j2 , G2 )
are commutative diagrams of set-functions

in which œï is required to be a group homomorphism.
The reader will be reminded of the categories we considered in Example I.3.7:
the only diÔ¨Äerence here is that we are mixing objects and morphisms of one cate-
gory (that is, Grp) with objects and morphisms of another (related) category (that
is, Set). The fact that we are considering all possible functions A ‚Üí G is a way to
implement the fact that we have no a priori group-theoretic information about A:
we do not want to put any restriction on what may happen to the elements of A
once they are mapped to a group G; hence we consider all possibilities at once.
A free group F (A) on A will be (the group component of) an initial object
in F A . This choice implements the fact that A should map to F (A) in the ‚Äòmost
eÔ¨Écient way‚Äô: any other way to map A to a group can be reconstructed from
this one, by composing with a group homomorphism. In the language of universal
properties, we can state this as follows: F (A) is a free group on the set A if there is a
set-function j : A ‚Üí F (A) such that, for all groups G and set-functions f : A ‚Üí G,
there exists a unique group homomorphism œï : F (A) ‚Üí G such that the diagram

5.3. Concrete construction. As we know, terminal objects of a category need
not exist. So I have to convince the reader that free groups F (A) exist, for every
set A.
Given any set A, we are going to think of A as an ‚Äòalphabet‚Äô and construct
‚Äòwords‚Äô whose letters are elements of A or ‚Äòinverses‚Äô of elements of A. To formalize
this, consider a set A isomorphic to A and disjoint from it; call a‚àí1 the element
in A corresponding to a ‚àà A. A word on the set A is an ordered list
(a1 , a2 , ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ , an ),
which we denote by the juxtaposition
w = a1 a2 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ an ,
where each ‚Äòletter‚Äô ai is either an element a ‚àà A or an element a‚àí1 ‚àà A . I will
denote the set of words on A by W (A); the number n of letters is the ‚Äòlength‚Äô of w;
I include in W (A) the ‚Äòempty word‚Äô w = ( ), consisting of no letters.
For example, if A = {a} is a singleton, then an element of W (A) may look like
a‚àí1a‚àí1aaaa‚àí1aa‚àí1 .
An element of W ({x, y}) may look like
xxx‚àí1 yy ‚àí1 xxy‚àí1 x‚àí1 yy ‚àí1 xy ‚àí1 x.
Now the notation I have chosen hints that elements in W (A) may be redundant:
for example,
xyy ‚àí1x

are distinct words, but they ought to end up being the same element of a group
having to do with words. Therefore, we want to have a process of ‚Äòreduction‚Äô which
takes a word and cleans it up by performing all cancellations. Note that we have
to do this ‚Äòby hand‚Äô, since we have not come close yet to deÔ¨Åning an operation or
‚àí1making formal sense of considering a to be the ‚Äòinverse‚Äô of a.
Describing the reduction process is invariably awkward‚Äîit is a completely ev-
ident procedure, but writing it down precisely and elegantly is a challenge. I will
settle for the following.

‚Ä¢ DeÔ¨Åne an ‚Äòelementary‚Äô reduction r : W (A) ‚Üí W (A): given w ‚àà W (A), search

‚Ä¢ Note that r(w) = w precisely when ‚Äòno cancellation is possible‚Äô; We say that

Lemma 5.1. If w ‚àà W (A) has length n, then22 r n
 2 (w) is a reduced word.
Proof. Indeed, either r(w) = w or the length of r(w) is less than the length of w;
but one cannot decrease the length of w more than n/2 times, since each non-
identity application of r decreases the length by two.

Let F (A) be the set of reduced words on A, that is, the image of the reduction

We are ready to (Ô¨Ånally) deÔ¨Åne free groups ‚Äòconcretely‚Äô. DeÔ¨Åne a binary oper-

It is essentially evident that F (A) is a group under this operation:
‚Ä¢ The operation is associative.
‚Ä¢ The empty word e = ( ) is the identity in F (A), since ew = we = w (no
reduction is necessary).
‚Ä¢ If w is a reduced word, the inverse of w is obtained by reversing the order of
the letters of w and replacing each a ‚àà A by a‚àí1 ‚àà A and each a‚àí1 by a.
The most cumbersome of these statements to prove formally is associativity; it
follows easily from (for example) Exercise 5.4.

Proof. This is also essentially evident, once one has absorbed all the notation.
Any function f : A ‚Üí G to a group extends uniquely to a map œï : F (A) ‚Üí

6. Subgroups
6.1. DeÔ¨Ånition. Let (G, ¬∑) be a group, and let (H, ‚Ä¢) be another group, whose
underlying set H is a subset of G.
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.1. (H, ‚Ä¢) is a subgroup of G if the inclusion function i : H ‚Üí G is a
group homomorphism.
 
For example, the trivial group consisting of the single element eG is a subgroup
of G.
If (H, ‚Ä¢) is a subgroup of (G, ¬∑), then ‚àÄh1 , h2 ‚àà H:
(*)
 i(h1 ‚Ä¢ h2 ) = i(h1 ) ¬∑ i(h2).
We say that the operation ‚Ä¢ on H is ‚Äòinduced‚Äô from the operation ¬∑ on G; in practice
one omits explicit mention of i and of the operations, and (*) guarantees that no
ambiguity will arise from this.
The subgroup condition may be streamlined. A subset H of a group G deter-
mines a subgroup if the operation ¬∑ in G induces (by (*)) a binary operation in H
(we say that H is closed with respect to the operation in G), satisfying the group
axioms. Since the identity and inverses are preserved through homomorphisms
(Proposition 3.2), the identity eH of H will have to coincide with the identity eG
of G and the inverse of an element h ‚àà H has to be the same as the inverse of that
element in G. The most economical way to say all this is
Proposition 6.2. A nonempty subset H of a group G is a subgroup if and only if
(‚àÄa, b ‚àà H) :
 ab‚àí1 ‚àà H.
Proof. It is clear that if H is a subgroup, then the stated condition holds: indeed,
if b ‚àà H, then the inverse of b must also be in H and H is closed under the operation
of G.
Conversely, assume the stated condition holds; we have to check that H is
closed under the operation of G, the induced operation on H is associative, and
it admits an identity element and inverses (that is, it contains eG and is closed
under taking inverses in G). Since H is nonempty, we can Ô¨Ånd an element h ‚àà H.
Choosing a = b = h, we see that
eG = hh‚àí1 = ab‚àí1 ‚àà H;
thus H contains the identity. Given any h ‚àà H, choosing a = eG and b = h shows
that
h‚àí1 = eG h‚àí1 = ab‚àí1 ‚àà H;
thus H contains the inverse of any of its elements. Given any h1 , h2 ‚àà H, choose
‚àí1
a = h1 , b = h2 ; the stated condition says that
h1 h2 = h1 ((h2 )‚àí1 )‚àí1 = ab‚àí1 ‚àà H,
proving that H is closed under the operation.
Finally, the fact that the operation is associative in G implies immediately that
the induced operation is associative in H, concluding the proof that H, with the
induced operation, is a group.

6.2. Examples: Kernel and image. Every group homomorphism œï : G ‚Üí G
determines two interesting subgroups:
‚Ä¢ the kernel of œï, ker œï ‚äÜ G; and
‚Ä¢ the image of œï, im œï ‚äÜ G .
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.5. The kernel of œï : G ‚Üí G is the subset of G consisting of elements
mapping to the identity in G :
ker œï := {g ‚àà G | œï(g) = eG } = œï‚àí1 (eG ).
 
Since {eG } is a subgroup of G , Lemma 6.4 shows that ker œï is indeed a sub-
group of G. For an (even) more explicit argument, note that ker œï is nonempty,
since eG ‚àà ker œï; and if a, b are in ker œï, then
œï(ab‚àí1 ) = œï(a)œï(b)‚àí1 = e G e‚àí1
 G = eG ,
proving that ab‚àí1 ‚àà ker œï. This shows that ker œï is a subgroup of G, by Proposi-
tion 6.2.
The veriÔ¨Åcation that im œï is a subgroup is left to the reader. In fact, the reader
should check that the image of any subgroup of G is a subgroup of G .
We will soon (¬ß7.1) see that kernels are ‚Äòspecial‚Äô subgroups. As with most
constructions of importance in algebra, they satisfy a universal property, which
may be expressed as follows.

6.3. Example: Subgroup generated by a subset. If A ‚äÜ G is any subset, we
have a unique group homomorphism
œïA : F (A) ‚Üí G
extending this inclusion, by the universal property of free groups. The image of
this homomorphism is a subgroup of G, the subgroup generated by A in G, often
denoted27 A.
Of course, if G is abelian, then œïA factors through F ab (A), so we may replace
F (A) by F ab (A) in this case.
The ‚Äòconcrete‚Äô description of free groups (¬ß5.3) leads to the following description
of A: it consists of all products in G of the form
a1 a2 a3 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ an
where each ai is either an element of A, the inverse of an element of A, or the
identity. This is clearly the most ‚Äòeconomical‚Äô way to manufacture a subgroup
of G, given the elements of A.

The reader who has not (yet) developed a taste for free groups may prefer

Indeed, the intersection on the right-hand side is a subgroup of G by Lemma 6.3,
it contains A, and it is clearly the smallest subgroup satisfying this condition.
If A = {g} consists of a single element, then F (A) = Z and œïA : Z ‚Üí G is
nothing but the ‚Äòexponential map‚Äô g (cf. ¬ß4.1); A = g is then the image of this

g = im(g ) = {. . . , g‚àí2 , g ‚àí1 , e, g, g 2 , . . . }.
The subgroup g is the ‚Äòcyclic subgroup generated by g‚Äô: indeed, g is cyclic in the
sense of DeÔ¨Ånition 4.7; the reader can easily check this fact already (Exercise 6.4); it

DeÔ¨Ånition 6.8. A group G is Ô¨Ånitely generated if there exists a Ô¨Ånite subset A ‚äÜ G
such that G = A.
 
For examples, cyclic groups are Ô¨Ånitely generated (in fact, they are generated
by a singleton). By deÔ¨Ånition, a group is Ô¨Ånitely generated if and only if there is a
surjective homomorphism
F ({1, . . . , n})  G
for some n. One of the most memorable results proven in this book will give
a classiÔ¨Åcation of Ô¨Ånitely generated abelian groups: we will be able to prove that
every such group is a direct sum of cyclic groups (Theorem IV.6.6, Exercise VI.2.19,
and the generalization given in Theorem VI.5.6). The situation for general groups
is considerably more complex. The classiÔ¨Åcation of Ô¨Ånite (simple) groups is one of
the major achievements of twentieth-century mathematics, and it is spread over at
least 10,000 pages of research articles. To appreciate the diÔ¨Äerence in complexity,
note that there are 42 abelian groups of order 1024 up to isomorphism (as the
reader will be able to establish in due time: Exercise IV.6.6); allegedly, there are
49,487,365,402 if we count noncommutative ones as well28 .
6.4. Example: Subgroups of cyclic groups. We are ready to determine all
subgroups of all cyclic groups, that is, all subgroups of Z and of Z/nZ, for all n > 0
(because every cyclic group is isomorphic to one of these; cf. DeÔ¨Ånition 4.7). The
result is easy to remember: subgroups of cyclic groups are themselves cyclic groups.
It is convenient to start from Z. For d ‚àà Z we let
dZ := d = {m ‚àà Z | ‚àÉq ‚àà Z, m = dq};
that is, dZ denotes the set of integer multiples of d. Of course this is nothing but
the ‚Äòcyclic subgroup of Z generated by d‚Äô.
Proposition 6.9. Let G ‚äÜ Z be a subgroup. Then G = dZ for some d ‚â• 0.
28 This comparison is a little unfair, however, since it so happens that more than 99% of all
groups of order < 2000 have order 1024.
6. Subgroups
 83
The proof will actually show that if G ‚äÜ Z is nontrivial, then d is the smallest
positive element of G, and the reader is invited to remember this useful fact.
Remark 6.10. By Proposition 6.9, every nontrivial subgroup of Z is in fact iso-
morphic to Z. Putting this a little strangely, it says that every subgroup of the
free group on one generator is free. It is in fact true that every subgroup of a
(Ô¨Ånitely generated) free group is free; we will not prove this fact, although the dili-
gent reader will get a taste of the argument in Exercise 9.16. In any case, beware
that free groups on two generators already contain subgroups isomorphic to free
groups on arbitrarily many generators. Indeed, the commutator subgroup (cf. Ex-
ercise 7.12) [F, F ] for F = F ({x, y}) is isomorphic to a free group on inÔ¨Ånitely many
generators (unfortunately, we will not prove this beautiful statement either).
 
Proof of Proposition 6.9. If G = {0}, then G = 0Z. If not, note that G must
contain positive integers: indeed, if a ‚àà G and a < 0, then ‚àía ‚àà G and ‚àía > 0.
We can then let d be the smallest positive integer 29 in G, and I claim G = dZ.
The inclusion dZ ‚äÜ G is clear. To verify the inclusion G ‚äÜ dZ, let m ‚àà G, and
apply ‚Äòdivision with remainder‚Äô to write
m = dq + r,
with 0 ‚â§ r < d. Since m ‚àà G and dZ ‚äÜ G and since G is a subgroup, we see that
r = m ‚àí dq ‚àà G.
But d is the smallest positive integer in G, and r ‚àà G is smaller than d; so r cannot
be positive. This shows r = 0, that is, m = qd ‚àà dZ; G ‚äÜ dZ follows, and we are
done.
 
The ‚Äòquotient‚Äô homomorphism œÄn : Z ‚Üí Z/nZ (cf. ¬ß4.1) allows us to establish
the analogous result for Ô¨Ånite cyclic groups:
Proposition 6.11. Let n > 0 be an integer and let G ‚äÜ Z/nZ be a subgroup. Then
G is the cyclic subgroup of Z/nZ generated by [d]n , for some divisor d of n.
Proof. Let œÄ n : Z ‚Üí Z/nZ be the quotient map, and consider G := œÄn ‚àí1(G). By
Lemma 6.4, G is a subgroup of Z; by Proposition 6.9, G is a cyclic subgroup of Z,
generated by a nonnegative integer d. It follows that
G = œÄn (G) = œÄn (d) = [d]n ;
thus G is indeed a cyclic subgroup of Z/nZ, generated by a class [d]n. Further, since
n ‚àà G (because œÄn(n) = [n]n = [0]n ‚àà G) and G = dZ, we see that d divides n, as
claimed.
 
As a consequence of Proposition 6.11, there is a bijection between the set of
subgroups of Z/nZ and the set of positive divisors of n. For example, Z/12Z has
29 I am secretly appealing to the ‚Äòwell-ordering principle‚Äô. That every set of positive integers
should have a smallest element is one of those fact about Z‚Äîlike the availability of division-with-
remainder‚Äîthat I am assuming the reader is already familiar with.
84
 II. Groups, Ô¨Årst encounter
exactly 6 subgroups, because 12 has 6 positive divisors: 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, and 12. Here
is the corresponding list of subgroups:
[1]12  = {[0]12, [1]12 , [2]12 , [3]12, [4]12, [5]12 , [6]12 , [7]12, [8]12 , [9]12 , [10]12 , [11]12 },
[2]12  = {[0]12, [2]12 , [4]12 , [6]12, [8]12, [10]12},
[3]12  = {[0]12, [3]12 , [6]12 , [9]12},
[4]12  = {[0]12, [4]12 , [8]12 },
[6]12  = {[0]12, [6]12 },
[12]12  = {[0]12}.
Also note that if d1 , d2 are both divisors of n, and d1 | d2 , then [d1 ]n  ‚äá [d2]n .
That is, the correspondence between subgroups of Z/nZ and divisors of n preserves
the natural lattice structure carried by these sets. We can draw these lattices
for Z/6Z as follows:

Contemplating subgroups of cyclic groups has pretty (and useful) ‚Äònumber-
theoretic‚Äô consequences; cf. Exercise 6.14.
6.5. Monomorphisms. I end this section with some categorical considerations.
If H is a subgroup of G, the inclusion H ‚Üí G is an example of a monomorphism
in Grp in the ‚Äòcategorical‚Äô sense of ¬ßI.4.2. In fact, it is easy to characterize all
monomorphisms œï ‚àà HomGrp (G, G ) (where G, G are any groups):
Proposition 6.12. The following are equivalent:
(a) œï is a monomorphism;
(b) ker œï = {e G};
(c) œï : G ‚Üí G is injective (as a set-function).
Proof. (a) =‚áí (b): Assume (a) holds, and consider the two parallel compositions
ker œï
 i
 // G
 œï
 / G ,
e
Exercises
 85
where i is the inclusion and e is the trivial map. Both œï ‚ó¶ i and œï ‚ó¶ e are the trivial
map; since œï is a monomorphism, this implies i = e. But i = e implies that ker œï
is trivial, that is, (b) holds.
(b) =‚áí (c): Assume ker œï = {eG }. Then
‚àí1 ‚àí1œï(g1) = œï(g2) =‚áí œï(g1 )œï(g2) = eG =‚áí œï(g1 g2 ) = eG
=‚áí g1 g2 ‚àí1 ‚àà ker œï =‚áí g1 g2 ‚àí1 = eG =‚áí g1 = g2 .
This shows that œï is injective, as needed.
(c) =‚áí (a): If œï is injective, then it satisÔ¨Åes the deÔ¨Åning property for monomor-
phisms in Set: that is, for any set Z and any two set-functions Œ± , Œ±  : Z ‚Üí G,
œï ‚ó¶ Œ±  = œï ‚ó¶ Œ± ‚áê‚áí Œ± = Œ± .
This must hold in particular if Z has a group structure and Œ± , Œ± are group
homomorphisms, so œï is a monomorphism in Grp.
 
The equivalence (a) ‚áê‚áí (c) may lead the reader to think that from the point
of view of monomorphisms, Grp and Set are pretty much alike. This is not quite so:
while it is true that homomorphisms with a left-inverse are necessarily monomor-
phisms, as in Set (cf. Exercise 6.15), the converse is not true in Grp (cf. Exer-
cise 6.16).

7. Quotient groups
7.1. Normal subgroups. Before tackling ‚Äòquotient groups‚Äô, I should clarify in
what sense kernels are special subgroups, as claimed in ¬ß6.2.
DeÔ¨Ånition 7.1. A subgroup N of a group G is normal if ‚àÄg ‚àà G, ‚àÄn ‚àà N ,
gng‚àí1 ‚àà N.
 
Note that every subgroup of a commutative group is normal (because then ‚àÄg ‚àà
G, gng ‚àí1 = n ‚àà N ). However, in general not all subgroups are normal: examples
may be found already in S3 (cf. Exercise 7.1). There exist noncommutative groups
in which every subgroup is normal (one example is the ‚Äòquaternionic group‚Äô Q8 ;
cf. Exercise III.1.12 (iv)), but they are very rare.
Lemma 7.2. If œï : G ‚Üí G is any group homomorphism, then ker œï is a normal
subgroup of G.
Proof. We already know that ker œï is a subgroup of G; to verify it is normal note
that ‚àÄg ‚àà G, ‚àÄn ‚àà ker œï
œï(gng ‚àí1 ) = œï(g)œï(n)œï(g‚àí1 ) = œï(g)eG œï(g)‚àí1 = eG ,
proving that gng‚àí1 ‚àà ker œï.
 
Loosely speaking, therefore, kernel =‚áí normal. In fact more is true, as we
will see in a little while; for now I don‚Äôt want to spoil the surprise for the reader.
(Can the reader guess?)
There is a convenient shorthand to express conditions such as normality: if
g ‚àà G and A ‚äÜ G is any subset, we denote by gA, Ag, respectively, the following
subsets of G:
gA := {h ‚àà G | (‚àÉa ‚àà A) : h = ga},
7. Quotient groups
 89
Ag := {h ‚àà G | (‚àÉa ‚àà A) : h = ag}.
Then the normality condition can be expressed by
(‚àÄg ‚àà G) :
 gN g ‚àí1 ‚äÜ N,
or in a number of other ways:
gN g ‚àí1 = N
 or
 gN ‚äÜ N g
 or
 gN = N g
for all g ‚àà G. The reader should check that these are indeed equivalent conditions
(Exercise 7.3) and keep in mind that ‚ÄògN = N g‚Äô does not mean that g commutes
with every element of N ; it means that if n ‚àà N , then there are elements n , n ‚àà N ,
in general diÔ¨Äerent from n, such that gn = n g (so that gN ‚äÜ N g) and ng = gn
(so that N g ‚äÜ gN ).
7.2. Quotient group. Recall that we have the notion of a quotient of a set by
an equivalence relation (¬ßI.1.5) and that this notion satisÔ¨Åes a universal property
(clumsily stated in ¬ßI.5.3). It is natural to investigate this notion in Grp.
We consider then an equivalence relation ‚àº on (the set underlying) a group G;
we seek a group G/‚àº and a group homomorphism œÄ : G ‚Üí G/‚àº satisfying the ap-
propriate universal property, that is, initial with respect to group homomorphisms
œï : G ‚Üí G such that a ‚àº b =‚áí œï(a) = œï(b).
It is natural to try to construct the group G/‚àº by deÔ¨Åning an operation ‚Ä¢ on the
set G/‚àº. The situation is tightly constrained by the requirement that the quotient
map œÄ : G ‚Üí G/‚àº (as in ¬ßI.2.6) be a group homomorphism: for if [a] = œÄ(a),
[b] = œÄ(b) are elements of G/‚àº (that is, equivalence classes with respect to ‚àº),
then the homomorphism condition forces
[a] ‚Ä¢ [b] = œÄ(a) ‚Ä¢ œÄ(b) = œÄ(ab) = [ab].
But is this operation well-deÔ¨Åned? This amounts to conditions on the equivalence
relation, which we proceed to unearth.
For the operation to be well-deÔ¨Åned ‚Äòin the Ô¨Årst factor‚Äô, it is necessary that if
[a] = [a ], then [ab] = [ab] regardless of what b is; that is,
(‚àÄg ‚àà G) :
 a ‚àº a =‚áí ag ‚àº ag.
Similarly, for the operation to be well-deÔ¨Åned in the second factor we need
(‚àÄg ‚àà G) :
 a ‚àº a =‚áí ga ‚àº ga .
Luckily, this is all that there is to it:
Proposition 7.3. With notation as above, the operation
[a] ‚Ä¢ [b] := [ab]
deÔ¨Ånes a group structure on G/‚àº if and only if ‚àÄa, a, g ‚àà G
a ‚àº a =‚áí ga ‚àº ga and ag ‚àº a g.
In this case the quotient function œÄ : G ‚Üí G/‚àº is a homomorphism and is universal
with respect to homomorphisms œï : G ‚Üí G such that a ‚àº a =‚áí œï(a) = œï(a ).

Proof. We have already noted that the condition is necessary. To prove it is
suÔ¨Écient, with the stated consequences, assume ‚àÄa, a , g ‚àà G
a ‚àº a =‚áí ga ‚àº ga and ag ‚àº a g.
Then the operation
[a] ‚Ä¢ [b] := [ab]
is well-deÔ¨Åned, and we have to verify that it deÔ¨Ånes a group structure on G/‚àº. The
associativity of ‚Ä¢ is inherited from the associativity of G: ‚àÄa, b, c ‚àà G
([a] ‚Ä¢ [b]) ‚Ä¢ [c] = [ab] ‚Ä¢ [c] = [(ab)c] = [a(bc)] = [a] ‚Ä¢ [bc] = [a] ‚Ä¢ ([b] ‚Ä¢ [c]).
The class [eG ] is an identity with respect to this operation: ‚àÄg ‚àà G
[g] ‚Ä¢ [eG ] = [geG ] = [g],
 [eG ] ‚Ä¢ [g] = [eG g] = [g].
The class [g
‚àí1
 ] is the inverse of [g]:
[g ‚àí1] ‚Ä¢ [g] = [g‚àí1 g] = [eG ],
 [g] ‚Ä¢ [g ‚àí1] = [gg ‚àí1 ] = [eG ].
This shows G/‚àº is indeed a group, and we have already observed that œÄ : G ‚Üí G/‚àº
is a homomorphism: this is what led us to the deÔ¨Ånition of ‚Ä¢.
To prove that G/‚àº satisÔ¨Åes the universal property, assume
œï : G ‚Üí G
is a group homomorphism such that a ‚àº a =‚áí œï(a) = œï(a). Since (cf. ¬ßI.5.3)
the set G/‚àº satisÔ¨Åes the corresponding universal property in Set, we know that
there exists a unique set-function
œïÃÉ : G/‚àº ‚Üí G ,
deÔ¨Åned31 by œïÃÉ([a]) := œï(a). So we only need to check that this function œïÃÉ is in fact
a group homomorphism, and this is immediate:
œïÃÉ([a] ‚Ä¢ [b]) = œïÃÉ([ab]) = œï(ab) = œï(a)œï(b) = œïÃÉ([a])œïÃÉ([b])
for all [a], [b] ‚àà G/‚àº, as needed.
 
I will say that ‚àº is compatible with the group structure of G if the condition given
in Proposition 7.3 holds. Since the operation ‚Ä¢ on the quotient G/‚àº is uniquely
determined by the operation on G, I yield to the usual abuse of language and omit
it. If ‚àº is compatible, I will call G/‚àº the quotient group of G by ‚àº.
7.3. Cosets. The conditions obtained in Proposition 7.3,
(‚ÄÝ)
 (‚àÄg ‚àà G) :
 a ‚àº b =‚áí ga ‚àº gb,
(‚ÄÝ‚ÄÝ)
 (‚àÄg ‚àà G) :
 a ‚àº b =‚áí ag ‚àº bg,
lead to a complete description of all compatible relations on a group G. In fact,
each of these two conditions leads to a description of the relations satisfying it, and
we will analyze them separately; the reader should keep in mind that we have a
group structure on G/‚àº only if both are satisÔ¨Åed.
Let‚Äôs begin with (‚ÄÝ). Here is the description:
31 The point of ¬ßI.5.3 is precisely that this function is well-deÔ¨Åned.

Proposition 7.4. Let ‚àº be an equivalence relation on a group G, satisfying (‚ÄÝ).
Then
‚Ä¢ the equivalence class of eG is a subgroup H of G; and
‚Ä¢ a ‚àº b ‚áê‚áí a‚àí1b ‚àà H ‚áê‚áí aH = bH.
Proof. Let H ‚äÜ G be the equivalence class of the identity; H = ‚àÖ as eG ‚àà H. For
a, b ‚àà H, we have eG ‚àº b and hence b‚àí1 ‚àº eG (applying (‚ÄÝ), multiplying on the left
by b‚àí1 ); hence ab‚àí1 ‚àº a (by (‚ÄÝ) again, multiplying on the left by a); and hence
ab‚àí1 ‚àº a ‚àº eG
by the transitivity of ‚àº and since a ‚àà H. This shows that ab‚àí1 ‚àà H for all a, b ‚àà H,
proving that H is a subgroup (by Proposition 6.2).
Next, assume a, b ‚àà G and a ‚àº b. Multiplying on the left by a‚àí1 , (‚ÄÝ) implies
eG ‚àº a‚àí1b, that is, a‚àí1b ‚àà H. Since H is closed under the operation, this implies
a‚àí1 bH ‚äÜ H, hence bH ‚äÜ aH; as ‚àº is symmetric, the same reasoning gives aH ‚äÜ
bH; and hence aH = bH. Thus, we have proved
a ‚àº b =‚áí a‚àí1b ‚àà H =‚áí aH = bH.
Finally, assume aH = bH. Then a = aeG ‚àà bH, and hence a‚àí1 b ‚àà H. By deÔ¨Ånition
of H, this means eG ‚àº a‚àí1b. Multiplying on the left by a shows (by (‚ÄÝ) again)
that a ‚àº b, completing the proof.
 
Proposition 7.4 shows that the equivalence classes of an equivalence relation
satisfying (‚ÄÝ) are in fact all of the form
aH
for a Ô¨Åxed subgroup H, as a ranges in G. These important subsets determined by
a subgroup H deserve a name.
DeÔ¨Ånition 7.5. The left-cosets of a subgroup H in a group G are the sets aH, for
a ‚àà G. The right-cosets of H are the sets Ha, a ‚àà G.
 
Now, a ‚Äòconverse‚Äô to Proposition 7.4 holds:
Proposition 7.6. If H is any subgroup of a group G, the relation ‚àºL deÔ¨Åned by
(‚àÄa, b ‚àà G) :
 a ‚àºL b ‚áê‚áí a‚àí1 b ‚àà H
is an equivalence relation satisfying (‚ÄÝ).
Proof. This is straightforward and is mostly left to the reader (Exercise 7.8). To
see that the relation satisÔ¨Åes (‚ÄÝ), note that
a ‚àºL b =‚áí a‚àí1 b ‚àà H =‚áí a‚àí1 (g‚àí1 g)b ‚àà H =‚áí (ga)‚àí1 (gb) ‚àà H =‚áí ga ‚àºL gb
for all g ‚àà G.
 
Taken together, Propositions 7.4 and 7.6 show
Proposition 7.7. There is a one-to-one correspondence between subgroups of G
and equivalence relations on G satisfying (‚ÄÝ); for the relation ‚àºL corresponding to
a subgroup H, G/‚àºL may be described as the set of left-cosets aH of H.
92
 II. Groups, Ô¨Årst encounter
The reader should have no diÔ¨Éculty producing the mirror statements (and
proofs) giving a similarly exhaustive description of all equivalence relations satisfy-
ing (‚ÄÝ‚ÄÝ). The end result will be
Proposition 7.8. There is a one-to-one correspondence between subgroups of G
and equivalence relations on G satisfying (‚ÄÝ‚ÄÝ); for the relation ‚àºR corresponding to
a subgroup H, G/‚àºR may be described as the set of right-cosets Ha of H.
The relation corresponding to H in this second way is deÔ¨Åned by
a ‚àºR b ‚áê‚áí ab‚àí1 ‚àà H ‚áê‚áí Ha = Hb.
What may be surprising at Ô¨Årst is that the relations ‚àºL and ‚àºR corresponding
to the same subgroup H may very well not be the same relation. That is, left-cosets
and right-cosets of a subgroup need not coincide. Of course eH = He = H, and
more generally
(‚àÄh ‚àà H) : hH = Hh = H.
Further
(‚àÄa ‚àà G) : a ‚àà aH ‚à© Ha;
hence, if aH = Hb for any b, then in fact necessarily aH = Ha. This is of course
automatically true if G is commutative, but it is simply not the case in general.

7.4. Quotient by normal subgroups. To stress the main point again, an arbi-
trary subgroup H of G leads to two partitions of G, which we have denoted
G/‚àºL = {aH | a ‚àà G},
 G/‚àºR = {Ha | a ‚àà G}.
The relation ‚àºL satisÔ¨Åes property (‚ÄÝ) listed at the beginning of ¬ß7.3; ‚àºR satisÔ¨Åes
(‚ÄÝ‚ÄÝ). A priori, these relations, and hence the corresponding partitions, are diÔ¨Äerent.
The condition that ‚àºL and ‚àºR coincide (as is necessarily the case, for example,
if G is commutative) translates into a condition on H: for such ‚Äòspecial‚Äô subgroups,
(‚ÄÝ) and (‚ÄÝ‚ÄÝ) get back together. The good news is that this condition is easy to
identify and is not new to the reader.
7. Quotient groups
 93
Proposition 7.10. The relations ‚àºL , ‚àºR corresponding to a subgroup H coincide
if and only if H is normal.
Proof. Two relations coincide if the corresponding partitions agree. Therefore
‚àºL =‚àº R ‚áê‚áí left- and right-cosets of H coincide ‚áê‚áí (‚àÄg ‚àà G) : gH = Hg.
But this is one of the equivalent conditions deÔ¨Åning the notion of normal subgroup
(cf ¬ß7.1), proving the statement.
 
The innocent-looking Proposition 7.10 is of fundamental importance. If H is
normal, then the one equivalence relation ‚àº=‚àºL =‚àºR corresponding to H satisÔ¨Åes
both (‚ÄÝ) and (‚ÄÝ‚ÄÝ) (by Proposition 7.4 and its mirror statement), and hence (by
Proposition 7.3) the quotient
G/‚àº = {aH | a ‚àà G} = {Ha | a ‚àà G}
has a natural group structure.
DeÔ¨Ånition 7.11. Let H be a normal subgroup of a group G. The quotient group
of G modulo H, denoted32 G/H, is the group G/‚àº obtained from the relation ‚àº
deÔ¨Åned above. In terms of (left-) cosets, the product in G/H is deÔ¨Åned by
(aH)(bH) := (ab)H.
The identity element eG/H of the quotient group G/H is the coset of the identity,
eG H = H.

7.5. Example. The reader is already very familiar with an important class of

(‚àÄa, b ‚àà Z) :

b ‚àí a ‚àà nZ,
which is the relation ‚àºL corresponding (in ‚Äòabelian‚Äô notation) to the subgroup nZ
of Z. This subgroup is of course normal, since Z is abelian. The ‚Äòcongruence classes
mod n‚Äô are nothing but the cosets of the subgroup nZ in Z; using abelian notation
for cosets, we could write
[a]n = a + (nZ).
Of course the operation deÔ¨Åned on Z/nZ in ¬ß2.3 matches precisely the one deÔ¨Åned
above for quotient groups. This justiÔ¨Åes the notation Z/nZ introduced in ¬ß2.3.

7.6. kernel ‚áê‚áí normal. If H is a normal subgroup, we have now constructed
in gory detail a group G/H and a surjective homomorphism
œÄ : G ‚Üí G/H.
What is the kernel of œÄ? The identity of G/H is the coset eG H, that is, H itself.
Therefore
ker œÄ = {g ‚àà G | gH = H} = H.
This observation completes the circle of ideas begun in ¬ß7.1: there we had noticed
that every kernel (of a group homomorphism) is a normal subgroup; and now
we have veriÔ¨Åed that every normal subgroup is in fact a kernel (of some group
homomorphism). I encapsulate this in the slogan
kernel ‚áê‚áí normal :
in group theory33 , ‚Äòkernel‚Äô and ‚Äònormal subgroup‚Äô are equivalent concepts.
For example, every subgroup in an abelian group is the kernel of some homo-
morphism: yet another indication that life is simpler in Ab than in Grp.

8. Canonical decomposition and Lagrange‚Äôs theorem
I will collect in this section a number of observations on the structure of quotient
groups. All these results are straightforward, given the background work done so
far. Some of them are often given fancy names such as Ô¨Årst isomorphism theorem
in the literature; I am not too fond of such terminology: the universal property
proven in Theorem 7.12 is really the only thing I need to take along, and it serves
me wonderfully well. The ‚Äòisomorphism theorems‚Äô are all immediate applications
of this universal property.
8. Canonical decomposition and Lagrange‚Äôs theorem
 97
8.1. Canonical decomposition. The Ô¨Årst observation comes from the canoni-
cal decomposition for set-functions, obtained in ¬ßI.2.8: every set-functions may be
viewed as the composition of a surjective map, followed by a bijective map, followed
by an injective map. We now know enough to state the corresponding (very useful)
results in Grp:
Theorem 8.1. Every group homomorphism œï : G ‚Üí G may be decomposed as
follows:

decomposition is the same one obtained at the level of set-functions in ¬ßI.2.8; in
particular, the function in the middle is a bijection. Since bijective homomorphisms
are isomorphisms (Proposition 4.3), it is an isomorphism.

8.2. Presentations. Every group is a quotient of a free group, and every abelian
group is a quotient of a free abelian group. Indeed, every group G can be surjected
upon by a free group, and in many ways (at the very least, F (G) will do!). Abelian
groups may be likewise surjected upon by free abelian groups. Then Corollary 8.2
produces the needed isomorphism of G with a quotient of a free group.
A presentation of a group G is an explicit isomorphism
G ‚àº
 =
 F (A)
R
where A is a set and R is a subgroup of ‚Äòrelations‚Äô. In other words, a presentation
is an explicit surjection
œÅ : F (A)  G
of which R is the kernel. This is especially useful if A is small, and R may be
described very explicitly; usually this is done by listing ‚Äòenough‚Äô relations, that is,
a set R of words rŒ± ‚àà R = ker œÅ generating it in the sense that R is the smallest
normal subgroup35 of F (A) containing R.
Thus, a presentation of a group G is usually encoded as a pair (A|R), where A
is a set and R ‚äÜ F (A) is a set of words, such that G ‚àº
 = F (A)/R with R as above.
A group is Ô¨Ånitely presented if it admits a presentation (A|R) in which both
A and R are Ô¨Ånite. Finitely presented groups are not (necessarily) ‚Äòsmall‚Äô: for
example, the free group on Ô¨Ånitely many generators is (trivially) Ô¨Ånitely presented.
We have already run into several examples of presentations. For instance,
the free group F (A) is presented by (A|‚àÖ). More interestingly, the description
of S3 given in ¬ß2.1 ‚Äòpresents‚Äô S3 as a quotient of the free group F ({x, y}) (cf. Ex-
ample 5.3) by the smallest normal subgroup containing x2, y 3 , and yx = xy 2 :
(x, y|x2 , y3 , xyxy) in shorthand. From this point of view, it is clear that groups
admitting the same presentation (example: S3 and D6 ) are isomorphic.
The situation is less idyllic than it may seem at Ô¨Årst, though: even if a presen-
tation of a group G is known, it may be very hard to establish whether two explicit
35 Note that this is a diÔ¨Äerent requirement than the one adopted in ¬ß6.3, in which normality
plays no role.
100
 II. Groups, Ô¨Årst encounter
combinations of the generators coincide in G. This is known as the word problem,
and it has been shown to be undecidable in general36 .
In any case, now that we know about presentations of groups, Ô¨Ånding coprod-
ucts in Grp should be straightforward: see Exercise 8.7.
There is a ‚Äòmirror‚Äô statement analogous to the fact that all groups are quotients
of free groups: every group may be realized as a subgroup of a symmetric group.
This elementary observation goes under the name of Cayley‚Äôs theorem; its natural
place is within the discussion of group actions (cf. Theorem 9.5).
8.3. Subgroups of quotients. The lattice of subgroups (cf. ¬ß6.4) of a quo-
tient G/H can be described very explicitly in terms of the lattice of subgroups
of the group G: simply keep the part of the lattice of subgroups of G corresponding
to subgroups which contain H.

Here is why this works. First note that if H ‚äÜ K are subgroups of a group G
and H is normal in G, then H is normal in K.
Proposition 8.9. Let H be a normal subgroup of a group G. Then for every
subgroup K of G containing H, K/H may be identiÔ¨Åed with a subgroup of G/H.
The function
u : {subgroups K of G containing H} ‚Üí {subgroups of G/H}
deÔ¨Åned by u(K) = K/H is a bijection preserving inclusions.
Proof. The group K/H consists of the cosets aH ‚àà G/H with a ‚àà K, and in
this sense it is a subset (and clearly a subgroup) of G/H. It is also clear that if
H ‚äÜ K ‚äÜ L, then u(K) = K/H ‚äÜ L/H = u(L); that is, u preserves inclusions.

Lemma 6.4) and contains H (because H = œÄ ‚àí1(e) and e ‚àà K ). The reader will
check that u and v are inverses of each other.

follows immediately from Corollary 8.2.

8.4. HK/H vs. K/(H ‚à©K). Section 8.3 deals with two ‚Äònested‚Äô subgroups H ‚äÜ K
of a group G. What if H, K are not nested?
The notation introduced in ¬ß7.1 extends to subsets of G: if A ‚äÜ G, B ‚äÜ G,
then AB denotes the subset
AB := {ab | a ‚àà A, b ‚àà B}.
It would be nice if HK were guaranteed to be a subgroup of G as soon as H and K
are subgroups, but this is simply not the case in general, if G is not commutative.
It is, however, the case if one of the subgroups is normal. The following is often
called the second isomorphism theorem.

8.5. The index and Lagrange‚Äôs theorem. The notation G/H is used to denote
the set of left-cosets 37 of H, even when H is not normal in G. Thus G/H is a set
in general, and it is a group when H is in fact normal in G.
DeÔ¨Ånition 8.12. The index of H in G, denoted [G : H], is the number of elements
|G/H| of G/H, when this is Ô¨Ånite, and ‚àû otherwise.
 
Thus, [G : H] (if Ô¨Ånite) denotes the number of left-cosets of H in G, regardless
of whether H is normal in G.
Lemma 8.13. Let H be a subgroup of a group G. Then ‚àÄg ‚àà G the functions
H ‚Üí gH,

are bijections.
37 This may seem an arbitrary choice (why not right-cosets?). It is. Writing from left to right

Proof. Both functions are surjective by deÔ¨Ånition of coset. Cancellation implies

Corollary 8.14 (Lagrange‚Äôs theorem). If G is a Ô¨Ånite group and H ‚äÜ G is a
subgroup, then |G| = [G : H] ¬∑ |H|. In particular, |H| is a divisor of |G|.
Proof. Indeed, G is the disjoint union of |G/H| distinct cosets gH, and |gH| = |H|
by Lemma 8.13.
 
Lagrange‚Äôs theorem is more useful than it may appear at Ô¨Årst.
Example 8.15. The order |g| of any element g of a Ô¨Ånite group G is a divisor
of |G|: indeed, |g| equals the order of the subgroup g generated by g.
Note: Therefore, g |G| = eG for all Ô¨Ånite groups G, all g ‚àà G.
 
Example 8.16. If |G| is a prime integer p, then necessarily G ‚àº
 = Z/pZ.
Indeed, let g ‚àà G be any element other than the identity; then g is a subgroup
of G, of order > 1. By Lagrange‚Äôs theorem, |g| = p = |G|; that is, G ‚àº
 = g is
cyclic of order p, as claimed.
 
Example 8.17 (Fermat‚Äôs little theorem). Let p be a prime integer, and let a be
any integer. Then ap ‚â° a mod p.
Indeed, this is immediate if a is a multiple of p; if a is not a multiple of p, then
the class [a]p modulo p is nonzero, so it is an element of the group (Z/pZ)‚àó , which
has order p ‚àí 1. Thus
[a]p‚àí1
 p
 = [1]p
(Example 8.15); hence [a]p p
 = [a]p as claimed.
 
Warning: However, do not ask too much of Lagrange‚Äôs theorem. For example,
it does not say that if d is a divisor of |G|, then there exists a subgroup of G of
order d (the smallest counterexample is A4, a group of order 12, which does not
contain subgroups of order 6; the reader will verify this in Exercise IV.4.17); it
does not even say that if p is a prime divisor of |G|, then there is an element of
order p in G. This latter statement happens to be true, but for ‚Äòdeeper‚Äô reasons.
The abelian case is easy (cf. Exercise 8.17). The general case is called Cauchy‚Äôs
theorem, and we will deal with it later on (cf. Theorem IV.2.1).
The index is a well-behaved invariant. It is clearly multiplicative, in the sense
that if H ‚äÜ K are subgroups of G, then
[G : H] = [G : K] ¬∑ [K : H],
provided that these numbers are Ô¨Ånite. Also, if H and K are subgroups of G and H
is normal (so that HK is a subgroup as well; cf. Proposition 8.11), then
|H| ¬∑ |K|
|HK| =
|H ‚à© K|
(again, provided this has a chance of making sense, that is, if the orders are Ô¨Ånite):
this follows immediately from the isomorphism in Proposition 8.11 and index con-
siderations. In fact, the formula holds even without assuming that one of the
subgroups is normal in G. Do you see why? (Exercise 8.21.)
