Further, if H and G are Ô¨Ånite, then Lemma 8.13 implies immediately that the
index of H in G, deÔ¨Åned as the number of left-cosets of H in G, also equals the
number of right-cosets of H. It is in fact easy to show that there always is a bijection
between the set G/H of left-cosets and the set of right-cosets (cf. Exercise 9.10),
regardless of Ô¨Åniteness hypotheses. The set of right-cosets of H in G is often
(reasonably) denoted H\G.
8.6. Epimorphisms and cokernels. The reader may expect that a mirror state-
ment to Proposition 6.12 should hold for group epimorphisms. This is almost true:
a homomorphism œï : G ‚Üí H is an epimorphism (in the category Grp) if and only
if it is surjective. However, while one implication is easy, the proofs I know for
epimorphism =‚áí surjective in Grp are somewhat cumbersome.
The situation is leaner (as usual) in Ab: there is in Ab a good notion of cokernel;
this is part of what makes Ab an ‚Äòabelian category‚Äô.
As is often the case, the reader may now want to pause a moment and try
to guess the right deÔ¨Ånition. Keeping in mind the universal property for kernels
(Proposition 6.6), can the reader come up with the universal property deÔ¨Åning
‚Äòcokernels‚Äô ? Can the reader prove that these exist in Ab and detect epimorphisms?
Don‚Äôt look ahead!
Here is how the story goes. The universal property is (of course) obtained by
reversing the arrows in the property for kernels: given a homomorphism œï : G ‚Üí G
of abelian groups, we want an abelian group coker œï equipped with a homomorphism
œÄ : G ‚Üí coker œï
which is initial with respect to all morphisms Œ± such that Œ± ‚ó¶ œï = 0. That is, every
homomorphism Œ± : G ‚Üí L such that Œ± ‚ó¶œï is the trivial map must factor (uniquely)
through coker œï:

9. Group actions
9.1. Actions. As mentioned in ¬ß4.1, an action of a group G on an object A of a
category C is simply a homomorphism
œÉ : G ‚Üí AutC (A).
The way to interpret this is that every element g ‚àà G determines a ‚Äòtransformation
of A into itself‚Äô, i.e., an isomorphism of A in C, and this happens compatibly with
the operation of G and composition in C.
In a rather strong sense, we really only care about groups because they act on
things: knowing that G acts on A tells us something about A; group actions are
one key tool in the study of geometric and algebraic entities.
In fact, group actions are one key tool in the study of groups themselves: one
of the best ways to ‚Äòunderstand‚Äô a group is to let it act on an object A, hoping
that the corresponding homomorphism œÉ is an isomorphism, or at least an injective
monomorphism. For example, we were lucky with D6 in ¬ß2.2: we let D6 act on a
set with three elements (the vertices of an equilateral triangle) and observed that
the resulting œÉ is an isomorphism. Thus D6 = ‚àº
 S3 . We would be almost as lucky
9. Group actions
 109
by letting D8 act on the vertices of a square: then œÉ would at least realize D8 as
an explicit subgroup of S4 , which simpliÔ¨Åes its analysis.
DeÔ¨Ånition 9.1. An action of a group G on an object A of a category C is faithful
(or eÔ¨Äective) if the corresponding œÉ : G ‚Üí AutC(A) is injective.
 
The case C = Set is already very rich, and we focus on it in this chapter.
9.2. Actions on sets. Spelling out our deÔ¨Ånition of action in case A is a set, so
that AutC(A) is the symmetric group SA , we get the following:
DeÔ¨Ånition 9.2. An action of a group G on a set A is a set-function
œÅ : G √ó A ‚Üí A
such that œÅ(eG , a) = a for all a ‚àà A and
(‚àÄg, h ‚àà G), (‚àÄa ‚àà A) :
 œÅ(gh, a) = œÅ(g, œÅ(h, a)).
 
Indeed, given a function œÅ satisfying these conditions, we can deÔ¨Åne œÉ : G ‚Üí
HomSet (A, A) by œÉ(g)(a) = œÅ(g, a). (This deÔ¨Ånes œÉ(g) as a set-function A ‚Üí A, as
needed.) This function preserves the operation, because
œÉ(gh)(a) = œÅ(gh, a) = œÅ(g, œÅ(h, a)) = œÉ(g)(œÅ(h, a)) = œÉ(g)(œÉ(h)(a))
= œÉ(g) ‚ó¶ œÉ(h)(a).
In particular, this veriÔ¨Åes that œÉ(g ‚àí1 ) acts as the inverse of œÉ(g): because (‚àÄa ‚àà A)
œÉ(g ‚àí1 ) ‚ó¶ œÉ(g)(a) = œÉ(g‚àí1 g)(a) = œÉ(eG )(a) = œÅ(eG , a) = a.
Thus the image of œÉ consists of invertible set-functions; œÉ is acting as a function
œÉ : G ‚Üí SA ,
and we have veriÔ¨Åed that this is a homomorphism, as needed.
Conversely, given a homomorphism œÉ : G ‚Üí SA , deÔ¨Åne œÅ : G √ó A ‚Üí A by
œÅ(g, a) = œÉ(g)(a); the same argument (read backwards) shows that œÅ satisÔ¨Åes the
needed properties.
It is unpleasant to carry œÅ along. In practice, one just writes ga for œÅ(g, a); the
requirements in DeÔ¨Ånition 9.2 amount then to e Ga = a for all a ‚àà A and
(‚àÄg, h ‚àà G), (‚àÄa ‚àà A) :
 (gh)a = g(ha),
‚Äòas if‚Äô œÅ deÔ¨Åned an associative operation.
If G acts on A, then eG a = a for all a ‚àà A; the action of a group G on a set A
is faithful if and only if the identity eG is the only element g of G such that ga = a
for all a ‚àà A, that is, ‚ÄòÔ¨Åxing‚Äô every element of A. An action is free if the identity eG
is the only element Ô¨Åxing any element of A.
Example 9.3. Every group G acts in a natural way on the underlying set G. The
function œÅ : G √ó G ‚Üí G is simply the operation in the group:
(‚àÄg, a ‚àà G) :
 œÅ(g, a) = ga.
110
 II. Groups, Ô¨Årst encounter
In this case the deÔ¨Åning property really is associativity. This is referred to as the
action by left-multiplication 41 of G on itself. There is (at least) another very natural
way to act with G on itself, by conjugation: deÔ¨Åne œÅ : G √ó G ‚Üí G by
‚àí1œÅ(g, h) = ghg .
This is indeed an action: ‚àÄg, h, k ‚àà G,
œÅ(g, œÅ(h, k)) = gœÅ(h, k)g‚àí1 = g(hkh‚àí1)g‚àí1 = (gh)k(gh)‚àí1 = œÅ(gh, k).
 
Example 9.4. More generally, G acts by left-multiplication on the set G/H of
left-cosets (cf. ¬ß8.5) of any subgroup H: act by g ‚àà G on aH ‚àà G/H by sending it
to (ga)H.
 
These examples of actions are extremely useful in studying groups, as we will see
in Chapter IV. For instance, an immediate consequence is the following counterpart
to ¬ß8.2:
Theorem 9.5 (Cayley‚Äôs theorem). Every group acts faithfully on some set. That
is, every group may be realized as a subgroup of a permutation group.
Proof. Indeed, simply observe that the left-multiplication action of G on itself is
manifestly faithful.
 
The notion deÔ¨Åned in DeÔ¨Ånition 9.2 is, for the sake of precision, called a left-
action. A right-action would associate to each pair (g, a) with g ‚àà G and a ‚àà A an
element ag ‚àà A; our make-believe associativity would now say
a(gh) = (ag)h
for all a ‚àà A and g, h ‚àà G. This is a diÔ¨Äerent requirement than the one given in
DeÔ¨Ånition 9.2; multiplication on the right in a group G gives a prototypical example
of a right-action of G (on itself).
Every right-action may be turned into a left-action with due care (cf. Exer-
cise 9.3). Therefore it is not restrictive to just consider left-actions; from now on,
an ‚Äòaction‚Äô will be understood to be a left-action, unless stated otherwise.
9.3. Transitive actions and the category G-Set.
DeÔ¨Ånition 9.6. An action of a group G on a (nonempty) set A is transitive if
‚àÄa, b ‚àà A ‚àÉg ‚àà G such that b = ga.
 
For example, the left-multiplication action of a group on itself is transitive.
Transitive actions are the basic ingredients making up every action; this is seen by
means of the following important concepts.
DeÔ¨Ånition 9.7. The orbit of a ‚àà A under an action of a group G is the set
OG(a) := {ga | g ‚àà G}.
 
41 This is left-multiplication in the sense that the ‚Äòacting‚Äô element g of G is placed to the left
of the element a ‚Äòacted upon‚Äô.
9. Group actions
 111
DeÔ¨Ånition 9.8. Let G act on a set A, and let a ‚àà A. The stabilizer subgroup of
a consists of the elements of G which Ô¨Åx a:
StabG(a) := {g ‚àà G | ga = a}.
 
Orbits of an action of a group G on a set A form a partition of A; and we
have an induced, transitive action of G on each orbit. Therefore we can, in a
sense, ‚Äòunderstand‚Äô all actions if we understand transitive actions. This will be
accomplished in a moment, by studying actions related to stabilizers.
For any group G, sets endowed with a (left) G-action form in a natural way
a category G-Set: objects are pairs (œÅ, A), where œÅ : G √ó A ‚Üí A is an action (as
in DeÔ¨Ånition 9.2) and morphisms between two objects are set-functions which are
compatible with the actions. That is, a morphism
(œÅ, A) ‚Üí (œÅ , A )
in G-Set amounts to a set-function œï : A ‚Üí A such that the diagram
G √ó A

commutes. In the usual shorthand notation omitting the œÅ‚Äôs, this means that

that is, the action ‚Äòcommutes‚Äô with œï. Such functions are called (G-)equivariant.
We therefore have a notion of isomorphism of G-sets (deÔ¨Åned as in ¬ßI.4.1); the
reader should expect (and should verify) that these are nothing but the equivariant
bijections.
Among G-sets we single out the sets G/H of left-cosets of subgroups H of G;
as noted in Example 9.4, G acts on G/H by left-multiplication.
Proposition 9.9. Every transitive left-action of G on a nonempty set A is isomor-
phic to the left-multiplication of G on G/H, for H = the stabilizer of any a ‚àà A.
Proof. Let G act transitively on a set A, let a ‚àà A be any element, and let
H = StabG (a). I claim that there is an equivariant bijection
œï : G/H ‚Üí A
deÔ¨Åned by
œï(gH) := ga
for all g ‚àà G.
Indeed, Ô¨Årst of all œï is well-deÔ¨Åned: if g1H = g2 H, then g1 ‚àí1 g2 ‚àà H, hence
‚àí1
(g1 g2 )a = a, and it follows that g1a = g2 a as needed. To verify that œï is bijective,
deÔ¨Åne a function œà : A ‚Üí G/H by sending an element ga of A to gH; œà is well-
deÔ¨Åned because if g1 a = g2 a, then g1 ‚àí1 (g2a) = a, so g1 ‚àí1 g2 ‚àà H and g1H = g2 H. It
is clear that œï and œà are inverses of each other; hence œï is a bijection.
Equivariance is immediate: œï(g (gH)) = g  ga = g  œï(gH).

 II. Groups, Ô¨Årst encounter
Corollary 9.10. If O is an orbit of the action of a Ô¨Ånite group G on a set A, then
O is a Ô¨Ånite set and
|O|
 divides
 |G|.
Proof. By Proposition 9.9 there is a bijection between O and G/ StabG (a) for any
element a ‚àà O; thus
|O| ¬∑ | StabG(a)| = |G|
by Corollary 8.14.
 
Corollary 9.10 upgrades Lagrange‚Äôs theorem to orbits of any action; it is ex-
tremely useful, as it provides a very strong constraint on group actions.
Example 9.11. There are no transitive actions of S3 on a set with 5 elements.
Indeed, 5 does not divide 6.
 
Ultimately, almost everything we will prove in Chapter IV on the structure of
Ô¨Ånite groups will be a consequence of ‚Äòcounting arguments‚Äô stemming from applica-
tions of Corollary 9.10 to actions of a group by conjugation or left-multiplication.
There may seem to be an element of arbitrariness in the statement of Propo-
sition 9.9: what if we change the element a of which we are taking the stabilizer?
The stabilizer may change, but it does so in a controlled way:
Proposition 9.12. Suppose a group G acts on a set A, and let a ‚àà A, g ‚àà G,
b = ga. Then
‚àí1StabG (b) = g StabG (a)g .
Proof. Indeed, assume h ‚àà StabG (a); then
‚àí1 ‚àí1(ghg )(b) = gh(g g)a = gha = ga = b :
thus ghg‚àí1 ‚àà StabG (b). This proves the ‚äá inclusion; ‚äÜ follows by the same argu-
ment, noting that a = g‚àí1 b.
 
For example, if StabG (a) happens to be normal, then it is really independent
of a (in any given orbit). In any case, there is an isomorphism of G-sets beween
‚àí1G/H and G/(gHg ), as follows from these considerations (and as the reader will
independently check in Exercise 9.13).

10. Group objects in categories
10.1. Categorical viewpoint. The deÔ¨Ånition of group (DeÔ¨Ånition 1.2) is Ô¨Årmly
grounded on the category Set: A group is a set G endowed with a binary opera-
tion. . . . However, we have noticed along the way (for example, in ¬ß3) that what is
really behind it is a pair of functions:
m : G √ó G ‚Üí G,
 Œπ : G ‚Üí G
satisfying certain properties (which translate into associativity, existence of inverses,
etc.). Much of what we have seen could be expressed exclusively in terms of these
functions, systematically replacing considerations on ‚Äòelements‚Äô by suitable commu-
tative diagrams and enforcing universal properties as a means to deÔ¨Åne key notions
such as the quotient of a group by a subgroup. For example, homomorphisms may
be deÔ¨Åned purely in terms of the commutativity of a diagram: cf. DeÔ¨Ånition 3.1.
This point of view may be transferred easily to categories other than Set, and
the corresponding notions are very important in modern mathematics.
DeÔ¨Ånition 10.1. Let C be a category with (Ô¨Ånite) products and with a Ô¨Ånal
object 1.
A group object in C consists of an object G of C and of morphisms

a topological group is a group object in the category of topological spaces; a Lie
group is a group object in the category of diÔ¨Äerentiable manifolds, etc.

Rings and modules
Chapter III
1. DeÔ¨Ånition of ring
In this chapter we will do for rings and modules what we have done in Chapter II for
groups: describe them in general terms, with particular attention to distinguished
subobjects and quotients. More detailed information on these structures will be
deferred to later chapters: in Chapter V we will look more carefully at several
interesting classes of rings, and modules (over commutative rings) will take center
stage in our rapid overview of linear algebra in Chapter VI and following. In this
chapter I will also include a brief jaunt into homological algebra, a topic that will
entertain us greatly in Chapter IX.
1.1. DeÔ¨Ånition. Rings (and modules) are deÔ¨Åned by ‚Äòdecorating‚Äô abelian groups
with additional data. As motivation for the introduction of such structures, note
that all number-based examples of groups that we have encountered, such as Z or R,
are endowed with an operation of multiplication as well as the ‚Äòaddition‚Äô making
them into (abelian) groups. The ‚Äòring axioms‚Äô will reÔ¨Çect closely the properties and
compatibilities of these two operations in such examples.
These examples are, however, very special. A more sophisticated motivation
for the introduction of rings arises by analyzing further the structure of homomor-
phisms of abelian groups. Recall (¬ßII.4.4) that if G, H are abelian groups, then
HomAb (G, H) is also an abelian group. In particular, if G is an abelian group, then
so is the set of endomorphisms EndAb(G) = HomAb (G, G). More is true: mor-
phisms from an object of a category to itself may be composed with each other (by
deÔ¨Ånition of category!). Thus, two operations coexist in EndAb (G): addition (in-
herited from G, making EndAb (G) an abelian group), and composition. These two
operations are compatible with each other in a sense captured by the ring axioms:
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.1. A ring (R, +, ¬∑) is an abelian group (R, +) endowed with a second
binary operation ¬∑, satisfying on its own the requirements of being associative and
having a two-sided identity, i.e.,
119
120
 III. Rings and modules
‚Ä¢ (‚àÄr, s, t ‚àà R) :
 (r ¬∑ s) ¬∑ t = r ¬∑ (s ¬∑ t),
‚Ä¢ (‚àÉ1R ‚àà R) (‚àÄr ‚àà R) :
 r ¬∑ 1R = r = 1R ¬∑ r
(which make (R, ¬∑) a monoid ), and further interacting with + via the following
distributive properties:
‚Ä¢ (‚àÄr, s, t ‚àà R) :
 (r + s) ¬∑ t = r ¬∑ t + s ¬∑ t and t ¬∑ (r + s) = t ¬∑ r + t ¬∑ s.
 
The notation ¬∑ is often omitted in formulas, and we usually refer to rings by
the name of the underlying set.
Warning: What I am calling a ‚Äòring‚Äô, others may call a ‚Äòring with identity‚Äô
or a ‚Äòring with 1‚Äô: it is not uncommon to exclude the axiom of existence of a
‚Äòmultiplicative identity‚Äô from the list of axioms deÔ¨Åning a ring. Ri ngs without
identity are sometimes called rngs, but I am not sure this should be encouraged1 .
The reader should check conventions carefully when approaching the literature.
Examples of structures without a multiplicative identity abound: for example, the
set 2Z of even integers, with the usual addition and multiplication, satisÔ¨Åes all the
ring axioms given above with the exception of the existence of 1 (and is therefore
a rng). But in these notes all rings will have 1.
Of course the multiplicative identity is necessarily unique: the argument given
for Proposition II.1.6 works verbatim.
The identity element of the abelian group underlying a ring is denoted 0R (or
simply 0, in context) and is called the ‚Äòadditive‚Äô identity. This is a special element
with respect to multiplication:
Lemma 1.2. In a ring R,
for all r ‚àà R.
0 ¬∑ r =0= r ¬∑ 0
Proof. Indeed, 0 = 0 + 0; hence, applying distributivity,
r ¬∑ 0 = r ¬∑ (0 + 0) = r ¬∑ 0 + r ¬∑ 0,
from which r ¬∑ 0 = 0 by cancellation (in the group (R, +)). The equality 0 ¬∑ r = 0 is
proven similarly.
 
It is equally easy to check that multiplication behaves as expected on ‚Äòsubtrac-
tion‚Äô. In fact, if ‚àí1 denotes the additive inverse of 1, then the additive inverse ‚àír
of any r ‚àà R is the result of the multiplication (‚àí1) ¬∑ r: indeed, using distributivity,
r + (‚àí1) ¬∑ r = 1 ¬∑ r + (‚àí1) ¬∑ r = (1 ‚àí 1) ¬∑ r = 0 ¬∑ r = 0
(by Lemma 1.2) from which (‚àí1)r = ‚àír follows by (additive) cancellation.
1The term ‚Äòrng‚Äô was introduced with this meaning by Jacobson; but essentially at the same
time Mac Lane introduced Rng as the name for the category of rings with identity. Hoping to steer
clear of this clash of terminology I have opted to call this category ‚ÄòRing‚Äô.
1. DeÔ¨Ånition of ring
 121
1.2. First examples and special classes of rings.
Example 1.3. We can deÔ¨Åne a ring structure on a trivial group {‚àó} by letting
‚àó ¬∑ ‚àó = ‚àó (as well as ‚àó + ‚àó = ‚àó); this is often called the zero-ring. Note that 0 = 1
in this ring (cf. Exercise 1.1).
 
Example 1.4. More interesting examples are the number-based groups such as Z
or R, with the usual operations. These are very well known to our reader, who will
realize immediately that they satisfy the requirements given in DeÔ¨Ånition 1.1; but
they are very special. Why?
 
To begin with, note that multiplication is commutative in these examples; this
is not among the requirements we have posed on rings in the oÔ¨Écial deÔ¨Ånition given
above.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.5. A ring R is commutative if
‚Ä¢ (‚àÄr, s ‚àà R) :
 r ¬∑ s = s ¬∑ r.
 
Commutative rings (with identity) form an extremely important class of rings;
commutative algebra is the subÔ¨Åeld of algebra studying them. We will focus on
commutative rings in later chapters; in this chapter we will develop some of the
basic theory for the more general case of arbitrary rings (with 1).
Example 1.6. An example of a noncommutative ring that is (likely) familiar to
the readers is the ring of 2 √ó 2 matrices with, say, real entries: matrices can be
added ‚Äòcomponentwise‚Äô, and they can be multiplied as recalled in Example II.1.5;
the two operations satisfy the requirements in DeÔ¨Ånition 1.1.
Square matrices of any size, and with entries in any ring, form a ring (Exer-
cise 1.4).
 
Example 1.7. The reader is already familiar with a large class of (commutative)
rings: the groups Z/nZ, endowed with the multiplication deÔ¨Åned in ¬ßII.2.3 (that is:
[a]n ¬∑ [b]n := [ab]n ; this is well-deÔ¨Åned (cf. Exercise II.2.14)) satisfy the ring axioms
listed above.
 
The rings Z/nZ prompt me to highlight an important point. Another reason
why rings such as Z, Q, R, . . . are special is that multiplicative cancellation by
nonzero elements holds in these rings. Of course additive cancellation is automatic,
since rings are in particular (abelian) groups; and multiplicative cancellation clearly
fails in general since one cannot ‚Äòcancel 0‚Äô (by Lemma 1.2). But even the fact that
(‚àÄa ‚àà R, a = 0) :
 a ¬∑ b = a ¬∑ c =‚áí b = c,
which holds, for example, in Z, does not follow from the ring axioms.
Indeed, this cancellation property does not hold in all rings; it may well fail in
the rings Z/nZ. For example,
[2]6 ¬∑ [4]6 = [8]6 = [2]6 = [2]6 ¬∑ [1]6
even though [4]6 = [1]6 .
The problem here is that in Z/6Z there are elements a = 0 such that a ¬∑ b = 0
for some b = 0 (take a = [2]6 , b = [3]6 ).
122
 III. Rings and modules
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.8. An element a in a ring R is a left-zero-divisor if there exist ele-
ments b = 0 in R for which ab = 0.
 
The reader will have no diÔ¨Éculty Ô¨Åguring out what a right-zero-divisor should
be. The element 0 is a zero-divisor in all nonzero rings R; the zero ring is the only
ring without zero-divisors(!).
Proposition 1.9. In a ring R, a ‚àà R is not a left- (resp., right-) zero-divisor if
and only if left (resp., right) multiplication by a is an injective function R ‚Üí R.
In other words, a is not a left- (resp., right-) zero-divisor if and only if multi-
plicative left- (resp., right-) cancellation by the element a holds in R.
Proof. Let‚Äôs verify the ‚Äòleft‚Äô statement (the ‚Äòright‚Äô statement is of course entirely
analogous). Assume a is not a left-zero-divisor and ab = ac for b, c ‚àà R. Then, by
distributivity,
a(b ‚àí c) = ab ‚àí ac = 0,
and this implies b ‚àí c = 0 since a is not a left-zero-divisor; that is, b = c. This
proves that left-multiplication is injective in this case.
Conversely, if a is a left-zero-divisor, then ‚àÉb = 0 such that ab = 0 = a ¬∑ 0; this
shows that left-multiplication is not injective in this case, concluding the proof. 
Rings such as Z, Q, etc., are commutative rings without (nonzero) zero-divisors.
Such rings are very special, but very important, and they deserve their own termi-
nology:
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.10. An integral domain is a nonzero commutative ring R (with 1)
such that
(‚àÄa, b ‚àà R) : ab = 0 =‚áí a = 0 or b = 0.
 
Chapter V will be entirely devoted to integral domains.
An element which is not a zero-divisor is called a non-zero-divisor. Thus, inte-
gral domains are those nonzero commutative rings in which every nonzero element
is a non-zero-divisor. By Proposition 1.9, multiplicative cancellation by nonzero
elements holds in integral domains. The rings Z, Q, R, C are all integral domains.
As we have seen, some Z/nZ are not integral domains.
Here is one of those places where the reader can do him/herself a great favor by
pausing a moment and Ô¨Åguring something out: answer the question, which Z/nZ
are integral domains? This is entirely within reach, given what the reader knows
already. Don‚Äôt read ahead before Ô¨Åguring this out‚Äîthis question will be answered
within a few short paragraphs, spoiling all the fun.
There are even subtler reasons why Z is a very special ring: we will see in due
time that it is a ‚ÄòUFD‚Äô (unique factorization domain); in fact, it is a ‚ÄòPID‚Äô (principal
ideal domain); in fact, it is more special still!, as it is a ‚ÄòEuclidean domain‚Äô. All of
this will be discussed in Chapter V, particularly ¬ßV.2.
However, Q, R, C are more special than all of that and then some, since they
are Ô¨Åelds.
1. DeÔ¨Ånition of ring
 123
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.11. An element u of a ring R is a left-unit if ‚àÉv ‚àà R such that uv = 1;
it is a right-unit if ‚àÉv ‚àà R such that vu = 1. Units are two-sided units.
 
Proposition 1.12. In a ring R:
‚Ä¢ u is a left- (resp., right-) unit if and only if left- (resp., right-) multiplication
by u is a surjective functions R ‚Üí R;
‚Ä¢ if u is a left- (resp., right-) unit, then right- (resp., left-) multiplication by u
is injective; that is, u is not a right- (resp., left-) zero-divisor;
‚Ä¢ the inverse of a two-sided unit is unique;
‚Ä¢ two-sided units form a group under multiplication.
Proof. These assertions are all straightforward. For example, denote by œÅu : R ‚Üí
R right-multiplication by u, so that œÅu (r) = ru. If u is a right-unit, let v ‚àà R be
such that vu = 1; then ‚àÄr ‚àà R
œÅu ‚ó¶ œÅv (r) = œÅu(rv) = (rv)u = r(vu) = r1R = r.
That is, œÅv is a right-inverse to œÅu , and therefore œÅu is surjective (Proposition I.2.1).
Conversely, if œÅu is surjective, then there exists a v such that 1R = œÅ(u) (v) = vu,
so that u is a right-unit.
This checks the Ô¨Årst statement, for right-units.
For the second statement, denote by Œªu : R ‚Üí R left-multiplication by u:
Œªu (r) = ur. Assume u is a right-unit, and let v be such that vu = 1R ; then ‚àÄr ‚àà R
Œªv ‚ó¶ Œªu (r) = Œªv (ur) = v(ur) = (vu)r = 1Rr = r.
That is, Œªv is a left-inverse to Œªu, so Œªu is injective (Proposition I.2.1 again).
The rest of the proof is left to the reader (Exercise 1.9).
 
Since the inverse of a two-sided unit u is unique, we can give it a name; of
course we denote it by u‚àí1 . The reader should keep in mind that inverses of left- or
right-units are not unique in general, so the ‚Äòinverse notation‚Äô is not appropriate
for them.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.13. A division ring is a ring in which every nonzero element is a
two-sided unit.
 
We will mostly be concerned with the commutative case, which has its own
name:
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.14. A Ô¨Åeld is a nonzero commutative ring R (with 1) in which every
nonzero element is a unit.
 
The whole of Chapter VII will be devoted to studying Ô¨Åelds.
By Proposition 1.12 (second part), every Ô¨Åeld is an integral domain, but not
conversely: indeed, Z is an integral domain, but it is not a Ô¨Åeld. Remember:
Ô¨Åeld ‚áí integral domain,
integral domain  Ô¨Åeld.
There is a situation, however, in which the two notions coincide:
124
 III. Rings and modules
Proposition 1.15. Assume R is a Ô¨Ånite commutative ring; then R is an integral
domain if and only if it is a Ô¨Åeld.
Proof. One implication holds for all rings, as pointed out above; thus we only have
to verify that if R is a Ô¨Ånite integral domain, then it is a Ô¨Åeld. This amounts to
verifying that if a is a non-zero-divisor in a Ô¨Ånite (commutative) ring R, then it is
a unit in R.
Now, if a is a non-zero-divisor, then multiplication by a in R is injective (Propo-
sition 1.9); hence it is surjective, as the ring is Ô¨Ånite, by the pigeon-hole principle;
hence a is a unit, by Proposition 1.12.
 
Remark 1.16. A little surprisingly, the hypothesis of commutativity in Proposi-
tion 1.15 is actually superÔ¨Çuous: a theorem known as Wedderburn‚Äôs little theorem
shows that Ô¨Ånite division rings are necessarily commutative. The reader will prove
this fact in a distant future (Exercise VII.5.14).
 
Example 1.17. The group of units in the ring Z/nZ is precisely the group (Z/nZ)‚àó
introduced in ¬ßII.2.3: indeed, a class [m]n is a unit if and only if (right-) multipli-
cation by [m]n is surjective (by Proposition 1.12), if and only if the map a ‚Üí a[m]n
is surjective, if and only if [m]n generates Z/nZ, if and only if gcd(m, n) = 1
(Corollary II.2.5), if and only if [m]n ‚àà (Z/nZ)‚àó.
In particular, those n for which all nonzero elements of Z/nZ are units (that is,
for which Z/nZ is a Ô¨Åeld ) are precisely those n ‚àà Z for which gcd(m, n) = 1 for all
m that are not multiples of n; this is the case if and only if n is prime. Putting this
together with Proposition 1.15, we get the pretty classiÔ¨Åcation (for integers p = 0)
Z/pZ integral domain ‚áê‚áí Z/pZ Ô¨Åeld ‚áê‚áí p prime,
which the reader is well advised to remember Ô¨Årmly.
 
Example 1.18. The rings Z/pZ, with p prime, are not the only Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åelds. In
fact, for every prime p and every integer r > 0 there is a (unique, in a suitable
sense) multiplication on the product group
Z/pZ √ó ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ √ó Z/pZ

r times
making it into a Ô¨Åeld. A discussion of these Ô¨Åelds will have to wait until we have
accumulated much more material (cf. ¬ßVII.5.1), but the reader could already try
to construct small examples ‚Äòby hand‚Äô (cf. Exercise 1.11).

We will occasionally consider a polynomial ring in inÔ¨Ånitely many indetermi-

indeterminates. Keep in mind, however, that polynomials are Ô¨Ånite linear combi-

is denoted R[[x]]. Regrettably, we will only occasionally encounter these rings in
this book.
The ring R[x] is (clearly) commutative if R is commutative; it is an integral
domain if R is an integral domain (Exercise 1.15); but it has no chances of being
a Ô¨Åeld even if R is a Ô¨Åeld, since x has no inverse in R[x]. The question of which
properties of R are ‚Äòinherited‚Äô by R[x] is subtle and important, and we will give it
a great deal of attention in later sections.
1.4. Monoid rings. The polynomial ring is an instance of a rather general con-
struction, which is occasionally very useful. A semigroup is a set endowed with an
associative operation; a monoid is a semigroup with an identity element. Thus a
group is a monoid in which every element has an inverse; positive integers with
ordinary addition form a semigroup, while the set N of natural numbers (that is,
nonnegative integers2) is a monoid under addition.
Given a monoid (M, ¬∑) and a ring R, we can obtain a new ring R[M ] as follows.
Elements of R[M ] are formal linear combinations

2. The category Ring
2.1. Ring homomorphisms. Ring homomorphisms are deÔ¨Åned in the natural
way: if R, S are rings, a function œï : R ‚Üí S is a ring homomorphism if it preserves
both operations and the identity element. That is, œï must be a homomorphism of
the underlying abelian groups,
(‚àÄa, b ‚àà R) :
 œï(a + b) = œï(a) + œï(b),
it must preserve the operation of multiplication,
(‚àÄa, b ‚àà R) :
 œï(ab) = œï(a)œï(b),
and Ô¨Ånally
œï(1R ) = 1S .
It is evident that rings form a category, with ring homomorphisms as mor-
phisms. I will denote this category by Ring.
The zero-ring is clearly Ô¨Ånal in Ring. However, note that it is not initial:
because of the requirement that ring homomorphisms send 1 to 1, the only rings
to which zero-rings map homomorphically are the zero-rings (Exercise 2.1).
The category Ring does have initial objects: the ring of integers Z (with the
usual operations +, ¬∑) is initial in Ring. Indeed, for every ring R we can deÔ¨Åne a
group homomorphism œï : Z ‚Üí R by
(‚àÄn ‚àà Z) :
 œï(n) = n ¬∑ 1R ,
that is, as the ‚Äòexponential map‚Äô 1R corresponding to 1R ‚àà R; cf. ¬ßII.4.1. But œï is
in fact a ring homomorphism, since œï(1) = 1R , and
œï(mn) = (mn)1R = m(n1R ) = !
 (m1R) ¬∑ (n1R ) = œï(m) ¬∑ œï(n),
130
 III. Rings and modules
where the equality = !
 holds by the distributivity axiom4 . This ring homomorphism
is unique, since it is determined by the requirement that œï(1) = 1R and by the fact
that œï preserves addition. Thus for every ring R there exists one and only one ring
homomorphism Z ‚Üí R, showing that Z is initial in Ring.
This should already convince the reader that Z is a very special ring. There is
in fact nothing arbitrary about Z: knowledge of the group Z determines the ring
structure of Z, as will be underscored below. This is one of the main reasons why
I included the ‚Äòidentity‚Äô axiom in the list in DeÔ¨Ånition 1.1: there are many noniso-
morphic structures of ‚Äòring without identity‚Äô on the group (Z, +) (cf. Exercise 2.15),
but only one structure of ring with identity (Exercise 2.16).
Ring homomorphisms preserve units: that is, if u is a (left-, resp., right-) unit
in R and œï : R ‚Üí S is a ring homomorphism, then œï(u) is a (left-, resp., right-)
unit. Indeed, if v is a (right-, say) inverse of u, then
œï(u)œï(v) = œï(uv) = œï(1R ) = 1S ,
so that œï(v) is a (right-) inverse of œï(u).
On the other hand, the image of a non-zero-divisor by a ring homomorphism
may well be a zero-divisor: for example, the canonical projection œÄ : Z ‚Üí Z/6Z
is a ring homomorphism, and 2 is a non-zero-divisor in Z, yet œÄ(2) = [2]6 is a
zero-divisor.

2.3. Monomorphisms and epimorphisms. The kernel of a homomorphism œï :
R ‚Üí S of rings is
ker œï := {r ‚àà R | œï(r) = 0} :
that is, it is nothing but the kernel of œï when the latter is viewed as a homomor-
phism of groups. As such, ker œï is a subgroup of (R, +); it satisÔ¨Åes an even stronger
requirement7, which we will explore at great length in ¬ß3.
The reader may hope that an analog to Proposition II.6.12 holds in Ring, and
this is indeed the case:
Proposition 2.4. For a ring homomorphism œï : R ‚Üí S, the following are equiva-
lent:
(a) œï is a monomorphism;
(b) ker œï = {0};
(c) œï is injective (as a set-function).
Proof. We prove (a) =‚áí (b), leaving the rest to the reader. Assume œï : R ‚Üí S is
a monomorphism and r ‚àà ker œï. Applying the extension property of Example 2.2,
we obtain unique ring homomorphisms evr : Z[x] ‚Üí R such that evr (x) = r and
ev0 : Z[x] ‚Üí R such that ev(x) = 0. Consider the parallel ring homomorphisms:

By Proposition 2.4, if S ‚Üí R is a monomorphism, then S may be identiÔ¨Åed
with a subset of R; the following deÔ¨Ånition formalizes this situation.
DeÔ¨Ånition 2.5. A subring S of a ring R is a ring whose underlying set is a subset
of R and such that the inclusion function S ‚Üí R is a ring homomorphism.
 
Equivalently, a subring of R is a subset S ‚äÜ R which contains 1R and satisÔ¨Åes
the ring axioms with respect to the operations +, ¬∑ induced from R. It is immedi-
ately checked that S ‚äÜ R is a subring if it is a subgroup of (R, +), it is closed with
respect to ¬∑, and it contains 1R . As a nonexample, the zero-ring is not a subring of
a nonzero ring R (because it does not contain 1R ).
Proposition 2.4 may induce the reader to believe that Ring and Grp are rather
similar from this general categorical standpoint. But a new phenomenon occurs
concerning epimorphisms. A surjective map of rings is certainly an epimorphism
in Ring, since it is already an epimorphism in Set‚Äîwe have run into this argument
earlier (e.g., ‚Äò(c) =‚áí (a)‚Äô in Proposition II.8.18); but unlike as in Set, Grp, and Ab,
7 Note that ker œï cannot be a subring in any reasonable sense, according to our deÔ¨Ånition of
ring, since it does not contain a multiplicative identity in all but very pathological situations. It
is a subring if the identity requirement is omitted.

2.4. Products. Products exist in Ring: if R1 , R2 are rings, then R1 √ó R2 may
be deÔ¨Åned by endowing the direct product of groups R1 √ó R2 (cf. ¬ßII.3.4) with
componentwise multiplication. Thus, both operations on R1 √ó R2 are deÔ¨Åned com-
ponentwise: ‚àÄ(a1, a2 ), (b1 , b2 ) ‚àà R1 √ó R2 ,
(a1 , a2 ) + (b1 , b2 ) := (a1 + b1 , a2 + b2),
(a1 , a2 ) ¬∑ (b1 , b2 ) := (a1 ¬∑ b1 , a2 ¬∑ b2 ).
The identity in R1 √ó R2 is (1R1 , 1R2 ). The reader will check (Exercise 2.13) that
R1 √ó R2 is indeed a categorical product in Ring.
The reader should keep in mind that in general this is not the only ring struc-
ture one can deÔ¨Åne on the direct product of underlying groups. For example, as
mentioned in ¬ß1.2, one can deÔ¨Åne a Ô¨Åeld whose underlying group is Z/pZ √ó Z/pZ,
while the product ring Z/pZ √ó Z/pZ is very far from being a Ô¨Åeld (why?).
The situation with coproducts is more unfortunate‚Äîdealing with this in any
generality (even for the case of commutative rings) requires tensor products, and
by the time we develop tensor products (¬ßVIII.2), we will have almost forgotten
this question. But the universal property reviewed in ¬ß2.2 suÔ¨Éces to deal with
simple examples, and the reader should work out one template case now, for fun
(Exercise 2.14).
Incidentally‚Äîwith the case of abelian groups in mind (¬ßII.3.5), the reader may
be tempted to consider a ‚Äòdirect sum of rings‚Äô, agreeing with the product in the Ô¨Ånite

2.5. EndAb (G). This is as good a place as any to expand a little on one of the main
examples of rings, and one that is not quite as special as the examples reviewed
in ¬ß1.2.
For every abelian group G, the group EndAb(G) := HomAb(G, G) of endo-
morphisms of G is a ring, under the operations of addition and composition (as
discussed in the paragraph preceding DeÔ¨Ånition 1.1). Indeed, associativity follows
from the category axioms, and distributivity is immediately checked.
It is instructive to examine carefully the endomorphism ring of the abelian
group (Z, +):
Proposition 2.6. End Ab (Z) ‚àº
 = Z as rings.
Proof. Consider the function
œï : EndAb (Z) ‚Üí Z
deÔ¨Åned by
œï(Œ±) = Œ±(1)
for all group homomorphisms Œ± : Z ‚Üí Z. Then œï is a group homomorphism: the
addition in EndAb(Z) is deÔ¨Åned so that ‚àÄn ‚àà Z
(Œ± + Œ≤)(n) = Œ±(n) + Œ≤(n)
(cf. ¬ßII.4.4); in particular
œï(Œ± + Œ≤) = (Œ± + Œ≤)(1) = Œ±(1) + Œ≤(1) = œï(Œ±) + œï(Œ≤).
Further, œï is a ring homomorphism. Indeed, for Œ±, Œ≤ in EndAb (Z) denote Œ±(1)
by a; then
Œ±(n) = nŒ±(1) = na = an
for all n ‚àà Z; in particular,
Œ±(Œ≤(1)) = aŒ≤(1) = Œ±(1)Œ≤(1).
Therefore,
œï(Œ± ‚ó¶ Œ≤) = (Œ± ‚ó¶ Œ≤)(1) = Œ±(Œ≤(1)) = Œ±(1)Œ≤(1) = œï(Œ±)œï(Œ≤)
as needed. Also, œï(idZ ) = idZ (1) = 1.
Finally, œï has an inverse: for a ‚àà Z, let œà(a) be the homomorphism Œ± : Z ‚Üí Z
deÔ¨Åned by
(‚àÄn ‚àà Z) : Œ±(n) = an;
the reader will easily check that œà is a ring homomorphism and inverse to œï.
Therefore œï is a ring isomorphism, verifying the statement.

2. The category Ring
 135
Proposition 2.6 gives a sense in which the ring structure on Z is truly ‚Äònatu-
ral‚Äô: this structure arises naturally from categorical considerations, there is nothing
‚Äòarbitrary‚Äô about it.
More generally, the rings EndAb (G) and other rings arising as endomorphism
rings of other structures (e.g., vector spaces) are arguably the most important class
of examples of rings. The entire theory of modules is based on the observation that
EndAb (G) is a ring for every abelian group G.
Some of the notions reviewed in ¬ß1.2 are expressed very concretely in these
endomorphism rings; for example, the group of units in EndAb (G) is nothing but
AutAb (G).
Every ring R interacts with the ring of endomorphisms of the underlying abelian
group (R, +). For r ‚àà R, deÔ¨Åne left- and right-multiplication by r by Œªr , Œºr ,
respectively. That is, ‚àÄa ‚àà R
Œªr (a) = ra,
 Œºr (a) = ar.
The following observation is nothing more than easy notation juggling, but it is
useful; it is a ‚Äòring analog‚Äô of Cayley‚Äôs theorem (Theorem II.9.5):
Proposition 2.7. Let R be a ring. Then the function r ‚Üí Œªr is an injective ring
homomorphism
Œª : R ‚Üí EndAb (R).
Proof. For any r ‚àà R and for all a, b ‚àà R, distributivity gives
Œªr (a + b) = r(a + b) = ra + rb = Œªr (a) + Œªr (b) :
this shows that Œªr is indeed an endomorphism of the group (R, +), that is, Œªr ‚àà
EndAb (R).
The function Œª : R ‚Üí EndAb(R) deÔ¨Åned by the assignment r ‚Üí Œªr is clearly
injective, since if r = s, then
Œªr (1) = r = s = Œªs(1),
so that Œªr = Œªs .
We have to verify that Œª is in fact a homomorphism of rings. Recall that the
addition in EndAb (R) is inherited from R (cf. ¬ßII.4.4): for all r, s ‚àà R, Œªr + Œªs is
deÔ¨Åned by
(‚àÄa ‚àà R) : (Œªr + Œªs )(a) = Œªr (a) + Œªs (a).
Thus, the fact that Œª preserves addition is an immediate consequence of distribu-
tivity: for all r, s, a ‚àà R,
Œªr+s (a) = (r + s)a = ra + sa = Œªr (a) + Œªs (a).
As for multiplication, it is associativity‚Äôs turn to do its job:
Œªrs (a) = (rs)a = r(sa) = rŒªs (a) = Œªr (Œªs (a)) = (Œªr ‚ó¶ Œª s)(a).
Of course Œª1 is the identity, completing the veriÔ¨Åcation.

The function Œº : R ‚Üí EndAb(R) deÔ¨Åned by r ‚Üí Œºr is ‚Äòalmost‚Äô a ring homo-

Œºr+s = Œºr + Œºs ,
Œºrs = Œºs ‚ó¶ Œºr ,
and Œº1 = idR . That is, Œº would in fact be a ring homomorphism if we ‚Äòreversed‚Äô
multiplication11 in R. Of course this issue disappears if R happens to be commu-
tative (since then Œª = Œº).

3. Ideals and quotient rings
3.1. Ideals. In both Set and Grp we have been able to ‚Äòclassify‚Äô surjective mor-
phisms: in both cases, a surjective morphism is, up to natural identiÔ¨Åcations, a
quotient by a (suitable) equivalence relation. In Grp, we have seen that such equiv-
alence relations arise in fact from certain substructures: normal subgroups.
The situation in Ring is analogous. We will establish a canonical decomposition
for rings, modeled after Theorems I.2.7 and II.8.1; the corresponding version of the
‚ÄòÔ¨Årst isomorphism theorem‚Äô (Corollary II.8.2) will identify every surjective ring
homomorphism with a quotient by a suitable substructure. The role of normal
subgroups will be played by ideals.
DeÔ¨Ånition 3.1. Let R be a ring. A subgroup I of (R, +) is a left-idealrI ‚äÜ I for all r ‚àà R; that is,
(‚àÄr ‚àà R)(‚àÄa ‚àà I) :
 ra ‚àà I;
it is a right-ideal if Ir ‚äÜ I for all r ‚àà R; that is,
(‚àÄr ‚àà R)(‚àÄa ‚àà I) :
 ar ‚àà I.
A two-sided ideal is a subgroup I which is both a left- and a right-ideal.
of R if

Of course in a commutative ring there is no distinction between left- and right-
ideals. Even in the general setting, we will almost exclusively be concerned with
two-sided ideals; thus I will omit qualiÔ¨Åers, and an ideal of a ring will implicitly be
two-sided.
3. Ideals and quotient rings
 139
Remark 3.2. As seen in ¬ß2.3, a subring of R is a subset S ‚äÜ R which contains 1R
and satisÔ¨Åes the ring axioms with respect to the operations +, ¬∑ induced from R.
Ideals are close to being subrings: they are subgroups, and they are closed
with respect to multiplication. But the only ideal of a ring R containing 1R is
R itself: this is an immediate consequence of the ‚Äòabsorption properties‚Äô stated in
DeÔ¨Ånition 3.1. Thus ideals are in general not subrings; they are ‚Äòrngs‚Äô.
 
Ideals are considerably more important than subrings in the development of
the theory of rings12 . Of course the image of a ring homomorphism is necessarily a
subring of the target; but a lesson learned from ¬ßII.8.1 and following is that kernels
really capture the structure of a homomorphism, and kernels are ideals:
Example 3.3. Let œï : R ‚Üí S be any ring homomorphism. Then ker œï is an ideal
of R.
Indeed, we know already that ker œï is a subgroup; we have to verify the absorp-
tion properties. These are an immediate consequence of Lemma 1.2: for all r ‚àà R,
all a ‚àà ker œï, we have
œï(ra) = œï(r)œï(a) = œï(r) ¬∑ 0 = 0,
œï(ar) = œï(a)œï(r) = 0 ¬∑ œï(r) = 0.
More generally, it is easy to verify that the inverse image of an ideal is an ideal
(Exercise 3.2) and {0S } is clearly an ideal of S.
Similarly to the situation with normal subgroups in the context of groups, we
will soon see that ‚Äòkernels of ring homomorphisms‚Äô and ‚Äòideals‚Äô are in fact equivalent
concepts.
 
3.2. Quotients. Let I be a subgroup of the abelian group (R, +) of a ring R.
Subgroups of abelian groups are automatically normal, so we have a quotient group
R/I, whose elements are the cosets of I:
r + I
(written, of course, in additive notation). Further, we have a surjective group
homomorphism
R
œÄ : R ‚Üí , r ‚Üí r + I.
I
As we have explored in great detail for groups, this construction satisÔ¨Åes a suit-
able universal property with respect to group homomorphisms (Theorem II.7.12).
Of course we are now going to ask under what circumstances this construction can
be performed in Ring, satisfying the analogous universal property with respect to
ring homomorphisms.
That is, what should we ask of I, in order to have a ring structure on R/I,
so that œÄ becomes a ring homomorphism? Go Ô¨Ågure this out for yourself, before
reading ahead!
12Arguably, the reason is that ideals are precisely the submodules of a ring R.

As so often is the case, the requirement is precisely what tells us the answer.
Since œÄ will have to be a ring homomorphism, the multiplication in R/I must be
as follows: for all (a + I), (b + I) in R/I,
(a + I) ¬∑ (b + I) = œÄ(a) ¬∑ œÄ(b) = !?

where the equality = is forced if œÄ is to be a ring homomorphism.
This says that there is only one sensible ring structure on R/I, given by
(‚àÄa, b ‚àà R) :
 (a + I)(b + I) := ab + I.
The reader should realize right away that if this operation is well-deÔ¨Åned, then it
does make R/I into a ring: associativity will be inherited from the associativity
in R, and the identity will simply be the coset 1 + I of 1.
So all is well, if the proposed operation is well-deÔ¨Åned. But is it going to be
well-deÔ¨Åned?
Example 3.4. It need not be, if I is an arbitrary subgroup of R. For example,
take Z as a subgroup of Q; then

DeÔ¨Ånition 3.5. This ring R/I is called the quotient ring of R modulo I.
 
Example 3.6. We know that all subgroups of (Z, +) are of the form nZ for a
nonnegative integer n (Proposition II.6.9). It is immediately veriÔ¨Åed that all sub-
groups of Z are in fact ideals of the ring (Z, +, ¬∑). The quotients Z/nZ are of course
nothing but the rings so-denoted in ¬ß1.2 (and earlier).

The fact that Z is initial in Ring now prompts a natural deÔ¨Ånition. For a ring R,
let f : Z ‚Üí R be the unique ring homomorphism, deÔ¨Åned by a ‚Üí a ¬∑ 1R . Then
ker f = nZ for a well-deÔ¨Åned nonnegative integer n determined by R.
DeÔ¨Ånition 3.7. The characteristic of R is this nonnegative integer n.
 
Thus, the characteristic of R is n > 0 if the order of 1R as an element of (R, +)
is a positive integer n, while the characteristic is 0 if the order of 1R is ‚àû.

I have now fulÔ¨Ålled my promise to identify the notions of ideal and kernel of

As in the group case, the reader should develop the healthy instinctive reaction
of viewing every surjective homomorphism of rings as a quotient (by an ideal), up

Equally instinctive should be the realization that ideals of a quotient R/I are in

this observation is best packaged in the corresponding ‚Äòthird isomorphism theorem‚Äô:
Proposition 3.11. Let I be an ideal of a ring R, and let J be an ideal of R
containing I. Then J/I is an ideal of R/I, and

Proof. Since I ‚äÜ J = ker(R ‚Üí R/J), we have an induced ring homomorphism

4. Ideals and quotients: Remarks and examples. Prime and
maximal ideals
4.1. Basic operations. It is often convenient to deÔ¨Åne ideals in terms of a set of
generators.
Let a ‚àà R be any element of a ring. Then the subset I = Ra of R is a left-ideal
of R. Indeed, for all r ‚àà R we have
rI = rRa ‚äÜ Ra
as needed. Similarly, aR is right-ideal.
14After George Boole.
4. Ideals and quotients: Remarks and examples
 145
In the commutative case, these two subsets coincide and are denoted (a). This
is the principal ideal generated by a. For example, the zero-ideal {0} = (0) and the
whole ring R = (1) are both principal ideals.
In

sum Œ± IŒ± is an ideal of R. If aŒ± is any collection of elements of a commutative
ring R, then

(aŒ± )Œ±‚ààA :=

is the ideal generated by the elements aŒ±. In particular,
(a1, . . . , an ) = (a1) + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + (an )
is the smallest ideal of R containing a1 , . . . , a n; this ideal consists of the elements
of R that may be written as
r1 a1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + rn a n
for r1, . . . , rn ‚àà R. An ideal I of a commutative ring R is Ô¨Ånitely generated if
I = (a1, . . . , an ) for some a1 , . . . , an ‚àà R.
Example 4.1. It is a good idea to get used to a bit of ‚Äòcalculus‚Äô of ideals and
quotients in terms of generators; judicious use of the isomorphism theorems yields
convenient statements. For example, let R be a commutative ring, and let a, b ‚àà R;
denote by b the class of b in R/(a). Then

as ideals of R/(a).
 
Note that principal ideals are (very special) Ô¨Ånitely generated ideals. These
notions are so important that we give special names to rings in which they are
satisÔ¨Åed by every ideal.
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.2. A commutative ring R is Noetherian if every ideal of R is Ô¨Ånitely
generated.
 
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.3. An integral domain R is a PID (‚ÄòPrincipal Ideal Domain‚Äô) if every
ideal of R is principal.
 
Thus, PIDs are (very special) Noetherian rings. In due time we will deal at
length with these classes of rings (cf. Chapter V); Noetherian rings are very impor-
tant in number theory and algebraic geometry.
The reader is already familiar with an important PID:
Proposition 4.4. Z is a PID.
Proof. Let I ‚äÜ Z be an ideal. Since I is a subgroup, I = nZ for some n ‚àà Z, by
Proposition II.6.9. Since nZ = (n), this shows that I is principal.
 
146
 III. Rings and modules
The fact that Z is a PID captures precisely ‚Äòwhy‚Äô greatest common divisors
behave as they do in Z: if m, n are integers, then the ideal (m, n) must be principal,
and hence
(m, n) = (d)
for some (positive) integer d. This integer is manifestly the gcd of m and n: since
m ‚àà (d) and n ‚àà (d), then d | m and d | n, etc.
If k is a Ô¨Åeld, the ring of polynomials k[x] is also a PID; proving this is easy,
using the ‚Äòdivision with remainder‚Äô that we will run into very soon (¬ß4.2); the reader
should work this out on his/her own (Exercise 4.4) now. This fact will be absorbed
in the general theory when we review the general notion of ‚ÄòEuclidean domain‚Äô,
in ¬ßV.2.4.
By contrast, the ring Z[x] is not a PID: indeed, the reader should be able to
verify that the ideal (2, x) cannot be generated by a single element. As we will see
in due time, greatest common divisors make good sense in a ring such as Z[x], but
the matter is a little more delicate, since this ring is not a PID15 .
There are several more basic operations involving ideals; for now, the following
two will suÔ¨Éce.
‚Ä¢ Again assume
 that {I Œ± }Œ±‚ààA is a collection of ideals of a ring R. Then the
intersection Œ±‚ààA IŒ± is (clearly) an ideal of R; it is the largest ideal contained
in all of the ideals IŒ± .
‚Ä¢ If I, J are ideals of R, then IJ denotes the ideal generated by all products
ij with i ‚àà I, j ‚àà J. More generally, if I 1 , . . . , In are ideals in R, then the
‚Äòproduct‚Äô I1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ In denotes the ideal generated by all products i1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ in with
ik ‚àà Ik .
The reader should note the clash of notation: in the context of groups (especially
¬ßII.8.4) IJ would mean something else. Watch out!
It is clear that IJ ‚äÜ I ‚à©J: every element ij with i ‚àà I and j ‚àà J is in I (because
I is a right-ideal) and in J (because J is a left-ideal); therefore I ‚à© J contains all
products ij, and hence it must contain the ideal IJ they generate. Sometime the
product agrees with the intersection:
(4) ‚à© (3) = (12) = (4) ¬∑ (3)
 in Z;
and sometime it does not:
(4) ‚à© (6) = (12) = (24) = (4) ¬∑ (6).
The matter of whether IJ = I ‚à© J is often subtle; a prototype situation in which
this equality holds is given in Exercise 4.5.
4.2. Quotients of polynomial rings. I have already observed that the quotient
Z/nZ is our familiar ring of congruence classes modulo n. Quotients of polynomial
rings by principal ideals are a good source of ‚Äòconcrete‚Äô, but maybe less familiar,
examples.
15 It is, however, a ‚ÄòUFD‚Äô, that is, a ‚Äòunique factorization domain‚Äô. This suÔ¨Éces for a good
notion of gcd; cf. ¬ßV.2.1.
4. Ideals and quotients: Remarks and examples
 147
Let R be a (nonzero) ring, and let
f (x) = xd + ad‚àí1xd‚àí1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + a1x + a0 ‚àà R[x]
be a polynomial; for convenience, I am assuming that f (x) is monic, that is, its
leading coeÔ¨Écient (the coeÔ¨Écient of the highest power of x appearing in f (x)) is 1.
In terms of ideals, this is not a serious requirement if the coeÔ¨Écient ring R is a
Ô¨Åeld (Exercise 4.7), but it may be substantial otherwise: for example, (2x) ‚äÜ Z[x]
cannot be generated by a monic polynomial. Also note that a monic polynomial
is necessarily a non-zero-divisor (Exercise 4.8) and that if f (x) is monic, then
deg(f (x)q(x)) = deg f (x) + deg q(x) for all polynomials q(x).
It is convenient to assume that f (x) is monic because we can then divide
by f (x), with remainder. That is, if g(x) ‚àà R[x] is another polynomial, then
there exist polynomials q(x), r(x) ‚àà R[x] such that
g(x) = f (x)q(x) + r(x)
and16 deg r(x) < deg f (x). This is simply the process of ‚Äòlong division‚Äô of polyno-
mials, which is surely familiar to the reader, and can be performed over any ring
when dividing by monic polynomials17 .
The situation appears then to be similar to the situation in Z, where we also
have division with remainder. Quotients and remainders are uniquely18 determined
by g(x) and f (x):
Lemma 4.5. Let f (x) be a monic polynomial, and assume
f (x)q1 (x) + r1 (x) = f (x)q2 (x) + r2(x)
with both r1(x) and r2(x) polynomials of degree < deg f (x).and r1 (x) = r2(x).
Then q1 (x) = q2 (x)
Proof. Indeed, we have
f (x)(q1 (x) ‚àí q2(x)) = r2 (x) ‚àí r1 (x);
if r2 (x) = r1 (x), then r2 (x) ‚àí r1(x) has degree < deg f (x), while f (x)(q1 (x) ‚àí q2(x))
has degree ‚â• deg f (x), giving a contradiction. Therefore r1 (x) = r2 (x), and q1 (x) =
q2 (x) follows right away since monic polynomials are non-zero-divisors.
 
The preceding considerations may be summarized in a rather eÔ¨Écient way in
the language of ideals and cosets. We will now restrict ourselves to the commutative
case, mostly for notational convenience, but also because this will guarantee that
ideals are two-sided ideals, so that quotients are deÔ¨Åned as rings (cf. ¬ß3.2).
16 Note: With the convention that the degree of the polynomial 0 is ‚àí‚àû, the condition
deg r(x) < deg f (x) is satisÔ¨Åed by r(x) = 0.
17 The key point is that if n > d, then for all a ‚àà R we have axn = axn‚àíd f (x) + h(x) for some
polynomial h(x) of degree < n. Arguing inductively, this shows that we may perform division
by f (x) with remainder for all ‚Äòmonomials‚Äô axn , and hence (by linearity) for all polynomials
g(x) ‚àà R[x].

as cosets of the principal ideal (f (x)) in R[x].

The point is that the polynomial equation x2 + 1 = 0 has no solutions in R;
the quotient R[x]/(x2 + 1) produces a ring containing a copy of R and in which the
polynomial does have roots (that is, ¬± the class of x in the quotient). The fact that
this turns out to be isomorphic to C may not be too surprising, considering that C
is precisely a ring containing a copy of R and in which x2 + 1 does have roots (that
is, ¬±i).
Such constructions are the ‚Äúalgebraist‚Äôs way‚Äù to solve equations. We will come
back to them in ¬ßV.5.2, and in a sense the whole of Chapter VII will be devoted to
this topic.

Proposition 4.11. Let I = (1) be an ideal of a commutative ring R. Then
‚Ä¢ I is prime if and only if for all a, b ‚àà R
ab ‚àà I =‚áí (a ‚àà I or b ‚àà I);
‚Ä¢ I is maximal if and only if for all ideals J of R

Proof. The ring R/I is an integral domain if and only if ‚àÄa, b ‚àà R/I
a ¬∑ b = 0 =‚áí (a = 0 or b = 0).
This condition translates immediately to the given condition in R, with a = a + I,
b = b + I, since the 0 in R/I is I.
As for maximality, the given condition follows from the correspondence between
ideals of R/I and ideals of R containing I (¬ß3.3) and the observation that a commu-
tative ring is a Ô¨Åeld if and only if its only ideals are (0) and (1) (Exercise 3.8). 
From the formulation in terms of quotients, it is completely clear that

indeed, Ô¨Åelds are integral domains. This fact is of course easy to check in terms of
the other description, but the argument is a little more cumbersome (Exercise 4.14).
Prime ideals are not necessarily maximal, but note the following:
Proposition 4.12. Let I be an ideal of a commutative ring R. If R/I is Ô¨Ånite,
then I is prime if and only if it is maximal.
Proof. This follows immediately from Proposition 1.15.

ample 1.17.
In general, the set of prime ideals of a commutative ring R is called19 the
spectrum of R, denoted Spec R. We can ‚Äòdraw‚Äô Spec Z as follows:
Actually, attributing the fact that nonzero prime ideals of Z are maximal to
the Ô¨Åniteness of the quotients (as I have just done) is slightly misleading; a better
‚Äòexplanation‚Äô is that Z is a PID, and this phenomenon is common to all PIDs:
Proposition 4.13. Let R be a PID, and let I be a nonzero ideal in R. Then I is
prime if and only if it is maximal.
Proof. Maximal ideals are prime in every ring, so we only need to verify that
nonzero prime ideals are maximal in a PID; we will use the characterization of
prime and maximal ideals obtained in Proposition 4.11. Let I = (a) be a prime
ideal in R, with a = 0, and assume I ‚äÜ J for an ideal of R. As R is a PID, J = (b)
19Believe it or not, the term is borrowed from functional analysis.
152
 III. Rings and modules
for some b ‚àà R. Since I = (a) ‚äÜ (b) = J, we have that a = bc for some c ‚àà R. But
then b ‚àà (a) or c ‚àà (a), since I = (a) is prime.
If b ‚àà (a), then (b) ‚äÜ (a); and I = J follows. If c ‚àà (a), then c = da for some
d ‚àà R. But then
a = bc = bda,
from which bd = 1 since cancellation by the nonzero a holds in R (since R is an
integral domain). This implies that b is a unit, and hence J = (b) = R.
That is, we have shown that if I ‚äÜ J, then either I = J or J = R: thus I is
maximal, by Proposition 4.11.
 
Example 4.14. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld. Then nonzero prime ideals in k[x] are maximal,
since k[x] is a PID (as the reader has hopefully checked by now; cf. Exercise 4.4).
Therefore, a picture of Spec k[x] would look pretty much like the picture of Spec Z
shown above: with the maximal ideals at one level, all containing the prime (and
nonmaximal) ideal (0).
This picture is particularly appealing for Ô¨Åelds such as k = C, which are al-
gebraically closed, that is, in which for every nonconstant f (x) ‚àà k[x] there exists
r ‚àà k such that f (r) = 0. It would take us too far aÔ¨Åeld to discuss this notion at
any length now; but the reader should be aware that C is algebraically closed. We
will come back to this20 . Assuming this fact, it is easy to verify (Exercise 4.21)
that the maximal ideals in C[x] are all and only the ideals
(x ‚àí z)
where z ranges over all complex numbers. That is, stretching our imagination a
little, we could come up with the following picture for Spec C[x]:
There is a ‚Äòcomplex line21 worth‚Äô of maximal ideals: for each z ‚àà C we have the
maximal (x ‚àí z); the prime (0) is contained in all the maximal ideals; and there
are no other prime ideals.
The picture for Spec C[x] may serve as justiÔ¨Åcation for the fact that, in algebraic
geometry, C[x] is the ring corresponding to the ‚ÄòaÔ¨Éne line‚Äô C; it is the ring of an
algebraic curve. It turns out that the fact that there is exactly ‚Äòone level‚Äô of maximal
20 A particularly pleasant proof may be given using elementary complex analysis, as a conse-
quence of Liouville‚Äôs theorem, or the maximum modulus principle; cf. ¬ßV.5.3.
21I know: it looks like a plane. But it is a line, as a complex entity.
Exercises
 153
ideals over (0) in C[x] reÔ¨Çects precisely the fact that the corresponding geometric
object has dimension 1.
In general, the (Krull) dimension of a commutative ring R is the length of the
longest chain of prime ideals in R. Thus, Proposition 4.13 tells us that PIDs other
than Ô¨Åelds, such as Z, have ‚Äòdimension 1‚Äô. In the lingo of algebraic geometry, they
all correspond to curves.
 
Example 4.15. For examples of rings of higher dimension, consider
k[x1 , . . . , xn ],
where k is a Ô¨Åeld. Note that there are chains of prime ideals of length n
(0)  (x1 )  (x1 , x2 )  ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑  (x1, . . . , xn )
in this ring. (Why are these ideals prime? Cf. Exercise 4.13.) This says that
k[x1 , . . . , xn] has dimension ‚â• n. One can show that n is in fact the longest length of
a chain of prime ideals in k[x1 , . . . , xn ]; that is, the Krull dimension of k[x 1 , . . . , xn ]
is precisely n. In algebraic geometry, the ring C[x1, . . . , xn ] corresponds to the
n-dimensional complex space Cn .
Dealing precisely with these notions is not so easy, however. Even the seemingly
simple statement that the maximal ideals of C[x1 , . . . , xn ] are all and only the ideals
(x1 ‚àí z1, . . . , xn ‚àí zn ), for (z1, . . . , zn ) ‚àà Cn , requires a rather deep result, known
as Hilbert‚Äôs Nullstellensatz.
 
We will come back to all of this and get a very small taste of algebraic geometry
in ¬ßVII.2, after we develop (much) more machinery.

5. Modules over a ring
I have emphasized the parallels between the basic theory of groups and the basic
theory of rings; there are also important diÔ¨Äerences. In Grp, one takes the quotient
of a group, by a (normal sub)group, and the results is a group: throughout the
process one never leaves Grp. The situation is in a sense even better in Ab, where
the normality condition is automatic; one simply takes the quotient of an abelian
group by an abelian (sub)group, obtaining an abelian group.
The situation in Ring is not nearly as neat. One of the three characters in the
story is an ideal: which is not a ring according to the axioms listed in DeÔ¨Ånition 1.1,
in all but the most pathological cases. In other words, the kernel of a homomorphism
of rings is (usually) not a ring. Also, cokernels do not behave as one would hope
(cf. Exercise 2.12). Even if one relaxes the ring deÔ¨Ånition, giving up the identity
and making ideals subrings, there is no reasonably large class of examples in which
the ‚Äòideal‚Äô condition is automatically satisÔ¨Åed by substructures. In short, Ring is
not a particularly pleasant category.
Modules will Ô¨Åx all these problems. If R is a ring and I ‚äÜ R is a two-sided
ideal, then all three structures R, I, and R/I are modules over R. The category
of R-modules is the prime example of a well-behaved category: in this category
kernels and cokernels exist and do precisely what they ought to. The category
Ab is a particular case of this construction, since it is the category of modules
over Z in disguise (Example 5.4). The category of modules over a ring R will share
many of the excellent properties of Ab; and we will get a brand new, well-behaved
category for each ring R. These are all examples23 of the important notion of
abelian category.
5.1. DeÔ¨Ånition of (left-)R-module. In short, R-modules are abelian groups
endowed with an action of R. To Ô¨Çesh out this idea, recall that ‚Äòactions‚Äô in general
denote homomorphisms into some kind of endomorphism structure: for example,
we deÔ¨Åned group actions in ¬ßII.9.1 as group homomorphisms from a Ô¨Åxed group to
the groups of automorphisms of objects of a category.
We can give an analogous deÔ¨Ånition of the action of a ring on an abelian group.
Indeed, recall that if M is an abelian group, then EndAb(M ) := HomAb (M, M ) is
a ring in a natural way (cf. ¬ß2.5). A left-action of a ring R on M is then simply a
homomorphism of rings
œÉ : R ‚Üí EndAb (M );
23 In fact, it can be shown (‚ÄòFreyd-Mitchell‚Äôs embedding theorem‚Äô) that every small abelian
category is equivalent to a subcategory of the category of left-modules over a ring. So to some
extent we can understand abelian categories by understanding categories of modules well enough.
We will come back to this in Chapter IX.
5. Modules over a ring
 157
we will say that œÉ makes M into a left-R-module.
Similarly to the situation with group actions, it is convenient to spell out this
deÔ¨Ånition.
Claim 5.1. The datum of a homomorphism œÉ as above is precisely the same as the
datum of a function
œÅ : R √ó M ‚Üí M
satisfying the following requirements: (‚àÄr, s ‚àà R) (‚àÄm, n ‚àà M )
‚Ä¢ œÅ(r, m + n) = œÅ(r, m) + œÅ(r, n);
‚Ä¢ œÅ(r + s, m) = œÅ(r, m) + œÅ(s, m);
‚Ä¢ œÅ(rs, m) = œÅ(r, œÅ(s, m));
‚Ä¢ œÅ(1, m) = m.
The proof of this claim follows precisely the pattern of the discussion in the
beginning of ¬ßII.9.2, so it is left to the reader (Exercise 5.2). Of course the relation
between œÅ and œÉ is given by
œÅ(r, m) = œÉ(r)(m).
As always, carrying œÅ around is inconvenient, so it is common to omit mention of
it: œÅ(r, m) is often just denoted rm; this makes the requirements listed above a
little more readable. Summarizing and adopting this shorthand,
DeÔ¨Ånition 5.2. A left-R-module structure on an abelian group M consists of a
map R √ó M ‚Üí M , (r, m) ‚Üí rm, such that
‚Ä¢ r(m + n) = rm + rn;
‚Ä¢ (r + s)m = rm + sm;
‚Ä¢ (rs)m = r(sm);
‚Ä¢ 1m = m.

Right-R-modules are deÔ¨Åned analogously. The reader can glance back at ¬ßII.9,
especially Exercise II.9.3, for a reminder on right- vs. left-actions; the issues here are
analogous. Thus, for example, a right-R-module structure may be identiÔ¨Åed with
a left-R‚ó¶ -module structure, where R‚ó¶ is the ‚Äòopposite‚Äô ring obtained by reversing
the order of multiplication (Exercise 5.1). However, note that R and R‚ó¶ have no
good reasons to be isomorphic in general (while every group is isomorphic to its
opposite).
These issues become immaterial if R is commutative: then the identity R ‚Üí R‚ó¶
is an isomorphism, and left-modules/right-modules are identical concepts. The
reader will not miss much by adopting the blanket assumption that all rings men-
tioned in this section are commutative. It is occasionally important to make this
hypothesis explicit (for example in dealing with algebras, cf. Example 5.6), but most
of the material we are going to review works verbatim for, say, left-modules over
an arbitrary ring as for modules over a commutative ring. I will write ‚Äòmodule‚Äô for
‚Äòleft-module‚Äô, for convenience; it will be the reader‚Äôs responsibility to take care of
appropriate changes, if necessary, to adapt the various concepts to right-modules.
158
 III. Rings and modules
5.2. The category R-Mod. The reader should spend some time getting familiar
with the notion of module, by proving simple properties such as
(‚àÄm ‚àà M ) : 0 ¬∑ m = 0,
(‚àÄm ‚àà M ) : (‚àí1) ¬∑ m = ‚àím,
where M is a module over some ring (Exercise 5.3). The following fancier-sounding
(but equally trivial) property is also useful in developing a feel for modules:
Proposition 5.3. Every abelian group is a Z-module, in exactly one way.
Proof. Let G be an abelian group. A Z-module structure on G is a ring homo-
morphism
Z ‚Üí EndAb (G).
Since Z is initial in Ring (¬ß2.1), there exists exactly one such homomorphism, prov-
ing the statement.
 
Thus, ‚Äòabelian group‚Äô and ‚ÄòZ-module‚Äô are one and the same notion. Quite
concretely, the action of n ‚àà Z on an element a of an abelian group simply yields
the ordinary ‚Äòmultiple‚Äô na; this operation is trivially compatible with the operations
in Z.
A homomorphism of R-modules is a homomorphism of (abelian) groups which
is compatible with the module structure. That is, if M , N are R-modules and
œï : M ‚Üí N is a function, then œï is a homomorphism of R-modules if and only if
‚Ä¢ (‚àÄm1 ‚àà M )(‚àÄm2 ‚àà M ) :
 œï(m1 + m2) = œï(m1 ) + œï(m2 );
‚Ä¢ (‚àÄr ‚àà R)(‚àÄm ‚àà M ) :
 œï(rm) = rœï(m).
It is hopefully clear that the composition of two R-module homomorphisms is an
R-module homomorphism and that the identity is an R-module homomorphism:
R-modules form a category
which I will denote24 ‚ÄòR-Mod‚Äô.
Example 5.4. The category Z-Mod of Z-modules is ‚Äòthe same as‚Äô the category Ab:
indeed, every abelian group is a Z-module in exactly one way (Proposition 5.3),
and Z-module homomorphisms are simply homomorphisms of abelian groups. 
Example 5.5. If R = k is a Ô¨Åeld, R-modules are called k-vector spaces. I will
call the category of vector spaces over a Ô¨Åeld k ‚Äòk-Vect‚Äô; this is just another name
for k-Mod. Morphisms in k-Vect are often called25 linear maps. ‚ÄòLinear algebra‚Äô is
the study of k-Vect (extended to R-Mod when possible); Chapters VI and VIII will
be devoted to this subject.
 
24 If R is not commutative, we should agree on whether R-Mod denotes the category of left-
modules or of right-modules. I will mean ‚Äòleft-modules‚Äô.
25 This term is also used for homomorphisms of R modules for more general rings R, but not
as frequently.
5. Modules over a ring
 159
Example 5.6. Any homomorphism of rings Œ± : R ‚Üí S may be used to deÔ¨Åne an
interesting R-module: deÔ¨Åne œÅ : R √ó S ‚Üí S by
œÅ(r, s) := Œ±(r)s
for all r ‚àà R and s ‚àà S. The operation on the right is simply multiplication in S,
and the axioms of DeÔ¨Ånition 5.2 are immediate consequence of the ring axioms and
of the fact that Œ± is a homomorphism. For instance, taking S = R and Œ± = idR
makes R a (left-) module over itself.
It is common to write rs rather than Œ±(r)s.
The ring operation in S and the R-module structure induced by Œ± are linked
even more tightly if we require R to be commutative and Œ± to map R to the center
of S: that is, if we require Œ±(r), s to commute for every r ‚àà R, s ‚àà S. Indeed, in this
case the left-module structure deÔ¨Åned above and the right-module structure deÔ¨Åned
analogously would coincide; further, with these requirements the ring operation in S
(s1 , s2 ) ‚Üí s1s2
is compatible with the R-module structure in the sense that26
(r1s1)(r2 s2 ) = Œ±(r1 )s1Œ±(r 2 )s2 = Œ±(r1 )Œ±(r2 )s1 s2 = (r1 r2)(s1 s2 )
‚àÄr1 , r2 ‚àà R, ‚àÄs1 , s2 ‚àà S: that is, we can ‚Äòmove‚Äô the action of R at will through
products in S.
Due to their importance, these examples deserve their own oÔ¨Écial name:
DeÔ¨Ånition 5.7. Let R be a commutative ring. An R-algebra is a ring homomor-
phism Œ± : R ‚Üí S such that Œ±(R) is contained in the center of S.
 
The usual abuse of language leads us to refer to an R-algebra by the name of
the target S of the homomorphism. Thus, an R-algebra ‚Äòis‚Äô an R-module S with a
compatible ring structure, or, if you prefer, a ring S with a compatible R-module
structure. An R-algebra S is a division algebra if S is a division ring.
There is an evident notion of ‚ÄòR-algebra homomorphism‚Äô (preserving both the
ring and module structure), and we thus get a category R-Alg. The situation
simpliÔ¨Åes substantially if S is itself commutative, in which case the condition on
the center is unnecessary. ‚ÄòCommutative R-algebras‚Äô form a category, which the
attentive reader will recognize as a coslice category (Example I.3.7) in the category
of commutative rings.
Also, note that Z-Alg is just another name for Ring (why?).
The polynomial rings R[x1 , . . . , xn], as well as all their quotients, are commu-
tative R-algebras. This is a particularly important class of examples; for R = k
an algebraically closed Ô¨Åeld, these are the rings used in ‚Äòclassical‚Äô aÔ¨Éne algebraic
geometry (cf. ¬ßVII.2.3).
 
The trivial group 0 has a unique module structure over any ring R and is a zero-
object in R-Mod, that is, it is both initial and Ô¨Ånal. As in the other main categories
we have encountered, a bijective homomorphism of R-modules is automatically an
26More generally, this choice makes the multiplication in S ‚ÄòR-bilinear‚Äô.
160
 III. Rings and modules
isomorphism in R-Mod (Exercise 5.12). In these and many other respects, the
category R-Mod (for any commutative ring R) and Ab are similar.
If R is commutative, the similarity goes further: just as in the category Ab, each
set HomR-Mod(M, N ) may itself be seen as an object of the category (cf. ¬ßII.4.4)27 .
Indeed, let M and N be R-modules. Since homomorphisms of R-modules are in
particular homomorphisms of abelian groups,
HomR-Mod (M, N ) ‚äÜ HomAb (M, N )
as sets (up to natural identiÔ¨Åcations). The operation making HomAb (M, N ) into an
abelian group, as in ¬ßII.4.4, clearly preserves HomR-Mod (M, N ); it follows that the
latter is an abelian group. For r ‚àà R and œï ‚àà HomR-Mod(M, N ), the prescription
(‚àÄm ‚àà M ) :
 (rœï)(m) := r œï(m)
deÔ¨Ånes a function28 rœï : M ‚Üí N . This function is an R-module homomorphism if
R is commutative, because (‚àÄa ‚àà R), (‚àÄm ‚àà M )
!
(rœï)(am) = rœï(am) = (ra)œï(m) = (ar)œï(m) = a(rœï(m)).
Thus, we have a natural action of R on the abelian group HomR-Mod (M, N ), and it
is immediate to verify that this makes HomR-Mod (M, N ) into an R-module.
Watch out: if R is not commutative, then in general HomR-Mod (M, N ) is ‚Äòjust‚Äô
an abelian group. More structure is available if M , N are bimodules; cf. ¬ßVIII.3.2.
5.3. Submodules and quotients. Since R-modules are an ‚Äòenriched‚Äô version of
abelian groups, we can progress through the usual constructions very quickly, by
just pointing out that the analogous constructions in Ab are preserved by the R-
module structure.
A submodule N of an R-module M is a subgroup preserved by the action of R.
That is, for all r ‚àà R and n ‚àà N , the element rn (deÔ¨Åned by the R-module structure
of M ) is in fact in N . Put otherwise, and perhaps more transparently, N is itself
an R-module, and the inclusion N ‚äÜ M is an R-module homomorphism.
Example 5.8. We can view R itself as a (left-)R-module (cf. Example 5.6); the
submodules of R are then precisely the (left-)ideals of R.
 
Example 5.9. Both the kernel and the image of a homomorphism œï : M ‚Üí M 
of R-modules are submodules (of M , M  , respectively).
 
Example 5.10. If r is in the center of R and M is an R-module, then rM =
{rm
 | m ‚àà M } is a submodule of M . If I is any (left-)ideal of R, then IM =
{ i ri mi | ri ‚àà I, mi ‚àà M } is a submodule of M .
If N is a submodule of M , then it is in particular a (normal) subgroup of the
abelian group (M, +); thus we may deÔ¨Åne the quotient M/N as an abelian group.

Of course it would be desirable to see this as a module, and as usual there is only

œÄ : M ‚Üí M/N
to be an R-module homomorphism, and this forces

for all m ‚àà M . That is, we are led to deÔ¨Åne the action of R on M/N by

Claim 5.11. For all submodules N , this prescription does deÔ¨Åne a structure of
R-module on M/N .
The proof of this claim is immediate and is left to the reader. The R-module
M/N is (of course) called the quotient of M by N .
Example 5.12. If R is a ring and I is a two-sided ideal of R, then all three of I,
R, and the quotient ring R/I are R-modules: I is a submodule of R, and the rings
R and R/I are in fact R-algebras if R is commutative (cf. Example 5.6).
 
Example 5.13. If R is not commutative and I is just a (say) left-ideal, then
the quotient R/I is not deÔ¨Åned as a ring, but it is deÔ¨Åned as a left-module (the
quotient of the module R by the submodule I). The action of R on R/I is given
by left-multiplication: r(a + I) = ra + I.

6. Products, coproducts, etc., in R-Mod
I have stated several times that categories such as R-Mod are ‚Äòwell-behaved‚Äô. We
will explore in this section the sense in which this can be formalized at this stage.
The bottom line is that these categories enjoy the same nice properties that we
have noted along the way for the category Ab.
I will also include here some general considerations on Ô¨Ånitely generated mod-
ules and algebras.
As in the previous section, I will write ‚Äòmodule‚Äô for ‚Äòleft-module‚Äô; the reader
should make appropriate adaptations to the case of right-modules. Little will be
lost by assuming that all rings appearing here are commutative (thereby removing
the distinction between left- and right-modules).
6.1. Products and coproducts. As in Ab, products and coproducts exist, and
Ô¨Ånite products and coproducts coincide, in R-Mod. Indeed, recall the construction
of the direct sum of two abelian groups (¬ßII.3.5): if M and N are abelian groups,
then M ‚äï N denotes their product, with componentwise operation. If M and N are
R-modules, we can give an R-module structure to M ‚äï N by prescribing ‚àÄr ‚àà R
r(m, n) := (rm, rn).
This deÔ¨Ånes the direct sum of M , N , as an R-module. Note that M ‚äï N comes
together with several homomorphisms of R-modules:
œÄM : M ‚äï N ‚Üí M,
 œÄN : M ‚äï N ‚Üí N
6. Products, coproducts, etc., in R-Mod

iM : M ‚Üí M ‚äï N,

sending m to (m, 0) and n to (0, n).

the product and the coproduct of M and N .

Since it is the unique R-module homomorphism making the diagram commute, this

The reader should also work out the Ô¨Åbered versions of these constructions;
cf. Exercises 6.10 and 6.11.
The fact that Ô¨Ånite products and coproducts agree in R-Mod does not extend
to the inÔ¨Ånite case (Exercise 6.7).
6.2. Kernels and cokernels. The facts that R-Mod has a zero-object (the 0-
module), its Hom sets are abelian groups (¬ß5.2), and it has (Ô¨Ånite) products and
coproducts make R-Mod an additive category. The fact that R-Mod has well-
behaved kernels and cokernels, which we review next, upgrades it to the status of
abelian category. (We will come back to these general deÔ¨Ånitions in ¬ßIX.1.)
In general, monomorphisms and epimorphisms do not automatically satisfy
good properties, even when objects of a given category are realized by adding
structure to sets. For example, we have seen that the precise relationship between
‚Äòsurjective morphism‚Äô and ‚Äòepimorphism‚Äô may be rather subtle: epimorphisms are
surjective in Grp, but for complicated reasons (¬ßII.8.6); and there are epimorphisms
that are not surjective in Ring (¬ß2.3).
The situation in R-Mod is as simple as it can be. Recall that we have identiÔ¨Åed
universal properties for kernels and cokernels (cf. ¬ßII.8.6); in the category R-Mod
these would go as follows: if

Proposition 6.2. The following hold in R-Mod:
‚Ä¢ kernels and cokernels exist;
6. Products, coproducts, etc., in R-Mod
 167
‚Ä¢ œï is a monomorphism ‚áê‚áí ker œï is trivial ‚áê‚áí œï is injective as a set-
function;
‚Ä¢ œï is an epimorphism ‚áê‚áí coker œï is trivial ‚áê‚áí œï is surjective as a set-
function.
Further, every monomorphism identiÔ¨Åes its source with the kernel of some mor-
phism, and every epimorphism identiÔ¨Åes its target with the cokernel of some mor-
phism.
This proposition of course simply generalizes to R-Mod facts we know already
from our study of Ab, and a quick review should suÔ¨Éce for the careful reader. Ker-
nels exist: indeed, the ‚Äòstandard‚Äô deÔ¨Ånition of kernel satisÔ¨Åes the universal proper-

N/im œï does satisfy the universal property for cokernels.
The proofs of all the implications in the second and third points in Proposi-
tion 6.2 follow familiar patterns from (for example) Proposition II.6.12 and Propo-
sition II.8.18. The last sentence of Proposition 6.2 simply reiterates the slogan
submodule ‚áê‚áí kernel and its mirror statement (which is just as true). Further
details are left to the reader.
By the way, whatever happened to the conditions characterizing monomor-
phisms and epimorphisms in Set (Proposition I.2.1)? In Set, a function with non-
empty source is a monomorphism if and only if it has a left-inverse, and it is an
epimorphism if and only if it has a right-inverse. We have learned not to expect any
of this to happen in more general categories. Modules are not an exception: the
function Z ‚Üí Z deÔ¨Åned by ‚Äòmultiplication by 2‚Äô is a monomorphism without a left-
inverse, and the projection Z ‚Üí Z/2Z is an epimorphism without a right-inverse.
We will come back to this point in ¬ß7.2.
6.3. Free modules and free algebras. The universal property of free R-modules
is modeled after the properties deÔ¨Åning the other free objects we have encountered:
the goal is to deÔ¨Åne the R-module containing a given set A ‚Äòin the most eÔ¨Écient
way‚Äô. Again, the situation will match the case of abelian groups closely, so the
reader may want to refer back to ¬ßII.5.4.

It is not diÔ¨Écult to identify free objects in the larger category R-Alg: they
consist of ‚Äònoncommutative polynomial rings‚Äô RA, with variables from the set A
but without any condition relating ab and ba for a = b in A. More precisely, RA
is isomorphic to the monoid ring (cf. ¬ß1.4) over the free monoid on A, consisting of
all Ô¨Ånite strings of elements in A, with operation deÔ¨Åned by concatenation30 . We
will encounter this ring again, but only in the distant future (Example VIII.4.17).

Finitely generated R-modules are tremendously important, but they are not as
well-behaved as one might hope at Ô¨Årst. For example, it may be that a module M
is Ô¨Ånitely generated, but some submodule of M is not Ô¨Ånitely generated!

(x 1 , x2 , . . . )

DeÔ¨Ånition 6.6. An R-module M is Noetherian if every submodule of M is Ô¨Ånitely
generated as an R-module.
 
Thus, a ring R is Noetherian in the sense of DeÔ¨Ånition 4.2 if and only if it is
Noetherian ‚Äòas a module over itself‚Äô. The ring in Example 6.5 is not Noetherian.
We will study the Noetherian condition more carefully later on (¬ßV.1.1); but
we can already see one reason why this is a good, ‚Äòsolid‚Äô notion.
Proposition 6.7. Let M be an R-module, and let N be a submodule of M . Then
M is Noetherian if and only if both N and M/N are Noetherian.
Proof. If M is Noetherian, then so is M/N (same proof as for Exercise 4.2), and so
is N (because every submodule of N is a submodule of M , so it is Ô¨Ånitely generated
because M is Noetherian). This proves the ‚Äòonly if‚Äô part of the statement.
For the converse, assume N and M/N are Noetherian, and let P be a submodule
of M ; we have to prove that P is Ô¨Ånitely generated. Since P ‚à© N is a submodule
of N and N is Noetherian, P ‚à© N is Ô¨Ånitely generated. By the ‚Äòsecond isomorphism
theorem‚Äô, Proposition 5.18,
