and hence P/(P ‚à© N ) is isomorphic to a submodule of M/N . Since M/N is Noe-
therian, this shows that P/(P ‚à© N ) is Ô¨Ånitely generated.
It follows that P itself is Ô¨Ånitely generated, by Exercise 6.18.

Corollary 6.8. Let R be a Noetherian ring, and let M be a Ô¨Ånitely generated
R-module. Then M is Noetherian (as an R-module).
Proof. Indeed, by hypothesis there is an onto homomorphism R‚äïn  M of R-
modules; hence (by the Ô¨Årst isomorphism theorem, Corollary 5.16) M is isomorphic
to a quotient of R‚äïn . By Proposition 6.7, it suÔ¨Éces to prove that R‚äïn is Noetherian.
This may be done by induction. The statement is true for n = 1 by hypothesis.
For n > 1, assume we know that R‚äï(n‚àí1) is Noetherian; since R‚äï(n‚àí1) may be
viewed as a submodule of R‚äïn , in such a way that

6.5. Finitely generated vs. Ô¨Ånite type. If S is an R-algebra, it may be ‚ÄòÔ¨Ånitely
generated‚Äô in two very diÔ¨Äerent ways: as an R-module and as an R-algebra. It is
important to keep these two concepts well distinct, although unfortunately the
language used to express them is very similar.
The following deÔ¨Ånitions diÔ¨Äer in three small details. . .
‚ÄúS is Ô¨Ånitely generated as a module
over R if there is an onto homomor-
phism of R-modules from the free R-
module on a Ô¨Ånite set to S.‚Äù
‚ÄúS is Ô¨Ånitely generated as an algebra
over R if there is an onto homomor-
phism of R-algebras from the free R-
algebra on a Ô¨Ånite set to S.‚Äù
The mathematical diÔ¨Äerence is more substantial than it may appear. As we
have seen in ¬ß6.3, the free R-module over a Ô¨Ånite set A = {1, . . . , n} is isomorphic
‚äïnto R ; the free commutative R-algebra over A is isomorphic to R[x1 , . . . , xn ].
Thus, a commutative31 ring S is Ô¨Ånitely generated as an R-module if there is an
onto homomorphism of R-modules
R‚äïn  S
for some n; it is Ô¨Ånitely generated as an R-algebra if there is an onto homomorphism
of R-algebras
R[x1 , . . . , xn]  S
for some n. In other words, S is Ô¨Ånitely generated as an R module if and only if
S ‚àº
 = R‚äïn/M for some n and a submodule M of R‚äïn ; it is a Ô¨Ånite-type R-algebra
if and only if S ‚àº
 = R[x1 , . . . , xn ]/I for some n and an ideal I of R[x1 , . . . , xn].
We say that S is Ô¨Ånite in the Ô¨Årst case32 and of Ô¨Ånite type in the second. It
is clear that ‚ÄòÔ¨Ånite‚Äô =‚áí ‚ÄòÔ¨Ånite type‚Äô; it should be just as clear that the converse
does not hold.
Example 6.9. The polynomial ring R[x] is a Ô¨Ånite-type R-algebra, but it is not
Ô¨Ånite as an R-module.
 
The distinction, while macroscopic in general, may evaporate in special, impor-
tant cases. For example, one can prove that if k and K are Ô¨Åelds and k ‚äÜ K, then
K is of Ô¨Ånite type over k if and only if it is in fact Ô¨Ånite as a k-module (that is, it is
a Ô¨Ånite-dimensional k-vector space). This is one version of Hilbert‚Äôs Nullstellensatz,
a deep result we already mentioned in Example 4.15 and that we will prove (in an
important class of examples) in ¬ßVII.2.2.
David Hilbert‚Äôs name is associated to another important result concerning
Ô¨Ånite-type R-algebras: if R is Noetherian (as a ring, that is, as an R-module)
31We are mostly interested in the commutative case, so I will make this hypothesis here;
the only change in the general case is typographical: ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ rather than [¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ]. Also, note that a
commutative ring is Ô¨Ånitely generated as an algebra if and only if it is Ô¨Ånitely generated as a
commutative algebra; cf. Exercise 6.15.
32This is particularly unfortunate, since S may very well be an inÔ¨Ånite set.

The proof of Hilbert‚Äôs basis theorem is completely elementary: it could be given

7. Complexes and homology
In many contexts, modules arise not ‚Äòone at a time‚Äô but in whole series: for example,
a real manifold of dimension d has one ‚Äòhomology‚Äô group for each dimension from 0
to d. It is necessary to develop a language capable of dealing with whole sequences
of modules at once. This is the language of homological algebra, of which we will
get a tiny taste in this section, and a slightly heartier course in Chapter IX.
7.1. Complexes and exact sequences. A chain complex of R-modules (or, for
simplicity, a complex ) is a sequence of R-modules and R-module homomorphisms

 di
 / Mi‚àí1 di‚àí1 / ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑
such that (‚àÄi) : di ‚ó¶ di+1 = 0.
The notation (M‚Ä¢ , d‚Ä¢ ) may be used to denote a complex, or simply M‚Ä¢ for
simplicity (but do not forget that the homomorphisms di are part of the information
carried by a complex).
A complex may be inÔ¨Ånite in both directions; ‚Äòtails‚Äô of 0‚Äôs are (usually) omitted.
Several possible alternative conventions may be used: for example, indices may be
increasing rather than decreasing, giving a cochain complex (whose homology is
called cohomology; this will be our choice in Chapter IX). Such choices are clearly
mathematically immaterial, at least for the simple considerations which follow.
The homomorphisms di are called boundary, or diÔ¨Äerentials, due to important
examples from geometry. Note that the deÔ¨Åning condition
di ‚ó¶ di+1 = 0
is equivalent to the requirement
im di+1 ‚äÜ ker di .
I carry in my mind an image such as
7. Complexes and homology
 175
when I think of a complex. The ovals are the modules Mi ; the fat black dots are the
0 elements; the gray ovals, getting squashed to zero at each step, are the kernels;
and I thus visualize the fact that the image of ‚Äòthe preceding homomorphism‚Äô falls
inside the kernel of ‚Äòthe next homomorphism‚Äô.
The picture is inaccurate in that it hints that the ‚ÄòdiÔ¨Äerence‚Äô between the image
of di+1 and the kernel of di (that is, the areas colored in a lighter shade of gray)
should be the same for all i; this is of course not the case in general. In fact, almost
the whole point about complexes is to ‚Äòmeasure‚Äô this diÔ¨Äerence, which is called the
homology of the complex (cf. ¬ß7.3). We say that a complex is exact ‚Äòat Mi ‚Äô if it has
no homology there; that is,
im di+1 = ker di .
Visually,
This complex appears to be exact at the oval in the middle.
For example, if Mi = a trivial module (usually denoted simply by 0), then the
complex is necessarily exact at Mi , since then im di+1 = ker di = 0.
A complex is exact and is often called an exact sequence if it is exact at all its
modules.
Example 7.1. A complex
¬∑¬∑¬∑
 / 0
 / L
 Œ±
 / M
 / ¬∑¬∑¬∑
is exact at L if and only if Œ± is a monomorphism.
Indeed, exactness at L is equivalent to ker Œ± = image of the trivial homomor-
phism 0 ‚Üí L, that is, to
ker Œ± = 0.
This is equivalent to the injectivity of Œ± (Proposition 6.2).

Example 7.2. A complex

is exact at N if and only if Œ≤ is an epimorphism.
Indeed, the complex is exact at N if and only if im Œ≤ = kernel of the trivial
homomorphism N ‚Üí 0, that is, im Œ≤ = N .

As seen in the previous two examples, exactness at L and N is equivalent to Œ±

im Œ± = ker Œ≤;

(via the isomorphism induced by the surjective map Œ≤, under the auspices of the
Ô¨Årst isomorphism theorem).

Splitting sequences give us the opportunity to go back to a question we left
dangling at the end of ¬ß6.2: what should we make of the condition of ‚Äòhaving a
left- (resp., right-) inverse‚Äô for a homomorphism? We realized that this condition is
stronger than the requirement of being a monomorphisms (resp., an epimorphism);
can we give a more explicit description of such morphisms?

Because of Proposition 7.5, R-module homomorphisms with a left-inverse are
called split monomorphisms, and homomorphisms with a right-inverse are called
split epimorphisms.
We will come back to split exact sequences (in the more demanding context
of Grp) in ¬ßIV.5.2 and then later again when we return to modules and, more
generally, to abelian categories (Chapters VIII and IX).

I will end this very brief excursion into more abstract territories by indicating
how a commutative diagram involving two short exact sequences generates a ‚Äòlong
exact sequence‚Äô in homology. This is actually a particular case of a more general
construction‚Äîaccording to which a suitable commutative diagram involving three
complexes yields a really long ‚Äòlong exact homology sequence‚Äô. We will come back to
this general construction when we deal more extensively with homological algebra
in Chapter IX. The reader is also likely to learn about it in a course on algebraic
topology, where this fact is put to impressive use in studying invariants of manifolds.
In the simple form we will analyze, this is aÔ¨Äectionately known as the snake
lemma. Consider two short exact sequences linked by homomorphisms, so as to
form a commutative diagram34 :

Proving the snake lemma is something that should not be done in public, and

executed by pointing several Ô¨Ångers at diÔ¨Äerent parts of a diagram on a blackboard,
while enunciating the elements one is manipulating and stating their fate35 .

The rest of the proof (left to the reader(!) but I am not listing this as an

Dear reader: don‚Äôt shy away from trying this, for it is excellent, indispens-
able practice. Miss this opportunity and you will forever feel unsure about such
manipulations.
The snake lemma streamlines several facts, which would not be hard to prove
individually, but become really straightforward once the lemma is settled. For
example,
Corollary 7.12. In the same situation presented in the snake lemma (notation as
in ¬ß7.3), assume that Œº is surjective and ŒΩ is injective. Then Œª is surjective and ŒΩ
is an isomorphism.

Groups, second encounter
Chapter IV
In this chapter we return to Grp and study several topics of a less ‚Äògeneral‚Äô nature
than those considered in Chapter II. Most of what we do here will apply exclusively
to Ô¨Ånite groups; this is an important example in its own right, as it has spectacular
applications (for example, in Galois theory; cf. ¬ßVII.7), and it is a good subject
from the expository point of view, since it gives us the opportunity to see several
general concepts at work in a context that is complex enough to carry substance,
but simple enough (in this tiny selection of elementary topics) to be appreciated
easily.
1. The conjugation action
1.1. Actions of groups on sets, reminder. Groups really shine when you let
them act on something. This section will make this point very eÔ¨Äectively, since
we will get surprisingly precise results on Ô¨Ånite groups by extremely simple-minded
applications of the elementary facts concerning group actions that we established
back in ¬ßII.9.
Recall that we proved (Proposition II.9.9) that every transitive (left-) action of
a group G on a set S is, up to a natural notion of isomorphism, ‚Äòleft-multiplication
on the set of left-cosets G/H‚Äô. Here, H may be taken to be the stabilizer Stab G(a)
of any element a ‚àà S, that is (DeÔ¨Ånition II.9.8) the subgroup of G Ô¨Åxing a. This
fact applies to the orbits of every left-action of G on a set; in particular, the number
of elements in a Ô¨Ånite orbit O equals the index of the stabilizer of any a ‚àà O; in
particular (Corollary II.9.10) the number of elements |O| of an orbit must divide
the order |G| of G, if G is Ô¨Ånite.
These considerations may be packaged into a useful ‚Äòcounting‚Äô formula, which
we could call the class formula for that action; this name is usually reserved to the
particular case of the action of G onto itself by conjugation, which we will explore
more carefully below.
187
188
 IV. Groups, second encounter
In order to state the formula, assume G acts on a set S; for a ‚àà S, let Ga
denote the stabilizer StabG (a). Also, let Z be the set of Ô¨Åxed points of the action:
Z = {a ‚àà S | (‚àÄg ‚àà G) : ga = a}.
Note that a ‚àà Z ‚áê‚áí Ga = G; we could say that a ‚àà Z if and only if the orbit
of a is ‚Äòtrivial‚Äô, in the sense that it consists of a alone.
Proposition 1.1. Let S be a Ô¨Ånite set, and let G be a group acting on S. With
notation as above,

|S| = |Z| +
 [G : Ga],
a‚ààA
where A ‚äÜ S has exactly one element for each nontrivial orbit of the action.
Proof. The orbits form a partition of S, and Z collects the trivial orbits; hence

|S| = |Z| +
 |Oa |,
a‚ààA
where Oa denotes the orbit of a. By Proposition II.9.9, the order |Oa | equals the
index of the stabilizer of a, yielding the statement.
 
The main strength of Proposition 1.1 rests in the fact that, if G is Ô¨Ånite, each
summand [G : Ga ] divides the order of G (and is > 1). This can be a strong
constraint, when some information is known about |G|. For example, let‚Äôs see what
this says when G is a p-group:
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.2. A p-group is a Ô¨Ånite group whose order is a power of a prime
integer p.
 
Corollary 1.3. Let G be a p-group acting on a Ô¨Ånite set S, and let Z be the Ô¨Åxed
point set of the action. Then
|Z| ‚â° |S| mod p.
Proof. Indeed, each summand [G : Ga ] in Proposition 1.1 is a number larger
than 1, and a power of p; hence it is 0 mod p.
 
For instance, in certain situations this can be used to establish1 that Z = ‚àÖ: see
Exercise 1.1. Such immediate consequences of Proposition 1.1 will assist us below,
in the proof of Sylow‚Äôs theorems.
1 In this sense, Proposition 1.1 is an instance of a class of results known as ‚ÄòÔ¨Åxed point
theorems‚Äô. The reader will likely encounter a few such theorems in topology courses, where the
role of the ‚Äòsize‚Äô of a set may be played by (for example) the Euler characteristic of a topological
space.
1. The conjugation action
 189
1.2. Center, centralizer, conjugacy classes. Recall (Example II.9.3) that ev-
ery group G acts on itself in at least two interesting ways: by (left-) multiplication
and by conjugation. The latter action is deÔ¨Åned by the following œÅ : G √ó G ‚Üí G:
œÅ(g, a) = gag ‚àí1.
As we know (¬ßII.9.2), this datum is equivalent to the datum of a certain group
homomorphism:
œÉ : G ‚Üí SG
from G to the permutation group on G.
This action highlights several interesting objects:
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.4. The center of G, denoted Z(G), is the subgroup ker œÉ of G.
 
Concretely, the center2 of G is
Z(G) = {g ‚àà G | (‚àÄa ‚àà G) : ga = ag}.
Indeed, œÉ(g) is the identity in S G if and only if œÉ(g) acts as the identity on G; that
is, if and only if gag‚àí1 = a for all a ‚àà G; that is, if and only if g commutes with all
elements of G. In other words, the center is the set of Ô¨Åxed points in G under the
conjugation action.
Note that the center of a group G is automatically normal in G: this is nearly
immediate to check ‚Äòby hand‚Äô, but there is no need to do so since it is a kernel by
deÔ¨Ånition and kernels are normal.
A group G is commutative if and only if Z(G) = G, that is, if and only if the
conjugation action is trivial on G.
In general, feel happy when you discover that the center of a group is not trivial:
this will often allow you to set up proofs by induction on the number of elements
of the group, by mod-ing out by the center (this is, roughly, how we will prove the
Ô¨Årst Sylow theorem). Or note the following useful fact, which comes in handy when
trying to prove that a group is commutative:
Lemma 1.5. Let G be a Ô¨Ånite group, and assume G/Z(G) is cyclic. Then G is
commutative (and hence G/Z(G) is in fact trivial).
Proof. (Cf. Exercise 1.5.) As G/Z(G) is cyclic, there exists an element g ‚àà G such
that the class gZ(G) generates G/Z(G). Then ‚àÄa ‚àà G
aZ(G) = (gZ(G))r
for some r ‚àà Z; that is, there is an element z ‚àà Z(G) of the center such that
a = g r z.
If now a, b are in G, use this fact to write
a = g r z,
 b = g s w
for some s ‚àà Z and w ‚àà Z(G); but then
ab = (g r z)(g sw) = g r+szw = (g s w)(g r z) = ba,
2 Why ‚ÄòZ‚Äô ? ‚ÄòCenter‚Äô
 iƒÜ Zentrum auf Deutsƒö.
190
 IV. Groups, second encounter
where I have used the fact that z and w commute with every element of G. As a
and b were arbitrary, this proves that G is commutative.
 
Next, the stabilizer of a ‚àà G under conjugation has a special name:
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.6. The centralizer (or normalizer ) ZG (a) of a ‚àà G is its stabilizer
under conjugation.
 
Thus,
ZG (a) = {g ‚àà G | gag‚àí1 = a} = {g ‚àà G | ga = ag}
consists of those elements in G which commute with a. In particular, Z(G) ‚äÜ ZG(a)

for all a ‚àà G; in fact, Z(G) = a‚ààG ZG (a). Clearly a ‚àà Z(G) ‚áê‚áí ZG(a) = G.
If there is no ambiguity concerning the group G containing a, the index G may
be dropped.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.7. The conjugacy class of a ‚àà G is the orbit [a] of a under the
conjugation action. Two elements a, b of G are conjugate if they belong to the
same conjugacy class.
 
The notation [a] is not standard; C(a) is used more frequently, but I am not
fond of it. Using [a] reminds us that these are nothing but the equivalence classes
of elements of G under a certain interesting equivalence relation.
Note that [a] = {a} if and only if gag ‚àí1 = a for all g ‚àà G; that is, if and only
if ga = ag for all g ‚àà G; that is, if and only if a ‚àà Z(G).
1.3. The Class Formula. The ‚ÄòoÔ¨Écial‚Äô Class Formula for a Ô¨Ånite group G is the
particular case of Proposition 1.1 for the conjugation action.
Proposition 1.8 (Class formula). Let G be a Ô¨Ånite group. Then

|G| = |Z(G)| +
 [G : Z(a)],
a‚ààA
where A ‚äÜ G is a set containing one representative for each nontrivial conjugacy
class in G.
Proof. The set of Ô¨Åxed points is Z(G), and the stabilizer of a is the centralizer Z(a);
apply Proposition 1.1.
 
The class formula is surprisingly useful. In applying it, keep in mind that every
summand on the right (that is, both |Z(G)| and each [G : Z(a)]) is a divisor of |G|;
this fact alone often suÔ¨Éces to draw striking conclusions about G.
Possibly the most famous such application is to p-groups, via Corollary 1.3:
Corollary 1.9. Let G be a nontrivial p-group. Then G has a nontrivial center.
Proof. Since |Z(G)| ‚â° |G| mod p and |G| > 1 is a power of p, necessarily |Z(G)| is
a multiple of p. As Z(G) = ‚àÖ (since eG ‚àà Z(G)), this implies |Z(G)| ‚â• p.
 
1. The conjugation action
 191
For example, it follows immediately (from Corollary 1.9 and Lemma 1.5; cf. Ex-
ercise 1.6) that if p is prime, then every group of order p2 is commutative.
In general, the class formula poses a strong constraint on what can go on in a
group.
Example 1.10. Consider a group G of order 6; what are the possibilities for its
class formula?
If G is commutative, then the class formula will tell us very little:
6 = 6.
If G is not commutative, then its center must be trivial (as a consequence of La-
grange‚Äôs theorem and Lemma 1.5); so the class formula is 6 = 1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ , where ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑
collects the sizes of the nontrivial conjugacy classes. But each of these summands
must be larger than 1, smaller than 6, and must divide 6; that is, there are no
choices:
6=1+2+3
is the only possibility. The reader should check that this is indeed the class formula
for S3 ; in fact, S3 is the only noncommutative group of order 6 up to isomorphism
(Exercise 1.13).
 
Another useful observation is that normal subgroups must be unions of conju-
gacy classes: because if H is a normal subgroup, a ‚àà H, and b = gag‚àí1 is conjugate
to a, then
b ‚àà gHg‚àí1 = H.
To stick with the |G| = 6 example, note that every subgroup of a group must
contain the identity and its size must divide the order of the group; it follows that
a normal subgroup of a noncommutative group of order 6 cannot have order 2, since
2 cannot be written as sums of orders of conjugacy classes (including the class of
the identity).
1.4. Conjugation of subsets and subgroups. We may also act by conjugation
on the power set of G: if A ‚äÜ G is a subset and g ‚àà G, the conjugate of A is
the subset gAg ‚àí1 . By cancellation, the conjugation map a ‚Üí gag‚àí1 is a bijection
between A and gAg ‚àí1.
This leads to terminology analogous to the one introduced in ¬ß1.2.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.11. The normalizer NG (A) of A is its stabilizer under conjugation.
The centralizer of A is the subgroup ZG (A) ‚äÜ NG (A) Ô¨Åxing each element of A. 
Thus, g ‚àà NG (A) if and only if3 gAg‚àí1 = A, and g ‚àà ZG(A) if and only if
‚àÄa ‚àà A, gag ‚àí1 = a.
For A = {a} a singleton, we have NG ({a}) = ZG ({a}) = ZG (a). In general,
ZG (A)  NG (A).
If H is a subgroup of G, every conjugate gHg‚àí1 of H is also a subgroup of G;
conjugate subgroups have the same order.
3 If A is Ô¨Ånite (but not in general), this condition is equivalent to gAg‚àí1 ‚äÜ A.

Remark 1.12. The deÔ¨Ånition implies immediately that H ‚äÜ NG (H) and that H
is normal in G if and only if NG (H) = G. More generally, the normalizer NG (H)
of H in G is (clearly) the largest subgroup of G in which H is normal.
 
One could apply Proposition 1.1 to the conjugation action on subsets or sub-
group; however, there are too many subsets, and one has little control over the
number of subgroups. Other numerical considerations involving the number of
conjugates of a given subset or subgroups may be very useful.
Lemma 1.13. Let H ‚äÜ G be a subgroup. Then (if Ô¨Ånite) the number of subgroups
conjugate to H equals the index [G : NG (H)] of the normalizer of H in G.
Proof. This is again an immediate consequence of Proposition II.9.9.
 
Corollary 1.14. If [G : H] is Ô¨Ånite, then the number of subgroups conjugate to H
is Ô¨Ånite and divides [G : H].
Proof.
(cf. ¬ßII.8.5).
[G : H] = [G : NG (H)] ¬∑ [NG (H) : H]

One of the celebrated Sylow theorems will strengthen this statement substan-
tially in the case in which H is a maximal p-group contained in a Ô¨Ånite group G.
For a statement concerning the size of the normalizer of an arbitrary p-subgroup of
a group, see Lemma 2.9.
Another useful numerical tool is the observation that if H and K are subgroups
of a group G and H ‚äÜ NG (K)‚Äîso that gKg ‚àí1 = K for all g ‚àà H‚Äîthen conjuga-
tion by g ‚àà H gives an automorphism of K. Indeed, I have already observed that
conjugation is a bijection, and it is immediate to see that it is a homomorphism:
‚àÄk 1 , k2 ‚àà K
(gk1 g ‚àí1 )(gk2 g‚àí1 ) = gk 1 (g ‚àí1 g)k2g ‚àí1 = g(k1k2 )g ‚àí1 .
Thus, conjugation gives a set-function
Œ≥ : H ‚Üí AutGrp (K).
The reader will check that this is a group homomorphism and will determine ker Œ≥
(Exercise 1.21).
This is especially useful if H is Ô¨Ånite and some information is available con-
cerning AutGrp (K) (for an example, see Exercise 4.14). A classic application is
presented in Exercise 1.22.

2. The Sylow theorems
2.1. Cauchy‚Äôs theorem. The ‚ÄòSylow theorems‚Äô consist of three statements con-
cerning p-subgroups (cf. DeÔ¨Ånition 1.2) of a given Ô¨Ånite group G. The form I will
2. The Sylow theorems
 195
give for the Ô¨Årst of these statements will tell us that G contains p-groups of all sizes
allowed by Lagrange‚Äôs theorem: if p is a prime and pk divides |G|, then G contains
a subgroup of order pk . The proof of this statement is an easy induction, provided
the statement for k = 1 is known: that is, provided that one has established
Theorem 2.1 (Cauchy‚Äôs theorem). Let G be a Ô¨Ånite group, and let p be a prime
divisor of |G|. Then G contains an element of order p.
As it happens, only the abelian version of this statement is needed for the proof
of the Ô¨Årst Sylow theorem; then the full statement of Cauchy‚Äôs theorem follows
from the Ô¨Årst Sylow theorem itself. Since the (diligent) reader has already proved
Cauchy‚Äôs theorem for abelian groups (in Exercise II.8.17), we could directly move
on to Sylow theorems.
However, there is a quick proof4 of the full statement of Cauchy‚Äôs theorem
which does not rely on Sylow and is a good illustration of the power of the general
‚Äòclass formula for arbitrary actions‚Äô (Proposition 1.1). I will present this proof,
while also encouraging the reader to go back and (re)do Exercise II.8.17 now.
Proof of Theorem 2.1. Consider the set S of ordered p-tuples of elements of G:
(a1 , . . . , ap )
such that a1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ap = e. I claim that |S| = |G|p‚àí1 : indeed, once a1, . . . , ap‚àí1 are
chosen (arbitrarily), then ap is determined as it is the inverse of a1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ a p‚àí1 .
Therefore, p divides the order of S as it divides the order of G.
Also note that if a1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ap = e, then
a2 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ apa1 = e
(even if G is not commutative): because if a1 is a left-inverse to a2 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ap, then it is
also a right-inverse to it.
Therefore, we may act with the group Z/pZ on S: given [m] in Z/pZ, with
0 ‚â§ m < p, act by [m] on
(a1 , . . . , ap )
by sending it to
(am+1 , . . . , ap , a1 , . . . , am) :
as we just observed, this is still an element of S.
Now Corollary 1.3 implies
|Z| ‚â° |S| ‚â° 0 mod p,
where Z is the set of Ô¨Åxed points of this action. Fixed points are p-tuples of the
form
(*)
 (a, . . . , a);
and note that Z = ‚àÖ, since {e, . . . , e} ‚àà Z. Since p ‚â• 2 and p divides |Z|, we
conclude that |Z| > 1; therefore there exists some element in Z of the form (*),
with a = e.

 IV. Groups, second encounter
This says that there exists an a ‚àà G, a = e, such that ap = e, proving the
statement.
 
We should remark that the proof given here proves a more precise result than
the raw statement of Theorem 2.1: every element of order p in G generates a cyclic
subgroup of G of order p, and we are able to say something about the number of
such subgroups.
Claim 2.2. Let G be a Ô¨Ånite group, let p be a prime divisor of |G|, and let N be
the number of cyclic subgroups of G of order p. Then N ‚â° 1 mod p.
The proof of this fact is left to the reader (as an incentive to really understand
the proof of Theorem 2.1).
Claim 2.2, coupled with the simple observation that if there is only 1 cyclic
subgroup H of order p, then that subgroup must be normal (Exercise 2.2), suÔ¨Éces
for interesting applications.
DeÔ¨Ånition 2.3. A group G is simple if it is nontrivial and its only normal sub-
groups are {e} and G itself.
 
Simple groups occupy a special place in the theory of groups: one can ‚Äòbreak
up‚Äô any Ô¨Ånite group into basic constituents which are simple groups; we will see
how this is done in ¬ß3.1. Thus, it is important to be able to tell whether a group is
simple or not5.
Example 2.4. Let p be a positive prime integer. If |G| = mp, with 1 < m < p,
then G is not simple.
Indeed, consider the subgroups of G with p elements. By Claim 2.2, the number
of such subgroups is ‚â° 1 mod p. Thus, if there is more than one such subgroup,
then there must be at least p + 1. Any two distinct subgroups of prime order can
only meet at the identity (why?); therefore this would account for at least
1 + (p + 1)(p ‚àí 1) = p2
elements in G. Since |G| = mp < p 2 , this is impossible. Therefore there is only
one cyclic subgroup of order p in G, which must be normal as mentioned above,
proving that G is not simple.
 
2.2. Sylow I. Let p be a prime integer. A p-Sylow subgroup of a Ô¨Ånite group G
is a subgroup of order pr , where |G| = pr m and gcd(p, m) = 1. That is, P ‚äÜ G is
a p-Sylow subgroup if it is a p-group and p does not divide [G : P ].
If p does not divide the order of G, then G contains a p-Sylow subgroup: namely,
{e}. This is not very interesting; what is interesting is that G contains a p-Sylow
subgroup even when p does divide the order of G:
Theorem 2.5 (First Sylow theorem). Every Ô¨Ånite group contains a p-Sylow sub-
group, for all primes p.
5 In fact, a complete list of all Ô¨Ånite simple groups is known: this is the classiÔ¨Åcation result
mentioned at the end of ¬ßII.6.3, arguably one of the deepest and hardest results in mathematics.
2. The Sylow theorems
 197
The Ô¨Årst Sylow theorem follows from the seemingly stronger statement:
Proposition 2.6. If pk divides the order of G, then G has a subgroup of order pk .
The statements are actually easily seen to be equivalent, by Exercise 1.8; in
any case, the standard argument proving Theorem 2.5 proves Proposition 2.6, and
I see no reason to hide this fact. Here is the argument:
Proof of Proposition 2.6. If k = 0, there is nothing to prove, so we may assume
k ‚â• 1 and in particular that |G| is a multiple of p.
Argue by induction on |G|: if |G| = p, again there is nothing to prove; if |G| > p
and G contains a proper subgroup H such that [G : H] is relatively prime to p,
then pk divides the order of H, and hence H contains a subgroup of order pk by
the induction hypothesis, and thus so does G.
Therefore, we may assume that all proper subgroups of G have index divisible
by p. By the class formula (Proposition 1.8), p divides the order of the center Z(G).
By Cauchy‚Äôs theorem6 , ‚àÉa ‚àà Z(G) such that a has order p. The cyclic subgroup
N = a is contained in Z(G), and hence it is normal in G (Exercise 1.4). Therefore
we can consider the quotient G/N .
Since |G/N | = |G|/p and pk divides |G| by hypothesis, we have that pk‚àí1
divides the order of G/N . By the induction hypothesis, we may conclude that
G/N contains a subgroup of order pk‚àí1 . By the structure of the subgroups of
a quotient (¬ßII.8.3, especially Proposition II.8.9), this subgroup must be of the
form P/N , for P a subgroup of G.
But then |P | = |P/N | ¬∑ |N | = pk‚àí1 ¬∑ p = pk , as needed.
 
There are slicker ways to prove Theorem 2.5. We will see a pretty (and in-
sightful) alternative in ¬ß2.3; but the proof given above is easy to remember and is
a good template for similar arguments.
Remark 2.7. The diligent reader worked out in Exercise II.8.20 a stronger state-
ment than Proposition 2.6, for abelian groups. The arguments are similar; the
advantage in the abelian case is that any cyclic subgroup produced by Cauchy‚Äôs
theorem is automatically normal, while ensuring normality requires a few twists
and turns in the general case (and, as a result, yields a weaker statement).
 
2.3. Sylow II. Theorem 2.5 tells us that some maximal p-group in G attains
the largest size allowed by Lagrange‚Äôs theorem, that is, the maximal power of the
prime p dividing |G|.
One can be more precise: the second Sylow theorem tells us that every maximal
p-group in |G| is in fact a p-Sylow subgroup. It is as large as is allowed by Lagrange‚Äôs
theorem.
The situation is in fact even better: all p-Sylow subgroups are conjugates of
each other7 . Moreover, even better than this, every p-group inside G must be
contained in a conjugate of any Ô¨Åxed p-Sylow subgroup.
6 Note that, as mentioned in ¬ß2.1, we only need the abelian case of this theorem.
7 Of course if P is a p-Sylow subgroup of G, then so are all conjugates gP g‚àí1 of P .
198
 IV. Groups, second encounter
The proof of this very precise result is very easy!
Theorem 2.8 (Second Sylow theorem). Let G be a Ô¨Ånite group, let P be a p-Sylow
subgroup, and let H ‚äÜ G be a p-group. Then H is contained in a conjugate of P :
there exists g ‚àà G such that H ‚äÜ gP g‚àí1 .
Proof. Act with H on the set of left-cosets of P , by left-multiplication. Since there
are [G : P ] cosets and p does not divide [G : P ], we know this action must have
Ô¨Åxed points (Exercise 1.1): let gP be one of them. This means that ‚àÄh ‚àà H:
hgP = gP ;
that is, g ‚àí1 hgP = P for all h in H; that is, g‚àí1 Hg ‚äÜ P ; that is, H ‚äÜ gP g ‚àí1, as
needed.
 
We can obtain an even more complete picture of the situation. Suppose we
have constructed a chain
H0 = {e} ‚äÜ H1 ‚äÜ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚äÜ Hk
of p-subgroups of a group G, where |Hi | = pi . By Theorem 2.8 we know that Hk
is contained in some p-Sylow subgroup, of order pr = the maximum power of p
dividing the order of G. But I claim that the chain can in fact be continued one
step at a time all the way up to the Sylow subgroup:
H0 = {e} ‚äÜ H1 ‚äÜ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚äÜ Hk ‚äÜ Hk+1 ‚äÜ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚äÜ Hr ;
and, further, Hk may be assumed to be normal in Hk+1. The following lemma will
simplify the proof of this fact considerably and will also help us prove the third
Sylow theorem.
Lemma 2.9. Let H be a p-group contained in a Ô¨Ånite group G. Then
[NG (H) : H] ‚â° [G : H]
 mod p.
Proof. If H is trivial, then NG (H) = G and the two numbers are equal.
Assume then that H is nontrivial, and act with H on the set of left-cosets of H
in G, by left-multiplication. The Ô¨Åxed points of this action are the cosets gH such
that ‚àÄh ‚àà H
hgH = gH,
that is, such that g‚àí1 hg ‚àà H for all h ‚àà H; in other words, H ‚äÜ gHg ‚àí1, and hence
(by order considerations) gHg ‚àí1 = H. This means precisely that g ‚àà NG (H).
Therefore, the set of Ô¨Åxed points of the action consists of the set of cosets of H
in NG (H).
The statement then follows immediately from Corollary 1.3.
 
As a consequence, if Hk is not a p-Sylow subgroup ‚Äòalready‚Äô, in the sense that p
‚Äòstill‚Äô divides [G : Hk ], then p must also divide [NG(Hk ) : Hk ]. Another application
of Cauchy‚Äôs theorem tells us how to obtain the next subgroup Hk+1 in the chain.
More precisely, we have the following result.
2. The Sylow theorems

Proposition 2.10. Let H be a p-subgroup of a Ô¨Ånite group G, and assume that H
is not a p-Sylow subgroup. Then there exists a p-subgroup H of G containing H,
 such that [H : H] = p and H is normal in H .
Proof. Since H is not a p-Sylow subgroup of G, p divides [NG (H) : H], by
Lemma 2.9. Since H is normal in NG (H), we may consider the quotient group
NG (H)/H, and p divides the order of this group. By Theorem 2.1, NG (H)/H has
an element of order p; this generates a subgroup of order p of NG (H)/H, which
must be (cf. ¬ßII.8.3) of the form H  /H for a subgroup H  of NG (H).
It is straightforward to verify that H  satisÔ¨Åes the stated requirements.
 
The statement about ‚Äòchains of p-subgroups‚Äô follows immediately from this
result.
Note that Cauchy‚Äôs theorem and Proposition 2.10 provide a new proof of Propo-
sition 2.6 and hence of the Ô¨Årst Sylow theorem.
2.4. Sylow III. The third (and last) Sylow theorem gives a good handle on the
number of p-Sylow subgroups of a given Ô¨Ånite group G. This is especially useful in
establishing the existence of normal subgroups of G: since all p-Sylow subgroups
of a group are conjugates of each other (by the second Sylow theorem), if there is
only one p-Sylow subgroup, then that subgroup must be normal8 .
Theorem 2.11 (Third Sylow theorem). Let p be a prime integer, and let G be
a Ô¨Ånite group of order |G| = pr m. Assume that p does not divide m. Then the
number of p-Sylow subgroups of G divides m and is congruent to 1 modulo p.
Proof. Let Np denote the number of p-Sylow subgroups of G.
By Theorem 2.8, the p-Sylow subgroups of G are the conjugates of any given
p-Sylow subgroup P . By Lemma 1.13, Np is the index of the normalizer NG (P )
of P ; thus (Corollary 1.14) it divides the index m of P . In fact,
m = [G : P ] = [G : NG(P )] ¬∑ [NG (P ) : P ] = Np ¬∑ [NG (P ) : P ].
Now, by Lemma 2.9 we have
m = [G : P ] ‚â° [NG (P ) : P ]multiplying by Np , we get
mNp ‚â° m
 mod p.
Since m ‚â° 0 mod p and p is prime, this implies
Np ‚â° 1 mod p,
as needed.

 IV. Groups, second encounter
2.5. Applications. Consequences stemming from the group actions we have en-
countered, and especially the Sylow theorems, may be applied to establish exquisitely
precise facts about individual groups as well as whole classes of groups; this is often
based on some simple but clever numerology.
The following examples are exceedingly simple-minded but will hopefully con-
vey the Ô¨Çavor of what can be done with the tools we have built in the previous
two sections. More examples may be found among the exercises at the end of this
section.
2.5.1. More nonsimple groups.
Claim 2.12. Let G be a group of order mpr , where p is a prime integer and
1 < m < p. Then G is not simple.
(Cf. Example 2.4.)
Proof. By the third Sylow theorem, the number Np of p-Sylow subgroups divides m
and is of the form 1 + kp. Since m < p, this forces k = 0, Np = 1. Therefore G has
a normal subgroup of order pr ; hence it is not simple.
 
Of course the same argument gives the same conclusion for every group of order
mpr , where (m, p) = 1 and the only divisor d of m such that d ‚â° 1 mod p is d = 1.
Example 2.13. There are no simple groups of order 2002.
Indeed9 ,
2002 = 2 ¬∑ 7 ¬∑ 11 ¬∑ 13;
the divisors of 2 ¬∑ 7 ¬∑ 13 are
1 , 2 , 7 , 13 , 14 , 26 , 91 , 182 :
of these, only 1 is congruent to 1 mod 11.order 11 in every group of order 2002.
Thus there is a normal subgroup of

The reader should not expect the third Sylow theorem to always yield its fruits
so readily, however.
Example 2.14. There are no simple groups of order 12.
Note that 3 ‚â° 1 mod 2 and 4 ‚â° 1 mod 3: thus the argument used above does not
guarantee the existence of either a normal 2-Sylow subgroup or a normal 3-Sylow
subgroup.
However, suppose that there is more than one 3-Sylow subgroup. Then there
must be 4, by the third Sylow theorem. Since any two such subgroups must intersect
in the identity, this accounts for exactly 8 elements of order 3. Excluding these
leaves us with the identity and 3 elements of order 2 or 4; that is just enough room
to Ô¨Åt one 2-Sylow subgroup. This subgroup will then have to be normal.
Thus, either there is a 3-Sylow normal subgroup or there is a 2-Sylow normal
subgroup‚Äîeither way, the group is not simple.
 
9 It is safe to guess that this statement has been assigned on hundreds of algebra tests across
the world in the year 2002.
2. The Sylow theorems
 201
Even this more reÔ¨Åned counting will often fail, and one has to dig deeper.
Example 2.15. There are no simple groups of order 24.
Indeed, let G be a group of order 24, and consider its 2-Sylow subgroups; by
the third Sylow theorem, there are either 1 or 3 such subgroups. If there is 1, the
2-Sylow subgroup is normal and G is not simple. Otherwise, G acts (nontrivially)
by conjugation on this set of three 2-Sylow subgroups; this action gives a nontrivial
homomorphism G ‚Üí S3 , whose kernel is a proper, nontrivial normal subgroup
of G‚Äîthus again G is not simple.
 
The reader should practice by selecting a random number n and trying to say
as much as he/she can, in general, about groups of order n. Beware: such problems
are a common feature of qualifying exams.
2.5.2. Groups of order pq, p < q prime.
Claim 2.16. Assume p < q are prime integers and q ‚â° 1 mod p. Let G be a group
of order pq. Then G is cyclic.
Proof. By the third Sylow theorem, G has a unique (hence normal) subgroup H
of order p. Indeed, the number Np of p-Sylow subgroups must divide q, and q is
prime, so Np = 1 or q. Necessarily Np ‚â° 1 mod p, and q ‚â° 1 mod p by hypothesis;
therefore Np = 1.
Since H is normal, conjugation gives an action of G on H, hence (by Exer-
cise 1.21) a homomorphism Œ≥ : G ‚Üí Aut(H). Now H is cyclic of order p, so
| Aut(H)| = p ‚àí 1 (Exercise II.4.14); the order of Œ≥(G) must divide both pq and
p ‚àí 1, and it follows that Œ≥ is the trivial map.
Therefore, conjugation is trivial on H: that is, H ‚äÜ Z(G). Lemma 1.5 implies
that G is abelian.
Finally, an abelian group of order pq, with p < q primes, is necessarily cyclic:
indeed it must contain elements g, h of order p, q, respectively (for example by
Cauchy‚Äôs theorem), and then |gh| = pq by Exercise II.1.14.
 
For example, this statement ‚ÄòclassiÔ¨Åes‚Äô all groups of order 15, 33, 35, 51, . . . :
such groups are necessarily cyclic.
The argument given in the proof is rather ‚Äòhigh-brow‚Äô, as it involves the auto-
morphism group of H; that is precisely why I gave it. For low-brow alternatives,
see Exercise 2.18 or Remark 5.4.
The condition q ‚â° 1 mod p in Claim 2.16 is clearly necessary: indeed, |S3| = 2¬∑3
is the product of two distinct primes, and yet S3 is not cyclic. The argument given
in the proof shows that if |G| = pq, with p < q prime, and G has a normal subgroup
of order p, then G is cyclic. If q ‚â° 1 mod p, it can be shown that there is in fact
a unique noncommutative group of order pq up to isomorphism: the reader will
work this out after learning about semidirect products (Exercise 5.12). But we are
in fact already in the position of obtaining rather sophisticated information about
this group, even without knowing its construction in general (Exercise 2.19).
For fun, let‚Äôs tackle the case in which p = 2.
202

Claim 2.17. Let q be an odd prime, and let G be a noncommutative group of
order 2q. Then G ‚àº
 = D2q , the dihedral group.
Proof. By Cauchy‚Äôs theorem, ‚àÉy ‚àà G such that y has order q. By the third Sylow
theorem, y is the unique subgroup of order q in G (and is therefore normal).
Since G is not commutative and in particular it is not cyclic, it has no elements of
order 2q; therefore, every element in the complement of y has order 2; let x be
any such element.
The conjugate xyx‚àí1 of y by x is an element of order q, so xyx‚àí1 ‚àà y. Thus,
xyx‚àí1

Now observe that

since |x| = 2. Therefore, y r2

by Corollary II.1.11. Since q is prime, this says that q | (r ‚àí 1) or q | (r + 1); since
0 ‚â§ r ‚â§ q ‚àí 1, it follows that r = 1 or r = q ‚àí 1.
If r = 1, then xyx‚àí1 = y; that is, xy = yx. But then the order of xy is 2q (by
Exercise II.1.14), and G is cyclic, a contradiction.
Therefore r = q ‚àí 1, and we have established the relations

Claim 2.17 yields a classiÔ¨Åcation of groups of order 2q, for q an odd prime: such
a group must be either abelian (and hence cyclic, by the usual considerations) or
isomorphic to a dihedral group. For q = 3, we recover the result of Exercise 1.13:
every noncommutative group of order 6 is isomorphic to D6 ‚àº

3. Composition series and solvability
I have claimed that simple groups (in the sense of DeÔ¨Ånition 2.3) are the ‚Äòbasic
constituents‚Äô of all Ô¨Ånite groups. Among other things, the material in this section
will (partially) justify this claim.
3.1. The Jordan-H√∂lder theorem. A series of subgroups Gi of a group G is a
decreasing sequence of subgroups starting from G:
G = G0  G1  G2  ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ .
The length of a series is the number of strict inclusions.
A series is normal if Gi+1 is normal in Gi for all i. We will be interested in
the maximal length of a normal series in G; if Ô¨Ånite, I will denote this number11
by (G). The number (G) is a measure of how far G is from being simple. Indeed,
(G) = 0 if and only if G is trivial, and (G) = 1 if and only if G is simple: for a
simple group, the only maximal normal series is
G  {e}.
10 The reader will prove later (Exercise 4.22) that there is in fact only one simple group of
order 60 up to isomorphism and that this group contains exactly Ô¨Åve 2-Sylow subgroups. The
result obtained here will be needed to establish this fact.
11There does not appear to be a standard notation for this concept.
206
 IV. Groups, second encounter
DeÔ¨Ånition 3.1. A composition series for G is a normal series
G = G0  G1  G2  ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑  Gn = {e}
such that the successive quotients Gi /Gi+1 are simple.

It is clear (by induction on the order) that Ô¨Ånite groups have composition series,
while inÔ¨Ånite groups do not necessarily have one (Exercise 3.3). It is also clear that
if a normal series has maximal length (G), then it is a composition series. What
is not clear is that the converse holds: conceivably, there could exist composition
series of diÔ¨Äerent lengths (the longest ones having length (G)). For example, why
can‚Äôt there be a Ô¨Ånite group G with (G) = 3 and two diÔ¨Äerent composition series
G  G1  G2  {e}
and
G
 
 G 1
 
 {e}
(that is: a Ô¨Ånite group G with (G) = 3 and a simple normal subgroup G 1 such
that G/G 1 is simple)?
Part of the content of the Jordan-H√∂lder theorem is that (luckily) this cannot
happen. In fact, the theorem is much more precise: not only do all composition
series have the same length, but they also have the same quotients (appearing,
however, in possibly diÔ¨Äerent orders).
Theorem 3.2 (Jordan-H√∂lder). Let G be a group, and let
G = G0  G1  G2  ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑  Gn = {e},
G = G 0  G 1  G 2  ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑  Gm  = {e}
be two composition series for G. Then m = n, and the lists of quotient groups
Hi = Gi /Gi+1 , Hi  = G i /G i+1 agree (up to isomorphism) after a permutation of
the indices.
Proof.(*)
Let
G = G0  G1  G2  ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑  Gn = {e}
be a composition series. Argue by induction on n: if n = 0, then G is trivial, and
there is nothing to prove. Assume n > 0, and let
(**)

be another composition series for G. If G1 = G 1 , then the result follows from the
induction hypothesis, since G1 has a composition series of length n ‚àí 1 < n.
We may then assume G1 = G 1 . Note that G1G 1 = G: indeed, G1 G 1 is normal
in G (Exercise 3.5), and G1  G1G 1 ; but there are no proper normal subgroups
between G1 and G since G/G1 is simple.

 
3.2. Composition factors; Schreier‚Äôs theorem. Two normal series are equiv-
alent if they have the same length and the same quotients (up to order). The
Jordan-H√∂lder theorem shows that any two maximal Ô¨Ånite series of a group are
equivalent. That is, the (isomorphism classes of the) quotients of a composition
series depend only on the group, not on the chosen series. These are the compo-
sition factors of the group. They form a multiset12 of simple groups: the ‚Äòbasic
constituents‚Äô of our loose comment back in ¬ß2.
It is clear that two isomorphic groups must have the same composition factors.
Unfortunately, it is not possible to reconstruct a group from its composition factors
alone (Exercise 3.4). One has to take into account the way the simple groups are
‚Äòglued‚Äô together; we will come back to this point in ¬ß5.2.
The intuition that the composition factors of a group are its basic constituents
is reinforced by the following fact: if G is a group with a composition series, then
the composition factors of every normal subgroup N of G are composition factors
of G and the remaining ones are the composition factors of the quotient G/N .
12 See ¬ßI.2.2 for a reminder on multisets: they are sets of elements counted with multiplicity.
For example, the composition factors of Z/4Z form the multiset consisting of two copies of Z/2Z.
208
 IV. Groups, second encounter
Example 3.3. Let G = Z/6Z = {[0], [1], [2], [3], [4], [5]}. Then
{[0], [1], [2], [3], [4], [5]}  {[0], [3]}  {[0]}
is a composition series for G; the quotients are Z/3Z, Z/2Z, respectively. The (nor-
mal) subgroup N = {[0], [2], [4]} ‚Äòturns oÔ¨Ä‚Äô the second factor: indeed, intersecting
the series with N gives
{[0], [2], [4]}  {[0]} = {[0]},
a series with composition factor Z/3Z. On the other hand, ‚Äòmod-ing out by N ‚Äô
turns oÔ¨Ä the Ô¨Årst factor: keeping in mind [3] + N = [1] + N , etc., we Ô¨Ånd
{[0] + N, [1] + N } = {[0] + N, [1] + N }  {[0] + N },
a series with lone composition factor Z/2Z.
 
This phenomenon holds in complete generality:
Proposition 3.4. Let G be a group, and let N be a normal subgroup of G. Then
G has a composition series if and only if both N and G/N have composition series.
Further, if this is the case, then
(G) = (N ) + (G/N ),
and the composition factors of G consist of the collection of composition factors
of N and of G/N .
Proof. If G/N has a composition series, the subgroups appearing in it correspond
to subgroups of G containing N , with isomorphic quotients, by Proposition II.8.10
(the ‚Äúthird isomorphism theorem‚Äù). Thus, if both G/N and N have composition
series, juxtaposing them produces a composition series for G, with the stated con-
sequence on composition factors.
The converse is a little trickier. Assume that G has a composition series
G = G0  G1  G2  ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑  Gn = {e}
and that N is a normal subgroup of G. Intersecting the series with N gives a
sequence of subgroups of the latter:

Summarizing, we have shown that if G has a composition series and N is normal
in G, then both N and G/N have composition series. The Ô¨Årst part of the argument

One nice consequence of the Jordan-H√∂lder theorem is the following observa-
tion. A series is a reÔ¨Ånement of another series if all terms of the Ô¨Årst appear in the
second.
Proposition 3.5. Any two normal series of a Ô¨Ånite group ending with {e} admit
equivalent reÔ¨Ånements.
Proof. ReÔ¨Åne the series to a composition series; then apply the Jordan-H√∂lder
theorem.
 
In fact, Schreier‚Äôs theorem asserts that this holds for all groups (while the
argument given here only works for groups admitting a composition series, e.g.,
Ô¨Ånite groups). Proving this in general is reasonably straightforward, from judicious
applications of the second isomorphism theorem (cf. Exercise 3.7).

3.3. The commutator subgroup, derived series, and solvability. It has
been a while since we have encountered a universal object; here is one. For any
group G, consider the category whose objects are group homomorphisms Œ± : G ‚Üí A
from G to a commutative group and whose morphisms Œ± ‚Üí Œ≤ are (as the reader
should expect) commutative diagrams
G 4
 4Œ±
 4
 4 Œ≤
4 4
 œï
 
A
 / B
where œï is a homomorphism.
Does this category have an initial object? That is, given a group G, does there
exist a commutative group which is universal with respect to the property of being
a homomorphic image of G?
Yes.
Such a group may well be thought of as the closest ‚Äòcommutative approximation‚Äô
of the given group G. To verify that this universal object exists, we introduce the
following important notion. (The diligent reader has begun exploring this territory
already, in Exercise II.7.11.)
DeÔ¨Ånition 3.6. Let G be a group. The commutator subgroup of G is the subgroup
generated by all elements
‚àí1 ‚àí1
ghg hwith g, h ‚àà G.
 
The element ghg ‚àí1 h‚àí1 is often denoted [g, h] and is called the commutator of g
and h. Thus, g, h commute with each other if and only if [g, h] = e.
In the same notational style, the commutator subgroup of G should be denoted
[G, G]; this is a bit heavy, and the common shorthand for it is G , which oÔ¨Äers
the possibility of iterating the notation. Thus, G may be used to denote the
commutator subgroup of the commutator subgroup of G, and G(i) denotes the i-
th iterate. I will adopt this notation in this subsection for convenience, but not
elsewhere in this book (as I want to be able to ‚Äòprime‚Äô any letter I wish, for any
reason).
First we record the following trivial, but useful, remark:
Lemma 3.7. Let œï : G1 ‚Üí G2 be a group homomorphism. Then ‚àÄg, h ‚àà G 1 we
have
œï([g, h]) = [œï(g), œï(h)]
and œï(G1 
 ) ‚äÜ G2 
 .
This simple observation makes the key properties of the commutator subgroup
essentially immediate (cf. Exercise II.7.11):
Proposition 3.8. Let G be the commutator subgroup of G. Then
‚Ä¢ G is normal in G;
‚Ä¢ the quotient G/G is commutative;
3. Composition series and solvability
 211
‚Ä¢ if Œ± : G ‚Üí A is a homomorphism of G to a commutative group, then G ‚äÜ
ker Œ±;
‚Ä¢ the natural projection G ‚Üí G/G is universal in the sense explained above.
Proof. These are all easy consequences of Lemma 3.7:
‚ÄîBy Lemma 3.7, the commutator subgroup is characteristic, hence normal
(cf. Exercise 2.2).
‚ÄîBy Lemma 3.7, the commutator of any two cosets gG, hG is the coset of the
commutator [g, h]; hence it is the identity in G/G. As noted above, this implies
that G/G is commutative.
‚ÄîLet Œ± : G ‚Üí A be a homomorphism to a commutative group. By Lemma 3.7,
Œ±(G ) ‚äÜ A = {e}: that is, G ‚äÜ ker Œ±.
‚ÄîThe universality follows from the previous point and from the universal prop-
erty of quotients (Theorem II.7.12).
 
Taking successive commutators of a group produces a descending sequence of
subgroups,
G ‚äá G ‚äá G ‚äá G ‚äá ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ,
which is ‚Äònormal‚Äô in the sense indicated in ¬ß3.1.
DeÔ¨Ånition 3.9. Let G be a group. The derived series of G is the sequence of
subgroups
G ‚äá G ‚äá G ‚äá G ‚äá ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ .
 
The derived series may or may not end with the identity of G. For example,
if G is commutative, then the sequence gets there right away:
G ‚äá G = {e};
however, if G is simple and noncommutative, then it gets stuck at the Ô¨Årst step:
G = G  = G  = ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑
(indeed, G is normal and = {e} as G is noncommutative; but then G  = G since
G is simple).
DeÔ¨Ånition 3.10. A group is solvable if its derived series terminates with the iden-
tity.
 
For example, abelian groups are solvable.
The importance of this notion will be most apparent in the relatively distant
future because of a brilliant application of Galois theory (¬ßVII.7.4). But we can
already appreciate it in the way it relates to the material we just covered. A normal
series is abelian, resp., cyclic, if all quotients are abelian, resp., cyclic13 .
Proposition 3.11. For a Ô¨Ånite group G, the following are equivalent:
(i) All composition factors of G are cyclic.
(ii) G admits a cyclic series ending in {e}.
13Thus, a composition series should be called ‚Äòsimple‚Äô; to our knowledge, it is not.
212
 IV. Groups, second encounter
(iii) G admits an abelian series ending in {e}.
(iv) G is solvable.
Proof. (i) =‚áí (ii) =‚áí (iii) are trivial. (iii) =‚áí (i) is obtained by reÔ¨Åning an
abelian series to a composition series (keeping in mind that the simple abelian
groups are cyclic p-groups).
(iv) =‚áí (iii) is also trivial, since the derived series is abelian (by the second
point in Proposition 3.8).
Thus, we only have to prove (iii) =‚áí (iv). For this, let
G = G0  G1  G2  ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑  Gn = {e}
be an abelian series. Then I claim that G(i) ‚äÜ Gi for all i, where G(i) denotes the
i-th ‚Äòiterated‚Äô commutator subgroup.
This can be veriÔ¨Åed by induction. For i = 1, G/G 1 is commutative; thus
G ‚äÜ G1, by the third point in Proposition 3.8. Assuming we know G(i) ‚äÜ Gi , the
fact that Gi /G i+1 is abelian implies G i ‚äÜ G i+1, and hence
G(i+1) = (G(i) ) ‚äÜ G i ‚äÜ Gi+1 ,
as claimed.
In particular we obtain that G(n) ‚äÜ Gn = {e}: that is, the derived series
terminates at {e}, as needed.
 
Example 3.12. All p-groups are solvable. Indeed, the composition factors of a
p-group are simple p-groups (what else could they be?), hence cyclic.
 
Corollary 3.13. Let N be a normal subgroup of a group G. Then G is solvable if
and only if both N and G/N are solvable.
Proof. This follows immediately from Proposition 3.4 and the formulation of solv-
ability in terms of composition factors given in Proposition 3.11.
 
It is worth mentioning that any subgroup H of a solvable group is solvable:
indeed, the commutator H  of H is a subgroup of the commutator G of G, hence
H  ‚äÜ G , H  ‚äÜ G, and so on.
The Feit-Thompson theorem asserts that every Ô¨Ånite group of odd order is
solvable. This result is many orders of magnitude beyond the scope of this book:
the original 1963 proof runs about 250 pages.

4. The symmetric group
4.1. Cycle notation. It is time to give a second look at symmetric groups. Recall
that Sn denotes the group of permutations (i.e., automorphisms in Set) of the set
{1, . . . , n}. In ¬ßII.2 we denoted elements of Sn in a straightforward but inconvenient
way:
1 2 3 4 5 6 7 8
œÉ =
8 1 2 7 5 3 4 6
would stand for the element in S8 sending 1 to 8, 2 to 1, etc.
There is clearly ‚Äútoo much‚Äù information here (the Ô¨Årst row should be implicit),
and at the same time it seems hard to Ô¨Ånd out anything interesting about a per-
mutation from this notation. For example, can the reader say anything about the
conjugates of œÉ in S8 ? For maximal enlightenment, try to do Exercise 4.1 now, and
then try again after absorbing the material in ¬ß4.2. In short, we should be able to
do better.
As often is the case, thinking in terms of actions helps. By its very deÔ¨Ånition,
the group Sn acts on the set {1, . . . , n}; so does every subgroup of Sn . Given a
permutation œÉ ‚àà Sn , consider the cyclic group œÉ generated by œÉ and its action on
{1, . . . , n}. The orbits of this action form a partition of {1, . . . , n}; therefore, every
15This property characterizes Ô¨Ånite nilpotent groups; cf. Exercise 5.1.
4. The symmetric group
 215
œÉ ‚àà Sn determines a partition of {1, . . . , n}. For example, the element œÉ ‚àà S8 given
above splits {1, . . . , 8} into three orbits:
{1, 2, 3, 6, 8},
 {4, 7},
 {5}.
The action of œÉ is transitive on each orbit. This means that one can get from
any element of the orbit to any other element and then back to the original one by
applying œÉ enough times. In the example,
1 ‚Üí 8 ‚Üí 6 ‚Üí 3 ‚Üí 2 ‚Üí 1,

DeÔ¨Ånition 4.1. A (nontrivial) cycle is an element of Sn with exactly one nontrivial
orbit. For distinct a1 , . . . , ar in {1, . . . , n}, the notation
(a1 a2 . . . ar )
denotes the cycle in Sn with nontrivial orbit {a1, . . . , ar }, acting as
a1 ‚Üí a 2 ‚Üí ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚Üí ar ‚Üí a1.
In this case, r is the length of the cycle. A cycle of length r is called an r-cycle. 
The identity is considered a cycle of length 1 in a trivial way and is denoted by
(1) (and could just as well be denoted by (i) for any i).
Note that (a1 a2 . . . ar ) = (a2 . . . ar a1 ) according to the notation introduced
in DeÔ¨Ånition 4.1: the notation determines the cycle, but a nontrivial cycle only
determines the notation ‚Äòup to a cyclic permutation‚Äô.
Two cycles are disjoint if their nontrivial orbits are. The following observation
deserves to be highlighted, but it does not seem to deserve a proof:
Lemma 4.2. Disjoint cycles commute.
The next one gives us the alternative notation we were looking for.
Lemma 4.3. Every œÉ ‚àà Sn , œÉ = e, can be written as a product of disjoint nontrivial
cycles, in a unique way up to permutations of the factors.
Proof. As we have seen, every œÉ ‚àà Sn determines a partition of {1, . . . , n} into
orbits under the action of œÉ. If œÉ = e, then œÉ has nontrivial orbits. As œÉ acts
as a cycle on each orbit, it follows that œÉ may be written as a product of cycles.
The proof of the uniqueness is left to the reader (Exercise 4.2).
 
The cycle notation for œÉ ‚àà Sn is the (essentially) unique expression of œÉ as a
product of disjoint cycles found in Lemma 4.3 (or (1) for œÉ = e). In our running
example,
œÉ = (18632)(47),
and keep in mind that this expression is unique cum grano salis: we could write
œÉ = (63218)(47) = (74)(21863) = (32186)(74) = ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ,
and all of these are ‚Äòthe same‚Äô cycle notation for œÉ.

 IV. Groups, second encounter
4.2. Type and conjugacy classes in Sn . The cycle notation has obviously an-
noying features‚Äîsuch as the not-too-unique uniqueness pointed out a moment ago,
or the fact that
(123)
can be an element of S3 just as well as of S100000 , and only the context can tell.
However, it is invaluable as it gives easy access to quite a bit of important informa-
tion on a given permutation. In fact, much of this information is carried already
by something even simpler than the cycle decomposition.
A partition of an integer n > 0 is a nonincreasing16 sequence of positive integers
whose sum is n. It is easy to enumerate partitions for small values of n. For example,
5 has 7 distinct partitions:
5=1+1+1+1+1
=2+1+1+1
=2+2+1
=3+1+1
=3+2
=4+1
= 5.
The partition Œª1 ‚â• Œª2 ‚â• ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚â• Œªr may be denoted
[Œª1 , . . . , Œªr ];
for example, the fourth partition listed above would be denoted [3, 1, 1]. A nicer
‚Äòvisual‚Äô representation is by means of the corresponding Young (or Ferrers) diagram,
obtained by stacking Œª1 boxes on top of Œª2 boxes on top of Œª3 boxes on top of. . . .
For example, the diagrams corresponding to the seven partitions listed above are
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.4. The type of œÉ ‚àà Sn is the partition of n given by the sizes of the
orbits of the action of œÉ on {1, . . . , n}.
 
It is hopefully clear (from the argument proving Lemma 4.3) that the type
of œÉ ‚àà Sn is simply given by the lengths of the cycles in the decomposition of œÉ as
the product of disjoint cycles, together with as many 1‚Äôs as needed. In our running
example,
œÉ = (18632)(47) ‚àà S8
has type [5, 2, 1]:

The main reason why ‚Äòtypes‚Äô are introduced is a consequence of the following
simple observation.
Lemma 4.5. Let œÑ ‚àà Sn, and let (a1 . . . ar ) be a cycle. Then
‚àí1 ‚àí1 ‚àí1œÑ (a1 . . . ar )œÑ = (a1 œÑ . . . ar œÑ ).
‚àí1 ‚àí1The funny notation a1 œÑ stands for the action of the permutation œÑ on
a1 ‚àà {1, . . . , n}; recall that we agreed in ¬ßII.2.1 that we would let our permutations
act on the right, for consistency with the usual notation for products in groups.
Proof. This is veriÔ¨Åed by checking that both sides act in the same way on {1, . . . , n}.
For example, for 1 ‚â§ i < r

‚àí1By the usual trick of judiciously inserting identity factors œÑ œÑ , this formula
for computing conjugates extends immediately to any product of cycles:
œÑ (a1 . . . ar ) ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ (b1 . . . bs )œÑ ‚àí1 = (a1œÑ ‚àí1 . . . ar œÑ ‚àí1 ) ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ (b1 œÑ ‚àí1 . . . bs œÑ ‚àí1 ).
This holds whether the cycles are disjoint or not. However, since œÑ is a bijection,
disjoint cycles remain disjoint after conjugation. This is essentially all there is to
the following important observation:
Proposition 4.6. Two elements of Sn are conjugate in Sn if and only if they have
the same type.
Proof. The ‚Äòonly if‚Äô part of this statement follows immediately from the preceding
considerations: conjugating a permutation yields a permutation of the same type.
As for the ‚Äòif‚Äô part, suppose
œÉ1 = (a1 . . . ar )(b1 . . . bs) ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ (c1 . . . ct )
and
œÉ2 = (a 1 . . . a r )(b 1 . . . b s) ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ (c 1 . . . c t )
are two permutations with the same type, written in cycle notation, with r ‚â• s ‚â•
¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚â• t (so the type is [r, s, . . . , t]). Let œÑ be any permutation such that ai = a i œÑ ,
bj = b j œÑ , . . . , ck = c k œÑ for all i, j, . . . , k. Then Lemma 4.5 implies œÉ2 = œÑ œÉ1 œÑ ‚àí1 ,
so œÉ1 and œÉ2 are conjugate, as needed.

Summarizing, the type (or the corresponding Young diagram) tells us every-

Corollary 4.8. The number of conjugacy classes in Sn equals the number of par-
titions of n.
For example, there are 7 conjugacy classes in S5 , indexed by the Tetris look-
alikes drawn above.
It is also reasonably straightforward to compute the number of elements in each
conjugacy class, in terms of the type. For example, in order to count the number
of permutations of type [2, 2, 1] in S5 , note that there are 5! = 120 ways to Ô¨Åll the
corresponding Young diagram with the numbers 1, . . . , 5:
a1a3a5
a2
a4
that is, 120 ways to write a permutation as a product of two 2-cycles:
(a1a2 )(a3 a4 );
but switching a1 ‚Üî a2 and a3 ‚Üî a4 , as well as switching the two cycles, gives the
same permutation. Therefore there are
120
= 15
2 ¬∑ 2 ¬∑ 2
permutations of type [2, 2, 1]. Performing this computation for all Young diagrams
for S5 gives us the size of each conjugacy class, that is, the class formula (cf. ¬ß1.2)
for S5 :
120 = 1 + 10 + 15 + 20 + 20 + 30 + 24.
Example 4.9. There are no normal subgroups of size 30 in S5 .
Indeed, normal subgroups are unions of conjugacy classes (¬ß1.3); since the
identity is in every subgroup and 30 ‚àí 1 = 29 cannot be written as a sum of the
numbers appearing in the class formula for S5 , there is no such subgroup.
 
This observation will be dwarfed by much stronger results that we will prove
soon (such as Theorem 4.20, Corollary 4.21); but it is remarkable that such precise
statements can be established with so little work.

Here is a diÔ¨Äerent viewpoint on this sign function. A transposition is a cycle of
length 2. Every permutation is a product of transpositions:
Lemma 4.11. Transpositions generate Sn .
Proof. Indeed, by Lemma 4.3 it suÔ¨Éces to show that every cycle is a product of
transpositions, and indeed
(a1 . . . ar ) = (a1 a2 )(a1 a3 ) ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ (a1 ar ),
as may be checked by applying18 both sides to every element of {1, . . . , n}.

Of course a given permutation may be written as a product of transpositions
in many diÔ¨Äerent ways. However, whether an even number of transpositions or an
odd number is needed only depends on œÉ. Indeed,
Lemma 4.12. Let œÉ = œÑ1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ œÑr be a product of transpositions. Then œÉ is even,
resp., odd, according to whether r is even, resp., odd.
Proof. This follows immediately from the facts that  is a homomorphism and the
sign of a transposition is ‚àí1: indeed, (ij) acts on Œîn by permuting its factors and
changing the sign of an odd number of factors (for i < j, the factor (xi ‚àí xj ) and
the pairs of factors (xi ‚àí xk ), (xk ‚àí xj ) for all i < k < j).
 
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.13. The alternating group on {1, . . . , n}, denoted An , consists of all
even permutations œÉ ‚àà Sn.
 
The alternating group is a normal subgroup of Sn , and
[Sn : An ] = 2
for n ‚â• 2: indeed, An = ker , and  is surjective for n ‚â• 2.
It is very easy to tell whether a permutation œÉ belongs to An in terms of the
type of œÉ. Indeed (by the argument proving Lemma 4.11) a cycle is even, resp., odd,
if it has odd, resp., even, length19. Pictorially, a permutation œÉ ‚àà Sn is even if and
only if n and the number of rows in the Young diagram of œÉ have the same parity.
Take n = 5 for example:
The starred types correspond to even permutations. Adding up the sizes of the
corresponding conjugacy classes,
1 + 15 + 20 + 24 = 60,
conÔ¨Årms that A5 has index 2 in S5 .
However, do not read too much into this computation: I am not claiming that
these are the sizes of the conjugacy classes in A5 , only that these are the conjugacy
classes in S5 making up the normal subgroup A5 . Conjugacy in An is rather
interesting and ties in nicely with the issue of the simplicity of An.
4.4. Conjugacy in An ; simplicity of An and solvability of Sn . Denote by
[œÉ]Sn , resp., [œÉ]An , the conjugacy class of an even permutation œÉ in Sn , resp., An .
Clearly [œÉ]An ‚äÜ [œÉ]Sn ; we proceed to compare these two sets.
Lemma 4.14. Let n ‚â• 2, and let œÉ ‚àà A n. Then [œÉ]An = [œÉ]Sn or the size of [œÉ]An
is half the size of [œÉ]Sn , according to whether the centralizer ZSn (œÉ) is not or is
contained in An .

Proof. (Cf. Exercise 1.16.) Note that
ZAn (œÉ) = An ‚à© ZSn (œÉ) :
this follows immediately from the deÔ¨Ånition of centralizer (DeÔ¨Ånition 1.6). Now
recall that the centralizer of œÉ is its stabilizer under conjugation, and therefore the
size of the conjugacy class of œÉ equals the index of its centralizer.
If ZSn (œÉ) ‚äÜ An , then ZAn (œÉ) = ZSn (œÉ), so that
[Sn : ZSn (œÉ)] = [Sn : ZAn (œÉ)] = [Sn : An ][An : ZAn (œÉ)] = 2 ¬∑ [An : ZAn (œÉ)];
therefore, [œÉ]An is half the size of [œÉ]Sn in this case.

(cf. Exercise II.8.21)
[An : ZAn (œÉ)] = [An : An ‚à© ZSn (œÉ)] = [A nZSn (œÉ) : ZSn (œÉ)] = [Sn : ZSn (œÉ)],
so the classes have the same size. Since [œÉ]An ‚äÜ [œÉ]Sn in any case, it follows that
[œÉ]An = [œÉ]Sn , completing the proof.
 
Therefore, conjugacy classes of even permutations either are preserved from Sn
to An or they split into two distinct, equal-sized classes. We are now in a position
to give precise conditions determining which happens when.
Proposition 4.15. Let œÉ ‚àà An , n ‚â• 2. Then the conjugacy class of œÉ in Sn splits
into two conjugacy classes in An precisely if the type of œÉ consists of distinct odd
numbers.
Proof. By Lemma 4.14, we have to verify that ZSn (œÉ) is contained in An precisely
when the stated condition is satisÔ¨Åed; that is, we have to show that

precisely when the type of œÉ consists of distinct odd numbers.

and recall (Lemma 4.5) that

‚àí1Assume that Œª, Œº . . . , ŒΩ are odd and distinct. If œÑ œÉœÑ = œÉ, then conjugation
by œÑ must preserve each cycle in œÉ, as all cycle lengths are distinct:
‚àí1œÑ (a1 . . . aŒª )œÑ = (a1 . . . aŒª),

for suitable r, s, . . . , t. Since all cycles have odd lengths, each cycle is an even

This proves that ZSn (œÉ) ‚äÜ An if the stated condition holds.
Conversely, assume that the stated condition does not hold: that is, either some

two of the cycles have the same length.
In the Ô¨Årst case, let œÑ be an even-length cycle in the cycle decomposition of œÉ.
‚àí1Note that œÑ œÉœÑ = œÉ: indeed, œÑ commutes with itself and with all cycles in œÉ other

Example 4.16. Looking again at A5 , we have noted in ¬ß4.3 that the types of
the even permutations in S5 are [1, 1, 1, 1, 1], [2, 2, 1], [3, 1, 1], and [5]. By Proposi-
tion 4.15 the conjugacy classes corresponding to the Ô¨Årst three types are preserved
in A5, while the last one splits.
Therefore there are exactly 5 conjugacy classes in A5 , and the class formula
for A5 is
60 = 1 + 15 + 20 + 12 + 12.
 
Finally! We can now complete a circle of thought begun in the Ô¨Årst section of
this chapter. Along the way, the reader has hopefully checked that every simple
group of order < 60 is commutative (Exercise 2.24); and we now see why 60 is
special:
Corollary 4.17. The alternating group A5 is a simple noncommutative group of
order 60.
Proof. A normal subgroup of A5 is necessarily the union of conjugacy classes,
contains the identity, and has order equal to a divisor of 60 (by Lagrange‚Äôs theorem).
The divisors of 60 other than 1 and 60 are
2 , 3 , 4 , 5 , 6 , 10 , 12 , 15 , 20 , 30;
counting the elements other than the identity would give one of
1 , 2 , 3 , 4 , 5 , 9 , 11 , 14 , 19 , 29
as a sum of numbers = 1 from the class formula for A5. But this simply does not
happen.
 
The reader will check that A6 is simple, by the same method (Exercise 4.21).
It is in fact the case that all groups An , n ‚â• 5, are simple (and noncommutative),
implying that S n is not solvable for n ‚â• 5, and these facts are rather important in
view of applications to Galois theory (cf., e.g., Corollary VII.7.16). Note that A2
is trivial, A3 ‚àº
 = Z/3Z is simple and abelian, and A4 is not simple (Exercise 2.24).
4. The symmetric group
 223
The alternating group A5 is also called the icosahedral (rotation) group: indeed,
it is the group of symmetries of an icosahedron obtained through rigid motions.
(Can the reader verify20 this fact?)
The simplicity of An for n ‚â• 5 may be established by studying 3-cycles. First
of all, it is natural to wonder whether every even permutation may be written as a
product of 3-cycles, and this is indeed so:
Lemma 4.18. The alternating group An is generated by 3-cycles.
Proof. Since every even permutation is a product of an even number of 2-cycles,
it suÔ¨Éces to show that every product of two 2-cycles may be written as product of
3-cycles. Therefore, consider a product
(ab)(cd)
with a = b, c = d. If (ab) = (cd), then this product is the identity, and there is
nothing to prove. If {a, b}, {c, d} have exactly one element in common, then we
may assume c = a and observe
(ab)(ad) = (abd).
If {a, b}, {c, d} are disjoint, then
(ab)(cd) = (abc)(adc),
and we are done.
 
Now we can capitalize on our study of conjugacy in An:
Claim 4.19. Let n ‚â• 5. If a normal subgroup of An contains a 3-cycle, then it
contains all 3-cycles.
Proof. Normal subgroups are unions of conjugacy classes, so we just need to verify
that 3-cycles form a conjugacy class in An , for n ‚â• 5. But they do in Sn , and the
type of a 3-cycle is [3, 1, 1, . . . ] for n ‚â• 5; hence the conjugacy class does not split
in An , by Proposition 4.15.
 
The general statement now follows easily by tying up loose ends:
Theorem 4.20. The alternating group An is simple for n ‚â• 5.
Proof. We have already checked this for n = 5, and the reader has checked it for
n = 6. For n > 6, let N be a nontrivial normal subgroup of An ; we will show that
necessarily N = An , by proving that N contains 3-cycles.
Let œÑ ‚àà N , œÑ = (1), and let œÉ ‚àà An be a 3-cycle. Since the center of An is
trivial (Exercise 4.14) and 3-cycles generate An, we may assume that œÑ and œÉ do
not commute, that is, the commutator
[œÑ, œÉ] = œÑ (œÉœÑ ‚àí1œÉ‚àí1 ) = (œÑ œÉœÑ ‚àí1)œÉ‚àí1
20 It is good practice to start with smaller examples: for instance, the tetrahedral rotation
group is isomorphic to A4 .
224
 IV. Groups, second encounter
is not the identity. This element is in N (as is evident from the Ô¨Årst expression,
as N is normal) and is a product of two 3-cycles (as is evident from the second
expression, since the conjugate of a 3-cycle is a 3-cycle).
Therefore, replacing œÑ by [œÑ, œÉ] if necessary, we may assume that œÑ ‚àà N is
a nonidentity permutation acting on ‚â§ 6 elements: that is, on a subset of a set
T ‚äÜ {1, . . . , n} with |T | = 6. Now we may view A6 as a subgroup of An , by letting
it act on T . The subgroup N ‚à© A6 of A6 is then normal (because N is normal) and
nontrivial (because œÑ ‚àà N ‚à© A6 and œÑ = (1)). Since A6 is simple (Exercise 4.21),
this implies N ‚à© A6 = A6 . In particular, N contains 3-cycles.
By Claim 4.19, this implies that N contains all 3-cycles. By Lemma 4.18, it
follows that N = An , as needed.
 
Corollary 4.21. For n ‚â• 5, the group Sn is not solvable.
Proof. Since An is simple, the sequence
Sn  An  {(1)}
is a composition series for Sn. It follows that the composition factors of Sn are
Z/2Z and An . By Proposition 3.11, Sn is not solvable.
 
In particular, S5 is a nonsolvable group of order 120. This is in fact the smallest
order of a nonsimple, nonsolvable group; cf. Exercise 3.16.

5. Products of groups
We already know that products exist in Grp (see ¬ßII.3.4); here we analyze this notion
further and explore variations on the same theme, with an eye towards the question
of determining the information needed to reconstruct a group from its composition
factors.
5.1. The direct product. Recall from ¬ßII.3.4 that the (direct) product of two
groups H, K is the group supported on the set H √ó K, with operation deÔ¨Åned com-
ponentwise. We have checked (Proposition II.3.4) that the direct product satisÔ¨Åes
the universal property deÔ¨Åning products in the category Grp.
There are situations in which the direct product of two subgroups N , H of a
group G may be realized as a subgroup of G. Recall (Proposition II.8.11) that if
one of the subgroups is normal, then the subset N H of G is in fact a subgroup
of G. The relation between N H and N √ó H depends on how N and H intersect in
G, so we take a look at this intersection.
The ‚Äòcommutator‚Äô [A, B] of two subsets A, B of G (see ¬ß3.3) is the subgroup
generated by all commutators [a, b] with a ‚àà A, b ‚àà B.
Lemma 5.1. Let N , H be normal subgroups of a group G. Then
[N, H] ‚äÜ N ‚à© H.
21 You can think algebraically if you want; if you prefer geometry, visualize pairs of opposite
sides on a tetrahedron.
5. Products of groups
 227
Proof. It suÔ¨Éces to verify this on generators; that is, it suÔ¨Éces to check that
[n, h] = n(hn‚àí1 h‚àí1) = (nhn‚àí1 )h‚àí1 ‚àà N ‚à© H
for all n ‚àà N , h ‚àà H. But the Ô¨Årst expression and the normality of N show that
[n, h] ‚àà N ; the second expression and the normality of H show that [n, h] ‚àà H. 
Corollary 5.2. Let N , H be normal subgroups of a group G. Assume N ‚à©H = {e}.
Then N , H commute with each other:
(‚àÄn ‚àà N ) (‚àÄh ‚àà H)
 nh = hn.
Proof. By Lemma 5.1, [N, H] = {e} if N ‚à©H = {e}; the result follows immediately.

In fact, under the same hypothesis more is true:
Proposition 5.3. Let N , H be normal subgroups of a group G, such that N ‚à© H =
{e}. Then N H ‚àº
 = N √ó H.
Proof. Consider the function
œï : N √ó H ‚Üí NH
deÔ¨Åned by œï(n, h) = nh. Under the stated hypothesis, œï is a group homomorphism:
indeed
œï((n1, h1 ) ¬∑ (n2 , h2)) = œï((n1 n2 , h1 h2 ))
= n1 n2 h1 h2
= n1 h1n2 h2
since N , H commute by Corollary 5.2
= œï((n1 , h1 )) ¬∑ œï((n2 , h2 )).
The homomorphism œï is surjective by deÔ¨Ånition of N H. To verify it is injective,
consider its kernel:
ker œï = {(n, h) ‚àà N √ó H | nh = e}.
If nh = e, then n ‚àà N and n = h‚àí1 ‚àà H; thus n = e since N ‚à© H = {e}. Using the
same token for h, we conclude h = e; hence (n, h) = the identity in N √ó H, proving
that œï is injective.
Thus œï is an isomorphism, as needed.
 
Remark 5.4. This result gives an alternative argument for the proof of Claim 2.16:
if |G| = pq, with p < q prime integers, and G contains normal subgroups H, K of
order p, q, respectively (as is the case if q ‚â° 1 mod p, by Sylow), then H ‚à© K = {e}
necessarily, and then Proposition 5.3 shows HK = ‚àº
 H √ó K. As |HK| = |G| = pq,
this proves G ‚àº
 = H √ó K ‚àº
 = Z/pZ √ó Z/qZ. Finally, (1, 1) has order pq in this group,
so G is cyclic, with the same conclusion we obtained in Claim 2.16.

 IV. Groups, second encounter
5.2. Exact sequences of groups; extension problem. Of course, the hypoth-
esis that both subgroups N , H are normal is necessary for the result of Proposi-
tion 5.3: for example, the permutations (123) and (12) generate subgroups N , H
of S3 meeting only at {e}, and N is normal in S3 , but S3 = N H is not isomor-
phic to the direct product of N and H. It is natural to examine this more general
situation.
Let N , H be subgroups of a group G, with N normal (but with no a priori
assumptions on H) and such that N ‚à© H = {e}; assume G = N H. We are after a
description of the structure of G in terms of the structure of N and H.
It is notationally convenient to use the language of exact sequences, introduced
for modules in ¬ßIII.7.1. A (short) exact sequence of groups is a sequence of groups
and group homomorphisms
1
 / N
 œï
 / G
 œà

 / 1
where œà is surjective and œï identiÔ¨Åes N with ker œà. In other words (by the Ô¨Årst
isomorphism theorem), use œï to identify N with a subgroup of G; then the sequence
is exact if N is normal in G and œà induces an isomorphism G/N ‚Üí H.
The reader should pause a moment and check that if G, N , H are abelian, then
this notion matches precisely the notion of short exact sequence of abelian groups
(i.e., Z-modules) from ¬ßIII.7.1; a notational diÔ¨Äerence is that here the trivial group
is denoted22 ‚Äò1‚Äô rather than ‚Äò0‚Äô.
Of course there always is an exact sequence

there is an exact sequence of groups

The extension problem aims to describe all extensions of two given groups,
up to isomorphism. For example, there are two extensions of C2 by C3 : namely
C6 ‚àº
 = C3 √ó C2 and S3 ; we will soon be able to verify that, up to isomorphism, there
are no other extensions.
The extension problem is the ‚Äòsecond half‚Äô of the classiÔ¨Åcation problem: the
Ô¨Årst half consists of determining all simple groups, and the second half consists of
Ô¨Åguring out how these can be put together to construct any group23. For example,
22 This is not unreasonable, since groups are more often written ‚Äòmultiplicatively‚Äô rather than

apparent mismatch evaporates because of Proposition 5.3: in the abelian case,
every split extension (according to DeÔ¨Ånition 5.6) is in fact a direct product.
Of course split extensions are anyway very special, since quotients of a group G
are usually not isomorphic to subgroups of G, even in the abelian case (cf. Exer-
cise 5.4).
Lemma 5.7. Let N be a normal subgroup of a group G, and let H be a subgroup
of G such that G = N H and N ‚à© H = {e}. Then G is a split extension of H by N .

5.3. Internal/semidirect products. The attentive reader should have noticed
that the key to Proposition 5.3 is really Corollary 5.2: if both N and H are normal
and N ‚à©H = {e}, then N and H commute with each other. This is what ultimately
causes the extension N H to be trivial. Now, recall that as soon as N is normal,
then every subgroup H of G acts on N by conjugation: in fact (cf. Exercise 1.21)
conjugation determines a homomorphism
Œ≥ : H ‚Üí AutGrp (N ),
 h ‚Üí Œ≥h .
(Explicitly, for h ‚àà H the automorphism Œ≥h : N ‚Üí N acts by Œ≥h (n) := hnh‚àí1 .)
The subgroups H and N commute precisely when Œ≥ is trivial. Corollary 5.2 shows
that if N and H are both normal and N ‚à© H = {e}, then Œ≥ is indeed trivial.
This is the crucial remark. The next several considerations may be summarized
as follows: if N is normal in G, H is a subgroup of G, N ‚à© H = {e} and G = N H,
then the extension G of H by N may be reconstructed from the conjugation action
Œ≥ : H ‚Üí AutGrp (N ). The reader is advised to stare at the following triviality, which
is the motivating observation for the general discussion:
(*)

This says that if we know the conjugation action of H on N , then we can recover
the operation in G from this information and from the operations in N and H.
Here is the general discussion. It is natural to abstract the situation and begin
with any two groups N , H and an arbitrary homomorphism24
