When realized ‚Äòwithin‚Äô a group, as in the previous proposition, the semidirect
product is sometime called an internal product.
Remark 5.12. If N and H commute, then the conjugation action of H on N is
trivial; therefore Œ≥ is the trivial map, and the semidirect product N Œ≥ H is the
direct product N √ó H. Thus, Proposition 5.11 recovers the result of Proposition 5.3
in this case.

begun in ¬ß2.5.2 and show that if q ‚â° 1 mod p, then there is exactly one such non-

Exercises
 233
The reader would in fact be well-advised to try to use semidirect products to
classify groups of small order: if a nontrivial normal subgroup N is found (typically
by applying Sylow‚Äôs theorems), with some luck the classiÔ¨Åcation is reduced to the
study of possible homomorphisms from known groups to AutGrp (N ) and can be
carried out. See Exercise 5.15 for a further illustration of this technique.

6. Finite abelian groups
I will end this chapter by treating in some detail the classiÔ¨Åcation theorem for Ô¨Ånite
abelian groups mentioned in ¬ßII.6.3.
6.1. ClassiÔ¨Åcation of Ô¨Ånite abelian groups. Now that we have acquired more
familiarity with products, we are in a position to classify all Ô¨Ånite abelian groups26 .
26Of course fancier semidirect products will not be needed here; cf. Exercise 5.9.
6. Finite abelian groups
 235
In due time (Proposition VI.2.11, Exercise VI.2.19) we will in fact be able to
classify all Ô¨Ånitely generated abelian groups: as mentioned in Example II.6.3, all
such groups are products of cyclic groups27. In particular, this is the case for Ô¨Ånite
abelian groups: this is what we prove in this section.
Since in this section we exclusively deal with abelian groups, we revert to the
abelian style of notations: thus the operation will be denoted +; the identity will
be 0; direct products will be called direct sums (and denoted ‚äï); and so on.
First of all, I will congeal into an explicit statement a simple observation that
has been with us in one form or another since at least as far back as 28 Exercise II.4.9.
Lemma 6.1. Let G be an abelian group, and let H, K be subgroups such that |H|,
|K| are relatively prime. Then H + K ‚àº
 = H ‚äï K.
Proof. By Lagrange‚Äôs theorem (Corollary II.8.14), H ‚à© K = {0}. Since subgroups
of abelian groups are automatically normal, the statement follows from Proposi-
tion 5.3.
 
Now let G be a Ô¨Ånite abelian group. For each prime p, the p-Sylow subgroup
of G is unique, since it is automatically normal in G. Since the distinct nontrivial
Sylow subgroups of G are p-groups for diÔ¨Äerent primes p, Lemma 6.1 immediately
implies the following result.
Corollary 6.2. Every Ô¨Ånite abelian group is the direct sum of its nontrivial Sylow
subgroups.
(The diligent reader knew already that this had to be the case, since abelian groups
are nilpotent; cf. Exercise 5.1.) Thus, we already know that every Ô¨Ånite abelian
group is a direct sum of p-groups, and our main task amounts to classifying abelian
p-groups for a Ô¨Åxed prime p. This is somewhat technical; we will get there by a
seemingly roundabout path.
Lemma 6.3. Let G be an abelian p-group, and let g ‚àà G be an element of maximal
order. Then the exact sequence

 / G/g
 / 0
splits.
Put otherwise, there is a subgroup L of G such that L maps isomorphically to
G/g via the canonical projection, that is, such that g‚à©L = {0} and g+L = G.
Note that it will follow that G ‚àº

The main technicality needed in order to prove this lemma is the following
particular case:
Lemma 6.4. Let p be a prime integer and r ‚â• 1. Let G be a noncyclic abelian
group of order pr+1 , and let g ‚àà G be an element of order pr . Then there exists an
element h ‚àà G, h ‚àà g, such that |h| = p.

Lemma 6.4 is a special case of Lemma 6.3 in the sense that, with notation as in
the statement, necessarily h ‚àº
 = G/g, and in fact G = ‚àº
 g ‚äï h (and the reader
is warmly encouraged to understand this before proceeding!). That is, we can split
oÔ¨Ä the ‚Äòlarge‚Äô cyclic subgroup g as a direct summand of G, provided that G is
not cyclic and not much larger than g. Lemma 6.3 claims that this can be done
whenever g is a maximal cyclic subgroup of G. We will be able to prove this more
general statement easily once the particular case is settled.
Proof of Lemma 6.4. Denote g by K, and let h be any element of G, h ‚àà K.
The subgroup K is normal in G since G is abelian; the quotient group G/K has
order p. Since h ‚àà K, the coset h + K has order p in G/K; that is, ph ‚àà K. Let
k = ph .
Note that |k| divides pr ; hence it is a power of p. Also |k| = p r , otherwise
|h
| = pr+1 and G would be cyclic, contrary to the hypothesis.
Therefore |k| = ps for some s < r; k generates a subgroup k of the cyclic
group K, of order ps . By Proposition II.6.11, k = pr‚àís g. Since s < r, k ‚äÜ
pg; thus, k = mpg for some m ‚àà Z.
Then let h = h ‚àí mg: h = 0 (since h ‚àà K), and
ph = ph ‚àí p(mg) = k ‚àí k = 0,
showing that |h| = p, as stated.
 
Proof of Lemma 6.3. Argue by induction on the order of G; the case |G| = p0 =
1 requires no proof. Thus we will assume that G is nontrivial and that the statement
is true for every p-group smaller than G.
Let g ‚àà G be an element of maximal order, say pr , and denote by K the
subgroup g generated by g; this subgroup is normal, as G is abelian. If G = K,
then the statement holds trivially. If not, G/K is a nontrivial p-group, and hence it
contains an element of order p by Cauchy‚Äôs theorem (Theorem 2.1). This element
generates a subgroup of order p in G/K, corresponding to a subgroup G of G of
order pr+1 , containing K. This subgroup is not cyclic (otherwise the order of g is
not maximal).
That is, we are in the situation of Lemma 6.4: hence we can conclude that there
is an element h ‚àà G (and hence h ‚àà G) with h ‚àà K and |h| = p. Let H = h ‚äÜ G
be the subgroup generated by h, and note that K ‚à© H = {0}.
Now work modulo H. The quotient group G/H has smaller size than G, and
g + H generates a cyclic subgroup K  = (K + H)/H = ‚àº
 K/(K ‚à© H) = ‚àº
 K of maximal
order in G/H. By the induction hypothesis, there is a subgroup L of G/H such
that K  + L = G/H and K  ‚à© L = {0G/H }. This subgroup L corresponds to a
subgroup L of G containing H.
Now I claim that (i) K + L = G and (ii) K ‚à© L = {0}. Indeed, we have the
following:
(i) For any a ‚àà G, there exist mg + H ‚àà K  , + H ‚àà L such that a + H =
mg + + H (since K  + L = G/H). This implies a ‚àí mg ‚àà L, and hence
a ‚àà K + L as needed.
6. Finite abelian groups
 237
(ii) If a ‚àà K ‚à© L, then a + H ‚àà K  ‚à© L = {0G/H }, and hence a ‚àà H. In particular,
a ‚àà K ‚à© H = {0}, forcing a = 0, as needed.
(i) and (ii) imply the lemma, as observed in the comments following the statement.

Now we are ready to state the classiÔ¨Åcation theorem; the proof is quite straight-
forward after all this preparation work. We Ô¨Årst give the statement in a somewhat
coarse form, as a corollary of the previous considerations:
Corollary 6.5. Let G be a Ô¨Ånite abelian group. Then G is a direct sum of cyclic
groups, which may be assumed to be cyclic p-groups.
Proof. As noted in Corollary 6.2, G is a direct sum of p-groups (as a consequence
of the Sylow theorems). I claim that every abelian p-group P is a direct sum of
cyclic p-groups.
To establish this, argue by induction on |P |. There is nothing to prove if P is
trivial. If P is not trivial, let g be an element of P of maximal order. By Lemma 6.3
P = g ‚äï P 
 for some subgroup P of P ; by the induction hypothesis P is a direct sum of cyclic
p-groups, concluding the proof.

6.3. Application: Finite subgroups of multiplicative groups of Ô¨Åelds. Any
classiÔ¨Åcation theorem is useful in that it potentially reduces the proof of general
facts to explicit veriÔ¨Åcations. Here is one example illustrating this strategy:
Lemma 6.9. Let G be a Ô¨Ånite abelian group, and assume that for every integer
n > 0 the number of elements g ‚àà G such that ng = 0 is at most n. Then G is
cyclic.
The reader should try to prove this ‚Äòby hand‚Äô, to appreciate the fact that
it is not entirely trivial. It does become essentially immediate once we take the
classiÔ¨Åcation of Ô¨Ånite abelian groups into account. Indeed, by Theorem 6.6
Z
 Z
G ‚àº
 =
 ‚äï ¬∑¬∑¬∑ ‚äï
d1 Z
 ds Z
for some positive integers 1 < d1 | ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ | ds . But if s > 1, then |G| > ds and d s g = 0
for all g ‚àà G (so that the order of g divides ds ), contradicting the hypothesis.
Therefore s = 1; that is, G is cyclic.
Lemma 6.9 is the key to a particularly nice proof of the following important
fact, a weak form of which30 we ran across back in Example II.4.6. Recall that the
‚àóset F of nonzero elements of a Ô¨Åeld F is a commutative group under multiplication.
Also recall (Example III.4.7) that a polynomial f (x) ‚àà F [x] is divisible by (x ‚àí a)
if and only if f (a) = 0; since a nonzero polynomial of degree n over a Ô¨Åeld can
have at most n linear factors, this shows31 that if f (x) ‚àà F [x] has degree n, then
f (a) = 0 for at most n distinct elements a ‚àà F .
Theorem 6.10. Let F be a Ô¨Åeld, and let G be a Ô¨Ånite subgroup of the multiplicative
group (F ‚àó , ¬∑). Then G is cyclic.
Proof. By the considerations preceding the statement, for every n there are at
most n elements a ‚àà F such that an ‚àí 1 = 0, that is, at most n elements a ‚àà G
such that an = 1. Lemma 6.9 implies then that G is cyclic.
 
30 The diligent reader has proved that particular case in Exercise II.4.11. The proof hinted
at in that exercise upgrades easily to the general case presented here. The point is not that the
classiÔ¨Åcation theorem is necessary in order to prove statements such as Theorem 6.10; the point
is that it makes such statements nearly evident.
31 Unique factorization in F [x] is secretly needed here. We will deal with this issue more
formally later; cf. Lemma V.5.1.

As a (very) particular case, the multiplicative group ((Z/pZ)‚àó , ¬∑) is cyclic: this
is the fact pointed to in Example II.4.6.
Preview of coming attractions: Finitely generated (as opposed to just Ô¨Ånite)
abelian groups are also direct sums of cyclic groups. The only diÔ¨Äerence between
the classiÔ¨Åcation of Ô¨Ånitely generated abelian groups and the classiÔ¨Åcation of Ô¨Ånite
abelian groups explored here is the possible presence of a ‚Äòfree‚Äô factor Z‚äïr in the
decomposition. The reader will Ô¨Årst prove this fact in Exercise VI.2.19, as a conse-
quence of ‚ÄòGaussian elimination over integral domains‚Äô, and then recover it again
as a particular case of the classiÔ¨Åcation theorem for Ô¨Ånitely generated modules
over PIDs, Theorem VI.5.6. Neither Gaussian elimination nor the very general
Theorem VI.5.6 are any harder to prove than the particular case of Ô¨Ånite abelian
groups laboriously worked out by hand in this section‚Äîa common beneÔ¨Åt of Ô¨Ånding
the right general point of view is that, as a rule, proofs simplify. Technical work
such as that performed in order to prove Lemma 6.3 is absorbed into the work
necessary to build up the more general apparatus; the need for such technicalities
evaporates in the process.

Chapter V
Irreducibility and
factorization in integral
domains
We move our attention back to rings and analyze several useful classes of integral
domains. One guiding theme in this chapter is the issue of factorization: we will
address the problem of existence and uniqueness of factorizations of elements in a
ring, abstracting good factorization properties of rings such as Z or k[x] (for k a
Ô¨Åeld) to whole classes of integral domains. The reader may want to associate the
following picture with the Ô¨Årst part of this chapter:
Blanket assumption: all rings considered in this chapter will be commutative1 . In
fact, most of the special classes of rings we will consider will be integral domains,
1 Also, recall that all our rings have 1; cf. DeÔ¨Ånition III.1.1.
243
244
 V. Irreducibility and factorization in integral domains
that is, commutative rings with 1 and with no nonzero zero-divisors (cf. DeÔ¨Åni-
tion III.1.10).
1. Chain conditions and existence of factorizations
1.1. Noetherian rings revisited. Let R be a commutative ring. Recall that R
is said to be Noetherian if every ideal of R is Ô¨Ånitely generated (DeÔ¨Ånition III.4.2).
In fact, this is a special case of the corresponding deÔ¨Ånition for modules: a mod-
ule M over a ring R is Noetherian if every submodule of M is Ô¨Ånitely generated
(DeÔ¨Ånition III.6.6). In ¬ßIII.6.4 we have veriÔ¨Åed that this condition is preserved
through exact sequences: if M, N, P are R-modules and
0
 / N
 / M
 / P
 / 0
is an exact sequence of R-modules, then M is Noetherian if and only if both N
and P are Noetherian (Proposition III.6.7). An easy and useful consequence of this
fact is that every Ô¨Ånitely generated module over a Noetherian ring is Noetherian
(Corollary III.6.8).
The Noetherian condition may be expressed in alternative ways, and it is useful
to acquire some familiarity with them.
Proposition 1.1. Let R be a commutative ring, and let M be an R-module. Then
the following are equivalent:
(1) M is Noetherian; that is, every submodule of M is Ô¨Ånitely generated.
(2) Every ascending chain of submodules of M stabilizes; that is, if
N1 ‚äÜ N2 ‚äÜ N3 ‚äÜ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑
is a chain of submodules of M , then ‚àÉi such that Ni = Ni+1 = Ni+2 = . . . .
(3) Every nonempty family of submodules of M has a maximal element w.r.t. in-
clusion.
The second condition listed here is called the ascending chain condition (a.c.c.)
for submodules. For M = R, Proposition 1.1 tells us (among other things) that a
ring is Noetherian if and only if the ascending chain condition holds for its ideals.
Proof. (1) =‚áí (2): Assume that M is Noetherian, and let

be a chain of submodules of M . Consider the union

this implies that M is Noetherian.
 
Noetherian rings are a very useful and Ô¨Çexible class of rings. In ¬ßIII.6.5 I
mentioned the important fact that every Ô¨Ånite-type algebra over a Noetherian ring is
Noetherian. ‚ÄòFinite-type (commutative) algebra‚Äô is just a fancy name for a quotient
of a polynomial ring (¬ßIII.6.5), so this is what the fact states:
Theorem 1.2. Let R be a Noetherian ring, and let J be an ideal of the polynomial
ring R[x1 , . . . , xn ]. Then the ring R[x1 , . . . , xn ]/J is Noetherian.
Note that Ô¨Ånite-type R algebras are (in general) very far from being Ô¨Ånitely
generated as modules over R (cf. again ¬ßIII.6.5), so it would be foolish to expect
them to be Noetherian as R-modules. The fact that they turn out to be Noetherian
as rings (that is, as modules over themselves) provides us with a huge class of
examples of Noetherian rings, among which are the rings of (classical) algebraic
geometry and number theory. Thus, entire Ô¨Åelds of mathematics are a little more
manageable thanks to Theorem 1.2.
The proof of this deep fact is surprisingly easy. By Exercise 1.1, it suÔ¨Éces to
prove that
R Noetherian =‚áí R[x1 , . . . , xn ] Noetherian;
and an immediate induction reduces the statement to the following particular case,
which carries a distinguished name:
Lemma 1.3 (Hilbert‚Äôs basis theorem). R Noetherian =‚áí R[x] Noetherian.
Proof. Assume R is Noetherian, and let I be an ideal of R[x]. We have to prove
that I is Ô¨Ånitely generated.
Recall that if f (x) = adxd + ad‚àí1xd‚àí1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + a0 ‚àà R[x] and ad = 0, then ad is
called the leading coeÔ¨Écient of f (x). Consider the following subset of R:
A = {0} ‚à™ {a ‚àà R | a is a leading coeÔ¨Écient of an element of I}.
It is clear that A is an ideal of R (Exercise 1.6); since R is Noetherian, A is Ô¨Ånitely
generated. Thus there exist elements f1 (x), . . . , fr (x) ‚àà I whose leading coeÔ¨Écients
a1 , . . . , ar generate A as an ideal of R.

1.2. Prime and irreducible elements. Let R be a (commutative) ring, and let
a, b ‚àà R. We say that a divides b, or that a is a divisor of b, or that b is a multiple
of a, if b ‚àà (a), that is,
(‚àÉc ‚àà R), b = ac.
We use the notation a | b.
Two elements a, b are associates if (a) = (b), that is, if a | b and b | a.
Lemma 1.5. Let a, b be nonzero elements of an integral domain R. Then a and b
are associates if and only if a = ub, for u a unit in R.
1. Chain conditions and existence of factorizations
 247
Proof. Assume a and b are associates. Then ‚àÉc, d ‚àà R such that
b = ac,
 a = bd;
therefore a = bd = acd, i.e.,
a(1 ‚àí cd) = 0.
Since cancellation by nonzero elements hold in integral domains, this implies cd = 1.
Thus c is a unit, as needed.
The converse is left to the reader.
 
Incidentally, here the reader sees why it is convenient to restrict our attention to
integral domains. This argument really shows that if (a) = (b) = (0) in an integral
domain, and b = ca, then c is necessarily a unit. Away from the comfortable
environment of integral domains, even such harmless-looking statements may fail:
in Z/6Z the classes [2]6 , [4]6 of 2 and 4 are associates according to our deÔ¨Ånition,
and [4]6 = [2]6 ¬∑ [2]6 , yet [2]6 is not a unit. However, [4]6 = [5]6 ¬∑ [2]6 and [5]6 is a
unit, so this is not a counterexample to Lemma 1.5. In fact, Lemma 1.5 may fail
over rings with ‚Äònon-harmless‚Äô zero-divisors (yes, there is such a notion).
The notions reviewed above generalize directly the corresponding notions in Z.
We are going to explore analogues of other common notions in Z, such as ‚Äòprimality‚Äô
and ‚Äòirreducibility‚Äô, in more general integral domains.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.6. Let R be an integral domain.
‚Ä¢ An element a ‚àà R is prime if the ideal (a) is prime; that is, a is not a unit
and (cf. Proposition III.4.11)
a | bc =‚áí (a | b
 or
 a | c).
‚Ä¢ An element a ‚àà R is irreducible if a is not a unit and
a = bc =‚áí (b is a unit or c is a unit).
Note that 0 is always reducible (integral domains are nonzero rings!). For
nonzero elements, there are useful alternative ways to think about the notion of
‚Äòirreducible‚Äô: a nonunit a = 0 is irreducible if and only if
‚Ä¢ a = bc implies that a is an associate of b or of c;
‚Ä¢ a = bc implies that (a) = (b) or (a) = (c) (Lemma 1.5);
‚Ä¢ (a) ‚äÜ (b) =‚áí (b) = (a) or (b) = (1) (Exercise 1.12);
‚Ä¢ (a) is maximal among proper principal ideals (rephrasing the previous point!).
It is important to realize that primality and irreducibility are not equivalent,
even for nonzero elements; this is somewhat counterintuitive since they are equiv-
alent in Z, as the reader should verify2 (Exercise 1.13). What is true in general is
that prime is stronger than irreducible:
Lemma 1.7. Let R be an integral domain, and let a ‚àà R be a nonzero prime
element. Then a is irreducible.
2 This fact will be fully explained by the general theory, so the reader should work this out
right away.
248

Proof. Since (a) is prime, (a) = (1); hence a is not a unit. If a = bc, then
bc = a ‚àà (a); therefore b ‚àà (a) or c ‚àà (a) since (a) is prime. Assuming without
loss of generality b ‚àà (a), we have (b) ‚äÜ (a). On the other hand a = bc implies
(a) ‚äÜ (b): hence (a) = (b), that is, a and b are associates, as needed.
 
We will soon see under what circumstances the converse statement holds.
1.3. Factorization into irreducibles; domains with factorizations.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.8. Let R be an integral domain. An element r ‚àà R has a factorization
(or decomposition) into irreducibles if there exist irreducible elements q1, . . . , qn
such that r = q1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ qn .
This factorization is unique if the elements qi are determined by r up to order
and associates, that is, if whenever
r = q1  ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ qm
 
is another factorization of r into irreducibles, then m = n and qi is an associate
of qi after (possibly) shuÔ¨Ñing the factors.
 
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.9. An integral domain R is a domain with factorizations (or ‚Äòfactor-
izations exist in R‚Äô ) if every nonzero, nonunit element r ‚àà R has a factorization
into irreducibles.
 
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.10. An integral domain R is factorial, or a unique factorization
domain (abbreviated UFD), if every nonzero, nonunit element r ‚àà R has a unique
factorization into irreducibles.
 
The terminology introduced in DeÔ¨Ånition 1.9 does not appear to be too stan-
dard; by contrast, UFDs are famous.
We will study the unique factorization condition in ¬ß2. For now, it seems
worth spending a little time contemplating the mere existence of factorizations.
Interestingly, this condition is implied by an ascending chain condition, for a special
class of ideals.
Proposition 1.11. Let R be an integral domain, and let r be a nonzero, nonunit
element of R. Assume that every ascending chain of principal ideals
(r) ‚äÜ (r1 ) ‚äÜ (r2 ) ‚äÜ (r3 ) ‚äÜ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑
stabilizes. Then r has a factorization into irreducibles.
Proof. Assume that r does not have a factorization into irreducible elements. In
particular, r is itself not irreducible; thus ‚àÉr1 , s1 ‚àà R such that r = r1 s1 and
(r)  (r1 ), (r)  (s1 ). If both r1 , s1 have factorizations into irreducibles, the
product of these factorizations gives a factorization of r; thus we may assume that
(e.g.) r1 does not have a factorization into irreducibles. Therefore we have
(r)  (r1 )
and r1 does not have a factorization; iterating this argument constructs an inÔ¨Ånitely
increasing chain
(r)  (r1 )  (r2 )  (r3 )  ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ,
Exercises
 249
contradicting our hypothesis.
 
Thus, factorizations exist in integral domains in which the ascending chain con-
dition holds for principal ideals. This has the following immediate and convenient
consequence:
Corollary 1.12. Let R be a Noetherian domain. Then factorizations exist in R.
Proof. By Proposition 1.1, Noetherian domains satisfy the ascending chain condi-
tion for all ideals.
 
Corollary 1.12 veriÔ¨Åes part of the picture presented at the beginning of the
chapter: the class of Noetherian domains is contained in the class of domains with
factorization. This inclusion is proper: for instance, the standard example of a
non-Noetherian ring,
Z[x1 , x2 , x3 , . . . ],
(Example III.6.5) does have factorizations. Indeed, every given polynomial f ‚àà
Z[x1 , x2 , . . . ] involves only Ô¨Ånitely many variables3, so it belongs to a subring
Z[x1 , ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ , xn ] isomorphic to an ordinary polynomial ring over Z; further, this sub-
ring contains every divisor of f . It follows easily (cf. Exercise 1.15) that the ascend-
ing chain condition for principal ideals holds in Z[x1 , x2 , x3, . . . ], because it holds
in Z[x1 , . . . , xn ] (since this ring is Noetherian, by Hilbert‚Äôs basis theorem).

2. UFDs, PIDs, Euclidean domains
2.1. Irreducible factors and greatest common divisor. An integral domain
R is a UFD if factorizations exist in R and are unique in the sense of DeÔ¨Ånition 1.8.
Thus, in a UFD all elements (other than 0 and the units) determine a multiset
(a set of elements ‚Äòwith multiplicity‚Äô; cf. ¬ßI.1.1) of irreducible factors, determined
up to the associate relation. We can also agree that units have no factors; that is,
the corresponding multiset is ‚àÖ.
The following trivial remark is at the root of most elementary facts about
UFDs, such as the characterization of Theorem 2.5:
Lemma 2.1. Let R be a UFD, and let a, b, c be nonzero elements of R. Then
‚Ä¢ (a) ‚äÜ (b) ‚áê‚áí the multiset of irreducible factors of b is contained in the
multiset of irreducible factors of a;
‚Ä¢ a and b are associates (that is, (a) = (b)) ‚áê‚áí the two multisets coincide;
‚Ä¢ the irreducible factors of a product bc are the collection of all irreducible factors
of b and of c.
The proof is left to the reader (Exercise 2.1). The advantage of working in
a UFD resides in the fact that ring-theoretic statements about elements of the
ring often reduce to straightforward set-theoretic statements about multisets of
irreducible elements, by means of Lemma 2.1.
252
 V. Irreducibility and factorization in integral domains
One important instance of this mechanism is the existence of greatest common
divisors. I have liberally used this notion (at least for integers) in previous chapters;
now we can appreciate it from a more technical perspective.
DeÔ¨Ånition 2.2. Let R be an integral domain, and let a, b ‚àà R. An element d ‚àà R
is a greatest common divisor (often abbreviated ‚Äògcd‚Äô) of a and b if (a, b) ‚äÜ (d) and
(d) is the smallest principal ideal in R with this property.
 
In other words, d is a gcd of a and b if d | a, d | b, and
c | a , c | b =‚áí c | d.
This deÔ¨Ånition is immediately extended to any Ô¨Ånite number of elements.
Note that greatest common divisors are not deÔ¨Åned uniquely by this prescrip-
tion: if d is a greatest common divisor of a and b, so is every associate of d. Thus,
the notation ‚Äògcd(a, b)‚Äô should only be used for the associate class formed by all
greatest common divisors of a, b. Of course, language is often (harmlessly) abused
on this point. For example, the fact that we can talk about the greatest common
divisor of two integers is due to the fact that in Z there is a convenient way to choose
a distinguished element in each class of associate integers (that is, the nonnegative
one).
Also note that greatest common divisors need not exist (cf. Exercise 2.5); but
they do exist in UFDs:
Lemma 2.3. Let R be a UFD, and let a, b be nonzero elements of R. Then a, b
have a greatest common divisor.
Proof. We can write
a = uq1 Œ±1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ qr Œ±r ,
 b = vq1 Œ≤1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ qr Œ≤r
where u and v are units, the elements qi are irreducible, qi is not an associate of qj
for i = j, and Œ±i ‚â• 0, Œ≤i ‚â• 0 (so that the multisets of irreducible factors of a,
resp., b, consist of those qi for which Œ±i > 0, resp., Œ≤i > 0; the units u, v are
included since the irreducible factors are only deÔ¨Åned up to the associate relation).
I claim that
d = q1
 min(Œ±1 ,Œ≤1
 )
 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ qr min(Œ±r ,Œ≤r )
is a gcd of a and b. Indeed, d is clearly a divisor of a and b; and if c also divides
a and b, then the multiset of factors of c must be contained in both multisets of
factors for a and b (by Lemma 2.1); that is,
c = wq1 Œ≥1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ qr Œ≥r
with w a unit and Œ≥i ‚â§ Œ±i , Œ≥i ‚â§ Œ≤ i . This implies Œ≥i ‚â§ min(Œ±i , Œ≤i ), and hence c | d
(again by Lemma 2.1), as needed.
 
Of course the argument given in the proof generalizes one of the standard
ways to compute greatest common divisors in Z: Ô¨Ånd smallest exponents in prime
factorizations. But note that this is not the only way to compute the gcd in Z;
we will come back to this point in a moment. In fact, greatest common divisors
in Z have properties that should not be expected in a more general domain: for
2. UFDs, PIDs, Euclidean domains

example, the result of Exercise II.2.13 does not generalize to arbitrary domains
(and not even to arbitrary UFDs), as the reader will check in Exercise 2.4.
2.2. Characterization of UFDs. It is easy to construct integral domains where
unique factorization fails. The diligent reader has already analyzed one example
(Exercise 1.17); for another, in the domain5
C[x, y, z, w]
R =
(xw ‚àí yz)
the (classes of the) elements x, y, z, w are irreducible and not associates of one
another; since xw ‚àí yz = 0 in R, the element r = xw has two distinct factorizations
into irreducibles: r = xw = yz.
Note that this ring is Noetherian, by Theorem 1.2 (and in particular factor-
izations do exist in R). Thus, there are Noetherian integral domains that are not
UFDs.
Also note that this ring provides an example in which the converse to Lemma 1.7
does not hold: indeed, (the class of) x is irreducible, but the quotient
'
C[x, y, z, w]
 C[x, y, z, w]
 C[x, y, z, w]
(x) ‚àº
 =

(xw ‚àí yz)

is not an integral domain (because y = 0, z = 0, and yet yz = 0 in this ring); that
is, x is not prime.
In fact, and maybe a little surprisingly, the issue of unique factorization is
inextricably linked with the relation between primality and irreducibility. Indeed,
the ‚Äòconverse‚Äô to Lemma 1.7 does hold in UFDs:
Lemma 2.4. Let R be a UFD, and let a be an irreducible element of R. Then a
is prime.
Proof. The element a is not a unit, by deÔ¨Ånition of irreducible. Assume bc ‚àà (a):
thus (bc) ‚äÜ (a), and by Lemma 2.1 the irreducible factors of a, that is, a itself,
must be among the factors of b or of c. We have b ‚àà (a) in the Ô¨Årst case and c ‚àà (a)
in the second. This shows that (a) is a prime ideal, as needed.
 
In fact, more is true. Provided that the ascending chain condition for principal
ideals holds, then UFDs are characterized by the equivalence between irreducibility
and primality.
Theorem 2.5. An integral domain R is a UFD if and only if
‚Ä¢ the a.c.c. for principal ideals holds in R and
‚Ä¢ every irreducible element of R is prime.
Proof. ( =‚áí ) Assume that R is a UFD. Lemma 2.4 shows that irreducible ele-
ments of R are prime. To prove that the a.c.c. for principal ideals holds, consider
an ascending chain
(r1 )  (r2 )  (r3 )  ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ .
5 In algebraic geometry, this is the ring of a ‚Äòquadric cone in A4‚Äô. The vertex of this cone (at
the origin) is a singular point, and this has to do with the fact that R is not a UFD.
254
 V. Irreducibility and factorization in integral domains
By Lemma 2.1, this chain determines a corresponding descending chain of multisets
of irreducible factors. A descending chain of Ô¨Ånite multisets clearly stabilizes, and it
follows (by Lemma 2.1 again) that (ri ) = (ri+1 ) = (ri+2 ) = . . . for large enough i.
( ‚áê= ) Now assume that R satisÔ¨Åes the a.c.c. for principal ideals and irre-
ducibles are prime. Proposition 1.11 implies that factorizations exist in R; we have
 to verify uniqueness. Let q1 , . . . , qm and q1 , . . . , qn be irreducible elements of R,
and assume
q1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ qm = q1  ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ qn  .
Then q1  ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ qn  ‚àà (q1 ), and (q1 ) is a prime ideal (by hypothesis); thus qi  ‚àà (q1)
for some i, which we may assume to be 1 after changing the order of the factors.
Therefore q1  = uq1 for some u ‚àà R. Since q1  is irreducible and q1 is not a unit,
 
necessarily u is a unit. Thus q1 and q1 are associates. Canceling q1 and replacing q2by uq2, we Ô¨Ånd
q2 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ qm = q2  ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ qn  .
Repeating this process matches all factors one by one. It is clear that m = n,
because otherwise we will obtain 1 = a product of irreducibles, contradicting the
fact that irreducibles are not units.
 
Theorem 2.5 is a pleasant statement, but it does not make it particularly easy to
check whether a given ring is in fact a UFD. The situation is not unlike that with
Noetherian rings: the a.c.c. is a sharp equivalent formulation of the Noetherian
condition, but in practice one relies more often on other tools, such as Hilbert‚Äôs
basis theorem, in order to establish that a given ring is Noetherian. Does an analog
of Hilbert‚Äôs basis theorem hold for unique factorization domains?
Answering this question requires some preparatory work, and we will come back
to it in ¬ß4.
2.3. PID =‚áí UFD. There are simple ways to produce examples of UFDs.
Recall (from ¬ßIII.4) that a principal ideal domain (abbreviated PID) is an integral
domain in which every ideal is principal. In ¬ßIII.4 we have observed that
‚Ä¢ PIDs are Noetherian.
‚Ä¢ Z and k[x] (where k is a Ô¨Åeld) are PIDs.
‚Ä¢ If R is a PID and a, b ‚àà R, then d is a greatest common divisor for a and b if
and only if (a, b) = (d). In particular, if d is a greatest common divisor of a
and b in a PID R, then d is a linear combination of a and b: ‚àÉr, s ‚àà R such
that d = ra + sb.
‚Ä¢ If I is a nonzero ideal in a PID, then I is prime if and only if it is maximal.
We now add one important point to this list:
Proposition 2.6. If R is a PID, then it is a UFD.
Proof. Let R be a PID. The a.c.c. (for principal ideals, as all ideals in R are prin-
cipal!) holds in R since PIDs are Noetherian. We verify that irreducible elements
are prime in R, which implies that R is a UFD by Theorem 2.5.
2. UFDs, PIDs, Euclidean domains
 255
Let a ‚àà R be an irreducible element. Ideals generated by irreducible elements
are maximal among principal ideals (Exercise 1.12), hence (a) is a maximal ideal
in R as all ideals in R are principal. Since maximal ideals are prime it follows that
(a) is prime, as needed.
 
In particular, k[x] is a UFD if k is a Ô¨Åeld, and Z is a UFD‚Äîwhich hopefully
will not come as a big surprise to our readers6. Note that at this point we have
recovered the fact stated in Exercise 1.13 in full glory.
Proposition 2.6 justiÔ¨Åes another feature of the picture given at the beginning
of this chapter: the class of PIDs is contained in the class of UFDs. We will soon
see that the inclusion is proper, that is, that there are UFDs which are not PIDs;
for example, Z[x] is not a PID (Exercise 2.12), yet
Z[x] is a unique factorization domain
as we will soon be able to prove (Theorem 4.14). In fact, there are UFDs which
are not Noetherian, as represented in the picture; however, these examples are best
discussed after more material has been developed (Example 4.18).
The reader can already get a feel for the gap between UFD and PID, by con-
templating the fact (recalled above and essentially tautological) that, in a PID,
greatest common divisors of a, b are linear combinations of a and b. This is a
very strong requirement: for example, it characterizes PIDs among Noetherian do-
mains, as the reader will check (Exercise 2.7); it does not hold in general UFDs
(Exercise 2.4).
2.4. Euclidean domain =‚áí PID. The excellent properties of Z and k[x] (where
k is a Ô¨Åeld) make these rings even more special than PIDs: they are Euclidean
domains.
Informally, Euclidean domains are rings in which one may perform a ‚Äòdivision
with remainder‚Äô: this is the case for Z and for k[x], as observed in ¬ßIII.4. The point
is that in both Z and k[x] one can deÔ¨Åne a notion of ‚Äòsize‚Äô of an element: |n| for an
integer n and deg f (x) for a polynomial f (x). In both cases, one has control over
the size of the ‚Äòremainder‚Äô in a division. The deÔ¨Ånition of Euclidean domain simply
abstracts this mechanism.
For the purpose of this discussion7 , a valuation on an integral domain R is any
function v : R  {0} ‚Üí Z‚â•0 .
DeÔ¨Ånition 2.7. A Euclidean valuation on an integral domain R is a valuation
satisfying the following property 8 : for all a ‚àà R and all nonzero b ‚àà R there exist
q, r ‚àà R such that
a = qb + r,
with either r = 0 or v(r) < v(b). An integral domain R is a Euclidean domain if it
admits a Euclidean valuation.
 
6This fact is known as the fundamental theorem of arithmetic.
7 Entire libraries have been written on the subject of valuations, studying a more precise
notion than what is needed here.
8 It is not uncommon to also require that v(ab) ‚â• v(b) for all nonzero a, b ‚àà R; but this is not
needed in the considerations that follow, and cf. Exercise 2.15.
256
 V. Irreducibility and factorization in integral domains
We say that q is the quotient of the division and r is the remainder. Division
with remainder in Z and in k[x] (where k is a Ô¨Åeld) provide examples, so that Z
and k[x] are Euclidean domains.
Proposition 2.8. Let R be a Euclidean domain. Then R is a PID.
The proof is modeled after the instances encountered for Z (Proposition III.4.4)
and k[x] (which the reader has hopefully worked out in Exercise III.4.4).
Proof. Let I be an ideal of R; we have to prove that I is principal. If I = {0},
there is nothing to show; therefore, assume I = {0}. The valuation maps the
nonzero elements of I to a subset of Z‚â•0 ; let b ‚àà I be an element with the smallest
valuation. Then I claim that I = (b); therefore I is principal, as needed. Since
clearly (b) ‚äÜ I, we only need to verify that I ‚äÜ (b).
For this, let a ‚àà I and apply division with remainder: we have
a = qb + r
for some q, r in R, with r = 0 or v(r) < v(b). But
r = a ‚àí qb ‚àà I :
by the minimality of v(b) among nonzero elements of I, we cannot have v(r) < v(b).
Therefore r = 0, showing that a = qb ‚àà (b), as needed.
 
Proposition 2.8 justiÔ¨Åes one more feature of the picture at the beginning of the
chapter: the class of Euclidean domains is contained in the class of principal ideal
domains.
This inclusion is proper, as suggested in the picture. Producing an explicit
example of a PID which is not a Euclidean domain is not so easy, but the gap
between PIDs and Euclidean domains can in fact be described very sharply: PID
may be characterized as domains satisfying a weaker requirement than ‚Äòdivision
with remainder‚Äô.
More precisely, a ‚ÄòDedekind-Hasse valuation‚Äô is a valuation v such that ‚àÄa, b,
either (a, b) = (b) (that is, b divides a) or there exists r ‚àà (a, b) such that v(r) < v(b).
This latter condition amounts to requiring that there exist q, s ‚àà R such that
as = bq + r with v(r) < v(b); hence a Euclidean valuation (for which we may in fact
choose s = 1) is a Dedekind-Hasse valuation. It is not hard to show that an integral
domain is a PID if and only if it admits a Dedekind-Hasse valuation
 ‚àö
 (Exercise 2.21).
For example, this can be used to show that the ring Z[(1 + ‚àí19)/2] is a PID: the
norm considered in Exercise 2.18 in order to prove that this ring is not a Euclidean
domain turns out to be a Dedekind-Hasse valuation9 . Thus, this ring gives an
example of a PID that is not a Euclidean domain.
One excellent feature of Euclidean domains, and the one giving them their
names, is the presence of an eÔ¨Äective algorithm computing greatest common divi-
sors: the Euclidean algorithm. As Euclidean domains are PIDs, and hence UFDs,
we know that they do have greatest common divisors. However, the ‚Äòalgorithm‚Äô
9 This boils down to a case-by-case analysis, which I am happily leaving to my most patient
readers.
2. UFDs, PIDs, Euclidean domains
 257
obtained by distilling the proof of Lemma 2.3 is highly impractical: if we had to
factor two integers a, b in order to compute their gcd, this would make it essentially
impossible (with current technologies and factorization algorithms) for integers of
a few hundred digits. The Euclidean algorithm bypasses the necessity of factoriza-
tion: greatest common divisors of thousand-digit integers may be computed in a
fraction of a second.
The key lemma on which the algorithm is based is the following trivial general
fact:
Lemma 2.9. Let a = bq + r in a ring R. Then (a, b) = (b, r).
Proof. Indeed, r = a ‚àí bq ‚àà (a, b), proving (b, r) ‚äÜ (a, b); and a = bq + r ‚àà (b, r),
proving (a, b) ‚äÜ (b, r).
 
In particular,
(‚àÄc ‚àà R),
 (a, b) ‚äÜ (c) ‚áê‚áí (b, r) ‚äÜ (c);
that is, the set of common divisors of a, b and the set of common divisors of b, r
coincide. Therefore,
Corollary 2.10. Assume a = bq + r. Then a, b have a gcd if and only if b, r have
a gcd, and in this case gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, r).
Of course ‚Äògcd(a, b) = gcd(b, r)‚Äô means that the two classes of associate elements
coincide.
These considerations hold over any integral domain; assume now that R is a
Euclidean domain. Then we can use division with remainder to gain some control
over the remainders r. Given two elements a, b in R, with b = 0, we can apply
division with remainder repeatedly:
a = bq1 + r1 ,
b = r1 q2 + r2 ,
r1 = r2 q3 + r3 ,
¬∑¬∑¬∑
as long as the remainder ri is nonzero.
Claim 2.11. This process terminates: that is, rN = 0 for some N .
Proof. Each line in the table is a division with remainder. If no ri were zero, we
would have an inÔ¨Ånite decreasing sequence
v(b) > v(r1) > v(r2 ) > v(r3) > ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑
of nonnegative integers, which is nonsense.
 

Thus the table of divisions with remainders must be as follows: letting r0 = b,
a = r0 q1 + r1 ,
b = r1 q2 + r2 ,
r1 = r2 q3 + r3 ,
¬∑¬∑¬∑
rN ‚àí3 = rN ‚àí2 qN ‚àí1 + rN ‚àí1 ,
rN ‚àí2 = rN ‚àí1 qN
with rN ‚àí1 = 0.
Proposition 2.12. With notation as above, rN ‚àí1 is a gcd of a, b.
Proof. By Corollary 2.10,
gcd(a, b) = gcd(b, r1 ) = gcd(r1 , r2 ) = ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ = gcd(rN ‚àí2 , rN ‚àí1 ).

fore rN ‚àí1 is a gcd for rN ‚àí2 and rN ‚àí1 , hence for a and b, as needed.

The ring of integers and the polynomial ring over a Ô¨Åeld are both Euclidean
domains. Fields are Euclidean domains (as represented in the picture at the be-
ginning of the chapter), but not for a very interesting reason: the remainder of the
division by a nonzero element in a Ô¨Åeld is always zero, so every function qualiÔ¨Åes
as a ‚ÄòEuclidean valuation‚Äô for trivial reasons.
We will study another interesting Euclidean domain later in this chapter (¬ß6.2).

3. Intermezzo: Zorn‚Äôs lemma
3.1. Set theory, reprise. We leave ring theory for a moment and take a little
detour to contemplate an issue from set theory. As remarked at the very outset,
only naive set theory is used in this book; all set-theoretic operations we have used
so far are nothing more than a formalization of intuitive ideas regarding collections
of objects. However, I will occasionally need to refer to a less ‚Äòintuitively obvious‚Äô
set-theoretic statement: for example, this statement is needed in order to show that
every proper ideal in a ring is contained in a maximal ideal (Proposition 3.5).
This set-theoretic fact is Zorn‚Äôs lemma. An order relation on a set Z is a
relation  which is reÔ¨Çexive, transitive, and antisymmetric: the Ô¨Årst two terms are
familiar to the reader, and the third means that
(‚àÄa, b ‚àà Z),
 a  b and b  a =‚áí a = b.
Typical prototypes are the ‚â§ relation on Z or the inclusion relation ‚äÜ among subsets
of a given set. We use a ‚â∫ b to denote a  b and b = a.
A pair (Z, ), consisting of a set Z and an order relation  on Z, is called
a poset, for partially ordered set. The qualiÔ¨Åer ‚Äòpartially‚Äô is not necessary, but
convenient, as it reminds us that if a, b ‚àà Z, then it is not necessarily the case
that a  b or b  a: for example, ‚äÜ does not satisfy this additional requirement
in general (while (Z, ‚â§) does). An order is total if it does satisfy this additional
requirement. A totally ordered set is not called a ‚Äòtoset‚Äô, as would seem reasonable,
but rather a chain.
An element m of a poset Z is maximal if nothing comes properly ‚Äòafter‚Äô it in
the order:
(‚àÄa ‚àà Z), m  a =‚áí m = a.
For example, maximal ideals in a ring are maximal elements in the set of proper
ideals, that is, ideals = (1) (by Proposition III.4.11). An upper bound for a subset S
of a poset Z is an element u ‚àà Z coming after every element of S:
(‚àÄa ‚àà S),
 a  u.
262
 V. Irreducibility and factorization in integral domains
Notions of ‚Äòsmallest‚Äô (or, rather, ‚Äòleast‚Äô) or ‚Äòlargest‚Äô are deÔ¨Åned in the evident
way.
Posets may or may not have maximal elements, upper bounds, etc.: for exam-
ple, the family of Ô¨Ånite subsets of an inÔ¨Ånite set does not have maximal elements.
All this terminology comes together in the following statement:
Lemma 3.1 (Zorn‚Äôs lemma). Let Z be a nonempty poset. Assume that every chain
in Z has an upper bound in Z; then there exists a maximal element in Z.
The status of Zorn‚Äôs lemma is peculiar: on the one hand, it is complex enough
that no one I know of Ô¨Ånds it ‚Äòintuitively clear‚Äô; on the other hand, it turns out to
be logically equivalent 10 to the axiom of choice, which does look reasonable to most
people, and to the ‚Äòwell-ordering theorem‚Äô, which I Ô¨Ånd intuitively unreasonable.
I will not prove all these equivalences (the diligent reader will prove them in the
exercises and should in any case have no trouble locating more detailed proofs);
but I will attempt to describe the situation in general terms and explain why the
unreasonable statement implies the other two.
The axiom of choice states that if F is a family of disjoint nonempty subsets of
a set Z, then we can form a new set by selecting one element x from each X ‚àà F .
This may sound reasonable, but it raises rather subtle points: for example, it can
be shown that this axiom is independent of the other axioms of (Zermelo-Fr√§nkel)
set theory. Also, it has disturbingly counterintuitive consequences, such as the
Banach-Tarski paradox11 .
The subtlety of the axiom of choice boils down to the following question: how
does one choose the element x? This is not controversial if Z (and hence F ) is
Ô¨Ånite, but, not surprisingly, it becomes an issue if Z is inÔ¨Ånite.
A suitable order relation on Z would come in handy here: if  is an order
relation on Z such that every nonempty subset of Z has a least element, then we
could simply let x be the least element of X for each X ‚àà F . We say that Z
is well-ordered by , or that  is a well-ordering on Z, if this is the case. (The
abbreviation woset is also used in the literature, but not very often.) For example,
the set Z>0 of positive numbers is well-ordered by ‚â§; this fact is called the well-
ordering principle.
Thus, the statement of the axiom of choice is really ‚Äòclear‚Äô if the set Z is the
set Z>0 of positive numbers. It may seem somewhat less so for the set Z, since Z
is not well-ordered by ‚â§; however, it takes a moment (Exercise 3.4) to construct a
diÔ¨Äerent relation on Z, making Z a well-ordered set. Thus, the axiom of choice is
also completely transparent for Z = Z or any countable set (like Q) for that matter.
The well-ordering principle is at the basis of proofs by induction: in fact (cf. Ex-
ercise 3.5) it is equivalent to the so-called principle of induction. More is true,
however. If (Z, ) is any woset, then we can consider the following ‚Äòprinciple of
induction‚Äô on Z:
10 Equivalent in the sense that each can be derived from the other together with the other
axioms of ‚ÄòZermelo-Fr√§nkel‚Äô set theory.
11 One can subdivide a solid ball of radius 1 into Ô¨Ånitely many pieces, then reassemble them
after rotations and translations in 3-space and obtain two balls of radius 1.
3. Intermezzo: Zorn‚Äôs lemma
 263
Let S ‚äÜ Z be a subset such that ‚àÄa ‚àà Z
((‚àÄb ‚àà Z),
 b ‚â∫ a =‚áí b ‚àà S) =‚áí a ‚àà S;
then S = Z.
That is, Z is the only set S with the property that a ‚àà S if all b ‚â∫ a are in S.
(Note that the least element a of Z is automatically in S, since the condition on
all b ‚â∫ a is vacuously true in this case.)
The reader will recognize that for Z = Z>0 with the relation ‚â§, this is the
ordinary principle of induction.
Claim 3.2. Let Z be any woset. Then the principle of induction holds for Z.
Proof. Let S ‚äÜ Z be a subset with the property given above, and assume S  Z.
Then the complement T of S in Z is nonempty; hence it has a least element a.
Now if b ‚â∫ a, then necessarily b ‚àà S (otherwise b ‚àà T , contradicting the fact that a
is the least element in T ). But the property of S then forces a ‚àà S, contradiction.
Therefore T is empty; i.e., S = Z.
 
This is remarkable, since it extends induction to uncountable sets12 , provided
a well-ordering is available.
For example, if we had a well-ordering on R, then we could prove a statement
about all reals ‚Äòby induction‚Äô. Many people Ô¨Ånd this somewhat counterintuitive,
and hence so seems the following amazing claim:
Theorem 3.3 (Well-ordering theorem). Every set admits a well-ordering.
As argued above, this implies that the axiom of choice holds on every set: if you
accept the well-ordering theorem, the statement of the axiom of choice becomes as
evident for every set as it is for the set of positive integers. The catch is of course
that the axiom of choice is used in the proof of the well-ordering theorem; in fact,
the statements are equivalent.
In any case, the statement of the well-ordering theorem is easy to absorb (even
if perhaps less ‚Äòintuitively clear‚Äô than the axiom of choice). The well-ordering
theorem is equivalent to Zorn‚Äôs lemma, since they are both equivalent to the axiom
of choice. The good news is that the derivation of Zorn‚Äôs lemma from the well-
ordering theorem is reasonably straightforward:
Well-ordering theorem =‚áí Zorn‚Äôs lemma. Let (Z, ‚â§) be a nonempty poset such
that every chain in Z has an upper bound in Z. By the well-ordering theorem, there
is a well-ordering13  on Z. DeÔ¨Åne a function f from Z to the power set of Z as

tion.
13 Watch out: we are considering two orderings on Z: ‚â§ (about which we want to say
something) and  (about which we know something already).

Zorn‚Äôs lemma is the key to several basic results in algebra, the Ô¨Årst of which we
are about to encounter; the reader will sample a few more results in the exercises.
The reader will also encounter equally basic applications of Zorn‚Äôs lemma (or of
other manifestations of the axiom of choice) in other Ô¨Åelds: for example TychonoÔ¨Ä‚Äôs
theorem in topology and the Hahn-Banach theorem in functional analysis.
3.2. Application: Existence of maximal ideals. Recall (¬ßIII.4.3) that an
ideal m of a ring R is maximal if and only if R/m is a Ô¨Åeld, if and only if no
other ideal stands between m and R = (1), that is, if and only if m is maximal with
respect to inclusion, in the family of proper ideals of R. It is not obvious from this
deÔ¨Ånition that maximal ideals exist, but they do:
Proposition 3.5. Let I = (1) be a proper ideal of a commutative ring R. Then
there exists a maximal ideal m of R containing I.
14 The existence of maximal totally ordered subsets is known as the HausdorÔ¨Ä maximal prin-
ciple; it is also equivalent to the axiom of choice.
Exercises
 265
This is immediate if R is Noetherian, by applying condition (3) from Propo-
sition 1.1 to the family of proper ideals of R containing I. The argument for
arbitrary rings is a classic application of Zorn‚Äôs lemma. In fact, this is the Ô¨Årst
application given by M. Zorn in his 1935 article introducing a ‚Äòmaximum principle‚Äô
(now known as Zorn‚Äôs lemma). The result had earlier been proven by Krull, using
the well-ordering theorem.
Proof. The set I of proper ideals of R containing I is ordered by inclusion. Then
let C be a chain of proper ideals, and consider

U :=
 J.
J‚ààC
I claim that U is a proper ideal containing I; hence it is an upper bound for C
in I . This proves that every chain in I has an upper bound, and it follows that
I has maximal elements, by Zorn‚Äôs lemma.
To verify my claim, it is clear that U contains I and that it is an ideal (for
example, if a, b ‚àà U , then ‚àÉJ ‚àà C such that a, b ‚àà J; hence a ¬± b ‚àà J, and therefore
a ¬± b ‚àà U ). We have to check that U is proper. But if U = (1), then 1 ‚àà J for some
J ‚àà I , contradicting the fact that I consists of proper ideals.
 
Note that this argument relies crucially on the fact that R has a multiplicative
unit 1, and indeed the stated fact is not true in general for rings without 1 (Exer-
cise 3.9). Also, the use of Zorn‚Äôs lemma is not just a convenient trick; the statement
is known to be equivalent to the axiom of choice, by work of W. Hodges.

4. Unique factorization in polynomial rings
We now return to regular programming and study unique factorization in polyno-
mial rings; we will Ô¨Ånally establish the fact (already hinted at) that R[x] is a UFD
if R is a UFD.

 V. Irreducibility and factorization in integral domains
Among the necessary preparatory work we also discuss the Ô¨Åeld of fractions of
an integral domain; this is a particular instance of the process of localization of a
ring (or a module) at a multiplicative subset, which the diligent reader will explore
a little more in the exercises.
4.1. Primitivity and content; Gauss‚Äôs lemma. One issue that we have en-
countered already, and will encounter again, is the description of the ideals of
the polynomial ring R[x], given enough information about R. For example, we
have proved that the ideals of k[x] are principal if k is a Ô¨Åeld, and Hilbert‚Äôs basis
theorem shows that all ideals of R[x] are Ô¨Ånitely generated if all ideals of R are
(Exercise III.4.4 and Lemma 1.3).
An even more naive observation is simply that every ideal I of R generates an
ideal of R[x]:
IR[x] := {a0 + a1 x + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + adxd ‚àà R[x] | ‚àÄi, ai ‚àà I}.
Lemma 4.1. Let R be a ring, and let I be an ideal of R. Then
R[x] ‚àº = R

The proof of this lemma is a standard application of the Ô¨Årst isomorphism

Corollary 4.2. If I is a prime ideal of R, then IR[x] is prime in R[x].
Proof. If I is prime in R, then R/I is an integral domain; hence so is R[x]/IR[x] ‚àº

(R/I)[x], and therefore IR[x] is prime in R[x].

DeÔ¨Ånition 4.3. Let R be a commutative ring, and let

be a polynomial.
‚Ä¢ f is very primitive if for all prime ideals p of R, f ‚àà pR[x].
‚Ä¢ f is primitive if for all principal prime ideals p of R, f ‚àà pR[x].

The notion of ‚Äòprimitive‚Äô polynomial is standard, but it is not usually pre-

hope to ward oÔ¨Ä any possible confusion by being rather explicit on this point. Very
primitive polynomials are primitive, but the converse does not hold in general, even
for UFDs (cf. Exercise 4.3).
Perhaps the most important fact about ‚Äòprimitivity‚Äô is the following easy re-
mark.

Lemma 4.4. Let R be a commutative ring. Then for f, g ‚àà R[x]
f g is primitive ‚áê‚áí both f and g are primitive.
Proof. This is an easy consequence of Corollary 4.2:
f g primitive ‚áê‚áí ‚àÄp prime and principal in R, f g ‚àà pR[x]
‚áê‚áí ‚àÄp prime and principal in R, f ‚àà pR[x] and g ‚àà pR[x]

since pR[x] is prime if p is a prime ideal.

The analogous equivalence holds for very primitive polynomials (Exercise 4.4).
Lemma 4.4 will be ultimately responsible for the fact that R[x] is a UFD if R is
a UFD, which is my main objective in this section. If R is a UFD, the notion of
‚Äòprimitive‚Äô has the following interpretation; I accompany it with the ‚Äòvery primitive‚Äô
case, for comparison.
Lemma 4.5. Let R be a commutative ring and f = a0 + a1 x + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + a d xd ‚àà R[x]
as above.
‚Ä¢ f is very primitive if and only if (a0 , . . . , ad) = (1).
‚Ä¢ If R is a UFD, then f is primitive if and only if gcd(a0 , . . . , ad ) = 1.
Proof. If (a0 , . . . , ad ) = (1), then no prime ideal can contain all coeÔ¨Écients ai ,
and it follows that f is very primitive. Conversely, if f is very primitive, then the
coeÔ¨Écients of f are not all contained in any one prime ideal, and in particular they
are not all contained in any maximal ideal (since maximal ideals are prime). Thus,
(a0, . . . , ad ) = (1) in this case, since every proper ideal is contained in a maximal
ideal by Proposition 3.5. This proves the Ô¨Årst point.
For the second point note that, in a UFD, gcd(a0 , . . . , ad ) = 1 if and only if
there exists an irreducible element q ‚àà R such that (a0 , . . . , ad ) ‚äÜ (q). As (q) is
then prime (by Lemma 2.4), the second point follows as well.
 
With this in mind, in order to capitalize on Lemma 4.4, it is convenient to give
a name to the gcd of the coeÔ¨Écients of a polynomial. I now assume that R is a
UFD, since this is the case of greatest interest in the applications.
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.6. Let R be a UFD. The content of a nonzero polynomial f ‚àà R[x],
denoted contf , is the gcd of its coeÔ¨Écients.
 
Of course the content of a polynomial is only deÔ¨Åned up to units. I Ô¨Ånd this
ambiguity distasteful. What is uniquely determined is the principal ideal generated
by the content of f , which I will denote
(contf ) :
thus f is primitive precisely when (contf ) = (1). I will take a somewhat stubborn
approach and deal with principal ideals throughout, rather with individual (but
only deÔ¨Åned up to unit) elements. The reader should keep in mind that ideals may
be multiplied (cf. ¬ßIII.4.1), and if (a), (b) are principal ideals, then their product
(a)(b) is the principal ideal (ab).
270
 V. Irreducibility and factorization in integral domains
The proof of the following remarks is immediate from the deÔ¨Ånition; hence it
is left to the reader (Exercise 4.5):
Lemma 4.7. Let R be a UFD, and let f ‚àà R[x]. Then
‚Ä¢ (f ) = (contf )(f ), where f is primitive;
‚Ä¢ if (f ) = (c)(g), with c ‚àà R and g primitive, then (c) = (cont f ).
In view of its consequences, the following fact is rather profound, and it there-
fore deserves a name. Some references call the result of Lemma 4.4, or its main
consequence (Theorem 4.14), Gauss‚Äôs lemma. We prefer to use this name for the
following statement.
Proposition 4.8 (Gauss‚Äôs lemma). Let R be a UFD, and let f, g ‚àà R[x]. Then
(contf g ) = (contf )(cont g ).
Proof. This follows easily from our preparatory work. Write
(f g) = ((contf )(f )) ((contg )(g)) = (contf )(contg ) (f g),
with f , g primitive. By Lemma 4.4, f g is primitive; by Lemma 4.7 it follows that
(contf )(contg ) is the content of f g, as needed.

Corollary 4.9. Let R be a UFD, and let f, g ‚àà R[x]. Assume (f ) ‚äÜ (g). Then

4.2. The Ô¨Åeld of fractions of an integral domain. Gauss‚Äôs lemma is the key
to the important observation that if R is a UFD, then so is R[x]. However, we need
one more tool before we can prove this fact; this is one instance of the important
process of localization.
In the case we need for our immediate goal, the process starts from any integral
domain and produces a Ô¨Åeld, in precisely the same way the Ô¨Åeld Q of rational
numbers may be obtained from the integral domain Z. This construction is known
as the Ô¨Åeld of fractions (or Ô¨Åeld of quotients) of the integral domain R. It satisÔ¨Åes
the following beautiful universal property.
Given an integral domain R, consider the category R whose objects are pairs
(i, K),
where K is a Ô¨Åeld and i : R ‚Üí K is an injective ring homomorphism. Morphisms
are deÔ¨Åned in the style of every analogous construction we have encountered: that is,
a morphism (i, K) ‚Üí (j, L) is determined by a homomorphism of Ô¨Åelds Œ± : K ‚Üí L
making the following diagram

Note that Œ±, as every homomorphism of Ô¨Åelds, is necessarily injective (Exer-

to begin with. The injectivity of R ‚Üí K also forces R to be an integral domain,
since subrings of Ô¨Åelds are necessarily integral domains.
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.10. The Ô¨Åeld of fractions K(R) of R is an initial object of the cate-
gory R.
 
Thus, K(R) is the ‚Äòsmallest Ô¨Åeld containing R‚Äô.
The usual caveats apply to any such deÔ¨Ånition: the initial object of R carries
not only the information of a Ô¨Åeld K (the Ô¨Åeld of fractions proper), but also of a
speciÔ¨Åc realization of R as a subring of K; this distinction is blurred in common use
of the language. Also, of course this prescription only deÔ¨Ånes the Ô¨Åeld of fractions
up to isomorphism (as is true of any universal object; cf. Proposition I.5.4).
Finally, just stating the universal property does not guarantee that the sought-
for initial object exists. Thus, our next task is the construction of an initial object
for R. Fortunately, the construction is essentially straightforward: we just need to
formalize a notion of ‚Äòfraction‚Äô of elements of R.

proÔ¨Åting from this major hint.

Example 4.12. With the notation introduced above, K(Z) = Q.
The universal property implies immediately that F ‚Üí K(F ) is an isomorphism
if F is itself a Ô¨Åeld. Thus, the construction adds nothing to Q, R, C, Z/pZ, etc.
If R is any integral domain, so that R[x] is also an integral domain, K(R[x]) is

4.3. R UFD =‚áí R[x] UFD. We are now in a position to prove the analogue of
Hilbert‚Äôs basis theorem for unique factorization domains, that is,
Theorem 4.14. Let R be a UFD; then R[x] is a UFD.
For example, this result (and an immediate induction) shows that the rings
Z[x1 , . . . , xn ] and k[x1 , . . . , xn ] (for k a Ô¨Åeld) are UFDs. Theorem 4.14 is also often
called Gauss‚Äôs lemma.
As a measure of how delicate the statement of Theorem 4.14 is, note that the
power series ring R[[x]] is not necessarily a UFD if R is a UFD; examples of this
phenomenon are however not easy to construct. Of course k[[x]] is a UFD if k is a
Ô¨Åeld, since k[[x]] is a Euclidean domain in this case (Exercise 2.14).
By Theorem 2.5, in order to prove Theorem 4.14, we have to verify that R[x]
satisÔ¨Åes the a.c.c. for principal ideals and that every irreducible element in R[x]
is prime, provided that R is itself a UFD. The general idea is to reduce these
questions to matters in K[x], where K = K(R) is the Ô¨Åeld of fractions of R:
as we know, K[x] is a UFD (in fact it is a Euclidean domain, and Euclidean
domain =‚áí PID =‚áí UFD, as shown in ¬ß2).
The following lemma captures the most crucial ingredient of the interaction
between R[x] and K[x]:
274
 V. Irreducibility and factorization in integral domains
Lemma 4.15. Let R be a UFD, and let K = K(R) be its Ô¨Åeld of fractions. For
nonzero f, g ‚àà R[x], denote by (f ), (g) the principal ideals f R[x], gR[x] in R[x],
and denote by (f )K , (g)K the principal ideals f K[x], gK[x] in K[x]. Assume
‚Ä¢ (contg ) ‚äÜ (contf ) and
‚Ä¢ (g)K ‚äÜ (f )K .
Then (g) ‚äÜ (f ).
Proof. Since (g) K ‚äÜ (f )K , we have g = f h, where h ‚àà K[x]. Write h = ab h, where
a, b ‚àà R and h ‚àà R[x] is a primitive polynomial: this can be done by collecting
common denominators in h, then applying the Ô¨Årst point of Lemma 4.7. We then
have
bg = af h
in R[x]. By Gauss‚Äôs lemma and since h is primitive,
(a contf ) = (b contg );
and since (contg ) ‚äÜ (contf ) by hypothesis, we obtain
(a contf ) ‚äÜ (b cont f ).
Since R is an integral domain and (contf ) = (0), this implies
a = bc
for some c ‚àà R. But then h = ab h = ch ‚àà R[x], and g = f h ‚àà (f ); that is, (g) ‚äÜ (f )
in R[x], as needed.
 
The Ô¨Årst application is the following description of the irreducible elements
of R[x]; this will also be used in the proof of Theorem 4.14 and is independently
interesting.
Proposition 4.16. Let R be a UFD, and let K be its Ô¨Åeld of fractions. Let f ‚àà
R[x] be a nonconstant, irreducible polynomial. Then f is irreducible as an element
of K[x].
Proof. First note that f is primitive: otherwise we could factor out its content,
and f would not be irreducible.
Next, assume f = gh, with g, h ‚àà K[x]; we have to prove that either g or h is
a unit in K[x]. Let c, d ‚àà K such that
g = cg,
 h = dh,
and g, h are primitive polynomials in R[x]. By Lemma 4.4, gh is also primitive;
thus (contgh ) = (1) = (contf ); further,
(f )K = (gh)K
as cd = 0 is a unit in K. By Lemma 4.15 we obtain
(f ) = (gh)
as ideals of R[x]; that is, f = ugh with u ‚àà R[x] a unit. As f is irreducible in R[x],
this implies that either g or h is a unit in R[x]. But then g or h were units in K[x],
verifying that f is irreducible in K[x].
 
4. Unique factorization in polynomial rings
 275
I have always found this fact almost counterintuitive: K is ‚Äòlarger‚Äô than R, so
one may expect that it should be ‚Äòeasier‚Äô to factor polynomials over K than over R.
Proposition 4.16 tells us that this is not the case: with due attention to special
cases, irreducibility in R[x] is ‚Äòthe same as‚Äô irreducibility in K[x]. To be precise,
Corollary 4.17. Let R be a UFD and K the Ô¨Åeld of fractions of R. Let f ‚àà R[x]
be a nonconstant polynomial. Then f is irreducible in R[x] if and only if it is
irreducible in K[x] and primitive.
The proof amounts to tying up loose ends, and I leave it to the reader (Exer-
cise 4.21).
We can now prove the main result of this section. We will make systematic use
of the characterization of UFDs found in Theorem 2.5.
Proof of Theorem 4.14. We begin by verifying the a.c.c. for principal ideals
in R[x]. Let
(f1) ‚äÜ (f2 ) ‚äÜ (f 3 ) ‚äÜ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑

(contf1 ) ‚äÜ (contf2 ) ‚äÜ (contf3 ) ‚äÜ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑
in R; since R is a UFD, this chain stabilizes: that is, (contfi ) = (contfi+1 ) for16
i  0. On the other hand, with notation as in Lemma 4.15 we have
(f1 )K ‚äÜ (f2 )K ‚äÜ (f3 )K ‚äÜ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑
as a sequence of ideals in K[x]; since K[x] is a UFD (because it is a PID; cf. ¬ß2.3)
this sequence stabilizes. Therefore, (fi )K = (fi+1 )K for i  0.
By Lemma 4.15, (fi ) = (fi+1) for i  0; that is, the given chain of principal
ideals stabilizes, as needed.
Next, we consider an irreducible element f of R[x]. We verify that (f ) is a
prime ideal; by Theorem 2.5 it then follows that R[x] is a UFD, as stated.
If f is irreducible and constant, then f is prime in R as R is a UFD, and it
follows that f is prime in R[x] (by Corollary 4.2). Thus we may assume that f is
nonconstant and irreducible (and in particular primitive) in R[x].
By Proposition 4.16, f is irreducible as an element of K[x]; since K[x] is a PID,
(f )K is prime in K[x]. Consider the composition

Since the ring on the right is an integral domain (as (f )K is prime in K[x]), so is
the ring on the left. This proves that (f ) is prime in R[x], and we are done.
 
Summarizing, if R is a UFD, then factorization in R[x] is ‚Äòthe same as‚Äô factor-
ization in the polynomial ring K[x] over the Ô¨Åeld of quotients of R. If f (x) ‚àà R[x],
then f (x) has a prime factorization in K[x] for the simpler reason that K[x] is a
PID, hence a UFD; but if R itself is a UFD, then we know that each of the factors
may be assumed to be in R[x] to begin with (cf. Exercise 4.23).
Example 4.18. As mentioned already, Theorem 4.14 implies that several impor-
tant rings, such as Z[x1 , . . . , xn ] or C[x1, . . . , xn ], are UFDs; for example Z[x] is a
UFD, as announced in ¬ß2.3. Further, arguing as in Exercise 1.15 to reduce to the
case of Ô¨Ånitely many indeterminates, it follows that Z[x1 , x2, . . . ] is a UFD: this is
an example of a non-Noetherian UFD, promised a while back (and illustrating the
last missing feature of the picture presented at the beginning of the chapter).

some irreducible element q of R.
‚Ä¢ Prove that S ‚àí1R is also a UFD.
[4.16]
4.16. Let R be a Noetherian integral domain, and let s ‚àà R, s = 0, be a prime
element. Consider the multiplicatively closed subset S = {1, s, s2, . . . }. Prove that
‚àí1R is a UFD if and only if S R is a UFD. (Hint: By Exercise 2.10, it suÔ¨Éces to
show that every prime of height 1 is principal. Use Exercise 4.10 to relate prime
ideals in R to prime ideals in the localization.)
On the basis of results such as this and of Exercise 4.15, one might suspect
that being factorial is a local property, that is, that R is a UFD if and only if Rp
is a UFD for all primes p, if and only if Rm is a UFD for all maximals m. This
is regrettably not the case. A ring R is locally factorial if Rm is a UFD for all
maximal ideals m; factorial implies locally factorial by Exercise 4.15, but locally
factorial rings that are not factorial do exist.

5. Irreducibility of polynomials
Our work in ¬ß4.3, especially Corollary 4.17, links the irreducibility of polynomi-
als over a UFD with their irreducibility over the corresponding Ô¨Åeld of fractions.
For example, irreducibility of polynomials in Z[x] is ‚Äòessentially the same‚Äô as irre-
ducibility in Q[x]. This can be useful in both directions, provided we have ways
to eÔ¨Äectively verify irreducibility of polynomials over one kind of ring or the other.
I collect here several remarks aimed at establishing whether a given polynomial is
irreducible and brieÔ¨Çy discuss the notion of algebraically closed Ô¨Åeld.
5. Irreducibility of polynomials
 281
5.1. Roots and reducibility. Let R be a ring and f ‚àà R[x]. An element a ‚àà R
is a root of f if f (a) = 0. Recall (Example III.4.7) that a polynomial f (x) ‚àà R[x] is
divisible by (x ‚àí a) if and only if a is a root of f . More generally, we say that a is a
root of f with multiplicity r if (x ‚àí a)r divides f and (x ‚àí a)r+1 does not divide f .
The reader is probably familiar with this notion from experience with calculus. For
example, the graph of polynomials with roots of multiplicities 1, 2, and 3 at 0 look
(near the origin), respectively, like
Lemma 5.1. Let R be an integral domain, and let f ‚àà R[x] be a polynomial of
degree n. Then the number of roots of f , counted with multiplicity, is at most n.
Proof. The number of roots of f in R is less than or equal to the number of roots
of f viewed as a polynomial over the Ô¨Åeld of fractions K of R; so we may replace R
by K.
Now, K[x] is a UFD, and the roots of f correspond to the irreducible factors
of f of degree 1. Since the product of all irreducible factors of f has degree n, the
number of factors of degree 1 can be at most n, as claimed.
 
It is worth remembering the trick of replacing an integral domain R by its Ô¨Åeld
of fractions K, used in this proof: one is often able to use convenient properties
due to the fact that K is a Ô¨Åeld (the fact that K[x] is a UFD, in this case), even
if R is very far from satisfying such properties (R[x] may not be a UFD, since R
itself need not be a UFD).
Also note that the statement of Lemma 5.1 may fail if R is not an integral
domain. For example, the degree 2 polynomial x2 + x has four roots over Z/6Z.
282
 V. Irreducibility and factorization in integral domains
The innocent Lemma 5.1 has important applications: for example, we used
it (without much fanfare) in the proof of Theorem IV.6.10. Also, recall that a
polynomial f ‚àà R[x] determines an ‚Äòevaluation function‚Äô (cf. Example III.2.3) R ‚Üí
R, namely r ‚Üí f (r). The reader checked in Exercise III.2.7 that in general the
function does not determine the polynomial; but polynomials are determined by
the corresponding functions over inÔ¨Ånite integral domains:
Corollary 5.2. Let R be an inÔ¨Ånite integral domain, and let f, g ‚àà R[x] be poly-
nomials. Then f = g if and only if the evaluation functions r ‚Üí f (r), r ‚Üí g(r)
agree.
Proof. Indeed, the two functions agree if and only if every a ‚àà R is a root of
f ‚àí g; but a nonzero polynomial over R cannot have inÔ¨Ånitely many roots, by
Lemma 5.1.
 
Clearly, an irreducible polynomial of degree ‚â• 2 can have no roots. The con-
verse holds for degree 2 and 3, over a Ô¨Åeld:
Proposition 5.3. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld. A polynomial f ‚àà k[x] of degree 2 or 3 is
irreducible if and only if it has no roots.
Proof. Exercise 5.5.
 
Example 5.4. Let F2 be the Ô¨Åeld Z/2Z.
The polynomial f (t) = t2 + t + 1 ‚àà F2 [t] is irreducible, since it has no roots:
f (0) = f (1) = 1. Therefore the ideal (t2 + t + 1) is prime in F2 [t], hence maximal
(because F2 [t] is a PID: don‚Äôt forget Proposition III.4.13). This gives a one-second
construction of a Ô¨Åeld with four elements:
F2 [t]
.
(t2 + t + 1)
The reader (hopefully) constructed this Ô¨Åeld in Exercise III.1.11, by the tiresome
process of searching by hand for a suitable multiplication table. The reader will
now have no diÔ¨Éculty constructing much larger examples (cf. Exercise 5.6).
 
Proposition 5.3 is pleasant and can help to decide irreducibility over more
general rings: for example, a primitive polynomial f ‚àà Z[x] of degree 2 or 3 is
irreducible if and only if it has no roots in Q. Indeed, f is irreducible in Z[x]
if and only if it is irreducible in Q[x] (by Corollary 4.17). However, note that
e.g. 4x2 ‚àí 1 = (2x ‚àí 1)(2x + 1) is primitive and reducible in Z[x], although it has
no integer roots. Also, keep in mind that the statement of Proposition 5.3 may fail
for polynomials of degree ‚â• 4: for example, x4 + 2x2 + 1 = (x 2 + 1)2 is reducible
in Q[x], but it has no rational roots.
Looking for rational roots of a polynomial in Z[x] is in principle a Ô¨Ånite en-
deavor, due to the following observation (which holds over every UFD). This is
often called the ‚Äòrational root test‚Äô.

5.2. Adding roots; algebraically closed Ô¨Åelds. A homomorphism of Ô¨Åelds
i : k ‚Üí F
is necessarily injective (cf. Exercise III.3.10): indeed, its kernel is a proper ideal
of k, and the only proper ideal of a Ô¨Åeld is (0). In this situation we say that F
(or more properly the homomorphism i) is an extension of k. Abusing language a
little, we then think of k as contained in F : k ‚äÜ F . Keep in mind that this is the
case as soon as there is any ring homomorphism k ‚Üí F .
There is a standard procedure for constructing extensions in which a given
polynomial acquires a root; we have encountered an instance of this process in
Example III.4.8. In fact, the resulting Ô¨Åeld is almost universal with respect to this
requirement.

I have stopped short of claiming that the extension constructed in Proposi-

Example 5.8. For k = R and f (x) = x2 +1, the Ô¨Åeld constructed in Proposition 5.7
is (isomorphic to) C: this was checked carefully in Example III.4.8.
