The fact that the irreducible polynomial f ‚àà k[x] acquires a root in the exten-
sion F constructed in Proposition 5.7 implies that f has a linear (i.e., degree 1)
factor over F ; in particular, if deg(f ) > 1, then f is no longer irreducible over F .
Given any polynomial f ‚àà k[x], it is easy to construct an extension of k in
which f factors completely as a product of linear factors (Exercise 5.13). The case
in which this already happens in k itself for every nonzero f is very important, and
hence it is given a name.
5. Irreducibility of polynomials
 285
DeÔ¨Ånition 5.9. A Ô¨Åeld k is algebraically closed if all irreducible polynomials in k[x]
have degree 1.
 
We have encountered this notion in passing, back in Example III.4.14 (and
Exercise III.4.21). The following lemma is left to the reader:
Lemma 5.10. A Ô¨Åeld k is algebraically closed if and only if every nonconstant
polynomial f ‚àà k[x] factors completely as a product of linear factors, if and only if
every nonconstant polynomial f ‚àà k[x] has a root in k.
In other words, a Ô¨Åeld k is algebraically closed if the only polynomial equations
with coeÔ¨Écients in k and no solutions are equations ‚Äòof degree 0‚Äô, such as 1 = 0.
The result known as Nullstellensatz (also due to David Hilbert; I have mentioned
this result in Chapter III) is a vast generalization of this observation and one of
the pillars of (old-fashioned) algebraic geometry. We will come back to all of this
in ¬ßVII.2.
Is any of the Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åelds we have encountered (such as Z/pZ, for p prime)
algebraically closed? No. Adapting Euclid‚Äôs argument proving the inÔ¨Ånitude of
prime integers (Exercise 2.24) reveals that algebraically closed Ô¨Åelds are inÔ¨Ånite.
Proposition 5.11. Let k be an algebraically closed Ô¨Åeld. Then k is inÔ¨Ånite.
Proof. By contradiction, assume that k is algebraically closed and Ô¨Ånite; let the
elements of k be c1 , . . . , cN .
Then there are exactly N irreducible monic polynomials in k[x], namely (x ‚àí
c1), . . . , (x ‚àí cN ). Consider the polynomial
f (x) = (x ‚àí c1 ) ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ (x ‚àí cN ) + 1 :
for all c ‚àà k we have
f (c) = (c ‚àí c1 ) ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ (c ‚àí cN ) + 1 = 1 = 0,
since c equals one of the ci ‚Äôs. Thus f (x) is a nonconstant polynomial with no roots,
contradicting Lemma 5.10.
 
Since Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åelds are not algebraically closed, the reader may suspect that
algebraically closed Ô¨Åelds necessarily have characteristic 0 (cf. Exercise 4.17). This
is not so: there are algebraically closed Ô¨Åelds of any characteristic. In fact, as we will
see in due time, every Ô¨Åeld F may be embedded into an algebraically closed Ô¨Åeld;
the smallest such extension is called the ‚Äòalgebraic closure‚Äô of F and is denoted F .
Thus, for example, Z/2Z is an algebraically closed Ô¨Åeld of characteristic 2.
The algebraic closure Q is a countable subÔ¨Åeld of C. The extension Q ‚äÜ Q,
and especially its ‚ÄòGalois group‚Äô, cf. ¬ßVII.6, is considered one of the most important
objects of study in mathematics (cf. the discussion following Corollary VII.7.6).
We will construct the algebraic closure of a Ô¨Åeld rather explicitly, in ¬ßVII.2.1.
5.3. Irreducibility in C[x], R[x], Q[x]. Every polynomial f ‚àà C[x] factors com-
pletely over C. Indeed,
Theorem 5.12. C is algebraically closed.
286
 V. Irreducibility and factorization in integral domains
Gauss is credited with providing the Ô¨Årst proof21 of this fundamental theorem
(which is indeed known as the fundamental theorem of algebra.)
‚ÄòAlgebraic‚Äô proofs of the fundamental theorem of algebra require more than we
know at this point (we will encounter one in ¬ßVII.7.1, after we have seen a little
Galois theory); curiously, a little complex analysis makes the statement nearly
trivial. Here is a sketch of such an argument. Let f ‚àà C[x] be a nonconstant
polynomial; the task (cf. Lemma 5.10) consists of proving that f has a root in C.
Whatever f (0) is, we can Ô¨Ånd an r ‚àà R large enough that |f (z)| > |f (0)| for all z on
the circle |z| = r (since f is nonconstant, limz‚Üí‚àû |f (z)| = +‚àû). The disk |z| ‚â§ r
is compact, so the continuous function |f (z)| has a minimum on it; by the choice
of r, it must be somewhere in the interior of the disk, say at z = a. The minimum
modulus principle (cf. Exercise 5.16) then implies that f (a) = 0, q.e.d.
By Theorem 5.12, irreducibility of polynomials in C[x] is as simple as it can
be: a nonconstant polynomial f ‚àà C[x] is irreducible if and only if it has degree 1.
Every f ‚àà C[x] of degree ‚â• 2 is reducible.
Over R, the situation is almost as simple:
Proposition 5.13. Every polynomial f ‚àà R[x] of degree ‚â• 3 is reducible.
The nonconstant irreducible polynomials in R[x] are precisely the polynomials
of degree 1 and the quadratic polynomials
with b2 ‚àí 4ac < 0.
f = ax2 + bx + c
Proof. Let f ‚àà R[x] be a nonconstant polynomial:
f = a0 + a1 x + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + an xn ,
with all ai ‚àà R. By Theorem 5.12, f has a complex root z:
a0 + a1 z + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + an zn = 0.
Applying complex conjugation z ‚Üí z and noting that ai = ai since ai ‚àà R,
a0 + a1 z + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + an zn = a0 + a1 z + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + an z n = a0 + a1 z + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + an z n = 0 :
this says that z is also a root of f . There are two possibilities:
‚Ä¢ either z = z, that is, z = r was real to begin with, and hence (x ‚àí r) is a factor
of f in R[x]; or
‚Ä¢ z = z, and then (x ‚àí z) and (x ‚àí z) are nonassociate irreducible factors of f
in C[x]. In this case (since C[x] is a UFD!)
x 2 ‚àí (z + z)x + zz = (x ‚àí z)(x ‚àí z)
divides f .
21 Actually, Gauss‚Äôs Ô¨Årst proof apparently had a gap; the Ô¨Årst rigorous proof is due to Argand.
Later, Gauss Ô¨Åxed the gap in his proof and provided several other proofs.
5. Irreducibility of polynomials
 287
Since z+z and zz are both real numbers, this analysis shows that every nonconstant
f ‚àà R[x] has an irreducible factor of degree ‚â§ 2, proving the Ô¨Årst statement.
The second statement then follows immediately from Proposition 5.3 and the
fact that the quadratic polynomials in R[x] with no real roots are precisely the
polynomials ax2 + bx + c with b2 ‚àí 4ac < 0.
 
The following remark is an immediate consequence of Proposition 5.13 and is
otherwise evident from elementary calculus (Exercise 5.17):
Corollary 5.14. Every polynomial f ‚àà R[x] of odd degree has a real root.
Summarizing, the issue of irreducibility of polynomials in C[x] or R[x] is very
straightforward. By contrast, irreducibility in Q[x] is a complicated business: as we
have seen (Proposition 4.16), it is just as subtle as irreducibility in Z[x]. There is
no sharp characterization of irreducibility in Z[x]; but the following simple-minded
remarks (and Eisenstein‚Äôs criterion, discussed in ¬ß5.4) suÔ¨Éce in many interesting
cases.
One simple-minded remark is that if œï : R ‚Üí S is a homomorphism, and a
is reducible in R, then œï(a) is likely to be reducible in S. This is just because if
a = bc, then œï(a) = œï(b)œï(c).
Of course this is not quite right: for example because œï(a) and/or œï(b) and/or
œï(c) may be units in S. But with due care for special cases this observation may
be used to great eÔ¨Äect, especially in the contrapositive form: if œï(a) is irreducible
in S, then a is (likely. . . ) irreducible in R.
Here is a simple statement formalizing these remarks, for R = Z[x]. The natural
projection Z ‚Üí Z/pZ induces a surjective homomorphism œÄ : Z[x] ‚Üí Z/pZ[x]:
quite simply, œÄ(f ) is obtained from f by reading all coeÔ¨Écients modulo p; I will
write ‚Äòf mod p‚Äô for the result of this operation.
Proposition 5.15. Let f ‚àà Z[x] be a primitive polynomial, and let p be a prime
integer. Assume f mod p has the same degree as f and is irreducible in Z/pZ[x].
Then f is irreducible in Z[x].
Proof. Argue contrapositively: if f is primitive and reducible in Z[x] and deg f =
n, then f = gh with deg g = d, deg h = e, d + e = n, and both d, e, positive. But
then the same can be said of f mod p, so f mod p is also reducible.
 
The hypothesis on degrees is necessary, precisely to take care of the ‚Äòspecial
cases‚Äô mentioned in the discussion preceding the statement. For example, (2x3 +
3x2 + 3x + 1) equals (x2 + x + 1) mod 2, and the latter is irreducible in Z/2Z[x];
yet (2x + 1) is a factor of the former. The point is of course that 2x + 1 is a unit
mod 2.
Warning: As we will see in a fairly distant future (Example VII.5.3), there
are irreducible polynomials in Z[x] which are reducible modulo all primes! So
Proposition 5.15 cannot be turned into a characterization of irreducibility in Z[x].
It is however rather useful in producing examples. For instance,
288
 V. Irreducibility and factorization in integral domains
Corollary 5.16. There are irreducible polynomials in Z[x] and Q[x] of arbitrarily
large degree.
Proof. By Proposition 4.16, the statement for Z[x] implies the one for Q[x]. By
Proposition 5.15, it suÔ¨Éces to verify that there are irreducible polynomials in
Z/pZ[x] of arbitrarily large degree, for any prime integer p. This is a particular
case of Exercise 5.11.
 
5.4. Eisenstein‚Äôs criterion. I am giving this result in a separate subsection only
on account of the fact that it is very famous; it is also very simple-minded, like
the considerations leading up to Proposition 5.15, and further it is not really a
‚Äòcriterion‚Äô, in the sense that it also does not give a characterization of irreducibility.
The result is usually stated for Z, but it holds for every ring R.
Proposition 5.17. Let R be a (commutative) ring, and let p be a prime ideal of R.
Let

Then f is not the product of polynomials of degree < n in R[x].
Proof. Argue by contradiction. Assume f = gh in R[x], with both d = deg g
and e = deg h less than n = deg f ; write

Example 5.18. For all n and all primes p, the polynomial xn ‚àí p is irreducible
in Z[x]. This follows immediately from Eisenstein‚Äôs criterion and gives an alterna-

6. Further remarks and examples
6.1. Chinese remainder theorem. Suppose all you know about an integer is
its class modulo several numbers; can you reconstruct the integer? Also, if you
are given arbitrary classes in Z/nZ for several integers n, can you Ô¨Ånd an N ‚àà Z
satisfying all these congruences simultaneously?
The answer is no in both cases, for trivial reasons. If an integer N satisÔ¨Åes given
congruences modulo n1 , . . . , nk , then adding any multiple of n1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ nk to N produces
an integer satisfying the same congruences (so N cannot be entirely reconstructed
from the given data); and there is no integer N such that N ‚â° 1 mod 2 and N ‚â°
2 mod 4, so there are plenty of congruences which cannot be simultaneously satisÔ¨Åed.
The Chinese remainder theorem (CRT) sharpens these questions so that they
can in fact be answered aÔ¨Érmatively, and (in its modern form) it generalizes them
to a much broader setting. Its statement is most pleasant for PIDs; it is very
impressive23 even in the limited context of Z. Protoversions of the theorem have
already appeared here and there in this book, e.g., Lemma IV.6.1.
I will Ô¨Årst give the more general version, which is in some way simpler. Let R
be any commutative ring.
Theorem 6.1. Let I1 , . . . , Ik be ideals of R such that Ii + Ij = (1) for all i = j.

the argument).

and let n = n1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ nk ; for each i, let mi = n/ni. Then ni and mi are relatively

for all i.
The same procedure can be applied over any Euclidean domain R; see Exer-
cise 6.7 for an example.
6.2. Gaussian integers. The rings Z and k[x] (where k is a Ô¨Åeld) may be the
only examples of Euclidean domains known to our reader. The next most famous
example is the ring of Gaussian integers; this is a very pretty ring, and it has
elementary but striking applications in number theory (one instance of which we
will see in ¬ß6.3).
24This is a particular case of Lemma 6.2 and is clear anyway from gcd considerations.

Abstractly, we may deÔ¨Åne this ring as
Z[x]

The notation Z[i] is justiÔ¨Åed by the discussion in ¬ß5.2 (cf. especially Proposi-

that is, a square root i of ‚àí1. The natural embedding

represent the ideal (‚àí2 ‚àí i) in Z[i]: an enlarged, tilted lattice superimposed on

understand why this works out so neatly.
A Gaussian integer is a complex number, and as such it has a norm
N (a + bi) := (a + bi)(a ‚àí bi) = a2 + b2 .
Geometrically, this is the square of the distance from the origin to a + bi.
The norm of a Gaussian integer is a nonnegative integer; thus N is a function
Z[i] ‚Üí Z‚â•0 .
Lemma 6.5. The function N is a Euclidean valuation on Z[i]; further, N is mul-
tiplicative in the sense that ‚àÄz, w ‚àà Z[i]
N (zw) = N (z)N (w).
Proof. The multiplicativity is an immediate consequence of the elementary prop-
erties of complex conjugation:
N (zw) = (zw)(zw) = (zz)(ww) = N (z)N (w).
To see that N is a Euclidean valuation, we have to show how to perform a ‚Äòdivision
with remainder‚Äô in Z[i]. It is not hard, but it is a bit messy, to do this algebraically;
I will attempt to convince the reader that this is in fact visually evident (and then
the reader can have fun producing the needed algebraic computations).
Let z, w ‚àà Z[i], and assume w = 0. The ideal (w) is a lattice superimposed
on Z[i]. The given z is either one of the vertices of this lattice (in which case z is a
multiple of w, so that the division z/w can be performed in Z[i], with remainder 0)
or it sits inside one of the ‚Äòboxes‚Äô of the lattice. In the latter case, pick any of the
vertices of that box, that is, a multiple qw of w, and let r = z ‚àí qw. The situation
may look as follows:
w
z
qw
Then we have obtained
z = qw + r,
296
 V. Irreducibility and factorization in integral domains
and the norm of r is the square of the length of the segment between qw and z.
Since this segment is contained in a box, and the square of the size of the box
is N (w), we have achieved
N (r) < N (w),
completing the proof.
 
As we know, all sorts of amazing properties hold for the ring of Gaussian
integers as a consequence of Lemma 6.5: Z[i] is a PID and a UFD; irreducible
elements are prime in Z[i]; greatest common divisors exist and may be found by
applying the Euclidean algorithm, etc. Any one of these facts would seem fairly
challenging in itself, but they are all immediate consequences of the existence of a
Euclidean valuation and of the general considerations in ¬ß2.
The fact that the norm is multiplicative simpliÔ¨Åes further the analysis of the
ring Z[i].
Lemma 6.6. The units of Z[i] are ¬±1, ¬±i.
Proof. If u is a unit in Z[i], then there exists v ‚àà Z[i] such that uv = 1. But
then N (u)N (v) = N (uv) = N (1) = 1 by multiplicativity, so N (u) is a unit in Z.
This implies N (u) = 1, and the only elements in Z[i] with norm 1 are ¬±1, ¬±i. The
statement follows.
 
Lemma 6.7. Let q ‚àà Z[i] be a prime element. Then there is a prime integer p ‚àà Z
such that N (q) = p or N (q) = p 2 .
Proof. Since q is not a unit, N (q) = 1 (by Lemma 6.6). Thus N (q) is a nontrivial
product of (integer) primes, and since q is prime in Z[i] ‚äá Z, q must divide one
of the prime integer factors of N (q); let p be this integer prime. But then q | p
in Z[i], and it follows (by multiplicativity of the norm) that N (q) | N (p) = p2 .
Since N (q) = 1, the only possibilities are N (q) = p and N (q) = p2 , as claimed. 
Both possibilities presented in Lemma 6.7 occur, and studying this dichotomy
further will be key to the result in ¬ß6.3. But we can already work out several cases
‚Äòby hand‚Äô:
Example 6.8. The prime integer 3 is a prime element of Z[i]; this can be veriÔ¨Åed
by proving that 3 is irreducible in Z[i] (since Z[i] is a UFD). For this purpose, note
that since N (3) = 9, the norm of a factor of 3 would have to be a divisor of 9,
that is, 1, 3, or 9. Gaussian integers with norm 1 are units, and those with norm 9
are associates of 3 (Exercise 6.10); thus a nontrivial factor of 3 would necessarily
have norm equal to 3. But there are no such elements in Z[i]; hence 3 is indeed
irreducible.
The prime integer 5 is not a prime element of Z[i]: running through the same
argument as we just did for 3, we Ô¨Ånd that a factor of 5 should have norm 5, and
2 + i does. In fact, 5 = (2 + i)(2 ‚àí i) is a prime factorization of 5 in Z[i].
Visually, what happens is that the lattice generated by 3 in Z[i] cannot be
reÔ¨Åned further into a tighter lattice, while the one generated by 5 does admit a
reÔ¨Ånement:
6. Further remarks and examples
3
297
5
(the circles represent complex numbers with norm 3, 5, respectively).
 
The reader is encouraged to work out several other examples and to try to Ô¨Ågure
out what property of an integer prime makes it split in Z[i] (like 5 and unlike 3).
But this should be done before reading on!
6.3. Fermat‚Äôs theorem on sums of squares. Once armed with our knowledge
about rings, playing with Z[i] a little further teaches us something new about Z‚Äî
this is a beautiful and famous example of the success achieved by judicious use of
generalization over brute force.
First, let us complete the circle of thought begun with Lemma 6.7. Say that
a prime integer p splits in Z[i] if it is not a prime element of Z[i]; we have seen in
Example 6.8 that 5 splits in Z[i], while 3 does not.
Lemma 6.9. A positive integer prime p ‚àà Z splits in Z[i] if and only if it is the
sum of two squares in Z.
298
 V. Irreducibility and factorization in integral domains
Proof. First assume that p = a2 + b2 , with a, b ‚àà Z. Then
p = (a + bi)(a ‚àí bi)
in Z[i], and N (a ¬± bi) = a2 + b2 = p = 1, so neither of the two factors is a unit
in Z[i] (Lemma 6.6). Thus p is not irreducible, hence not prime, in Z[i].
Conversely, assume that p is not irreducible in Z[i]: then it has an irreducible
factor q ‚àà Z[i], which is not an associate of p. Since q | p, by multiplicativity of
the norm we have N (q) | N (p) = p2, and hence N (q) = p since q and p are not
associates (thus N (q) = p2 ) and q is not a unit (thus N (q) = 1). If q = a + bi, we
Ô¨Ånd
p = N (q) = a2 + b2 ,
verifying that p is the sum of two squares and completing the proof.
 
Thus, 2 splits (as 2 = 12 + 12 ), and so do
5 = 12 + 22 ,
 13 = 22 + 32 ,
17 = 12 + 42 ,
...
while
3,
 7,
 11,
 19,
 23,
 ...
remain prime if viewed as elements of Z[i].
The next puzzle is as follows: what else distinguishes the primes in the Ô¨Årst
list from the primes in the second list? Again, the reader who does not know the
answer already should pause and try to come up with a conjecture and then prove
that conjecture! Do not read the next statement before trying this on your own25 !
Lemma 6.10. A positive odd prime integer p splits in Z[i] if and only if it is
congruent to 1 modulo 4.
Proof. The question is whether p is prime as an element of Z[i], that is, whether
Z[i]/(p) is an integral domain. But we have isomorphisms

25 The point I am trying to make is that these are not diÔ¨Écult facts, and a conscientious reader

and had to wait about one hundred years before being proven rigorously (by Euler, using ‚ÄòinÔ¨Ånite
descent‚Äô). Proofs using Gaussian integers are due to Dedekind and had to wait another hundred
years. The moral is, of course, that the modern algebraic apparatus acts as a tremendous ampliÔ¨Åer
of our skills.

there is an integer n such that n2 ‚â° ‚àí1 mod p,
and we are reduced to verifying that this last condition is equivalent to p ‚â° 1 mod
4, provided that p is an odd prime.
For this purpose, recall (Theorem IV.6.10) that the multiplicative group G of
Z/pZ is cyclic; let g ‚àà G be a generator of G. Since p is assumed to be odd, p ‚àí 1
is an even number, say 2 : thus g has order |G| = 2 . Also, denote the class of an
integer n mod (p) by n. Since g is a generator of G, for every integer n ‚àà (p) there
is an integer m such that n = gm .
The class ‚àí1 generates the unique subgroup of order 2 of G (unique by the
classiÔ¨Åcation of subgroups of a cyclic group, Proposition II.6.11); since g  does the
same, we have
g = ‚àí1.
Therefore, with n = gm , we see that n2 ‚â° ‚àí1 mod p if and only if g2m = g  , that
is, if and only if
2m ‚â° mod 2 .
Summarizing, ‚àÉn ‚àà Z such that n2 ‚â° ‚àí1 mod p if and only if ‚àÉm ‚àà Z such that
2m ‚â° mod 2 . Now this is clearly the case if and only if is even, that is, if and
only if (p ‚àí 1) = 2 is a multiple of 4, that is, if and only if p ‚â° 1 mod 4; and we
are done.
 
Putting together Lemma 6.9 and Lemma 6.10 gives the following beautiful
number-theoretic statement:
Theorem 6.11 (Fermat). A positive odd prime p ‚àà Z is a sum of two squares if
and only if p ‚â° 1 mod 4.
Remark 6.12. Lagrange proved (in 1770) that every positive integer is the sum of
four squares. One way to prove this is not unlike the proof given for Theorem 6.11:
it boils down to analyzing the splitting of (integer) primes in a ring; the role of Z[i]
is taken here by a ring of ‚Äòintegral quaternions‚Äô.
 
The reader should pause and note that there is no mention of UFDs, Euclidean
domains, complex numbers, cyclic groups, etc., in the statement of Theorem 6.11,
although a large selection of these tools was used in its proof. This is of course the
dream of the generalizer: to set up an abstract machinery making interesting facts
(nearly) evident‚Äîfacts that would be extremely mysterious or that would require
Fermat-grade cleverness to be understood without that machinery.

Linear algebra
Chapter VI
In several branches of science, ‚Äòalgebra‚Äô means ‚Äòlinear algebra‚Äô: the study of vector
spaces and linear maps, that is, of the category of modules over a ring R in the very
special case in which R is a Ô¨Åeld (and often one restricts attention to very special
Ô¨Åelds, such as R or C).
This will be one of the main themes in this chapter. However, I will stress
that much of what can be done over a Ô¨Åeld can in fact be done over less special
rings. In fact, I will argue that working out the general theory over integral domains
produces invaluable tools when we are working over Ô¨Åelds: the paramount example
being canonical forms for matrices with entries in a Ô¨Åeld, which will be obtained as
a corollary of the classiÔ¨Åcation of Ô¨Ånitely generated modules over a PID.
Throughout the main body of this chapter (but not necessarily in the exercises)
R will denote an integral domain; most of the theory can be extended to arbitrary
commutative1 rings without major diÔ¨Éculty.
1. Free modules revisited
1.1. R-Mod. For generalities on modules, see ¬ßIII.5. A module over R is an abelian
group M , endowed with an action of R. The action of r ‚àà R on m ‚àà M is
denoted rm: there is a notational bias towards left-modules (but the distinction
between left- and right-modules will be immaterial here as R is commutative). The
deÔ¨Åning axioms of a module tell us that for all r1 , r2 , r ‚àà R and m, m1, m2 ‚àà M ,
‚Ä¢‚Ä¢‚Ä¢(r1 + r2 )m = r1m + r2 m,
1m = m and (r1r2 )m = r1 (r2 m),
r(m1 + m2 ) = rm1 + rm2 .
1The hypothesis of commutativity is very convenient, as it allows us to identify the notion of
left- and right-modules, and the fact that integral domains have Ô¨Åelds of fractions simpliÔ¨Åes many
arguments. The reader should keep in mind that many results in this chapter can be extended to
more general rings.
305
306
 VI. Linear algebra
Modules over a ring R form the category R-Mod, which we encountered in
Chapter III. This category reÔ¨Çects subtle and important properties of R, and we
are going to attempt to uncover some of these features. As a Ô¨Årst approximation,
we look at the ‚Äòfull subcategory‚Äô (cf. Exercise I.3.8) of R-Mod whose objects are
free modules and in which morphisms are ordinary morphisms in R-Mod, that is,
R-linear group homomorphisms.
The goal of the Ô¨Årst few sections of this chapter is to give a very explicit
description of this subcategory: in the case of Ô¨Ånitely generated free modules, this
can be done by means of matrices with entries in R. Later in the chapter, we
will see that matrices may also be used to describe important classes of nonfree
modules.
1.2. Linear independence and bases. The reader is invited to review the deÔ¨Å-
nition of free R-modules given in ¬ßIII.6.3: F R(S) denotes an R-module containing
a given set S and universal with respect to the existence of a set-map from S. We
proved (Claim III.6.3) that the module R‚äïS with ‚Äòone component for each element
of S‚Äô gives an explicit realization of F R (S).
The main point of this subsection will be that, for reasonable rings R, the
set S can be recovered2 ‚Äòabstractly‚Äô from the free module F R (S). For example, it
will follow easily that Rm = ‚àº
 Rn if and only if m = n; this is the Ô¨Årst indication
that the category of (Ô¨Ånitely generated) free modules does indeed admit a simple
description.
Our main tool will be the famous concepts of linearly independent subsets and
bases. It is easy to be imprecise in deÔ¨Åning these notions. In order to avoid obvious
traps, I will give the deÔ¨Ånitions for indexed sets (cf. ¬ßI.2.2), that is, for functions
i : I ‚Üí M from a (nonempty) indexing set I to a given module M . The reader
should think of i as a selection of elements of M , allowing for the possibility that
the elements mŒ± ‚àà M corresponding to Œ± ‚àà I may not all be distinct.
Recall that for all sets I there is a canonical injection j : I ‚Üí F R(I) and any
function i : I ‚Üí M determines a unique R-module homomorphism œï : F R (I) ‚Üí M
making the diagram
F R (I)

j

commute: this is precisely the universal property satisÔ¨Åed by F R (I).
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.1. We say that the indexed set i : I ‚Üí M is linearly independent if
œï is injective; i is linearly dependent otherwise. We say that i generates M if œï is
surjective.
 
Put in a slightly messier, but perhaps more common, way, an indexed set
S = {mŒ± }Œ±‚ààI of elements of M is linearly independent if the only vanishing linear

Using indexed sets in DeÔ¨Ånition 1.1 takes care of obvious particular cases such
as m and m being linearly dependent since 1 ¬∑ m + (‚àí1) ¬∑ m = 0: if i : I ‚Üí M is not
itself injective, then œï is surely not injective (by the commutativity of the diagram
and the injectivity of j), so that i is linearly dependent in this case. Because of
this fact, the datum of a linearly independent i : I ‚Üí M amounts to a (special)
choice of distinct elements of M ; the temptation to identify the elements of I with
the corresponding elements of M is historically irresistible and essentially harmless.
Thus, it is common to speak about linearly dependent/independent subsets of M .
I will conform to this common practice; the reader should parse the statements
carefully and correct obvious imprecisions that may arise.
A simple application of Zorn‚Äôs lemma shows that every module has maximal
linearly independent subsets. In fact, it gives the following conveniently stronger
statement:
Lemma 1.2. Let M be an R-module, and let S ‚äÜ M be a linearly independent
subset. Then there exists a maximal linearly independent subset of M containing
S.
Proof. Consider the family S of linearly independent subsets of M containing S,
ordered by inclusion. Since S is linearly independent, S = ‚àÖ. By Zorn‚Äôs lemma, it
suÔ¨Éces to verify that every chain in S has an upper bound. Indeed, the union of
a chain of linearly independent subsets containing S is also linearly independent:
because any relation of linear dependence only involves Ô¨Ånitely many elements and
these elements would all belong to one subset in the chain.
 
Remark 1.3. This statement is in fact known to be equivalent to the axiom of
choice; therefore, the use of Zorn‚Äôs lemma in one form or another cannot be by-
passed.
 
Note that the singleton {2} ‚äÜ Z is a ‚Äòmaximal linearly independent subset‚Äô of Z,
but it does not generate Z. In general this is an additional requirement, leading to
the deÔ¨Ånition of a basis.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.4. An indexed set B ‚Üí M is a basis if it generates M and is linearly
independent.
 
Again, one often talks of bases as ‚Äòsubsets‚Äô of the module M ; since the images
of all b ‚àà B are necessarily distinct elements, this is rather harmless. When B is
Ô¨Ånite (or at any rate countable), the extra information carried by the indexed set
308
 VI. Linear algebra
can be encoded by ordering the elements of B; to emphasize this, one talks about
ordered bases.
Bases are necessarily maximal linearly independent subsets and minimal gen-
erating subsets; this holds over every ring. What will make modules over a Ô¨Åeld,
i.e., vector spaces, so special is that the converse will also hold.
In any case, only very special modules admit bases:
Lemma 1.5. An R-module M is free if and only if it admits a basis. In fact,
B ‚äÜ M is a basis if and only if the natural homomorphism R‚äïB ‚Üí M is an
isomorphism.
Proof. This is immediate from DeÔ¨Ånition 1.1: if B ‚äÜ M is linearly independent
and generates M , then the corresponding homomorphism R‚äïB ‚Üí M is injective
and surjective. Conversely, if œï : R‚äïB ‚Üí M is an isomorphism, then B is identiÔ¨Åed
with a subset of M which generates it (because œï is surjective) and is linearly
independent (because œï is injective).
 
By Lemma 1.5, the choice of a basis B of a free module M amounts to the
choice of an isomorphism R‚äïB = ‚àº
 M ; this will be an important observation in due
time (e.g., in ¬ß2.2).
Once a basis B has been chosen for a free module M , then every element m ‚àà M
can be written uniquely as a linear combination

m =
 r b b
b‚ààB
with rb ‚àà R. As always, remember that all but Ô¨Ånitely many of the coeÔ¨Écients rb
are 0 in any such expression.
1.3. Vector spaces. Lemma 1.5 is all that is needed to prove the fundamental
observation that modules over a Ô¨Åeld are necessarily free. Recall that modules over
a Ô¨Åeld k are called k-vector spaces (Example III.5.5). Elements of a vector space
are called (surprise, surprise) vectors, while elements of the Ô¨Åeld are called3 scalars.
By Lemma 1.5, proving that vector spaces are free modules amounts to proving
that they admit bases; Lemma 1.2 reduces the matter to the following:
Lemma 1.6. Let R = k be a Ô¨Åeld, and let V be a k-vector space. Let B be a
maximal linearly independent subset of V ; then B is a basis of V .
Again, this should be contrasted with the situation over rings: {2} is a maximal
linearly independent subset of Z, but it is not a basis.
Proof. Let v ‚àà V , v ‚àà B. Then B ‚à™ {v} is not linearly independent, by the
maximality of B; therefore, there exist c0 , . . . , ct ‚àà k and (distinct) b1 , . . . , bt ‚àà B
such that
c0 v + c1 b1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + ct bt = 0,
3This terminology is also often used for free modules over any ring.

Summarizing,
Proposition 1.7. Let R = k be a Ô¨Åeld, and let V be a k-vector space. Let S
be a linearly independent set of vectors of V . Then there exists a basis B of V
containing S.
In particular, V is free as a k-module.
Proof. Put Lemma 1.2, Lemma 1.5, and Lemma 1.6 together.
 
We could also contemplate this situation from the ‚Äòmirror‚Äô point of view of
generating sets:
Lemma 1.8. Let R = k be a Ô¨Åeld, and let V be a k-vector space. Let B be a
minimal generating set for V ; then B is a basis of V .
Every set generating V contains a basis of V .
Proof. Exercise 1.6.
 
Lemma 1.8 also fails on more general rings (Exercise 1.5). To reiterate, over
Ô¨Åelds (but not over general rings) a subset B of a vector space is a basis ‚áê‚áí it is
a maximal linearly independent subset ‚áê‚áí it is a minimal generating set.
1.4. Recovering B from F R (B). We are ready for the ‚Äòreconstruction‚Äô of a set B
(up to a bijection!) from the corresponding free module F R (B). This is the result
justifying the notion of dimension of a vector space, or, more generally, the rank
of a free module. Again, we prove a somewhat stronger statement.
Proposition 1.9. Let R be an integral domain, and let M be a free R-module.
Let B be a maximal linearly independent subset of M , and let S be a linearly
independent subset. Then4 |S| ‚â§ |B|.
In particular, any two maximal linearly independent subsets of a free module
over an integral domain have the same cardinality.
Proof. By taking Ô¨Åelds of fractions, the general case over an integral domain is
easily reduced to the case of vector spaces over a Ô¨Åeld; see Exercise 1.7. We may
then assume that R = k is a Ô¨Åeld and M = V is a k-vector space.
We have to prove that there is an injective map j : S ‚Üí B, and this can be
done by an inductive process, replacing elements of B by elements of S ‚Äòone-by-
one‚Äô. For this, let ‚â§ be a well-ordering on S, let v ‚àà S, and assume we have deÔ¨Åned
j for all w ‚àà S with w < v. Let B  be the set obtained from B by replacing all

but the fact is true for ‚ÄòinÔ¨Ånite-dimensional spaces‚Äô as well, as the argument shows.

 
Example 1.10. An uncountable subset of C[x] is necessarily linearly dependent.
Indeed, C[x] has a countable basis over C: for example, {1, x, x2 , x3 , . . . }.
 
Corollary 1.11. Let R be an integral domain, and let A, B be sets. Then
F R (A) ‚àº
 = F R (B) ‚áê‚áí there is a bijection A ‚àº
 = B.
Proof. Exercise 1.8.
 
Remark 1.12. We have learned in Lemma 1.2 that we can ‚Äòcomplete‚Äô every lin-
early independent subset S to a maximal one. The argument used in the proof of
Proposition 1.9 shows that we can in fact do this by borrowing elements of a given
maximal linearly independent subset.
 
Remark 1.13. As a particular case of Corollary 1.11, we see that if R is an integral
domain, then Rm = ‚àº
 Rn if and only if m = n. This says that integral domains satisfy
the ‚ÄòIBN (Invariant Basis Number) property‚Äô.
Strange as it may seem, this obvious-looking fact does not hold over arbitrary
rings: for example, the ring of endomorphisms of an inÔ¨Ånite-dimensional vector
space does not satisfy the IBN property. On the other hand, integral domains are a
bit of an overshoot: all commutative rings satisfy the IBN property (Exercise 1.11).
5 This allows for the possibility that v ‚àà B ‚Äòalready‚Äô. In this case, the reader can check that
the process I am about to describe gives j(v) = v.
1. Free modules revisited
 311
One way to think about this is that the category of Ô¨Ånitely generated free
modules over (say) an integral domain is ‚ÄòclassiÔ¨Åed‚Äô by Z‚â•0 : up to isomorphisms,
there is exactly one Ô¨Ånitely generated free module for any nonnegative integer. The
task of describing this category then amounts to describing the homomorphisms
between objects corresponding to two given nonnegative integers; this will be done
in ¬ß2.1.
 
As a byproduct of the result of Proposition 1.9, we can now give the following
important deÔ¨Ånition.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.14. Let R be an integral domain. The rank of a free R-module M ,
denoted rkR M , is the cardinality of a maximal linearly independent subset of M .
The rank of a vector space is called the dimension, denoted dimk V .
 
This deÔ¨Ånition will in fact be adopted for more general Ô¨Ånitely generated mod-
ules when the time comes, in ¬ß5.3.
Finite-dimensional vector spaces over a Ô¨Åxed Ô¨Åeld form a category. Since vector
spaces are free modules (Proposition 1.7), Corollary 1.11 implies that two Ô¨Ånite-
dimensional vector spaces are isomorphic if and only if they have the same dimen-
sion.
The subscripts R, k are often omitted, if the context permits. But note that, for
example, viewing the complex numbers as a real vector space, we have dimR C = 2,
while dimC C = 1. So some care is warranted.
Proposition 1.9 tells us that every linearly independent subset S of a free R-
module M must have cardinality lower than or equal to rkR M . Similarly, every
generating set must have cardinality higher than or equal to the rank. Indeed,
Proposition 1.15. Let R be an integral domain, and let M be a free R-module;
assume that M is generated by S: M = S. Then S contains a maximal linearly
independent subset of M .
Proof. By Exercise 1.7 we may assume that R is a Ô¨Åeld and M = V is a vector
space. Use Zorn‚Äôs lemma to obtain a linearly independent subset B ‚äÜ S which is
maximal among subsets of S. Arguing as in the proof of Lemma 1.6 shows that
S is in the span of B, and it follows that B generates V . Thus B is a basis, and
hence a maximal linearly independent subset of V , as needed.
 
Remark 1.16. I have used again the trick of switching from an integral domain
to its Ô¨Åeld of fractions. The second part of the argument would not work over
an arbitrary integral domain, since maximal linearly independent subsets over an
integral domain are not generating sets in general.
Another standard method to reduce questions about modules over arbitrary
commutative rings to vector spaces is to mod out by a maximal ideal; cf. Exercise 1.9
and following.

1.1. ¬¨ Prove that R and C are isomorphic as Q-vector spaces. (In particular, (R, +)
and (C, +) are isomorphic as groups.) [II.4.4]
1.2. ¬¨ Prove that the sets listed in Exercise III.1.4 are all R-vector spaces, and
compute their dimensions. [1.3]
1.3. Prove that su(2) ‚àº
 = so3 (R) as R-vector spaces. (This is immediate, and not
particularly interesting, from the dimension computation of Exercise 1.2. However,
these two spaces may be viewed as the tangent spaces to SU(2), resp., SO3 (R),
at I; the surjective homomorphism SU(2) ‚Üí SO3(R) you constructed in Exer-
cise II.8.9 induces a more ‚Äòmeaningful‚Äô isomorphism su(2) ‚Üí so3(R). Can you Ô¨Ånd
this isomorphism?)
1.4. Let V be a vector space over a Ô¨Åeld k. A Lie bracket on V is an operation
[¬∑, ¬∑] : V √ó V ‚Üí V such that
‚Ä¢ (‚àÄu, v, w ‚àà V ), (‚àÄa, b ‚àà k),
[au + bv, w] = a[u, w] + b[v, w],
 [w, au + bv] = a[w, u] + b[w, v],
‚Ä¢ (‚àÄv ‚àà V ), [v, v] = 0,
‚Ä¢ and (‚àÄu, v, w ‚àà V ), [[u, v], w] + [[v, w], u] + [[w, u], v] = 0.
(This axiom is called the Jacobi identity.) A vector space endowed with a Lie
bracket is called a Lie algebra. DeÔ¨Åne a category of Lie algebras over a given Ô¨Åeld.
Prove the following:
‚Ä¢ In a Lie algebra V , [u, v] = ‚àí[v, u] for all u, v ‚àà V .
‚Ä¢ If V is a k-algebra (DeÔ¨Ånition III.5.7), then [v, w] := vw ‚àí wv deÔ¨Ånes a Lie
bracket on V , so that V is a Lie algebra in a natural way.
‚Ä¢ This makes gln (R), gln (C) into Lie algebras. The sets listed in Exercise III.1.4
are all Lie algebras, with respect to a Lie bracket induced from gl.
‚Ä¢ su(2) and so3 (R) are isomorphic as Lie algebras over R.

2. Homomorphisms of free modules, I
2.1. Matrices. As pointed out in ¬ß1, Corollary 1.11 amounts to a classiÔ¨Åcation
of free modules over an integral domain: if F is a free module, then there is a
set A (determined up to a bijection) such that F = ‚àº
 R‚äïA . The choice of such an
isomorphism is precisely the same thing as the choice of a basis of F (Lemma 1.5).
This is simultaneously good and bad news. The good news is that if F1 , F2 are
free, then it must be possible to ‚Äòunderstand‚Äô6
HomR(F1 , F2)
entirely in terms of the corresponding sets A1 , A2 such that F1 = ‚àº
 R‚äïA1 , F2 = ‚àº
 R‚äïA2 .
That is, this set of morphisms in R-Mod may be identiÔ¨Åed with
HomR (R‚äïA1 , R‚äïA2 ).
The bad news is that this identiÔ¨Åcation HomR(F1, F2 ) = ‚àº
 HomR (R‚äïA1 , R‚äïA2 )
is not ‚Äòcanonical‚Äô, because it depends on the chosen isomorphisms F1 ‚àº
 = R‚äïA1 ,
6 For notational simplicity I will denote Hom
R-Mod (M, N ) by HomR (M, N ); this is very com-
mon, and no confusion is likely.
2. Homomorphisms of free modules, I

F2 ‚àº
 = R‚äïA2 , that is, on the choice of bases. We will therefore have to do some work
to deal with this ambiguity (in ¬ß2.2).
The point of this subsection is to deal with the good news, that is, describe
HomR (R‚äïA1 , R‚äïA2 ). This can be done in a particularly convenient way when the
free modules are Ô¨Ånitely generated, the case we are going to analyze more carefully.
Also recall (from ¬ßIII.5.2) that one of the good features of the category R-Mod
is that the set of morphisms HomR (M, N ) between two R-modules is itself an
R-module, in a natural way. The task is then to describe as explicitly as possible7
HomR (Rn , Rm )
as an R-module, for every choice of m, n ‚àà Z‚â•0.
This will be done by means of matrices with entries in R. I trust that the
reader is familiar with the general notion of an m √ó n matrix; I have occasionally
used matrices in examples given in previous chapters, and they have showed up
in several exercises. An m √ó n matrix with entries in R is simply a choice of mn
elements of R. It is common to arrange these elements as an array consisting of m
rows and n columns:

as needed. The homomorphism induced by a nonzero matrix is manifestly nontriv-

The reader should attempt to remember the recipe associating a matrix A to

of R-modules

Proof. The reader will check that the correspondence is a bijective homomorphism

This is very good news, and it gets even better. Don‚Äôt forget that R-Mod is a
category; that is, we can compose morphisms. Therefore, there is a function9

Lemma 2.3. This diagram commutes. That is, the matrix corresponding to a
composition œï ‚ó¶ œà is the product of the matrices corresponding to œï and œà.
Proof. This follows immediately from the associativity of matrix multiplication:
for v ‚àà Rn and A ‚àà Mm,p (R), B ‚àà Mp,n (R),
A ¬∑ (B ¬∑ v) = (A ¬∑ B) ¬∑ v;
that is, the successive action of B and A is equivalent to the action of A ¬∑ B.
 
Here is a summary of the situation. Given an integral domain R, we can
construct a ‚Äòtoy category‚Äô as follows: we let Z‚â•0 be the set of objects, and we
deÔ¨Åne the set of morphisms Hom(n, m) to be the set of matrices Mm,n (R), with
composition given by the matrix product (cf. Exercise I.3.6). Then we have veriÔ¨Åed
that this toy category is ‚Äòessentially the same as‚Äô the category of Ô¨Ånitely generated
free R-modules and R-module homomorphisms10 .
For example, by virtue of Proposition 1.7, if R = k is a Ô¨Åeld, then R-Mod =
k-Vect consists exclusively of free k-modules. The toy category I just described is
a faithful snapshot of the category of Ô¨Ånite-dimensional k-vector spaces.
2.2. Change of basis. It is time to deal with the bad news.
Free modules are only deÔ¨Åned up to isomorphism, as is any structure satisfying
a universal property. Writing a free module as a direct sum R‚äïA amounts to
making the choice of one speciÔ¨Åc realization. As I pointed out at the end of ¬ß1.2,
by Lemma 1.5 this choice is equivalent to the choice of a basis of the module.
The price to pay for representing homomorphisms of free modules by matrices
is that this correspondence relies on the choice of a basis: we say that it is not
canonical. It is essential to be able to keep track of this choice. For Ô¨Ånitely generated
free modules, this also boils down to the action of a matrix, as we proceed to see.
Let F be a free module, and choose two bases A, B for F ; assume that F is

Summarizing this discussion,
Proposition 2.5. Let Œ± : F ‚Üí G be a homomorphism of Ô¨Ånitely generated free
modules, and let P be a matrix representing it with respect to any choice of bases
for F and G. Then the matrices representing Œ± with respect to any other choice of
bases are all and only the matrices of the form
M ¬∑ P ¬∑ N,
where M and N are invertible matrices.
DeÔ¨Ånition 2.6. Two matrices P, Q ‚àà Mm,n (R) are equivalent if they represent
the same homomorphism of free modules Rn ‚Üí Rm up to a choice of basis.
 
This is manifestly an equivalence relation. Properly speaking, the ‚Äòabstract‚Äô
homomorphism Œ± : F ‚Üí G is not represented by one matrix as much as by the whole
equivalence class with respect to this relation. Proposition 2.5 gives a computational
interpretation of equivalence of matrices: P and Q are equivalent if and only if there
are invertible M and N such that Q = M P N .
2.3. Elementary operations and Gaussian elimination. The idea is now to
capitalize on Proposition 2.5, as follows: given a homomorphism Œ± : F ‚Üí G between
two free modules, Ô¨Ånd ‚Äòspecial‚Äô bases in F and G so that the matrix of Œ± takes a
particularly convenient form. That is, look for a particularly convenient matrix in
each equivalence class with respect to the relation introduced in DeÔ¨Ånition 2.6.
For this, we can start with random bases in F and G, representing Œ± by a
matrix P and then (by Proposition 2.5) multiply P on the right and left by invertible
matrices, in order to bring P into whatever form is best suited for our needs.
There is an even more concrete way to deal with equivalence computationally.
Consider the following three ‚Äòelementary (row/column) operations‚Äô that can be
performed on a matrix P :
2. Homomorphisms of free modules, I
 321
‚Ä¢ switch two rows (or two columns) of P ;
‚Ä¢ add to one row (resp., column) a multiple of another row (resp., column);
‚Ä¢ multiply all entries in one row (or column) of P by a unit of R.
Proposition 2.7. Two matrices P, Q ‚àà Mm,n (R) are equivalent if Q may be
obtained from P by a sequence of elementary operations.
Proof. To see that elementary operations produce equivalent matrices, it suÔ¨Éces
(by Proposition 2.5) to express them as multiplications on the left or right12 by
invertible matrices. Indeed, these operations may be performed by suitably multi-
plying by the matrices obtained from the identity matrix by performing the same
operation. For example, multiplying on the left by
‚éõ
 ‚éû
1 0 0 0
‚éú0 0 0 1‚éü
‚éú

‚éù0 0 1 0‚éÝ
0 1 0 0
interchanges the second and fourth row of a 4 √ó n matrix; multiplying on the right
by
‚éõ

1 0 c

adds to the third column of a m √ó 3 matrix the c-multiple of the Ô¨Årst column. The
diligent reader will formalize this discussion and prove that all these matrices are
indeed invertible (Exercise 2.5).
 
The matrices corresponding to the elementary operations are called elementary
matrices. Linear algebra over arbitrary rings would be a great deal simpler if
Proposition 2.7 were an ‚Äòif and only if‚Äô statement. This boils down to the question
of whether every invertible matrix may be written as a product of elementary
matrices, that is, whether elementary matrices generate the ‚Äògeneral linear group‚Äô.
DeÔ¨Ånition 2.8. The n-th general linear group over the ring R, denoted GLn (R),
is the group of units in Mn (R), that is, the group of invertible n √ó n matrices with
entries in R.
 
Brief mentions of this notion have occurred already; cf. Example II.1.5 and
several exercises in previous chapters.
The elementary matrices are elements of GLn (R); in fact, the inverse of an
elementary matrix is (of course) again an elementary matrix. The following obser-
vation is surely known to the reader, in one form or another; in view of the foregoing
considerations, it says that the relation introduced in DeÔ¨Ånition 2.6 is under good
control over Ô¨Åelds.
12 Multiplying on the left acts on the rows of the matrix; multiplying on the right acts on its
columns.

is generated by elementary matrices.

Proposition 2.11 suÔ¨Éces to prove a weak form of one of our main goals (a classi-

Remark 2.12. As a consequence of the preceding considerations and arguing as
in the proof of Proposition 2.9, we see that GLn (R) is generated by elementary
matrices if R is a Euclidean domain. The reader may think that some cleverness in
handling Gaussian elimination may extend this to more general rings, but this will
not go too far: there are examples of PIDs R for which GLn(R) is not generated
by elementary matrices.
On the other hand, some cleverness does manage to produce a Smith normal
form for any matrix with entries in a PID: the presence of a good gcd suÔ¨Éces to
adapt the procedure sketched above (but one may need more than elementary row
and column operations). We will take a direct approach to this question in ¬ß5. 

2.6. ¬¨ A matrix with entries in a Ô¨Åeld is in row echelon form if
‚Ä¢ its nonzero rows are all above the zero rows and
‚Ä¢ the leftmost nonzero entry of each row is 1, and it is strictly to the right of the
leftmost nonzero entry of the row above it.
The matrix is further in reduced row echelon form if
‚Ä¢ the leftmost nonzero entry of each row is the only nonzero entry in its column.
The leftmost nonzero entries in a matrix in row echelon form are called pivots.
Prove that any matrix with entries in a Ô¨Åeld can be brought into reduced
echelon form by a sequence of elementary operations on rows. (This is what is
more properly called Gaussian elimination.) [2.7, 2.9]
2.7. ¬¨ Let M be a matrix with entries in a Ô¨Åeld and in reduced row echelon form

(Exercise 2.6). Prove that if a row vector r is a linear combination

nonzero rows of M , then ai equals the component of r at the position corresponding
to the pivot on the i-th row of M . Deduce that the nonzero rows of M are linearly
independent. [2.9]
2.8. ¬¨ Two matrices M , N are row-equivalent if M = P N for an invertible ma-
trix P . Prove that this is indeed an equivalence relation, and that two matrices
with entries in a Ô¨Åeld are row-equivalent if and only if one may be obtained from
the other by a sequence of elementary operations on rows. [2.9, 2.12]

2.10.  The row space of a matrix M is the span of its rows; the column space
of M is the span of its column. Prove that row-equivalent matrices have the same
row space and isomorphic column spaces. [2.12, ¬ß3.3]

spanned by the rows of M equals the number of nonzero rows in the reduced echelon

2.12. ¬¨ Let k be a Ô¨Åeld, and consider row-equivalence on Mm,n (k) (Exercise 2.8).

of kn of dimension ‚â§ m. [2.13, 2.14]
2.13. ¬¨ The set of subspaces of given dimension in a Ô¨Åxed vector space is called
a Grassmannian. In Exercise 2.12 you have constructed a bijection between the
Grassmannian of r-dimensional subspaces of k n and the set of reduced row echelon
matrices with n columns and r nonzero rows.
For r = 1, the Grassmannian is called the projective space. For a vector space V ,
the corresponding projective space PV is the set of ‚Äòlines‚Äô (1-dimensional subspaces)
in V . For V = kn , PV may be denoted Pn‚àí1
 k
 , and the Ô¨Åeld k may be omitted if it is
clear from the context. Show that Pn‚àí1
 k
 may

¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚à™ k1
 ‚à™ k0
 , and describe each of these subsets ‚Äògeometrically‚Äô.
Thus, P n‚àí1 is the union of n ‚Äòcells‚Äô15 , the largest one having dimension n ‚àí 1
(accounting for the choice of notation). Similarly, all Grassmannians may be written
as unions of cells. These are called Schubert cells.

cell decomposition entirely analogous to that of Pn‚àí1
 k
 . (This phenomenon will be
explained in Exercise VIII.5.17.) [VII.2.20, VIII.4.7, VIII.5.17]
2.14.  Show that the Grassmannian Grk (2, 4) of 2-dimensional subspaces of k4 is

3. Homomorphisms of free modules, II
The work in ¬ß2 accomplishes the goal of describing HomR (F, G), where F and G
are free R-modules of Ô¨Ånite rank; as we have seen, this can be done most explicitly
if, for example, R is a Ô¨Åeld or a Euclidean domain. We are not quite done, though:
even over Ô¨Åelds, it is important to ‚Äòunderstand the answer‚Äô‚Äîthat is, examine the
classiÔ¨Åcation of homomorphisms into Ô¨Ånitely many classes, highlighted at the end
of ¬ß2.3. Also, the frequent appearance of invertible matrices makes it necessary
to develop criteria to tell whether a given matrix is or is not in the general linear
group. I begin by pointing out a straightforward application of the classiÔ¨Åcation of
homomorphisms.
3.1. Solving systems of linear equations. A moment‚Äôs thought reveals that
Gaussian elimination, through the auspices of statements such as Proposition 2.9,
gives us a tool to solve systems of linear equations over a Ô¨Åeld or a Euclidean
domain16 . This is another point which does not seem to warrant a careful treatment
or memory gymnastic: writing things out carefully should take care of producing
tools as need be. To describe a typical situation, suppose

Such arguments can be packaged into convenient explicit procedures to solve
systems of linear equations. Again, it seems futile to list (or try to remember) any
such procedure; it seems more important to know what is behind such techniques,
so as to be able to come up with one when needed.
In any case, the reader should be able to justify such recipes rigorously. The
most famous one is possibly Cramer‚Äôs rule (Proposition 3.6) which relies on de-
terminants and is, incidentally, essentially useless in practice for any decent-size
problem.
3.2. The determinant. Let Œ± : F ‚Üí G be a homomorphism of free R-modules
of the same rank, and let A be the matrix representing Œ± with respect to a choice
of bases for F and G. As a consequence of Lemma 2.3 (cf. Exercise 2.3), Œ± is an
isomorphism if and only if A is a unit in Mn (R), that is, if and only if it is invertible
as a matrix with entries in R. This may be detected by computing the determinant
of A.

of A is the element

‚ÄîIf two rows or columns of a square matrix A agree, then det(A) = 0. Indeed,
it is enough to check this for matching columns; the case for rows follows by applying
the previous observation. If columns j and j of A are equal, the contribution to
det(A) due to a œÉ ‚àà Sn is equal and opposite in sign to the contribution due to the
product of œÉ and the transposition (jj ), so det(A) = 0.
‚ÄîSuppose A = (aij ) and B = (bij ) agree on all but at most one row: aij = bij
if i = k, for all j and some Ô¨Åxed k. Let cij := aij = bij for i = k, ckj := akj + bkj ,
and let C := (c ij ). Then
det(C) = det(A) + det(B).
This follows immediately from DeÔ¨Ånition 3.1 and distributivity. Applying this
observation to the transpose matrices gives an analogous statement for matrices
diÔ¨Äering at most along a column.
I will record more oÔ¨Écially the eÔ¨Äect of elementary operations on determinants:
Lemma 3.2. Let A be a square matrix with entries in an integral domain R.
‚Ä¢ Let A be obtained from A by switching two rows or two columns. Then
det(A ) = ‚àí det(A).
‚Ä¢ Let A be obtained from A by adding to a row (column) a multiple of another
row (column). Then det(A ) = det(A).
‚Ä¢ Let A be obtained from A by multiplying a row (column) by an element18
c ‚àà R. Then det(A ) = c det(A).
In other words, the eÔ¨Äect of an elementary operation on det(A) is the same as
multiplying det(A) by the determinant of the corresponding elementary matrix.

18For an elementary operation, c should be a unit; this restriction is not necessary here.

 
Of course Lemma 3.4 is simply stating the famous strategy computing a deter-
minant by expanding it with respect to your favorite row or column. This works
wonderfully for very small matrices and is totally useless for large ones, since the
number of computations needed to apply it grows as the factorial of the size of the
matrix. From a computational point of view, it makes much better sense to apply
Gaussian elimination and use the considerations preceding Proposition 3.3.
But Lemma 3.4 has the following important implication:

Proof of Proposition 3.3 for commutative rings. The Ô¨Årst point follows from
the second and from what we have just seen. Indeed, we have checked that
A ‚àà Mn (R) admits an inverse A‚àí1 ‚àà Mn (R) if det(A) is a unit in R; conversely,
if A admits an inverse A‚àí1 ‚àà Mn(R), then
det(A) det(A‚àí1) = det(AA‚àí1 ) = det(In ) = 1
by the second statement, so that det(A‚àí1 ) is the inverse of det(A) in R.
Thus, we just need to verify the second point, that is, the homomorphism
property of determinants. In order to verify this over every (commutative) ring,
it suÔ¨Éces to verify the ‚Äòuniversal‚Äô identity obtained by writing out the claimed
equality, for matrices with indeterminate entries. For example, for n = 2 the
statement is

any choice of x1 , . . . , y4 : indeed, Z is initial in Ring.
Now, we have veriÔ¨Åed that the homomorphism property holds over Ô¨Åelds; in
particular it holds over the Ô¨Åeld of fractions of Z[x11 , . . . , xnn, y11, . . . , ynn]. It
follows that it does hold in Z[x11 , . . . , xnn , y11, . . . , ynn ], and we are done.

carefully.

3.3. Rank and nullity. According to Proposition 2.10, each equivalence class of
matrices over a Ô¨Åeld has a representative of the type

 
Ir 0
.
0 0
The reason why two diÔ¨Äerent m √ó n matrices of this type are surely inequivalent
is that if Œ± : V ‚Üí W (with V and W free modules, vector spaces in this case) is
represented by a matrix of this form, then r is the dimension of the image of Œ±.
Therefore, matrices with diÔ¨Äerent r cannot represent the same Œ±.
This integer r is called the rank of the matrix and deserves some attention. I
will discuss it for matrices with entries in a Ô¨Åeld, leaving generalizations to more
general rings to the reader (for now at least).
The column (row) space of a matrix P over a Ô¨Åeld k is the span of the columns
(rows) of P . The column (row) rank of P is the dimension of the column (row)
space of P .
Proposition 3.7. The row rank of a matrix over a Ô¨Åeld k equals its column rank.
Proof. Equivalent matrices have the same ranks. Indeed, let P ‚àà Mm,n (k); the
row space of P consists of all row vectors

This shows that multiplication on the right by N maps the row space of P (isomor-
phically, since N is invertible) to the row space of Q; thus the two spaces have the
same dimension, as claimed. Minimal variations on the same argument show that
the column ranks of P and Q agree. (Cf. Exercise 2.10.)

The result upgrades easily to matrices over an arbitrary integral domain R
(applying the usual trick of embedding R in its Ô¨Åeld of fractions).
In view of Proposition 3.7, we can simply talk about the rank of a matrix:
DeÔ¨Ånition 3.8. Let M ‚àà Mm,n (k) be a matrix over a Ô¨Åeld k. The rank of M is
the dimension of its column (or, equivalently, row) space.
 
One way to rephrase Proposition 2.10 is that matrices over a Ô¨Åeld are classiÔ¨Åed
up to equivalence by their rank.
The foregoing considerations translate nicely in more abstract terms for a linear
map Œ± : V ‚Üí W between Ô¨Ånite-dimensional vector spaces over a Ô¨Åeld k. Using the
convenient language introduced in ¬ßIII.7.1, note that each Œ± determines an exact
sequence of vector spaces

DeÔ¨Ånition 3.9. The rank of Œ±, denoted rk Œ±, is the dimension of im Œ±. The nullity
of Œ± is dim(ker Œ±).
 
Claim 3.10. Let Œ± : V ‚Üí W be a linear map of Ô¨Ånite-dimensional vector spaces.
Then
(rank of Œ±) + (nullity of Œ±) = dim V.
Proof. Let n = dim V and m = dim W . By Proposition 2.10 we can represent Œ±
by an m √ó n matrix of the form

 
Ir 0
.
0 0
From this representation it is immediate that rk Œ± = r and the nullity of Œ± is n ‚àí r,
with the stated consequence.
 
Summarizing, rk Œ± equals the (column) rank of any matrix P representing Œ±;
similarly, the nullity of Œ± equals ‚Äòdim V minus the (row) rank‚Äô of P . Claim 3.10 is
the abstract version of the equality of row rank and column rank.

In terms of the topological motivation recalled above, Proposition 3.13 tells us
that the Euler characteristic of a manifold may be computed as the alternating sum
of the ranks of its homology, that is, of its Betti numbers.
Having come this far, I cannot refrain from mentioning the next, equally simple-
minded, generalization. The reader has surely noticed that the only tool used in
the proof of Proposition 3.13 was the ‚Äòadditivity‚Äô property of dimension, established
in Proposition 3.11: if

Proposition 3.13 is a formal consequence of this one property of dim.
With this in mind, we can reinterpret what we have just done in the following
curious way. Consider the category k-Vectf of Ô¨Ånite-dimensional k-vector spaces.

Claim 3.14. With notation as above, we have the following:
‚Ä¢ œáK ‚Äòis an Euler characteristic‚Äô, in the sense that it satisÔ¨Åes the formula given

The best way to convince the reader that this impressive claim is completely
trivial is to leave its proof to the reader (Exercise 3.16). The Ô¨Årst point is proved
by adapting the proof of Proposition 3.13; the second point is a harmless mixture
of universal properties; the third point follows from the second; and the last point
follows from the fact that dim(k) = 1 and the IBN property.
The last point is, in fact, rather anticlimactic: if the impressively abstract
Grothendieck group turns out to just be a copy of the integers, why bother deÔ¨Ån-
ing it? The answer is, of course, that this only stresses how special the category
k-Vectf is. The deÔ¨Ånition of the Grothendieck group can be given in any context in
338
 VI. Linear algebra
which complexes and a notion of exactness are available (for example, in the cate-
gory of Ô¨Ånitely generated20 modules over any ring). The formal arguments proving
Claim 3.14 will go through in any such context and provide us with a useful notion
of ‚Äòuniversal Euler characteristic‚Äô.
We will come back to complexes and homology in Chapter IX.

4. Presentations and resolutions
After this excursion into the idyllic world of free modules, we can come back to earth
and see if we have learned something that may be useful for more general situations.
Modules over a Ô¨Åeld are necessarily free (Proposition 1.7), not so for modules over
more general rings. In fact, this property will turn out to be a characterization of
Ô¨Åelds (Proposition 4.10).
It is important that we develop some understanding of nonfree modules. In this
section we will see that homomorphisms of free modules carry enough information
to allow us to deal with many nonfree modules.
4.1. Torsion. There are several ways in which a module M may fail to be free:
the most spectacular one is that M may have torsion.
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.1. Let M be an R-module. An element m ‚àà M is a torsion element
if {m} is linearly dependent, that is, if ‚àÉr ‚àà R, r = 0, such that rm = 0. The
subset of torsion elements of M is denoted TorR (M ). A module M is torsion-free
if TorR (M ) = {0}. A torsion module is a module M in which every element is a
torsion element.
 
The subscript R is usually omitted if there is no uncertainty about the base
ring.
A commutative ring is torsion-free as a module over itself if and only if it
is an integral domain; this is a good reason to limit the discussion to integral
domains in this chapter. Also, the reader will check (Exercise 4.1) that if R is an
integral domain, then Tor(M ) is a submodule of M . Equally easy is the following
observation:
Lemma 4.2. Submodules and direct sums of torsion-free modules are torsion-free.
Free modules over an integral domain are torsion-free.
Proof. The Ô¨Årst statement is immediate; the second follows from the Ô¨Årst, since
an integral domain is torsion-free as a module over itself.
 
Lemma 4.2 gives a good source of torsion-free modules: for example, ideals
in an integral domain R are torsion-free (because they are submodules of the free
module R1 ). In fact, ideals provide us with examples of another mechanism in
which a module may fail to be free.
Example 4.3. Let R = Z[x], and let I = (2, x). Then I is not a free R-module.
More generally, let I be any nonprincipal ideal of an integral domain R; then I is
a torsion-free module which is not free.
4. Presentations and resolutions
 341
Indeed, if I were free, then its rank would have to be 1 at most, by Propo-
sition 1.9 (a basis for I would be a linearly independent subset of R, and R has
rank 1 over itself); thus one element would suÔ¨Éce to generate I, and I would be
principal.
 
What is behind this example is a characterization of PIDs in terms of ‚Äòtorsion-
free submodules of a rank-1 free module‚Äô (Exercise 4.3). This is a facet of the main
result towards which we are heading, that is, the classiÔ¨Åcation of Ô¨Ånitely generated
modules over PIDs (Theorem 5.6). The gist of this classiÔ¨Åcation is that Ô¨Ånitely
generated modules over a PID can be decomposed into cyclic modules. We have
essentially proved this fact already for modules over Euclidean domains (it follows
from Proposition 2.11; see Exercise 2.19), and we have looked in great detail at the
particular case of Z-modules, a.k.a. abelian groups (¬ßIV.6); we are almost ready to
deal with the general case of PIDs.
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.4. An R-module M is cyclic if it is generated by a singleton, that is,
if M ‚àº
 = R/I for some ideal I of R.
 
The equivalence in the deÔ¨Ånition is hopefully clear to our reader, as an immedi-
ate consequence of the Ô¨Årst isomorphism theorem for modules (Corollary III.5.16).
If not, go back and (re)do Exercise III.6.16.
Cyclic modules are witness to the diÔ¨Äerence between Ô¨Åelds and more general
rings: over a Ô¨Åeld k, a cyclic module is just a 1-dimensional vector space, that is a
‚Äòcopy of k‚Äô; over more general rings, cyclic modules may be very interesting (think
of the many hours spent contemplating cyclic groups). In fact, we can tell that a
ring is a Ô¨Åeld by just looking at its cyclic modules:
Lemma 4.5. Let R be an integral domain. Assume that every cyclic R-module is
torsion-free. Then R is a Ô¨Åeld.
Proof. Let c ‚àà R, c = 0; then M = R/(c) is a cyclic module. Note that Tor(M ) =
M : indeed, the class of 1 generates R/(c) and belongs to Tor(M ) since c ¬∑ 1 is 0
mod (c) and c = 0. However, by hypothesis M is torsion-free; that is, Tor(M ) =
{0}. Therefore M = Tor(M ) is the zero module.
This shows R/(c) is the zero R-module; that is, (c) = (1). Therefore, c is a
unit. Thus every nonzero element of R is a unit, proving that R is a Ô¨Åeld.
 
Lemma 4.5 is a simple-minded illustration of the fact that we can study a ring R
by studying the module structure over R, that is, the category R-Mod, and that
we may not even need to look at the whole of R-Mod to be able to draw strong
conclusions about R.
4.2. Finitely presented modules and free resolutions. The ‚Äòright‚Äô way to
think of a cyclic R-module M is as a module which admits an epimorphism from R,
342
 VI. Linear algebra
viewing the latter as the free rank-1 R-module21 :
R1

 / 0.
The fact that M is surjected upon by a free R-module is nothing special. In fact,
every module M admits such an epimorphism:
R‚äïA
 / M
 / 0
provided that we are willing to take A large enough; if we are desperate, A = M
will surely do. This is immediate from the universal property of free modules; if
the reader does not agree, it is time to go back and review ¬ßIII.6.3. What makes
cyclic modules special is that A can be chosen to be a singleton.
We are now going to focus on a case which is also special, but not quite as
special as cyclic modules: Ô¨Ånitely generated modules are modules for which we can
choose A to be a Ô¨Ånite set (cf. ¬ßIII.6.4). Thus, we will assume that M admits an
epimorphism from a Ô¨Ånite-rank free module:
Rm

 / 0
for some integer m. The image by œÄ of the m vectors in a basis of Rm is a set of
generators for M .
Finitely generated modules are much easier to handle than arbitrary modules.
For example, an ideal of R can tell us whether a Ô¨Ånitely generated module is torsion.
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.6. The annihilator of an R-module M is
Ann R (M ) := {r ‚àà R | ‚àÄm ‚àà M, rm = 0}.
 
The subscript is usually omitted. The reader will check (Exercise 4.4) that
Ann(M ) is an ideal of R and that if M is a Ô¨Ånitely generated module and R is an
integral domain, then M is torsion if and only if Ann(M ) = 0.
We would like to develop tools to deal with Ô¨Ånitely generated modules. It
turns out that matrices allow us to describe a comfortably large collection of such
modules.
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.7. An R-module M is Ô¨Ånitely presented if for some positive integers
m, n there is an exact sequence
R n

 / 0.
Such a sequence is called a presentation of M .
 
In other words, Ô¨Ånitely presented modules are cokernels (cf. III.6.2) of homo-
morphisms between Ô¨Ånitely generated free modules. Everything about M must
be encoded in the homomorphism œï; therefore, we should be able to describe the
module M by studying the matrix corresponding to œï.
There is a gap between Ô¨Ånitely presented modules and Ô¨Ånitely generated mod-
ules, but on reasonable rings the two notions coincide:
21 In context the exactness of a sequence of R-modules will be understood, so the displayed
sequence is a way to denote the fact that there exists a surjective homomorphism of R-modules
from R to M ; cf. Example III.7.2. Also note the convention of denoting R by R1 when it is viewed
as a module over itself.

Lemma 4.8. If R is a Noetherian ring, then every Ô¨Ånitely generated R-module is
Ô¨Ånitely presented.
Proof. If M is a Ô¨Ånitely generated module, there is an exact sequence

 
Once we have gone one step to obtain generators and two steps to get a pre-
sentation, we should hit upon the idea to keep going:
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.9. A resolution of an R-module M by Ô¨Ånitely generated free modules
is an exact complex
...
 / Rm3
 / R m2
 / Rm1
 / Rm0
 / M
 / 0.
 
Iterating the argument proving Lemma 4.8 shows that if R is Noetherian, then
every Ô¨Ånitely generated module has a resolution as in DeÔ¨Ånition 4.9.
It is an important conceptual step to realize that M may be studied by studying
an exact complex of free modules
...
 / R m3
 / Rm2
 / Rm1
 / Rm0
resolving M , that is, such that M is the cokernel of the last map. The Rm0 piece
keeps track of the generators of M ; Rm1 accounts for the relations among these
generators; Rm2 records relations among the relations; and so on.
Developing this idea in full generality would take us too far for now: for exam-
ple, we would have to deal with the fact that every module admits many diÔ¨Äerent
resolutions (for example, we can bump up every mi by one by direct-summing each
term in the complex with a copy of R 1 , sent to itself by the maps in the complex).
We will do this very carefully later on, in Chapter IX.
However, we can already learn something by considering coarse questions, such
as ‚Äòhow long‚Äô a resolution can be. A priori, there is no reason to expect a free
resolutions to be ‚ÄòÔ¨Ånite‚Äô, that is, such that mi = 0 for i  0. Such Ô¨Åniteness
conditions tell us something special about the base ring R.
The Ô¨Årst natural question of this type is, for which rings R is it the case that
every Ô¨Ånitely generated R-module M has a free resolution ‚Äòof length 0‚Äô, that is,
stopping at m0 ? That would mean that there is an exact sequence
0
 / Rm0
 / M
 / 0.
Therefore, M itself must be free. What does this say about R?
Proposition 4.10. Let R be an integral domain. Then R is a Ô¨Åeld if and only if
every Ô¨Ånitely generated R-module is free.
344
 VI. Linear algebra
Proof. If R is a Ô¨Åeld, then every R-module is free, by Proposition 1.7. For the
converse, assume that every Ô¨Ånitely generated R-module is free; in particular, every
cyclic module is free; in particular, every cyclic module is torsion-free. But then R
is a Ô¨Åeld, by Lemma 4.5.
 
The next natural question concerns rings for which Ô¨Ånitely generated modules
admit free resolutions of length 1. It is convenient to phrase the question in stronger
terms, that is, to require that for every Ô¨Ånitely generated R-module M and every
beginning of a free resolution
R m0
 œÄ
 / M
 / 0,
the resolution can be completed to a length 1 free resolution. This would amount
to demanding that there exist an integer m1 and an R-module homomorphism
Rm1 ‚Üí Rm0 such that the sequence
0
 / Rm1
 / R m0
 œÄ
 / M
 / 0
is exact. Equivalently, this condition requires that the module ker œÄ of relations
among the m0 generators necessarily be free.
Claim 4.11. Let R be an integral domain satisfying this property. Then R is
a PID.
Proof. Let I be an ideal of R, and apply the condition to M = R/I. Since we
have an epimorphism
R1
 œÄ
 / R/I
 / 0,
the condition says that ker œÄ is free; that is, I is free. Since I is a free submodule
of R, which is free of rank 1, I must be free of rank ‚â§ 1 by Proposition 1.9.
Therefore I is generated by one element, as needed.
 
The classiÔ¨Åcation result for Ô¨Ånitely generated modules over PIDs (Theorem 5.6),
which I keep bringing up, will essentially be a converse to Claim 4.11: the mysterious
condition requiring free resolutions of Ô¨Ånitely generated modules to have length at
most 1 turns out to be a characterization of PIDs, just as the length 0 condition is
a characterization of Ô¨Åelds (as proved in Proposition 4.10). We will work this out
in ¬ß5.2.
4.3. Reading a presentation. Let us return to the brilliant idea of studying a
Ô¨Ånitely presented module M by studying a homomorphism of free modules
(*)

Incidentally, a number of software packages can perform sophisticated oper-

These packages rely precisely on the correspondence between modules and matrices:
with due care, every operation on modules (such as direct sums, tensors, quotients,
etc.) can be executed on the corresponding matrices. For example,

Proposition 4.13. Let A be a matrix with entries in an integral domain R, and
let B be obtained from A by any sequence of the following operations:
‚Ä¢ switch two rows or two columns;
‚Ä¢ add to one row (resp., column) a multiple of another row (resp., column);
‚Ä¢ multiply all entries in one row (or column) by a unit of R;
‚Ä¢ if a unit is the only nonzero entry in a row (or column), remove the row and
column containing that entry.
Then B represents the same R-module as A, up to isomorphism.
Proof. The Ô¨Årst three operations are the ‚Äòelementary operations‚Äô of ¬ß2.3, and they
transform a matrix into an equivalent one (by Proposition 2.7); as observed above,
this does not aÔ¨Äect the corresponding module, up to isomorphism.
As for the fourth operation, if u is a unit and the only nonzero entry in (say) a
row, then by applications of the second elementary operation we may assume that
u is also the only nonzero entry in its column; without loss of generality we may
assume that u is in fact the (1,1) entry of the matrix; that is, the matrix is in block
form:

Exercises

By virtue of Gaussian elimination, the ‚Äòalgorithm‚Äô implicitly described in Propo-
sition 4.13 will work without fail over Euclidean domains (e.g., over the polynomial
ring in one variable over a Ô¨Åeld), in the sense that it will identify the Ô¨Ånitely
generated module corresponding to a matrix with an explicit direct sum of cyclic
modules, as in Example 4.14. This is too much to expect over more general rings,
since in general elementary transformations do not generate GL; cf. Remark 2.12.
Exercises
4.1.  Prove that if R is an integral domain and M is an R-module, then Tor(M )
is a submodule of M . Give an example showing that the hypothesis that R is an
integral domain is necessary. [¬ß4.1]
4.2.  Let M be a module over an integral domain R, and let N be a torsion-free
module. Prove that HomR(M, N ) is torsion-free. In particular, HomR (M, R) is
torsion-free. (We will run into this fact again; see Proposition VIII.5.16.) [¬ßVIII.5.5]
4.3.  Prove that an integral domain R is a PID if and only if every submodule
of R itself is free. [¬ß4.1, 5.13]
4.4.  Let R be a commutative ring and M an R-module.
‚Ä¢ Prove that Ann(M ) is an ideal of R.
‚Ä¢ If R is an integral domain and M is Ô¨Ånitely generated, prove that M is torsion
if and only if Ann(M ) = 0.
‚Ä¢ Give an example of a torsion module M over an integral domain, such that
Ann(M ) = 0. (Of course this example cannot be Ô¨Ånitely generated!)
[¬ß4.2, ¬ß5.3]
4.5. ¬¨ Let M be a module over a commutative ring R. Prove that an ideal I of R
is the annihilator of an element of M if and only if M contains an isomorphic copy
of R/I (viewed as an R-module).
The associated primes of M are the prime ideals among the ideals Ann(m),
for m ‚àà M . The set of the associated primes of a module M is denoted AssR (M ).
Note that every prime in AssR (M ) contains AnnR (M ). [4.6, 4.7, 5.16]
4.6. ¬¨ Let M be a module over a commutative ring R, and consider the family
of ideals Ann(m), as m ranges over the nonzero elements of M . Prove that the
maximal elements in this family are prime ideals of R. Conclude that if R is
Noetherian, then AssR(M ) = ‚àÖ (cf. Exercise 4.5). [4.7, 4.9]
4.7. ¬¨ Let R be a commutative Noetherian ring, and let M be a Ô¨Ånitely generated
module over R. Prove that M admits a Ô¨Ånite series
