5. ClassiÔ¨Åcation of Ô¨Ånitely generated modules over PIDs
It is Ô¨Ånally time to prove the classiÔ¨Åcation theorem for Ô¨Ånitely generated modules
over arbitrary PIDs. We have already proved this statement in the special case of
Ô¨Ånite Z-modules (¬ßIV.6), and the diligent reader has worked out a proof in the less
special case of Ô¨Ånitely generated modules over Euclidean domains, in Exercise 2.19.
Now we go for the real thing.
350
 VI. Linear algebra
5.1. Submodules of free modules. Recall (Lemma 4.2, Example 4.3) that a
submodule of a free module over an arbitrary integral domain R is necessarily
torsion-free but need not be free. For example, the ideal I = (x, y) of R = k[x, y]
(with k a Ô¨Åeld, for example) is torsion-free as an R-module, but not free: for this
to become really, really evident, it is helpful to rename x = a, y = b when these
are viewed as elements of I and observe that a and b are not linearly independent
over k[x, y], since ya ‚àí xb = 0.
On the other hand, submodules of a free module over a Ô¨Åeld are automatically
free: simply because every module over a Ô¨Åeld is free (Proposition 1.7). It is
reasonable to expect that ‚Äòsome property in between‚Äô being a Ô¨Åeld and being a
friendly UFD such as k[x, y] will guarantee that a submodule of a free module is
free. We will now prove that this property is precisely that of being a principal
ideal domain.
Proposition 5.1. Let R be a PID, let F be a Ô¨Ånitely generated free module over R,
and let M ‚äÜ F be a submodule. Then M is free.
We will actually prove a more precise result, in view of the full statement of
the classiÔ¨Åcation theorem: we will show that there is a basis (x1 , . . . , xn) of F and
elements a1 , . . . , am of R (with m ‚â§ n) such that
y1 = a1 x1 , . . . ,
 ym = am xm
form a basis of M . That is, not only do we prove that M is free, but we also show
that there are ‚Äòcompatible‚Äô bases of F and M .
In order to do this, we may of course assume that M = 0: otherwise there is
nothing to prove. Most of the work will then go into showing that if M = 0, we can
split one direct summand oÔ¨Ä M ; iterating this process will prove the proposition23 .
This is where the PID condition is used, so I will single out the main technical point
into the following statement.
Lemma 5.2. Let R be a PID, let F be a Ô¨Ånitely generated free module over R, and
let M ‚äÜ F be a nonzero submodule. Then there exist a ‚àà R, x ‚àà F , y ‚àà M , and
submodules F  ‚äÜ F and M  ‚äÜ M , such that y = ax = 0, M  = F  ‚à© M , and
F = x ‚äï F  ,
 M = y ‚äï M .
The reader would Ô¨Ånd it instructive to pause a moment and try to imagine how
the PID hypothesis may enter into the proof of this lemma. The PID hypothesis is
a hypothesis on R, so the question is, where is the special copy of R to which we
can apply it? Don‚Äôt read on until you have spent a moment thinking about this.
It is tempting to look for this copy of R among the ‚Äòfactors‚Äô of F = Rn : for
example, we could map F to R by projecting onto the Ô¨Årst component:
œÄ(r1 , . . . , rn ) = r1.
But there is nothing special about the Ô¨Årst component of R n; in fact there is
nothing special about the particular basis chosen for F , that is, the particular
23 In a loose sense, this is the same strategy we employed in the proof of the classiÔ¨Åcation
result for Ô¨Ånite abelian groups in ¬ßIV.6.
5. ClassiÔ¨Åcation of Ô¨Ånitely generated modules over PIDs
 351
representation of F as Rn . Therefore, this does not look too promising. The way
out of this bind is to democratically map F to R in every possible way: we consider
all homomorphisms œï : F ‚Üí R. This is a set that does not depend on any extra
choice, so it is more likely to carry the information we need.
For each œï, œï(M ) is a submodule of R, that is, an ideal of R; so we have a
chance to use the PID hypothesis with proÔ¨Åt. In fact, the much weaker fact that
R is Noetherian guarantees already that there must be some homomorphism Œ± for
which Œ±(M ) is maximal among all ideals œï(M ). This copy of R, that is, the target
of such a homomorphism Œ±, is special for a good reason, independent of inessential
choices. The fact that R is a PID tells us that Œ±(M ) is principal, and then we are
clearly in business.
Here is the formal argument:
Proof. For all œï ‚àà HomR (F, R), œï(M ) is a submodule of R, that is, an ideal.
The family of all these ideals is nonempty, and PIDs are Noetherian; therefore (by
Proposition V.1.1) there exists a maximal element in the family, say Œ±(M ), for a
homomorphism Œ± : M ‚Üí R. The fact that M = 0 implies immediately that some
œï(M ) = 0 (for example, take for œï the projection to a suitable factor of Rn ); hence
Œ±(M ) = 0.
Since R is a PID, Œ±(M ) is principal: Œ±(M ) = (a) for some a ‚àà R, a = 0. Since
a ‚àà Œ±(M ), there exists an element y ‚àà M , y = 0, such that Œ±(y) = a. These are
the elements a, y mentioned in the statement.
I claim that a divides œï(y) for all œï ‚àà HomR (F, R). Indeed, let b be a generator
of (a, œï(y)) (which exists since R is a PID; of course b is simply a gcd of a and œï(y)),
and let r, s ‚àà R such that b = ra+sœï(y); consider the homomorphism œà := rŒ±+sœï.
Since a ‚àà (b), we have Œ±(M ) ‚äÜ (b). On the other hand
b = ra + sœï(y) = (rŒ± + sœï)(y) = œà(y) ‚àà œà(M );
therefore (b) ‚äÜ œà(M ). It follows that Œ±(M ) ‚äÜ œà(M ), and by maximality Œ±(M ) =
œà(M ); hence (a) = (b), and in particular a divides œï(y), as claimed.
Let y = (s1 , . . . , sn ) as an element of F = Rn . Each s i is the image of y by a
homomorphism F ‚Üí R (that is, the i-th projection), so a divides all of them by
what we just proved. Therefore ‚àÉr1 , . . . , rn ‚àà R such that si = ari ; let
x = (r1 , . . . , rn ) ‚àà F.
This is the element x mentioned in the statement. By construction, y = ax. Fur-
ther, a = Œ±(y) = Œ±(ax) = aŒ±(x); since R is an integral domain and a = 0, this
implies Œ±(x) = 1.
Finally, we let F  = ker Œ± and M  = F  ‚à© M , and we can proceed to verify the
stated direct sums.
First, every z ‚àà F may be written as
z = Œ±(z)x + (z ‚àí Œ±(z)x);
by linearity

Once Lemma 5.2 is established, the proof of Proposition 5.1 is mere busywork:
Proof of Proposition 5.1. If M = 0, we are done. If not, applying Lemma 5.2
to M ‚äÜ F produces an element y1 ‚àà M and a submodule M (1) ‚äÜ M such that
M = y1 ‚äï M (1) .
If M (1) = 0, we are done; otherwise, apply Lemma 5.2 again (to M (1) ‚äÜ F ) to
obtain y2 ‚àà M (1) and M (2) ‚äÜ M (1) so that
M = y1  ‚äï (y2  ‚äï M (2)).
This process may be continued, producing elements y1 , . . . , ym ‚àà M such that
M = y1  ‚äï ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚äï ym  ‚äï M (m) ,
so long as the module M (m) is nonzero. However, by Proposition 1.9 we know
m ‚â§ n, since y1 , . . . , ym are linearly independent in F . It follows that the process
must stop; that is, M (m) = 0 for some m ‚â§ n. That is,
M = y1  ‚äï ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚äï ym 
is free, as needed.

Note that the proof has not used part of the result of Lemma 5.2, that is, the
fact that the ‚Äòfactor‚Äô y of M is a submodule of a corresponding factor x of F .
This is needed in order to upgrade Proposition 5.1 along the lines mentioned after
its statement. Here is that stronger statement:
Corollary 5.3. Let R be a PID, let F be a Ô¨Ånitely generated free module over R,
and let M ‚äÜ F be a submodule. Then there exist a basis (x1 , . . . , xn ) of F and
nonzero elements a1, . . . , am of R (m ‚â§ n) such that (a1 x1, . . . , am xm ) is a basis
of M . Further, we may assume a1 | a 2 | ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ | am .
This statement should be compared with Proposition 2.11: it amounts to a
‚ÄòSmith normal form over PIDs‚Äô; cf. Remark 2.12.

Proof. Now that we know that submodules of a free module are free, we see that
the submodule F  ‚äÜ F produced in Lemma 5.2 is free. The Ô¨Årst part of the
statement then follows from Lemma 5.2, by an inductive argument analogous to
the proof of Proposition 5.1, and is left to the reader.
The most delicate part of the statement is the divisibility condition. By induc-
tion it suÔ¨Éces to prove a1 | a2 , and for this we refer back to the proof of Lemma 5.2:
(a1) is maximal among the ideals œï(M ) of R, as œï ranges over all homomorphisms
F ‚Üí R. Consider then any24 homomorphism œï such that œï(x1 ) = œï(x2 ) = 1.
Since œï(y1) = œï(a1x1 ) = a1 , we have (a1 ) ‚äÜ œï(M ); by maximality (a1 ) = œï(M ).
Therefore a2 = œï(a2 x2 ) = œï(y2 ) ‚àà œï(M ) = (a1 ), proving a1 | a2 as needed.
 
The logic of the argument given in this section is somewhat tricky: it takes
Lemma 5.2 to prove Proposition 5.1; but then it takes Proposition 5.1 (proving
that F is free) to revisit Lemma 5.2 and squeeze from it the stronger Corollary 5.3.
5.2. PIDs and resolutions. Proposition 5.1 allows us to complete the circle of
ideas begun in ¬ß4.2.

Remark 5.8. To summarize, the information carried by a torsion module over a
PID is equivalent to the choice of a selection of nonzero, nonunit ideals (a1 ), . . . , (am )
such that
(a1) ‚äá (a2 ) ‚äá ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚äá (am ).
We have seen that (am) is in fact the annihilator ideal of the module; it would be
nice if we had a similarly explicit description of all invariants (a1 ), . . . , (am ). As a
preview of coming attractions, we could call the ideal
(a1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ am)
the characteristic ideal of the module27. In situations in which we can compute
both the annihilator and the characteristic ideal, comparing them may lead to
27 Warning: This does not seem to be standard terminology.
Exercises
 357
strong conclusions about the module. For example, a torsion module is cyclic if
and only if its annihilator and characteristic ideals coincide.
Further, the prime ideals appearing in Theorem 5.6 have been characterized
as the prime ideals containing the annihilator ideal. They may just as well be
characterized as those prime ideals containing the characteristic ideal, as the reader
will check (Exercise 5.15).
Finally, note that from this point of view it is immediate that the characteristic
ideal is contained in the annihilator ideal. We will run into this fact again, in an
important application (Theorem 6.11).

6. Linear transformations of a free module
One beautiful application of the classiÔ¨Åcation theorem for Ô¨Ånitely generated mod-
ules over PIDs is the determination of ‚Äòspecial‚Äô forms for matrices of linear maps of
a vector space to itself.
Several fundamental concepts are associated with the general notion of a linear
map from a vector space to itself, and we will review these concepts in this section.
Not surprisingly, much of the discussion may be carried out for free modules over
any integral domain R, and we will stay at this level of generality in (most of) the
section. Theorem 5.6 will be used with great proÔ¨Åt when R is a Ô¨Åeld, in the next
section.
6.1. Endomorphisms and similarity. Let R be an integral domain. We have
considered in some detail the module HomR (F, G) of R-module homomorphisms
F ‚Üí G
between two free modules. For example, I have argued that describing such homo-
morphisms for Ô¨Ånitely generated free modules F , G amounts to describing matrices
with entries in R, up to ‚Äòequivalence‚Äô: the equivalence relation introduced in ¬ß2.2
accounts for the (arbitrary) choice of bases for F and G.
We now shift the focus a little and consider the special case in which F = G,
that is, the R-module EndR (F ) of endomorphisms Œ± of a Ô¨Åxed free R-module F :
F
 Œ±
 / F .
Note that EndR (F ) is in fact an R-algebra: the operation of composition makes it
a ring, compatibly with the R-module structure (cf. Exercise 2.3).
From the point of view championed in ¬ß2, the two copies of F appearing
in EndR (F ) = HomR (F, F ) are unrelated; they are just (any) isomorphic repre-
sentatives of a free module of a given rank. There are circumstances, however, in
which we need to really choose one representative F and stick with it: that is, view
Œ± as acting from a selected free module F to itself, not just to an isomorphic copy
of itself. In this situation we also say that Œ± is a linear transformation of F , or an
operator on F .
From this diÔ¨Äerent point of view it makes sense to compare elements of F
‚Äòbefore and after‚Äô we apply Œ±; for example, we could ask whether for some v ‚àà F
360
 VI. Linear algebra
we may have Œ±(v) = Œªv for some Œª ‚àà R, or more generally whether a submodule
M of F may be sent to itself by Œ±. In other words, we can compare the action of Œ±
with the identity29 I : F ‚Üí F .
In terms of matrix representations (in the Ô¨Ånite rank case) the description of Œ±
can be carried out as we did in ¬ß2, but with one interesting twist. In ¬ß2.2 we dealt
with how the matrix representation of a homomorphism changes when we change
bases in the source and in the target. As we are now identifying source and target,
we must choose the same basis for both source and target. This leads to a diÔ¨Äerent
notion of equivalence of matrices:
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.1. Two square matrices A, B ‚àà Mn (R) are similar if they represent
the same homomorphism F ‚Üí F of a free rank-n module F to itself, up to the
choice of a basis for F .
 
This is clearly an equivalence relation. The analog of Proposition 2.5 for this
new notion is readily obtained:
Proposition 6.2. Two matrices A, B ‚àà Mn (R) are similar if and only if there
exists an invertible matrix P such that
‚àí1B = P AP .
The reader who has really understood Proposition 2.5 will not need any detailed
proof of this statement: it should be apparent from staring at the butterÔ¨Çy diagram

Proposition 6.2 suggests a useful equivalence relation among endomorphisms:
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.3. Two R-module homomorphisms of a free module F to itself,
Œ±, Œ≤ : F ‚Üí F,
29 The existence of the identity is one of the axioms of a category; every now and then, it is
handy to be able to invoke it.
6. Linear transformations of a free module
 361
are similar if there exists an automorphism œÄ : F ‚Üí F such that
Œ≤ = œÄ ‚ó¶ Œ± ‚ó¶ œÄ‚àí1 .

In the Ô¨Ånite rank case, similar endomorphisms are represented by similar matri-
ces, and two endomorphisms Œ±, Œ≤ are similar if and only if they may be represented
by the same matrix by choosing appropriate (possibly diÔ¨Äerent) bases on F . The
interesting invariants determined by similar endomorphisms will turn out (not sur-
prisingly) to be the same.
From a group-theoretic viewpoint, similarity is an eminently natural notion
to study: the group GL(F ) = AutR (F ) of automorphisms of a free module acts
on EndR (F ) by conjugation, and two endomorphisms Œ±, Œ≤ are similar if and only
if they are in the same orbit under this action. If the reader prefers matrices, the
group GLn (R) of invertible n √ó n matrices with entries in R acts by conjugation on
the module Mn (R) of square n √ó n matrices, and two matrices are similar if and
only if they are in the same orbit.
Natural questions arise in this context. For example, we should look for ways
to distinguish diÔ¨Äerent orbits: given two endomorphisms Œ±, Œ≤, can we eÔ¨Äectively
decide whether Œ± and Œ≤ are similar? One way to approach this question is to
determine invariants which can distinguish diÔ¨Äerent orbits. Better still, we can
look for ‚Äòspecial‚Äô representatives within each orbit, that is, of a given similarity
class. In other words, given an endomorphism Œ± : F ‚Üí F , Ô¨Ånd a basis of F with
respect to which the matrix description of Œ± has a particular, predictable shape.
Then Œ±, Œ≤ are similar if these distinguished representatives coincide.
This is what we are eventually going to squeeze out of Theorem 5.6, in the
friendly case of vector spaces over a Ô¨Åxed Ô¨Åeld.
6.2. The characteristic and minimal polynomials of an endomorphism.
Let Œ± ‚àà EndR(F ) be an endomorphism of a free R-module; henceforth I am often
tacitly going to assume that F is Ô¨Ånitely generated (this is necessary anyway as
we are aiming to translate what we do into the language of matrices). We want
to identify invariants of the similarity class of Œ±, that is, quantities that will not
change if we replace Œ± with a similar linear map Œ≤ ‚àà EndR (F ).
For example, the determinant is such a quantity:
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.4. Let Œ± ‚àà End R (F ). The determinant of Œ± is det(Œ±) := det(A),
where A is the matrix representing Œ± with respect to any choice of basis of F . 
Of course there is something to check here, that is, that det(A) does not depend
on the choice of basis. But we know (Proposition 6.2) that A, B represent the
same endomorphism Œ± if and only if there exists an invertible matrix P such that
‚àí1B = P AP ; then
‚àí1 ‚àí1det(B) = det(P AP ) = det(P ) det(A) det(P ) = det(A)
by Proposition 3.3. Thus the determinant is indeed independent of the choice of
basis. Essentially the same argument shows that if Œ± and Œ≤ are similar linear
transformations, then det(Œ±) = det(Œ≤) (Exercise 6.3).
362
 VI. Linear algebra
By Proposition 3.3, a linear transformation Œ± is invertible if and only if det(Œ±)
is a unit in R. If R is a Ô¨Åeld, this of course means simply det(Œ±) = 0. Even if R is
not a Ô¨Åeld, det(Œ±) = 0 says something interesting:
Proposition 6.5. Let Œ± be a linear transformation of a free R-module F = ‚àº
 Rn .
Then det(Œ±) = 0 if and only if Œ± is injective.
Proof. Embed R in its Ô¨Åeld of fractions K, and view Œ± as a linear transformation
of K n ; note that the determinant of Œ± is the same whether it is computed over R
or over K. Then Œ± is injective as a linear transformation Rn ‚Üí Rn if and only if it
is injective as a linear transformation K n ‚Üí K n , if and only if it is invertible as a
linear transformation K n ‚Üí K n , if and only if det(Œ±) = 0.
 
Of course over integral domains other than Ô¨Åelds Œ± may fail to be surjective even
if det(Œ±) = 0; and care is required even over Ô¨Åelds, if we abandon the hypothesis
that the free modules are Ô¨Ånitely generated (cf. Exercises 6.4 and 6.5).
Another quantity that is invariant under similarity is the trace. The trace of a
square matrix A = (aij ) ‚àà Mn (R) is

 n
tr(A) :=
 aii ,
i=1
that is, the sum of its diagonal entries.
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.6. Let Œ± ‚àà EndR (F ). The trace of Œ± is deÔ¨Åned to be tr(Œ±) := tr(A),
where A is the matrix representing Œ± with respect to any choice of basis of F . 
Again, we have to check that this is independent of the choice of basis; the key
is the following computation.
Lemma 6.7. Let A, B ‚àà Mn (R). Then tr(AB) = tr(BA).
n



 aik bki.
i=1 k=1
This expression is symmetric in A, B, so it must equal tr(BA).
 
With this understood, if B = P AP ‚àí1, then
tr(B) = tr((P A)P ‚àí1) = tr(P ‚àí1 (P A)) = tr((P ‚àí1 P )A) = tr(A),
showing (by Proposition 6.2) that similar matrices have the same trace, as needed.
Again, the reader will check that the same token shows that similar linear
transformations have the same trace.
The trace and determinant of an endomorphism Œ± of a rank-n free module are
in fact just two of a sequence of n invariants, which can be nicely collected together
into the characteristic polynomial of Œ±.
6. Linear transformations of a free module

DeÔ¨Ånition 6.8. Let F be a free R-module, and let Œ± ‚àà EndR (F ). Denote by I
the identity map F ‚Üí F . The characteristic polynomial of Œ± is the polynomial
PŒ± (t) := det(tI ‚àí Œ±) ‚àà R[t].

‚Ä¢ If Œ± and Œ≤ are similar, then PŒ± (t) = PŒ≤ (t).
Proof. The Ô¨Årst point is immediate, and the third is checked by setting t = 0. To

are invariant under similarity; as far as I know, trace and determinant are the only
ones that have special names.
Determinants, traces, and more generally the characteristic polynomial can
show very quickly that two linear transformations are not similar; but do they tell
us unfailingly when two transformations are similar? In general, the answer is no,
even over Ô¨Åelds. For example, the two matrices

‚àí1the identity and clearly only the identity is similar to the identity: P IP = I for

364
 VI. Linear algebra
Therefore, the equivalence relation deÔ¨Åned by prescribing that two transforma-
tions have the same characteristic polynomial is coarser than similarity. This hints
that there must be other interesting quantities associated with a linear transforma-
tion and invariant under similarity.
We are going to understand this much more thoroughly in a short while (at least
over Ô¨Åelds), but we can already gain some insight by contemplating another kind
of ‚Äòpolynomial‚Äô information, which also turns out to be invariant under similarity.
As EndR (F ) is an R-algebra, we can evaluate every polynomial
f (t) = rm tm + rm‚àí1 tm‚àí1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + r0 ‚àà R[t]
at any Œ± ‚àà EndR (F ):
f (Œ±) = rm Œ±m + rm‚àí1 Œ±m‚àí1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + r0 ‚àà EndR (F ).
In other words, we can perform these operations in the ring EndR (F ); multiplica-
tion by r ‚àà R amounts to composition with rI ‚àà EndR (F ), and Œ±k stands for the
k-fold composition Œ± ‚ó¶ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚ó¶ Œ± of Œ± with itself. The set of polynomials such that
f (Œ±) = 0 is an ideal of R[t] (Exercise 6.7), which I will denote IŒ± and call the
annihilator ideal of Œ±.
Lemma 6.10. If Œ± and Œ≤ are similar, then IŒ± = IŒ≤ .
Proof. By hypothesis there exists an invertible œÄ such that Œ≤ = œÄ ‚ó¶ Œ± ‚ó¶ œÄ‚àí1 . As
Œ≤ k = (œÄ ‚ó¶ Œ± ‚ó¶ œÄ ‚àí1 )k = (œÄ ‚ó¶ Œ± ‚ó¶ œÄ ‚àí1 ) ‚ó¶ (œÄ ‚ó¶ Œ± ‚ó¶ œÄ‚àí1 ) ‚ó¶ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚ó¶ (œÄ ‚ó¶ Œ± ‚ó¶ œÄ‚àí1 ) = œÄ ‚ó¶ Œ±k ‚ó¶ œÄ ‚àí1 ,
we see that for all f (t) ‚àà R[t] we have
f (Œ≤) = œÄ ‚ó¶ f (Œ±) ‚ó¶ œÄ ‚àí1 .
It follows immediately that f (Œ±) = 0 ‚áê‚áí f (Œ≤) = 0, which is the statement.

6. Linear transformations of a free module

Theorem 6.11 (Cayley-Hamilton). Let PŒ± (t) be the characteristic polynomial of
the linear transformation Œ± ‚àà End R (F ). Then
PŒ± (Œ±) = 0.
This beautiful observation can be proved directly by judicious use of Cramer‚Äôs
rule30 , in the form of Corollary 3.5; cf. Exercise 6.9. In any case, the Cayley-
Hamilton theorem will become essentially evident once we connect these linear
algebra considerations with the classiÔ¨Åcation theorem for Ô¨Ånitely generated modules
over a PID; the adventurous reader can already look back at Remark 5.8 and Ô¨Ågure
out why the Cayley-Hamilton theorem is obvious.
If R is an arbitrary integral domain, we cannot expect too much of R[t], and it
seems hard to say something a priori concerning IŒ± . However, consider the Ô¨Åeld of
fractions K of R (¬ßV.4.2); viewing Œ± as an element of EndK (K n ) (that is, viewing
the entries of a matrix representation of Œ± as elements of K, rather than R), Œ± will
have an annihilator ideal IŒ± (K)
 ‚Äòover K‚Äô, and it is clear that
IŒ± = I Œ± (K) ‚à© R[t].
The advantage of considering IŒ± (K)
 ‚äÜ K[t] is that K[t] is a PID, and it follows that
IŒ± (K)
 has a (unique) monic generator.
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.12. Let F be a free R-module, and let Œ± ‚àà EndR (F ). Let K be
the Ô¨Åeld of fractions of R. The minimal polynomial of Œ± is the monic generator
mŒ± (t) ‚àà K[t] of IŒ± (K)
 .
 
With this terminology, the Cayley-Hamilton theorem amounts to the assertion
that the minimal polynomial divides the characteristic polynomial: mŒ± (t) | PŒ± (t).
Of course the situation is simpliÔ¨Åed, at least from an expository point of view,
if R is itself a Ô¨Åeld: then K = R, mŒ± (t) ‚àà R[t], and IŒ± = (mŒ± (t)). This is one
reason why we will eventually assume that R is a Ô¨Åeld.
6.3. Eigenvalues, eigenvectors, eigenspaces.
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.13. Let F be a free R-module, and let Œ± ‚àà EndR (F ) be a linear
transformation of F . A scalar Œª ‚àà R is an eigenvalue for Œ± if there exists v ‚àà F ,
v = 0, such that
Œ±(v) = Œªv.
 
For example, 0 is an eigenvalue for Œ± precisely when Œ± has a nontrivial kernel.
The notion of eigenvalue is one of the most important in linear algebra, if not in
algebra, if not in mathematics, if not in the whole of science. The set of eigenvalues
of a linear transformation is called its spectrum. Spectra of operators show up
everywhere, from number theory to diÔ¨Äerential equations to quantum mechanics.
The spectrum of a ring, encountered brieÔ¨Çy in ¬ßIII.4.3, was so named because it
may be interpreted (in important motivating contexts) as a spectrum in the sense
30 Here is an even more direct ‚Äòproof‚Äô: PŒ±
 (Œ±) = det(Œ±I ‚àí Œ±) = det(0) = 0. Unfortunately this
does not work: in the deÔ¨Ånition det(tI ‚àí Œ±) of the characteristic polynomial, t is assumed to act
as a scalar and cannot be replaced by Œ±. Applying Cramer‚Äôs rule circumvents this obstacle.
366
 VI. Linear algebra
of DeÔ¨Ånition 6.13. The ‚Äòspectrum of the hydrogen atom‚Äô is also a spectrum in this
sense.
It is hopefully immediate that similar transformations have the same spectrum:
for if Œ≤ = œÄ ‚ó¶ Œ± ‚ó¶ œÄ ‚àí1 and Œ±(v) = Œªv, then
Œ≤(œÄ(v)) = œÄ ‚ó¶ Œ± ‚ó¶ œÄ ‚àí1(œÄ(v)) = œÄ ‚ó¶ (Œ±(v)) = œÄ(Œªv) = ŒªœÄ(v);
as œÄ(v) = 0 if v = 0, this shows that every eigenvalue of Œ± is an eigenvalue of Œ≤.
If F is Ô¨Ånitely generated, then we have the following useful translation of the
notion of eigenvalue:
Lemma 6.14. Let F be a Ô¨Ånitely generated R-module, and let Œ± ‚àà EndR (F ). Then
the set of eigenvalues of Œ± is precisely the set of roots in R of the characteristic
polynomial PŒ± (t).
Proof. This is a straightforward consequence of Proposition 6.5:
Œª is an eigenvalue for Œ± ‚áê‚áí ‚àÉv = 0 such that Œ±(v) = ŒªI(v)
‚áê‚áí ‚àÉv = 0 such that (ŒªI ‚àí Œ±)(v) = 0
‚áê‚áí ŒªI ‚àí Œ± is not injective
‚áê‚áí det(ŒªI ‚àí Œ±) = 0
‚áê‚áí PŒ± (Œª) = 0
as claimed.

It is an evident consequence of Lemma 6.14 that eigenvalue considerations
depend very much on the base ring R.
Example 6.15. The matrix
0 ‚àí1
1 0
has no eigenvalues over R, while it has eigenvalues over C: indeed, the characteristic
polynomial t2 + 1 has no real roots and two complex roots. The reader should
observe that, as a linear transformation of the real plane R2 , this matrix corresponds
to a 90‚ó¶ counterclockwise rotation; the reason why this transformation has no (real)
eigenvalues is that no direction in the plane is preserved through a 90‚ó¶ rotation. 
Example 6.16. An example with a diÔ¨Äerent Ô¨Çavor is given by the matrix
0 2
.
1 0
This has characteristic polynomial t2 ‚àí 2; hence it has eigenvalues over R, but
not over Q. Geometrically, the corresponding transformation Ô¨Çips the plane about
a line; but that line has irrational slope, so it contains no nonzero vectors with
rational components.
 
As another beneÔ¨Åt of Lemma 6.14, we may now introduce the following notion:
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.17. The algebraic multiplicity of an eigenvalue of a linear transfor-
mation Œ± of a Ô¨Ånitely generated free module is its multiplicity as a root of the
characteristic polynomial of Œ±.
 
6. Linear transformations of a free module
 367
For example, the identity on F has the single eigenvalue 1, with (algebraic)
multiplicity equal to the rank of F .
The sum of the algebraic multiplicities is bounded by the degree of the charac-
teristic polynomial, that is, the dimension of the space. In particular,
Corollary 6.18. The number of eigenvalues of a linear transformation of Rn is
at most n. If the base ring R is an algebraically closed Ô¨Åeld, then every linear
transformation has exactly n eigenvalues (counted with algebraic multiplicity).
Proof. Immediate from Lemmas 6.14, V.5.1, and V.5.10.
 
There is a diÔ¨Äerent notion of multiplicity of an eigenvalue, related to how big
the corresponding31 eigenspace may be.
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.19. Let Œª be an eigenvalue of a linear transformation Œ± of a free
R-module F . Then a nonzero v ‚àà F is an eigenvector for Œ±, corresponding to the
eigenvalue Œª, if Œ±(v) = Œªv, that is, if v ‚àà ker(ŒªI ‚àí Œ±). The submodule ker(ŒªI ‚àí Œ±)
is the eigenspace corresponding to Œª.
 
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.20. The geometric multiplicity of an eigenvalue Œª is the rank of its
eigenspace.
 
This is clearly invariant under similarity (Exercise 6.14). Geometric and alge-
braic multiplicities do not necessarily coincide: for example, the matrix
1 1
0 1
has characteristic polynomial (t ‚àí 1)2, and hence the
 single eigenvalue 1, with
v1algebraic multiplicity 2. However, for a vector v = ,
v21 1
 v1
 v1 + v2
=
0 1
 v2
 v2
equals 1v if and only if v2 = 0: that is, the eigenspace
  
 corresponding to the single
eigenvalue 1 consists of the span of the vector 10 and has dimension 1. Thus, the
geometric multiplicity of the eigenvalue is 1 in this example.
Contemplating this example will convince the reader that the geometric multi-
plicity of an eigenvalue is always less than its algebraic multiplicity. In the neatest
possible situation, an operator Œ± on a free module F of rank n may have all its n
eigenvalues in the base ring R, and each algebraic multiplicity may agree with the
corresponding geometric multiplicity. If this is the case, F may then be expressed as
a direct sum of the eigenspaces, producing the so-called spectral decomposition of F
determined by Œ± (cf. Exercise 6.15 for a concrete instance of this situation). The
action of Œ± on F is then completely transparent, as it amounts to simply applying
a (possibly) diÔ¨Äerent scaling factor on each piece of the spectral decomposition.

compelled to call this object the eigenmodule of Œ±. However, we are eventually going to restrict
our attention to the case in which the base ring is a Ô¨Åeld, so I will not bother.

6.18. ¬¨ Let V = Rn, endowed with the standard inner product deÔ¨Åned in Exer-
cise 6.16. A set of distinct vectors v1 , . . . , vr is orthonormal if (vi , vj ) = 0 for i = j
and 1 for i = j. Geometrically, this means that each vector has length 1, and dif-
ferent vectors are orthogonal. The same terminology may be used in Cn, w.r.t. the
standard hermitian product.
Prove that M ‚àà On (R) if and only if the columns of M are orthonormal, if and
only if the rows of M are orthonormal33 .
Formulate and prove an analogous statement for U(n). (The group U(n) is
called the unitary group. Note that, for n = 1, it consists of the complex numbers
of norm 1.) [6.19, 7.16, VIII.5.9]
6.19. ¬¨ Let v1 , . . . , vr form an orthonormal set of vectors in Rn .
32 See Exercise II.6.1 for the deÔ¨Ånitions of On
 (R) and U(n). I trust that basic facts on inner
products are not new to the reader. Among these, recall that for v, w ‚àà Rn, (v, v) equals the
square of the length of v, and (v, w) equals the product of the lengths of v and w and the cosine
of the angle formed by v and w. Thus, the reader is essentially asked to verify that M ‚àà On(R) if
and only if M preserves lengths and angles, and to check an analogous statement in the complex
environment.
33 The group On
 (R) is called the orthogonal group; orthonormal group would probably be a
more appropriate terminology.

For reasons such as these, it is convenient to work with orthonormal bases. Note
that, by Exercise 6.18, a matrix is in On (R) if and only if its columns form an
orthonormal basis of R n. Again, the reader should formulate parallel statements
for hermitian products in Cn . [6.20]
6.20. The Gram-Schmidt process takes as input a choice of linearly independent
vectors v1 , . . . , vr of Rn and returns vectors w1 , . . . , wr spanning the same space V
spanned by v1 , . . . , vr and such that (wi, wj ) = 0 for i = j. Scaling each wi by its
length, this yields an orthonormal basis for V .
Here is how the Gram-Schmidt process works:
‚Äî w1 := v1 .
‚Äî For k ‚â• 1, wk := vk ‚àí orthogonal projection of v k onto w1 , . . . , wk‚àí1 .
(Cf. Exercise 6.19.) Prove that this process accomplishes the stated goal.
6.21. ¬¨ A matrix M ‚àà Mn(Rn) is symmetric if M t = M . Prove that M is
symmetric if and only if (‚àÄv, w ‚àà Cn), (M v, w) = (v, M w).
A matrix M ‚àà Mn (Cn ) is hermitian if M ‚ÄÝ = M . Prove that M is hermitian if
and only if (‚àÄv, w ‚àà Cn ), (M v, w) = (v, M w).
In both cases, one may say that M is self-adjoint; this means that shuttling it
from one side of the product to the other does not change the result of the operation.
A hermitian matrix with real entries is symmetric. It is in fact useful to think
of real symmetric matrices as particular cases of hermitian matrices. [6.22]
6.22. ¬¨ Prove that the eigenvalues of a hermitian matrix (Exercise 6.21) are real.
Also, prove that if v, w are eigenvectors of a hermitian matrix, corresponding to
diÔ¨Äerent eigenvalues, then (v, w) = 0. (Thus, eigenvectors with distinct eigenvalues
for a real symmetric matrix are orthogonal.) [7.20]
7. Canonical forms
7.1. Linear transformations of free modules; actions of polynomial rings.
I have promised to use Theorem 5.6 to better understand the similarity relation
372
 VI. Linear algebra
and to obtain special forms for square matrices with entries in a Ô¨Åeld. I am now
ready to make good on my promise.
The questions the reader should be anxiously asking are, where is the PID?
Where is the Ô¨Ånitely generated module? Why would a classiÔ¨Åcation theorem for
these things have anything to tell us about matrices? Once these questions are
answered, everything else will follow easily. In a sense, this is the one issue that I
keep in my mind concerning all these considerations: once I remember how to get
an interesting module out of a linear transformation of vector spaces, the rest is
essentially an immediate consequence of standard notions.
Once more, while the main application will be to vector spaces and matrices
over a Ô¨Åeld, we do not need to preoccupy ourselves with specializing the situation
before we get to the key point. Therefore, let‚Äôs keep working for a while longer on
our given integral domain 34 R.
Claim 7.1. Giving a linear transformation on a free R-module F is the same as
giving an R[t]-module structure on F , compatible with its R-module structure.
Here R[t] is the polynomial ring in one indeterminate t over the ring R. The
claim is much less impressive than it sounds; in fact, it is completely tautological.
Giving an R[t]-module structure on F compatible with the R-module structure is
the same as giving a homomorphism of R-algebras
œï : R[t] ‚Üí EndR (F )
(this is an insigniÔ¨Åcant upgrade of the very deÔ¨Ånition of module from ¬ßIII.5.1).
By the universal property satisÔ¨Åed by polynomial rings (¬ßIII.2.2, especially Exam-
ple III.2.3), giving œï as an extension of the basic R-algebra structure of EndR (F ) is
just the same as specifying the image œï(t), that is, choosing an element of EndR (F ).
This is precisely what the claim says.
My propensity for the use of a certain language may obfuscate the matter,
which is very simple. Given a linear transformation Œ± of a free module F , we can
deÔ¨Åne the action of a polynomial
f (t) = rm tm + rm‚àí1 tm‚àí1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + r0 ‚àà R[t]
on F as follows: for every v ‚àà F , set
f (t)(v) := rm Œ±m (v) + rm‚àí1 Œ± m‚àí1 (v) + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + r0 v,
where Œ±k denotes the k-fold composition of Œ± with itself; we have already run into
this in ¬ß6.2. Conversely, an action of R[t] on F determines in particular a map
Œ± : F ‚Üí F , that is, ‚Äòmultiplication by t‚Äô:
v ‚Üí tv;
the compatibility with the R-module structure on F tells us precisely that Œ± is a
linear transformation of F . Again, this is the content of Claim 7.1.
Tautological as it is, Claim 7.1 packs a good amount of information. Indeed,
the R[t] module knows everything about similarity:
34 In fact, the requirements that R be an integral domain and that F be free are immaterial
here; cf. Exercise III.5.11.
7. Canonical forms
 373
Lemma 7.2. Let Œ±, Œ≤ be linear transformations of a free R-module F . Then the
corresponding R[t]-module structures on F are isomorphic if and only if Œ± and Œ≤
are similar.
Proof. Denote by FŒ±, FŒ≤ the two R[t]-modules deÔ¨Åned on F by Œ±, Œ≤ as per
Claim 7.1.
Assume Ô¨Årst that Œ± and Œ≤ are similar. Then there exists an invertible R-linear
transformation œÄ : F ‚Üí F such that
Œ≤ = œÄ ‚ó¶ Œ± ‚ó¶ œÄ‚àí1 ;
that is, œÄ ‚ó¶ Œ± = Œ≤ ‚ó¶ œÄ. We can view œÄ as an R-linear map
FŒ± ‚Üí FŒ≤ .
I claim that it is R[t]-linear: indeed, multiplication by t is Œ± in FŒ± and Œ≤ in FŒ≤ , so
œÄ(tv) = œÄ ‚ó¶ Œ±(v) = Œ≤ ‚ó¶ œÄ(v) = tœÄ(v).
Thus œÄ is an invertible R[t]-linear map FŒ± ‚Üí FŒ≤ , proving that FŒ± and FŒ≤ are
isomorphic as R[t]-modules.
The converse implication is obtained essentially by running this argument in
reverse and is left to the reader (Exercise 7.1).
 
Corollary 7.3. There is a one-to-one correspondence between the similarity classes
of R-linear transformations of a free R-module F and the isomorphism classes of
R[t]-module structures on F .
Of course the same statement holds for square matrices with entries in R, in
the Ô¨Ånite rank case.
Corollary 7.3 is the tool we were looking for, bridging between classifying sim-
ilarity classes of linear transformations or matrices and classifying modules. The
task now becomes that of translating the notions we have encountered in ¬ß6 into
the module language and seeing if this teaches us anything new.
In any case, since we have classiÔ¨Åed Ô¨Ånitely generated modules over PIDs,
it is clear that we will be able to classify similarity classes of transformations of
Ô¨Ånite-rank free modules‚Äîprovided that R[t] is a PID. This is where the additional
hypothesis that R be a Ô¨Åeld enters the discussion (cf. Exercise V.2.12).
7.2. k[t]-modules and the rational canonical form. It is Ô¨Ånally time to spe-
cialize to the case in which R = k is a Ô¨Åeld and F has Ô¨Ånite rank; so F = V is
simply a Ô¨Ånite-dimensional vector space. Let n = dim V .
By the preceding discussion, choosing a linear transformation of V is the same
as giving V a k[t]-module structure (compatible with its vector space structure);
similar linear transformations correspond to isomorphic k[t]-modules. Then V is
a Ô¨Ånitely generated module over k[t], and k[t] is a PID since k is a Ô¨Åeld (Exer-
cise III.4.4 and ¬ßV.2); therefore, we are precisely in the situation covered by the
classiÔ¨Åcation theorem.
Even before spelling out the result of applying Theorem 5.6, we can make use
of its slogan version: every Ô¨Ånitely generated module over a PID is a direct sum

of Ô¨Ånite-dimensional vector spaces, up to similarity, if we understand the linear

of a given linear transformation.

The rational canonical form of a square matrix is (of course) the rational canon-
ical form of the corresponding linear transformation. The following statement is an
immediate consequence of Theorem 7.5.
Corollary 7.7. Every linear transformation admits a rational canonical form. Two
linear transformations have the same rational canonical form if and only if they are
similar.
Remark 7.8. The ‚Äòrational‚Äô in rational canonical form has nothing to do with Q;
it is meant to remind the reader that this form can be found without leaving
the base Ô¨Åeld. The other canonical form we will encounter will have entries in
a possibly larger Ô¨Åeld, where the characteristic polynomial of the transformation
factors completely.

transformations. The rational canonical form is such a representative.

Corollary 7.10 (Cayley-Hamilton). The minimal polynomial of a linear transfor-
mation divides its characteristic polynomial.
Proof. This has now become evident, as promised in ¬ß6.2.
 
Finding the rational canonical form of a given matrix A amounts to working
out the classiÔ¨Åcation theorem for the speciÔ¨Åc k[t]-module corresponding to A as in
Claim 7.1. As k[t] is in fact a Euclidean domain, we know that this can be done by
Gaussian elimination (cf. ¬ß2.4). In practice, the process consists of compiling the
information obtained while diagonalizing tI ‚àí A over k[t] by elementary operations.
The reader is invited to either produce an original algorithm or at least look it up
in a standard reference to see what is involved.
Major shortcuts may come to our aid. For example, if the minimal and char-
acteristic polynomials coincide, then one knows a priori that the corresponding
module is cyclic (cf. Remark 5.8), and it follows that the rational canonical form is
simply the companion matrix of the characteristic polynomial.
7. Canonical forms
 377
In any case, the strength of a canonical form rests in the fact that it allows us
to reduce general facts to speciÔ¨Åc, standard cases. For example, the following state-
ment is somewhat mysterious if all one knows are the deÔ¨Ånitions, but it becomes
essentially trivial with a pinch of rational canonical forms:
Proposition 7.11. Let A ‚àà M n(k) be a square matrix. Then A is similar to its
transpose.
Proof. If B is similar to A and we can prove that B is similar to its transpose Bt ,
then A is similar to its transpose At : because B = P AP ‚àí1 , B t = QBQ‚àí1 give
At = (P t QP )A(P tQP )‚àí1 .
Therefore, it suÔ¨Éces to prove the statement for matrices in rational canonical form.
Further, to prove the statement for a block matrix, it clearly suÔ¨Éces to prove it
for each block; so we may assume that A is the companion matrix C of a polynomial
f (t). Since the characteristic and minimal polynomials of the transpose C t coincide
with those of C (Exercise 6.8), they are both equal to f (t). It follows that the
rational canonical form of C t is again the companion matrix to f (t); therefore C t
and C are similar, as needed.
 
7.3. Jordan canonical form. We have derived the rational canonical form from
the invariant factors of the module corresponding to a linear transformation. A
useful alternative can be obtained from the elementary divisors, at least in the case
in which the characteristic polynomial factors completely over the Ô¨Åeld k. If this is
not the case, one can enlarge k so as to include all the roots of the characteristic
polynomial: the reader proved this in Exercise V.5.13. The price to pay will be that
the Jordan canonical form of a linear transformation Œ± ‚àà Endk (V ) may be a matrix
with entries in a Ô¨Åeld larger than k. In any case, whether two transformations are
similar or not is independent of the base Ô¨Åeld (Exercise 7.4), so this does not aÔ¨Äect
the issue at hand.
Given Œ± ‚àà End k (V ), obtain the elementary divisor decomposition of the corre-
sponding k[t]-module, as in Theorem 7.5:

This canonical form is ‚Äòa little less canonical‚Äô than the rational canonical form,
in the sense that while the rational canonical form is really unique, some ambiguity
is left in the Jordan canonical form: there is no special way to choose the order
of the diÔ¨Äerent blocks any more than there is a way to order35 the eigenvalues
Œª1 , . . . , Œªs.
Therefore, the analog of Corollary 7.7 should state that two linear transforma-
tions are similar if and only if they have the same Jordan canonical form up to a
reordering of the blocks. As we have seen, every linear transformation Œ± ‚àà Endk (V )
admits a Jordan canonical form if PŒ± (t) factors completely over k; for example, we
can always Ô¨Ånd a Jordan canonical form with entries in the algebraic closure of k.

The Jordan canonical form clariÔ¨Åes the diÔ¨Äerence between algebraic and geo-
metric multiplicities of an eigenvalue. The algebraic multiplicity of Œª as an eigen-
value of a linear transformation Œ± is (of course) the number of times Œª appears in
the Jordan canonical form of Œ±. Recall that the geometric multiplicity of Œª equals
the dimension of the eigenspace of Œª.
Proposition 7.16. The geometric multiplicity of Œª as an eigenvalue of Œ± equals
the number of Jordan blocks corresponding to Œª in the Jordan canonical form of Œ±.

 
7.4. Diagonalizability. A linear transformation of a vector space V is diagonal-
izable if it can be represented by a diagonal matrix.
The question of whether a given linear transformation is or is not diagonalizable
is interesting and important: as I pointed out already in ¬ß6.3, when Œ± ‚àà Endk (V ) is
diagonalizable, then the vector space V admits a corresponding spectral decomposi-
tion: Œ± is diagonalizable if and only if V admits a basis of eigenvectors of Œ±. Much
more could be said on this important theme, but we are already in the position of
giving useful criteria for diagonalizability.
For example, the diagonal matrix representing Œ± will necessarily be a Jordan
canonical form for Œ±, consisting of blocks of size 1. Proposition 7.16 tells us that
this can be detected by the diÔ¨Äerence between algebraic and geometric multiplicity,
so we get the following characterization of diagonalizable transformations:
Corollary 7.17. Assume the characteristic polynomial of Œ± ‚àà Endk (V ) factors
completely over k. Then Œ± is diagonalizable if and only if the geometric and alge-
braic multiplicities of all its eigenvalues coincide.
Another view of the same result is the following:
Exercises
 381
Proposition 7.18. Assume the characteristic polynomial of Œ± ‚àà Endk (V ) factors
completely over k. Then Œ± is diagonalizable if and only if the minimal polynomial
of Œ± has no multiple roots.
Proof. Again, diagonalizability is equivalent to having all Jordan blocks of size 1
in the Jordan canonical form of Œ±. Therefore, if the characteristic polynomial of Œ±
factors completely, then Œ± is diagonalizable if and only if all exponents rij appearing
in Theorem 7.5 equal 1. By the second part of Lemma 7.12, this is equivalent to
the requirement that the minimal polynomial of Œ± have no multiple roots.
 
The condition of factorizability in these statements is necessary in order to
guarantee that a Jordan canonical form exists. Not surprisingly, whether a matrix
is diagonalizable or not depends on the ground Ô¨Åeld. For example,
0 ‚àí1
1 0
is not diagonalizable over R (cf. Example 6.15), while it is diagonalizable over C.
Endowing the vector space V with additional structure (such as an inner prod-
uct) singles out important classes of operators, for which one can obtain precise and
delicate results on the existence of spectral decompositions. For example, this can
be used to prove that real symmetric matrices are diagonalizable (Exercise 7.20.)
The reader will get a taste of these ‚Äòspectral theorems‚Äô in the exercises.

Fields
Chapter VII
The minuscule amount of algebra we have developed so far allows us to scratch the
surface of the unfathomable subject of Ô¨Åeld theory. Fields are of basic importance
in many subjects‚Äînumber theory and algebraic geometry come immediately to
mind‚Äîand it is not surprising that their study has been developed to a particularly
high level of sophistication. While my profoundly limited knowledge will prevent me
from even hinting at this sophistication, even a cursory overview of the basic notions
will allow us to deal with remarkable applications, such as the famous problems of
constructibility of geometric Ô¨Ågures. We will also get a glimpse of the beautiful
subject of Galois theory, an amazing interplay of the theory of Ô¨Åeld extensions, the
solvability of polynomials, and group theory.
1. Field extensions, I
We Ô¨Årst deal with several basic notions in Ô¨Åeld theory, mostly inspired by easy linear
algebra considerations. The keywords here are Ô¨Ånite, simple, Ô¨Ånitely generated,
algebraic.
1.1. Basic deÔ¨Ånitions. The study of Ô¨Åelds is (of course) the study of the category
Fld of Ô¨Åelds (DeÔ¨Ånition III.1.14), with ring homomorphisms as morphisms. The
task is to understand what Ô¨Åelds are and above all what they are in relation to one
another.
The place to start is a reminder from elementary ring theory1 : every ring ho-
momorphisms from a Ô¨Åeld to a nonzero ring is injective. Indeed, the kernel of
a ring homomorphism to a nonzero ring is a proper ideal (because a homomor-
phism maps 1 to 1 by deÔ¨Ånition and 1 = 0 in nonzero rings), and the only proper
ideal in a Ô¨Åeld is (0). In particular, every ring homomorphism of Ô¨Åelds is injective
(Ô¨Åelds are nonzero rings by deÔ¨Ånition!); every morphism in Fld is a monomorphism
(cf. Proposition III.2.4).
1 The last time I have made this point was back in ¬ßV.5.2.
385
386
 VII. Fields
Thus, every morphism k ‚Üí K between two Ô¨Åelds identiÔ¨Åes the Ô¨Årst with a
subÔ¨Åeld of the second. In other words, one can view K as a particular way to
enlarge k, that is, as an extension of k. Field theory is Ô¨Årst and foremost the study
of Ô¨Åeld extensions. I will denote a Ô¨Åeld extension by k ‚äÜ K (which is less than
optimal, because there may be many ways to embed a Ô¨Åeld into another); other
popular choices are K/k (which I do not like, as it suggests a quotienting operation)
and
K
k
(which we will avoid most of the time, as it is hard to typeset).
The coarsest invariant of a Ô¨Åeld k is its characteristic; cf. Exercise V.4.17. We
have a unique ring homomorphisms i : Z ‚Üí k (Z is initial in Ring: 1 must go to 1
by deÔ¨Ånition of homomorphism, and this Ô¨Åxes the value of i(n) for all n ‚àà Z); the
characteristic of k, char k, is deÔ¨Åned to be the nonnegative generator of the ideal
ker i; that is, char k = 0 if i is injective, and char k = p > 0 if ker i = (p) = (0).
Put otherwise, either n ¬∑ 1 is only 0 in k for n = 0, in which case char k = 0,
or n ¬∑ 1 = 0 for some n = 0; char k = p > 0 is then the smallest positive integer for
which p ¬∑ 1 = 0 in k. Since Ô¨Åelds are integral domains, the image i(Z) must be an
integral domain; hence ker i must be a prime ideal. Therefore, the characteristic of
a Ô¨Åeld is either 0 or a prime number.
If k ‚äÜ K is an extension, then char k = char K (Exercise 1.1). Thus, we could
deÔ¨Åne and study categories Fld0 , Fldp of Ô¨Åelds of a given characteristic, without
losing any information: these categories live within Fld, without interacting with
each other2 . Further, each of these categories has an initial object: Q is initial
in Fld0 , and3 Fp := Z/pZ in Fldp; the reader has checked this easy fact in Exer-
cise V.4.17, and now is an excellent time to contemplate it again. In each case,
the initial object is called the prime subÔ¨Åeld of the given Ô¨Åeld. Thus, every Ô¨Åeld is
in a canonical way an extension of its prime subÔ¨Åeld: studying Ô¨Åelds really means
studying Ô¨Åeld extensions. To a large extent, the ‚Äòsmall‚Äô Ô¨Åeld k will be Ô¨Åxed in what
follows, and our main object of study will be the category Fldk of extensions of k,
with the evident (and by now familiar to the reader) notions of morphisms.
The Ô¨Årst general remark concerning Ô¨Åeld extensions is that the larger Ô¨Åeld is
an algebra, and hence a vector space over the smaller one (by the very deÔ¨Ånition of
algebra; cf. Example III.5.6).
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.1. A Ô¨Åeld extension k ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånite, of degree n, if F has (Ô¨Ånite)
dimension dim F = n as a vector space over k. The extension is inÔ¨Ånite otherwise.
The degree of a Ô¨Ånite extension k ‚äÜ F is denoted by [F : k] (and we write
[F : k] = ‚àû if the extension is inÔ¨Ånite).
 
2 They do interact in other ways, however‚Äîfor example, because Z is initial in Ring, so Z
maps to all Ô¨Åelds.
3 I am going to denote the Ô¨Åeld Z/pZ by Fp
 in this chapter, to stress its role as a Ô¨Åeld (rather
than ‚Äòjust‚Äô as a group).
1. Field extensions, I
 387
In ¬ßV.5.2 we have encountered a prototypical example of Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åeld extension:
a procedure starting from an irreducible polynomial f (x) ‚àà k[x] with coeÔ¨Écients
in a Ô¨Åeld and producing an extension K of k in which f (t) has a root. Explicitly,
K =
k[t]
(f (t))
is such an extension (Proposition V.5.7). The quotient is a Ô¨Åeld because k[t] is
a PID; hence irreducible elements generate maximal ideals‚Äîprime because of the
UFD property (cf. Theorem V.2.5) and then maximal because nonzero prime ideals
are maximal in a PID (cf. Proposition III.4.13). The coset of t is a root of f (x)
when this is viewed as an element of K[x]. The degree of the extension k ‚äÜ K
equals the degree of the polynomial f (x) (this is hopefully clear at this point and
was checked carefully in Proposition III.4.6).
The reader may wonder whether perhaps all Ô¨Ånite extensions are of this type.
This is unfortunately not the case, but as it happens, it is the case in a large class of
examples. As I often do, I promise that the situation will clarify itself considerably
once we have accumulated more general knowledge; in this case, the relevant fact
will be Proposition 5.19.
Also recall that we proved that these extensions are almost universal with
respect to the problem of extending k so that the polynomial f (t) acquires a root.
I pointed out, however, that the uni in uni versal is missing; that is, if k ‚äÜ F is an
extension of k in which f (t) has a root, there may be many diÔ¨Äerent ways to put K
in between:
k ‚äÜ K ‚äÜ F.
Such questions are central to the study of extensions, so we begin by giving a second
look at this situation.
1.2. Simple extensions. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Åeld extension, and let Œ± ‚àà F . The
smallest subÔ¨Åeld of F containing both k and Œ± is denoted k(Œ±); that is, k(Œ±) is the
intersection of all subÔ¨Åelds of F containing k and Œ±.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.2. A Ô¨Åeld extension k ‚äÜ F is simple if there exists an element Œ± ‚àà F
such that F = k(Œ±).
 
The extensions recalled above are of this kind: if K = k[t]/(f (t)) and Œ± denotes
the coset of t, then K = k(Œ±): indeed, if a subÔ¨Åeld of K contains the coset of t,
then it must contain (the coset of) every polynomial expression in t, and hence it
must be the whole of K.
I have always found the notation k(Œ±) somewhat unfortunate, since it suggests
that all such extensions are in some way isomorphic and possibly all isomorphic to
the Ô¨Åeld k(t) of rational functions in one indeterminate t (cf. DeÔ¨Ånition V.4.13).
This is not true, although it is clear that every element of k(Œ±) may be written as
a rational function in Œ± with coeÔ¨Écients in k (Exercise 1.3). In any case, it is easy
to classify simple extensions: they are either isomorphic to k(t) or they are of the
prototypical kind recalled above. Here is the precise statement.
Proposition 1.3. Let k ‚äÜ k(Œ±) be a simple extension. Consider the evaluation
map  : k[t] ‚Üí k(Œ±), deÔ¨Åned by f (t) ‚Üí f (Œ±). Then we have the following:
388

k(Œ±) is isomorphic to the Ô¨Åeld of rational functions k(t).
‚Ä¢  is not injective if and only if k ‚äÜ k(Œ±) is Ô¨Ånite. In this case there exists
a unique monic irreducible nonconstant polynomial p(t) ‚àà k[t] of degree n =

the monic polynomial of smallest degree in k[t] such that p(Œ±) = 0 in k(Œ±).
The polynomial p(t) appearing in this statement is called the minimal polyno-
mial of Œ± over k.

Example 1.4. Consider the extension Q ‚äÜ R.

Ay, there‚Äôs the rub. One of our main goals in this chapter will be to single out
a condition on an extension k ‚äÜ F that guarantees that no matter how we embed F
in a larger extension, the images of these (possibly many diÔ¨Äerent) embeddings will
all coincide. Up

Thus, Q ‚äÜ Q( 2) will be a Galois extension, while Q ‚äÜ Q( 2) will not be a Galois
extension. But Galois extensions will have to wait until ¬ß6, and the reader can put
this issue aside for now.
 
One way to recover uniqueness is to incorporate the choice of the root in the
data. This leads to the following reÔ¨Ånement of the (uni)versality statement, adapted
for future applications.
Proposition 1.5. Let k1 ‚äÜ F1 = k1 (Œ±1 ), k2 ‚äÜ F2 = k2 (Œ±2 ) be two Ô¨Ånite simple
extensions. Let p1 (t) ‚àà k1[t], resp., p2 (t) ‚àà k2 [t], be the minimal polynomials of Œ±1 ,
resp., Œ±2 . Let i : k1 ‚Üí k2 be an isomorphism, such that4
i(p1 (t)) = p2 (t).
Then there exists a unique isomorphism j : F1 ‚Üí F2 agreeing with i on k1 and such
that j(Œ±1 ) = Œ±2 .
Proof. Since every element of k1 (Œ±1 ) is a linear combination of powers of Œ±1 with
coeÔ¨Écients in k1 , j is determined by its action on k1 (which agrees with i) and
by j(Œ±1 ), which is prescribed to be Œ±2 . Thus an isomorphism j as in the statement
is uniquely determined.
To see that the isomorphism j exists, note that as i maps p1(t) to p2 (t), it
induces an isomorphism

Thus, isomorphisms lift uniquely to simple extensions, so long as they preserve
minimal polynomials. We may say that j extends i, and draw the following diagram
of extensions:

We will be particularly interested in considering isomorphisms of a Ô¨Åeld to
itself, subject to the condition of Ô¨Åxing a speciÔ¨Åed subÔ¨Åeld k (that is, extending the
identity on k), that is, automorphisms in Fldk . Explicitly, for k ‚äÜ F an extension,
‚àº
we will analyze isomorphisms j : F ‚Üí F such that ‚àÄc ‚àà k, j(c) = c.
I will denote the group of such automorphisms by Autk (F ). This is probably
the most important object of study in this chapter, so I should make its deÔ¨Ånition
oÔ¨Écial:
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.6. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Åeld extension. The group of automorphisms of
the extension, denoted Autk (F ), is the group of Ô¨Åeld automorphisms j : F ‚Üí F
such that j|k = idk .
 
Corollary 1.7. Let k ‚äÜ F = k(Œ±) be a simple Ô¨Ånite extension, and let p(x) be
the minimal polynomial of Œ± over k. Then | Autk (F )| equals the number of distinct
roots of p(x) in F ; in particular,
| Autk (F )| ‚â§ [F : k],
with equality if and only if p(x) factors over F as a product of distinct linear poly-
nomials.
Proof. Let j ‚àà Autk (F ). Since every element of F is a polynomial expression in Œ±
with coeÔ¨Écients in k, and j extends the identity on k, j is determined by j(Œ±).
Now
p(j(Œ±)) = j(p(Œ±)) = j(0) = 0 :
therefore, j(Œ±) is necessarily a root of p(x). This shows that | Autk (F )| is no larger
than the number of roots of p(x).
On the other hand, by Proposition 1.5 every choice of a root of p(t) determines a
lift of the identity to an element j ‚àà Autk (F ), establishing the other inequality. 
Corollary 1.7 is our Ô¨Årst hint of the powerful interaction between group theory
and the theory of Ô¨Åeld extensions; this theme will haunt us throughout the chapter.
The proof of Corollary 1.7 really sets up a bijection between the roots of an
irreducible polynomial p(t) ‚àà k[t] in a larger Ô¨Åeld F and the group Autk (F ). A
particularly optimistic reader may then hope that the roots of p(t) come endowed
1. Field extensions, I
 391
with a group structure, but this is hoping for too much: the bijection depends on
the choice of one root Œ±, and no one root of p(t) is ‚Äòmore beautiful‚Äô than the others.
However, we have established that if F = k(Œ±) is a simple extension of k, then the
group Autk (F ) acts faithfully and transitively on the set of roots of p(t) in F . In
other words, Autk (F ) can be identiÔ¨Åed with a certain subgroup of the symmetric
group acting on the set of roots of p(t).
More generally (Exercise 1.6) the automorphisms of any extension act on roots
of polynomials. One conclusion we can draw from these preliminary considerations
is that the analysis of Autk (F ) is substantially simpliÔ¨Åed if k ‚äÜ F is a simple
extension k(Œ±) and further if the minimal polynomial p(x) of Œ± factors into deg p(x)
distinct linear terms in F . It will not come as a surprise that we will focus our
attention on such extensions in later sections: an extension is Galois precisely if it
is of this type. (The reader is invited to remember this fact, but its import will not
be fully appreciated until we develop substantially more material.)
1.3. Finite and algebraic extensions. The dichotomy encountered in Proposi-
tion 1.3 provides us with one of the most important deÔ¨Ånitions in Ô¨Åeld theory:
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.8. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Åeld extension, and let Œ± ‚àà F . Then Œ± is algebraic
over k, of degree n if n = [k(Œ±) : k] is Ô¨Ånite; Œ± is transcendental over k otherwise.
The extension k ‚äÜ F is algebraic if every Œ± ‚àà F is algebraic over k.
 
By Proposition 1.3, Œ± ‚àà F is algebraic over k if and only if there exists a
nonzero polynomial f (x) ‚àà k[x] such that f (Œ±) = 0. The minimal polynomial of Œ±
is the monic polynomial of smallest degree satisfying this condition; as we have
seen, it is necessarily irreducible.
Also note that if Œ± is algebraic over k, then every element of k(Œ±) may in fact
be written as a polynomial with coeÔ¨Écients in k.
Finite extensions are necessarily algebraic:
Lemma 1.9. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Ånite extension. Then every Œ± ‚àà F is algebraic
over k, of degree ‚â§ [F : k].
Proof. Since k ‚äÜ k(Œ±) ‚äÜ F , the dimension of k(Œ±) as a k-vector space is bounded
by dimk F = [F : k].
 
Concretely, if k ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånite and Œ± ‚àà F , then the powers 1, Œ±, Œ± 2 . . . are
necessarily linearly dependent; and any nontrivial linear dependence relation among
them provides us with a nonzero polynomial f (x) ‚àà k[x] such that f (Œ±) = 0.
The literal converse of the claim that ‚ÄòÔ¨Ånite extensions are algebraic‚Äô is not true;
we will see an example in a moment. However, something along these lines holds;
understanding the situation requires a more careful look at Ô¨Åniteness conditions.
First of all, compositions of Ô¨Ånite extensions are Ô¨Ånite extensions, and the
degree behaves nicely with respect to this operation:
Proposition 1.10. Let k ‚äÜ E ‚äÜ F be Ô¨Åeld extensions. Then k ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånite if and
only if both k ‚äÜ E and E ‚äÜ F are Ô¨Ånite. In this case,
[F : k] = [F : E][E : k].
392

Proof. If F is Ô¨Ånite-dimensional as a vector space over k, then so is its subspace E;
and any linear dependence relation of elements of F over the Ô¨Åeld k gives one over
the larger Ô¨Åeld E. It follows that if k ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånite, then so are k ‚äÜ E and E ‚äÜ F .
Conversely, assume k ‚äÜ E and E ‚äÜ F are both Ô¨Ånite. Let (e1 , . . . , em ) be a
basis for E over k, and let (f1 , . . . , fn ) be a basis for F over E. It will suÔ¨Éce to
show that the mn products
(e1 f1 , e1 f2 , . . . , em fn )
form a basis for F over k.

The formula given in the statement should look familiar to the reader: glance
at the end of ¬ßII.8.5. Of course this is not accidental; the reader should take it as
another hint that the world of groups and the world of Ô¨Åelds have deep interac-
tion. As in the case of groups, we draw the immediate (but powerful) consequence,
reminiscent of Lagrange‚Äôs theorem:
Corollary 1.11. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Ånite extension, and let E be an intermediate
Ô¨Åeld (that is, k ‚äÜ E ‚äÜ F ). Then both [E : k] and [F : E] divide [F : k].
As with Lagrange‚Äôs theorem, judicious use of this result will make otherwise
mysterious statements melt into trivialities.
1. Field extensions, I
 393
Example 1.12. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Åeld extension, and let Œ± ‚àà F be an algebraic
element over k, of odd degree. Then I claim that Œ± may be written as a polynomial
in Œ±2 , with coeÔ¨Écients in k.
Indeed, k(Œ±2 ) is intermediate between k and k(Œ±):
k ‚äÜ k(Œ±2) ‚äÜ k(Œ±);
what can we say about the degree d of k(Œ±) over k(Œ±2 )? Since Œ± satisÔ¨Åes the
polynomial t2 ‚àí Œ±2 ‚àà k(Œ±2 )[t], d ‚â§ 2. On the other hand, d divides [k(Œ±) : k]
by Corollary 1.11, and [k(Œ±) : k] is odd, so d = 2. Therefore d = 1, proving
k(Œ±) = k(Œ±2 ), and in particular Œ± ‚àà k(Œ±2 ), which is the claim.
 
Here is something else that should evoke fond memories for the reader: back
in ¬ßIII.6.5 we had encountered an important distinction between Ô¨Ånite algebras
and algebras of Ô¨Ånite type. An algebra is Ô¨Ånite over the base ring if it is Ô¨Ånitely
generated as a module, that is, if it admits an onto homomorphism (of modules)
from a Ô¨Ånitely generated free module; a commutative algebra is of Ô¨Ånite type if
it admits an onto homomorphism (of algebras) from a polynomial ring in Ô¨Ånitely
many variables.
Something along the same lines is going to occur here. An extension k ‚äÜ F is
Ô¨Ånite if and only if dimk F is Ô¨Ånite, that is, if and only if F is a Ô¨Ånite k-algebra.
The other Ô¨Åniteness condition takes the following form.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.13. A Ô¨Åeld extension k ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånitely generated if there exist
Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±n ‚àà F such that
F = k(Œ±1 )(Œ±2 ) . . . (Œ±n).
 
Remark 1.14. This is not the same as saying that F is generated by the Œ±i ‚Äôs as a
(Ô¨Ånite-type) k-algebra: even in the case of simple extensions, if Œ± is transcendental,
then the natural evaluation map  : k[t] ‚Üí k(Œ±) of Proposition 1.3 is not onto.
However, this is an epimorphism of rings in the categorical sense, as the reader will
check (Exercise 1.17); thus, the ‚Äòcategorical spirit‚Äô behind the Ô¨Ånite-type condition
is preserved in this context.
This subtlety raises an interesting question: what if a Ô¨Åeld extension k ‚äÜ F
really is a Ô¨Ånite-type k-algebra? This turns out to be an important issue, and we
will come back to it in ¬ß2.2.
 
We will use the notation
k(Œ±1 , Œ±2 , . . . , Œ±n)
for the Ô¨Åeld k(Œ±1 )(Œ±2 ) . . . (Œ±n). For k ‚äÜ F and Œ±1, . . . , Œ±n ‚àà F , the elements
of the Ô¨Åeld F = k(Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±n) are all the elements of F which may be written
as rational functions in the Œ±i ‚Äôs, with coeÔ¨Écients in k (cf. Exercise 1.3). Put
otherwise, k(Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±n ) is the smallest subÔ¨Åeld of F containing k(Œ±1 ), . . . , k(Œ±n )
(it is the composite of these subÔ¨Åelds). It is clear that the order of the elements Œ±i
is irrelevant.
Coming back to the issue of Ô¨Ånite vs. algebraic, these two notions do coincide
for Ô¨Ånitely generated extensions.
394

Proposition 1.15. Let k ‚äÜ F = k(Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±n ) be a Ô¨Ånitely generated Ô¨Åeld exten-
sion. Then the following are equivalent:
(i) k ‚äÜ F is a Ô¨Ånite extension.
(ii) k ‚äÜ F is an algebraic extension.
(iii) Each Œ±i is algebraic over k.
If these conditions are satisÔ¨Åed, then [F : k] ‚â§ the product of the degrees of Œ±i
over k.
Proof. Lemma 1.9 shows that (i) =‚áí (ii); (ii) =‚áí (iii) trivially. Thus, we only
need to prove that (iii) =‚áí (i), and to bound the degree of F over k in the process.
Assume that each Œ±i is algebraic over k, and let di be the degree of Œ±i over k.
By deÔ¨Ånition, k ‚äÜ k(Œ±i ) is Ô¨Ånite, of degree di. By Exercise 1.16, each extension
k(Œ±1, . . . , Œ±i‚àí1 ) ‚äÜ k(Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±i )
is Ô¨Ånite, of degree ‚â§ di . Applying Proposition 1.10 to the composition of extensions
k ‚äÜ k(Œ±1 ) ‚äÜ k(Œ±1 , Œ±2 ) ‚äÜ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚äÜ k(Œ±1, . . . , Œ±n ) = F
proves that k ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånite and [F : k] ‚â§ d1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ dn, as needed.
 
While rather straightforward, Proposition 1.15 has always seemed remarkable
to us: it says (in particular) that if Œ± and Œ≤ are algebraic over a Ô¨Åeld k, then so are
Œ± ¬± Œ≤, Œ±Œ≤, Œ±Œ≤ ‚àí1, and any other rational function of Œ± and Œ≤. If all we knew were
the simple-minded deÔ¨Ånition of ‚Äòalgebraic‚Äô (that is, ‚Äòroot of a polynomial‚Äô), this
would seem rather mysterious: given a polynomial f (x) of which Œ± is a root and a
polynomial g(x) of which Œ≤ is a root, how do we construct a polynomial h(x) such
that (for example) h(Œ± + Œ≤) = 0? The answer is that we do not need to perform
any such construction, by virtue of Proposition 1.15.
One immediate consequence of this observation is that the set of algebraic
elements of any extension forms a Ô¨Åeld:
Corollary 1.16. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Åeld extension. Let
E = {Œ± ‚àà F | Œ± is algebraic over k}.
Then E is a Ô¨Åeld.
Example 1.17. Let Q ‚äÜ C be the set of complex numbers that are algebraic
over Q; then Q is a Ô¨Åeld, by Corollary 1.16, and the extension Q ‚äÜ Q is (tau-
tologically) algebraic. Note that Q ‚äÜ Q is not a Ô¨Ånite extension, because in it
there are elements of arbitrarily high degree over Q: indeed, there exist irreducible
polynomials in Q[x] of arbitrarily high degree, as we observed in Corollary V.5.16.
Elements of Q are called algebraic numbers; the complex numbers in the com-
plement of Q are transcendental over Q (by deÔ¨Ånition); they are simply called
transcendental numbers. Elementary cardinality considerations show that Q is
countable; thus, the set of transcendental numbers is uncountable. Surprisingly,
it may be substantially diÔ¨Écult to prove that a given number is transcendental:
for example, the fact that e is transcendental was only proved around 1870, by
Charles Hermite. The number œÄ is transcendental (Lindemann, ca. 1880); eœÄ is
1. Field extensions, I
 395
transcendental (Gelfond-Schneider, 1934). No one knows whether œÄe, œÄ + e, or œÄe
are transcendental.
 
Another consequence of Proposition 1.15 is the important fact that composi-
tions of algebraic extensions are algebraic (whether Ô¨Ånitely generated or not):
Corollary 1.18. Let k ‚äÜ E ‚äÜ F be Ô¨Åeld extensions. Then k ‚äÜ F is algebraic if
and only if both k ‚äÜ E and E ‚äÜ F are algebraic.
Proof. If k ‚äÜ F is algebraic, then every element of F is algebraic over k, hence
over E, and every element of E is algebraic over k; thus E ‚äÜ F and k ‚äÜ E are
algebraic.
Conversely, assume k ‚äÜ E and E ‚äÜ F are both algebraic, and let Œ± ‚àà F . Since
Œ± is algebraic over E, there exists a polynomial
f (x) = xn + en‚àí1 xn‚àí1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + e0 ‚àà E[x]
such that f (Œ±) = 0. This implies that in fact Œ± is ‚Äòalready‚Äô algebraic over the
subÔ¨Åeld k(e0 , . . . , en‚àí1 ) ‚äÜ E; therefore,
k(e0 , . . . , en‚àí1 ) ‚äÜ k(e0 , . . . , en‚àí1 , Œ±)
is a Ô¨Ånite extension. On the other hand,
k ‚äÜ k(e0, . . . , en‚àí1 )
is a Ô¨Ånite extension by Proposition 1.15, since each ei is in E and therefore algebraic
over k. By Proposition 1.10
k ‚äÜ k(e0 , . . . , en‚àí1 , Œ±)
is Ô¨Ånite. This implies that Œ± is algebraic over k, as needed, by Lemma 1.9.
 
We will essentially deal only with Ô¨Ånitely generated extensions, and Proposi-
tion 1.15 will simplify our work considerably. Also, since Ô¨Ånitely generated exten-
sions are compositions of simple extensions, the reader should expect that we will
give a careful look at automorphisms of such extensions.
It may in fact come as a surprise that, in many cases, Ô¨Ånitely generated ex-
tensions turn out to be simple to begin with; we will prove a precise statement to
this eÔ¨Äect in due time (Proposition 5.19). The reader already has enough tools to
contemplate easy (but interesting) examples, such as the following. This should
serve as an encouragement to look at many more.

1.1.  Prove that if k ‚äÜ K is a Ô¨Åeld extension, then char k = char K. Prove that
the category Fld has no initial object. [¬ß1.1]
1.2. DeÔ¨Åne carefully the category Fldk of extensions of k.
1.3.  Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Åeld extension, and let Œ± ‚àà F . Prove that the Ô¨Åeld k(Œ±)
consists of all the elements of F which may be written as rational functions in Œ±,
with coeÔ¨Écients in k. Why does this not give (in general) an onto homomorphism
k(t) ‚Üí k(Œ±)? [¬ß1.2, ¬ß1.3]
1.4. Let k ‚äÜ k(Œ±) be a simple extension, with Œ± transcendental over k. Let E be a
subÔ¨Åeld of k(Œ±) properly containing k. Prove that k(Œ±) is a Ô¨Ånite extension of E.
1.5.  (Cf. Example 1.4.)
‚Ä¢ Prove that there is exactly one subÔ¨Åeld of R isomorphic to Q[t]/(t2 ‚àí 2).
‚Ä¢ Prove that there are exactly three subÔ¨Åelds of C isomorphic to Q[t]/(t3 ‚àí 2).
From a ‚Äòtopological‚Äô point of view, one of these copies of Q[t]/(t3 ‚àí 2) looks very
diÔ¨Äerent from the other two: it is not dense in C, but the others are. [¬ß1.2]
1.6.  Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Åeld extension, and let f (x) ‚àà k[x] be a polynomial. Prove
that Autk (F ) acts on the set of roots of f (x) contained in F . Provide examples
showing that this action need not be transitive or faithful. [¬ß1.2, ¬ß1.3]
1.7. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Åeld extension, and let Œ± ‚àà F be algebraic over k.
‚Ä¢ Suppose p(x) ‚àà k[x] is an irreducible monic polynomial such that p(Œ±) = 0;
prove that p(x) is the minimal polynomial of Œ± over k, in the sense of Propo-
sition 1.3.
‚Ä¢ Let f (x) ‚àà k[x]. Prove that f (Œ±) = 0 if and only if p(x) | f (x).
‚Ä¢ Show that the minimal polynomial of Œ± is the minimal polynomial of a certain
k-linear transformation of F , in the sense of DeÔ¨Ånition VI.6.12.

be the roots of f (x) in an extension of k where the polynomial

2. Algebraic closure, Nullstellensatz, and a little algebraic
geometry
One of the most important extensions of a Ô¨Åeld k is its algebraic closure k ‚äÜ k; this
was mentioned already in ¬ßV.5.2, and we can now prove that k exists and is unique
(up to isomorphism). Once this circle of ideas is approached, the temptation to say
a few words about the important result known as Hilbert‚Äôs Nullstellensatz, at the
cost of a small digression into algebraic geometry, will simply be irresistible.
2.1. Algebraic closure. Recall (DeÔ¨Ånition V.5.9) that a Ô¨Åeld K is algebraically
closed if all irreducible polynomials in K[x] have degree 1, that is, if every polyno-
mial in K[x] factors completely as a product of linear terms. Equivalently (Exer-
cise III.4.21) every maximal ideal in K[x] is of the form (x ‚àí c), for c ‚àà K.
Now that we have a little more vocabulary, we can recast this deÔ¨Ånition yet
again, as follows.
2. Algebraic closure, Nullstellensatz, and a little algebraic geometry
 401
Lemma 2.1. For a Ô¨Åeld K, the following are equivalent:
‚Ä¢ K is algebraically closed.
‚Ä¢ K has no nontrivial algebraic extensions.
‚Ä¢ If K ‚äÜ L is any extension and Œ± ‚àà L is algebraic over K, then Œ± ‚àà K.
The proof is a straightforward application of the deÔ¨Ånitions and a good exercise
(Exercise 2.1).
DeÔ¨Ånition 2.2. An algebraic closure of a Ô¨Åeld k is an algebraic extension k ‚äÜ k
such that k is algebraically closed.
 
Of course, every polynomial f (x) ‚àà k[x] will split into linear factors over k: so
does every polynomial in the much larger k[x], as k is algebraically closed. The
requirement that k ‚äÜ k be algebraic ensures that no other intermediate Ô¨Åeld L,
k ‚äÜ L  k,
may be algebraically closed: indeed, L ‚äÜ k would be a nontrivial algebraic exten-
sion, contradicting Lemma 2.1. In fact, the only subÔ¨Åeld of k containing all roots
of all nonconstant polynomials in k[x] is k itself (Exercise 2.2); thus, the algebraic
closure of a Ô¨Åeld is ‚Äòas small as possible‚Äô subject to this requirement. Not surpris-
ingly, k turns out to be unique up to isomorphism, so that we can speak of the
algebraic closure of k.
Theorem 2.3. Every Ô¨Åeld k admits an algebraic closure k ‚äÜ k; this extension is
unique up to isomorphism.
Concerning existence, the idea is to construct ‚Äòby hand‚Äô a huge extension K
of k where every polynomial f (x) ‚àà k[x] factors completely. The elements of K
which are algebraic over k will form an algebraic closure of k.
The construction is done in steps, each step including ‚Äòone more root‚Äô of all
nonconstant polynomials in k[x]. Here is a formalization of this step.
Lemma 2.4. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld. Then there exists an extension k ‚äÜ K such that
every nonconstant polynomial f (x) ‚àà k[x] has at least one root in K.
Proof. (This construction is apparently due to Emil Artin.) Consider a set T =
{tf } in bijection with the set of nonconstant monic polynomials f (x) ‚àà k[x], and
let k[T ] be the corresponding polynomial ring5 in all the indeterminates tf . Let
I ‚äÜ k[T ] be the ideal generated by all polynomials f (tf ).
Then I is a proper ideal. Indeed, otherwise we could write

 
The Ô¨Åeld constructed in Lemma 2.4 contains at least one root of each noncon-
stant polynomial f (x) ‚àà k[x], but this is not good enough: we are seeking a Ô¨Åeld
which contains all roots of such polynomials. Equivalently, not only should f (x)
have (at least) one linear factor x ‚àí Œ± in K[x], but we need the quotient polynomial
f (x)/(x ‚àí Œ±) ‚àà K[x] to have a linear factor and the quotient by that new factor to
have one, etc. This prompts us to consider a whole chain of extensions
k ‚äÜ K1 ‚äÜ K2 ‚äÜ K3 ‚äÜ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑
where K1 is obtained from k by applying Lemma 2.4, K2 is similarly obtained
from K1 , etc.
Now consider the union L of this chain6 . For every two a, b ‚àà L, there is an i
such that a, b ‚àà Ki; we can deÔ¨Åne a + b, a ¬∑ b in L by adopting their deÔ¨Ånition in Ki ,
and the result does not depend on the choice of i. It follows easily that L is a Ô¨Åeld.
Claim 2.5. The Ô¨Åeld L is algebraically closed.
Proof. If f (x) ‚àà L[x] is a nonconstant polynomial, then f (x) ‚àà Ki [x] for some i;
hence f (x) has a root in Ki+1 ‚äÜ L. That is, every nonconstant polynomial in L[x]
has a root in L, as needed.
 
Proof of the existence of algebraic closures. The existence of algebraic clo-
sures is now completely transparent, because of the following simple observation:
Lemma 2.6. Let k ‚äÜ L be a Ô¨Åeld extension, with L algebraically closed. Let
k := {Œ± ‚àà L | Œ± is algebraic over k}.
Then k is an algebraic closure of k.
The construction reviewed above provides us with an algebraically closed Ô¨Åeld L
containing any given Ô¨Åeld k, so the lemma is all we need to prove.
By Corollary 1.16, k is a Ô¨Åeld, and the extension k ‚äÜ k is tautologically alge-
braic. To verify that k is algebraically closed, let k ‚äÜ k(Œ±) be a simple algebraic
extension. The minimal polynomial of Œ± has a root in L since L is algebraically
6 This is an example of direct limit, a notion which we will introduce more formally in
¬ßVIII.1.4.
2. Algebraic closure, Nullstellensatz, and a little algebraic geometry
 403
closed, so by versality (Proposition V.5.7) there exists an embedding k(Œ±) ‚äÜ L. We
can then view Œ± as an element of L; k ‚äÜ k ‚äÜ k(Œ±) is a composition of algebraic ex-
tensions, so k ‚äÜ k(Œ±) is algebraic (Corollary 1.18), and in particular Œ± is algebraic
over k. But then Œ± ‚àà k, by deÔ¨Ånition of the latter. It follows that k is algebraically
closed, by Lemma 2.1.
 
Remark 2.7. Zorn‚Äôs lemma was sneakily used in this proof (we used the existence
of maximal ideals, which relies upon Zorn‚Äôs lemma), and it is natural to wonder
whether the existence of algebraic closures may be another statement equivalent to
the axiom of choice. Apparently, this is not the case7 .
 
Next, we deal with uniqueness. It may be tempting to try to set things up
in such a way that algebraic closures would end up being solutions to a universal
problem; this would guarantee uniqueness by abstract nonsense. However, we run
into the same obstacle encountered in Proposition V.5.7: morphisms between ex-
tensions depend on choices of roots of polynomials, so that algebraic closures are
not ‚Äòuniversal‚Äô in the most straightforward sense of the term.
Therefore, a bit of independent work is needed.
Lemma 2.8. Let k ‚äÜ L be a Ô¨Åeld extension, with L algebraically closed. Let k ‚äÜ F
be any algebraic extension. Then there exists a morphism of extensions i : F ‚Üí L.
As pointed out above, i is by no means unique!
Proof. This argument also relies on Zorn‚Äôs lemma. Consider the set Z of homo-
morphisms
iK : K ‚Üí L
where K is an intermediate Ô¨Åeld, k ‚äÜ K ‚äÜ F , and iK restricts to the identity on k;
Z is nonempty, since the extension ik : k ‚äÜ L deÔ¨Ånes an element of Z. We give a
poset structure to Z by deÔ¨Åning
iK  iK 
if K ‚äÜ K  ‚äÜ F and iK  restricts to iK on K. To verify that every chain C in Z has
an upper bound in Z, let KC be the union of the sources of all iK ‚àà C (KC is clearly
a Ô¨Åeld); if Œ± ‚àà KC , deÔ¨Åne iKC (Œ±) to be iK (Œ±), where iK is any element of C such
that Œ± ‚àà K. This prescription is clearly independent of the chosen K and deÔ¨Ånes
a homomorphism KC ‚Üí L restricting to the identity on k. This homomorphism is
an upper bound for C.
By Zorn‚Äôs lemma, Z admits a maximal element iG , corresponding to an inter-
mediate Ô¨Åeld k ‚äÜ G ‚äÜ F . Let H = iG (G) be the image of G in L.
I claim that G = F : this will prove the statement, because it will imply that
there is a homomorphism iF : F ‚Üí L extending the identity on k.
Arguing by contradiction, assume that there exists an Œ± ‚àà F  G, and consider
the extension G ‚äÜ G(Œ±). Since Œ± ‚àà F is algebraic over k, it is algebraic over G;
7 Allegedly, the existence of algebraic closures is a consequence of the compactness theorem
for Ô¨Årst-order logic, which is known to be weaker than the axiom of choice.
