thus, it is a root of an irreducible polynomial g(x) ‚àà G[x]. Consider the induced
homomorphism
iG : G[x] ‚Üí H[x],
and let h(x) = iG(g(x)). Then h(x) is an irreducible polynomial over H, and it has
a root Œ≤ in L (this is where we use the hypothesis that L is algebraically closed!).
We are in the situation considered in Proposition 1.5: we have an isomorphism of
Ô¨Åelds iG : G ‚Üí H; we have simple extensions G(Œ±), H(Œ≤); and Œ±, Œ≤ are roots of
irreducible polynomials g(x), h(x) = iG (g(x)). By Proposition 1.5, iG lifts to an
isomorphism
iG(Œ±) : G(Œ±) ‚Üí H(Œ≤) ‚äÜ L
sending Œ± to Œ≤. This contradicts the maximality of iG ; hence G = F , concluding
the argument.
 
Proof of uniqueness of algebraic closures. Let k ‚äÜ k, k ‚äÜ k1 be two algebraic
closures of k; we have to prove that there exists an isomorphism k 1 ‚Üí k extending
the identity on k.
Since k ‚äÜ k1 is algebraic and k is algebraically closed, by Lemma 2.8 there
exists a homomorphism i : k 1 ‚Üí k extending the identity on k; this homomorphism
is trivially injective, since k1 is a Ô¨Åeld. It is also surjective, by Lemma 2.1: oth-
erwise k1 ‚äÜ k is a nontrivial algebraic extension, contradicting the fact that k1 is
algebraically closed. Therefore i is an isomorphism, as needed.
 
2.2. The Nullstellensatz. If K is an algebraically closed Ô¨Åeld, then every maxi-
mal ideal in K[x] is of the form (x‚àíc), for c ‚àà K (Exercise III.4.21). This statement
has a straightforward-looking generalization to polynomial rings in more indeter-
minates: if K is algebraically closed, then every maximal ideal in K[x1 , . . . , xn] is
of the form (x1 ‚àí c1 , . . . , xn ‚àí cn ).
Proving this statement is more challenging than it may seem from the looks of
it; it is one facet of the famous theorem known as Hilbert‚Äôs Nullstellensatz (‚Äútheorem
on the position of zeros‚Äù). The Nullstellensatz has made a few cameo appearances
earlier (see Example III.4.15 and ¬ßIII.6.5 in particular), and we will spend a little
time on it now. We will not prove the Nullstellensatz in its natural generality, that
is, for all Ô¨Åelds; this would take us too far. Actually, there are reasonably short
proofs of the theorem in this generality, but the short arguments I have run into
end up replacing a wider (and useful) context with ingenious cleverness; I do not
Ô¨Ånd these arguments particularly insightful or memorable.
By contrast, the theorem has a very simple and memorable proof if we make
the further assumption that the Ô¨Åeld is uncountable. Therefore, the reader will have
a complete proof of the theorem (in the form given below, which does not assume
the Ô¨Åeld to be algebraically closed) for Ô¨Åelds such as R and C but will have to trust
on faith regarding other extremely important Ô¨Åelds such as Q.
The most vivid applications of the Nullstellensatz involve basic deÔ¨Ånitions in
algebraic geometry, and we will get a small taste of this in the next section; but the
theorem itself is best understood in the context of the considerations on ‚ÄòÔ¨Åniteness‚Äô
mentioned in ¬ß1.3; see especially Remark 1.14. I pointed out that if k ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånitely
2. Algebraic closure, Nullstellensatz, and a little algebraic geometry
 405
generated as a Ô¨Åeld extension, then F need not be Ô¨Ånitely generated (that is, ‚ÄòÔ¨Ånite-
type‚Äô) as a k-algebra, and I raised the question of understanding extensions F that
are Ô¨Ånite-type k-algebras. The following result answers this question. It is my
favorite version of the Nullstellensatz; therefore I will name it so:
Theorem 2.9 (Nullstellensatz). Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Åeld extension, and assume that F
is a Ô¨Ånite-type k-algebra. Then k ‚äÜ F is a Ô¨Ånite (hence algebraic) extension.
The reader should pause and savor this statement for a moment. As pointed
out in ¬ßIII.6.5, an algebra k ‚Üí S may very well be of Ô¨Ånite type (as an algebra),
without being Ô¨Ånite (i.e., Ô¨Ånitely generated as a module): S = k[t] is an obvious
example. The content of Theorem 2.9 is that the distinction between Ô¨Ånite-type
and Ô¨Ånite disappears if S is a Ô¨Åeld.
As mentioned above, we will only prove this statement under an additional
(and somewhat unnatural) assumption, which reduces the argument to elementary
linear algebra.
Proof for uncountable Ô¨Åelds. Assume that k is uncountable.
Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Åeld extension, and assume that F is Ô¨Ånitely generated as an
algebra over k; in particular, it is Ô¨Ånitely generated as a Ô¨Åeld extension. We have
to prove that k ‚äÜ F is a Ô¨Ånite extension, or equivalently (by Proposition 1.15) that
it is algebraic, that is, that every Œ± ‚àà F  k is the root of a nonzero f (x) ‚àà k[x].
Now, by hypothesis F is the quotient of a polynomial ring over k:
k[x1 , . . . , xn ]
 / / F ;
this implies that there is a countable basis of F as a vector space over k, because
this is the case for k[x1 , . . . , xn ]. Consider then the set

this is an uncountable subset of F ; therefore it is linearly dependent over k (Propo-

Regardless of its proof, the form of the Nullstellensatz given in Theorem 2.9
does not look much like the other results I have mentioned as associated with it.
For one thing, it is not too clear why Theorem 2.9 should be called a theorem on
the ‚Äòposition of zeros‚Äô. The result deserves a little more attention.

Note how very (trivially) false the statement of Corollary 2.10 is over Ô¨Åelds
which are not algebraically closed: (x2 + 1) is maximal in R[x]. No such silliness
may occur over C, for any number of variables.
2.3. A little aÔ¨Éne algebraic geometry. Corollary 2.10 is one of the main
reasons why ‚Äòclassical‚Äô algebraic geometry developed over C rather than Ô¨Åelds such
as R or Q: it may be interpreted as saying that if K is algebraically closed, then there
is a natural bijection between the points of the product space K n and the maximal
ideals in the ring K[x1 , . . . , xn ]. This is the beginning of a fruitful dictionary
translating geometry into algebra and conversely. It is worthwhile formalizing this
statement and exploring other ‚Äògeometric‚Äô consequences of the Nullstellensatz.
For K a Ô¨Åeld, AnK denotes the aÔ¨Éne space of dimension n over K, that is, the
set of n-tuples of elements of K:
AnK = {(c1 , . . . , cn ) | ci ‚àà K}.
Elements of AnK are called points.
Objection: Why don‚Äôt we just use ‚ÄòK n‚Äô for this object? Because the latter
already stands for the standard n-dimensional vector space over K; so it carries
with it a certain baggage: the tuple (0, . . . , 0) is special, so are the vector subspaces
of K n, etc. By constrast, no point of AnK is ‚Äòspecial‚Äô; we can carry any point to
any other point by a simple translation. Similarly, although it is useful to consider
2. Algebraic closure, Nullstellensatz, and a little algebraic geometry
 407
aÔ¨Éne subspaces, these (unlike vector subspaces) are not required to go through the
origin. AÔ¨Éne geometry and linear algebra are diÔ¨Äerent in many respects.
We have already established that, by the Nullstellensatz, if K is algebraically
closed, then there is a natural bijection between the points of AnK and the maximal
ideals of the polynomial ring K[x1, . . . , xn ]. Concretely, the bijection works like
this: the point p = (c1 , . . . , cn ) corresponds to the set of polynomials
I (p) := {f (x) ‚àà K[x1 , . . . , xn ] | f (p) = 0},
where f (p) is the result of applying the evaluation map:
f (x1 , . . . , xn ) ‚Üí f (c1 , . . . , cn ) ‚àà K.
This of course is nothing but the homomorphism
œï : K[x1, . . . , xn ] ‚Üí K
deÔ¨Åned by prescribing xi ‚Üí ci; the set I (p) is simply ker œï, which is the maximal
ideal
(x 1 ‚àí c1 , . . . , xn ‚àí cn ).
This correspondence p ‚Üí I (p) may be deÔ¨Åned over every Ô¨Åeld; the content of
Corollary 2.10 is that every maximal ideal of K[x1 , . . . , xn ] corresponds to a point
of AnK in this way, if K is algebraically closed.
It is natural to upgrade the correspondence as follows (again, over arbitrary
Ô¨Åelds): for every subset S ‚äÜ AnK , consider the ideal
I (S) := {f (x) ‚àà K[x1 , . . . , xn ] | ‚àÄp ‚àà S, f (p) = 0},
that is, the set of polynomials ‚Äòvanishing along S‚Äô; it is immediately checked that
this is indeed an ideal of K[x1 , . . . , xn ]. We can also consider a correspondence
in the reverse direction, from ideals of K[x1 , . . . , xn] to subsets of AnK , deÔ¨Åned by
setting for every ideal I,
V (I) := {p = (c1 , . . . , cn ) ‚àà AnK | ‚àÄf ‚àà I, f (c1 , . . . , cn) = 0}.
Thus, V (I) is the set of common solutions of all the polynomial equations f = 0
as f ‚àà I: the set of points ‚Äòcut out in AnK ‚Äô by the polynomials in I.
In its most elementary manifestation, the dictionary mentioned in the beginning
of the section consists precisely of the pair of correspondences
I
 /{subsets of AnK } o
 {ideals in K[x1
 , . . . , xn
 ]} .
V
The set V (I) is often (somewhat improperly) called the variety of I, while I (S)
is (also improperly) the ideal of S.
The function V can be deÔ¨Åned (using the same prescription) for any set A ‚äÜ
K[x1 , . . . , xn], whether A is an ideal or not; it is clear that V (A) = V (I) where I is
the ideal generated by A (Exercise 2.5). Hilbert‚Äôs basis theorem (Theorem V.1.2)
tells us something rather interesting: K[x1, . . . , xn ] is Noetherian when K is a Ô¨Åeld;
hence every ideal I is generated by a Ô¨Ånite number of elements. Thus, for every
ideal I there exist polynomials f1 , . . . , fr such that (abusing notation a little)
V (I) = V (f1 , . . . , fr ).
408
 VII. Fields
In other words, if a set may be deÔ¨Åned by any set of polynomial equations, it may
in fact be deÔ¨Åned by a Ô¨Ånite set of polynomial equations8 .
I will pass in silence many simple-minded properties of the functions V , I
(they reverse inclusions, they behave reasonably well with respect to unions and
intersections, etc.); the reader should feel free to research the topic at will. But
I want to focus the reader‚Äôs attention on the fact that (of course) V , I are not
bijections; this is a problem, if we really want to construct a dictionary between
‚Äògeometry‚Äô and ‚Äòalgebra‚Äô.
For example, there are (in general) lots of subsets of AnK which are not cut out
by a set of polynomial equations, and there are lots of ideals in K[x1 , . . . , xn] which
are not obtained as I (S) for any subset S ‚äÜ AnK .
I will leave to the reader the task of Ô¨Ånding examples of the Ô¨Årst phenomenon
(Exercise 2.6). The way around it is to restrict our attention to subsets of AnK
which are of the form V (I) for some ideal I.
DeÔ¨Ånition 2.11. An (aÔ¨Éne) algebraic set is a subset S ‚äÜ AnK such that there
exists an ideal I ‚äÜ K[x1 , . . . , xn ] for which S = V (I).
 
This deÔ¨Ånition may seem like a cheap patch: we do not really know what the
image of V is, so we label it in some way and proceed. This is not entirely true‚Äîthe
family of algebraic subsets of a given AnK has a solid, recognizable structure: it is
the family of closed subsets in a topology on AnK (Exercise 2.7); this topology is
called the Zariski topology.
Studying aÔ¨Éne algebraic sets is the business of aÔ¨Éne algebraic geometry. The
aim is to understand ‚Äògeometric‚Äô properties of these sets in terms of ‚Äòalgebraic‚Äô
properties of corresponding ideals or other algebraic entities associated with them.
Example 2.12. Here are pictures of two algebraic subsets of A2 R :
The Ô¨Årst is V ((y ‚àí x2 )); the second is V ((y2 ‚àí x3 )). What feature of the ideal
(y 2 ‚àí x3 ) is responsible for the ‚Äòcusp‚Äô at (0, 0) in the second picture? The reader
will Ô¨Ånd out in any course in elementary algebraic geometry.

The addition of the variable y in the proof looks more reasonable (and less like

hyperbola:

of the origin (that is, V (x)) in the ‚Äúx-axis‚Äù (viewed as A1 K ). Similarly, V (1 ‚àí f y)
is a way to realize the Zariski open set AnK  V (f ) as a Zariski closed subset in a

The proof of Proposition 2.17 hinges on this fact, and this fact is also an
important building block in the basic set-up of algebraic geometry, since it can be
used to show that the Zariski topology has a basis consisting of (sets which are
isomorphic in a suitable sense to) aÔ¨Éne algebraic sets.
I will oÔ¨Écially record the conclusion we were able to establish concerning the
functions V , I :

Proof. Proposition 2.17 shows that I ‚ó¶ V is the identity on radical ideals, so V is

 
Summarizing: If K is algebraically closed, then studying sets deÔ¨Åned by poly-
nomial equations in a space K n is ‚Äòthe same thing as‚Äô studying radical ideals in a
polynomial ring K[x1 , . . . , xn ].
This correspondence is actually realized even more eÔ¨Äectively at the level of
K-algebras. We say that a ring is reduced if it has no nonzero nilpotents; then R/I
is reduced if and only if I is a radical ideal (Exercise 2.8).
2. Algebraic closure, Nullstellensatz, and a little algebraic geometry
 413
DeÔ¨Ånition 2.19. Let S ‚äÜ AnK be an algebraic set. The coordinate ring of S is the
quotient10
K[x1 , . . . , xn ]
K[S] :=
 .
 
I (S)
Thus, the coordinate ring of an algebraic set is a reduced, commutative K-
algebra of Ô¨Ånite type. The reader will establish a ‚Äòconcrete‚Äô interpretation of this
ring, as the ring of ‚Äòpolynomial functions‚Äô on S, in Exercise 2.12. One way to think
about the Nullstellensatz (in its manifestation as Corollary 2.18) is that, if K is
algebraically closed, then every reduced commutative K-algebra of Ô¨Ånite type R
may be realized as the coordinate ring of an aÔ¨Éne algebraic set S; the points of S
correspond in a natural way to the maximal ideals of R (Exercise 2.14).
In fact, in basic algebraic geometry one deÔ¨Ånes the category of aÔ¨Éne algebraic
sets over a Ô¨Åeld K in terms of this correspondence: morphisms of algebraic sets
are deÔ¨Åned so that they match homomorphisms of the corresponding (reduced,
Ô¨Ånite-type) K-algebras. (The reader will encounter a more precise deÔ¨Ånition in
Example VIII.1.9.)
This dictionary allows us to translate every ‚Äògeometric‚Äô feature of an algebraic
set (such as dimension, smoothness, etc.) into corresponding ‚Äòalgebraic‚Äô features of
the corresponding coordinate rings. Once these key algebraic features have been
identiÔ¨Åed, then one can throw away the restriction of working on reduced Ô¨Ånite-
type algebras over an algebraically closed Ô¨Åeld and try to ‚Äòdo geometry‚Äô on any (say
commutative, Noetherian) ring. For example, it turns out that PIDs correspond
to (certain) smooth curves in the aÔ¨Éne geometric world; and then one can try to
think of Z as a ‚Äòsmooth curve‚Äô (although Z is not a reduced Ô¨Ånite-type K-algebra
over any Ô¨Åeld K!) and try to use theorems inspired by the geometry of curves to
understand features of Z‚Äîthat is, use geometry to do number theory11.
Another direction in which these simple considerations may be generalized is
by ‚Äògluing‚Äô aÔ¨Éne algebraic sets into manifold-like objects: sets which may not
be aÔ¨Éne algebraic sets ‚Äòglobally‚Äô but may be covered by aÔ¨Éne algebraic sets. A
concrete way to do this is by working in projective space rather than aÔ¨Éne space;
the globalization process can then be carried out in a straightforward way at the
algebraic level, where it translates into the study of ‚Äògraded‚Äô rings and modules‚Äî
more notions for which, regretfully, I will Ô¨Ånd no room in this book (except for a
glance, in ¬ßVIII.4.3). In the second half of the twentieth century a more abstract
viewpoint, championed by Alexander Grothendieck and others, has proven to be
extremely eÔ¨Äective; it has led to the intense study of schemes, the current language
of choice in algebraic geometry. We have encountered the simplest kind of schemes
when we introduced the spectrum of a ring R, Spec R, back in ¬ßIII.4.3.

polynomial rings; hopefully the context takes care of this. It makes sense to incorporate the name
of the Ô¨Åeld in the notation, since one could in principle change the base Ô¨Åeld while keeping the
‚Äòsame‚Äô equations encoded in the ideal I (S).

An irreducible algebraic set is ‚Äòall in one piece‚Äô, like AnK itself, and unlike (for
example) V (xy) in the aÔ¨Éne plane A2 K with coordinates x, y. Irreducible aÔ¨Éne
algebraic sets are called (aÔ¨Éne algebraic) varieties. [2.18]

it can be proven that the Krull dimension of the coordinate ring of a variety equals
the transcendence degree of its Ô¨Åeld of rational functions. This is a consequence of
Noether‚Äôs normalization theorem, a cornerstone of commutative algebra.

3. Geometric impossibilities
Very simple considerations in Ô¨Åeld theory dispose easily of a whole class of geometric
problems which had seemed unapproachable for a very long time‚Äîproblems which
preoccupied the Greeks and were only solved in the relatively recent past.
These problems have to do with the construction of certain geometric Ô¨Åg-
ures with ‚Äòstraightedge and compass‚Äô, that is, subject to certain very strict rules.
The practical utility of these constructions would appear to be absolute zero, but
the intellectual satisfaction of being able to thoroughly understand matters which
stumped extremely intelligent people for a very long time is well worth the little
side trip.
3.1. Constructions by straightedge and compass. We begin with two points
O, P in the ordinary, real plane. You are allowed to mark (‚Äòconstruct‚Äô) more points
and other geometric Ô¨Ågures in the plane, but only according to the following rules:
‚Ä¢ If you have constructed two points A, B, then you can draw the line joining
them (using your straightedge).
‚Ä¢ If you have constructed two points A, B, then you can draw the circle with
center at A and containing B (using your compass).
‚Ä¢ You can mark any number of points of intersection of any two distinct lines,
line and circle, or circles that you have drawn already.
Performing these actions leads to complex (and beautiful) collections of lines and
circles; we say that we have ‚Äòconstructed‚Äô a geometric Ô¨Ågure if that Ô¨Ågure appears
as a subset of the whole picture.
418
 VII. Fields
For example, here is a recipe to construct an equilateral triangle with O, P as
two of its vertices:
(1) draw the circle with center O, through P ;
(2) draw the circle with center P , through O;
(3) let Q be any of the two points of intersection of the two circles;
(4) draw the lines through O, P ; O, Q; and P , Q.
Of course O, P , Q are vertices of an equilateral triangle.
It is a pleasant exercise to try to come up with a speciÔ¨Åc sequence of basic moves
constructing a given Ô¨Ågure or geometric conÔ¨Åguration. The enterprising reader
could try to produce the vertices of a pentagon by a straightedge-and-compass
construction, without looking it up and before we learn enough to thwart pure
geometric intuition.
The general question is to decide whether a Ô¨Ågure can or cannot be constructed
this way. Three problems of this kind became famous in antiquity:
‚Ä¢ trisecting angles;
‚Ä¢ squaring circles;
‚Ä¢ doubling cubes.
For example, one assumes one has already constructed two lines forming an angle Œ∏
and asks whether one can construct two lines forming Œ∏/3. This is trivially possible
for some constructible Œ∏: we just constructed an angle of œÄ/3, so evidently we were
able to trisect the angle œÄ; but can this be done for all constructible angles?
The answer is no. Similarly, it is not possible to construct a square whose area
equals the area of a (constructible) circle, and it is not possible to construct the
side of a cube whose volume is twice as large as a cube with given (constructible)
side.
Field theory will allow us to establish all this13 . Later we will return to these
constructions and study the question of the constructibility of regular polygons
13 However, for the impossibility of squaring circles we will use the transcendence of œÄ, which
we are taking on faith.
3. Geometric impossibilities
 419
with a given side: it is very easy to construct equilateral triangles (we just did),
squares, hexagons; it is not too hard to construct pentagons; but the question
of which polygons are constructible and which are not connects beautifully with
deeper questions in Ô¨Åeld theory.
The three problems listed above have an illustrious history; they apparently
date back to at least 414 B.C., if one is to take this fragment from Aristophanes‚Äô
‚ÄúThe birds‚Äù as an indication:
METON: By measuring with a straightedge there
the circle becomes a square with a court within.14
The problems depend on the precise restrictions imposed on the constructions.
For example, one can trisect angles if one is allowed to mark points on the straight-
edge (thus making it a ruler ); in fact, this was already known to Archimedes (Ex-
ercise 3.13).
Translating these geometric problems into algebra requires establishing the fea-
sibility of a few basic constructions.
First of all, one can construct a line containing a given point A and perpendic-
ular to a given line (thus, contains at least another constructible point B). For
this, draw the circle with center A and containing B;

It follows that determining which Ô¨Ågures are constructible by straightedge and
compass is equivalent to determining which numbers may be realized as coordinates
of a constructible point.
DeÔ¨Ånition 3.1. A real number r is constructible if the point (r, 0) is constructible
with straightedge and compass (assuming O = (0, 0) and P = (1, 0), as above). I
will denote by CR ‚äÜ R the set of constructible real numbers.
 
Also, we can identify the real plane with C, placing O at 0 and P at 1, and we
say that z = x+iy is constructible if the point (x, y) is constructible by straightedge
and compass. I will denote by CC ‚äÜ C the set of constructible complex numbers.
Summarizing the foregoing discussion, we have proved:
Lemma 3.2. A point (x, y) is constructible by straightedge and compass if and only
if x + iy ‚àà CC , if and only if x, y ‚àà CR .
The next obvious question is what kind of structure these sets of constructible
numbers carry, and this prompts us to look at a few more basic straightedge-and-
compass constructions.
Lemma 3.3. The subset CR ‚äÜ R of constructible numbers is a subÔ¨Åeld of R.
Likewise, CC is a subÔ¨Åeld of C, and in fact CC = CR (i).
Proof. The set CR ‚äÜ R is nonempty, so in order to show it is a Ô¨Åeld, we only
need to show that it is closed with respect to subtraction and division by a nonzero
constructible number (cf. Proposition II.6.2).
The reader will check that CR is closed under subtraction (Exercise 3.2). To
see that CR is closed under division, let a, b ‚àà CR, with b = 0. Since A = (0, a) and
B = (b, 0) are constructible by hypothesis, we can construct C as the y-intersect
of the line through P = (1, 0) and parallel to the line through A and B, as in the
following picture15 :
15 In the picture I am assuming a > 0 and b > 1; the reader will check that other possibilities
lead to the same conclusion.
422
 VII. Fields
Since the triangles AOB and COP are similar, we see that C = (0, a/b); it follows
that a/b is constructible.
The fact that CC is a subÔ¨Åeld of C is an immediate consequence of the fact that
CR is a Ô¨Åeld, and the proof of this is left to the enjoyment of the reader (Exercise 3.7).
The fact that CC = CR (i) is a restatement of the fact that x + iy ‚àà CC if and only
if x and y are in CR.
 
We may view CR and CC as extensions of Q, drawing a bridge between con-
structibility by straightedge and compass and Ô¨Åeld theory: we will be able to under-
stand constructibility of geometric Ô¨Ågures if we can understand the Ô¨Åeld extensions
Q ‚äÜ CR ‚äÜ CC.
Happily, we can understand these extensions!
3.2. Constructible numbers and quadratic extensions. Our goal is to prove
the following amazingly explicit description of CR (immediately implying one for CC).
Theorem 3.4. Let Œ≥ ‚àà R. Then Œ≥ ‚àà CR if and only if there exist real numbers
Œ¥1 , . . . , Œ¥k such that ‚àÄj = 1, . . . , k
[Q(Œ¥1, . . . , Œ¥j ) : Q(Œ¥1 , . . . , Œ¥j‚àí1 )] = 2
and Œ≥ ‚àà Q(Œ¥1, . . . , Œ¥k ).
In other words, Œ≥ ‚àà R is constructible if and only if it can be placed in the
top Ô¨Åeld of a sequence of (real) quadratic extensions over Q. Since CC = CR (i),
the same statement holds for constructible complex numbers, including i in the list
of Œ¥j (or simply allowing the Œ¥j to be complex numbers; cf. Exercise 3.9).
The proof of this theorem is as explicit as one can possibly hope. From a given
straightedge-and-compass construction of a point (x, y) one can obtain an explicit
sequence Œ¥1 , . . . , Œ¥k as in the statement, such that x and y belong to the extension
Q(Œ¥1 , . . . , Œ¥k ); conversely, from any element Œ≥ of any such extension one can obtain
an explicit construction of a point (Œ≥, 0) by straightedge and compass.
Proof. Let‚Äôs Ô¨Årst argue in the ‚Äògeometry to algebra‚Äô direction. A conÔ¨Åguration
of points, lines, and circles obtained by a straightedge-and-compass construction
may be described by the coordinates of the points and the equations of the lines

and circles. Suppose that at one stage in a given construction all coordinates of all

number of steps in the construction, since at the beginning the conÔ¨Åguration (that
is, the pair of points O = (0, 0), P = (1, 0)) is deÔ¨Åned over Q.
Verifying my claim amounts to verifying it for the basic operations deÔ¨Åning
straightedge-and-compass constructions. The reader will check (Exercise 3.5) that
the point of intersection of two lines deÔ¨Åned over F has coordinates in F and that
lines and circles determined by points with coordinates in F are deÔ¨Åned over F . So
Œ¥ = 1 works in all these cases.

3.3. Famous impossibilities. Theorem 3.4 easily settles the three problems men-
tioned in ¬ß3.1, via the following immediate consequence:
Corollary 3.6. Let Œ≥ ‚àà CC be a constructible number. Then [Q(Œ≥) : Q] is a power
of 2.
Proof. By Lemma 3.3 and Theorem 3.4, there exist Œ¥1 . . . , Œ¥k ‚àà R such that
Œ≥ ‚àà Q(Œ¥1 , . . . , Œ¥k , i),
and each Œ¥j has degree ‚â§ 2 over Q(Œ¥1, . . . , Œ¥j‚àí1). Repeated application of Proposi-
tion 1.10 shows that
[Q(Œ¥1 , . . . , Œ¥k , i) : Q]
is a power of 2, and since
Q ‚äÜ Q(Œ≥) ‚äÜ Q(Œ¥1, . . . , Œ¥k , i),
the statement follows from Corollary 1.11.

In particular, constructible real numbers must satisfy the same condition.
Consider the problem of trisecting an angle. We know we can construct an
angle of 60‚ó¶ (this is a subproduct of the construction of an equilateral triangle).
Constructing an angle of 20‚ó¶ is equivalent (Exercise 3.10) to constructing the com-
plex number Œ≥ on the unit circle and with argument œÄ/9:

t9 + 1 = (t3 + 1)(t6 ‚àí t3 + 1).
It does not satisfy t3 + 1; therefore its minimal polynomial over Q is a factor of
t6 ‚àí t3 + 1.

By Corollary 3.6, Œ≥ is not constructible. Therefore there exist constructible angles

reveals that if p is prime, then the regular p-gon can be constructed only if p ‚àí 1 is
a power of 2. This is even more restrictive than it looks at Ô¨Årst, since if p = 2k + 1

4. Field extensions, II
It is time to continue our survey of diÔ¨Äerent Ô¨Çavors of Ô¨Åeld extensions. The keywords
here are splitting Ô¨Åelds, normal, separable.
4. Field extensions, II
 429
4.1. Splitting Ô¨Åelds and normal extensions. In ¬ß2 we have constructed the
algebraic closure k of any given Ô¨Åeld k: every polynomial in k[x] factors as a
product of linear terms (that is, ‚Äòsplits‚Äô ) in k[x], and k is the ‚Äòsmallest‚Äô extension
of k satisfying this property.
Here is an analogous, but more modest, requirement: given a subset F ‚äÜ k[x]
of polynomials, construct an extension k ‚äÜ F such that every polynomial in F
splits as a product of linear terms over F , and require F to be as small as pos-
sible with this property. We then call F the splitting Ô¨Åeld for F . In practice we
will only be interested in the case in which F is a Ô¨Ånite collection of polynomi-
als f1 (x), . . . , fr (x); then requiring that each fi (x) splits over F is equivalent to
requiring that the product f 1 (x) ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ fr (x) splits over F .
That is, for our purposes it is not restrictive to assume that F consists of a
single polynomial f (x) ‚àà k[x].
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.1. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld, and let f (x) ‚àà k[x] be a polynomial of degree d.
The splitting Ô¨Åeld for f (x) over k is an extension F of k such that

in F .

Note that it is clear that a splitting Ô¨Åeld exists: given f (x) ‚àà k[x], the subÔ¨Åeld
F ‚äÜ k generated over k by the roots of f (x) in k satisÔ¨Åes the requirements in
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.1. But I have written the splitting Ô¨Åeld, and this is justiÔ¨Åed by the
uniqueness part of the following basic observation, which also evaluates ‚Äòhow big‚Äô
this extension can be.
Lemma 4.2. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld, and let f (x) ‚àà k[x]. Then the splitting Ô¨Åeld F for

k to any splitting Ô¨Åeld of f (x) over k.

uniqueness up to isomorphism.

Examples such as these have always left me with the impression that Ô¨Åeld
theory must be rather mysterious: innocent-looking variations on the parameters
of a problem may cause dramatic changes in the corresponding Ô¨Åeld extensions. On
the plus side, this hints that Ô¨Åeld theory can indeed be a very precise tool in (for
example) the study of polynomials.
Splitting Ô¨Åelds will play an important role in the rest of the story. They are
even more special than they may appear to be at Ô¨Årst: it turns out that not only do
they split the given polynomial, but they also automatically split any irreducible
polynomial which dares touch them with a root. This makes splitting Ô¨Åelds normal
extensions:
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.7. A Ô¨Åeld extension k ‚äÜ F is normal if for every irreducible poly-
nomial f (x) ‚àà k[x], f (x) has a root in F if and only if f (x) splits as a product of
linear factors over F .
 
Theorem 4.8. A Ô¨Åeld extension k ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånite and normal if and only if F is the
splitting Ô¨Åeld of some polynomial f (x) ‚àà k[x].
Proof. Assume k ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånite and normal. Then F is Ô¨Ånitely generated: F =
k(Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±r ) with Œ±i algebraic over k. Let pi(t) be the minimal polynomial of Œ±i
over k. As F is normal over k, each pi(t) splits completely over F , and hence so
does f (t) = p1 (t) ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ pr (t). It follows that F is the splitting Ô¨Åeld of f (x).
Conversely, assume that F is a splitting Ô¨Åeld for a polynomial f (x) ‚àà k[x], and
let p(x) ‚àà k[x] be an irreducible polynomial, such that F contains a root Œ± of p(x).
Viewing F as a subÔ¨Åeld of the algebraic closure k, let Œ≤ ‚àà k be any other root
of p(x); we are going to show that Œ≤ ‚àà F . This will prove that F contains all roots
of p(x), implying that k ‚äÜ F is normal, and k ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånite by Lemma 4.2.

4.2. Separable polynomials. Our intuition may lead us to think that if a poly-
nomial factors as a product of linear factors and we are not ‚Äòpurposely‚Äô repeating
one of the factors (as in (x ‚àí 1)2 (x ‚àí 2)), then these will be distinct. For example,
surely irreducible polynomials necessarily split as products of distinct factors in an
algebraic closure, right? Wrong.
Example 4.11. Let p be a prime, and consider the Ô¨Åeld Fp (t) of rational functions
over Fp . Then the polynomial
xp ‚àí t ‚àà Fp(t)[x]
is irreducible: by Eisenstein‚Äôs criterion it is irreducible in Fp [t][x] (since (t) is prime
in Fp [t]), hence in Fp (t)[x] by Proposition V.4.16. Let u be a root of this polynomial
in an extension L of Fp (t) (for example L could be the algebraic closure of Fp (t),
or more modestly a splitting Ô¨Åeld for the polynomial). Then (Exercise 4.8)
xp ‚àí t = (x ‚àí u)p
in L[x]; that is, u has multiplicity p as a root of f (x).
In other words, the minimal polynomial over Fp (t) of u ‚àà L vanishes p times
at u, and there is nothing to do about this: no smaller power of (x ‚àí u) than
(x ‚àí u)p = xp ‚àí t has coeÔ¨Écients in Fp(t) (a smaller power would give a nontrivial
factor of xp ‚àí t, and xp ‚àí t is irreducible).
 
I have always found this example diÔ¨Écult to visualize, because of intuition
developed in characteristic 0, and as we will see, no such pathology can occur in
characteristic 0.
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.12. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld. A polynomial f (x) ‚àà k[x] is separable if it
has no multiple factors over its splitting Ô¨Åeld; f (x) is inseparable if it has multiple
factors over its splitting Ô¨Åeld.
 
Thus, the polynomial xp ‚àí t in Example 4.11 is inseparable. I suppose the
terminology reÔ¨Çects the fact that we cannot ‚Äòseparate‚Äô its roots: they come clumped
together like quarks in a proton, and we cannot take them apart.
Of course DeÔ¨Ånition 4.12 does not depend on the chosen splitting Ô¨Åeld, since
these are unique up to isomorphism (Lemma 4.2). In fact, to detect separability,
we can use any Ô¨Åeld in which the polynomial splits as a product of linear factors
(by Exercise 4.1). The Ô¨Årst, somewhat surprising, observation about separability is
that we can in fact detect it without leaving the Ô¨Åeld of coeÔ¨Écients of f (x). This
fact uses a notion borrowed from calculus: for a polynomial
f (x) = a0 + a1 x + a2 x2 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + an xn ,
we denote by f (x) the ‚Äòderivative‚Äô
f  (x) = a1 + 2a2x + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + nan xn‚àí1.
434
 VII. Fields
Of course this is a purely formal operation; no limiting process is at work over
arbitrary Ô¨Åelds. Still, all expected properties of derivatives hold, as the reader
should check: for example, (f g) = f  g + f g as usual.
Lemma 4.13. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld, and let f (x) ‚àà k[x]. Then f (x) is separable if and
only if f (x) and f (x) are relatively prime.
By deÔ¨Ånition, f (x) and f (x) are relatively prime precisely when the greatest
common divisor of f (x) and f  (x) is 1. Note that if k ‚äÜ F , the gcd of f (x) and f  (x)
is the same whether it is considered in k[x] or in F [x]: for example because it can
be computed by applying the Euclidean algorithm (¬ßV.2.4), and this proceeds in
exactly the same way whether it is performed in k[x] or in F [x].
Proof. First assume that f (x) is not separable. Then f (x) has a multiple root in
a splitting Ô¨Åeld F ; that is,
f (x) = (x ‚àí Œ±)m g(x)
for some Œ± ‚àà F , g(x) ‚àà F [x], and m ‚â• 2. Therefore
f  (x) = m(x ‚àí Œ±)m‚àí1 g(x) + (x ‚àí Œ±)mg  (x),
and it follows that (x ‚àí Œ±) is a common factor of f (x) and f (x) in F [x]. Therefore
  gcd(f (x), f (x)) = 1 in F [x]; hence gcd(f (x), f (x)) = 1 in k[x]; that is, f (x), f (x)
are not relatively prime.
 Conversely, assume gcd(f (x), f (x)) = 1, so f (x) and f (x) have a common
irreducible factor (x ‚àí Œ±) in the algebraic closure k of k. Write f (x) = (x ‚àí Œ±)h(x);
we have
f  (x) = h(x) + (x ‚àí Œ±)h(x),
and it follows that x ‚àí Œ± divides h(x) since it divides f  (x). But then
(x ‚àí Œ±) 2 | f (x);
hence f (x) is inseparable.
 
For example, the polynomial xp ‚àí t of Example 4.11 may be seen to be insepa-
rable without invoking splitting Ô¨Åelds: the derivative of xp ‚àí t equals pxp‚àí1 = 0 in
characteristic p, and gcd(xp ‚àí t, 0) = xp ‚àí t = 1. This example captures one of the
key features of inseparability:
Lemma 4.14. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld, and let f (x) ‚àà k[x] be an inseparable irreducible
polynomial. Then f (x) = 0.
Proof. Since f (x) is inseparable, f (x) and f (x) have a common irreducible fac-
tor q(x) by Lemma 4.13; but as f (x) is itself irreducible, q(x) must be an associate
of f (x), and in particular its degree is larger than the degree of f (x) if f  (x) = 0.
As q(x) | f  (x), the only option is that f  (x) = 0.
 
This already tells us that irreducible polynomials are necessarily separable in
characteristic 0: in characteristic 0, the derivative of a nonconstant polynomial

clearly cannot vanish. In fact, Lemma 4.14 gives us a precise picture of what
inseparable, irreducible polynomials must look like. If

This leads us to a precise result linking separability and a Ô¨Çavor of Ô¨Åelds that

DeÔ¨Ånition 4.15. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld of characteristic p > 0. The Frobenius homo-
morphism is the map k ‚Üí k deÔ¨Åned by x ‚Üí xp .
 
The Frobenius homomorphism may not look like a homomorphism of rings, but
it is (Exercise 4.8). It must be injective, as is every nontrivial ring homomorphism
from a Ô¨Åeld; but it is not necessarily surjective.
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.16. A Ô¨Åeld k is perfect if char k = 0 or if char k > 0 and the Frobenius
homomorphism is surjective.
 
Proposition 4.17. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld. Then k is perfect if and only if all irreducible
polynomials in k[x] are separable.
Proof. I will prove that irreducible polynomials over a perfect Ô¨Åeld are separable,
leaving the other implication to the reader (Exercise 4.12).
We have already noted that irreducible polynomials are separable over Ô¨Åelds of
characteristic zero. In positive characteristic p, we have observed that an insepara-
ble irreducible polynomial must be of the form

 VII. Fields
Corollary 4.18. Finite Ô¨Åelds are perfect. Therefore, over Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åelds, irreducible
polynomials are separable.
Proof. The Frobenius map is injective (because it is a homomorphism of Ô¨Åelds), so
it is surjective over Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åelds, by the pigeon-hole principle. Therefore Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åelds
are perfect, and the second part of the statement follows from Proposition 4.17. 
The reader now sees why I have chosen the somewhat unusual Ô¨Åeld Fp (t) in
Example 4.11: we need a Ô¨Åeld of positive characteristic, but no example of irre-
ducible inseparable polynomial can be found over Fp , by Corollary 4.18; to concoct
an example, we need to enlarge the Ô¨Åeld so that it is inÔ¨Ånite and to throw in an
element (that is, t) for which there is no p-th root, that is, which is not in the image
of Frobenius.
4.3. Separable extensions and embeddings in algebraic closures. The ter-
minology examined in the previous section extends to the language of Ô¨Åeld exten-
sions. If k ‚äÜ F is an extension and Œ± ‚àà F is algebraic over k, we say that Œ± is
separable over k if the minimal polynomial of Œ± over k is separable; Œ± is inseparable
otherwise.
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.19. An algebraic Ô¨Åeld extension k ‚äÜ F is separable if every Œ± ‚àà F is
separable over k.
 
With this terminology, Proposition 4.17 may be restated in the following more
impressive form:
Proposition 4.20. A Ô¨Åeld k is perfect if and only if every algebraic extension of k
is separable.
In particular, algebraic extensions of Q (or any Ô¨Åeld of characteristic zero) and
of every Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åeld are necessarily separable.
The separability condition is exceedingly convenient, and we will essentially
adopt it henceforth: all extensions we will seriously consider will be separable. One
convenient feature of separable extensions is the following alternative description
of the separability condition.
We have seen (Lemma 2.8) that every algebraic extension k ‚äÜ F may be
embedded in an algebraic closure k ‚äÜ k, and I pointed out that this can in general
be done in many diÔ¨Äerent ways. If Ô¨Ånite, the number of diÔ¨Äerent homomorphisms
F ‚Üí k extending the identity on k is denoted by
[F : k]s .
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.21. This is the separable degree of F over k.
 
It is clear that this number is independent of the chosen algebraic closure k ‚äÜ k.
What does it have to do with separability?
Lemma 4.22. Let k ‚äÜ k(Œ±) be a simple algebraic extension. Then [k(Œ±) : k]s equals
the number of distinct roots in k of the minimal polynomial of Œ±. In particular,
[k(Œ±) : k]s ‚â§ [k(Œ±) : k], with equality if and only if Œ± is separable over k.
4. Field extensions, II
 437
Proof. The proof is essentially (and not by coincidence) a rehash of the proof of
Corollary 1.7. Associate with each Œπ : k(Œ±) ‚Üí k extending idk the image Œπ(Œ±), which
must be a root of the minimal polynomial of Œ±. This correspondence is injective,
since Œπ(Œ±) determines Œπ (as Œπ extends the identity on k). To see it is surjective, let
Œ≤ ‚àà k be any other root, and consider the extension k(Œ≤) ‚äÜ k; by Proposition 1.5
there is an isomorphism k(Œ±) ‚Üí k(Œ≤) sending Œ± to Œ≤, and composing with the
embedding k(Œ≤) ‚äÜ k deÔ¨Ånes the corresponding Œπ, as needed.
 
Thus, the ‚Äòseparable degree‚Äô does detect separability, for simple extensions.
Further, it is multiplicative over successive extensions:
Lemma 4.23. Let k ‚äÜ E ‚äÜ F be algebraic extensions. Then [F : k]s is Ô¨Ånite if
and only if both [F : E]s, [E : k]s are Ô¨Ånite, and in this case
[F : k]s = [F : E]s [E : k]s .
Proof. DiÔ¨Äerent embeddings of E into k extend to diÔ¨Äerent embeddings of F into k,
by Lemma 2.8; and embeddings of F into E = k extending the identity on E extend
a fortiori the identity on k. Therefore, if any of [F : E]s , [E : k]s is inÔ¨Ånite, then
so is [F : k]s .
For the converse implication, it suÔ¨Éces to prove the stated degree formula. But
every embedding F ‚äÜ k extending the identity on k may be obtained in two steps:
Ô¨Årst extend the identity to an embedding E ‚äÜ k, which can be done in [E : k]s ways;
and then extend the chosen embedding E ‚äÜ k = E to an embedding F ‚äÜ E = k,
which can be done in [F : E]s ways. There are precisely [F : E]s[E : k]s ways to
do this, as stated.
 
Lemmas 4.22 and 4.23 allow us to recast separability of Ô¨Ånite extensions entirely
in the light of counting embeddings in an algebraic closure:
Proposition 4.24. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Ånite extension. Then [F : k]s ‚â§ [F : k], and
the following are equivalent:
(i) F = k(Œ±1, . . . , Œ±r ), where each Œ±i is separable over k;
(ii) k ‚äÜ F is separable;
(iii) [F : k]s = [F : k].
Proof. Since F is Ô¨Ånite over k, it is Ô¨Ånitely generated. Let F = k(Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±r ).
Then using Lemma 4.23, Lemma 4.22, and Proposition 1.10,
[F : k]s = [k(Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±r‚àí1)(Œ±r ) : k(Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±r‚àí1 )]s ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ [k(Œ±1 ) : k]s
‚â§ [k(Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±r‚àí1)(Œ±r ) : k(Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±r‚àí1 )] ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ [k(Œ±1 ) : k]
= [F : k].
This proves the stated inequality.
(i) =‚áí (iii): If each Œ±i is separable over k, then it is separable over the Ô¨Åeld
k(Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±i‚àí1 ) (Exercise 4.15), so the inequality is an equality by Lemma 4.22.
438
 VII. Fields
(iii) =‚áí (ii): Assume [F : k]s = [F : k], and let Œ± ‚àà F . We have k ‚äÜ k(Œ±) ‚äÜ F ;
hence by Lemma 4.23
[F : k(Œ±)]s [k(Œ±) : k]s = [F : k]s = [F : k] = [F : k(Œ±)][k(Œ±) : k].
Since both separable degrees are less than or equal to their plain counterparts, the
equality implies
[k(Œ±) : k]s = [k(Œ±) : k],
proving that Œ± is separable, by Lemma 4.22. Therefore the extension k ‚äÜ F is
separable according to DeÔ¨Ånition 4.19.
(ii) =‚áí (i) is immediate from DeÔ¨Ånition 4.19, since Ô¨Ånite extensions are Ô¨Ånitely
generated.
 
For example, if Œ± is separable over k, then every Œ≤ ‚àà k(Œ±) is separable. This
would seem rather mysterious from the deÔ¨Ånition alone: why should the fact that
the minimal polynomial of Œ± has distinct roots in k imply the same for the minimal
polynomial of Œ≤? It does, by virtue of Proposition 4.24.
Remark 4.25. While we have only proved [F : k]s ‚â§ [F : k], one can in fact prove
that [F : k]s divides [F : k]: in fact, [F : k]s is the degree of a certain intermediate
Ô¨Åeld Fsep over k. The diligent reader will prove this in Exercise 4.18.

5. Field extensions, III
The material in ¬ß4 provides us with the main tools needed to tackle several key
examples. In this section we study Ô¨Ånite and cyclotomic Ô¨Åelds, and we return to
the question of when a Ô¨Ånite extension is in fact simple (cf. Example 1.19).
5. Field extensions, III
 441
5.1. Finite Ô¨Åelds. Let F be a Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åeld, and let p be its characteristic. We know
(¬ß1.1) that F may be viewed as an extension
Fp ‚äÜ F
of Fp = Z/pZ; let d = [F : Fp ]. Since F has dimension d as a vector space over Fp ,
it is isomorphic to F dp as a vector space, and in particular |F | = pd is a power of p.
The general question we pose is whether there exist Ô¨Åelds of cardinality equal
to every power of a prime, and we aim to classifying all Ô¨Åelds of a given cardinality.
The conclusion we will reach is as neat as it can be: for every prime power q there
exists exactly one Ô¨Åeld F with q elements, up to isomorphism.
Also recall (Remark III.1.16) that, by a theorem of Wedderburn, every Ô¨Ånite
division ring is in fact a Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åeld. Thus, relaxing the hypothesis of commutativity
in this subsection would not lead to a diÔ¨Äerent classiÔ¨Åcation.
Theorem 5.1. Let q = p d be a power of a prime integer p. Then the polynomial
xq ‚àí x is separable over Fp , and the splitting Ô¨Åeld of the polynomial xq ‚àí x over Fp
is a Ô¨Åeld with precisely q elements. Conversely, let F be a Ô¨Åeld with exactly q
elements; then F is a splitting Ô¨Åeld for xq ‚àí x over Fp.
Proof. Let F be the splitting Ô¨Åeld of xq ‚àí x over Fp . Let E be the set of roots
of f (x) = xq ‚àí x in F . Since f (x) = qxq‚àí1 ‚àí 1 = ‚àí1 (as q = 0 in characteristic p),
we have (f (x), f (x)) = 1; hence (Lemma 4.13) f (x) is separable, and E consists of
precisely q elements. I claim that E is a Ô¨Åeld, and it follows that E = F . Indeed, F
is generated by the roots of f (x); hence the smallest subÔ¨Åeld of F containing E is
F itself.
To see that E is a Ô¨Åeld, let a, b ‚àà E. Then aq = a and bq = b; it follows that
(a ‚àí b)q = aq + (‚àí1)q bq = a ‚àí b
(using Exercise 4.8; note that (‚àí1)q = ‚àí1 if p is odd and (‚àí1)q = +1 = ‚àí1 if
p = 2). If b = 0,
(ab‚àí1)q = aq (bq )‚àí1 = ab‚àí1 .
Thus E is closed under subtraction and division by a nonzero element, proving that
E is a Ô¨Åeld and concluding the proof of the Ô¨Årst statement.
To prove the second statement, let F be a Ô¨Åeld with exactly q elements. The
nonzero elements of F form a group under multiplication, consisting of q ‚àí 1 ele-
ments; therefore, the (multiplicative) order of every nonzero a ‚àà F divides q ‚àí 1
(Example II.8.15). Therefore,
a = 0 =‚áí aq‚àí1 = 1 =‚áí aq ‚àí a = 0;
of course 0q ‚àí 0 = 0. In other words, the polynomial xq ‚àí x has q roots in F (that
is, all elements of F !); it follows that F is a splitting Ô¨Åeld for xq ‚àí x, as stated. 
Corollary 5.2. For every prime power q there exists one and only one Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åeld
of order q, up to isomorphism.
Proof. This follows immediately from Theorem 5.1 and the uniqueness of splitting
Ô¨Åelds (Lemma 4.2).
 
442
 VII. Fields
Since there is exactly one isomorphism class of Ô¨Åelds of order q for any given
prime power q, we can devise a notation for a Ô¨Åeld of order q; it makes sense to
adopt18 Fq . This is called the Galois Ô¨Åeld of order q.
Example 5.3. Let p be a prime integer. Then I claim that the polynomial x4 + 1
is reducible 19 over Fp (and therefore over every Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åeld).
Since x4 + 1 = (x + 1)4 in F2 [x], the statement holds for p = 2. Thus, we may
assume that p is an odd prime. Then I claim that x4 + 1 divides xp2
 ‚àí x. Indeed,
the square of every odd number is congruent to 1 mod 8 (Exercise II.2.11); hence
8 | (p2 ‚àí 1); hence x8 ‚àí 1 divides xp2
 ‚àí1 ‚àí 1 (Exercise V.2.13); hence
(x4 + 1) | (x8 ‚àí 1) | (xp2
 ‚àí1 ‚àí 1) | (xp2
 ‚àí x).
It follows that x4 + 1 factors completely in the splitting Ô¨Åeld of xp2
 ‚àí x, that is,
in Fp2 . If Œ± is a root of x4 + 1 in Fp2 , we have the extensions
Fp ‚äÜ Fp (Œ±) ‚äÜ Fp2 ;
therefore (Corollary 1.11) [Fp(Œ±) : Fp ] divides [Fp2 : Fp ] = 2. That is, Œ± has degree 1
or 2 over Fp . But then its minimal polynomial is a factor of degree 1 or 2 of (x4 +1),
showing that the latter is reducible.
 
Theorem 5.1 has many interesting consequences, and we sample a few in the
rest of this subsection.
Corollary 5.4. Let p be a prime, and let d ‚â§ e be positive integers. Then there is
an extension Fpd ‚äÜ Fpe if and only if d | e. Further, if d | e, then there is exactly
one such extension, in the sense that Fpe contains a unique copy of Fp d .
All extensions F pd ‚äÜ Fpe are simple.
Proof. If there is an extension as stated, then Fp ‚äÜ Fp d ‚äÜ Fpe ; hence [Fpd : Fp ]
divides [Fpe : Fp ] by Corollary 1.11. This says precisely that d | e.
Conversely, assume that d | e. As

that is, of Fpd .
For the last statement, recall that the multiplicative group of nonzero elements

this group, then Œ± will generate Fpe over any subÔ¨Åeld; if d | e, this says Fpe = Fpd (Œ±),
so Fp d ‚äÜ Fpe is simple.

These results can be translated into rather precise information on the structure

Corollary 5.5. Let F be a Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åeld. Then for all integers n ‚â• 1 there exist
irreducible polynomials of degree n in F [x].
Proof. We know F = Fpd for some prime p and some d ‚â• 1. By Corollary 5.4 there
is an extension Fpd ‚äÜ Fpdn , generated by an element Œ±. Then [Fpdn : F pd ] = n,
and it follows that the minimal polynomial of Œ± over F = Fpd is an irreducible
polynomial of degree n in F [x] .

torizations, leading to an inductive algorithm to Ô¨Ånd all irreducible polynomials
in Fq [x]:

Proof. By Theorem 5.1, Fq n is the splitting Ô¨Åeld of xq n
 ‚àí x over Fp , and hence
over Fq = F .

if d | n, then there is an embedding of Fqd in Fq n . But then Œ± must be a root

The picture I am trying to convey is the following: the qn roots of xq n
 ‚àí x
clump into disjoint subsets, with each subset collecting the roots of each and every
irreducible polynomial of degree d | n in F [x].
Example 5.7. Let‚Äôs contemplate the case q = 2: F2 = Z/2Z.
‚Ä¢ n = 1: the polynomial x2 ‚àí x factors as the product of x and (x ‚àí 1) (which
we could write as (x + 1) just as well, since we are working over F2). These are all
the irreducible polynomials of degree 1 over F 2 .
‚Ä¢ n = 2: the polynomial x4 ‚àí x must factor as the product of all irreducible
polynomials of degree 1 and 2; in fact
x4 ‚àí x = x(x ‚àí 1)(x2 + x + 1),
and the conclusion is that there is exactly one irreducible polynomial of degree 2
over F2 , namely x2 + x + 1.

 
The picture drawn above for F64 means next to nothing, but it may help us focus
on one last element of information we are going to extract regarding Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åelds.
Note that the eÔ¨Äect of any automorphism of F64 must be to scramble elements in
each sector represented in the picture, without mixing elements in diÔ¨Äerent sectors
and without interchanging sectors. Indeed, every automorphism of an extension
sends roots of an irreducible polynomial to roots of the same polynomial.
Since extensions of Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åelds are simple extensions, our previous work allows
us to be much more precise. Restricting our attention to the extensions Fp ‚äÜ Fpd ,
for a prime p, we know these can be realized as simple extensions by an element
with minimal polynomial of degree d. This polynomial is necessarily separable
5. Field extensions, III
 445
(Fp is perfect), so Corollary 1.7 immediately gives us the size of the automorphism
group:
| AutFp (Fpd )| = d.
But what is this group?
Proposition 5.8. AutFp (Fpd ) is cyclic, generated by the Frobenius isomorphism.
Proof. Let œï be the Frobenius homomorphism Fpd ‚Üí Fpd : œï(x) = xp . The
Frobenius homomorphism is an isomorphism on a Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åeld (Corollary 4.18) and
restricts to the identity on Fp (Exercise 4.11), so œï ‚àà AutFp (Fpd ). Since we know a
priori the size of this group, all we have to show is that the order of œï is d.
Let e = |œï|. Then œïe = id; hence xpe
 = x for all x ‚àà Fpd . In other
words, the (nonzero) polynomial xpe
 ‚àí x has pd roots in Fpd ; this implies pd ‚â§ pe
(Lemma V.5.1). Equivalently, d ‚â§ e, yielding
| AutFp (Fpd )| ‚â§ |œï|.
But then these two numbers must be equal, since the order of an element always
divides the order of the group (Example II.8.15).

Two last comments on Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åelds are in order:
‚ÄîFor n large enough, the stupendously large (but Ô¨Ånite) Ô¨Åeld Fpn! works as an
approximation of the algebraic closure F p : indeed, this Ô¨Åeld contains roots of all
polynomials of degree ‚â§ n in Fp[x], by Corollary 5.6.
This observation can be turned into the explicit construction of an algebraic
closure of Fp: by Corollary 5.4 there are extensions
Fp ‚äÜ Fp2 ‚äÜ Fp6 ‚äÜ Fp4! ‚äÜ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑
and the union of this chain of Ô¨Åelds20 gives a copy of Fp .
‚ÄîFinite Ô¨Åelds form a category, which (by Corollary 5.4) should strongly remind
the reader of the ‚Äòtoy‚Äô category encountered in Exercise I.5.6. There is an impor-
tant diÔ¨Äerence, however: in that category every object has exactly one automor-
phism, while we have just checked that Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åelds may have many automorphisms.
Challenge: The reader has encountered a very similar situation in a diÔ¨Äerent (but
related) context. Where?
5.2. Cyclotomic polynomials and Ô¨Åelds. In the last several sections we have
often encountered roots of 1 in C; the extensions they generate are very important.

they form a cyclic subgroup of order n of the multiplicative group of C, which I
will denote Œºn .
Pictorially, the roots of 1 are placed at the vertices of a regular n-gon centered
at 0, with one vertex at 1.

The optimistic reader may now start guessing that the coeÔ¨Écients of Œ¶n (x)

The irreducibility of Œ¶n (x) is a bit trickier, in particular because separabil-

Proposition 5.14. For all positive n, Œ¶n (x) ‚àà Z[x] is irreducible over Q.
448
 VII. Fields
(Since Œ¶n (x) is monic, irreducibility in Q[x] is equivalent to irreducibility
in Z[x]; cf. Corollary V.4.17.)
Proof. Arguing by contradiction, assume Œ¶n (x) is reducible. Then its roots Œ∂n m ,
with (m, n) = 1, are divided among the factors; we can choose a root Œ∂n m of one
irreducible monic factor f (x), such that another root Œ∂n mp (for some prime p not
dividing n) is not a root of f (x). Write
Œ¶n (x) = f (x)g(x);
since Œ¶n (x) ‚àà Z[x] and Œ¶n(x), f (x) are monic, then f (x) and g(x) have integer
coeÔ¨Écients (cf. Exercise V.4.23). By our choice, f (x) is the minimal polynomial
of Œ∂n m over Q, and g(Œ∂ n mp ) = 0.
It follows that Œ∂n m is a root of g(xp ), and hence f (x) | g(xp ). Therefore we can
write
g(xp ) = f (x)h(x)
with h(x) ‚àà Z[x]. Reading the last equation modulo p, we get (again using Exer-
cise 4.8, and denoting cosets by underlining)
g(x)p = f (x)h(x) in F p [x];
in particular, f (x) and g(x) must have a nontrivial common factor (x) in Fp [x].
But then
(x)2 | f (x)g(x) :
the reduction of Œ¶n(x) modulo p must have a multiple factor.
This implies that xn ‚àí 1 ‚àà Fp [x] has a multiple factor; that is, it is inseparable.
However, its derivative nxn‚àí1 ‚àà Fp [x] is nonzero (because p does not divide n by
assumption), and Lemma 4.13 implies that xn ‚àí 1 is separable in Fp [x].
This contradiction shows that our assumption that Œ¶n(x) is reducible must be
nonsense, proving the statement.

this is evidently independent of the representative m. Then j is clearly injective,

The constructibility of the regular n-gon by straightedge and compass is of
course equivalent to the constructibility of the complex number Œ∂n . Since we now
know that [Q(Œ∂n ) : Q] = œÜ(n), Corollary 3.6 tells us that the regular n-gon is con-
structible by straightedge and compass only if œÜ(n) is a power of 2. This condition
can be chased a little further (Exercise 5.19). In any case, we shall return to this
theme one more time, after we absorb a little Galois theory.
5.3. Separability and simple extensions. All the examples of Ô¨Åeld extensions
we have encountered so far were simple extensions, although sometimes this may
not be completely apparent at Ô¨Årst (cf. Example 1.19). There is a good reason for
this: most of the examples we have seen were separable extensions, and we can now
prove that a Ô¨Ånite separable extension is necessarily simple.
Here is a nice criterion for simplicity (which does not involve any separability
condition):
Proposition 5.18. An algebraic extension k ‚äÜ F is simple if and only if the
number of distinct intermediate Ô¨Åelds k ‚äÜ E ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånite.
Proof. Assume F = k(Œ±) is simple and algebraic, and let qk (x) be the minimal
polynomial of Œ± over k. Embed F in an algebraic closure k of k. If E is an
intermediate Ô¨Åeld, then F = E(Œ±) is also a simple, algebraic extension; denote
by qE (x) the minimal polynomial of Œ± over E. Since qk (x) ‚àà E[x] for all E and
qk (Œ±) = 0, we know that each qE (x) is a factor of qk (x).
I claim that E is in fact determined by qE (x). Since qk (x) has Ô¨Ånitely many
factors in k, this proves that there are only Ô¨Ånitely many intermediate Ô¨Åelds, that
is, the ‚Äòonly if‚Äô part of the statement.
To verify my claim, I will show that E is generated (over k) by the coeÔ¨Écients
of qE (x). To see this, let E be the subÔ¨Åeld of E generated by k and the coeÔ¨Écients
of qE (x). Then qE (x) ‚àà E  [x], and since qE (x) is irreducible over E, it must be
irreducible over E  . Note that E  (Œ±) = F = E(Œ±). We have E  ‚äÜ E ‚äÜ F ; therefore
  deg(qE (x)) = [F : E ] = [F : E][E : E ] = deg(qE (x))[E : E ],
 from which [E : E ] = 1; that is, E = E as needed. This concludes the proof of
the ‚Äòonly if‚Äô part of the statement.
To prove the ‚Äòif‚Äô part, assume that there are only Ô¨Ånitely many intermediate
Ô¨Åelds k ‚äÜ E ‚äÜ F . The extension k ‚äÜ F must be Ô¨Ånitely generated (otherwise
450
 VII. Fields
we could construct an inÔ¨Ånite sequence of subextensions k ‚äÜ k(Œ±1 ) ‚äÜ k(Œ±1 , Œ±2) ‚äÜ
¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚äÜ F , and we would have inÔ¨Ånitely many intermediate Ô¨Åelds), hence Ô¨Ånite since
it is algebraic. If k is a Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åeld, then so is F , and the extension is automatically
simple by Corollary 5.4; thus we may assume that k is inÔ¨Ånite, and we have to show
that every Ô¨Ånitely generated algebraic extension F = k(Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±r ) is simple.
Arguing inductively, we may assume without loss of generality that F = k(Œ±, Œ≤).
For every c ‚àà k, we have the intermediate Ô¨Åeld

Proposition 5.19. Every Ô¨Ånite separable extension is simple.

By assumption the extension is separable, so [F : k]s = [F : k] by Proposition 4.24,
implying [k(Œ≥) : k] = [F : k] and Ô¨Ånally
F = k(Œ≥),
concluding the proof.
 
Proposition 5.19 is a good illustration of why separability is a technically desir-
able condition. To stress the point even further, note that we are now in a position
to extend the convenient inequality found in Corollary 1.7 to all Ô¨Ånite separable
extensions:
Corollary 5.20. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Ånite, separable extension. Then
| Autk (F )| ‚â§ [F : k],
with equality if and only if k ‚äÜ F is a normal extension.
Proof. Since k ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånite and separable, Proposition 5.19 implies it is simple:
F = k(Œ±) for some Œ± ‚àà F . The inequality follows immediately from Corollary 1.7,
and equality holds if and only if the minimal polynomial f (x) of Œ± factors into
distinct linear factors in F . In this case F is the splitting Ô¨Åeld of f (x), so it
is normal over k by Theorem 4.8. Conversely, if F is normal over k, then f (x)
splits completely in F and has distinct roots since Œ± is separable over k; therefore
| Autk (F )| = [F : k], again by Corollary 1.7.
 
Example 5.21. By Proposition 5.19, if we want to construct a nonsimple Ô¨Ånite
extension, we have to use inseparable elements; to produce an example, we jazz up
Example 4.11 a little. Consider the Ô¨Åeld of rational functions F = Fp (u, v) in two
variables over Fp and the subÔ¨Åeld k = Fp (s, t), where s = up and t = v p . Then
k ‚äÜ F is an algebraic extension; the minimal polynomial of u over k is xp ‚àí s; the
minimal polynomial of v over k(u) is y p ‚àí t; it follows that
[F : k] = p2.
As c ranges in k, we obtain intermediate Ô¨Åelds
k ‚äÜ k(cu + v) ‚äÜ F.
If any two choices c, c led to the same intermediate Ô¨Åeld, then we would deduce
that k(u, v) = k(cu+v) by arguing as in the proof of Proposition 5.18. In particular,
we would have
[k(cu + v) : k] = p2 .
But this is not the case, since (cu + v)p = cp s + t ‚àà k(s, t) = k; hence the minimal
polynomial of cu + v over k has degree at most p. Since k = Fp (s, t) is inÔ¨Ånite,
there are inÔ¨Ånitely many intermediate Ô¨Åelds, and it follows (by Proposition 5.18)
that F is not a simple extension of k.

5.16. If q is a prime that divides an ‚àí 1 and does not divide ad ‚àí 1 for any d < n,
then q is said to be a primitive prime divisor of an ‚àí 1. By Exercise 5.15, if q  n
and q | Œ¶n (a), then q is a primitive prime divisor of an ‚àí 1. The BirkhoÔ¨Ä-Vandiver
theorem asserts that an ‚àí 1 has primitive prime divisors for all but a very short
list of exceptions: n = 1, a = 2; n = 2, a + 1 a power of 2; and n = 6, a = 2.
Assuming this statement, we can give another proof of Wedderburn‚Äôs theorem
(published in 2003 by Nicolas Lichiardopol). Let R be a Ô¨Ånite division ring.
‚Ä¢ Prove that there is a prime p such that |R| = pn for some integer n.

a is in the center of R (Exercise III.2.9).
‚Ä¢ Prove that the center of R is R: this shows that R is commutative.
Thus, R is a Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åeld.

6. A little Galois theory
Galois theory is a beautiful interplay of Ô¨Åeld theory and group theory, originally
motivated by the problem of determining ‚Äòsymmetries‚Äô among roots of a polynomial.
Galois was interested in concrete relations which must necessarily hold among the
roots of a polynomial, the most trivial example of which being the fact that if Œ±, Œ≤
are the two roots of x2 + px + q, then Œ± + Œ≤ = ‚àíp and Œ±Œ≤ = q. Interchanging
the roots Œ±, Œ≤ has no eÔ¨Äect on the quantities Œ± + Œ≤, Œ±Œ≤. More generally, for a
higher degree polynomial there may be several quantities which are invariant under
certain permutations of the roots. These quantities and the corresponding groups of
permutations may be viewed as invariants determined by the polynomial, yielding
a sophisticated tool to study the polynomial.
6.1. The Galois correspondence and Galois extensions. In the language
of Ô¨Åeld theory, subsets of the roots of a polynomial f (x) determine intermediate
Ô¨Åelds of the splitting Ô¨Åeld of f (x), and groups of permutations of the roots give
automorphisms of these intermediate Ô¨Åelds. With this in mind, it is not surprising
that splitting Ô¨Åelds should come to the fore; the fact that one can characterize such
6. A little Galois theory
 455
Ô¨Åelds in terms of groups of automorphisms (Theorem 6.9 below) will be key to the
whole discussion.
Before wading into these waters, we should formalize the precise relation be-
tween groups of automorphisms of an extension and intermediate Ô¨Åelds.
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.1. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Åeld extension, and let G ‚äÜ Autk (F ) be a group
of automorphisms of the extension. The Ô¨Åxed Ô¨Åeld of G is the intermediate Ô¨Åeld
F G := {Œ± ‚àà F | ‚àÄg ‚àà G, gŒ± = Œ±}.
 
The fact that F G is indeed a subÔ¨Åeld of F containing k is immediate. The
notion of Ô¨Åxed Ô¨Åeld allows us to set up a correspondence
{intermediate Ô¨Åelds E: k ‚äÜ E ‚äÜ F } o
 / {subgroups of Autk
 (F )} ,
sending the intermediate Ô¨Åeld E to the subgroup AutE (F ) of Autk (F ) and the
subgroup G ‚äÜ Autk (F ) to the Ô¨Åxed Ô¨Åeld F G .
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.2. This is known as the Galois correspondence.
 

‚Ä¢ E ‚äÜ F AutE (F ) ;

In particular, the function associating with each intermediate Ô¨Åeld the correspond-

This example shows that the inclusion
 ‚àö

Proposition 6.5. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Ånite extension, and let G be a subgroup
of Autk (F ). Then |G| = [F : F G ], and
G = AutF G (F ).
In particular, the Galois correspondence (from intermediate Ô¨Åelds to automorphism
groups) is surjective for a Ô¨Ånite extension.
Proving this fact leads us to examining very carefully Ô¨Ånite extensions of the
type F G ‚äÜ F . Surprisingly, these turn out to satisfy just about every property we
have encountered so far:
Lemma 6.6. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Ånite extension, and let G be a subgroup of Autk (F ).
Then F G ‚äÜ F is a Ô¨Ånite, simple, normal, separable extension.
Remark 6.7. The key to this lemma is the following observation, which is worth
highlighting.
If Œ± ‚àà F and g ‚àà G, note that gŒ± must be a root of the minimal polynomial
of Œ± over k; there are only Ô¨Ånitely many roots, so the G-orbit of Œ± consists of Ô¨Ånitely
many elements Œ± = Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±n. The group G acts on the orbit by permuting its
elements; therefore, every element of G leaves the polynomial
qŒ± (t) = (t ‚àí Œ±1 ) ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ (t ‚àí Œ±n )
Ô¨Åxed. In other words, the coeÔ¨Écients of this polynomial must be in the Ô¨Åxed
Ô¨Åeld F G . Further, qŒ± (t) is separable since it has distinct roots. Finally, note
that deg qŒ± (t) ‚â§ |G|.
 
With this in mind, we are ready to prove the lemma.
Proof of Lemma 6.6. The extension F G ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånite because k ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånite.
Let Œ± ‚àà F ; by the remark following the statement of the lemma, Œ± is a root of
a separable polynomial qŒ± (t) with coeÔ¨Écients in F G . It follows that Œ± is separable
over F G ; hence the extension is separable according to DeÔ¨Ånition 4.12.
Since F G ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånite and separable, it is simple by Proposition 5.19; let Œ± be
a generator. The polynomial qŒ± (t) splits in F , and F is generated over F G by the
roots of qŒ±(t) (indeed, Œ±1 = Œ± suÔ¨Éces to generate F over F G ); therefore, F is a
splitting Ô¨Åeld for qŒ± (t) over F G according to DeÔ¨Ånition 4.1. Therefore F G ‚äÜ F is
normal by Theorem 4.8, and we are done.

Proposition 6.5 is a consequence of Lemma 6.6:

Proof of Proposition 6.5. By Lemma 6.3, G is a subgroup of AutF G (F ), and in
particular
|G| ‚â§ | AutF G (F )|.
In order to verify that G = AutF G , it suÔ¨Éces to prove the converse inequality. By
Lemma 6.6, F = F G(Œ±) for some Œ± ‚àà F ; therefore | AutF G (F )| equals the number
of distinct roots in F of the minimal polynomial of Œ± over F G (by Corollary 1.7).
With notation as in Remark 6.7, Œ± is a root of qŒ± (x) ‚àà F G [x]; hence the minimal
polynomial of Œ± is a factor of qŒ± (x). Since by construction the number of roots
of qŒ± (x) is ‚â§ |G|, we obtain
| AutF G (F )| ‚â§ |G|,
as needed.
To verify that [F : F G ] = |G|, just note that [F : F G ] = | AutF G (F )| by
Corollary 5.20, since F G ‚äÜ F is normal and separable by Lemma 6.6.

Remark 6.8. The hypothesis that the extension is Ô¨Ånite in Proposition 6.5 is
necessary: the Galois correspondence is not necessarily surjective in the inÔ¨Ånite
case. Not all is lost, though: one can give a suitable topology to the group of
automorphisms and limit the Galois correspondence to closed subgroups of the
automorphism group, recovering results such as Proposition 6.5. The reader is
welcome to explore this further‚Äîwe will not be able to take this more general
point of view here.
 
Theorem 6.9. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Ô¨Ånite Ô¨Åeld extension. Then the following are
equivalent:
(1) F is the splitting Ô¨Åeld of a separable polynomial f (t) ‚àà k[t] over k;
(2) k ‚äÜ F is normal and separable;
(3) | Autk (F )| = [F : k];
(4) k = F Autk (F ) is the Ô¨Åxed Ô¨Åeld of Autk (F );
(5) the Galois correspondence for k ‚äÜ F is a bijection;
(6) k ‚äÜ F is separable, and if F ‚äÜ K is an algebraic extension and œÉ ‚àà Autk (K),
then œÉ(F ) = F .
Proof. Most of the needed implications have been proven along the way.
(1) ‚áê‚áí (2) by Theorem 4.8; (2) =‚áí (3) by Corollary 5.20. (3) ‚áê‚áí (4) follows
from Proposition 6.5, applied to the extension F Autk (F ) ‚äÜ F : by Proposition 6.5,
we have
[F : F Autk (F ) ] = | Autk (F )|;
since k ‚äÜ F Aut k (F ) ‚äÜ F , it follows that k = F Autk (F ) if and only if | Autk (F )| =
[F : k]. (4) =‚áí (2) by Lemma 6.6.
(2) ‚áê‚áí (6) holds by Exercise 4.7, since Ô¨Ånite separable extensions are simple
(Proposition 5.19).
To prove (5) =‚áí (4), let E = F Autk (F ) ; by Proposition 6.5, AutE (F ) =
Autk (F ). Assuming that the Galois correspondence is bijective, it follows that
k = E = F Autk (F ) , giving (4).
458
 VII. Fields
Finally, we prove that (1) =‚áí (5). Since k ‚äÜ F is a Ô¨Ånite extension, we
already know that the Galois correspondence from intermediate Ô¨Åelds to subgroups
of Autk (F ) has a right inverse (Proposition 6.5), so it suÔ¨Éces to show it has a left-
inverse. Therefore, it suÔ¨Éces to verify that every intermediate Ô¨Åeld E equals the
Ô¨Åxed Ô¨Åeld of the corresponding subgroup AutE (F ). Then let E be an intermediate
Ô¨Åeld. By (1), F is the splitting Ô¨Åeld of a separable polynomial f (t) ‚àà k[t] ‚äÜ E[t];
therefore, condition (1) holds for the extension E ‚äÜ F . As we have already proved
that (1) ‚áê‚áí (4), this implies that E = F AutE (F ), and we are done.

DeÔ¨Ånition 6.10. A Ô¨Ånite extension k ‚äÜ F is Galois if it satisÔ¨Åes any of the
conditions listed in Theorem 6.9.
 
Warning: We will not study the Galois condition for inÔ¨Ånite extensions (cf. Re-
mark 6.8). Thus Galois extensions will implicitly be Ô¨Ånite in what follows.
Condition (6) is technically useful and helps with visualizing the Galois condi-
tion. If a Ô¨Ånite separable extension k ‚äÜ F is not Galois, then F can be embedded
in some larger extension k ‚äÜ K (for example, in the algebraic closure k ‚äÜ k) in
many possible ways:

If k ‚äÜ F is Galois, all these images must coincide:

Of course there are possibly still many ways21 to embed F in K, but they all have
the same image F .

DeÔ¨Ånition 6.11. If k ‚äÜ F is a Galois extension, the corresponding automorphism
group Autk (F ) is called the Galois group of the extension.

 3To reiterate, the extension Q ‚äÜ
 Q( 2) is not a Galois extension: as we have

 3observed in Example 6.4, AutQ (Q( 2)) is trivial (hence, for example, condition (3)
of Theorem 6.9 does not hold).
On the other hand, we have already run into several examples of Galois exten-
sions: the examples of splitting Ô¨Åelds we have seen in ¬ß4.1 are all Galois extensions.
The ‚ÄòGalois Ô¨Åelds‚Äô of ¬ß5.1 are (surprise, surprise) Galois extensions of their prime
subÔ¨Åelds, by Theorem 5.1. The cyclotomic Ô¨Åelds (¬ß5.2) are Galois extensions of Q.
A Galois extension k ‚äÜ F is cyclic, abelian, etc., if the corresponding group
of automorphisms Autk (F ) is cyclic, abelian, etc. For example, Q ‚äÜ Q(Œ∂8) is an
abelian, but not cyclic, Galois extension (cf. Proposition 5.16 and Example 5.17).
6.2. The fundamental theorem of Galois theory, I. The fundamental theo-
rem of Galois theory amounts to a more complete description of the Galois corre-
spondence for Galois extensions.
At this stage, this is what we know:
‚Ä¢ If k ‚äÜ F is a (Ô¨Ånite) Galois extension, then there is an inclusion-reversing
bijection
/{intermediate Ô¨Åelds E: k ‚äÜ E ‚äÜ F } o
 {subgroups of Autk
 (F )} :
through this bijection, the intermediate Ô¨Åeld E corresponds to the subgroup
AutE (F ) of Autk (F ), and the subgroup G ‚äÜ Autk (F ) corresponds to the Ô¨Åxed
Ô¨Åeld F G .
‚Ä¢ For every intermediate Ô¨Åeld E,
[F : E] = | Aut E (F )|.
The extension E ‚äÜ F is then also a Galois extension (Exercise 6.3), and
[E : k] = [Autk (F ) : AutE (F )] :
this follows from Lagrange‚Äôs theorem (Corollary II.8.14) and its Ô¨Åeld theory
counterpart (Proposition 1.10).
‚àö

6.3. The fundamental theorem of Galois theory, II. Another popular no-

Example 6.4 shows that the extension k ‚äÜ E need not be a Galois extension:
‚àö 3Q( 2) is an intermediate Ô¨Åeld for the extension of Q into the splitting Ô¨Åeld for the
polynomial t3 ‚àí 2 (which is Galois, by part (1) of Theorem 6.9), but as we have
seen, it is not Galois.
 ‚àö
 3 The splitting Ô¨Åeld has degree 6 over Q and is a quadratic
extension of Q( 2). This is in fact the typical situation: every Ô¨Ånite separable
extension may be enlarged to a Galois extension (Exercise 6.4).
In any case, the question of whether the extension of the base Ô¨Åeld k in an
intermediate Ô¨Åeld E of a Galois extension k ‚äÜ F is Galois is central to the theory.
The outcome of the discussion will be very neat, and this is possibly the most
striking part of the fundamental theorem of Galois theory:
Theorem 6.15. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Galois extension, and let E be an intermediate
Ô¨Åeld. Then k ‚äÜ E is Galois if and only if AutE (F ) is normal in Autk (F ); in this
case, there is an isomorphism
Autk (E) ‚àº
 =
 Autk (F )
 .
AutE (F )
462
 VII. Fields
‚àö
 3The ‚Äòproblem‚Äô with Q ‚äÜ Q( 2) is that the automorphism group of the splitting
Ô¨Åeld of t3 ‚àí 2 is S3 (as we will
 soon be able to verify: cf. Example 7.19). The
‚àö 3subgroup corresponding to Q( 2) has index 3; hence (as we know, since we are
well acquainted with S3 ) it is not normal.
The fact that the Galois group of k ‚äÜ E will turn out to be isomorphic to the
quotient Autk (F )/ AutE (F ) in the Galois case should not be too surprising, since
we know already that in any case [E : k] equals the index of AutE (F ) in Autk (F ),
and the index equals the order of the quotient if the subgroup is normal. In general,
[E : k] equals the number of cosets of AutE (F ) in Autk (F ), and it is worth shooting
for a concrete interpretation of these cosets, even when k ‚äÜ E does not turn out to
be a Galois extension.
We already know that the number of left-cosets of AutE (F ) equals the index
of AutE (F ) in Autk (F ), hence [E : k], hence [E : k]s (because k ‚äÜ E is separable,
as a subextension of a separable extension), and hence there must exist a bijection
between the set of left-cosets g AutE (F ) as g ranges in Autk (F ) and the set of
diÔ¨Äerent embeddings Œπ : E ‚Üí k in an algebraic closure of k, extending idk . Not
surprisingly, there is a reason for this‚Äîthat is, a ‚Äòmeaningful‚Äô bijection between
these two sets.
This is an excellent moment to review the material on group actions we covered
in ¬ßII.9, especially ¬ßII.9.3. Recall in particular that we were able to classify all
transitive actions of a group G on a set S (Proposition II.9.9): these G-sets are all
isomorphic to the set of left-cosets of a subgroup H of G under the natural action
of G; and H may be taken to be the stabilizer of any element of S.
We are going to deÔ¨Åne a natural action of Autk (F ) on the set I of embeddings
of E in k extending the identity, and prove that, as an Autk (F )-set, I is isomorphic
to the set of left-cosets of AutE (F ) in Autk (F ). This will be our ‚Äòmeaningful
bijection‚Äô, and after this is done Theorem 6.15 will look perfectly reasonable.
Fix an embedding F ‚äÜ k of F in an algebraic closure of k, and let I be the
set of embeddings Œπ : E ‚Üí k extending the identity on k. It is a good idea to
view k ‚äÜ E as an abstract extension, rather than identifying E with a speciÔ¨Åc
intermediate Ô¨Åeld in F ; the fact that we can view E as an intermediate Ô¨Åeld of the
extension k ‚äÜ F simply means that Œπ(E) ‚äÜ F for some Œπ ‚àà I.
‚Ä¢ For all Œπ ‚àà I, Œπ(E) ‚äÜ F .
Indeed, k ‚äÜ E is simple (Ô¨Ånite and separable =‚áí simple, Proposition 5.19). If
E = k(Œ±), then Œπ is determined by Œπ(Œ±), which is necessarily a root of the minimal
polynomial of Œ± over k. But k ‚äÜ F is normal and contains some root Œπ(Œ±) of this
polynomial; hence F contains all of them (DeÔ¨Ånition 4.7). That is, Œπ(Œ±) ‚àà F for all
Œπ ‚àà I, implying Œπ(k(Œ±)) ‚äÜ F for all Œπ ‚àà I.
‚Ä¢ Let Œπ ‚àà I, and let g ‚àà Autk (F ); then g ‚ó¶ Œπ ‚àà I. Therefore, Autk (F ) acts on I.
Here g ‚ó¶ Œπ is interpreted in the following way: Œπ(E) ‚äÜ F , as we have seen; hence
g restricts to a homomorphism Œπ(E) ‚Üí F , and g ‚ó¶ Œπ is the composition of this
homomorphism with Œπ.
‚Ä¢ The action of Autk (F ) on I is transitive.
6. A little Galois theory
 463
This follows from the ‚Äòuniqueness of splitting Ô¨Åelds‚Äô, Lemma 4.2. Indeed, let Œπ1 , Œπ2 ‚àà
I. Both Œπ1 (E), Œπ2 (E) contain k, and F is a splitting Ô¨Åeld over k (part (1) of
Theorem 6.9); hence F is a splitting Ô¨Åeld (for the same polynomial) over both Œπ1 (E)
and Œπ2(E). By Lemma 4.2, there exists an automorphism g : F ‚Üí F extending the
isomorphism
Œπ2 ‚ó¶ Œπ‚àí1
 1 : Œπ1 (E) ‚Üí Œπ2 (E).
Then g ‚àà Autk (F ) (since Œπ2 ‚ó¶ Œπ1 ‚àí1
 restricts to the identity on k), and the fact that
g restricts to Œπ2 ‚ó¶ Œπ‚àí1

Now choose one Œπ ‚àà I, and use it to identify E with an intermediate Ô¨Åeld
of k ‚äÜ F . Then AutE (F ) = AutŒπ(E)(F ) is the subgroup of Autk (F ) consisting of
those elements g which restrict to the identity on E = Œπ(E), that is, the stabilizer
of Œπ. At this point, Proposition II.9.9 is all that is needed to conclude the promised
result:
‚Ä¢ As an Autk (F )-set, I is isomorphic to the set of left-cosets of AutE (F ) in
Autk (F ).
I view this observation as an integral part of the fundamental theorem of Galois
theory. The conventional statement (Theorem 6.15) is an essentially immediate
consequence of this fact.
Proof of Theorem 6.15. As observed above, the Autk (F )-stabilizer of Œπ ‚àà I
equals AutŒπ(E) (F ). By Proposition II.9.12, stabilizers of diÔ¨Äerent Œπ are conjugates
of each other; if AutE (F ) is normal, it follows that for all Œπ ‚àà I
AutŒπ(E) (F ) = AutE (F ).
This implies that Œπ(E) = E for all Œπ ‚àà I, since the Galois correspondence is bijective
for the Galois extension k ‚äÜ F . It follows that k ‚äÜ E satisÔ¨Åes condition (6) of
Theorem 6.9; hence it is Galois.
Conversely, assume that k ‚äÜ E is Galois; by the same condition (6), Œπ(E) = E
for all Œπ ‚àà I. Restriction to E = Œπ(E) then deÔ¨Ånes a homomorphism
œÅ : Autk (F )
 / Aut k (E).
This homomorphism is surjective (because the Autk (F )-action on I is transitive),
and its kernel consists of those g ‚àà Autk (F ) which restrict to the identity on E,
that is, precisely of AutE (F ). This shows that AutE (F ) is normal and establishes
the stated isomorphism by virtue of the ‚ÄòÔ¨Årst isomorphism theorem‚Äô for groups,
Corollary II.8.2.
 
Remark 6.16. There is a parallel between Galois theory and the theory of covering
spaces in topology. In this analogy, Galois extensions correspond to regular covers;
the Galois group of an extension corresponds to the group of deck transformations;
and Theorem 6.15 corresponds to the fact that the quotient of a regular cover by a
normal subgroup of the group of deck transformations is again a regular cover.
More general (connected) covers correspond to more general algebraic exten-
sions. A space is simply connected if and only if it admits no nontrivial connected
covers, so this notion corresponds in Ô¨Åeld theory to the condition that a Ô¨Åeld K
admits no nontrivial algebraic extensions, that is, that K is algebraically closed.
464
 VII. Fields
Viewing the fundamental group of a space as the group of deck transformations
of its fundamental cover suggests that we should think of the Galois group of the
algebraic closure23 k ‚äÜ k as ‚Äòthe fundamental group‚Äô of a Ô¨Åeld k.
In algebraic geometry this analogy is carried out to its natural consequences.
A covering map of algebraic varieties X ‚Üí Y determines a Ô¨Åeld extension K(Y ) ‚äÜ
K(X), where K(X), K(Y ) are the ‚ÄòÔ¨Åelds of rational functions‚Äô (in the aÔ¨Éne case,
these are just the Ô¨Åelds of fractions of the corresponding coordinate rings; cf. Ex-
ercise 2.16). One can then use Galois theory to transfer to the algebro-geometric
environment notions such as the fundamental group, without appealing to topolog-
ical notions (such as ‚Äòcontinuous maps from S 1 ‚Äô), which would be problematic in,
e.g., positive characteristic.
 
6.4. Further remarks and examples. Suppose k ‚äÜ F and k ‚äÜ K are two Ô¨Ånite
extensions contained in a larger extension of k (for example, we could choose speciÔ¨Åc
embeddings F ‚äÜ k, K ‚äÜ k in an algebraic closure of k). Then we can consider the
composite KF (cf. Lemma 6.3) of F , K in the larger extension. It is natural to
question how the Galois condition behaves under this operation.
Proposition 6.17. Suppose k ‚äÜ F is a Galois extension and k ‚äÜ K is any Ô¨Ånite
extension. Then K ‚äÜ KF is a Galois extension, and AutK (KF ) ‚àº
 = AutF ‚à©K (F ).
Pictorially,
