Proof. As k ‚äÜ F is Galois, it is the splitting Ô¨Åeld of a separable polynomial
f (x) ‚àà k[x] ‚äÜ K[x]. The roots of f (x) generate F over k, so they generate KF
over K; in other words, KF is the splitting Ô¨Åeld of f (x) over K, so K ‚äÜ KF is
Galois.
If œÉ : KF ‚Üí KF is an automorphism extending the identity on K (and hence
on k), then œÉ restricts to a homomorphism F ‚Üí œÉ(F ) extending the identity on k.
Since k ‚äÜ F is Galois, œÉ(F ) = F ((6) in Theorem 6.9). Thus, ‚Äòrestriction‚Äô deÔ¨Ånes
a natural group homomorphism
œÅ : AutK (KF ) ‚àí‚Üí Aut k (F ).
I claim that œÅ is injective. Indeed, by deÔ¨Ånition every œÉ ‚àà AutK (KF ) is the identity
on K; if œÉ restricts to the identity on F , then œÉ is the identity on the composite KF ;
that is, œÉ = id in AutK (KF ).
Therefore, AutK (KF ) may be identiÔ¨Åed with a subgroup G of Autk (F ). Since
every œÉ ‚àà AutK (KF ) Ô¨Åxes K, the restriction œÅ(œÉ) Ô¨Åxes F ‚à©K; that is, F G ‚äá F ‚à©K.

Conversely, suppose Œ± ‚àà F G ; then K(Œ±) is in the Ô¨Åxed Ô¨Åeld of AutK (KF ), which

I will leave to the reader the pleasure of verifying that if both k ‚äÜ F and k ‚äÜ K
are Galois, then KF is also Galois over k, and so is F ‚à© K (Exercise 6.13).
Example 6.18. We have studied cyclotomic Ô¨Åelds Q(Œ∂n) as extensions of Q; Q(Œ∂n)
is the splitting Ô¨Åeld of xn ‚àí 1, so these extensions are Galois; we have proved
(Proposition 5.16) that AutQ (Q(Œ∂n)) is isomorphic to the group of units in Z/nZ.
Now let k be any Ô¨Åeld of characteristic zero. The splitting Ô¨Åeld of xn ‚àí 1 over k
is the composite k(Œ∂) of k and Q(Œ∂). By Proposition 6.17 the extension k ‚äÜ k(Œ∂) is
Galois, and Autk (k(Œ∂)) is isomorphic to a subgroup of the group of units of Z/nZ.
In particular, it is abelian.
 
The previous example gives an important class of abelian Galois extensions.
Remarkably, we can classify all cyclic Galois extensions, if we impose some restric-
tion on the base Ô¨Åeld k.
Proposition 6.19. Let k ‚äÜ F be an extension of degree m. Assume that k contains
a primitive m-th root of 1 and char k does not divide24 m. Then k ‚äÜ F is Galois
and cyclic if and only if F = k(Œ¥), with Œ¥m ‚àà k.
That is, under the given hypotheses m ‚àö
 on k, the cyclic Galois extensions of k
are precisely those of the form k ‚äÜ k( c), with c ‚àà k. This is part of Kummer
theory, a basic tool in the study of abelian extensions. Proposition 6.19 will be a
key ingredient in our application of Galois theory to the solvability of polynomial
equations.
Proof. Let Œ∂ ‚àà k be a primitive m-th root of 1.
First assume that F = k(Œ¥), with Œ¥m = c ‚àà k.polynomial xm ‚àí c,

part (1)) k ‚äÜ F is Galois.
To see that Autk (F ) is cyclic, note that every œï ‚àà Autk (F ) is determined
by œï(Œ¥), which is necessarily a root of xm ‚àí c; therefore œï(Œ¥) = Œ∂ i Œ¥ for some

with Z/mZ, as is immediately veriÔ¨Åed.
Conversely, assume that k ‚äÜ F is Galois and Autk (F ) is cyclic, generated by œï.

This is Hilbert‚Äôs theorem 90 (the 90-th theorem in Hilbert‚Äôs Zahlbericht, a report

7. Short march through applications of Galois theory
Galois theory is a pervasive tool, with wide-ranging applications. I have neither
the competence nor room in this book for an adequate survey of applications of the
theory, but even the few examples reviewed in this section should suÔ¨Éce to convey
its considerable power.
7.1. Fundamental theorem of algebra. Theorem 6.15 and a little group theory
are essentially all that is needed26 to give an algebraic proof of the fundamental
theorem of algebra, promised back in ¬ßV.5.3.
Theorem 7.1. C is algebraically closed.
Proof. Let f (x) ‚àà C[x] be a nonconstant polynomial; we have to prove that f (x)
has roots in C. Note that if f (x) has no roots in C, then neither does f (x)f (x) ‚àà
R[x]; that is, we may assume that f (x) has real coeÔ¨Écients. Let F be a splitting
Ô¨Åeld for f (x) over R; embed F in an algebraic closure of R (we ‚Äòdon‚Äôt know yet‚Äô
that C is algebraically closed!), and consider the extension
R ‚äÜ F (i).
This extension is Galois: it is the splitting Ô¨Åeld of the square-free part27 of f (x)(x2 +
1). Let G = AutR(F (i)).
26 I am cheating a little, actually, since the intermediate value theorem will also be used behind
the scenes. This is not too surprising: the completeness of R must enter the proof somewhere.
27 That is, take each irreducible factor of the polynomial with a power of exactly 1. Over a
perfect Ô¨Åeld, square-free polynomials are clearly separable.
7. Short march through applications of Galois theory
 469
By Sylow I (Theorem IV.2.5), G has a 2-Sylow subgroup H. The index [G : H]
is odd, so it corresponds to a Ô¨Ånite, separable extension R ‚äÜ E with [E : R] odd.
However, every polynomial of odd degree over R has a real root (Exercise V.5.17,
which only uses elementary calculus), and it follows that R ‚äÜ E is trivial (Exer-
cise 5.23).
Therefore [G : H] = 1, proving that G is a 2-group. Since C = R(i) ‚äÜ F (i),
the Galois group of the (Galois) extension
C ‚äÜ F (i)
is a subgroup of G; therefore it is a 2-group: | AutC (F (i))| = 2n for some n ‚â• 0.
Now recall (Proposition IV.2.6) that every group of order pn , with p prime,
contains subgroups of order pm for all 0 ‚â§ m ‚â§ n. In particular, if n ‚â• 1, then
AutC (F (i)) contains a subgroup of order 2n‚àí1, which corresponds to a quadratic
extension of C via the Galois correspondence. However, there are no quadratic
extensions of C, because there are no irreducible quadratic polynomials in C[x]
(Exercise 5.23 again).
This contradiction shows that n = 0; that is, AutC (F (i)) is necessarily trivial.
This proves that F (i) = C and in particular that C contains the roots of f (x). 
The argument can be simpliÔ¨Åed further, bypassing the use of Sylow‚Äôs theorem
at the price of a slight increase in length. But why should we attempt to bypass
Sylow‚Äôs theorem, after the eÔ¨Äort put into proving it in Chapter IV?
7.2. Constructibility of regular n-gons. We return one last time to the issue
of constructing regular n-gons. Simpler considerations have given us constraints
on n that must necessarily be fulÔ¨Ålled for the regular n-gon to be constructible: if
n = p is prime, we have found that p ‚àí 1 must be a power of 2 (¬ß3.3); this was
upgraded for any n to the condition that œÜ(n) is a power of 2, in ¬ß5.2 (and an
even more explicit condition is given in Exercise 5.19). But these are all ‚Äònegative‚Äô
results: we have not as yet proved that if œÜ(n) is a power of 2, then the regular
n-gon is constructible. This is where Galois theory comes into the picture.
Proposition 7.2. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Galois extension, and assume [F : k] = pr for
some prime p and r ‚â• 0. Then there exist intermediate Ô¨Åelds
k = E0 ‚äÜ E 1 ‚äÜ E2 ‚äÜ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚äÜ Er = F
such that [Ei : Ei‚àí1 ] = p for i = 1, . . . , r.
Proof. As the Galois correspondence is bijective for Galois extensions (Theo-
rem 6.9, part (5)), this statement follows immediately from the fact that a group
of order pr , with p prime, has a complete series of p-subgroups; cf. for example the
discussion following the statement of Theorem IV.2.8.
 
Theorem 7.3. The regular n-gon is constructible by straightedge and compass if
and only if œÜ(n) is a power of 2.
Proof. As recalled above, we have already established the =‚áí direction.

7.3. Fundamental theorem on symmetric functions. Let t1 , . . . , tn be inde-
terminates. The polynomial
Pn(x) := (x ‚àí t1 ) ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ (x ‚àí tn ) ‚àà Z[t1 , . . . , tn ][x]
is universal in the sense that every polynomial of degree n with coeÔ¨Écients in (say)
an integral domain may be obtained from Pn (x) by suitably specifying t1 , . . . , tn ,
possibly in a larger ring (Exercise 7.2). The coeÔ¨Écients of the expansion
P n(x) = xn ‚àí s1 (t1, . . . , tn)xn‚àí1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + (‚àí1)nsn (t1, . . . , tn )
472
 VII. Fields
are (up to sign) the elementary symmetric functions of t1 , . . . , tn . For example, for
n = 3,
s1(t1 , t2, t3 ) = t1 + t2 + t3 ,
s2(t1 , t2, t3 ) = t1 t2 + t1 t3 + t2t3 ,
s3(t1 , t2, t3 ) = t1 t2 t3.
The functions si (t1, . . . , tn) are symmetric in the sense that they are invariant
under permutations of t1 , . . . , tn (because Pn(x) is invariant); they are elementary
by virtue of the following important result, which has a particularly simple proof
thanks to Galois theory.
Theorem 7.4 (Fundamental theorem on symmetric functions). Let K be a Ô¨Åeld,
and let œï ‚àà K(t1 , . . . , tn ). Then œï is symmetric if and only if it is a rational
function (with coeÔ¨Écients in K) of the elementary symmetric functions s1 , . . . , sn .
Here we are viewing the si ‚Äôs as elements of K[t1 , . . . , tn ], which we can do
unambiguously since Z is initial in Ring.
Proof. Let F = K(t1, . . . , tn ), and let k = K(s1, . . . , sn ) be the subÔ¨Åeld generated
by the elementary symmetric functions over K.
Then F is a splitting Ô¨Åeld of the separable polynomial Pn (x) over k. In partic-
ular k ‚äÜ F is a Galois extension, and
| Autk (F )| = [F : k] ‚â§ n!
by Lemma 4.2. The symmetric group Sn acts (faithfully) on F by permuting
t1 , . . . , tn , and this action is the identity on k = K(s1, . . . , sn ) since each si is
symmetric. Thus Sn may be identiÔ¨Åed with a subgroup of Autk (F ); but |Sn | = n!,
so it follows that Autk (F ) = Sn.
Since k ‚äÜ F is Galois, we obtain k = F Sn . This says precisely that if œï ‚àà
K(t1, . . . , tn), then œï is invariant under the Sn action on t1, . . . , tn if and only if
œï ‚àà k = K(s1, . . . , sn ), which is the statement.
 
It is not diÔ¨Écult to strengthen the result of Theorem 7.4 and prove that every
symmetric polynomial is in fact a polynomial in the elementary symmetric functions.
Note that the argument given in the proof amounts to the following result,
which should be recorded for individual attention:
Lemma 7.5. Let K be a Ô¨Åeld, t1 , . . . , tn indeterminates, and let s1, . . . , sn be the
elementary symmetric functions on t1 , . . . , tn . Then the extension
K(s1 , . . . , sn) ‚äÜ K(t1 , . . . , tn )
is Galois, with Galois group Sn .
This result and Cayley‚Äôs theorem (Theorem II.9.5) immediately yield the fol-
lowing:
Corollary 7.6. Let G be a Ô¨Ånite group. Then there exists a Galois extension k ‚äÜ F
such that Autk (F ) ‚àº
 = G.

7.4. Solvability of polynomial equations by radicals. This is possibly the
most famous elementary application of Galois theory and one that is very close to
Galois‚Äô own original motivation.
Quadratic polynomial equations
x2 + bx + c = 0
are famously solved (in29 characteristic = 2) by the quadratic formula:
‚àö
‚àíb ¬± b2 ‚àí 4ac
x =
 .
2
Analogous, but more complicated, formulas exist for equations of degree 3 and 4
(Tartaglia/Cardano/Ferrari, around 1540; this generated a priority dispute which
degenerated into base personal attacks). Equations of degree 5 and higher resisted
the best eÔ¨Äorts of mathematicians for centuries. The most ambitious goal, in line
with what occurs for degrees 2, 3, and 4, would be to produce a formula for the
solutions to the ‚Äògeneral‚Äô polynomial equation
xn + an‚àí1xn‚àí2 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + a0 = 0
in terms of the coeÔ¨Écients ai and basic operations such as taking roots. This would
be a solution ‚Äòby radicals‚Äô of the equation.
Well, such a formula simply does not exist:
Theorem 7.8. The general polynomial equation of degree 5 or higher admits no
solution by radicals.
I am being intentionally vague about the ground Ô¨Åeld in which the problem
is posed; a ‚Äòformula‚Äô such as the quadratic formula ought to hold over any Ô¨Åeld
in which it makes sense; for example, the Ô¨Åeld should not have characteristic 2
in the case of the quadratic formula. An easy way to avoid any such snag is
to work in characteristic zero: this has the main technical advantage of ensuring
automatic separability of polynomials xm ‚àí 1, whose roots deÔ¨Åne the ‚Äòradicals‚Äô;
cf. Proposition 6.19. Therefore, we will assume in what follows that the ground
Ô¨Åeld has characteristic 0.
The ‚Äògeneral polynomial‚Äô is the ‚Äòuniversal‚Äô polynomial Pn (x) introduced in ¬ß7.3,
taken over a chosen ground Ô¨Åeld K: the general polynomial
xn ‚àí s1 xn‚àí1 + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + (‚àí1)n sn ‚àà k[x],
with k = K(s1, . . . , sn ) and si elementary symmetric functions in (‚Äòindeterminate‚Äô)
roots t1 , . . . , tn . It is in fact no harder to deal with the more general case of arbitrary
irreducible polynomials f (x) ‚àà k[x], over any Ô¨Åeld k (of characteristic zero); this is
what we will do.
A formula by radicals expresses a root ti of f (x) as an element of an extension
obtained from k in the following fashion:
k ‚äÜ k(Œ¥1 ) ‚äÜ k(Œ¥1, Œ¥2 ) ‚äÜ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚äÜ k(Œ¥1 , . . . , Œ¥r )
where for all i = 1, . . . , r we have Œ¥i mi ‚àà k(Œ¥1 , . . . , Œ¥i‚àí1) for some exponent mi .
29 In characteristic 2, one can give a formula in terms of ‚Äò2-roots‚Äô, which are roots of the
Artin-Schreier equation, and avoid the problematic division by 2; see Exercise 7.16.
7. Short march through applications of Galois theory
 475
DeÔ¨Ånition 7.9. Extensions of this type are called radical extensions.
 
Some facts about radical extensions are immediate from the deÔ¨Ånition: for ex-
ample, if k ‚äÜ F and k ‚äÜ K are two radical extensions and F , K are subÔ¨Åelds
of a larger Ô¨Åeld, then k ‚äÜ F K is radical (Exercise 7.5). Further, radical exten-
sions are composite of cyclic ones provided the appropriate roots of 1 are in k, by
Proposition 6.19. Also,
Lemma 7.10. Every separable radical extension is contained in a Galois radical
extension.
Proof. Let k ‚äÜ F be a separable radical extension. In particular k ‚äÜ F is Ô¨Ånite
and separable, so F = k(Œ±) for some Œ± ‚àà F (Proposition 5.19). Let p(x) be the
minimal polynomial of Œ± over k. The splitting Ô¨Åeld L of p(x) over k may be obtained
as the composite of œÉ(F ) as œÉ ranges over the embeddings of F in k, extending idk :
indeed, this composite is generated over k by all œÉ(Œ±), which range over all roots
of p(x). As a composite of radical extensions, L is radical over k. As a splitting
Ô¨Åeld of a separable polynomial, L is Galois over k (Theorem 6.9, part (1)), and we
are done.
 
Galois theory will allow us to relate solvability by radicals of a polynomial f (x)
with a precise statement on the splitting Ô¨Åeld of f (x). Here is a Ô¨Årst formalization
of this connection:
Lemma 7.11. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld of characteristic 0, and let f (x) ‚àà k[x] be an irre-
ducible polynomial. Then there exists a formula solving f (x) by radicals if and only
if the splitting Ô¨Åeld of f (x) is contained in a Galois radical extension.
Proof. If the splitting Ô¨Åeld of f (x) is contained in a radical extension (Galois or
not), then the roots may be written as combinations of Ô¨Åeld operations and radicals,
as needed.
For the converse, assume f (x) is solvable by radicals. A formula for a root t1
can be turned into a formula for any other root ti by applying an element œÉi of
(for example) Autk (k) sending t1 to ti (such an automorphism exists since f (x) is
irreducible). Thus, if a formula exists for one root, then a formula exists for all
roots. The composite of all the corresponding radical extensions gives one, possibly
larger, radical extension of k containing all roots of f (x). Therefore, the splitting
Ô¨Åeld of f (x) is contained in a radical extension of k. By Lemma 7.10, this extension
may be assumed to be Galois, as needed.
 
In a nutshell, we will understand solvability of polynomial equations by radicals
if we understand radical Galois extensions. As demanded by the general philosophy
underlying Galois theory, we should aim to characterize these extensions in terms
of their Galois groups. It turns out that the right condition (modulo technical
considerations) is that the Galois group should be solvable.
DeÔ¨Ånition 7.12. A Galois extension k ‚äÜ F is solvable if Autk (F ) is a solvable
group.
 
476
 VII. Fields
The reader may want to revisit ¬ßIV.3.3 now, for a reminder on solvability in
group theory. (We are approaching an explanation for the ‚Äòsolvable‚Äô terminology!)
Just as radical extensions decompose as sequence of cyclic extensions, solvable
groups have cyclic composition factors (Proposition IV.3.11). I will say30 that
a radical, resp., solvable, extension k ‚äÜ F ‚Äòhas enough roots of 1‚Äô if k contains a
primitive M -th root of 1 for a common multiple M of the order of the corresponding
cyclic factors.
Lemma 7.13. Let k ‚äÜ F be a Galois extension, with char k = 0. Provided it has
enough roots of 1, k ‚äÜ F is radical if and only if it is solvable.
Proof. Modulo the fundamental theorem of Galois theory, this is a straightforward
generalization of Proposition 6.19.
Indeed, assume that k ‚äÜ F is radical:
k ‚äÜ k(Œ¥1 ) ‚äÜ ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚äÜ k(Œ¥1 , . . . , Œ¥r ) = F
with Œ¥i mi ‚àà k(Œ¥1, . . . , Œ¥i‚àí1 ). By hypothesis, k contains primitive mi-th roots of 1 for
all i. Consider the corresponding series of subgroups of G = Autk (F (Œ∂)):
G = G0 ‚äá G 1 ‚äá ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚äá Gr = {e},
with Gi = Autk(Œ¥ 1 ,...,Œ¥i ) (F ). Note that F is Galois over each intermediate Ô¨Åeld.
Each extension
k(Œ¥1 , . . . , Œ¥i‚àí1 ) ‚äÜ k(Œ¥1 , . . . , Œ¥i )
satisÔ¨Åes the hypothesis of Proposition 6.19, and it follows that it is Galois and
cyclic. By the fundamental theorem (Theorem 6.15) each Gi is normal in the
previous Gi‚àí1 , with cyclic quotient, and it follows that G is solvable (Proposi-
tion IV.3.11 (ii)).
The same argument in reverse, using the converse implication in Proposi-
tion 6.19, proves that solvable extensions with enough roots of 1 are radical.
 
How do we account for the possible absence of the needed roots of 1? Surpris-
ingly, this aÔ¨Äects the statement in a rather minor way.
Proposition 7.14. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld of characteristic 0, and let k ‚äÜ F be a Galois
extension. Then k ‚äÜ F is solvable if and only if it is contained in a Galois radical
extension.
The characteristic 0 in Proposition 7.14 is an overshoot; as in Proposition 6.19,
it would suÔ¨Éce to require that char k does not divide the relevant degrees.
Proof. Assume Autk (F ) is solvable. Let M be a common multiple of the order
of the cyclic quotients, and let Œ∂ be a primitive M -th root of 1 in an algebraic
closure k of k. The splitting Ô¨Åeld k(Œ∂) of xM ‚àí 1 over k is Galois over k (xM ‚àí 1 is
separable since k has characteristic 0). By Proposition 6.17, the extension k(Œ∂) ‚äÜ
F (Œ∂) is also Galois, with Galois group isomorphic to Autk(Œ∂)‚à©F (F ). It follows that
Autk(Œ∂)(F (Œ∂)) is solvable, since Autk(Œ∂)‚à©F (F ) is a subgroup of Autk F and the latter
is solvable by assumption. (See the comment following Corollary IV.3.13.)
30Warning! This is nonstandard terminology.
7. Short march through applications of Galois theory
 477
Since k(Œ∂) ‚äÜ F (Œ∂) has enough roots of 1, Lemma 7.13 implies that it is rad-
ical. So is k ‚äÜ F (Œ∂), since k ‚äÜ k(Œ∂) is itself trivially radical. This proves that
k ‚äÜ F is contained in a radical extension, hence in a Galois radical extension by
Lemma 7.10.
Conversely, assume that k ‚äÜ F is Galois and contained in a radical Galois
extension k ‚äÜ L. Let M be a common multiple of the order of cyclic factors in this
extension, and let Œ∂ be a primitive M -th root of 1. The composite L(Œ∂) is Galois
and radical over k(Œ∂) (Proposition 6.17 and Exercise 7.5); further, it has enough
roots of 1.
By Lemma 7.13, k(Œ∂) ‚äÜ L(Œ∂) is solvable. It follows that k ‚äÜ L(Œ∂) is solvable.
Indeed, this extension is Galois (Exercise 6.13); apply the fundamental theorem to
k ‚äÜ k(Œ∂) ‚äÜ L(Œ∂)
to establish that Autk (k(Œ∂)) is isomorphic to the quotient of Autk (L(Œ∂)) by its nor-
mal subgroup Autk(Œ∂) (L(Œ∂)). Both Autk(Œ∂)(L(Œ∂)) (as shown above) and Autk (k(Œ∂))
(an abelian group; cf. Example 6.18) are solvable, and it follows that Autk (L(Œ∂))
is solvable as promised, by Corollary IV.3.13.
Since F is an intermediate Ô¨Åeld and Galois over k, by the fundamental theorem
Autk (F ) is a homomorphic image of Autk (L(Œ∂)). This shows that Autk (F ) is a
homomorphic image of a solvable group; hence it is itself solvable (Corollary IV.3.13
again), and we are done.
 
We may have lost sight of where we were heading; but we are ready to reconnect
with the study of solutions to polynomial equations.
DeÔ¨Ånition 7.15. The Galois group Galk (f (x)) of a separable polynomial f (x) ‚àà
k[x] is the Galois group of the splitting Ô¨Åeld of f (x) over k.
 
Corollary 7.16. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld of characteristic 0, and let f (x) ‚àà k[x] be an
irreducible polynomial. Then f (x) is solvable by radicals if and only if its Galois
group is solvable.
Proof. This is an immediate consequence of Lemma 7.11 and Proposition 7.14.
 
Corollary 7.16 is called Galois‚Äô criterion. RuÔ¨Éni (1799) and Abel (1824) had
previously established that general formulas in radicals for the solutions of equa-
tions of degree ‚â• 5 do not exist (that is, Theorem 7.8); but it was Galois who
identiÔ¨Åed the precise condition given in Corollary 7.16. Of course, Theorem 7.8
follows immediately from Galois‚Äô criterion:
Proof of Theorem 7.8. By Lemma 7.5, the Galois group of the general polyno-
mial of degree n is Sn . The group Sn is not solvable for n ‚â• 5 (Corollary IV.4.21);
hence the statement follows from Corollary 7.16.
 
In fact, we could now do more: we know that S3 and S4 are solvable (cf. Exer-
cise IV.3.16); from a composition series with cyclic quotients we could in principle
decompose explicitly the splitting Ô¨Åeld of general polynomials of degree 3 and 4
as radical extensions and as a consequence recover the Tartaglia/Cardano/Ferrari
formulas for their solutions.
478
 VII. Fields
7.5. Galois groups of polynomials. Impressive as Galois‚Äô criterion is, it does
not in itself produce a single polynomial of degree (say) 5 over Q that is not solvable
by radicals. Finding such polynomials requires a certain amount of extra work; we
will be able to do this by the end of this subsection.
Computing Galois groups of polynomials is in fact a popular sport among
algebraists, excelling at which would require much more information than the reader
will glean here. I will just list a few straightforward observations.
To begin with, recall that an element of Autk (F ) must send roots of a polyno-
mial f (x) ‚àà k[x] to roots of the same polynomial, and if f (x) is irreducible and F
is its splitting Ô¨Åeld, then there are automorphisms of F sending any root of f (x) to
any other root (Proposition 1.5, Lemma 4.2). This observation may be rephrased
as follows:
Lemma 7.17. Let f (x) ‚àà k[x] be a separable irreducible polynomial of degree n.
Then Galk (f (x)) acts transitively on the set of roots of f (x) in k. In particular,
Galk (f (x)) may be identiÔ¨Åed with a transitive subgroup of the symmetric group Sn .
Of course a subgroup of Sn is transitive if the corresponding action on {1, . . . , n}
is transitive; the diligent reader has run across this terminology in Exercise IV.4.12.
The reader will easily produce a statement analogous to Lemma 7.17 in case
f (x) is reducible. Of course the action is not transitive in this case, and if the
factors of f (x) have degrees n1 , . . . , nr , then Gal k (f (x)) is contained in a subgroup
of Sn isomorphic to Sn1 √ó ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ √ó Snr .
Lemma 7.17 (or its ‚Äòreducible‚Äô variations) already give some information. For
example, we see that the Galois group of a separable irreducible cubic can only be
A3 or S3 , since these are the only transitive subgroups of S3 . Similarly, the range
of possibilities for irreducible polynomials of degree 4 is rather restricted: S4 , A4 ,
and isomorphic copies of the dihedral group D8 , of Z/2Z √ó Z/2Z, and of Z/4Z
(Exercise 7.8). One approach to the computation of Galois groups of polynomials
amounts to deÔ¨Åning invariants imposing further restrictions, leading to algorithms
deciding which of such possibilities occurs for a given polynomial.
We have already encountered the most important of these invariants: if the
roots of a separable polynomial f (x) ‚àà k[x] in its splitting Ô¨Åeld are Œ±1 , . . . , Œ±n , the
discriminant of f (x) is the element D = Œî2 , where

Œî=
 (Œ±i ‚àí Œ±j ).
1‚â§i<j‚â§n
Every permutation of the roots Ô¨Åxes D, so D must be Ô¨Åxed by the whole Galois
group; therefore, D ‚àà k. Odd permutations move Œî (if char k = 2), and even
permutations Ô¨Åx it; therefore Œî is Ô¨Åxed by the Galois group G (in other words,
Œî ‚àà k) if and only if G ‚äÜ An . This proves
Lemma 7.18. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld of characteristic = 2, and let f (x) ‚àà k[x] be a sepa-
rable polynomial, with discriminant D. Then the Galois group of f (x) is contained
in the alternating group An if and only if D is a square in k.

Example 7.19. Lemma 7.18 and a discriminant computation are all that is needed
to compute the Galois group of an irreducible cubic polynomial
f (x) = x3 + ax2 + bx + c.
It would be futile to try to remember the discriminant
D = a2 b2 ‚àí 4a3 c ‚àí 4b3 + 18abc ‚àí 27c2 ;
but one may remember the trick of shifting x by a/3 (in characteristic = 3), with
the eÔ¨Äect of killing the coeÔ¨Écient of x2 :
a
f (x ‚àí ) = x3 + px + q
3
for suitable p and q. This does not change D (shifting all roots Œ±i by the same
amount has no eÔ¨Äect on the diÔ¨Äerences Œ±i ‚àí Œ±j ), yet
D = ‚àí4p3 ‚àí 27q 2
is a little more memorable.
By Lemma 7.18 and the preceding considerations, the Galois group is A3 if D
is a square and S3 otherwise. For example, x3 ‚àí 2 has Galois group S3 over Q, since
D = ‚àí108 is not a square in Q; x3 ‚àí 3x + 1 has discriminant 81 = 92, and therefore
it has Galois group A3 ‚àº

 
The reader will have no diÔ¨Éculty locating a discussion of the diÔ¨Äerent pos-
sibilities for polynomials of degree 4 and detailed information for higher degree
polynomials. I will just highlight the following simple observation.
Example 7.20. Let f (x) ‚àà Q[x] be an irreducible polynomial of degree p, where
p is prime. Assume that f (x) has p ‚àí 2 real roots and 2 nonreal, complex roots.
Then the Galois group of f (x) is S p .
Indeed, complex conjugation induces an automorphism of the splitting Ô¨Åeld and
acts by interchanging the two nonreal roots, so the Galois group G, as a subgroup
of Sp , contains a transposition. On the other hand, the degree of the splitting Ô¨Åeld
(and hence |G|) is divisible by p, because it contains a simple extension of order p,
obtained by adjoining any one root to Q. Since p is prime, G contains an element of
order p by Cauchy‚Äôs theorem (Theorem IV.2.1); the only elements of order p in Sp
are p-cycles, so G contains a p-cycle. It follows that G = Sp , by (a simple variation
of) Exercise IV.4.7.
For example, the Galois group of f (x) = x5 ‚àí 5x ‚àí 1 over Q is S5, giving a
concrete example of a quintic that cannot be solved by radicals (Exercise 7.10).
The reader will use this technique to produce polynomials of every prime de-
gree p in Z[x] whose Galois group is Sp (Exercise 7.11).
 
7.6. Abelian groups as Galois groups over Q. Having mentioned the inverse
Galois problem in ¬ß7.3, I should point out that the reader is now in the position of
proving that every Ô¨Ånite abelian group may be realized as the Galois group of an
extension over Q.
480
 VII. Fields
In fact, the reader can prove a much more precise result: every Ô¨Ånite abelian
group may be realized as the group of some intermediate Ô¨Åeld of the extension
Q ‚äÜ Q(Œ∂n) of Q in a cyclotomic Ô¨Åeld. This uses a number-theoretic fact:
For every integer N , there are inÔ¨Ånitely many primes p such that p ‚â° 1
mod N .
This is a particular case of Dirichlet‚Äôs theorem (1837), which states that if a, b are
positive integers and gcd(a, b) = 1, then there are inÔ¨Ånitely many primes of the form
a + nb with n > 0. The particular case a = 1, b = N needed here was apparently
already known to Euler and can be proven by elementary means (in fact, there is a
proof using cyclotomic polynomials: see Exercise 5.18). Assuming this fact, argue
as follows:
‚ÄîBy the classiÔ¨Åcation theorem (Theorem IV.6.6), every Ô¨Ånite abelian group G
is isomorphic to a product of cyclic groups
(Z/n1 Z) √ó ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ √ó (Z/nr Z).
‚ÄîBy Dirichlet‚Äôs theorem, we can then choose distinct primes pi such that pi ‚â° 1
mod ni .
‚ÄîLet n = p1 ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ pr ; by the Chinese Remainder Theorem (Theorem V.6.1)
(Z/nZ) ‚àó ‚àº
 = (Z/p1Z)‚àó √ó ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ √ó (Z/pr Z)‚àó ,
and the i-th factor on the right-hand side is cyclic of order pi ‚àí 1, a multiple of ni .
‚ÄîIt follows that (Z/nZ)‚àó has a subgroup H such that G ‚àº
 = (Z/nZ)‚àó /H.
‚ÄîSince (Z/nZ)‚àó ‚àº
 = AutQ(Q(Œ∂n )) (Proposition 5.16) and cyclotomic Ô¨Åelds are
Galois over Q, H corresponds to an intermediate Ô¨Åeld F , Q ‚äÜ F ‚äÜ Q(Œ∂n).
‚ÄîSince AutQ (Q(Œ∂n)) is abelian, H is automatically normal; hence, Q ‚äÜ F is
Galois, and AutQ (F ) ‚àº
 = G, as needed.
Thus, the ‚Äòinverse Galois problem‚Äô has an easy solution for abelian groups.
A much stronger result is true: it can be proven that every Ô¨Ånite abelian
extension of Q is contained in some cyclotomic Ô¨Åeld. This is the Kronecker-Weber
theorem, dating back to the second half of the nineteenth century. The natural
context for this result is class Ô¨Åeld theory and is well beyond the scope of this book.
The reader will be able to verify (Exercise 7.19) that every quadratic extension of Q
is contained in some cyclotomic Ô¨Åeld.

Linear algebra, reprise
Chapter VIII
We come back to linear algebra, with the task of studying slightly more sophis-
ticated constructions than those reviewed in Chapter VI. My main goals are to
discuss some aspects of multilinear algebra, tensor products, and Hom, with spe-
cial attention devoted to dual modules. Along the way we will meet several other
interesting notions, including a Ô¨Årst taste of projective and injective modules and
of Tor and Ext.
As in Chapter VI, I will attempt to deal with the more general case of R-
modules, specializing to vector spaces only when this provides a drastic simpli-
Ô¨Åcation or conforms to inveterate habits. Covering the material at this level of
generality gives me an excellent excuse to complement the preliminary notions de-
scribed in the very Ô¨Årst chapter of this book.
1. Preliminaries, reprise
1.1. Functors. In this book I have given an unusually early introduction to the
notion of category, motivated by the near omnipresence of universal problems at the
very foundation of algebra. The hope was that the unifying viewpoint oÔ¨Äered by
categories would help the reader in the task of organizing the information carried
by the basic constructions we have encountered.
However, so far we have never seriously had to ‚Äògo from one category to another‚Äô,
and therefore I have not felt compelled to complete the elementary presentation of
categories by discussing more formally how this is done. The careful reader has
likely seen it happen between the lines, at least in noninteresting ways: for example,
if we strip a ring of its multiplicative structure, we are left with an abelian group;
and if a Ô¨Åeld F is an extension of a Ô¨Åeld k, then we may view F as a k-vector space:
thus, there are evident ways to go from Ring to Ab or from the category Fldk of
extensions of k to k-Vect. More interesting examples are the group of units of a
ring, the homology of a complex, the automorphism group of a Galois Ô¨Åeld extension
483
484
 VIII. Linear algebra, reprise
(and note that this latter ‚Äòturns arrows around‚Äô; see below), etc. Implicitly, we have
in fact encountered many instances of such ‚Äòfunctions between categories‚Äô.
The notion of functor formalizes these operations. A functor between two
categories C, D will ‚Äòsend objects of C to objects of D‚Äô, and since categories carry
the information of morphisms, functors will have to deal with this information as
well.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.1. Let C, D be two categories. A covariant functor
F : C ‚Üí D
is an assignment of an object F (A) ‚àà Obj(D) for every A ‚àà Obj(C) and of a
function
Hom C (A, B) ‚Üí HomD (F (A), F (B))
for every pair of objects A, B in C; this function is also denoted1 F and must
preserve identities and compositions. That is,
F (1A) = 1F (A)
‚àÄA ‚àà Obj(C), and
F (Œ≤ ‚ó¶ Œ±) = F (Œ≤) ‚ó¶ F (Œ±)
‚àÄA, B, C ‚àà Obj(C), ‚àÄŒ± ‚àà HomC (A, B), ‚àÄŒ≤ ‚àà HomC(B, C).
A contravariant functor C ‚Üí D is a covariant functor Cop ‚Üí D from the
opposite category.
 
The opposite category Cop is obtained from C by ‚Äòreversing the arrows‚Äô; cf. Ex-
ercise I.3.1. The fact that contravariant functors G : C ‚Üí D (that is, covari-

G (C)
In both cases, commutative diagrams are sent to commutative diagrams.
In many contexts, contravariant functors are called presheaves. Thus, a presheaf
of sets on a category C is simply a contravariant functor C ‚Üí Set.
If HomC(A, B) carries more structure, we may require functors to preserve this
structure. For example, we have seen that for C = R-Mod, the category of left-
modules over a ring R, HomR-Mod (A, B) is itself an abelian group (end of ¬ßIII.5.2);
a functor F : R-Mod ‚Üí S-Mod is additive if it preserves this structure, that is, if
the corresponding function
HomR-Mod (A, B) ‚Üí HomS-Mod (F (A), F (B))
is a homomorphism of abelian groups (for all R-modules A, B).
1.2. Examples of functors. In practice, an assignment of objects of a category
for objects of another category often comes with a (psychologically) ‚Äònatural‚Äô com-
panion assignment for morphisms, which is evidently covariant or contravariant.
We say that this assignment is ‚Äòfunctorial‚Äô if it preserves compositions.
All the simple-minded operations mentioned at the beginning of ¬ß1.1 (such
as viewing a ring as an abelian group under addition) are covariant functors: for
example, the fact that a homomorphism of rings is in particular a homomorphism
of the underlying abelian groups amounts to the statement that labeling each ring
with its underlying abelian group deÔ¨Ånes a covariant functor Ring ‚Üí Ab. Since
such functors are obtained by ‚Äòforgetting‚Äô part of the structure of a given object,
they are memorably called forgetful functors.
I have already mentioned brieÔ¨Çy a few slightly more imaginative examples; here
they are again.
Example 1.2. If R is a ring, we have denoted by R‚àó the group of units in R;
every ring homomorphism R ‚Üí S induces a group homomorphism R‚àó ‚Üí S ‚àó , and
this assignment is compatible with compositions; therefore this operation deÔ¨Ånes a
covariant functor Ring ‚Üí Grp.
 
486
 VIII. Linear algebra, reprise
Example 1.3. The operation Autk ( ) from Galois Ô¨Åeld extensions to groups is
contravariantly functorial. Indeed, if k ‚äÜ E ‚äÜ F is viewed as a morphism of two
Galois extensions (k ‚äÜ E to k ‚äÜ F ), we have a corresponding group homomorphism
Autk (F ) ‚Üí Autk (E)
deÔ¨Åned by restriction: œï ‚Üí œï|E ; the latter maps E to E by virtue of the Galois
condition. The composition of two restrictions is a restriction, so the assignment is
indeed functorial.
 
Example 1.4. In ¬ßIII.4.3 I have deÔ¨Åned the spectrum of a commutative ring R,
Spec R, as the set of prime ideals of R. If R, S are commutative rings and œï : R ‚Üí S
is a ring homomorphism, then the inverse image œï‚àí1 (p) of a prime ideal p is a prime
ideal; thus, œï induces a set-function
œï‚àó : Spec(S) ‚Üí Spec(R) .
This assignment is clearly functorial, so we can view Spec as a contravariant functor
from the category of commutative rings to Set. The reader will assign a topology
to Spec(R) (Exercise 1.7), making this example (even) more interesting.
 
Example 1.5. If S is a set, recall (Example I.3.4) that one can construct a cate-
gory ≈ú whose objects are subsets of S and where morphisms correspond to inclusions
of subsets. A presheaf of sets on S is a contravariant functor ≈ú ‚Üí Set. The pro-
totypical example consists of the functor which associates to U ‚äÜ S the set of
functions from U to a Ô¨Åxed set; the inclusion U ‚äÜ V is mapped to the restriction
œÅ ‚Üí œÅ|U . More interesting (and very useful) examples may be obtained by consid-
ering richer structures. For instance, if T is a topological space, one can deÔ¨Åne a
category whose objects are the open sets in T and whose morphisms are inclusions;
this leads to the notion of presheaf on a topological space. Standard notions such
as ‚Äò(the groups of) continuous complex-valued functions on open sets of T ‚Äô are most
naturally viewed as the datum of a presheaf of abelian groups on T . Such concrete
examples often satisfy additional hypotheses, which make them sheaves, but that is
a topic for another book. In a sense, studying a branch of geometry (e.g., complex
diÔ¨Äerential geometry) amounts to studying spaces endowed with a suitable sheaf
(e.g., the sheaf of complex diÔ¨Äerentiable functions).
 
An important class of functors that are available on any category may be ob-
tained as follows, and this will be a source of examples in the next few sections.

The brave reader may contemplate the fact that this technique associates to
each object X of a category C a presheaf of sets hX on C. This raises natural and
important questions, such as how to tell whether a given presheaf of sets F in fact
equals hX for some object X of C (this makes F ‚Äòrepresentable‚Äô) or whether an
object X may be reconstructed from the corresponding presheaf hX . For example,
if Top is the category of topological spaces and p denotes the Ô¨Ånal object of Top
(that is, the one-point topological space), then hX (p) is nothing but X, viewed
as a set. For this reason, in such ‚Äògeometric‚Äô contexts (for example, in algebraic
geometry) hX (A) is called the ‚Äòset of A-points of X‚Äô; hX is the functor of points
of X.
1.3. When are two categories ‚Äòequivalent‚Äô ? Essentially every notion of ‚Äòiso-
morphism‚Äô encountered so far has boiled down to ‚Äòstructure-preserving bijection‚Äô,
and the reader may well expect that the natural notion identifying two categories
should be drawn from the same model: a functor matching objects of one category
precisely with objects of another, preserving the structure of morphisms.
One could certainly introduce such a notion, but it would be exceedingly re-
strictive. Recall that solutions to universal problems, that is, just about every
construction we have run across, are only deÔ¨Åned up to isomorphism (Proposi-
tion I.5.4). Requiring solutions in one context to match exactly with solutions in
another would be problematic. The structure of an object in a category is ad-
equately carried by its isomorphism class2 , and a natural notion of ‚Äòequivalence‚Äô
of categories should aim at matching isomorphism classes, rather than individual
objects. The morphisms are a more essential piece of information; the quality of a
functor is Ô¨Årst of all measured on how it acts on morphisms.
2One should not take this viewpoint too far. It is tempting to think of isomorphic objects
in a category as ‚Äòthe same‚Äô, but this is also problematic and does not lead (as far as I know) to
a workable alternative. For example, some objects in a category may have ‚Äòmore automorphisms‚Äô
than others, and this information would be discarded if we simply chopped up the category into
isomorphism classes, draconially promoting all isomorphisms to identity morphisms.
488
 VIII. Linear algebra, reprise
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.6. Let C, D be two categories. A covariant functor F : C ‚Üí D is
faithful if for all objects A, B of C, the induced function
Hom C (A, B) ‚Üí HomD (F (A), F (B))
is injective; it is full if this function is surjective, for all objects A, B.
 
‚ÄòFully faithful‚Äô functors F : C ‚Üí D are bijective on morphisms, and it follows
easily that they preserve isomorphism classes: A ‚àº
 = B in C if and only if F (A) =
 ‚àº
F (B) in D (Exercise 1.2). For all intents and purposes, a fully faithful functor
F : C ‚Üí D identiÔ¨Åes C with a full3 subcategory of D, even if ‚Äòon objects‚Äô F may
be neither injective nor surjective onto that subcategory. This idea is captured by
the following deÔ¨Ånition:
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.7. A covariant functor F : C ‚Üí D is an equivalence of categories if
it is fully faithful and ‚Äòessentially surjective‚Äô; that is, F induces bijections on Hom-
sets, and for every object Y of D there is an object X of C such that F (X) ‚àº
 = Y
in D.
 
Two categories C, D are equivalent if there is a functor F : C ‚Üí D satisfying
the requirements in DeÔ¨Ånition 1.7. It is not at all immediate that this is indeed an
equivalence relation (why is it symmetric?), but this turns out to be the case. In
fact, an equivalence of categories F has an ‚Äòinverse‚Äô, in a suitably weakened sense:
a functor G : D ‚Üí C such that there are ‚Äònatural isomorphisms‚Äô (see ¬ß1.5) from
F ‚ó¶G and G ‚ó¶F to the identity. Since we will not use these notions too extensively,
I will let the interested reader research the issue and Ô¨Ånd proofs.
Example 1.8. (Cf. Exercise I.3.6.) Construct a category by taking the objects to
be nonnegative integers and Hom(m, n) to be the set of n √ó m matrices with entries
in a Ô¨Åeld k, with composition deÔ¨Åned by product of matrices (and suitable care
concerning matrices with no rows or columns). The resulting category is equivalent
to the category of Ô¨Ånite-dimensional k-vector spaces. Indeed, we obtain a functor
from the former to the latter by sending n to the vector space k n (endowed with
the standard basis) and each matrix to the corresponding linear map. This functor
is clearly fully faithful, and it is essentially surjective because vector spaces are
classiÔ¨Åed by their dimension. This example makes (more) precise the heuristic
considerations at the end of ¬ßVI.2.1.
 
Example 1.9. Recall (DeÔ¨Ånition VII.2.19) that the coordinate ring of an aÔ¨Éne
algebraic set over a Ô¨Åeld K is a reduced, commutative, Ô¨Ånite-type K-algebra. We
can deÔ¨Åne the category K-AÔ¨Ä of aÔ¨Éne K-algebraic sets by prescribing that the
objects be algebraic subsets of some aÔ¨Éne K-space and deÔ¨Åning4 HomK-AÔ¨Ä (S, T )
to be HomK-Alg (K[T ], K[S]).

1.4. Limits and colimits. The various universal properties encountered along
the way in this book are all particular cases of the notion of categorical limit,
which is worth mentioning explicitly. Let F : I ‚Üí C be a covariant functor, where
one thinks of I as a category ‚Äòof indices‚Äô. The limit of F is (if it exists) an object L
of C, endowed with morphisms ŒªI : L ‚Üí F (I) for all objects I of I, satisfying the
following properties.
‚Ä¢ If Œ± : I ‚Üí J is a morphism in I, then ŒªJ = F (Œ±) ‚ó¶ ŒªI :

a unique morphism M ‚Üí L making all relevant diagrams commute5 .
The limit L (if it exists) is unique up to isomorphism, as is every notion deÔ¨Åned
by a universal property. It is denoted lim F . The ‚Äòleft-pointing‚Äô arrow reminds

isomorphisms‚Äîthe ‚Äòlast‚Äô object that may do so). This notion is also called inverse,
or projective, limit.
Example 1.10 (Products). Let I be the ‚Äòdiscrete category‚Äô consisting of two objects
1, 2, with only identity morphisms6 , and let A be a functor from I to any category C;
let A1 = A (1), A2 = A (2) be the two objects of C ‚Äòindexed‚Äô by I. Then lim A is
‚Üê‚àí
nothing but the product of A1 and A2 in C, as deÔ¨Åned in ¬ßI.5.4: a limit exists if
and only if a product of A1 and A2 exists in C.
We can similarly deÔ¨Åne the product of any (possibly inÔ¨Ånite) family of objects in
a category as the limit over the corresponding discrete indexing category, provided
of course that this limit exists.

1.5. Comparing functors. Having introduced functors as the natural notion of
‚Äòmorphisms between categories‚Äô, the next natural step is to consider ‚Äòmorphisms
between functors‚Äô and other ways to compare two given functors. A detailed de-
scription of these notions is not needed in this book, but I want to mention brieÔ¨Çy a
few concepts and essential remarks, again because they oÔ¨Äer a unifying viewpoint.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.15. Let C, D be categories, and let F , G be (say, covariant) functors
C ‚Üí D. A natural transformation 7 F ; G is the datum of a morphism ŒΩX :
F (X) ‚Üí G (X) in D for every object X in C, such that ‚àÄŒ± : X ‚Üí Y in C the
diagram
F (X)
 F (Œ±)
 / F (Y )
ŒΩX
 ŒΩY

 G (Œ±)
 
G (X)
 / G (Y )
commutes. A natural isomorphism is a natural transformation ŒΩ such that ŒΩX is
an isomorphism for every X.
 
Natural transformations arise in many contexts, for example when comparing
diÔ¨Äerent notions of homology or cohomology in topology. The reader is likely
to have run into the Hurewicz homomorphism (from œÄ1 to H1); it is a natural
transformation. In fact, any statement such as ‚Äòthere is a natural (or canonical )
homomorphism. . . ‚Äô is likely hiding a natural transformation between two functors.
This technical meaning of the word ‚Äònatural‚Äô matches, as a rule, its psychological
use.
A particularly basic and important example is the notion of adjoint functors,
which I will mention at a rather informal level, leaving to the inextinguishable
reader the task of making it formal by suitable use of natural transformations. Let
C, D be categories, and let F : C ‚Üí D, G : D ‚Üí C be functors. We say that F
and G are adjoint (and we say that G is right-adjoint to F and F is left-adjoint
to G ) if there are natural isomorphisms
HomC (X, G (Y ))
 ‚àº
 / HomD (F (X), Y )
for all objects X of C and Y of D. (More precisely, there should be a natural
‚àº
isomorphism of ‚Äòbifunctors‚Äô Cop √ó D ‚Üí Set: HomC ( , G ( )) ; HomD(F ( ), ).)
Once again, several constructions we have encountered along the way may be
recast in these terms, and we will run into more instances of this situation in this
chapter.
Example 1.16. The construction of the free group on a given set (¬ßII.5) is con-
cocted so that giving a set-function from a set A to a group G is ‚Äòthe same as‚Äô
giving a group homomorphism from F (A) to G (¬ßII.5.2). What this really means
is that for all sets A and all groups G there are natural identiÔ¨Åcations
HomSet (A, S(G)) ‚àº
 = HomGrp (F (A), G) ,
7 The symbol ‚áí is more standard than ;, but it looks a little too much like the logical
connective =‚áí for my taste.
1. Preliminaries, reprise
 493
where S(G) ‚Äòforgets‚Äô the group structure of G. That is, the functor F : Set ‚Üí Grp
constructing free groups is left-adjoint to the forgetful functor S : Grp ‚Üí Set.
This of course applies to every other construction of ‚Äòfree‚Äô objects we have
encountered: the free functor is, as a rule, left-adjoint to the forgetful functor. 
Thus, interesting functors may turn out to be adjoints of harmless-looking
functors. This has technical advantages: properties of the interesting ones may be
translated into properties of the harmless ones, thereby giving easier proofs of these
properties.
In fact, the very fact that a functor has an adjoint will endow that functor
with convenient features. We say that F is a left-adjoint functor if it has a right-
adjoint, and that G is a right-adjoint functor if it has a left-adjoint. Some properties
of (say) right-adjoint functors may be established without even knowing what the
companion left-adjoint functor may be. Here is the prototypical example of this
phenomenon.
Lemma 1.17. Right-adjoint functors commute with limits.
That is, if G : D ‚Üí C has a left-adjoint F : C ‚Üí D and A : I ‚Üí D is another
functor, then there is a canonical isomorphism
G (lim A ) ‚Üí ‚àº
 lim(G ‚ó¶ A )
‚Üê‚àí
 ‚Üê‚àí
(if the limits exist, of course). As every good calculus student should readily
understand, this says that right-adjoint functors are continuous. (Needless to say,
left-adjoint functors turn out to commute with colimits and would rightly be termed
cocontinuous.)
We will not prove Lemma 1.17 in gory detail, but I will endeavor to convince
the reader that such statements are easier than they look and just boil down to
suitable applications of the universal properties deÔ¨Åning the various concepts. By
contrast, proving that a speciÔ¨Åc given functor preserves products (for instance),
without appealing to abstract nonsense, may sometimes appear to involve some
‚Äòreal‚Äô work.

What is missing from this outline is the explicit veriÔ¨Åcation of the fact that
every needed diagram commutes, which is necessary in order to apply the universal
properties as stated. The reader will likely agree that such veriÔ¨Åcations, while
possibly somewhat involved, must be routine.
Lemma 1.17 implies, for example, that right-adjoint functors preserve products
and that they must ‚Äòpreserve kernels‚Äô when kernels make sense.
1. Preliminaries, reprise

Example 1.18 (Exact functors). A functor is exact if it preserves exactness, that
is, it sends exact sequences to exact sequences. So far we have only studied exactness
in the context of modules over a ring (¬ßIII.7.1), so let R, S be rings and let F :
R-Mod ‚Üí S-Mod be an additive functor; F is exact if and only if whenever

complex is exact (Exercise 1.23).
A common and very interesting situation occurs when a functor is not exact
but preserves ‚Äòsome‚Äô of the exactness of sequences (we will encounter such examples
later in this chapter, for example in ¬ß2.3). We say that an additive functor F is
left-exact if whenever

It turns out that a left-exact functor gives rise to a whole sequence of ‚Äònew‚Äô, related
(‚Äòderived‚Äô ) functors; this hugely important phenomenon is studied in homological
algebra, and we will come back to it in Chapter IX.
Claim 1.19. Right-adjoint additive functors R-Mod ‚Üí S-Mod are left-exact.
As the reader will verify (Exercise 1.27), this follows from the fact that right-
adjoint functors preserve kernels. (Note that the exactness of the sequence amounts

2. Tensor products and the Tor functors
In the rest of the chapter we will work in the category R-Mod of modules over a
commutative ring R. Essentially everything we will see can be upgraded to the
noncommutative case without diÔ¨Éculty, but a bit of structure is lost in that case.
For example, if R is not commutative, then in the category R-Mod of left-R-modules
the Hom-sets HomR-Mod (M, N ) are ‚Äòonly‚Äô abelian groups (cf. the end of ¬ßIII.5.2).
A tensor product M ‚äóR N can only be deÔ¨Åned if M is a right-R-module and N is
a left-R-module (in a sense, the two module structures annihilate each other, and
what is left is an abelian group). By contrast, in the commutative case we will be
able to deÔ¨Åne M ‚äóR N simply as an R-module. In general, the theory goes through
as in the commutative case if the modules carry compatible left- and right-module
structures, except in questions such as the commutativity of tensors, where it would
be unreasonable to expect the commutativity of R to have no bearing. All in all, the
2. Tensor products and the Tor functors
 501
commutative case is a little leaner, and (I believe) it suÔ¨Éces in terms of conveying
the basic intuition on the general features of the theory.
Thus, R will denote a Ô¨Åxed commutative ring, unless stated otherwise.
2.1. Bilinear maps and the deÔ¨Ånition of tensor product. If M and N are
R-modules, we observed in the distant past (¬ßIII.6.1) that M ‚äï N serves as both
the product and coproduct of M and N : a situation in which a limit coincides with
a colimit. As a set, M ‚äï N is just M √ó N ; the R-module structure on M ‚äï N
is deÔ¨Åned by componentwise addition and multiplication by scalars. An R-module
homomorphism
M ‚äï N ‚Üí P
is determined by R-module homomorphisms M ‚Üí P and N ‚Üí P (this is what
makes M ‚äï N into a coproduct).
But there is another way to map M √ó N to an R-module P , compatibly with
the R-module structures.
DeÔ¨Ånition 2.1. Let M , N , P be R-modules. A function œï : M √ó N ‚Üí P is
R-bilinear if
‚Ä¢ ‚àÄm ‚àà M , the function n ‚Üí œï(m, n) is an R-module homomorphism N ‚Üí P ,
‚Ä¢ ‚àÄn ‚àà N , the function m ‚Üí œï(m, n) is an R-module homomorphism M ‚Üí P .
Thus, if œï : M √ó N ‚Üí P is R-bilinear, then ‚àÄm ‚àà M , ‚àÄn 1 , n2 ‚àà N , ‚àÄr1 , r2 ‚àà R,
œï(m, r1n1 + r2 n2 ) = r1 œï(m, n1) + r2 œï(m, n2 ),
and similarly for œï( , n).
Note that œï itself is not linear, even if we view M √ó N as the R-module M ‚äï N ,
as recalled above. On the other hand, there ought to be a way to deal with R-
bilinear maps ‚Äòas if‚Äô they were R-linear, because such maps abound in the context
of R-modules. For example, the very multiplication on R is itself an R-bilinear map
R √ó R ‚Üí R.
Our experience with universal properties suggests the natural way to approach this
question. What we need is a new R-module M ‚äóR N , with an R-bilinear map
‚äó : M √ó N ‚Üí M ‚äóR N,
such that every R-bilinear map M √ó N ‚Üí P factors uniquely through this new
module M ‚äóR N ,
M ‚äó
√ó  N
 v
 v
 v v v ‚àÉ!œï
 v
 œï
 v
 v v
 /: P
M ‚äóR N
in such a way that the map œï is a usual R-module homomorphism.
Thus, M ‚äóR N would be the ‚Äòbest approximation‚Äô to M √óN available in R-Mod,
if we want to view R-bilinear maps from M √ó N as R-linear. The module M ‚äóR N
is called the tensor product of M and N over R. The subscript R is very important:
if M and N are modules over two rings R, S, then S-bilinearity is not the same
502
 VIII. Linear algebra, reprise
as R-bilinearity, so M ‚äóR N and M ‚äóS N may be completely diÔ¨Äerent objects. In
context, it is not unusual to drop the subscript if the base ring is understood, but
I do not recommend this practice.
The prescription given above expresses the tensor product as the solution to
a universal problem; therefore we know right away that it will be unique up to
isomorphism, if it exists (Proposition I.5.4 once more), and we could proceed to
study it by systematically using the universal property.

Elements of the form m ‚äó n (that is, needing only one summand in the expres-
sion) are called pure tensors. Dear reader, please remember that pure tensors are
special: usually, not every element of the tensor product is a pure tensor. See Exer-
cise 2.1 for one situation in which every tensor happens to be pure, and appreciate
how special that is.
Pure tensors are nevertheless very useful, as a set of generators for the tensor
product. For example, if two homomorphisms Œ±, Œ≤ : M ‚äóR N ‚Üí P coincide on
pure tensors, then Œ± = Œ≤. Frequently, computations involving tensor products are
reduced to simple veriÔ¨Åcations for pure tensors.
2.2. Adjunction with Hom and explicit computations. The tensor product
is left-adjoint to Hom. Once we parse what this rough statement means, it will
be a near triviality; but as we have found out in ¬ß1.5, the mere fact that ‚äóR is
left-adjoint to any functor is enough to draw interesting conclusions about it.
First, we note that every R-module N deÔ¨Ånes, via ‚äóR , a new covariant functor
R-Mod ‚Üí R-Mod, deÔ¨Åned on objects by
M ‚Üí M ‚äóR N.
To see how this works on morphisms, let
Œ± : M1 ‚Üí M2
be an R-module homomorphism. Crossing with N and composing with ‚äó deÔ¨Ånes
an R-bilinear map
M1 √ó N ‚Üí M2 √ó N ‚Üí M2 ‚äó N,
and hence an induced R-linear map
Œ± ‚äó N : M1 ‚äó N ‚Üí M2 ‚äó N.
On pure tensors, this map is simply given by m ‚äó n ‚Üí Œ±(m) ‚äó n, and functoriality
follows immediately: if Œ≤ : M0 ‚Üí M1 is a second homomorphism, then (Œ± ‚äó N ) ‚ó¶
(Œ≤ ‚äó N ) and (Œ± ‚ó¶ Œ≤) ‚äó N both map pure tensors m ‚äó n to Œ±(Œ≤(m)) ‚äó n, so they
must agree on all tensors.

The adjunction statement given at the beginning of this subsection compares
this functor with the covariant functor P ‚Üí HomR-Mod (N, P ); cf. ¬ß1.2. Let‚Äôs see
more precisely how it works.
We have deÔ¨Åned M ‚äóR N so that giving an R-linear map M ‚äó R N ‚Üí P to an
R-module P is ‚Äòthe same as‚Äô giving an R-bilinear map M √ó N ‚Üí P . Now recall
the deÔ¨Ånition of R-bilinear map: œï : M √ó N ‚Üí P is R-bilinear if both œï(m, )
and œï( , n) are R-linear maps, for all m ‚àà M and n ‚àà N . The Ô¨Årst part of this
prescription says that œï determines a function
M ‚Üí HomR (N, P );
the second part says that this is an R-module homomorphism. Therefore, an R-
bilinear map is ‚Äòthe same as‚Äô an element of
HomR (M, HomR (N, P )).
These simple considerations should be enough to make the following seemingly
complicated statement rather natural:
Lemma 2.4. For all R-modules M , N , P , there is an isomorphism of R-modules
HomR (M, HomR(N, P )) ‚àº
 = HomR(M ‚äóR N, P ).
Proof. As noted before the statement, every Œ± ‚àà HomR (M, HomR (N, P )) deter-
mines an R-bilinear map œï : M √ó N ‚Üí P , by
(m, n) ‚Üí Œ±(m)(n).
By the universal property, œï factors uniquely through an R-linear map œï : M ‚äóR
N ‚Üí P . Therefore, Œ± determines a well-deÔ¨Åned element œï ‚àà HomR (M ‚äóR N, P ).
The reader will check (Exercise 2.11) that this map Œ± ‚Üí œï is R-linear and construct
an inverse.

are more interesting over more general rings.

Corollary 2.8 is a template example for a basic application of ‚äó: tensor prod-

ideal I) to constructions involving R-modules (such as quotienting by a correspond-

a look at localization in Exercise 2.5.
2.3. Exactness properties of tensor; Ô¨Çatness. It is important to remember
that the tensor product is not an exact functor: left-exactness may very well fail.
This can already be observed in the sequence appearing in the discussion following
Corollary 2.8: for an ideal I of R and an R-module N , the map

induced by the inclusion I ‚äÜ R after tensoring by N may not be injective.

We have already checked that Z/2Z is not a Ô¨Çat Z-module, while free modules
are Ô¨Çat. Flat modules are hugely important: in algebraic geometry, ‚ÄòÔ¨Çatness‚Äô is
the condition expressing the fact that the objects in a family vary ‚Äòcontinuously‚Äô,
preserving certain key invariants.

PID; cf. Exercise 2.17).
Note that Torki vanishes for i > 0 if k is a Ô¨Åeld, as vector spaces are Ô¨Çat,
and TorR
 i vanishes for i > 1 if R is a PID (Exercise 2.17). These facts are not
surprising, in view of the procedure described above for computing Tor and of the
considerations at the end of ¬ßVI.5.2: a bound on the length of free resolutions for
modules over a ring R will imply a bound on nonzero Tor‚Äôs. For particularly nice
rings (such as the rings corresponding to ‚Äòsmooth‚Äô points in algebraic geometry)
this bound agrees with the Krull dimension; but precise results of this sort are
beyond the scope of this book.

3. Base change
I have championed several times the viewpoint that deep properties of a ring R are
encoded in the category R-Mod of R-modules; one extreme position is to simply
replace R with R-Mod as the main object of study. The question then arises as to
how to deal with ring homomorphisms from this point of view, or more generally
how the categories R-Mod, S-Mod of modules over two (commutative) rings R,
S may relate to each other. The reader should expect this to happen by way of
functors between the two categories and that the situation at the categorical level
will be substantially richer than at the ring level.
3.1. Balanced maps. Before we can survey the basic deÔ¨Ånitions, we must up-
grade our understanding of tensor products. It turns out that M ‚äóR N satisÔ¨Åes a
more encompassing universal property than the one examined in ¬ß2.1. Let M , N
be modules over a commutative ring R, as in ¬ß2.1, and let G be an abelian group,
i.e., a Z-module.
DeÔ¨Ånition 3.1. A Z-bilinear map œï : M √ó N ‚Üí G is R-balanced if ‚àÄm ‚àà M ,
‚àÄn ‚àà N , ‚àÄr ‚àà R,
œï(rm, n) = œï(m, rn).
 
If G is an R-module and œï : M √ó N ‚Üí G is R-bilinear, then it is R-balanced12 .
But in general the notion of ‚ÄòR-balanced‚Äô appears to be quite a bit more general,
since G is not even required to be an R-module. This may lead the reader to
suspect that a solution to the universal problem of factoring balanced maps may
be a diÔ¨Äerent gadget than the ‚Äòordinary‚Äô tensor product, but we are in luck in this
case, and the ordinary tensor product does the universal job for balanced maps as
well.
To understand this, recall that we constructed M ‚äóR N as a quotient

3.2. Bimodules; adjunction again. The enhanced universal property for the
tensor will allow us to upgrade the adjunction formula given in Lemma 2.4. This
requires the introduction of yet another notion.
DeÔ¨Ånition 3.4. Let R, S be two commutative13 rings. An (R, S)-bimodule is an
abelian group N endowed with compatible R-module and S-module structures, in
the sense that ‚àÄn ‚àà N , ‚àÄr ‚àà R, ‚àÄs ‚àà S,
r(sn) = s(rn).
 
For example, as R is commutative, every R-module N is an (R, R)-bimodule:
‚àÄr1 , r2 ‚àà R and ‚àÄn ‚àà N ,
r1 (r2 n) = (r1 r2 )n = (r2r1 )n = r2(r1n).
If M is an R-module and N is an (R, S)-bimodule, then the tensor prod-
uct M ‚äóR N acquires an S-module structure: deÔ¨Åne the action of s ‚àà S on pure
tensors m ‚äó n by
s(m ‚äó n) := m ‚äó (sn),
and extend to all tensors by linearity. In fact, this gives M ‚äóR N an (R, S)-bimodule
structure.
Similarly, if N is an (R, S)-bimodule and P is an S-module, then the abelian
group HomS (N, P ) is an (R, S)-bimodule: the R-module structure is deÔ¨Åned by
setting (rŒ±)(n) = Œ±(rn) for all r ‚àà R, n ‚àà N , and Œ± ‚àà HomS (N, P ).
This mess is needed to even make sense of the promised upgrade of adjunction.
As with most such results, the proof is not diÔ¨Écult once one understands what the
statement says.
Lemma 3.5. Suppose M is an R-module, N is an (R, S)-bimodule, and P is an
S-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of abelian groups
HomR (M, HomS (N, P )) ‚àº

3.3. Restriction and extension of scalars. Coming back to the theme men-
tioned at the beginning of this section, consider the case in which we have a ho-
momorphism f : R ‚Üí S of (commutative) rings. It is natural to look for functors
between the categories R-Mod and S-Mod of modules over R, S, respectively. There
is a rather simple-minded functor from S-Mod to R-Mod (‚Äòrestriction of scalars‚Äô),
while tensor products allow us to deÔ¨Åne a functor from R-Mod to S-Mod (‚Äòexten-
sion of scalars‚Äô). A third important functor R-Mod ‚Üí S-Mod may be deÔ¨Åned, also
‚Äòextending scalars‚Äô, but for which I do not know a good name.
Restriction of scalars. Let f : R ‚Üí S be a ring homomorphism, and let N be
an S-module. Recall (¬ßIII.5.1) that this means that we have chosen an action of
the ring S on the abelian group N , that is, a ring homomorphism
œÉ : S ‚Üí EndAb (N ).
Composing with f ,
œÉ ‚ó¶ f : R ‚Üí S ‚Üí EndAb(N )
deÔ¨Ånes an action of R on the abelian group N , and hence an R-module structure
on N .
(Even) more explicitly, if r ‚àà R and n ‚àà N , deÔ¨Åne the action of r on n by
setting
rn := f (r)n.
Since S is commutative, this deÔ¨Ånes in fact an (R, S)-bimodule structure on N .
Further, S-linear homomorphisms are in particular R-linear; this assignment is
(covariantly) functorial S-Mod ‚Üí R-Mod.
If f is injective, so that R may be viewed as a subring of S, then all we are doing
is viewing N as a module on a ‚Äòrestricted‚Äô range of scalars, hence the terminology.
For example, this is how we view a complex vector space as a real vector space, in
the simplest possible way.
I will denote by f‚àó this functor S-Mod ‚Üí R-Mod induced from f by restriction
of scalars. Note that f‚àó is trivially exact, because the kernels and images of a
homomorphism of modules are the same regardless of the base ring. In view of the
3. Base change
 519
considerations in Example 1.18, this hints that f‚àó may have both a left-adjoint and
a right-adjoint functor, and this will be precisely the case (Proposition 3.6).
Extension of scalars is deÔ¨Åned from R-Mod to S-Mod, by associating to an R-
module M the tensor product f ‚àó (M ) := M ‚äóR S, which (as we have seen in ¬ß3.2)
carries naturally an S-module structure. This association is evidently covariantly
functorial. If
R‚äïB ‚Üí R‚äïA ‚Üí M ‚Üí 0
is a presentation of M (cf. ¬ßVI.4.2), tensoring by S gives (by the right-exactness of
‚àótensor) a presentation of f (M ):
S ‚äïB ‚Üí S ‚äïA ‚Üí M ‚äóR S ‚Üí 0.
‚àóIntuitively, this says that f (M ) is the module deÔ¨Åned by ‚Äòthe same generators and
relations‚Äô as M , but with coeÔ¨Écients in S.
The third functor, denoted f ! , also acts from R-Mod to S-Mod and is yet
another natural way to combine the ingredients we have at our disposal: if M is
an R-module, I have pointed out14 in ¬ß3.2 that
!f (M ) := HomR (S, M )
may be given a natural S-module structure (by setting sŒ±(s ) := Œ±(ss)). This is
again evidently a covariantly functorial prescription.
Proposition 3.6. Let f : R ‚Üí S be a homomorphism of commutative rings. Then,
‚àó !with notation as above, f‚àó is right-adjoint to f and left-adjoint to f . In particular,
‚àó !f‚àó is exact, f is right-exact, and f is left-exact.
Proof. Let M , resp., N , be an R-module, resp., an S-module. Note that, trivially,
HomS (S, N ) is canonically isomorphic to N (as an S-module) and to f‚àó (N ) (as an
R-module). Thus15
HomR (M, f‚àó (N )) ‚àº
 = HomR (M, HomS (S, N ))
‚àº
 = HomS (M ‚äóR S, N ) = HomS (f ‚àó (M ), N )
where I have used Lemma 3.5. These bijections are canonical16 , proving that f ‚àó is
left-adjoint to f‚àó .
Similarly, there is a canonical isomorphism N ‚àº
 = N ‚äóS S (Example 2.2); thus
N ‚äóS S ‚àº
 = f‚àó (N ) as R-modules, and for every R-module M
HomR (f‚àó (N ), M ) ‚àº
 = HomR(N ‚äóS S, M )
‚àº
 = HomS (N, HomR (S, M )) = HomS (N, f ! (M ))
again by Lemma 3.5. This shows that f ! is right-adjoint to f‚àó, concluding the
proof.

 VIII. Linear algebra, reprise
Remark 3.7 (Warning). My choice of notation, f‚àó , etc., is somewhat nonstandard,
and the reader should not take it too literally. It is inspired by analogs in the
context of sheaf theory over schemes, but some of the properties reviewed above
require crucial adjustments in that wider context: for example f‚àó is not exact as a
sheaf operation on schemes.

3.15. Let f : R ‚Üí S be a homomorphism of (commutative) rings, and assume that

‚Ä¢ Prove that there is a homomorphism of rings g : S ‚Üí EndAb (R) such that the

over itself (that is, the homomorphism studied in Proposition III.2.7).
‚Ä¢ Use the facts that S is commutative and f‚àó is fully faithful to deduce that g(S)
is isomorphic to R. Deduce that f has a left-inverse g : S ‚Üí R.
‚Ä¢ Therefore, f‚àó ‚ó¶ g‚àó is naturally isomorphic to the identity; in particular, f‚àó ‚ó¶
(S) ‚àº

g‚àóa ‚àà ker g, prove that a is in the annihilator of f‚àó ‚ó¶ g‚àó(S).)
‚Ä¢ Conclude that f is an isomorphism.
Two rings are Morita equivalent if their categories of left-modules are equiv-
alent. The result of this exercise is a (very) particular case of the fact that two
commutative rings are Morita equivalent if and only if they are isomorphic. In
fact, this more general statement is perhaps easier (!) to prove than the particu-
lar case worked out in this exercise. Recall (Exercise 1.28) that if R is any ring,
then the center of R-Mod is isomorphic to the center of R. It is not diÔ¨Écult to
deduce from this that if R-Mod and S-Mod are equivalent, then the centers of R
and S are isomorphic. So if two commutative rings R and S are Morita equivalent,
then they must be isomorphic. The commutativity is crucial in this statement:
for example, it can be shown that any ring R is Morita equivalent to the ring of
matrices17 Mn,n (R), for all n > 0.
4. Multilinear algebra
4.1. Multilinear, symmetric, alternating maps. Multilinear maps may be
deÔ¨Åned similarly to bilinear maps: if M1 , . . . , M, P are R-modules, a function
œï : M1 √ó ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ √ó M ‚Üí P
is R-multilinear if it is R-linear in each factor, that is, if the function obtained
by arbitrarily Ô¨Åxing all but the i-th component is R-linear in the i-th factor, for
i = 1, . . . , r.
Again it is natural to ask whether R-multilinear maps may be turned into R-
linear maps: whether there exists an R-module M1 ‚äóR ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ ‚äóR M through which

to C, which is commutative.

Now we can impose further restrictions on œï and again study the corresponding
universal problems. Popular possibilities are
‚Ä¢ œï may be required to be symmetric; that is, for all œÉ ‚àà S and all m1, . . . , m ,
require that
œï(mœÉ(1) , . . . , mœÉ() ) = œï(m1 , . . . , m );
‚Ä¢ or œï may be required to be alternating; that is,
œï(m1, . . . , m ) = 0 whenever mi = mj for some i = j.
The reader may have expected the second prescription to read for all œÉ ‚àà S
and all m1, . . . , m , require that
œï(mœÉ(1), . . . , mœÉ() ) = (‚àí1)œÉ œï(m1 , . . . , m ).
The problem with this version is that if the base ring has characteristic 2, then
there would be no diÔ¨Äerence between symmetric and alternating maps. But behold
the following:
Lemma 4.2. Let œï : M  ‚Üí P be an R-multilinear function.
If œï is alternating, then for all œÉ ‚àà S , and all m1, . . . , m ,
œï(mœÉ(1), . . . , mœÉ() ) = (‚àí1)œÉ œï(m1 , . . . , m ).
If 2 is a unit in R, the converse holds as well.
Proof. For the Ô¨Årst statement, it suÔ¨Éces to show that interchanging any two fac-
tors switches the sign of an alternating function (since transpositions generate the
symmetric group). Since the other factors have no eÔ¨Äect on this operation, this
reduces the question to the case = 2. Therefore, we only have to show that
if œï(m, m) = 0 for all m ‚àà M , then
œï(m2, m1) = ‚àíœï(m1 , m2)
for all m1 , m2 ‚àà M . For this, use bilinearity to expand 0 = œï(m1 + m2 , m1 + m 2 ),
and again use the alternating condition.
For the second statement, again we can reduce to the = 2 case. For m1 =
m2 = m, œï(m2 , m1 ) = ‚àíœï(m1 , m2 ) says œï(m, m) = ‚àíœï(m, m); therefore
2œï(m, m) = 0.
If 2 is a unit in R, this implies œï(m, m) = 0, as needed.

Thus, alternating maps do satisfy the (perhaps) more natural prescription, and
this in fact characterizes alternating maps in most cases.

4.3. Very small detour: Graded algebra. We have obtained tensor, symmet-
ric, exterior powers of a module M for every nonnegative integer . As it happens,
in each case it is useful to consider ‚Äòall at once‚Äô. The structures we obtain by
doing this are particular cases of a very important notion, which would deserve
much more space than we can allow.
A graded ring is a (not necessarily commutative) ring S endowed with a de-
composition
!
S =
 Si
i‚â•0
of the abelian group (S, +) into a direct sum of abelian groups Si , for nonnegative
integers21 i, such that
Si ¬∑ Sj ‚äÜ Si+j .
This prescription is parsed as follows: identify Si with its image in S by the natural
map; nonzero elements of Si are called homogeneous, of degree i; the condition pre-
scribes that the product of two homogeneous elements of degree i, j is homogeneous,
of degree i + j (provided it is not 0).
Example 4.6. The polynomial ring R[x1 , . . . , xn ] over any ring R carries a natural
grading, given by the (ordinary) degree of polynomials. We may write
R[x1 , . . . , xn] = R ‚äï x1 , . . . , xn  ‚äï x21, x1 x2 , . . . , x2 n  ‚äï ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ .
 
20 I am not aware of a universally accepted notation to denote ‚Äòsymmetric‚Äô tensors; a sensible
choice would be a simple ¬∑, as in the special case of polynomial rings.
21 Of course, much more general gradings may be considered, but this is the case that will
occur in the applications in this section.
528
 VIII. Linear algebra, reprise
The reader should have no diÔ¨Éculty providing
 " the notions of graded modules
and algebras. For " example, a graded ring S =
 i Si is a graded algebra over a
graded ring R = i Ri if it carries an action of R (making it into a ‚Äòconventional‚Äô
R-algebra) and further Ri ¬∑ Sj ‚äÜ Si+j . In particular, if R = R0 (so that R is a
commutative ring viewed as a graded ring by ‚Äòconcentrating‚Äô it in degree 0), we are
just requiring each graded piece of S to be an R-module.
For a concrete example, the commutative ring R[x1 , . . . , xn ] is a graded R-
algebra, generated (as an algebra over R) by the piece of degree 1. This is an
important template for many interesting situations.
I cannot resist the temptation to mention another example from commutative
algebra:
Example 4.7. Let R be a commutative ring, and let I ‚äÜ R be an ideal. Then the
direct sum of R-modules
!
I  = R ‚äï I ‚äï I 2 ‚äï I 3 ‚äï ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑
‚â•0
has a natural ring structure, since I i ¬∑ I j ‚äÜ I i+j . The resulting graded R-algebra is
called the Rees algebra of I, Rees R (I), and is of fundamental importance in algebraic
geometry (as it translates into the notion of blow-up, a particularly important class
of regular maps). The brave reader will try to explore the Rees algebra of the
ideal I = (x, y) in the polynomial ring k[x, y] over a Ô¨Åeld k (Exercise 4.21), which
is the simplest interesting instance of this construction (well, the second simplest:
the simplest example may be Exercise III.5.17).
 
The additional information carried by the grading on a ring is very substan-
tial, and the theory of graded rings and algebras is extremely rich. For example,
projective algebraic geometry may be developed by using graded rings along the
same lines we sketched in ¬ßVII.2.3 for the aÔ¨Éne case. As I mentioned at the end of
¬ßVII.2.3, this provides a ‚Äòglobalization‚Äô procedure which leads to important techni-
cal advantages.
For the (very modest) purpose of this section, the following general remarks
are all we need.
Graded rings form a category under ring homomorphisms, but they form a
more interesting " one if we require
 " the homomorphisms to preserve the grading;
that is, if S =

i

phisms œï : S ‚Üí T such that œï(Si) ‚äÜ Ti . Such homomorphisms are called graded
homomorphisms. Of course, singling out a special class of homomorphisms will
then single out a special class of ideals, that is, those ideals which appear as kernels
of graded homomorphisms.

the following are equivalent:
(i) I is homogeneous;

Proof. (i) ‚áê‚áí (ii) is the very deÔ¨Ånition of homogeneous ideal; (ii) ‚áê‚áí (iii) is left
to the reader (Exercise 4.10).
(ii) =‚áí (iv): Assuming (ii) holds, deÔ¨Åne a grading on S/I by letting the piece
of degree i consist of (0 and) the cosets of the elements of degree i in S. This is well-
deÔ¨Åned by (ii), and it is immediately checked that it induces a graded ring structure
on S/I. The ideal I is then the kernel of the graded homomorphism S ‚Üí S/I,
verifying (iv).

Note that a graded homomorphism œï : S ‚Üí T induces a homomorphism of
abelian groups œïi : Si ‚Üí Ti for each i. The ideal ker œï is then the direct sum of all
ideals ker œïi .
Example 4.10. The ideal I = (y ‚àí x2) is not homogeneous in the ring k[x, y],
if this is given the grading by the usual degree: indeed, y ‚àí x2 ‚àà I while y ‚àà I,
contradicting condition (ii) of Lemma 4.9. The ideal (y, y ‚àí x2 ) is homogeneous,
since it equals (y, x2 ) and both y, x2 are homogeneous.
The conventional grading of a polynomial ring is not the only option: we could
decide to grade k[x, y] by placing constants in degree 0 and assigning degree 1 to
the indeterminate x and degree 2 to y. With such a grading, the ideal (y ‚àí x2 ) is
homogeneous.
 
Projective algebraic geometry (almost) follows the blueprint of aÔ¨Éne algebraic
geometry reviewed in ¬ßVII.2.3, but using only homogeneous ideals in k[x 1 , . . . , xn ]
(taken with the usual grading).

4.13. Let k be a Ô¨Åeld of characteristic zero. A diÔ¨Äerential operator in one variable,
with polynomial coeÔ¨Écients, is a linear combination
(*)
 a0 (x) + a1 (x)‚àÇx + ¬∑ ¬∑ ¬∑ + ar (x)‚àÇx r
where ai (x) ‚àà k[x]. Here x acts on a polynomial f (x) ‚àà k[x] by multiplication
by x, while ‚àÇx acts by taking a formal derivative (as in ¬ßVII.4.2). With the evident
operations, diÔ¨Äerential operators form a ring, called the (Ô¨Årst) Weyl algebra. Note
that the Weyl algebra is noncommutative: for example,
(x‚àÇx )f (x) = xf (x),
while
(‚àÇx x)f (x) = ‚àÇx (xf (x)) = f (x) + xf (x).
Therefore, (‚àÇxx ‚àí x‚àÇx )f (x) = f (x), or put otherwise
(**)
 ‚àÇx x ‚àí x‚àÇx = 1
in the Weyl algebra. Prove that the Weyl algebra is isomorphic to the quotient
T‚àó k (x, y)/(yx ‚àí xy ‚àí 1). (Use (**) to write any element of the Weyl algebra in the
form given in (*). Show that this representation is unique, and deduce that (**)
generates all relations among x and ‚àÇx . Then use the Ô¨Årst isomorphism theorem.)
The relation (**) expresses (up to a factor) the basic fact that in quantum
mechanics the position and momentum operators do not commute. The Weyl
algebra was introduced to study this phenomenon. Modules over rings of diÔ¨Äerential
operators are called D-modules.

5. Hom and duals
Our rapid overview of tensor products has occasionally brought us into close proxim-
ity with the Hom functors, and I will close this chapter by devoting some attention
to these functors. As in the case of tensors, we will be preoccupied with adjunc-
tion and exactness properties, since concrete computations depend heavily on these
properties. We will deal with these properties more carefully in the next (and last)
section; in this section we will concentrate on one important special case of Hom,
that is, the duality functor.
As in the rest of the chapter, R denotes a Ô¨Åxed commutative ring.
536
 VIII. Linear algebra, reprise
5.1. Adjunction again. The careful reader will agree that almost everything
we know about the tensor product follows from the fact that it is a left-adjoint
functor. This fact is spelled out in Lemma 2.4, whose Ô¨Çip side gives us just as much
information concerning the covariant Ô¨Çavor of the Hom functor:
L ‚Üí HomR-Mod(N, L).
Explicitly, the following is an exact restatement of Corollary 2.5:
Corollary 5.1. For every R-module N , the functor HomR (N, ) is right-adjoint
to the functor ‚äóR N .
What about the ‚Äòcontravariant‚Äô Ô¨Çavor of Hom:
M ‚Üí hN (M ) := HomR-Mod (M, N )
(this functor was discussed in general terms in ¬ß1.2)?
Proposition 5.2. For every R-module N , the functor HomR ( , N ) is right-adjoint
to itself.
This statement should be parsed carefully, because HomR( , N ) is a contravari-
ant functor. The statement of Proposition 5.2 may lead us astray into thinking that
HomR ( , N ) must also be its own left-adjoint, and this is not the case (indeed this
would make it a right-exact functor, and we will soon see that HomR ( , N ) is not
right-exact in general).
The point is that, by deÔ¨Ånition of contravariant functor, hN = HomR ( , N )
should be viewed as a covariant functor from the opposite category:
hN : R-Modop ‚Üí R-Mod;
it can also be viewed as a covariant functor to the opposite category,
hop

by simply reversing arrows after the fact rather than before. A more precise state-
ment of Proposition 5.2 is that
op

Proof. Let L, M , N denote R-modules. Recall (cf. the considerations preceding
Lemma 2.4) that R-bilinear maps

5.5. Duals and matrices; biduality. Even without the beneÔ¨Åt of full exactness,

of the dual of Œ±:
0

 / Rm Œ±‚à®
 / Rn .
Here I am applying isomorphisms (R m )‚à® ‚àº
 = Rm , (Rn )‚à® = ‚àº
