‚à®
The map Œ± is obtained by applying the functor HomR ( , R) to the map Œ±. Once
bases are chosen, Œ± is determined by an m √ó n matrix; likewise, Œ±‚à® corresponds
to an n √ó m matrix. What is the relation between these two matrices? Of course
there is only one sensible answer to this question, and it is correct:
Lemma 5.15. Let A be the matrix representing a linear map Œ± : Rn ‚Üí Rm
with respect to the standard bases. Then the dual map Œ±‚à® : (R m )‚à® ‚Üí (Rn )‚à® is
represented by the transpose of A with respect to the corresponding dual bases.
The (easy) veriÔ¨Åcation of this fact is left to the reader (Exercise 5.10). The
upshot is that the dual of the cokernel of a matrix A is the kernel of the transpose
of A.
Left-exactness of duality implies restrictions on which modules may appear as
duals of other modules:
Proposition 5.16. Let R be an integral domain, and let M be an R-module. Then
‚à®M is torsion-free.
Proof. There is a surjection R‚äïS  M , thus, an exact sequence
R‚äïT ‚Üí R‚äïS ‚Üí M ‚Üí 0.
Dualizing, M ‚à® is realized as the kernel of the induced map RS ‚Üí RT ; hence M ‚à®
may be identiÔ¨Åed with a submodule of a product RS . The latter has no nonzero
torsion, so neither does M ‚à® .
 
542
 VIII. Linear algebra, reprise
The diligent reader has proved this already in Exercise VI.4.2 and probably
by a more direct way than what we did just now, but never mind. In any case,
remember that dualizing kills torsion. Practice this by working out Exercise 5.11.
Even if not every module is the dual of a module, we may wonder whether an
arbitrarily given module M has some relation with a suitably chosen dual, and this
is indeed the case: there is a canonical map to the ‚Äòdouble-dual‚Äô,
œâ :
 M ‚Üí M ‚à®‚à® .
To deÔ¨Åne this, consider the R-bilinear map
M ‚à® √ó M ‚Üí R
sending (f, m) to f (m). For every m ‚àà M , we get23 an R-linear map M ‚à® ‚Üí R,
f ‚Üí f (m), in other words, an element œâ(m) of M ‚à®‚à® . It is immediately checked
that œâ is R-linear.
‚à®‚à®By Proposition 5.16, M is torsion-free. In fact, the double-dual construction
is a standard way to ‚Äòclean up‚Äô a module, removing its torsion.
The map œâ is interesting even when torsion is not an issue. As we have seen
in Corollary 5.7, if F is a Ô¨Ånite-rank free R-module, then a choice of basis for F
determines an isomorphism F = ‚àº
 F ‚à® ; but this does indeed depend on the basis, so
it is not a canonical isomorphism. Doing it twice,
F ‚àº
 = F ‚à® ‚àº
 = F ‚à®‚à® ,
may seem a priori bound to make the situation even worse‚Äîsurely, if we com-
pose two noncanonical maps, then we cannot expect to get something canonical,
right? Well, we do in this case, since this composition is nothing but the map œâ
(Exercise 5.13) and œâ knows nothing about choices of bases or other such ambigui-
ties. Therefore, our slogan admits the following addendum: Ô¨Ånite-rank free modules
are noncanonically isomorphic to their duals and canonically isomorphic to their
double-duals.
In terms of matrices (and keeping in mind that duality is exact on free modules)
this is perhaps not too surprising. After all, taking the transpose of a matrix twice
simply gives the matrix back; thus, even if making sense of the homomorphism
corresponding to the transpose of a matrix may depend on the choice of a basis,
surely such a choice is inessential if the transpose is taken twice.
Incidentally, a module M is reÔ¨Çexive if the corresponding map œâ is an iso-
morphism. Thus, reÔ¨Çexive modules are necessarily torsion-free and Ô¨Ånite-rank free
modules are reÔ¨Çexive.

following:
‚ÄîDuality is a contravariant, exact functor on k-Vect.

6. Projective and injective modules and the Ext functors
We have seen in ¬ß5.1 that the Hom functors are left-exact, and not exact in general.
Studying this matter further leads us along a path parallel to the one traveled in
the case of tensor products. In that case, we singled out a class of special (‚ÄòÔ¨Çat‚Äô)
modules for which ‚äó is exact, and we discovered that the lack of exactness of tensor
products may be precisely measured in terms of a whole collection of new (‚ÄòTor‚Äô)
functors. Precisely the same situation occurs for the covariant and contravariant
Ô¨Çavors of Hom, and I will close the chapter by summarizing this story.
This brief overview should suÔ¨Éce for standard applications the reader may
encounter. I am also hoping that it will serve as motivation for the next chapter: the
wonderful facts that I will present here without proof (on Ext functors, analogous to
the facts about Tor that were left without proof in ¬ß2.4) could be proven in detail
by ad hoc methods, without too much eÔ¨Äort; but they Ô¨Ånd their most natural
setting in the wider apparatus of homological algebra and were in fact at the root
26 The matter is not moot: it is true that there is a bijection between PV and Gr(n ‚àí 1, V ),
but this bijection depends on the choice of a basis. Thus we cannot simply identify PV and
Gr(n ‚àí 1, V ). We can identify PV and Gr(n ‚àí 1, V ‚à® ), and it is sometime useful to do so.
546
 VIII. Linear algebra, reprise
of its development. The reader who bears with us throughout Chapter IX will see
complete proofs of all the facts advertised here and substantially more.
As in the rest of the chapter, R will be a commutative ring. As elsewhere, the
hypothesis of commutativity is essentially unnecessary; it has the convenient conse-
quence that the objects deÔ¨Åned below (such as ExtiR (M, N ) for two R-modules M
and N ) are naturally endowed with an R-module structure. If R is not necessarily
commutative, they are ‚Äòjust‚Äô abelian groups.
6.1. Projectives and injectives.
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.1. Let R be a (commutative) ring.
‚Ä¢ An R-module P is projective if the functor HomR (P, ) is exact.
‚Ä¢ An R-module Q is injective if the functor HomR ( , Q) is exact.

reader (Exercise 6.1).
6.2. Projective modules. Lemma 6.2 does not necessarily help in acquiring a
feeling for projective or injective modules. I am reasonably happy with projective
modules, because of the following characterization:

of a free module, that is, if and only if there exists a free R-module F , an R-
module K, and an isomorphism K ‚äï P ‚àº

For example, free modules are projective. Proposition 6.4 streamlines the ver-
iÔ¨Åcation of simple properties of projective modules:
Corollary 6.5. Let P1 , P2 be projective R-modules. Then P1 ‚äï P2 and P1 ‚äóR P2
are projective. Projective modules are Ô¨Çat.
Proof. These statements follow easily from Proposition 6.4, the fact that ‚äó is
distributive with respect to ‚äï, and the fact that free modules are Ô¨Çat.
 
The categorically minded reader should look for alternate proofs of these facts.
For example, if P1 and P2 are both projective, the functor HomR(P1 , HomR (P2, ))
is exact; by adjunction, this is the functor HomR (P1 ‚äóR P2 , ); since this is exact,
P 1 ‚äóR P2 is projective.
A proof via adjunction of the fact that projective modules are Ô¨Çat is straightfor-
ward (Exercise 6.7), using the (nontrivial) fact that R-Mod has enough injectives,
discussed below. As it happens, Ô¨Ånitely generated Ô¨Çat modules over, e.g., Noether-
ian rings, are projective; the diligent reader has already done all the work necessary
to prove this, and will wrap it up in Exercise 6.12.
The fact that free modules are projective implies that R-Mod has enough pro-
jectives: this means that every R-module M admits a projective resolution, that is,
an exact sequence
¬∑¬∑¬∑
 / P3
 / P2
 / P1
 / P0
 / M .
Indeed, a free resolution of M is in particular a projective resolution. Since there
are in general ‚Äòmore‚Äô projective modules than free modules, it can in principle be
‚Äòeasier‚Äô to construct a projective resolution. The magic of homological algebra shows
that projective resolutions may be used in place of free resolutions in (for example)
computing Tor-modules. This will be clear once we go through some homological
algebra machinery, in Chapter IX, especially ¬ßIX.8.
6.3. Injective modules. I may have inadvertently communicated to the reader
the message that every categorical notion leads in a straightforward way to a mirror
notion, by ‚Äòjust reversing arrows‚Äô. This would seem to be the case for injective
vs. projective modules: the deÔ¨Ånition of one kind is indeed obtained from the
deÔ¨Ånition of the other by simply reversing arrows in the key diagrams.
However, this is as far as this naive perception can go. A seemingly unavoidable
asymmetry of nature manifests itself here, making injective modules look more
mysterious than projectives. For example, there is no ‚Äòeasy‚Äô description of injective
modules in the style of Proposition 6.4 (however, cf. Remark 6.13), and while it is
trivially the case that an arbitrary module is surjected upon by a projective (e.g.,
free) module, the ‚Äòmirror‚Äô statement for injectives is certainly not trivial.
6. Projective and injective modules and the Ext functors

This criterion is attributed to Reinhold Baer, who Ô¨Årst introduced and studied
injective modules (ca. 1940).

Theorem 6.6 completely clariÔ¨Åes the notion of injective modules in the case of
PIDs. An R-module D is divisible if rD = D for every non-zero-divisor r ‚àà R, that
is, if we can ‚Äòdivide‚Äô every element of D by every non-zero-divisor of R.
Corollary 6.7. Let R be a PID. Then an R-module Q is injective if and only if it
is divisible.
Proof. Exercise 6.14.
 
Example 6.8. Viewed as abelian groups (i.e., Z-modules), Q and Q/Z are injective.
More generally, if D is any divisible abelian group and K ‚äÜ D, then D/K is
injective. (Indeed, it is trivially divisible!)
 
Going from friendly PIDs like Z to arbitrary rings may still seem challenging,
but here is where some of our previous work pays oÔ¨Ä. Recall that for every ring
homomorphism f : S ‚Üí R we have deÔ¨Åned in ¬ß3.3 a functor27 f ! : S-Mod ‚Üí R-Mod,
by setting f ! (M ) = HomS (R, M ). This functor preserves injectives:
Lemma 6.9. Let f : S ‚Üí R be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and let Q
be an injective S-module. Then f ! (Q) is an injective R-module.
Remark 6.10. Similarly, f ‚àó preserves projectives (Exercise 6.6).
 
Proof. By adjunction (Lemma 3.5),
HomR ( , f ! (Q)) ‚àº

as functors R-Mod ‚Üí Ab. Since f‚àó is exact (Proposition 3.6) and HomS ( , Q) is
exact by hypothesis, HomR ( , f ! (Q)) must be exact. This is precisely the statement.

Since every ring R admits a (unique) map Œπ : Z ‚Üí R, we have a good source of
injective objects in R-Mod for every R:
Corollary 6.11. Let R be a commutative ring. If D is any divisible abelian group,
then Œπ!(D) = HomZ (R, D) is injective in R-Mod.

The fact that R-Mod has enough injectives has a generalization to sheaves,

important tool in modern algebraic geometry.
Remark 6.13. Corollary 6.12 may be reÔ¨Åned, proving that every module can in
fact be realized as a submodule of a product HomZ (R, Q/Z)S (this is injective, by
Corollary 6.11 and Exercise 6.15). That is, products of Œπ! (Q/Z) = HomZ (R, Q/Z)
play for injectives the role played by free modules (= coproducts of R = Œπ‚àó (Z))
for projectives. (Not surprisingly, they are said to be cofree.) It follows that every
injective R-module is a direct summand of HomZ (R, Q/Z)S for some S.

Chapter IX
Homological algebra
The limited exposure to the Tor and Ext functors given in ¬ßVIII.2 and ¬ßVIII.6
should assist the reader in occasional encounters with these functors. However, it is
very worthwhile to go back, dotting all i‚Äôs and crossing all t‚Äôs as necessary to prove
the wonderful facts responsible for the existence and the behavior of these functors.
This is one motivation for the material covered in this chapter. Tor and Ext
are examples of derived functors, a machinery that would give us access to several
other important constructions, such as group or sheaf cohomology. Exploring any of
these applications would take us too far, but understanding the basics of homological
algebra, including a thorough look at derived functors, is in itself a very worthwhile
goal. The material covered here should arm the reader with enough information to
be able to absorb any application quickly when the time comes and the opportunity
arises. All the wonderful mysteries about Tor and Ext stated in Chapter VIII will
be recovered as very particular cases of the results presented in this chapter.
The natural context in which to play this game is that of abelian categories:
these are categories carrying good notions of kernels and cokernels, with properties
to which the reader is accustomed due to long exposure to the category R-Mod
of modules over a ring. In fact, elementary (and not so elementary) presenta-
tions of homological algebra often take the standpoint that one may as well work
with R-Mod; allegedly, nothing of substance is lost by doing this rather than trying
to work in the more natural context of abelian categories.
I am not completely convinced. This pedagogical device has strong theoretical
backing, provided by the Freyd-Mitchell embedding theorem: every small abelian
category is equivalent to a full subcategory of the category of left-modules over
a (not necessarily commutative) ring. However, I believe that some exposure to
purely ‚Äòarrow-theoretic‚Äô arguments is intrinsically beneÔ¨Åcial, and the reader should
have the chance to acquire a taste for this type of reasoning. In any case, I dislike
the idea of simply asking the reader to believe that ‚Äòmost of what follows works in
a more general setting‚Äô, without providing some evidence for this fact.
559
560
 IX. Homological algebra
Therefore, we will take a little time to look at abelian categories before getting
into homological algebra proper; this will be done in the Ô¨Årst two sections of this
chapter, which an impatient reader could probably skip over without much harm.
While we will not prove the Freyd-Mitchell embedding theorem, I will give a solid
justiÔ¨Åcation for the fact that we can indeed perform diagram chases by working with
‚Äòelements‚Äô, in the sense of (for example) checking whether a diagram is commutative
or whether a given sequence is exact. Proving this will give us good practice in
maneuvering arrow-theoretic arguments; once we are done with it, we can indeed
develop homological algebra using conventional ‚Äòelement-theoretic‚Äô arguments, with
a relatively clean conscience.
As for homological algebra proper, my general guiding principle will be to hunt
for the ‚Äòessence of (co)homology‚Äô: what information in a complex (see ¬ßIII.7) is
really responsible for its homology. This will motivate most constructions in this
chapter, including derived functors. A guiding beacon throughout the chapter will
be the notion of derived category: I will not construct derived categories in full
generality, but the reader should nevertheless emerge with a basic understanding
of what they are. One reason for this compromise is that I felt that delving into
triangulated categories would take us too far; they are only mentioned in passing
at the end of the chapter. Balancing Tor and Ext will motivate the introduction
of double complexes, and these in turn will motivate a quick treatment of spectral
sequences, in the last section.
1. (Un)necessary categorical preliminaries
1.1. Undesirable features of otherwise reasonable categories. The cate-
gory of modules over a ring R is the template example of an abelian category: a
category for which it makes sense to talk about ‚Äòkernels‚Äô and ‚Äòcokernels‚Äô, ‚Äòcom-
plexes‚Äô, ‚Äòexactness‚Äô, etc., and in which convenient results such as the ‚Äòsnake lemma‚Äô
hold. We begin by trying to concretize these thoughts a little.
As motivation for the key deÔ¨Ånitions, I will Ô¨Årst list a few annoying features of
some of the categories we have encountered.
‚Ä¢ A category may not have initial or Ô¨Ånal objects; even if they are there, they
may not be the same objects (as in Set).
‚Ä¢ Products, coproducts may not exist (if the product of F2 and F3 existed in Fld,
what would its characteristic be?).
‚Ä¢ It may make no sense to talk about kernels and cokernels in a category; even
when it does make sense, they may not exist (kernels do not necessarily exist
in the category of Ô¨Ånitely generated modules over a ring; cf. Example III.6.5).
‚Ä¢ Even when there are kernels and cokernels, monomorphisms need not be ker-
nels (nonnormal subgroups are not kernels: remember ¬ßII.7.6), and epimor-
phisms need not be cokernels (the injection Z ‚Üí Q is an epimorphism in Ring;
see ¬ßIII.2.3).
‚Ä¢ A morphism may be a monomorphism and an epimorphism without being an
isomorphism (Ring again, same example).
1. (Un)necessary categorical preliminaries
 561
Homological algebra will deal extensively with kernels, cokernels, exact sequences,
and the like: the material covered in ¬ßIII.7 will be our starting point. That material
was developed for the category R-Mod of (left-)modules over a ring, for which
none of the annoying things listed above occurs: in R-Mod every epimorphism is
a cokernel, a map is an isomorphism if and only if it is a monomorphism and an
epimorphism, there is a zero-object (= both initial and Ô¨Ånal), and so on.
The notions of ‚Äòadditive‚Äô and ‚Äòabelian‚Äô category extract the few key properties
that guarantee that no pathologies such as those listed above may occur. Working
in such categories ‚Äòfeels‚Äô quite a bit like working in R-Mod, in the sense that all
expected properties of kernels and cokernels hold. The Freyd-Mitchell embedding
theorem (see ¬ß2.4) will provide a mathematical reason supporting this psychological
fact.
1.2. Additive categories.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.1. A category A is additive if it has a zero-object, A has both Ô¨Ånite
products and Ô¨Ånite coproducts, and each set of morphisms HomA(A, B) is endowed
with an abelian group structure, in such a way that the composition maps are
bilinear. A functor between two additive categories is additive if it preserves the
abelian group structures on Hom-sets.
 
None of the categories mentioned in the list in ¬ß1.1 is additive. Even Grp (which
has kernels and cokernels, products, etc.) is not an additive category: the Hom-sets
in Grp do not have a natural abelian group structure. Of course Ab is additive, and
so are all categories R-Mod.
If a category A is additive, it makes sense to talk about ‚Äòzero-morphisms‚Äô (which
I will denote by 0) and to add or subtract morphisms; two morphisms Œ±, Œ≤ are equal
if and only if Œ±‚àíŒ≤ = 0. One can also talk about kernels and cokernels in A, by adopt-
ing the categorical deÔ¨Ånitions as suitable limits and colimits (Example VIII.1.11).
Here are those deÔ¨Ånitions again, expanded for intelligibility:
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.2. Let œï : A ‚Üí B be a morphism in an additive category A. A
morphism Œπ : K ‚Üí A is a kernel of œï if œï ‚ó¶ Œπ = 0 and for all morphisms Œ∂ : Z ‚Üí A
such that œï ‚ó¶ Œ∂ = 0 there exists a unique Œ∂ÃÉ : Z ‚Üí K making the diagram

morphisms Œ≤ : B ‚Üí Z such that Œ≤ ‚ó¶ œï = 0 there exists a unique Œ≤ÃÉ : C ‚Üí Z making

 IX. Homological algebra
Here is the same in sound-bite format:
If œï ‚ó¶ Œ∂ = 0, then Œ∂ factors uniquely through ker œï.
If Œ≤ ‚ó¶ œï = 0, then Œ≤ factors uniquely through coker œï.
We have to get used to the fact that morphisms may have ‚Äòmany‚Äô kernels (or
cokernels), uniquely identiÔ¨Åed with each other by virtue of being answers to a
universal question (Proposition I.5.4); it is common to harmlessly abuse language
and talk about the kernel and the cokernel of a morphism. Further, we have to get
used to the fact that the kernel Œπ : K ‚Üí A of a morphism A ‚Üí B is a morphism. In
a category such as Ab we are used to thinking of the kernel as a subobject of A, but
this is really nothing but the datum of an inclusion map: the kernel is really that
map. In an arbitrary category one cannot talk about ‚Äòsubobjects‚Äô or ‚Äòinclusions‚Äô;
the closest one can get to these notions is monomorphism. Similarly, ‚Äòsurjective‚Äô is
not really an option; epimorphism is the appropriate replacement. Recall (¬ßI.2.6)
that œï : A ‚Üí B is a monomorphism if for all parallel morphisms Œ∂1 , Œ∂2 : Z ‚Üí A,
Z

Œ∂2
œï ‚ó¶ Œ∂1 = œï ‚ó¶ Œ∂2 =‚áí Œ∂ 1 = Œ∂2 . It is an epimorphism if for all parallel Œ≤1 , Œ≤2 : B ‚Üí Z,
A

Œ≤2
Œ≤1 ‚ó¶ œï = Œ≤2 ‚ó¶ œï =‚áí Œ≤1 = Œ≤2 . One beneÔ¨Åt of working in an additive category is
that these deÔ¨Ånitions simplify a little:
Lemma 1.3. A morphism œï : A ‚Üí B in an additive category is a monomorphism
if and only if for all Œ∂ : Z ‚Üí A,
œï ‚ó¶ Œ∂ = 0 =‚áí Œ∂ = 0.
It is an epimorphism if and only if for all Œ≤ : B ‚Üí Z,
Œ≤ ‚ó¶ œï = 0 =‚áí Œ≤ = 0.
Proof. This is simply because two morphisms with the same source and target
are equal if and only if their diÔ¨Äerence in the corresponding Hom-set (which is an
abelian group by hypothesis) is 0.
 
Are kernels necessarily monomorphisms? Yes:
Lemma 1.4. In any additive category, kernels are monomorphisms and cokernels
are epimorphisms.
We will run into several such ‚Äòdual‚Äô statements, and I will give the proof for
one half, leaving the other half to the reader; as a rule, the two proofs mirror each
other closely. There is a good reason for this: the opposite (cf. Exercise I.3.1) of
an additive category is additive, and kernels, monomorphisms, etc., in one corre-
spond to cokernels, epimorphisms, etc., in the other. Thus, proving one of these
statements for all additive categories establishes at the same time the truth of its
dual statement.
1. (Un)necessary categorical preliminaries
 563
However, going through the motions necessary to produce stand-alone proofs
of the dual statements makes for good practice, and I invite the reader to work
out the corresponding exercises at the end of this section. It is invariably the case
that these arguments are relatively easy to understand and picture in one‚Äôs mind
but rather awkward to write down precisely. This seems to be a feature of many
arrow-theoretic arguments and probably reÔ¨Çects inveterate biases acquired by early
exposure to Set.

The proof that kernels are monomorphisms is analogous and is left to the reader
(Exercise 1.9).
 
Certain limits are guaranteed to exist in an additive category: Ô¨Ånite products
and coproducts do. On the other hand, kernels and cokernels (which are also limits)
do not necessarily exist in an additive category. For example, the category of Ô¨Ånitely
generated modules over a ring is additive, but it does not have kernels in general
(essentially because a submodule of a Ô¨Ånitely generated module is not necessarily
Ô¨Ånitely generated). But as soon as a morphism œï does have kernels or cokernels
in an additive category, the basic qualities of that morphism can be detected ‚Äòas
usual‚Äô by looking at ker œï and coker œï. This is the case for monomorphisms and
epimorphisms:
Lemma 1.5. Let œï : A ‚Üí B be a morphism in an additive category. Then œï is a
monomorphism if and only if 0 ‚Üí A is its kernel, and œï is an epimorphism if and
only if B ‚Üí 0 is its cokernel.
Compare this statement with Proposition III.6.2!
Proof. Let‚Äôs do kernels this time.

such that the composition Z ‚Üí A ‚Üí B is 0, then Œ∂ is 0 by Lemma 1.3, and in
particular Œ∂ factors (uniquely) through 0 ‚Üí A. This proves that 0 ‚Üí A is a kernel
of œï, as stated.
Conversely, assume that 0 ‚Üí A is a kernel for œï : A ‚Üí B, and let Œ∂ : Z ‚Üí A

The statement about epimorphisms and cokernels is left to the reader (Exer-

In view of Lemma 1.5, we should be able to use diagrams
0 ‚Üí A ‚Üí B,

to signal that A ‚Üí B is a monomorphism, resp., an epimorphism: think ‚Äòexact‚Äô.
However, the fact that kernels and cokernels do not necessarily exist makes talking
about exactness problematic in a category that is ‚Äòonly‚Äô additive. This situation
will be rectiÔ¨Åed very soon.
Incidentally, it is common to denote monomorphisms and epimorphisms by
suitably decorated arrows; popular choices are / / and
 / / , respectively.
1.3. Abelian categories. The moral at this point is that if a morphism in an
additive category has kernels and cokernels, then these will behave as expected. But
kernels and cokernels do not necessarily exist, and this prevents us from going much
further. Also, while (as we have seen) kernels are monomorphisms and cokernels are
epimorphisms in an additive category, there is no guarantee that monomorphisms
should necessarily be kernels and epimorphisms should be cokernels. In the end,
we simply demand these additional features explicitly.
DeÔ¨Ånition 1.6. An additive category A is abelian if kernels and cokernels exist
in A; every monomorphism is the kernel of some morphism; and every epimorphism
is the cokernel of some morphism.
 
As mentioned already, R-Mod is an abelian category, for every ring R. The
prototype of an abelian category is Ab: this1 is why these categories are called
abelian.
Since kernels are necessarily monomorphisms (by Lemma 1.4), we see that in
an abelian category we can adopt a mantra entirely analogous to the useful ‚Äòkernel
‚áê‚áí submodule‚Äô of ¬ßIII.5.3 vintage: in abelian categories, the slogan would be
‚Äòkernel ‚áê‚áí monomorphism‚Äô (and similarly for cokernels vs. epimorphisms).
Remark 1.7. Just as it is convenient to think of monomorphisms A / / B as deÔ¨Ån-
ing A as a ‚Äòsubobject‚Äô of B, it is occasionally convenient to think of epimorphisms
as ‚Äòquotients‚Äô: if œï : A / / B is a monomorphism, we can use B/A to denote (the
target of) coker œï. We will have no real use for this notation in this section, but it
will come in handy later on.
 
1There is nothing particularly ‚Äòcommutative‚Äô about an abelian category.

The very existence of kernels and cokernels links these two notions tightly in
an abelian category:
Lemma 1.8. In an abelian category A, every kernel is the kernel of its cokernel;
every cokernel is the cokernel of its kernel.
Proof. I will prove the second half and leave the Ô¨Årst half to the reader (Exer-
cise 1.9).

Putting together Lemma 1.8 and Lemma 1.5, we can rephrase DeÔ¨Ånition 1.6
by listing the following requirements on a category A:
‚Ä¢ A is additive;
‚Ä¢ kernels and cokernels exist in A;
‚Ä¢ if œï : A ‚Üí B is a morphism whose kernel is 0, then œï is the kernel of its
cokernel;
‚Ä¢ if œà : B ‚Üí C is a morphism whose cokernel is 0, then œà is the cokernel of its
kernel.

1. (Un)necessary categorical preliminaries
 567
The reader should note the arrow-theoretic nature of this argument. In the
category R-Mod we could have given the following (possibly) simpler argument:
by Proposition III.6.2, monomorphisms are injective and epimorphisms are surjec-
tive; therefore œï is bijective, and bijective homomorphisms are isomorphisms by
Exercise III.5.12. Such set-theoretic arguments are not an option in an arbitrary
abelian category (at least until we develop the material of ¬ß2), since the objects of
an abelian category are not given as ‚Äòsets‚Äô. Judicious use of appropriate universal
properties accomplishes the same goal and may be argued to convey a ‚Äòdeeper‚Äô sense
of why the proven statement is true.
1.4. Products, coproducts, and direct sums. I will denote2 the product of
two objects A, B of an abelian category A by A √ó B, and I will denote their
coproduct by A  B. Both exist, since A is additive.
The presence of these objects, and of kernels and cokernels, gives us access to
other interesting constructions.

1.5. Images; canonical decomposition of morphisms. Yet another example
of the application of an arrow-theoretic perspective is the existence of ‚Äòcanonical
decompositions‚Äô of morphisms in any abelian category, in terms completely anal-
ogous to the decompositions studied in Set (¬ßI.2.8), Grp (¬ßII.8.1), Ring (¬ßIII.3.3),
R-Mod (¬ßIII.5.4). In each of these previous examples we could simply argue that
the decomposition was the original decomposition obtained in Set, enriched as the
case may be with additional structures adapted to the category under examination.
This is not an option in the context of an abelian category, and yet the decomposi-
tion holds just as well: every morphism may be written as an epimorphism, followed
by an isomorphism, followed by a monomorphism. Here is the precise statement:
Theorem 1.13. Every morphism œï : A ‚Üí B in an abelian category A may be
decomposed as
œï
A

cokernel of œï. The induced morphism œïÃÉ : C ‚Üí K is uniquely determined and is an
isomorphism.

statement with its set-theoretic counterpart suggests that

image of œï, denoted im œï, is ker(coker œï). The coimage of œï, denoted coim œï, is
coker(ker œï).

of im œï is only deÔ¨Åned up to (unique) isomorphism, as is every solution to a universal
problem.) As for the second deÔ¨Ånition, the reader will formulate the universal
property satisÔ¨Åed by coim œï (Exercise 1.19). With the convention mentioned in
Remark 1.7 and allowing for some abuse of language, the (target of the) coimage
of œï : A ‚Üí B is the ‚Äòquotient‚Äô A/ ker œï.

Therefore, K  ‚Üí B is a monomorphism through which œï factors and ‚Äòpreceding‚Äô
im œï : K ‚Üí B. Since the image of œï is initial among such morphisms (as proven in
Lemma 1.14), necessarily im œï is an isomorphism. It follows that 0 = coker im œï =
coker œï, proving that œï is an epimorphism by Lemma 1.5.

epimorphisms and monomorphisms factoring a given morphism œï in an abelian
category:

We now see that this amounts to the statement that in an abelian category, there
is an induced isomorphism linking the coimage and the image of every morphism.
Proof of Theorem 1.13. A unique morphism œà : K ‚Üí C making the above
diagram commute exists, by the universal property of im œï (Lemma 1.14), as well
as by the universal property of coim œï. Since œï ‚ó¶ œà = im œï is a monomorphism,
so is œà. Since œà ‚ó¶ œï = coim œï is an epimorphism, so is œà. It follows that œà is an
isomorphism (Lemma 1.9), and letting œïÃÉ : C ‚Üí K be the inverse of œà concludes
the proof.
 
Loosely speaking, we can say that, in an abelian category, ‚Äòimage and coimage
are naturally isomorphic‚Äô. This is imprecise, since image and coimage are them-
selves morphisms; the isomorphism holds between the target of coimage and the
source of image. Of course, for friendly categories such as R-Mod this distinction
is immaterial.

4 Watch out! Both presheaves C , C ‚àó satisfy the sheaf condition; cf. Exercise VIII.1.6. Sheaves
of abelian groups form a full subcategory of the category of presheaves, and this category is also
abelian, but the inclusion functor is in general not exact. For example, the exponential map
C ‚Üí C ‚àó considered above is an epimorphism of sheaves.
Also, for every open set U of T , the assignment F ‚Üí F (U ) deÔ¨Ånes a functor both on the
category of presheaves of abelian groups and on the category of sheaves of abelian groups. This
functor is exact on presheaves (this is a particular case of Exercise 1.11), but the example given
above shows that it is not exact in general on sheaves. This lack of exactness is responsible for
the existence of sheaf cohomology.

2. Working in abelian categories
2.1. Exactness in abelian categories. Now that we have a good notion of image
in any abelian category, we can formalize the meaning of ‚Äòexact sequence‚Äô. Consider
a sequence of objects and morphisms in an abelian category:
¬∑¬∑¬∑
 / A
 œï
 / B
 œà
 / C
 / ¬∑¬∑¬∑ .
The sequence is exact at B if
(1) œà ‚ó¶ œï = 0 and
(2) coker œï ‚ó¶ ker œà = 0.
2. Working in abelian categories
 577
The Ô¨Årst condition makes the sequence a ‚Äòcomplex‚Äô; it tells us that œï factors through
ker œà. As ker œà is a monomorphism, the universal property of images (Lemma 1.14)
yields a unique factorization of im œï through ker œà. Similarly, the second condition
tells us that ker œà factors through ker(coker œï) = im œï. This implies that im œï and
ker œà coincide. The conditions deÔ¨Åning exactness can therefore be summarized as
‚Ä¢ im œï = ker œà.
Of course this recovers precisely the condition used to deÔ¨Åne the notion of exactness
of a sequence of R-modules, in ¬ßIII.7.1. All expected elementary connotations of
exactness hold in the context of abelian categories as in R-Mod, as envisioned
already in ¬ß1.2‚Äì1.3. With notation as above, if the sequence is exact at B, then

œï is an epimorphism if and only if œà is the zero-morphism;

2. Working in abelian categories
 581
2.3. Working with ‚Äòelements‚Äô in a small abelian category. My personal
feeling is that arrow-theoretic arguments are invariably more insightful than their
element-theoretic counterparts, even if they are ‚Äòharder‚Äô as a rule. At the very
least, they are more elegant: for example, in the construction of the connecting
morphism Œ¥ just given above, there was no need to verify that the result was
‚Äòwell-deÔ¨Åned‚Äô, while this requires an argument in the set-theoretic construction in
¬ßIII.7.3. Such issues are usually resolved once and for all at the start, when the key
universal properties are established, and it is not necessary to belabor them later.
Still, it is true that element-theoretic arguments are usually easier to concoct.
Lemma 2.3 appears to be a good example: in terms of elements the statement
of this lemma is completely obvious, less so from the diagrammatic point of view.
Exercise 2.12 gives another example: providing a purely arrow-theoretic proof of the
innocent result stated there is not so easy, while this result is essentially immediate
from a more mundane viewpoint. The reader could wonder whether it may not ‚Äòbe
enough‚Äô to prove such facts‚Äîfor example, check the commutativity of a diagram
or the exactness of a sequence‚Äîby simpler element-theoretic considerations.
This is indeed the case. I will present a construction which would suÔ¨Éce for,
e.g., the purpose of completing the proof of the snake lemma by chasing elements
rather than arrows. I Ô¨Ånd this construction instructive and pretty, so I will take the
time to go through it in some detail. However, be warned that the Freyd-Mitchell
embedding theorem (discussed in ¬ß2.4) will obliterate the actual usefulness of the
construction, since it provides us with a much stronger result. Thus, I will ask the
reader to wade through some material, with the understanding that the result that
I will quote thereafter (Theorem 2.9) will make that material immediately obsolete.
My excuse is that while proving Theorem 2.9 is beyond the scope of this book, the
construction which I am about to describe is entirely within our reach, and good
propaganda for the philosophy underlying the functor of points; this is important
in, e.g., algebraic geometry and takes some practice to get used to. In the end, the
reader is likely to gain a better understanding of the situation by appreciating a
weaker result that we can prove entirely than by leaning on a stronger result to be
taken on faith. However, the hurried reader should feel free to skip this material
and jump directly to ¬ß2.4.
The idea is to deÔ¨Åne an appropriate notion of an ‚Äòelement‚Äô of an object of an
abelian category6. The reader should look back at the brief discussion at the end
of ¬ßVIII.1.2: the ‚Äòfunctor of points‚Äô hA = HomA ( , A) associates to every object Z
the set of morphisms Z ‚Üí A; in several categories, applying this functor to a
Ô¨Ånal object in A ‚Äòrecovers‚Äô A as a set. Working with Ô¨Ånal objects with this purpose
cannot work too well in categories with zero-objects (why?), but the idea underlying
the functor of points suggests that we look at morphisms
z : Z ‚Üí A
for a Ô¨Åxed object A in an abelian category A as (Z-Ô¨Çavored?!) ‚Äòelements‚Äô z of A.
If œï : A ‚Üí B is a morphism in A, it makes sense to talk about the ‚Äòelement‚Äô œï(z)
6I will systematically put quotes around this term, i.e., write ‚Äòelement‚Äô, to indicate that I am
referring to the relatively fancy notion I will introduce. The ‚Äòelements‚Äô will be ordinary elements
of an ordinary (pointed) set determined by the object of the category.
582
 IX. Homological algebra

is not right-exact, so it does not preserve epimorphisms.
Maybe surprisingly, there is a way to Ô¨Åx this problem.
As a preliminary note, recall that in the distant past (Example I.3.8) we con-
‚àósidered the category Set of ‚Äòpointed sets‚Äô, whose objects consist of sets endowed
with the choice of a distinguished point (here I will denote by 0 the distinguished
point). All the algebraic structures we have encountered are pointed sets, often
in more than one way: for example, stripping an abelian group of all its structure
except its supporting set and the choice of the identity element leaves us with a
pointed set (in other words, this deÔ¨Ånes an evident forgetful functor Ab ‚Üí Set‚àó ).
The category Set‚àó has a zero-object 0 (the singleton {0}); hence we can talk of
‚àó ‚àó‚Äòzero-morphisms‚Äô in Set . Further, the category Set has enough structure to make
sense of the notion of an ‚Äòexact sequence of pointed sets‚Äô: we can stipulate that
¬∑¬∑¬∑
 / S

is ‚Äòexact‚Äô at T if the (set-theoretic!) image of f equals the ‚Äòkernel‚Äô of g (that is, the
preimage g ‚àí1(0)). This notion should be taken with a grain of salt: while it is the
case that S ‚Üí T ‚Üí 0 is exact at T if and only if S ‚Üí T is surjective, it is not true
that 0 ‚Üí T ‚Üí U is exact at T in Set‚àó if and only if T ‚Üí U is injective (the only
requirement posed by the exactness of 0 ‚Üí T ‚Üí U is that the only element of T
mapping to the distinguished element in U is the distinguished element of T ; this
‚àóis weaker than injectivity). The category Set is, after all, not additive.
Now we are going to associate to every object A of a (small) abelian category A
a pointed set √Ç. This will in fact give a functor A ‚Üí Set‚àó with amazing properties:
a morphism will be a monomorphism, resp., an epimorphism, in A if and only if
2. Working in abelian categories
 583
the corresponding function of pointed sets is injective, resp., surjective; the (arrow-
theoretic) notions of kernel and image in A will correspond precisely to the (set-
‚àótheoretic) kernel and image in Set ; a sequence of objects in A will be exact if and
only if the corresponding sequence of pointed sets is exact; and it will be possible
‚àóto verify whether a diagram commutes in A by working in Set .
The upshot will be that in a sense we can chase a diagram in any small abelian
category by ‚Äòchasing elements‚Äô. For example, working with ‚Äòelements‚Äô suÔ¨Éces to
complete the proof of the snake lemma, in the sense that the exactness (in A) of the
sequence obtained with the aid of the connecting morphism deÔ¨Åned in ¬ß2.2 may be
veriÔ¨Åed by chasing ‚Äòelements‚Äô, since this veriÔ¨Åes exactness in Set‚àó . A proof in R-Mod
would transfer word-for-word to give a proof in any small7 abelian category. This
hints that the arrow-theoretic viewpoint that I have tried to defend in the past
several subsections, while elegant, is not really strictly necessary; I will come back
to this point in ¬ß2.4.
The construction can be given as an application of the abstract language devel-
oped in ¬ßVIII.1; but I promise that the resulting notion will be easy to contemplate,
independently of this language. The idea is simply to take the functor of points up
to a relation identifying the ‚Äòelement‚Äô Z ‚Üí A with all ‚Äòelements‚Äô W  Z ‚Üí A.
The following abstract nonsense will have a simple-minded explanation. I will
(often silently) assume that A is small, to steer clear of any possible set-theoretic
subtlety. It is convenient to consider a companion category A , with the same
objects as A but in which

of arrows; this is in order that the functor that I am about to deÔ¨Åne be covariant.

 
2.4. What is missing? Pretty as it is, the construction presented in the previous
section has several shortcomings:
‚Ä¢ The structure of ‚Äòpointed set‚Äô is less rigid than, say, that of an abelian group;
since Ab is itself an abelian category, it would be more natural to land in Ab
than Set‚àó , if possible10 .
‚Ä¢ While we can check that a diagram commutes in A by performing some com-
‚àóputation in Set (cf. the comments following Lemma 2.5), I have stopped short
of claiming that the functor A ‚Üí √Ç is faithful, that is, that it induces injective
functions HomA (A, B) ‚Üí HomSet ‚àó ( √Ç, BÃÇ) for all A, B. In fact, the functor is
not faithful (Exercise 2.10).
10 One way to do this would be to take the colimit in Ab rather than Set‚àó, but I will not
pursue this possibility here.
588
 IX. Homological algebra
‚Ä¢ The functor A ‚Üí Set‚àó is not full; that is, it is not surjective on Hom-sets. In
other words, there will be many functions of pointed sets that are not induced
by morphisms in A. Thus, we cannot use ‚Äòelements‚Äô to construct morphisms
in A: the arrow-theoretic work performed in ¬ß2.2 in order to construct the con-
necting morphism remains necessary (even if the properties of this morphism
can then be veriÔ¨Åed by using ‚Äòelements‚Äô).
Here another miracle occurs: it is in fact possible to construct fully faithful,
exact functors from any given (small) abelian category to a category of modules.
Here is the precise statement, which will be left without proof in this book:
Theorem 2.9 (Freyd-Mitchell theorem). Let A be a small abelian category. Then
there is a fully faithful, exact functor A ‚Üí R-Mod for a suitable ring R.
Caveat: In this statement R is not necessarily commutative; R-Mod denotes
the category of left-R-modules.
For a reminder on the full & faithful terminology, see DeÔ¨Ånition VIII.1.6.
BrieÔ¨Çy, a functor F : A ‚Üí C is fully faithful if it induces a bijection HomA (A, B) ‚Üí
HomC(F (A), F (B)) for all objects A, B in A. In practice, such a functor identiÔ¨Åes
A with a subcategory of C. The exactness (cf. Example VIII.1.18) of the functor
A ‚Üí R-Mod ensures that kernels and cokernels match in the two environments, and
so on (note that the functor is automatically faithfully exact; see Exercise 2.16):
everything we laboriously checked in ¬ß2.3 for our poor man‚Äôs functor A ‚Üí Set‚àó
holds for the much fancier functor provided by the Freyd-Mitchell theorem.
This functor is fully faithful: this means that one can in fact construct mor-
phisms in an arbitrary (small) abelian category by working with elements. Indeed,
this amounts to constructing the appropriate morphisms in the ambient category
R-Mod, and fullness guarantees that these morphisms ‚Äòalready‚Äô exist in A.
So, at the end of the day we are really allowed to forget all our arrow-theoretic
work. Element-theoretic considerations suÔ¨Éce, even for the purpose of constructing
morphisms such as the ‚Äòconnecting morphism‚Äô discussed in ¬ß2.2. Monomorphisms
really can be assimilated with ‚Äòsubobjects‚Äô, and ‚Äòquotients‚Äô in an abelian category
really are quotients in the ordinary sense.
This is impressive: it says that doing homological algebra in the category of
left-modules over a ring is essentially as general as doing it in an arbitrary (small)
abelian category, at least for what concerns constructions that do not require the
category to have special objects (such considerations will play a role in ¬ß5 and
following). In the rest of the chapter I will adopt the common compromise of dis-
cussing the basic notions of homological algebra in the context of abelian categories
while reverting back to element-theoretic considerations whenever this makes life
substantially easier. The Freyd-Mitchell theorem allows us to do this unapologeti-
cally, and often so would the simpler considerations of ¬ß2.3.

3. Complexes and homology, again

2.17. Upgrade the Yoneda lemma (Exercise VIII.1.10) to prove that every small
abelian category A is equivalent to a full subcategory of the category F of Exer-
cise 2.15, by means of the functor assigning to each object X in A the functor
hX = HomA ( , X).
Prove that this Yoneda embedding is left-exact and ‚ÄòreÔ¨Çects exactness‚Äô in the
sense that X ‚Üí Y ‚Üí Z is exact in A if the corresponding sequence hX ‚Üí hY ‚Üí hZ
is exact at hY (that is, if hX (A) ‚Üí hY (A) ‚Üí hZ (A) is exact at hY (A) for all A
in A).
This is the beginning of a proof of the Freyd-Mitchell theorem. Since each hX
is left-exact, the Yoneda embedding lands in the subcategory L of F consisting
of left-exact additive contravariant functors. It turns out that L is abelian in its
own right (although its embedding in F is not exact; thus, Exercise 2.14 doesn‚Äôt
help), and the Yoneda embedding of A in L is exact. Finally, one proves that L
is equivalent to the category of modules over a ring (the endomorphism ring of a
‚Äòfaithfully projective‚Äô object), and the Freyd-Mitchell theorem follows.
3. Complexes and homology, again
After all these preliminary considerations, it is time to begin thinking in earnest
about homological algebra. We will be working in a Ô¨Åxed abelian category A; by
what we have seen in ¬ß2, we are allowed to pretend that the objects of A are
ordinary modules over a ring, at least if A is small. Thus we will deal with objects
and morphisms with the aid of usual set-theoretic considerations12 . Until we get
to more sophisticated material, the reader will lose little or nothing by taking A to
be R-Mod for some ring13 R.
I will start oÔ¨Ä with a reminder of what we did in ¬ßIII.7.1. Once more, as we
now know, everything can in fact be done in the more general setting of an abelian
category.

Remark 3.1. In an abelian category, the deÔ¨Ånition of cohomology should be parsed

a good shorthand for this operation.

The following deÔ¨Ånition will undergo a few reÔ¨Ånements later in the chapter:
DeÔ¨Ånition 3.2. (Preliminary) A resolution of an object A is a complex whose
cohomology is concentrated in degree 0 and isomorphic to A.
 
To be more precise, the datum of a resolution of A should include a speciÔ¨Åc
isomorphism of the cohomology of the complex with A; a common abuse of language
glosses over this point. In fact, this isomorphism in cohomology should be induced
by a morphism at the level of complexes, from the given complex to the complex
called Œπ(A) later in this section. The notion of quasi-isomorphism (DeÔ¨Ånition 4.3)
will give us the correct framework in which to talk about resolutions precisely.
‚Ä¢It is common to assume that resolutions M are ‚Äòbounded‚Äô above or below,
that is, M i = 0 for i > 0 or i < 0. For example, a complex

3. Complexes and homology, again
 593

is a resolution of A, but not a very interesting one. Resolutions become useful if
the objects M i are ‚Äòspecial‚Äô in some sense (usually, A should not be expected to be
special): for example, in the context of modules over a ring we could require the
objects M i to be free, Ô¨Çat, projective, etc. We will then say that the resolution
itself is free, Ô¨Çat, projective, etc.
There are many contexts in which the following strategy produces interesting
invariants:
mathematical object

an associated (co)chain complex

the (co)homology of this complex
Both Ext and Tor are examples of this general plan of attack: start with two R-
modules M , N ; Ô¨Ånd (for example) a free resolution of M , and tensor it by N ,
obtaining a chain complex; then the homology of this complex computes the mod-
ules TorR
 i (M, N ) (cf. ¬ßVIII.2.4). Algebraic topology is a source of many other
examples and was in fact historically the main motivation driving the development
of homological algebra. In the 1950‚Äôs it became clear that this tool would be ex-
tremely useful in other Ô¨Åelds; for example, it had a tremendous impact on algebraic
geometry, through the work of Alexander Grothendieck14 and others.
Now consider this strategy carefully. In every single useful application, there are
in fact many ways to associate a complex to a mathematical object: in the example
of Tor recalled above, there are inÔ¨Ånitely many diÔ¨Äerent free resolutions of a given
module; I have in fact claimed (in ¬ßVIII.6.4) that resolutions by projective modules,
or even Ô¨Çat modules, would all yield the same Tor modules. In general, there is a
gigantic degree of freedom in the choice of the appropriate complex associated to
the given mathematical object, and one of our main goals here will be to show that
(for a large class of interesting examples) the (co)homology of the complex will not
depend on this choice.
The inquiring reader should not stop there, however. Precisely because of this
large ambiguity, the informational gap from the complex associated to a mathemati-
cal entity to its cohomology is large. Could one do better than ‚Äòtaking cohomology‚Äô ?
The natural approach would be to determine precisely where the ambiguity lies and
‚Äòmod out‚Äô the choice of a complex by this ambiguity. Whatever one gets from this,
it must be enough to determine the cohomology; but there may be a large amount
14 Grothendieck‚Äôs 1957 paper ‚ÄúSur quelques points d‚Äôalg√®bre homologique‚Äù was so inÔ¨Çuential
that you can just refer to it by naming the journal in which it appeared. If you say ‚ÄúThis is
proven in T√¥hoku‚Äù, without other qualiÔ¨Åers, most algebraic geometers will understand that you
mean this one paper of the thousands published in this journal in the past 50 years.
594
 IX. Homological algebra
of interesting information carried by ‚Äòcomplexes modulo ambiguity‚Äô which is lost
by going ‚Äòall the way down‚Äô to cohomology.
This is what we are up to: proving that the cohomology of the associated
complex is indeed independent of the choices, while keeping an eye out to detect
where the key information is really stored.

Lemma 3.3. C(A) is an abelian category.
I will leave to the reader the careful veriÔ¨Åcation of this fact (Exercise 3.3).
In broad terms, morphisms between two given complexes form an abelian group,
essentially because if Œ±i and Œ≤ i : M i ‚Üí N i are both collections of morphisms
making the appropriate diagram commute, so is the collection of their sums Œ±i +Œ≤ i .
Finite products and coproducts exist in C(A) as an easy consequence of the fact that
they do in A. As for kernels and cokernels, the commutativity of each square in (*)
and the universal properties of kernels and cokernels in A guarantee the existence
of morphisms making the larger diagrams

‚Ä¢ ‚Ä¢commute. The resulting sequences K , C are immediately checked to be com-
plexes, and the collections ker Œ±i : K i ‚Üí M i , coker Œ±i : N i ‚Üí C i give morphisms

and every epimorphism is a cokernel by using the fact that this is the case in A.
Thus, we have a way to concoct a new abelian category from an old one. The
new category C(A) is a natural starting place to study resolutions of objects in A,
and it comes with the same general package of tools available in every abelian
category: we can talk about exact sequences of complexes, we have a canonical
decomposition of morphisms of complexes, and so on. A sequence of complexes

Popular variations on the deÔ¨Ånition of C(A) require the complexes to be bounded
above or below:
‚Ä¢ C+ (A) denote the full16 subcategory of C(A) determined by complexes L‚Ä¢
which are bounded below, i.e., for which Li = 0 for i % 0.
‚Ä¢ C‚àí(A) likewise denotes the full subcategory of bounded-above complexes.
These are also abelian categories, and so are further variations such as C‚â•0 (A)
(complexes L‚Ä¢ for which L i = 0 for i < 0), C‚â§0 (A), and so on. These bounded
variants become unavoidable when dealing with resolutions.
It will also be necessary to consider categories of complexes in which the ob-
jects are restricted to belonging to a subcategory of A. We will devote particular
attention to the full subcategories P and I consisting of projective, resp., injective,
objects in A (see ¬ß5.3); thus, C‚â§0 (P), C‚â•0 (I) and other variations on the same
themes are fair game17 . In the generalities that follow I will only deal with C(A),
to keep notation under control. The reader should have no diÔ¨Éculty determining
what extends immediately to the diÔ¨Äerent variants of C(A) and what does not.

generalizing the sequence for H 0 and H 1 obtained in the snake lemma. The atten-
tive reader must have seen the next result coming all along:
Theorem 3.5 (Long exact cohomology sequence). The sequence determined as
above by a short exact sequence of complexes is an exact sequence.
The snake lemma is recovered as a particular case of this statement (or con-
versely, depending on your taste; cf. Exercise 3.10).
Proof. The proof is a diagram chase, which everyone should perform once by
oneself in his or her lifetime. So it is mostly left as an exercise for the reader
(Exercise 3.9). But I will stress the extent to which H i is exact ‚Äòon the nose‚Äô: part
of the claim in this theorem is that if
0
 / L‚Ä¢
 Œ±‚Ä¢

 Œ≤ ‚Ä¢

 / 0
is exact, then the sequence induced by functoriality of H i
0
 / H i (L‚Ä¢ )
 Œ±
 / H i (M ‚Ä¢ )
 Œ≤
 / H i (N ‚Ä¢ )
 / 0
is exact at H i (M ‚Ä¢ ). This sequence is not exact in general at H i (L‚Ä¢) and H i (N ‚Ä¢ );
the moral of the long exact cohomology sequence is that the failure of exactness
is precisely measured by other cohomology objects, by means of the connecting
morphisms.
Let‚Äôs check exactness at H i (M ). The sequence is a complex, simply because H i
is a functor; thus we only have to verify that ker Œ≤ ‚äÜ im Œ±. If m is a class in H i (M ‚Ä¢ )

Verifying the exactness at the other places is similarly uninspiring and is left
to the reader19 .

602
 IX. Homological algebra
This time the morphism marked +1 is the interesting connecting morphism (while
the morphisms in the cohomology complexes are zero; see the end of ¬ß3.2).
Thus, the ‚Äòlong exact cohomology sequence‚Äô takes us from a certain exact trian-
gle to another exact triangle. The Ô¨Årst triangle arises from a short exact sequence
of complexes (the ‚Äòconnecting morphism‚Äô is 0), while the second does not (the con-
necting morphism is in general nonzero).
Clearly something interesting is going on. Triangles obtained from short exact
sequences of complexes in C(A) appear to be ‚Äòspecial‚Äô and give rise to other exact
triangles through cohomology.
Actually, the situation may make the reader somewhat uncomfortable. While
two sides of the cohomology triangle are obtained by simply applying the coho-
mology functor to the corresponding sides of the original triangle, the third one is
obtained by a diÔ¨Äerent process, involving the connecting morphism. The reader
may feel that there should be a mechanism to go from a triangle deÔ¨Åned in terms
of an exact sequence to its counterpart in cohomology by simply applying the co-
homology functor.
This is indeed so. There is an important notion of ‚Äòdistinguished‚Äô triangle, in a
category K(A) closely associated with C(A) and that we will encounter later 21 (¬ß5).
As with the ‚Äòspecial‚Äô triangles given above, distinguished triangles lead to long exact
sequences in cohomology, and in a somewhat more direct fashion. Distinguished
triangles satisfy a number of axioms, which deÔ¨Åne K(A) as a triangulated category.
Exploring these concepts at any level of depth is well beyond the scope of this
book, but towards the end of the chapter I will try to clarify these last cryptic
remarks (¬ß9.2).

4. Cones and homotopies
If Œ±‚Ä¢ : L‚Ä¢ ‚Üí M ‚Ä¢ is a morphism of cochain complexes, it is natural to try to describe
the morphism induced in cohomology:
H ‚Ä¢ (Œ±‚Ä¢) : H ‚Ä¢ (L‚Ä¢ ) ‚Üí H ‚Ä¢(M ‚Ä¢ ).
‚Ä¢However, I have pointed out that H is not an exact functor, and this seems to
pose an obstacle to simple-minded strategies aimed at describing (for example) the
‚Ä¢ ‚Ä¢ ‚Ä¢kernel or cokernel of H (Œ± ) in terms of the kernel or cokernel of Œ± . The long
exact cohomology sequence may be used to compensate for this lack of exactness;
we will see how in ¬ß4.1. We will also begin exploring a very important condition
(‚Äòhomotopy‚Äô) guaranteeing that two given morphisms of complexes induce the same
morphism in cohomology. Homotopies will be so important that they will lead us
later on to change our notion of morphisms between complexes and construct a
new ‚Äòhomotopic category‚Äô of complexes (see ¬ß5).

Corollary 4.2. Let Œ±‚Ä¢ : L‚Ä¢ ‚Üí M ‚Ä¢ be a morphism of cochain complexes. Then
the induced morphism H ‚Ä¢ (L‚Ä¢) ‚Üí H ‚Ä¢ (M ‚Ä¢ ) is an isomorphism if and only if the
mapping cone M C(Œ±)‚Ä¢ is an exact complex.
(Cf. Exercise 3.14.)
I should mention that mapping cones arose in topology: the mapping cone of
a continuous map f : X ‚Üí Y is obtained by considering X √ó [0, 1], identifying
X √ó 0 to a point, and stitching X √ó 1 to Y by means of f . Analyzing chains of this
space leads to the (homological version of the) ‚Äòalgebraic‚Äô mapping cone considered
above.
4.2. Quasi-isomorphisms and derived categories. A component of our main
strategy consists of understanding when cochain complexes ‚Äòhave a good reason‚Äô
to have the same cohomology. Corollary 4.2 provides us with such a reason. The
morphisms singled out in that statement have a name:
DeÔ¨Ånition 4.3. A morphism Œ±‚Ä¢ of cochain complexes is a quasi-isomorphism if it
induces an isomorphism in cohomology.
 
By Corollary 4.2, this condition is equivalent to the exactness of the mapping
cone of Œ±‚Ä¢ .

gory A, as in DeÔ¨Ånition 3.2 and with M i = 0 for i > 0, is the same as the datum
of a quasi-isomorphism

Remark 4.5. Quasi-isomorphisms with Ô¨Åxed target (resp., source) form a category
in a natural way, which I will leave to the reader to make precise. In particular,
‚â§0 ‚â•0resolutions in C (A) (resp., C (A)) of a given object of A, as in Example 4.4,
form a category.
 
Wouldn‚Äôt it be nice if quasi-isomorphisms were actual isomorphisms, that is,
if they were invertible? In general, they are not. The following example will arise
several times to ward oÔ¨Ä any such hopes.
Example 4.6. In C(Ab), the morphism of complexes
¬∑¬∑¬∑

where œÄ is the natural projection, is a quasi-isomorphism, but it does not have an
inverse since there are no nontrivial homomorphisms Z/2Z ‚Üí ?
 Z.
 
In fact, this example shows that ‚Äòquasi-isomorphism‚Äô is not even an equivalence
relation (it is not symmetric). This is too bad‚Äîif it were, then the ‚Äòequivalence
class of a complex‚Äô would be the most natural candidate for the entity carrying the
maximal amount of cohomological information of a complex.
I will stop short of deÔ¨Åning a ‚Äòquasi-isomorphism relation‚Äô: in this book, quasi-
isomorphism is simply a particular quality of a morphism of cochain complexes, not
a relation. In any case recall that, even in the context of sets, taking the quotient
by an equivalence relation is not the ‚Äòprimary‚Äô object of interest: the quotient is
just the solution to the natural universal problem of studying functions to other
sets with identical behavior on ‚Äòequivalent‚Äô elements. This is the primary objective.
4. Cones and homotopies
 609
Similarly, the primary objective in the present context is not so much to identify
together all complexes linked by a quasi-isomorphism, as it is to be able to shuttle
information back and forth between such complexes. The natural way to accomplish
this is to study environments in which quasi-isomorphisms do have inverses. More
precisely, it consists of studying additive functors
F : C(A) ‚àí‚Üí D
such that F (œÅ‚Ä¢
 ) is an isomorphism for every quasi-isomorphism œÅ‚Ä¢ . (Cohomology
is such a functor.)
This sounds like yet another universal problem, and the natural line of attack
would be to solve it as such, that is, to deÔ¨Åne (if possible) a category D(A) endowed
with an additive functor C(A) ‚Üí D(A) such that quasi-isomorphisms in C(A) are
mapped to isomorphisms in D(A) and through which every F as above must factor
uniquely:
C(A)
 F
 / D
 |
 |
 | | | ‚àÉ!
 |
 |
 | =
D(A)
To be honest, we should take such a statement with a grain of salt: the natural
environment in which we should pose this universal problem would be a ‚Äòcategory
of categories‚Äô, and we have not deÔ¨Åned such a thing in this book. A category D(A)
as above does exist, at least if a certain ‚Äòlocalization‚Äô process can be carried out23 :
it is the derived category of A. ‚ÄòBounded‚Äô versions D‚àí (A), D+ (A), and more, are
solutions to the corresponding universal problems for appropriately bounded com-
plexes.
These derived categories answer the main question underlying our strategy, of
understanding ‚Äòwhat‚Äô in a complex really determines its cohomology: the answer
amounts to viewing the complex in the appropriate derived category. The con-
struction of D(A) consists roughly of taking the same objects as C(A) and formally
inverting the quasi-isomorphisms. The details of this construction are somewhat
involved and are best left to more advanced texts. But we will be able to capture its
essence in this book, at least in lucky cases (when the abelian category has ‚Äòenough
injectives‚Äô or ‚Äòenough projectives‚Äô; see DeÔ¨Ånition 5.7).
The derived category has the unfortunate reputation of being an overly abstract
notion, and there are fundamental questions about whether it really is the best
approach to an abstract study of cohomology. This is partly because the derived
category of an abelian category is not an abelian category and simple notions such as
kernel, cokernel, exact sequences are not available in D(A). This adds a substantial
layer of complication to the theory.
On the other hand, going past these diÔ¨Éculties, one Ô¨Ånds that enough structure
remains to do much homological algebra: objects in D(A) have cohomology, and
there are ‚Äòdistinguished triangles‚Äô (cf. ¬ß3.4) abstracting exact sequences and giving
23 There may be set-theoretic issues with this process. However, these play no role in the
cases we will consider, in which A has ‚Äòenough projectives‚Äô or ‚Äòenough injectives‚Äô.
610
 IX. Homological algebra
long exact cohomology sequences. The derived category is a triangulated category,
like the more manageable homotopic category K(A) that we will soon deÔ¨Åne. Its
uses go beyond the conÔ¨Ånes of algebra or even mathematics: an approach to the
understanding of ‚ÄòD-branes‚Äô in string theory is based on derived categories.
To get a sense of how counterintuitive D(A) must be, note that the zero-
morphism 0 : M ‚Ä¢ ‚Üí M ‚Ä¢ may well be a quasi-isomorphism: in fact, this is so
‚Ä¢precisely when M is exact. Well, in any category D as above (and in particular
in the derived category D(A)) this zero-morphism remains the zero-morphism, but
it comes equipped with an inverse when it is a quasi-isomorphism. Thus, in the
derived category the zero-morphism on a complex involving nonzero objects may
well be invertible! This says that complexes that are nonzero in C(A) may become
zero-objects in the derived category.

In the next several sections I will chase the notion of derived category, aiming
to understand it rather concretely in particularly favorable circumstances. We will
take it for granted that these categories exist. A detailed deÔ¨Ånition of these objects,
or a treatment of triangulated categories, is beyond the scope of this book.
4. Cones and homotopies
 611
4.3. Homotopy. The foregoing considerations put our strategy into focus: we are
after constructions that determine cochain complexes ‚Äòup to quasi-isomorphism‚Äô.
However, quasi-isomorphisms appear hard to deal with directly. Thus, we look for
more manageable notions that may work as an eÔ¨Äective replacement.
Like the mapping cone, this line of approach also owes its origins to topology:
in topology, ‚Äòhomeomorphism‚Äô is often too harsh a requirement, while ‚Äòhomotopy
equivalence‚Äô is a more malleable but still adequate notion. For example, homotopy
equivalent topological spaces have the same homology. Distilling the algebra out of
this notion leads to an analog at the level of complexes.
To see how this is done, recall that if f , g are homotopic continuous functions
between two topological spaces X and Y , then f and g induce the same map on the
homology of the spaces. To remind yourself of how this works, look at the picture:
Œ¥+
f (a)
 h(a)
 g(a)
Œ¥‚àí
This is supposed to represent the action of a homotopy between f and g on a chain a
in X: h(a) is obtained by mapping a √ó [0, 1] to Y so as to get f (a) when restricting
to a √ó {0} and to get g(a) when restricting to a √ó {1}. Note that h(a) is a chain
of dimension 1 higher than the dimension of a, and that the boundary ‚àÇh(a) of
this chain consists of f (a), g(a) and of the restriction of h to the boundary ‚àÇa of a:
taking the boundary ‚Äòcounterclockwise‚Äô,
‚àÇh(a) = g(a) ‚àí Œ¥+ ‚àí f (a) + Œ¥‚àí = g(a) ‚àí f (a) ‚àí h(‚àÇa)
(or so it would seem from the picture! As presented here, this is of course at best a
plausibility argument; it can be made rigorous, but that is someone else‚Äôs business).
That is,
g(a) ‚àí f (a) = ‚àÇh(a) + h(‚àÇa).
Since boundaries vanish in homology, f (a) and g(a) will agree in homology.
Here is the translation into algebra of this pleasant geometric situation:

Corollary 4.11. Homotopy equivalent complexes have isomorphic cohomology.
4. Cones and homotopies
 613
Proof. Indeed, morphisms Œ±‚Ä¢ : L‚Ä¢ ‚Üí M ‚Ä¢ , Œ≤ ‚Ä¢ : M ‚Ä¢ ‚Üí L‚Ä¢ such that Œ≤ ‚Ä¢ ‚ó¶ Œ± ‚Ä¢ and

 
Remark 4.12. In other words, homotopy equivalences of complexes are quasi-
isomorphisms. This does not yet solve the ‚Äòproblem‚Äô that quasi-isomorphisms are
not invertible, since Example 4.6 shows that quasi-isomorphisms may well not be
invertible even up to homotopy. In other words, ‚Äòhomotopy equivalence‚Äô is a more
restrictive notion than ‚Äòquasi-isomorphism‚Äô. One reason to study complexes of in-
jective/projective modules, as we will do in ¬ß5.3, is precisely that quasi-isomorphism
and homotopy equivalence are equivalent notions for (bounded) complexes of in-
jective or projective modules. Since homotopy equivalence is a ready-made equiva-
lence relation on complexes, this will bypass any technicality needed to make sense
of ‚Äòquasi-isomorphism‚Äô as an equivalence relation.
 
Corollary 4.11 is the main technical reason that makes the strategy presented
in ¬ß3.1 work, in a class of interesting examples. As we will see in due time, in
these examples the cohomology of the relevant cochain complexes will end up being
independent of the choices, because these choices will produce homotopic complexes.
Another key observation will be that homotopy equivalence is preserved by any
additive functor. Suppose A, B are abelian categories and
F : A ‚àí‚Üí B
is an additive functor (DeÔ¨Ånition 1.1). Then F induces an additive functor pre-
serving the grading
C(F ) : C(A) ‚àí‚Üí C(B).

in C(B), and if L‚Ä¢ and M ‚Ä¢ are homotopy equivalent complexes in C(A), then
C(F )(L‚Ä¢ ) and C(F )(M ‚Ä¢ ) are homotopy equivalent complexes in C(B).
Proof. The second assertion follows from the Ô¨Årst. The Ô¨Årst is an immediate
consequence of the fact that F is additive. Indeed, if h is a homotopy between

Lemma 4.13 implies that the conclusion of Corollary 4.11 holds true after ap-

614
 IX. Homological algebra
Theorem 4.14. Let F : A ‚Üí B be an additive functor between two abelian cate-
gories. If L‚Ä¢ , M ‚Ä¢ are homotopy equivalent complexes in C(A), then the cohomology
complexes
H ‚Ä¢ (C(F )(L‚Ä¢ )), H ‚Ä¢ (C(F )(M ‚Ä¢))
are isomorphic.
The proof of this statement is essentially immediate after all our preparatory
work, so it is left to the reader (Exercise 4.16).
I am gracing this statement with theorem status because it is at the root of
the strategy I am pursuing. The content of Theorem 4.14 is that any mechanism
associating to a mathematical object a cochain complex determined up to homotopy
will give rise to a slew of interesting invariants: apply your favorite additive functor
to any such complex, take cohomology, and Theorem 4.14 guarantees that the
result will be independent of the speciÔ¨Åc chosen complex. This is (part of) the
reason underlying the various claims of independence on choices made in ¬ßVIII.2.4
and ¬ßVIII.6.4, concerning the deÔ¨Ånition of the Tor and Ext functors, as we will see
later in the chapter (¬ß7, especially Examples 7.6 and 7.7).

5. The homotopic category. Complexes of projectives and
injectives
One message I have tried to convey in ¬ß4 is that while quasi-isomorphisms are
(by deÔ¨Ånition!) the morphisms of cochain complexes preserving cohomology and
therefore may be deemed to ‚Äòcapture the cohomology of a complex‚Äô, the stronger
notion of homotopy equivalence is in fact more natural from the point of view of
applications. This is the moral of Theorem 4.14: homotopy equivalent complexes
have the same cohomology for a much better reason than complexes linked by a
quasi-isomorphism. If our general aim is to understand what it means to make
all quasi-isomorphisms invertible (that is, understand the derived category D(A)),
we may begin by making homotopy equivalences invertible. This produces a new
category, the ‚Äòhomotopic category‚Äô of complexes, that we approach in this section.
We also examine the privileged position of bounded complexes of projective and
injective objects regarding homotopy: for these complexes, quasi-isomorphisms are
necessarily homotopy equivalences. Verifying this requires rather technical consid-
erations, but it will be necessary in order to gain some understanding of the derived
category, in ¬ß6.
5.1. Homotopic maps are identiÔ¨Åed in the derived category. To see that
this ‚Äòhomotopic category‚Äô is a necessary stop on the way from C(A) to D(A), go back
to our higher-brow considerations at the end of ¬ß4.2. Again consider any additive
functor
F : C(A) ‚àí‚Üí D
transforming quasi-isomorphisms into isomorphisms. The simplest such functor is
cohomology; the universal one is the functor from C(A) to the mystifying derived
category D(A). We have veriÔ¨Åed in Proposition 4.10 that homotopic maps induce
the same morphism in cohomology. Might this not be the case for any functor F
as above? It is!
Lemma 5.1. Let F : C(A) ‚àí‚Üí D be an additive functor such that F (œÅ‚Ä¢ ) is an
isomorphism in D for all quasi-isomorphisms œÅ‚Ä¢ in C(A).
5. The homotopic category. Complexes of projectives and injectives

in D.

618
 IX. Homological algebra
Lemma 5.1 tells us that every functor transforming quasi-isomorphisms into
isomorphisms must factor through the category obtained from C(A) by identifying
together homotopic morphisms. It is time to deÔ¨Åne this category.
5.2. DeÔ¨Ånition of the homotopic category of complexes.
DeÔ¨Ånition 5.2. Let A be an abelian category. The homotopy category K(A) of
cochain complexes in A is the category whose objects are the cochain complexes
in A (that is, the same objects of C(A)) and whose morphisms are
Hom K(A) (L‚Ä¢ , M ‚Ä¢ ) := Hom C(A) (L‚Ä¢ , M ‚Ä¢)/ ‚àº
where ‚àº is the homotopy relation.
 
Yes, but is this a category? Recall that the homotopy relation ‚àº respects
composition (Exercise 4.6); in other words, the operation of composition
HomC(A) (L‚Ä¢ , M ‚Ä¢) √ó HomC(A) (M ‚Ä¢ , N ‚Ä¢ ) ‚àí‚Üí HomC(A)(L‚Ä¢, N ‚Ä¢ )
doesdescend to an operation
HomK(A) (L‚Ä¢ , M ‚Ä¢ ) √ó HomK(A) (M ‚Ä¢ , N ‚Ä¢ )‚àí‚Üí HomK(A) (L‚Ä¢ , N ‚Ä¢ ).
Checking the category axioms is routine and is left to the patient reader.
Bounded variations K‚àí (A), K+ (A), etc., are obtained likewise from C‚àí (A),
C+
 (A), etc. The considerations which follow will apply to all these versions. The
further variations K‚àí (P), resp., K+ (I), in which the complexes will be required
to consist of projective, resp., injective, objects from A (cf. ¬ß5.3) will also play a
crucially important role.
As to the general structure underlying the homotopic category,
Lemma 5.3. Let A be an abelian category. Then the homotopic category K(A) of
complexes is an additive category.
Proof. Exercise 5.1.
 
However, note that, in general, the homotopic category is not abelian. Indeed,
homotopic maps do not have the same kernel or cokernel in general, so deÔ¨Åning
these notions becomes problematic. As I already mentioned in ¬ß3.4, K(A) is a
triangulated category; the ‚Äòdistinguished triangles‚Äô are the triangles arising from
the cones of morphisms, as in ¬ß4.1. (I will brieÔ¨Çy describe the situation in ¬ß9.2.)
The main motivation for the introduction of the homotopic category is that
homotopy equivalences become isomorphisms in K(A). More precisely, by deÔ¨Ånition
there is a functor
C(A) ‚àí‚Üí K(A),
mapping every object to ‚Äòitself‚Äô and every morphism to its homotopy class. Ho-
motopy equivalences in C(A) become isomorphisms in K(A) because the relation
Œ± ‚Ä¢ ‚ó¶ Œ≤ ‚Ä¢ ‚àº id in C(A) becomes Œ±‚Ä¢ ‚ó¶ Œ≤ ‚Ä¢ = id in K(A). The homotopic category is
obtained from C(A) by making all homotopy equivalences invertible on the nose.
We can then reinterpret Lemma 5.1 as the following ‚Äòfactorization‚Äô result:
5. The homotopic category. Complexes of projectives and injectives

Proposition 5.4. Let F : C(A) ‚àí‚Üí D be an additive functor such that F (œÅ‚Ä¢)
is an isomorphism in D for all quasi-isomorphisms œÅ‚Ä¢ in C(A). Then F factors
uniquely through K(A):

Proof. Exercise 5.2.
 
In particular, there must be a unique functor from K(A) to the subtler derived
category D(A). The situation is of course analogous for all bounded versions of
these objects: the natural functors from the appropriately bounded complexes to
the corresponding derived categories factor uniquely through the corresponding
homotopic categories:
C‚àí (A)

A particular case of Proposition 5.4 is the fact that the cohomology functor on C(A)
(resp., C ‚àí(A), C+(A)) descends to a functor on K(A) (resp., K‚àí (A), K+ (A)). This
fact also follows directly from Proposition 4.10: homotopic maps induce the same
morphism in cohomology.
5.3. Complexes of projective and injective objects. We should now ask ‚Äòhow
far‚Äô K(A) may be from D(A). We have inverted all homotopy equivalences in K(A),
but not all quasi-isomorphisms are homotopy equivalences: keep Example 4.6 in
mind. If we want to think of K(A) as a Ô¨Årst approximation to D(A), we should
focus on complexes for which there is essentially no distinction between quasi-
isomorphisms and homotopy equivalences.
It turns out that there are such complexes: those consisting of projective or
injective objects.
Recall (DeÔ¨Ånition VIII.6.1) that an R-module M is ‚Äòprojective‚Äô if the func-
tor HomR (M, ) is exact and it is ‚Äòinjective‚Äô if HomR ( , M ) is exact. We can adopt
these deÔ¨Ånitions in any abelian category:
DeÔ¨Ånition 5.5. Let A be an abelian category. An object P of A is projective if the
functor Hom A(P, ) is exact. An object Q is injective if the functor HomA ( , Q) is
exact.
 
General remarks such as Lemma VIII.6.2 or the comments about splitting of
sequences at the end of ¬ßVIII.6.1 hold in any abelian category, and a good exercise
620
 IX. Homological algebra
for the reader is to collect all such information and verify that it does hold in this
new context.
Also note that, at this level of generality, the parts of the theory dealing with
injective objects are a faithful mirror of the parts dealing with projective objects.
This is of course not a coincidence: the opposite of an abelian category is again
abelian (Exercise 1.10), and projectives in one become injectives in the other. Thus,
it is really only necessary to prove statements for, say, projectives in arbitrary
abelian categories: the corresponding statements for injectives will automatically
follow.
This dual state of aÔ¨Äairs should not be taken too far: projectives and injectives
in a Ô¨Åxed abelian category may display very diÔ¨Äerent features. For example, the
characterizations worked out in ¬ßVIII.6.2 and ¬ßVIII.6.3 use the speciÔ¨Åc properties of
the categories R-Mod, so they should not be expected to have a simple counterpart
in arbitrary abelian categories.
I will denote by P, resp., I, the full subcategories of A determined by the pro-
jective, resp., injective, objects. Of course these are not abelian categories in any
interesting case. Note that an abelian category may well have no nontrivial projec-
tive or injective objects.
Example 5.6. The category of Ô¨Ånite abelian groups is abelian (surprise, surprise),
but contains no nontrivial projective or injective objects (Exercise 5.5).
 
DeÔ¨Ånition 5.7. An abelian category A has enough projectives if for every object A
in A there exists a projective object P in A and an epimorphism P
 / / A . The
category has enough injectives if for every object A in A there is an injective object Q
in A and a monomorphism A / / Q .
 
These deÔ¨Ånitions are not new to the reader, since we ran across them in ¬ßVIII.6:
in particular, we have already observed that, for every commutative ring R, R-Mod
has enough projectives (this is not challenging: free modules and their direct sum-
mands are projective, Proposition VIII.6.4) and enough injectives (this is challeng-
ing; we veriÔ¨Åed it in Corollary VIII.6.12).
Example 5.6 shows that we should not expect an abelian category A to have
either property. An important case in which one can show that there are enough
injectives is the category of sheaves of abelian groups over a topological space;
this is a key step in the deÔ¨Ånition of sheaf cohomology as a ‚Äòderived functor‚Äô. In
general, categories of sheaves do not have enough projectives. On the other hand,
the category of Ô¨Ånitely generated abelian groups has enough projectives but not
enough (indeed, no nontrivial) injectives. So it goes.
5.4. Homotopy equivalences vs. quasi-isomorphisms in K(A). As I already
mentioned, we will be interested in certain subcategories of K(A) determined by
complexes of projective or injective objects of A.
‚àíDeÔ¨Ånition 5.8. I will denote by K (P) the full subcategory of K(A) consisting of
bounded-above complexes of projective objects of A, and I will denote by K+ (I) the
full subcategory of K(A) consisting of bounded-below complexes of injective objects
of A.
 
5. The homotopic category. Complexes of projectives and injectives
 621
The main result of the rest of this section is the following:
Theorem 5.9. Let A be an abelian category, and let Œ±‚Ä¢ : P0 ‚Ä¢ ‚Üí P1 ‚Ä¢ , resp., Œ±‚Ä¢ :
Q‚Ä¢ 0 ‚Üí Q‚Ä¢ 1 , be a quasi-isomorphism between bounded-above complexes of projectives,
resp., bounded-below complexes of injectives, in A. Then Œ±‚Ä¢ is a homotopy equiva-
lence.
Corollary 5.10. In K‚àí (P) and K+ (I), (homotopy classes of ) quasi-isomorphisms
are isomorphisms.
That is, all quasi-isomorphisms between bounded complexes of projectives or
injectives are ‚Äòalready‚Äô inverted in K‚àí (P), K+ (I). Thus, these categories are very
close to the corresponding derived categories. In fact, we will see that if A has
‚àí ‚àíenough projectives, then K (P) ‚ÄòsuÔ¨Éces‚Äô to describe D (A) (in a sense that will
be made more precise); similarly, K+ (I) acts as a concrete realization of D+(A) if
A has enough injectives.
The proof of Theorem 5.9 will require a few preliminaries, which further clarify
the role played by complexes of projective and injective objects with regard to
homotopies.
‚Ä¢A complicated way of saying that a complex N in C(A) is exact is to assert
that the identity map idN ‚Ä¢ and the trivial map 0 induce the same morphism in co-
homology, as they would if they were homotopic to each other. It is however easy to
construct examples of exact complexes for which the identity is not homotopic to 0.
As the reader will verify (Exercise 5.11), this has to do with whether the complex
‚Ä¢splits or not; in general, a complex N is said to be ‚Äòsplit exact‚Äô if idN ‚Ä¢ is homo-
topic to 0. Keeping in mind that projective or injective objects ‚Äòcause‚Äô sequences
to split, it may not be too surprising that for bounded exact complexes consisting
of projective or injective objects the identity does turn out to be homotopic to 0.
To verify this, we study special conditions that ensure that morphisms are
homotopic to 0.
‚Ä¢Lemma 5.11. Let P be a complex of projective objects of an abelian category A

for i < 0.
