Corollary 5.12. Let P be a bounded-above cochain complex of projectives of an
abelian category A, and let L‚Ä¢ be an exact complex in C(A).
Then every morphism of complexes P ‚Ä¢ ‚Üí L‚Ä¢ is homotopic to 0.
This follows immediately from (a harmless shift of) Lemma 5.11, since every
morphism to an exact complex has no choice but to induce the zero-morphism in
cohomology.
5. The homotopic category. Complexes of projectives and injectives
 623
The ‚Äòinjective‚Äô version of Corollary 5.12 is that morphisms from an exact com-
plex L‚Ä¢ to a bounded-below complex Q‚Ä¢ of injectives are necessarily homotopy
equivalent to 0.
Note that in all these considerations the complexes P ‚Ä¢ , Q‚Ä¢ are required to
live in the ‚Äòbounded‚Äô versions C‚àí (P), C +(I): all objects should vanish in degree
suÔ¨Éciently high or low. This furnishes the ‚Äòstart‚Äô of the inductive construction of
the homotopy in the proof of Lemma 5.11. It is an essentially inescapable feature
of the theory.
Corollary 5.13. Let P ‚Ä¢ (resp., Q‚Ä¢ ) be a bounded-above exact complex of projec-
tives (resp., a bounded-below exact complex of injectives). Then P ‚Ä¢ (resp., Q‚Ä¢ ) is
homotopy equivalent to the zero-complex.
Proof. Exercise 5.12.
 
Corollary 5.13 is the Ô¨Årst manifestation of the principle captured more fully
by Theorem 5.9: we have just veriÔ¨Åed that, for suitably bounded complexes of
projectives or injectives, ‚Äòquasi-isomorphic to 0‚Äô is the same as ‚Äòhomotopy equivalent
to 0‚Äô.
Another consequence of Lemma 5.11 is the following remark, showing that
quasi-isomorphisms are ‚Äònon-zero-divisors‚Äô up to homotopy, with respect to mor-
phisms from complexes of projectives. This will be useful in the next section, for
example to show that certain lifts are uniquely deÔ¨Åned up to homotopy.

5.5. Proof of Theorem 5.9. Theorem 5.9 will follow from a more general obser-
vation: a quasi-isomorphism to a complex of projectives, resp., from a complex of
injectives25, has a right, resp., left, homotopy inverse.

(For the injective version, if Q‚Ä¢ is a bounded-below complex of injectives and

6. Projective and injective resolutions and the derived category
Theorem 5.9 is very good news. It tells us that if we are willing and able to change
our viewpoint and adopt bounded complexes of projectives or injectives as our
natural environment, then we will have an excellent handle on the subtle notion
of quasi-isomorphism: because quasi-isomorphisms become homotopy equivalences
in that environment and homotopy equivalences are better behaved than arbitrary
quasi-isomorphisms. For example, homotopy equivalence of complexes is manifestly
an equivalence relation (Exercise 4.4), while quasi-isomorphism is not (see the dis-
cussion following Example 4.6). Further, homotopy equivalences are preserved by
every additive functor (Theorem 4.14), while general quasi-isomorphisms are not
(Exercise 4.15).
The question is therefore how to switch to a homotopic environment and adopt
complexes of projectives or injectives as our primary object of study, while keeping
intact the essential information carried by the objects of A or even the complexes
in C(A).
This can be done if A has enough projectives or enough injectives; in this
situation, we will be able to produce an adequate description of the derived category
(¬ß6.3).
6. Projective and injective resolutions and the derived category
 629
6.1. Recovering A. Recall once more that a ‚Äòcopy‚Äô of an abelian category A is
available within C(A), by associating with every object A in A the complex Œπ(A)
having A in degree 0 and 0 elsewhere. Composing with the functor C(A) ‚Üí K(A)
realizes A as a full subcategory of K(A) (Exercise 6.1). We will look for ‚Äòmore
interesting‚Äô copies of A in the homotopic category.
Let us begin by recalling (and sharpening) our deÔ¨Ånition of projective/injective
resolutions of an object A.
DeÔ¨Ånition 6.1. Let A be an object of an abelian category A.
A projective resolution of A is a quasi-isomorphism P ‚Ä¢ ‚Üí Œπ(A), where P ‚Ä¢ is a
‚â§0complex in C (P).
An injective resolution of A is a quasi-isomorphism Œπ(A) ‚Üí Q‚Ä¢ , where Q‚Ä¢ is a
complex in C‚â•0 (I).
 
By common abuse of language, I will often refer to P ‚Ä¢ or Q‚Ä¢ as the resolution,
leaving the quasi-isomorphism understood.
Remark 6.2. The terminology is potentially confusing, since it hints that the
resolutions themselves may be projective/injective as objects of the abelian cate-
gory C(A), or of its bounded variations. This is not the case (Exercise 6.3).
 
Projective or injective resolutions need not exist: as I pointed out (Exam-
ple 5.6), an abelian category may have no nontrivial projective or injective objects.
It is however clear that such resolutions exist if the category has enough projec-
tives/injectives: if A has enough projectives, then given any object A of A there
is a projective P 0 with an epimorphism œÄ : P 0 ‚Üí A; and then a projective P ‚àí1
with an epimorphism d‚àí1 : P ‚àí1 ‚Üí ker œÄ; and then a projective P ‚àí2 with an
epimorphism d‚àí2 : P ‚àí2 ‚Üí ker d‚àí1 ; and so on. Letting diP ‚Ä¢ be the composition
P i / ker di+1 / / P i+1 gives
d‚àí2
 P ‚Ä¢

 P ‚Ä¢
¬∑¬∑¬∑
 / P ‚àí2
 / P ‚àí1
 / P 0
 / 0
 / ¬∑¬∑¬∑ ,
a projective resolution of A. Just as clearly, if A has enough injectives, then every
object A has an injective resolution.
Now consider the category of homotopy classes of quasi-isomorphisms with tar-
get Œπ(A) (cf. Remark 4.5): this is a ‚Äòhomotopic category of resolutions‚Äô of A. I claim
that projective resolutions, if they exist, are initial in this category. (Analogously,
injective resolutions are Ô¨Ånal in the homotopic category of quasi-isomorphism with
source Œπ(A).)
As the reader knows, this means that projective resolutions are supposed to
map uniquely (in K(A)) to every resolution of A. The following key lemma proves
this and more:
‚Ä¢Lemma 6.3. Let A be an object of an abelian category A. Let M be a resolution
‚Ä¢ ‚â§0of A, and let P be any complex in C (P). Let

be an arbitrary morphism. Then

of H 0 ;
‚Ä¢ diÔ¨Äerent morphisms satisfying the previous requirement are necessarily homo-
topy equivalent.
Of course an analogous statement holds for complexes Q‚Ä¢ in C‚â•0 (I), giving
morphisms of cochain complexes M ‚Ä¢ ‚Üí Q‚Ä¢ inducing a given morphism in H 0.

 
Summarizing, if A contains enough projectives, then every object A of A ad-
mits a projective resolution, and further this is initial among all resolutions with
target Œπ(A). If A has enough injectives, then every object A of A admits an injective
resolution, and this is Ô¨Ånal among all resolutions with source Œπ(A).
Recall that initial, resp., Ô¨Ånal, objects of a category are always unique up to
isomorphism (Proposition I.5.4). Here, this says the following:
6. Projective and injective resolutions and the derived category
 631
Proposition 6.4. Any two projective (resp., injective) resolutions of an object A
of an abelian category A are homotopy equivalent.
(This is also a direct consequence of Lemma 6.3.)
The moral I extract from these considerations is that if an abelian category A
has enough (say) projectives, then we can associate with each object A of A an
‚â§0object of K (P), determined up to homotopy. This can in fact be done functorially,
in the sense that morphisms in A can be lifted to morphisms of corresponding
projective resolutions:
‚Ä¢Proposition 6.5. Let A0 , A1 be objects of an abelian category A, and let Pi be
a projective resolution of Ai, i = 0, 1. Then every morphism œï : A0 ‚Üí A1 in A is
induced by a morphism Œ±‚Ä¢ : P0 ‚Ä¢ ‚Üí P1 ‚Ä¢ , uniquely determined up to homotopy.
Of course an analogous statement holds for injective resolutions.
Proof. By hypothesis, œï is a morphism H 0 (P0 ‚Ä¢ ) ‚Üí H 0 (P1 ‚Ä¢ ). The complex P0 ‚Ä¢
consists of projectives, and P1 ‚Ä¢ is a resolution of A1 ; therefore a lift Œ±‚Ä¢ exists and
is unique up to homotopy, as an immediate consequence of Lemma 6.3.
 
The assignment provided by Proposition 6.5 is clearly covariant. The bottom
line is that the part of A consisting of objects with (say) projective resolutions
‚àíis equivalent to a full subcategory of K (P). Dear reader, please formalize this
assertion, for it is a key point (Exercise 6.2). If A has enough projectives, this gives
‚àíthe promised ‚Äòcopy‚Äô of A within K (P).
Of course the situation is entirely analogous concerning the subcategory of A
consisting of objects admitting an injective resolution and K+ (I).
6.2. From objects to complexes. Suppose that an abelian category A has
enough projectives. At this point we hopefully agree (Exercise 6.2) that the ho-
‚àímotopic category K (P) contains a full subcategory which is equivalent to A: the
(homotopy) category of projective resolutions of objects of A. While a priori each
object of A corresponds to many projective resolutions, these are all homotopy
‚àíequivalent to one another (by Proposition 6.4), hence isomorphic in K (P); mor-
phisms in A correspond precisely to homotopy classes of morphisms between pro-
‚àíjective resolutions (by Proposition 6.5), hence to morphisms in K (P).
‚àíIn practice, this says that we can use this subcategory of K (P) as a replace-
ment for the original abelian category A. We are very close to achieving our goal of
identifying the ‚Äòessential nature‚Äô of cohomology: since homotopy equivalent com-
plexes have the same cohomology (by Theorem 4.14) and additive functors preserve
‚àíhomotopy equivalence, objects of K (P) are ideal carriers of cohomology invariants:
while applying an additive functor to objects of A in general destroys cohomolog-
‚àíical information, applying ‚Äòthe same functor‚Äô to a corresponding object in K (P)
preserves that information.
We will follow this lead in the next section; the functors Tori and Exti encoun-
tered in Chapter VIII will arise precisely in this fashion and will stand as our poster
examples of derived functors.
632
 IX. Homological algebra
Of course, exactly the same situation will occur if A has enough injectives, in
which case the appropriate replacement for A is found in K+ (I). I will say a word
about the general situation when projectives or injectives are not available, in ¬ß9.1.
The reader should now wonder whether the categories K‚àí (P), K+ (I) may in fact
be used to extract cohomological information for any (bounded) complex, not just
for resolutions of a given object of A. As observed in ¬ß4.2, resolutions (in C‚àí (A)
or C+ (A)) are just special cases of quasi-isomorphisms; the natural question is
‚àíwhether every (suitably bounded) complex has a ‚Äòquasi-isomorphic copy‚Äô in K (P)
or K+ (I), again uniquely deÔ¨Åned up to isomorphism in the homotopy category (that
is, up to homotopy equivalence in C(A)).
This is indeed the case.
Theorem 6.6. Assume the abelian category A has enough projectives, and let L‚Ä¢ be
‚àí ‚Ä¢
a complex in C (A). Then there exists a bounded-above complex of projectives Pand a quasi-isomorphism P ‚Ä¢ ‚Üí L‚Ä¢ , and P ‚Ä¢ is uniquely deÔ¨Åned up to homotopy
equivalence. Further, every morphism Œ± ‚Ä¢ of complexes in C‚àí(A) lifts to a morphism
of the corresponding projective resolutions in K‚àí (P), also uniquely determined (up
to homotopy).
The lift obtained in the last part of this statement exists in the homotopy
category: any representative in C‚àí(P) will be a ‚Äòhomotopy lift‚Äô of Œ±‚Ä¢ , in the sense
that the relevant diagram will only be guaranteed to commute up to homotopy.
I will call a complex P ‚Ä¢ as in the statement a ‚Äòprojective resolution of L‚Ä¢ ‚Äô;
this is a slight abuse of language, since it forgets the speciÔ¨Åc quasi-isomorphism
P ‚Ä¢ ‚Üí L‚Ä¢ .
As always, there is a mirror statement to Theorem 6.6 for injectives, which I
will leave to the reader to formulate precisely. It is a particularly good exercise for
the reader to construct a stand-alone proof for the injective case.
Proof. The proof of this result is admittedly rather technical, as it involves many
of the tools that we have developed.

6. Projective and injective resolutions and the derived category
 635
6.3. Poor man‚Äôs derived category. We are now in a position to close a relatively
large circle of ideas. I claim that if the abelian category A has enough projectives,
resp., injectives, then K‚àí (P), resp., K+ (I), is a solution ‚Äòup to isomorphisms‚Äô of the
universal problem presented in ¬ß4.2. The precise statement is the following.
By Proposition 5.4, the functor C(A) ‚Üí D(A) factors through the homotopic
category; this of course holds for the bounded versions of these categories as well.
Thus, we have functors K‚àí (A) ‚Üí D‚àí(A) and K+ (A) ‚Üí D+ (A). By restriction, we
obtain functors
K‚àí(P) ‚Üí D‚àí(A),
 K+ (I) ‚Üí D+ (A).
Theorem 6.7. Let A be an abelian category with enough projectives. Then the
functor K‚àí (P) ‚Üí D‚àí (A) is an equivalence of categories.
If A has enough injectives, then the functor K+ (I) ‚Üí D+ (A) is an equivalence
of categories.
Theorem 6.7 is proven in full detail in any more complete treatment of homo-
logical algebra. Since we have not actually constructed D‚àí (A), we cannot really
prove this statement here; but we now know enough to appreciate why Theorem 6.7
should be true, in the sense that K‚àí (P), K+(I) satisfy the expected universal prop-
erties. I will deal with the projective side of the story, since the injective side
mirrors it faithfully.
Assume that A has enough projectives. DeÔ¨Åne a functor P : C‚àí (A) ‚Üí
K‚àí
 (P) by associating with every bounded-above complex L‚Ä¢ any projective res-
olution P(L‚Ä¢ ) of L‚Ä¢ (which exists by Theorem 6.6) and with every morphism
Œ± ‚Ä¢ : L‚Ä¢ ‚Üí M ‚Ä¢ in C‚àí (A) the morphism P(Œ±‚Ä¢ ) in K‚àí (P) lifting Œ±‚Ä¢ .
The morphism P(Œ±‚Ä¢ ) is the homotopy class of a homotopy lift of Œ±‚Ä¢ ; we have
proved that any two such lifts are homotopy equivalent. It also follows that P is
indeed a functor: for example, if Œ±‚Ä¢ : L‚Ä¢ ‚Üí M ‚Ä¢ and Œ≤ ‚Ä¢ : M ‚Ä¢ ‚Üí N ‚Ä¢ are morphisms
in C‚àí (A), then both P(Œ≤‚Ä¢ ) ‚ó¶ P(Œ± ‚Ä¢ ) and P(Œ≤ ‚Ä¢ ‚ó¶ Œ±‚Ä¢ ) are classes of homotopy lifts
of Œ≤ ‚Ä¢ ‚ó¶ Œ±‚Ä¢ ; therefore P(Œ≤ ‚Ä¢ ) ‚ó¶ P(Œ±‚Ä¢ ) = P(Œ≤ ‚Ä¢ ‚ó¶ Œ±‚Ä¢ ) by uniqueness, as needed.
Note that there is a very large element of arbitrariness in the choice of P: I
am not prescribing any particular recipe for choosing a projective resolution rather
than another. But all such resolutions are homotopically equivalent, as proven in
Theorem 6.6; therefore diÔ¨Äerent choices would only move the resolutions in their
‚àíisomorphism class in K (P).
Remark 6.8. There is an eÔ¨Écient way to clarify (and defuse) this arbitrariness.
Suppose P, P  are two choices for the resolution functor C‚àí (A) ‚Üí K‚àí (P). Thus,
we have chosen two projective resolutions P(L‚Ä¢ ), P  (L‚Ä¢ ) for each object L‚Ä¢
in C‚àí(A). By Theorem 6.6, there is a homotopy equivalence between P(L‚Ä¢ )
and P  (L‚Ä¢ ), that is, an isomorphism œÅL‚Ä¢ : P(L‚Ä¢ ) ‚Üí P  (L‚Ä¢ ) in K‚àí(P); this
isomorphism is unique (since it lifts the identity on L‚Ä¢ and lifts are unique up to
homotopy). Further, for every morphism of cochain complexes Œ±‚Ä¢ : L‚Ä¢ 0 ‚Üí L‚Ä¢ 1 , the

This says that there is a unique natural isomorphism 29 between any two choices
P, P  of functors of projective resolutions. In a categorical context, this is essen-
tially as good as uniqueness. Thus the arbitrariness in the choice of P is less
dramatic than it may seem at Ô¨Årst.

29 ‚ÄòNatural transformations‚Äô were rapidly deÔ¨Åned in ¬ßVIII.1.5, DeÔ¨Ånition VIII.1.15. They are
the most sensible notion of a morphism between functors. A natural isomorphism is a natural
transformation that is an isomorphism on each object.

If A has enough injectives, we can deÔ¨Åne a functor Q : C+ (A) ‚Üí K+ (I) by
choosing an injective resolution for every bounded-below complex. The situation
concerning this functor is precisely analogous to the situation just reviewed for the
functor P, modulo reversing arrows as appropriate.
Summary. If an abelian category A has enough projectives (resp., enough in-
jectives), then the homotopic category K ‚àí (P) (resp., K+ (I)) works as a replacement
of the derived category D‚àí(A) (resp., D+ (A)). As I have argued earlier, viewing
a complex in these categories is the most natural way to extract the maximum
amount of ‚Äòcohomological‚Äô information from the complex. The category A itself (or
a category equivalent to A) sits as a full subcategory of these categories.
To reiterate the key point, there is a clear advantage in stopping short of taking
cohomology. Resolving a complex L‚Ä¢ by a complex P ‚Ä¢ of (say) projectives, thanks
to Theorem 6.6, gives us an entity which certainly captures the cohomological
information of L‚Ä¢ but that can still be manipulated with the entire range of tools
‚Ä¢available in dealing with complexes. For example, we can apply to P any additive
functor and still obtain a complex whose cohomology only depends on the original
complex L‚Ä¢ (by Theorem 4.14). The cohomology of L‚Ä¢ is only one example of the
cohomological invariants that may be extracted from L‚Ä¢ ; viewing L‚Ä¢ in the derived
category gives direct access to all these invariants.
We will capitalize on these considerations in the next section.
Exercises

in degree 0 is a copy of the abelian category A. [¬ß6.1]

‚àíof K (P) consisting of complexes with cohomology concentrated in degree 0 is
equivalent to A. (Prove that H 0 is fully faithful on this subcategory.) [¬ß6.1, ¬ß6.2,
6.13, ¬ß7.1]

an object of C(A).
‚Ä¢ Prove that each P i is projective in A.

6.4.  Let A be an abelian category with enough projectives, and let P : C‚àí(A) ‚Üí
‚àíK (P) be a functor choosing a projective resolution for every bounded-above com-

denote by P. (Use Lemma 5.14.) Next, let I be the embedding K‚àí (P) ‚Üí K‚àí (A).

7. Derived functors
I will now adopt the blanket assumption that A is an abelian category with enough
projectives, so that‚Äîas explained in ¬ß6.3‚Äîthe homotopic category K‚àí (P) is a con-
crete realization (up to equivalence of categories) of the derived category of the
bounded-above complexes in A. Of course everything would go through, with ap-
propriate changes, for bounded-below complexes in the presence of enough injec-
tives. These assumptions are not necessary in order to develop this material, but
they simplify it substantially.
7.1. Viewpoint shift. After deriving categories, it should not seem too far-fetched
to derive functors.
We have seen (Exercises 6.2 and 6.13) that, if A has enough projectives, then
the full subcategory √Ç of K‚àí (P) whose objects are complexes with cohomology
concentrated in degree 0 is equivalent to A: H 0 is a fully faithful, surjective functor
√Ç ‚Üí A. With notation as in ¬ß6.3, the functor P ‚ó¶ Œπ associating with every object
of A a projective resolution gives a ‚Äòweak‚Äô inverse to H 0. As pointed out in ¬ß6.3,
there are many diÔ¨Äerent possible P; each of them will determine a copy P ‚ó¶ Œπ(A)
‚àíof A in K (P).
It may be argued that any such realization of (a category equivalent to) A is
‚Äòbetter‚Äô than A itself. Indeed, I have repeatedly stressed that all the cohomological
information carried by a complex is ideally captured by viewing that complex in
the derived category, and for the complex Œπ(M ) determined in the simplest way by
an object M of A, this simply means replacing Œπ(M ) with any projective resolution
‚àíof M , viewed as an object of K (P).
This, however, raises a question: if F : A ‚Üí B is a functor and we are serious
‚àíabout replacing A with its counterpart(s) in D (A), then there should be a way to
‚Äòreinterpret‚Äô F in this new context: induce in some natural way a ‚Äòderived functor‚Äô
D‚àí (A) ‚Üí D‚àí (B). One would hope that this functor should carry at least as much
information as F and satisfy better properties.
Now I claim that there indeed is an evident way to induce a functor between
‚àíthe realizations of the derived categories in terms of K (P). It is a little less clear
what question this functor answers‚Äîthat is, what kind of universal property it
satisÔ¨Åes. The reader is invited to sort all of this out before I do it.
642
 IX. Homological algebra
Say that F : A ‚Üí B is an additive functor between two abelian categories. It
is clear that F extends to a functor
C(F )

C(F )(L‚Ä¢ ) whose objects are F (Li ) and whose diÔ¨Äerentials are F (diL‚Ä¢ ). Note that
the functoriality and additivity of F imply that

cochain complexes in C(A), then the collection F (Œ±i) deÔ¨Ånes a morphism in C(B),
and that this assignment is functorial. Now, we already checked (Lemma 4.13) that
homotopic morphisms in C(A) are sent to homotopic morphisms in C(B); it follows
that F induces a functor K(F ) : K(A) ‚Üí K(B). The diagram

If now it were the case that additive functors send projective objects to projec-
‚àí ‚àítive objects, then we could restrict K(F ) to K (P(A)) and land in K (P(B)) (where
P(A) and P(B) denote the classes of projective objects in A, resp., B); given our
discussion in ¬ß6.3, this would give us a natural candidate for the ‚Äòderived functor‚Äô
of F .
However, additive functors do not preserve projectivity or injectivity in gen-
eral. Why should they? For example, ‚Äòtensor‚Äô does not preserve projectives30 (Ex-
ercise 7.1). Assuming that B has enough projectives, the natural way to ‚ÄòÔ¨Åx‚Äô this
problem is to apply a corresponding functor PB constructed as in ¬ß6.3, associating
with each complex a projective resolution. By Lemma 5.14, PB sends homotopic
morphisms to homotopic morphisms; hence PB also descends to a functor deÔ¨Åned
on the homotopic category (cf. Exercise 6.4).

7. Derived functors
 643
7.2. Universal property of the derived functor. The deÔ¨Ånition I have given
for the derived functor depends on the chosen resolution functor PB , so that LF :
K‚àí (P(A)) ‚Üí K‚àí (P(B)) is really only deÔ¨Åned up to a natural isomorphism. In any
case, the derived functor should be thought of as the natural counterpart of F at
the level of the derived categories of A and B: up to equivalences of categories, LF
acts as D‚àí (A) ‚Üí D‚àí (B).
Still, it is not too clear exactly what question this deÔ¨Ånition answers. For
example, the derived functor does not Ô¨Åt into a commutative diagram analogous to
the one displayed above:
√Ç  _

‚àíK (P(A))
??
LF
/ BÃÇ 
 _

/ K‚àí (P(B))
Here √Ç and BÃÇ are the replacements for A, B in the corresponding derived categories,
discussed at the beginning of ¬ß7.1, that is, the subcategories whose objects have
cohomology concentrated in degree 0. The point is that there is no reason why
applying LF to a complex whose cohomology is concentrated in degree 0 should
yield a complex with the same property, unless F is very special to begin with.
This may be viewed as a nuisance. On the contrary, it is one of the main values
of deriving categories and functors. Recasting an additive functors F : A ‚Üí B at
the level of derived categories, one gets access to interesting new invariants even
when starting from (the equivalent copy in the derived category of) A itself.
Exactness plays an important role in these considerations:
Example 7.2. Suppose that F is (additive and) exact. Then I claim that √Ç is
sent to BÃÇ by LF .
Indeed, let P ‚Ä¢ be a complex in √Ç: H i (P ‚Ä¢) = 0 for i = 0. The image LF (P ‚Ä¢)
is obtained by choosing a projective resolution PF ‚Ä¢ (P ‚Ä¢ ) of F (P ‚Ä¢ ). Since quasi-
isomorphisms preserve cohomology and exact functors commute with cohomology
(Exercise 3.7),
H i (LF (P ‚Ä¢)) = H i (PF ‚Ä¢ (P ‚Ä¢)) ‚àº
 = H i (F (P ‚Ä¢)) = ‚àº
 F (H i (P ‚Ä¢)) = 0
‚Ä¢for i = 0. Therefore LF (P ) is an object of BÃÇ.
We also see that H 0 (LF (P ‚Ä¢ )) ‚àº
 = F (H 0 (P ‚Ä¢ )) in this case: this says that the
restriction of LF to √Ç ‚Üí BÃÇ agrees with F if F is exact, modulo the equivalences
of categories between A, resp., B, and √Ç, resp., BÃÇ.
 
The moral of Example 7.2 is that deriving exact functors is relatively straight-
forward, and we cannot expect to learn anything new about an exact functor by
deriving it. We will mostly be interested in deriving functors that are not exact on
the nose but preserve a certain amount of exactness.

Proposition 7.3. The left-derived functor LF satisÔ¨Åes the following universal
property:
‚Ä¢ There is a natural transformation
LF ‚ó¶ PA ; PB ‚ó¶ K(F );
‚Ä¢ for every functor G : K‚àí (P(A)) ‚Üí K‚àí (P(B)) and every natural transformation
Œ≥ : G ‚ó¶PA ; PB ‚ó¶K(F ), there is a unique (up to natural isomorphism) natural
transformation G ; LF inducing a factorization of Œ≥: G ‚ó¶ PA ; LF ‚ó¶ PA ;
PB ‚ó¶ K(F ).
Thus, excuse our poor diagram, for it is doing its best to commute, and any
choice of a bottom side other than LF would make it commute even less.
Proof. If we have done our homework (and in particular Exercise 6.4), then we
know that there is a natural transformation
IA ‚ó¶ PA ; idK‚àí (A) ;
composing on the left by PB ‚ó¶ K(F ) gives the Ô¨Årst point, since LF = PB ‚ó¶ K(F ) ‚ó¶
IA , by deÔ¨Ånition.
To verify the second point, note that every natural transformation G ; LF
will induce a natural transformation Œ≥ as in the statement, by the Ô¨Årst point; on
the other hand, every factorization of Œ≥,
G ‚ó¶ PA ; LF ‚ó¶ PA ; P B ‚ó¶ K(F ),
comes from one and only one (up to natural isomorphism) natural transformation
G ; LF , because this can be recovered by composing on the right by idI A (and
again using Exercise 6.4):
‚àº
G ; G ‚ó¶ PA ‚ó¶ IA ; PB ‚ó¶ K(F ) ‚ó¶ IA = LF .
This concludes the proof.

Remark 7.4. The fact that there is a natural transformation I ‚ó¶ P ; id (Exer-
cise 6.4), used in the proof of Proposition 7.3, has another interesting consequence.
Let A, B, C be abelian categories, and let F : A ‚Üí B, G : B ‚Üí C be additive func-
tors. Assume that A and B have enough projectives, so that the derived functors
LF and LG are deÔ¨Åned, as well as the derived functor L(G ‚ó¶ F ). Then there is a
natural transformation
LG ‚ó¶ LF ; L(G ‚ó¶ F ).
Indeed, in the same notational style as above, LG ‚ó¶ LF expands to
PC ‚ó¶ K(G ) ‚ó¶ IB ‚ó¶ P B ‚ó¶ K(F ) ‚ó¶ IA ,
and contracting IB ‚ó¶ PB to idK‚àí (B) takes this to
PC ‚ó¶ K(G ) ‚ó¶ K(F ) ‚ó¶ IA = L(G ‚ó¶ F ).
If additional conditions are satisÔ¨Åed (for example, if F preserves projectives), then
this natural transformation is a natural isomorphism, so L ‚Äòdistributes‚Äô through
compositions in this case (Exercise 7.4).
Towards the end of the chapter the reader will look again at the relation between
LG ‚ó¶ LF and L(G ‚ó¶ F ) from a diÔ¨Äerent point of view, using spectral sequences
(Exercises 8.8 and 9.10).

7.3. Taking cohomology. The content of Proposition 7.3 is again in line with our
main strategy: in moving from A to B, the left derived functor F is the functor that
preserves ‚Äòas much cohomological information as possible‚Äô regarding bounded-above
complexes, in the sense that it is the closest one can get to extending F : A ‚Üí B
to a functor D‚àí (A) ‚Üí D‚àí (B).
Applying cohomology extracts this information; actually, when deriving on the
left, the indexing works better if we take homology rather than cohomology. The
i-th left-derived functor of F is the functor Li F := H ‚àíi ‚ó¶ LF . Note that
LiF (P ‚Ä¢ ) = H ‚àíi (PB (C(F )(P ‚Ä¢))) ‚àº
 = H ‚àíi (C(F )(P ‚Ä¢)),
since PB(C(F )(P ‚Ä¢ )) is quasi-isomorphic to C(F )(P ‚Ä¢ ). Thus, if cohomology is all
‚Ä¢we are interested in, it is not necessary to Ô¨Ånd a projective resolution of C(F )(P ).
(In particular, we can deÔ¨Åne Li F even if B does not have enough projectives.)
At this point we can come down to earth and deÔ¨Åne each Li F as a functor A ‚Üí
B: we know that this is a somewhat limited scope (because Li F can properly be
‚àídeÔ¨Åned on the much subtler D (A)), but it will be the source of the only applications
we will really consider at any level of depth, and historically this is how derived
functors arose originally.
DeÔ¨Ånition 7.5. Let A, B be abelian categories, and assume that A has enough
projectives. Let F : A ‚Üí B be an additive functor. The i-th left-derived functor
Li F of F is the functor A ‚Üí B given by
LiF = H ‚àíi ‚ó¶ LF ‚ó¶ PA ‚ó¶ ŒπA .
For an object M in A, the complex in C(B) with Li F (M ) in degree ‚àíi and with
vanishing diÔ¨Äerentials is denoted by L‚Ä¢F (M ).
 
646
 IX. Homological algebra
Let‚Äôs spell this out. Given an object M of A, Li F (M ) is obtained by Ô¨Ånding
‚Ä¢
 ‚Ä¢
any projective resolution PM
 of M , applying the functor C(F ) to PM
 to obtain
a complex in C(B), and taking the (‚àíi)-th cohomology of this complex31 . Up to
isomorphism, the result does not depend on the choice of the projective resolution:
this is clear from the path of concepts that led us here, and it is directly implied by
‚Ä¢ Proposition 6.4, showing that any two projective resolutions are homotopy
equivalent, in conjunction with
‚Ä¢ Theorem 4.14, showing that the images of homotopy equivalent complexes via
an additive functor have isomorphic cohomology.
Of course we can similarly deÔ¨Åne the i-th right-derived functor Ri F of an
additive functor F : A ‚Üí B and complexes R ‚Ä¢ F (M ), provided that A has enough
injectives: concretely, Ri F (M ) is the cohomology of the image of an injective
resolution of M , i.e.,
Ri F = H i ‚ó¶ RF ‚ó¶ QA ‚ó¶ ŒπA .
So far, I have always implicitly assumed that F was a covariant functor. Con-
travariant functors A ‚Üí B should be viewed as covariant functors Aop ‚Üí B (DeÔ¨Åni-
tion VIII.1.1); the roles of injectives and projectives should therefore be swapped.
Thus, the right-derived functors of an additive contravariant functor A ‚Üí B will
be deÔ¨Åned if A has enough projectives: Aop will then have enough injectives, as
needed.
The attentive reader already knows two families of examples of derived func-
tors, both deÔ¨Åned from the category R-Mod of modules over a (commutative) ring
to itself. Recall that R-Mod has both enough projectives and injectives (as seen
in ¬ßVIII.6.2 and ¬ßVIII.6.3); thus every covariant/contravariant functor R-Mod ‚Üí
R-Mod can be derived on the left and on the right.
Example 7.6. Every R-module N determines a functor
 ‚äóR N : M ‚Üí M ‚äóR N

free resolution of M ; free modules are projective, so this was simply a convenient
way to choose a projective resolution. The fact that we could use any projective
resolution of M to compute TorR

was attributed there to the ‚Äòmagic of homological algebra‚Äô. This piece of magic has
now been explained in gory detail.
However, I also mentioned that Ô¨Çat resolutions may be used in place of pro-

applies to the fact, mentioned in ¬ßVIII.2.4, that Tori (M, N ) may in fact be com-

Example 7.7. Similarly, HomR admits a right-derived functor R HomR , and its
manifestations as the right-derived functors of HomR (M, ) are the Ext modules:
ExtiR (M, N ) is (isomorphic to) the i-th cohomology of HomR (M, N ‚Ä¢ ), where N ‚Ä¢ is
an injective resolution of N . As mentioned in ¬ßVIII.6.4, it is also the i-th cohomol-
ogy of HomR (M ‚Ä¢, N ), where M ‚Ä¢ is a projective resolution of M . This projective
resolution should really be viewed as an injective resolution in the opposite category
R-Modop , since the functor HomR ( , N ) is contravariant.
At this point we understand why the results of these operations are independent
of the chosen injective/projective resolution. We are, however, not quite ready to
verify that the two strategies lead to the same Ext functor; this last point will also
be clariÔ¨Åed in ¬ß8.
Also note that we could deÔ¨Åne the Ext functors as functors to Ab on any
abelian category with enough injectives and/or projectives: any abelian category
has left-exact Hom functors32 to Ab.
 
7.4. Long exact sequence of derived functors. The most remarkable property
of the functors Tori and Exti mentioned in Chapter VIII is probably that they
‚Äòrepair‚Äô the lack of exactness of ‚äó, Hom, respectively, in the sense that they agree
with these functors in degree 0 and they Ô¨Åt into long exact sequences. I stated the
existence of these exact sequences in ¬ßVIII.2.4 and ¬ßVIII.6.4, without proof (save
for indications in case the base ring R is a PID); now we are ready to understand
fully why these sequences exist, in the general context of derived functors.
I will keep assuming that A has enough projectives. We will prove that every
short exact sequence
0
 / L
 / M
 / N
 / 0
in A induces a ‚Äòlong exact sequence of derived functors‚Äô; the sequences for Tor
and Ext encountered in Chapter VIII will be particular cases. Surely the reader
expects this general fact to follow one way or the other from the long exact coho-
mology sequence (Theorem 3.5); that reader will not be disappointed.
From a more sophisticated perspective, what happens is that derived functors
Ô¨Åt the vertices of a ‚Äòdistinguished triangle‚Äô in the derived category: as I mentioned
in passing in ¬ß4.2, these triangles play the role of exact sequences in the homotopic
and derived categories, which do not happen to be abelian. Distinguished triangles
give rise to long exact sequences, in much the same way as do exact sequences of
complexes in the abelian case explored in ¬ß3.3.
Since we do not have the machinery of triangulated categories at our disposal,
we have to resort to bringing the action back to the ordinary category of complexes,
with the aim of using Theorem 3.5. The key point is therefore the following:
assuming that A has enough projectives and that
0
 / L
 / M
 / N
 / 0
is an exact sequence in A, can we arrange for projective resolutions of L, M , N to
form an exact sequence in C(A)?
32In fact, the presence of injectives or projectives may be bypassed if we adopt the ‚ÄòYoneda‚Äô
viewpoint on Ext.
648
 IX. Homological algebra
Yes. This is often called the ‚Äòhorseshoe lemma‚Äô, after the shape of the main
diagram appearing in its proof.
Lemma 7.8. Let
(*)
 0
 / L

 / N
 / 0
be an exact sequence in an abelian category A with enough projectives. Assume
‚Ä¢ ‚Ä¢P L , PN are projective resolutions of L, N , respectively. Then there exists an exact
sequence
(**)
 0
 / P ‚Ä¢
 / P ‚Ä¢
 / P ‚Ä¢
 / 0
L
 M

‚Ä¢
where PM
 is a projective resolution of M , inducing (*) in cohomology.
By ‚Äòinducing (*) in cohomology‚Äô I mean that the (not too) long exact cohomol-
ogy sequence induced by (**) reduces to the H 0 part, since all other cohomology
objects of a resolution vanish; identifying the zero-th cohomology of the resolutions

7.5. Relating F , Li F , RiF . The reader may have noticed that ‚äó was derived
to the left to obtain Tor, while Hom was derived to the right to obtain Ext. The
asymmetry mandating this choice lies in the fact that ‚äó is right-exact, while Hom is
left-exact: any additive functor can be derived to the left or to the right (in the pres-
ence of enough projectives, resp., enough injectives), and the derived functors will
Ô¨Åt in long exact sequences as proven in Theorem 7.12; but only functors satisfying
a measure of exactness can be recovered directly from their derived versions.
To state this fact more precisely, we go back to general considerations for the
left-derived functor of an additive functor F : A ‚Üí B, where A and B are abelian
categories and A has enough projectives, and we now assume that F is right-exact.
Proposition 7.13. Let F : A ‚Üí B be a right-exact additive functor. Then Li F =
0 for i < 0, and L0 F is naturally isomorphic to F .

No identiÔ¨Åcation of F with any of its derived functors should be expected if F
does not satisfy some exactness property. If F is exact on the nose, then both L0 F
and R0 F agree with F (up to natural isomorphism); but this is no cause for great
excitement, since all other Li F , RiF vanish in this case (Exercise 7.8). As already
pointed out in Example 7.2, one should not expect to learn anything new from an
exact functor by deriving it.
7.6. Example: A little group cohomology. In the beginning of this chapter
I pointed out that the general strategy informing the development of homological
algebra has numerous applications and I mentioned group and sheaf cohomology
as examples. Here are a few words on group cohomology (sheaf cohomology would
take us too far).
How do we extract ‚Äòcohomological invariants‚Äô from a group G? Consider the
category G-Mod of abelian groups endowed with a left-G-action, equivalently, the
category of left-Z[G]-modules, where Z[G] is the group ring brieÔ¨Çy encountered
in ¬ßIII.1.4. Objects of G-Mod may be called G-modules.
For a G-module M , M G denotes the set of elements that are Ô¨Åxed under the
action of G: these are reasonably called the invariants of the action. Note that
M G is an abelian group carrying a trivial action of G; it is clear that setting
M ‚Üí M G deÔ¨Ånes a covariant functor ¬∑G : G-Mod ‚Üí Ab. Both G-Mod and Ab
are abelian categories, and it takes a moment to realize that ¬∑G is a left-exact
functor: the reader should either check this directly or do Exercise 7.16 and then
remember Claim VIII.1.19. The reader should in fact contemplate why this functor
is not right-exact: if G acts trivially on a coset [m] of a quotient M/L, there is no
reason a priori why G should act trivially on a representative m.
We would have drawn this conclusion without diÔ¨Éculty before delving into ho-
mological algebra. Homological algebra tells us how to address this type of situation
and ‚Äòquantify‚Äô precisely the failure of exactness, by means of an appropriate derived
functor.
The i-th right-derived functor of ¬∑G is denoted H i(G, ). Therefore, H 0 (G, M ) =
M G
, and for every short exact sequence of G-modules
0

 / M
 / N
 / 0
we obtain (from Theorem 7.12) a long exact sequence of abelian groups:
0

 / H 1 (G, M )
 / ¬∑¬∑¬∑ .
656

Note that M G = HomZ[G] (Z, M ), where Z is given the trivial module structure.
Thus, H i (G, M ) = Exti Z[G](Z, M ); as we know already (and as will Ô¨Ånally be veriÔ¨Åed
in ¬ß8), this can be computed by an injective resolution of M or by a projective
resolution of Z.

7.14. ¬¨ ‚ÄòMorphisms‚Äô {T i } ‚Üí {S i} of Œ¥-functors (Exercise 7.13) are natural trans-
formations of the individual functors T i ; S i that induce morphisms of the cor-
responding long exact sequences (‚Ä°), for every short exact sequence (‚ÄÝ) (in other
words, that preserve the connecting morphisms Œ¥).
A Œ¥-functor {T i } is universal if for every Œ¥-functor {S i }, every natural trans-
formation T 0 ; S 0 extends to a unique morphism of Œ¥-functors {T i } ‚Üí {S i }.
Universal Œ¥-functors are of course unique up to isomorphism.
It is not hard (but it involves a fair number of diagram chases) to show that a
Œ¥-functor {T i } is universal if for every i > 0 and every object A of A there exists a
monomorphism i : A ‚Üí B for some B, such that T i (i) = 0. This makes each T i ,
i > 0, eÔ¨Äaceable. Figure out how the proof begins: assume that {T i } and {S i} are
Œ¥-functors and that T 1 is eÔ¨Äaceable; show how to extend a natural transformation

morphisms. (Hint: If T 1 is eÔ¨Äaced by i : A ‚Üí B, let C = coker i and consider the
exact sequence 0 ‚Üí A ‚Üí B ‚Üí C ‚Üí 0.) [7.15]

(Hint: To eÔ¨Äace RiF for i > 0, use injectives. To eÔ¨Äace H i for i > 0, stare at the
sequence of complexes mentioned right before the statement of Proposition 4.1.)
The upshot is that in order to verify that a given collection of functors agrees
with the derived functors of a given (say) left-exact functor, it suÔ¨Éces to verify that
they form a cohomological Œ¥-functor and that the eÔ¨Äaceability condition holds. This
is used, for example, to obtain concrete realizations of sheaf cohomology. [8.14]

8. Double complexes
A few mysteries remain on the table concerning Tor and Ext: the fact that, e.g.,
Ô¨Çat resolutions may be used in place of projective ones in order to compute Tor and
the fact that resolving ‚Äòeither argument‚Äô leads to the same functors. For example,
ExtiR (M, N ) may be computed by using a projective resolution of M or an injective
resolution of N .
Both mysteries are (of course) instances of general features of the theory, which
we examine in this section. The Ô¨Årst can be dispelled with a pleasant inductive
662
 IX. Homological algebra
argument that seems worth presenting (¬ß8.1). But everything is clariÔ¨Åed substan-
tially by going one step further and considering double complexes, as we do in the
remaining subsections.
8.1. Resolution by acyclic objects. Let A be an abelian category with enough
projectives, and let F : A ‚Üí B be an additive functor to another abelian category.
At this point we know how to construct left-derived functors Li F : A ‚Üí B of F :
in two words, Li F (M ) is computed by applying F to a projective resolution of M
and taking (co)homology of the resulting complex.
It follows in particular that Li F (P ) = 0 for i ‚â• 1 if P is projective (cf. Exer-
cise 7.9): if P is projective, then Œπ(P ):
¬∑¬∑¬∑

is a projective resolution of P , so L‚Ä¢ F (P ) is the cohomology of
¬∑¬∑¬∑

i = 0.
This says that projective objects are ‚ÄòF -acyclic‚Äô with respect to left-derived func-
tors:
DeÔ¨Ånition 8.1. Let F be an additive functor. An object M of A is F -acyclic
(w.r.t. left-derived functors) if Li F (M ) = 0 for i = 0.
 
An object M is F -acyclic with respect to right-derived functors if Ri F (M ) = 0
for i = 0. In practice, this notion is really useful only for right-exact or left-exact
functors; the Ô¨Årst are derived on the left, and the second on the right (cf. ¬ß7.5), so
in context we can just talk about ‚ÄòF -acyclic‚Äô objects.
While projectives are acyclic for every right-exact functor, a given functor F
may admit other F -acyclic objects.
Example 8.2. Let R be a commutative ring. Recall (DeÔ¨Ånition VIII.2.13) that an
R-module M is Ô¨Çat if ‚äóR M is exact, or equivalently (by the symmetry of ‚äó) if
M ‚äóR is exact. Flat modules are acyclic with respect to tensor products, in a very
strong sense: if N is Ô¨Çat, then Tori (M, N ) = 0 for i = 0 and all M ; see ¬ßVIII.2.4 to
be reminded of why, or‚Äîbetter‚Äîprove it anew. Further, since (as we will Ô¨Ånally
prove in this section) Tor functors may be computed by resolving either argument,
we also know that, ‚Äòsymmetrically‚Äô, if M is Ô¨Çat, then Tori (M, N ) = 0 for all i = 0
and all modules N . Thus, a Ô¨Çat module is F -acyclic for every functor F deÔ¨Åned
by ‚äóR N , for all modules N .
 
Here is the punch line. In ¬ßVIII.6.4 I had claimed that Ô¨Çat resolutions could
be used in place of free or projective resolutions, in order to compute Tor. We are
going to verify that F -acyclic resolutions suÔ¨Éce in order to compute Li F .

reader will run into such examples in more advanced treatments of the subject.
(See Exercise 8.14 for a typical situation.)

Taking direct sums of the objects and morphisms on each row, as in the case of the
mapping cone, determines a new complex, called the total complex T C(M )‚Ä¢ of the
given complex of complexes.
Double complexes capture this construction:

Double complexes, with or without appropriate boundedness conditions (such as
requiring M i,j = 0 for i > 0, j > 0, as in the case considered above), form a
category in an evident way. It is equally evident that this category is equivalent
to the correspondingly bounded category of complexes of complexes: every object
‚â§0 ‚â§0 i,jof C (C (A)) determines a double complex by setting Œ¥v = (‚àí1)i di,j

Changing the signs of odd rows rather than columns leads to an isomorphic double
complex, Exercise 8.4; in fact, other choices may be convenient depending on the
situation.

Claim 8.7. (T C(M )‚Ä¢ , d‚Ä¢ tot ) is indeed a complex.
Proof. The question is whether the composition of two consecutive diÔ¨Äerentials
vanishes, as it should. Insisting with our shorthand,
dtot ‚ó¶ dtot = (dh + Œ¥v ) ‚ó¶(dh + Œ¥v ) = dh ‚ó¶ dh + dh ‚ó¶ Œ¥v + Œ¥v ‚ó¶ dh + Œ¥ v ‚ó¶ Œ¥v = 0.
The reader will formalize this computation (Exercise 8.3).

‚Ä¢It is clear from the deÔ¨Ånition that T C(M ) agrees with the mapping cone,
when M ‚Ä¢,‚Ä¢ is concentrated in degrees ‚àí1 and 0.
The total complex is deÔ¨Åned even without any boundedness hypothesis, but
then one has to choose whether to use direct sums or direct products as objects of
the complex. Since only bounded complexes will be needed in the applications we
will see, I will happily leave such complications aside and limit the discussion to
the bounded case.

8.3. Exactness of the total complex. The cohomology of the total complex of
a double complex can be computed by an extremely clever device called spectral
sequence, which we will (brieÔ¨Çy) encounter in ¬ß9.3. There are standard situations,
however, in which one can conclude immediately that the total complex is exact,
and this is at the root of the applications I will present. The statement is neat and
memorable:
Theorem 8.9. Let A be an abelian category, and let
(*)
 ¬∑¬∑¬∑
 / M ‚àí3,‚Ä¢
 / M ‚àí2,‚Ä¢
 / M ‚àí1,‚Ä¢
 / M 0,‚Ä¢
 / 0
 / ¬∑¬∑¬∑
‚â§0 ‚â§0 ‚Ä¢be a complex in C (C (A)). Let T be the corresponding total complex. Then
‚Ä¢ if (*) is exact, then T ‚Ä¢ is exact;
‚Ä¢ if each complex M i,‚Ä¢ is exact, then T ‚Ä¢ is exact.
The proof will yield a more precise statement on the vanishing of individual
degrees of the cohomology of the total complex (Exercise 8.6).
‚â•0 ‚â•0Mutatis mutandis, Theorem 8.9 holds for complexes in C (C (A)), with mi-
croscopic adjustments to the proof; I will take this for granted in the applications.
In fact, the boundedness hypothesis can be relaxed: one of the two given exactness
statements will still hold if only one of the C is bounded. The point is that the
proof relies on an inductive argument, which goes through as soon as it has a place
to start. The reader is welcome to explore all these ramiÔ¨Åcations.
In terms of double complexes, Theorem 8.9 says that the total complex of a
(suitably bounded) double complex is exact if the rows or the columns of the double
complex are exact.
Proof. It is enough to prove the second statement: the Ô¨Årst one follows by Ô¨Çipping
the double complex corresponding to (*) (cf. Exercise 8.4).
34A clever way out of the sign quagmire in this computation is to choose another way to
get a double complex out of HomA (L‚Ä¢ , M ‚Ä¢). For example, replacing the vertical diÔ¨Äerentials di,j
 v
by (‚àí1)i+j+1di,j
 v still makes the array anticommutative as needed and irons out annoying sign
mismatches elsewhere.

The little induction that accomplishes this is conceptually very simple, but
notationally challenging. I believe that viewing it in action in a simple case (I will
choose i = 2) suÔ¨Éces to convey all that needs to be conveyed. Thus, assume that
m = (m0 , m‚àí1 , m‚àí2 ) is in the kernel of dtot ; that is,

dh (m0) + Œ¥v (m‚àí1 ) = 0,
‚àí1 ‚àí2dh (m ) + Œ¥v (m ) = 0.
I choose n0 = 0. Since M ‚àí2,‚Ä¢ is exact, there exists n‚àí1 ‚àà M ‚àí2,‚àí1 such that
Œ¥v (n‚àí1 ) = m0 . Since the diagram anticommutes,
Œ¥v ‚ó¶ dh (n‚àí1 ) = ‚àídh ‚ó¶ Œ¥v (n‚àí1 ) = ‚àídh (m0 ) = Œ¥v (m‚àí1 ).
Therefore, m‚àí1 ‚àí dh (n‚àí1 ) ‚àà ker Œ¥ v , and since M ‚àí1,‚Ä¢ is exact, there exists n‚àí2 ‚àà
M ‚àí1,‚àí2 such that Œ¥v (n‚àí2) = m‚àí1 ‚àí d h (n‚àí1 ). That is,
‚àí1 ‚àí2 ‚àí1d h (n ) + Œ¥v (n ) = m .
The next step is entirely analogous:
Œ¥v ‚ó¶ dh (n‚àí2 ) = ‚àídh ‚ó¶ Œ¥v (n‚àí2 ) = ‚àídh (m‚àí1 ‚àí d h (n‚àí1 )) = ‚àídh(m‚àí1 ) = Œ¥v (m‚àí2 )
(using the fact that dh ‚ó¶ dh = 0); therefore m‚àí2 ‚àí dh (n‚àí2 ) ‚àà ker Œ¥v , and since M 0,‚Ä¢
is exact, there exists n‚àí3 ‚àà M 0,‚àí2 such that Œ¥v (n‚àí3 ) = m‚àí2 ‚àí dh (n‚àí2 ). That is,
d h (n‚àí2 ) + Œ¥v (n‚àí3) = m‚àí2 .

With these choices, dtot (n) equals

as needed.
The case for arbitrary i simply requires additional repetitions of the basic steps
performed for i = 2.

‚Ä¢Example 8.10. Here is a taste of how convenient Theorem 8.9 is. Let P be a
complex in C‚â§0(A), where each P i is projective, and let L‚Ä¢ be an exact complex
in C‚â•0(A). Since P i is projective, HomA (P i , L‚Ä¢ ) is still exact. Therefore, the
complex
¬∑¬∑¬∑
 / 0
 / HomA (P 0, L‚Ä¢ )
 / HomA (P ‚àí1 , L‚Ä¢ )
 / HomA(P ‚àí2 , L‚Ä¢ )
 / ¬∑¬∑¬∑
satisÔ¨Åes the hypothesis of the second statement given in Theorem 8.9 (in the sym-
metrically bounded version). According to Theorem 8.9, the corresponding to-
tal complex is exact; as seen in Example 8.8 (cf. Exercise 8.5), this says that
HomK(A) (P ‚Ä¢ , L[i]‚Ä¢ ) = 0 for all i. In other words, every cochain morphism from a
bounded-above complex of projectives to a bounded-below exact complex is homo-
topic to 0. This recovers Corollary 5.12, up to easy adjustments, taking care of the
boundedness hypothesis on L‚Ä¢ .
 
8.4. Total complexes and resolutions. Example 8.10 illustrates well the power
of results such as Theorem 8.9: the nuts-and-bolts-style arguments used in ¬ß5 are
bypassed, and statements such as Corollary 5.12 are placed in a clarifying context35 .
The same strategy will allow us to revisit the question examined in ¬ß8.1, letting a
double complex draw the conclusion of Theorem 8.3. We will then Ô¨Ånally be able
to go back to the last remaining mystery concerning Tor and Ext, dealing with why
we can compute these functors ‚Äòby resolving either argument‚Äô. This will conclude
a large circle of ideas, begun in Chapter VIII.
These applications rely on one more remark concerning double complexes and
their simples. The heavy notation poses a substantial obstacle in appreciating what
follows; the reader should take comfort in my assurance that notation is the only
obstacle here‚Äîthe constructions are actually straightforward.
We have studied in ¬ß6.2 the problem of constructing complexes of projectives
‚Ä¢(for example) that are quasi-isomorphic to a given complex N : Theorem 6.6 ac-
complishes this. I have called the corresponding quasi-isomorphism a (projective)
‚Ä¢ ‚â§0 ‚â§0resolution of N . Now that we are working in C (C (A)), this looks like an even
‚Ä¢worse abuse of language: viewing a (bounded-above) complex N as an object of
 ‚â§0 ‚Ä¢the abelian category A = C (A), a (bounded-above) resolution of N ought to be
‚Ä¢,‚Ä¢ ‚â§0 a complex M in C (A ) endowed with a quasi-isomorphism

The statement of Theorem 8.12 extends that of Theorem 8.9; its proof is a
nearly immediate consequence of the latter. As I mentioned at the beginning of ¬ß8.3,
these statements are vastly generalized by the machinery of spectral sequences,
which provide a general framework for the computation of the cohomology of the
total complex of a double complex. We will recover Theorem 8.12 once we have
learned a bit about spectral sequences in ¬ß9.3, but it is not hard (and it is a good
exercise) to prove this statement by hand, as I proceed to do.
Proof. The second statement follows from the Ô¨Årst, by Ô¨Çipping the corresponding
double complex about the main diagonal.

To appreciate the usefulness of Theorem 8.12, again in the context of our (hard)
work in ¬ß6, note that it provides us with a convenient bridge between resolving
objects (see Lemma 6.3) and resolving complexes (as in Theorem 6.6). Indeed, by
Corollary 7.9 (which relies on the horseshoe lemma, therefore on resolving individual
‚Ä¢objects of A) every bounded-above complex N in an abelian category with enough
projectives may be viewed as the complex induced in H 0 from a complex

reproduces the main content of Theorem 6.6, bypassing the nastiest details of the
proof given in ¬ß6.
As mentioned in Remark 7.10, double complexes of projectives (or, working
‚â•0in C , injectives) resolving a given complex (and satisfying the additional condition
explained in Remark 7.10) are called Cartan-Eilenberg resolutions. Their systematic
use can simplify the material I presented in ¬ß6 but at the price of dealing early with
double complexes.

back to the last mysteries remaining concerning Tor and Ext. As a warm-up (and

tells us how to Ô¨Åll the rest of the array: start from

This example illustrates the general strategy of other applications of double
complexes: the cohomologies of two complexes are shown to be isomorphic by
showing that there is an exact double complex interpolating between them. This
strategy Ô¨Ånally justiÔ¨Åes the claims that Tor and Ext functors can be computed by
resolving either one of their arguments.

‚Ä¢
Theorem 8.14. Let M, N be modules over a commutative ring R, and let PM
 ,
resp., Q‚Ä¢ N , be a projective resolution of M , resp., an injective resolution of N .
Then
H i (HomR(PM
 ‚Ä¢
 , N )) ‚àº
 = H i(HomR (M, Q‚Ä¢ N )) (‚àº
 = ExtiR (M, N )).
The proof of Theorem 8.14 is left to the reader (Exercise 8.9): Example 8.8
and the strategy extensively discussed above will hopefully make this a very easy
task.
This statement completes the veriÔ¨Åcation of all claims concerning Tor and Ext
made in Chapter VIII, in the sense that the extent to which we have developed the
general theory of homological algebra makes those seemingly magical claims now
essentially evident. Hopefully, the same will apply to other encounters the reader
may have with simple applications of homological algebra.
In any case, this seems a Ô¨Åtting place to end the main body of this last chapter.
In ¬ß9 we will give a brief look at material that really lies beyond the scope of these
notes.

8.13. (See Exercises 8.11 and 8.12.) Let A be an abelian category with enough
injectives and projectives. Prove that the supremum of pd(A), A ‚àà Obj(A), equals
the supremum of id(B), B ‚àà Obj(A), and give an Ext interpretation for this num-
ber.
This is called the global dimension of A. If R is a ring, the global dimension of R
is the global dimension of R-Mod. This invariant is of fundamental importance in
commutative algebra. The relation between this number and the Krull dimension
of R is subtle and important.
8.14.  Let A, B, C be categories. A bifunctor F : A √ó B ‚Üí C is an assignment of
an object F (A, B) of C for every object A of A and B of B, such that for all objects
A in A, B in B, F A := F (A, ) is a (say, covariant) functor and FB := F ( , B) is
a (say, contravariant) functor; further, for all morphisms A 1 ‚Üí A2 in A, B1 ‚Üí B2
in B, the induced diagram
F (A1, B1 )
 / F (A 1 , B2 )
O

F (A2, B1 )
 / F (A 2 , B2 )
is required to commute. (Arrows in this diagram should be directed according to
the covariance of F in its arguments.)
For example, HomA ( , ) is a bifunctor in this sense from A √ó A to Ab. For R
a commutative ring, ‚äóR is a bifunctor R-Mod √ó R-Mod ‚Üí R-Mod, covariant in
both arguments.

Now assume that A, B, C are abelian categories and that F : A √ó B ‚Üí C is a
bifunctor, such that F A is additive and covariant, FB is additive and contravariant,
and both are left-exact. Also, assume that B has enough injectives.
Say that an object E of A is F -exact if the functor F E is exact. An ‚ÄòF -exact
resolution‚Äô of an object A of A is a resolution in C‚â§0 (A) consisting of F -exact
objects. The category A has ‚Äòenough F -exact objects‚Äô if every object A admits an
epimorphism E ‚Üí A with F -exact source. Assume that this is the case, so that
every object of A admits an F -exact resolution.
Note that we are not assuming that A has enough projectives: in principle,
therefore, we should not be able to deÔ¨Åne a right-derived functor for FB . We are
going to look for an adequate replacement, using the fact that FB is one component
of a bifunctor.
Since B has enough injectives, the functor F A can be right-derived for every
object A. DeÔ¨Åne Ti FB (A) to be Ri F A (B); note that T0 FB (A) = F A (B) =
F (A, B) = FB (A).

Then Ti F B ‚àº
 = H i (FB (E ‚Ä¢)). (Hint: Mimic the proof of Theorem 8.3.)
The upshot is that the interplay of the two functors F A and FB allows us to
deÔ¨Åne TiFB as if it were a right-derived functor, using T -exact objects in place of
projectives. This gives a universal Œ¥-functor, which agrees with the standard right-
derived functor when A has enough projectives (by Exercise 7.15 and the uniqueness
of universal Œ¥-functors). [¬ß8.1]
9. Further topics
This last section touches upon issues that arise naturally in the context of this
chapter and for which a (much) more extensive treatment would be needed. This
will hopefully whet the reader‚Äôs appetite for more and encourage further study of
homological algebra beyond the basics covered here. I will be somewhat detailed
in ¬ß9.3, illustrating the spectral sequence associated to a double complex, since
9. Further topics
 681
this can be done reasonably quickly (but delving into any other application of
spectral sequences would simply take us too far). For the other themes in this
section (derived and triangulated categories) I will really do no more than a little
propaganda.
9.1. Derived categories. Derived categories made their appearance in ¬ß4.2 and
have since served as a guiding concept motivating the natural development of the
subject, but we have stopped short of constructing them in general. The closest we
have come to a concrete description of the derived category is in the ‚Äòpoor man‚Äôs
version‚Äô of ¬ß6.3, which should actually be labeled the ‚Äòrich man‚Äôs version‚Äô, since it
relies on the fortunate presence of enough projectives/injectives. Also, the versions
D‚àí (A), D+ (A) considered there are necessarily bounded, as they rely on bounded
resolutions of objects and complexes in A.
Derived categories may be deÔ¨Åned without such restrictions. For an abelian
category A, the objects of D(A) are taken to be the same as in C(A), that is,
cochain complexes; boundedness conditions may be included here, if desired. As
for morphisms, remember that the whole point of the derived category is to ‚Äòinvert
quasi-isomorphisms‚Äô; the most direct way to do this is essentially the same as the
process that allows us to ‚Äòinvert all nonzero elements of an integral domain‚Äô, leading
to the construction of the Ô¨Åeld of fractions (see ¬ßV.4.2). This process, in a more
general version dealing with inverting ‚Äòmultiplicative subsets‚Äô of a ring, is called
localization (Exercise V.4.7). Categories may be localized as well. For example, the
homotopic category K(A) may be viewed as the localization of C(A) with respect
to homotopy equivalences: these become isomorphisms in K(A). Localizing K(A)
with respect to homotopy classes of quasi-isomorphisms yields D(A).
In necessarily oversimpliÔ¨Åed terms, suppose you want to make a class of mor-
phisms of a category C invertible. In the new category, any ‚Äòroof‚Äô diagram
~~
 Œ± ~
 ~ Z
 @ @ @ @ @
 Œ≤
 @ @
 
~~
~~
A
 _
 _
 _
 _
 _
 _
 _/ B
with Œ± in that class will determine a morphism Œ≤ ‚ó¶ Œ±‚àí1 : A ‚Üí B; that is, the dashed
arrow will have to exist as a morphism in the new category. These are the analogs
of the ‚Äòfractions‚Äô in the Ô¨Åeld of fractions; the localization of C may be deÔ¨Åned by
taking the same objects as in C and setting morphisms in the new category to be
compositions of ‚Äòroofs‚Äô, up to a suitable equivalence relation. For example, all roofs

will work as the inverse of Œ± in the localization.
As the reader can imagine, working out the construction in earnest requires
dealing with nontrivial technical issues. These are more manageable when the
class of morphisms to be inverted is ‚Äòlocalizing‚Äô (for example, the composition of
two such morphisms should also be a morphism in the class); for example, this
condition allows us to represent the composition of two roofs as a roof. To mention
one set-theoretic diÔ¨Éculty, one should make sure that morphisms A ‚Üí B in the
localization still form a set, even if the roofs connecting A to B may a priori form
a proper class.
With all these caveats in mind, the localization process can be carried out
on K(A) with respect to quasi-isomorphisms and does produce a category D(A)
satisfying the universal property for derived categories described in ¬ß4.2. If A has
‚àí(for example) enough projectives, then the bounded version D (A) is equivalent to
‚àíthe homotopy category K (P) of complexes of projectives, as I have endeavored to
justify in ¬ß6.3. The latter should be viewed as a convenient way to ‚Äòcompute‚Äô the
former in favorable circumstances.
Summarizing, a morphism L‚Ä¢ ‚Üí M ‚Ä¢ in the derived category D(A) of an abelian
category A can be represented as a roof diagram
‚Ä¢quasi-isomorphism }{

L‚Ä¢

of (homotopy classes of) morphisms of cochain complexes, modulo a suitable equiv-
alence relation. Note that the construction is applied to K(A), rather than the
simpler-minded C(A): for one thing, one might as well start from K(A), since the
functor C(A) ‚Üí D(A) will have to factor through the homotopic category (Propo-
sition 5.4). In any case it just so happens that, unfortunately, quasi-isomorphisms
do not form a localizing class of morphisms in C(A), while their homotopy classes
are localizing in K(A). So it goes.
Once we believe that this prescription does deÔ¨Åne a category‚Äîfor example, that
the composition of two roofs can be represented by a roof and that this composition
is associative‚Äîthen it is essentially clear that D(A) satisÔ¨Åes the universal property
speciÔ¨Åed in ¬ß4.2. However, the real beneÔ¨Åts of introducing the derived category lie
deeper, for example in its structure as a triangulated category. A brief discussion
of this concept follows.
The reader should check that the composition of two roofs is indeed a roof
(Exercise 9.2); this may be easier after acquiring a little familiarity with distin-
guished triangles. Also, note that the diagram in Exercise 9.2 does not commute
9. Further topics
 683
in C(A), but it does in K(A). This is an indication that it would be problematic to
go ‚Äòdirectly‚Äô from C(A) to D(A).
9.2. Triangulated categories. One reason for not treating derived categories to
any real extent in this book is that to do so would require us to deÔ¨Åne precisely the
notion of triangulated category. The same applies in fact to homotopic categories of
complexes. I have pointed out in ¬ß5.2 that these categories should not be expected
to be (and indeed are not) abelian, but they preserve enough structure to make sense
of, for example, long exact cohomology sequences. The fact that derived functors
ended up having long exact sequences associated to them (¬ß7.4) bears witness to
this fact.
The essential ingredients of a triangulated (additive) category are a ‚Äòtranslation‚Äô
functor, which in the case of K(A) or D(A) is realized as the shift functor L‚Ä¢ ‚Üí L[1]‚Ä¢ ;
and a class of diagrams

obtained in Proposition 4.1. In general, a cohomological functor on a triangulated
category is an additive functor to an abelian category, mapping distinguished trian-

is a cohomological functor on K(A); this will not surprise the reader. The functors
Hom(A, ) and Hom( , A) (to Ab) are cohomological functors on every triangulated
category, for every object A.
The derived category D(A) and its bounded versions are triangulated categories,
with distinguished triangles described as above‚Äîthat is, isomorphic (in D(A)) to
the basic triangles determined by mapping cones. The natural functor K(A) ‚Üí D(A)
is a functor of triangulated categories, in the sense that it preserves the translation
functor and it sends distinguished triangles to distinguished triangles.
Isomorphism in the derived category is a less stringent notion than in the ho-
motopic category, so there are ‚Äòmore‚Äô distinguished triangles in D(A) than in K(A).

If F : A ‚Üí B is a functor between abelian categories, the induced functor
K(F ) : K(A) ‚Üí K(B) is a functor of triangulated categories, and so are the bounded
variations K¬± (F ) : K¬± (A) ‚Üí K¬± (B). The derived functors LF , RF : D¬± (A) ‚Üí
D¬± (B) encountered in ¬ß7.2 are also functors of triangulated categories. This fact
is responsible for the good properties of derived functors, such as the long exact
sequences studied in ¬ß7.4.
9.3. Spectral sequences. I mentioned in ¬ß8.3 that ‚Äòspectral sequences‚Äô may be
used to compute the cohomology of a double complex. There unfortunately does not
seem to be an easy entry point in the subject of spectral sequences, which is marred
by intrinsic notational complexity. I will limit myself to a very scant description of
the concept and some information linking it back to ¬ß8.4. My motivation is that,
in the literature, references to results such as Theorem 8.12 are often replaced by
sentences such as ‚Äòby an immediate spectral sequence argument. . . ‚Äô, and I should
try to explain to the reader what this means.

The datum of an exact triangle as above is called an exact couple, which I Ô¨Ånd
confusing since a triangle has three vertices (but it is true that only two objects
are involved here); the new exact triangle (couple) obtained in Claim 9.1 is the
derived couple, which I Ô¨Ånd even more confusing since there is no derived category
or functor in sight. Exact couples arose in topology (they are due to W. Massey),
and the terminology reÔ¨Çects their origin.
Of course we can turn the crank at will and get derived couple after derived
couple: let A1 = A, E1 = E, etc., and inductively deÔ¨Åne Ai+1 := A i , Ei+1 := Ei  ,

‚Ä¢,‚Ä¢DeÔ¨Ånition 9.3. The spectral sequence of the double complex M (with respect to
the vertical Ô¨Åltration) is the spectral sequence determined by this exact couple. 
I will denote this spectral sequence by v E, with the absurdly positioned index v
recording that v E arises from the vertical Ô¨Åltration. The horizontal Ô¨Åltration leads
likewise to a spectral sequence h E.
All terms v E r of the spectral sequence are subquotients of the Ô¨Årst one, v E 1 =
H ‚Ä¢ (gr‚Ä¢ v (T )).
Remark 9.4. The diÔ¨Äerential dr = Œ≤r ‚ó¶ Œ≥r acts by increasing the cohomological
degree k by 1 and the Ô¨Åltration degree m by r: indeed, Œ≥r is simply induced by Œ≥,
so it increases both k and m by 1 (as observed above); Œ≤r is obtained by applying Œ≤
after undoing (r ‚àí 1) times the eÔ¨Äect of Œ± (as pointed out after Claim 9.1), so it
does not change k but increases m by (r ‚àí 1).
 
Interest in the sequence rests on the interpretation for its limit in the statement
that follows, which also summarizes the situation.
‚Ä¢,‚Ä¢Theorem 9.5. Let M be a double complex in an abelian category. Assume that
i,j ‚Ä¢ ‚Ä¢,‚Ä¢M = 0 for i < 0, j < 0, and let T be the total complex of M . Then, with
notation as above, there exists a spectral sequence {( v E i , di )}i such that

In other words, ‚Äòturning the crank‚Äô moves the gr from inside the cohomology
to outside of it. The limit v E ‚àû does not quite compute the cohomology of the
total complex, as I glibly announced in ¬ß8.3, but it computes the graded object
determined by a Ô¨Åltration on the cohomology of the total complex, and this is good
enough for many applications.

In general, v E r is the cohomology of v E r‚àí1, and its diÔ¨Äerential increases m by r
and k by 1 (Remark 9.4); that is, (i, j) ‚Üí (i+r, j ‚àí(r ‚àí1)). Pictorially, diÔ¨Äerentials
at diÔ¨Äerent stages act like this:
v E1
 v E2
 v E3
and so on. This picture of a ‚Äòrotating‚Äô diÔ¨Äerential may be the image that most people
have in mind when they think of a spectral sequence. The argument sketched
above, deriving Theorem 8.12 from Theorem 9.5, is probably clearer from this
point of view: if the cohomology of the columns is concentrated in degree 0, then
v E 1 is concentrated along the bottom row of the array. It is then clear that all
diÔ¨Äerentials di for i ‚â• 2 will have to be zero, since either their source or their target
is zero. Thus the sequence collapses at v E 2 and yields the cohomology of the total
complex by Theorem 9.5. The other situation considered in Theorem 8.12 can be
analyzed by using the sequence hE i obtained from the horizontal Ô¨Åltration.
Exercises
 695
Of course, spectral sequences are not limited to these simple applications. The
Grothendieck spectral sequence ‚Äòcomputes‚Äô the derived functor of the composition
of two functors42 : for example, if F : A ‚Üí B and G : B ‚Üí C are two right-exact
functors and F sends projectives to projectives, then there is a spectral sequence
whose E2 term collects the compositions Lp G ‚ó¶ Lq F and that abuts to Lp+q (G ‚ó¶ F ).
For instance, if f : R ‚Üí S is a homomorphism of commutative rings (so that
S may be viewed as an R-module), A is an R-module, and B is an S-module
(and hence an R-module, via f ), then there is a ‚Äòchange-of-ring spectral sequence‚Äô
TorSp (TorR
 q (A, S), B) ‚áí Tor‚Ä¢ R
 (A, B). In topology, the Serre spectral sequence can
be used to compute the homology of a Ô¨Åbration in terms of the homology of the base
and of the Ô¨Åber. It would be completely futile for me to attempt to do any justice to
the range of applications of spectral sequences: the set of mathematicians X such
that there is an important X-spectral sequence includes (but is not limited to)
Adams, Atiyah, Barratt, Bloch, Bockstein, BousÔ¨Åeld, Cartan, Connes, Eilenberg,
Federer, Fr√∂licher, Green, Grothendieck, Hirzebruch, Hochschild, Hodge, Hurewicz,
van Kampen, Kan, Kunneth, Leray, Lichtenbaum, Lyndon, May, Miller, Milnor,
Moore, Novikov, de Rham, Quillen, Rothenberg, Serre, Steenrod, . . . .
Many important spectral sequences may be derived as particular instances of
the Grothendieck spectral sequence mentioned above. The reader will have the
pleasure of seeing how this spectral sequence is put together by working out Exer-
cise 9.10.

 h P Uthe beginning graduate or upper undergraduate level. The primary
 S F / e edistinguishing feature of the book, compared to standard textbooks
 L s l a yin algebra, is the early introduction of categories, used as a unifying
 R f o ytheme in the presentation of the main topics. A second feature
 s e t r u oconsists of an emphasis on homological algebra: basic notions on
 Ccomplexes are presented as soon as modules have been introduced, and an extensive
last chapter on homological algebra can form the basis for a follow-up introduc-

practice to consolidate the understanding of the main body of the text and offer the

and algebraic geometry. This will allow instructors to adapt the textbook to their
